For the full breakdown of every standard, pull the skinny for that standard. The skinny has the 2.0 / 3.0 / 4.0 breakdown in plain language — read it before you finalize your choice.

→ Skinny Library

docs.title: Picking just one (or two)
docs.text:
Most studios have one primary standard. You're proving you can do one specific thing at a 3.0 level — that's the goal.

You can add a crosswalk standard if your studio genuinely spans both ELA and SS — but only if the demo can actually show evidence for both. A crosswalk studio is more work, not less. If you're not sure, start with one standard and add the crosswalk in your contract if Reese agrees.

docs.title: Decision 3 — Your Texts
docs.text: You need a minimum of one primary source and one secondary source. Three or four texts is typical for a full six-week studio.

docs.title: Start in the Reading Library
docs.text:
The Reading Library has 57 entries — primary sources and literary texts with embedded passages, standard connections, and studio ideas. This is your first stop.

How to find texts that fit your EQ: 1. Think about which era or topic your EQ connects to 2. Go to that section of the Reading Library 3. Read the plain-language description of each entry — if it connects to your question, open the entry 4. Check the "Studio Ideas" section of the entry — if your question is similar to one of those, you're in the right place

docs.title: Also check the Studio Packets
docs.text: Even if you're building your own studio, the Studio Packet Archive is worth browsing — each packet lists four recommended texts for its essential question. If your EQ is close to an existing packet's question, those texts probably work for you too.

docs.title: How to judge if a text works for your EQ
docs.text:
A text works if you can answer yes to both of these:

  • Can I use this text as evidence to argue an answer to my EQ? Not just "it's about the same topic" — but specifically: does this text help me take a position?
  • Does this text connect to my standard? If your standard is R.9, the text needs to be making an argument you can analyze. If your standard is R.8, the text needs to have literary elements worth analyzing.

docs.title: What to do if the text you want isn't in the library
docs.text: Bring the title and author to Reese. He'll help you determine if it fits and whether it can be added to the Reading Library. Don't build a whole studio around a text before getting that conversation in.

docs.title: Decision 4 — Your Demo Mode
docs.text: Your Demo of Learning is how you prove you understand the standard. There are six modes. Pick the one that fits your question, your standard, and how you work best.

docs.title: The six modes — plain language
docs.text:
Mode 1 — Written Assessment A focused written response to a specific prompt. Usually 300–600 words. Structured, clear, direct. Reese gives you the prompt or you propose one.

Best if: You want clear structure and a defined prompt. You work well under constraints.

Mode 2 — Extended Writing Your own essay, analysis, or argument piece — longer and more fully developed than a written assessment. 600–900 words. You set the structure and argument.

Best if: You're a writer who wants space to build a full argument. You have a lot to say about your EQ.

Mode 3 — Verbal Conversation A one-on-one conversation with Reese. You walk him through your understanding — your claim, your evidence, your counterargument. He documents what you show him.

Best if: You think better out loud than on paper. You get stuck when writing but can explain yourself clearly when talking.

Mode 4 — Visual/Creative A sketchnote, annotated diagram, concept map, or infographic that shows your understanding visually. Includes written annotations connecting each element to your standard.

Best if: You think visually. You want to show how ideas connect rather than just explain them linearly.

Mode 5 — Multimedia/Performance A podcast episode, documentary video, structured debate, or recorded presentation. Minimum 8 minutes. You demonstrate the full standard through what you made.

Best if: You want to make something people can watch or listen to. You're comfortable with audio or video production.

Mode 6 — Portfolio Annotation You select two pieces of work from previous studios and annotate them for a new standard. You write a metacognitive commentary explaining how the work demonstrates the standard and what you now understand differently.

Best if: You have prior work that connects directly to the new standard. You want to make your growth visible across studios.

docs.title: Things to consider before you choose
docs.text:
• Does this mode fit my standard? W.4 (argument writing) is hard to demonstrate in a sketchnote. R.8 (literary analysis) is hard to demonstrate in a debate. Make sure the mode lets you actually show the evidence the standard requires.
  • Does this mode fit my EQ? Some questions are better answered in writing. Others naturally become conversations or visuals.
  • What do you want to get better at? SDA is the place to push yourself. If you always do written assessments, consider trying extended writing or a verbal conversation.

docs.title: You're Ready for Your Contract
docs.text:
Once you've made these four decisions — EQ, standard, texts, demo mode — you have everything you need to fill out your Studio Contract.

→ Your Contract & Getting Approved

Design Your Studio · SDA Commons Wiki · South High School See also: Skinnies · Reading Library · Standards · Studio Packets

docs.title: When You're Done
docs.text:
SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

You've finished your studio. Your Demo is submitted, your Reflection is complete, and Reese has given you a score. This page covers what happens next — where your work goes, how it gets recorded, and what finishing a studio actually means.

docs.title: Step 1 — Submit Your Demo
docs.text:
Your Demo of Learning is submitted to Reese directly. Depending on your demo mode:

Demo Mode How to submit Written Assessment Google Doc shared with Reese from your studio folder Extended Writing Google Doc shared with Reese from your studio folder Verbal Conversation Schedule a 10–15 minute conversation with Reese during the week you finish Visual/Creative Image or PDF uploaded to your studio folder; share with Reese Multimedia/Performance Video, podcast, or presentation file shared via Drive or presented live Portfolio Annotation Google Doc with your annotated work and commentary, shared with Reese

Make sure your demo is in your studio folder before you share it. Reese scores from what's in the folder — not from an email attachment or a file you shared separately.

docs.title: Step 2 — Complete Your Reflection
docs.text:
Your Studio Reflection is a short document (2 pages) where you think back on the studio: what you learned, how the process went, and what you'd do differently. Complete it before or at the same time as you submit your Demo.

→ Download Studio Reflection (PDF)

The Reflection has four parts: 1. Standard & Score — What you targeted and what you think you earned 2. Process — How the six weeks actually went (honestly) 3. What You Learned — Beyond the standard — what you understand now that you didn't before 4. Ownership — What you chose, what you'd choose differently, what you want to do next

Fill it out honestly. Reese reads these. He's not grading them for content — but he uses them to understand where you are and what you need next.

docs.title: Step 3 — File Your Work in Google Drive
docs.text:
All finished work goes into your studio folder in The Commons. The folder structure is:

The Commons
  └── [Your Subject Area]
        └── [Your Last Name, First Name]
              └── [Studio Title]
                    ├── Studio Contract — [Start Date].gdoc
                    ├── Source Log.gdoc
                    ├── [Your Demo] (essay, sketchnote, recording, etc.)
                    └── Studio Reflection.gdoc

If you don't have a studio folder set up, ask Reese — he'll get you into the correct shared drive.

Everything goes in the folder before Reese signs off. Not after, not "I'll add it later." The folder is the complete record.

docs.title: Step 4 — If This Is a Week 6 Studio — Exhibit Your Deliverable
docs.text: If your contract had a Deliverable (Week 6 studio), exhibiting it is part of finishing. Your Deliverable goes to a real audience — not just to Reese.

docs.title: Common exhibition formats
docs.text:
Published to the SDA Commons (digital) Work published to the wiki (essays, research summaries, explainers, annotated primary sources) goes through Reese. He'll add it to the appropriate section of the wiki. This is how student work becomes part of the permanent SDA Commons.

Hallway or classroom display Posters, visual projects, and physical artifacts can be displayed in the SDA space or the hallway outside. Coordinate with Reese for timing — some exhibitions are set up during specific exhibition windows.

Presentation to an audience If your contract listed a specific audience (other students, another class, parents, a community partner), Reese helps you coordinate the date and format. Give yourself at least a week before your target date to schedule this.

Where does the Deliverable file go? Even if your Deliverable is physical or performed, put the digital version (or a photo/recording of it) in your studio folder alongside everything else.

docs.title: Step 5 — Get Your Score
docs.text:
Reese scores your Demo using the 1.0–4.0 scale from your contract's Proficiency Targets. He'll review:

  • Your Demo
  • Your Reflection
  • Your Source Log

And compare all three against what you wrote in Part 5 of your contract.

How scoring works:

Score What it means 1.0 With help, you could do this. You haven't demonstrated it independently yet. 2.0 You can do the basic version independently, but haven't yet hit the full 3.0 target. 3.0 You've met the standard for this studio. This is the target. 4.0 You've gone beyond the standard in a documented, specific way.

A 3.0 is a genuine accomplishment — it means you've proven you can do the thing the standard asks for, independently, without a template. Don't treat it as "just okay."

If you scored lower than you hoped:

Talk to Reese. Not to argue about the score — but to understand specifically what you didn't demonstrate and whether there's a path to revise and resubmit. In some cases, he'll allow a targeted revision. In others, he'll note it as in-progress learning and you build on it in a future studio.

docs.title: Step 6 — Recording Your Score in Headrush
docs.text:
This section will be updated once Headrush is set up for SDA.

Headrush is the platform where your demonstrations of learning are formally recorded. Once you've earned a score from Reese, it will be entered into Headrush so it counts toward your SDA record.

Until Headrush is configured, Reese tracks scores manually. Your contract, your Reflection, and the file in your studio folder are your official record.

docs.title: What Finishing a Studio Means
docs.text:
One completed studio means:

  • You've earned evidence toward a standard
  • You have a documented research and writing process
  • You have a body of work in your Google Drive folder
  • You've practiced the six-week arc — and you know what you'd do differently next time

Your reflection is your roadmap for the next studio. What you wrote in Part 4 (Ownership) is where you start planning.

docs.title: Ready for Another Studio?
docs.text:
If you're planning a pre-built studio next, go to the Studio Packet Archive and pick your next packet.

If you want to build your own again, start at Design Your Studio.

Either way — the process starts the same way: with a question you actually want to answer.

When You're Done · SDA Commons Wiki · South High School See also: Design Your Studio · Your Contract · Student Workflow · Studio Archive

docs.title: Exhibition Prep Guide
docs.text:
South High School · Self-Directed Academy

Every Studio ends with a public exhibition. This is not a class presentation — it is a real audience event. This guide tells you exactly what to expect and how to prepare.

docs.title: What Exhibition Is (and Isn't)
docs.text:
Exhibition is: A public showing of the work you built during the Studio. Your deliverable goes in front of an audience — other students, parents, community members, or experts depending on the Studio.

Exhibition is not: The moment you earn your proficiency score. Your demonstration of learning evidence is completed and scored in Week 5. The exhibition is where you make your work visible. The communication standards (C.1 and C.6) are what get assessed during the exhibition itself.

The core question your exhibition answers: What did you make, and what does it mean?

docs.title: The Four Named Exhibitions
docs.text:
SDA runs four public exhibitions per year, one at the end of each Era:

Exhibition Era Focus Historian's Toolkit Presentation Era 1 — Ancient Civilizations Analyzing primary sources and early political systems Civilization Systems Expo Era 2 — Middle Ages How belief systems and political structures shaped daily life Revolution Debate Night Era 3 — Age of Revolutions Arguing whether revolutions achieved their stated goals "The Past Built the Present" Public Symposium Era 4 / Capstone Connecting the full year's history to something in the present world

Your advisor will tell you the specific format, audience, and logistics for each exhibition. The details vary by Studio. The structure below applies every time.

docs.title: Monday — Advisor Check-In
docs.text:
Your deliverable should be complete. Come to your advisor with the finished product — not a draft. If something is not done, say so clearly and make a plan. Last-minute surprises help no one.

What to have ready: - Final deliverable (essay, podcast, video, visual essay, website — whatever you proposed) - Reflection draft started - List of anything still uncertain about your exhibition setup

docs.title: Tuesday–Wednesday — Rehearsal
docs.text:
Practice your presentation aloud. This is not optional. Students who don't practice out loud almost always underperform. Use a timer. Practice in front of at least one person.

Minimum rehearsal: Two full run-throughs of your presentation.

What a run-through includes: - Your opening (what is your question and why does it matter?) - A walk-through of your deliverable (what did you make?) - Your key claim (what's the answer to your inquiry question?) - Your evidence (what did you find that led you to that answer?) - A closing (what changed in how you think about this topic?)

Then: Prepare for questions. Think of three questions someone might ask you and practice your answers.

docs.title: Thursday — Setup and Tech Check
docs.text:
If your exhibition involves technology (slides, audio, video), do a full tech check on Thursday. Do not wait until exhibition day to discover your file won't open or your video won't play.

If you're presenting a physical artifact or visual essay, set it up as it will appear during the exhibition.

docs.title: Friday (or Exhibition Day) — Go
docs.text: Show up early. You're a presenter, not an attendee.

docs.title: What You're Being Assessed On
docs.text:
Two communication standards are assessed at every exhibition:

C.1 — Formal Presentation You're being assessed on how effectively you communicate your ideas to an audience. This includes: - Format: Is your deliverable organized and purposeful? - Delivery: Do you speak clearly and engage your audience, or do you read from a script? - Multimedia choices: If you use slides, images, or audio, do they support your point?

C.6 — Discussion and Debate You're being assessed on how you handle the Q&A and audience interaction: - Do you listen to questions before answering? - Do you respond with evidence, not just opinion? - Can you hold your position while engaging with a challenge?

Your proficiency score on the standard you studied all Studio (the WH or ELA reading/writing standard) comes from your Week 5 demonstration of learning — not from the exhibition. The exhibition is C.1 and C.6.

docs.title: How to Prepare for Questions
docs.text:
The hardest part for most students is the question-and-answer portion. Here is how to prepare:

Step 1: Identify your claim. What is the one-sentence answer to your inquiry question? Write it down. Practice saying it out loud until it feels natural.

Step 2: Know your two strongest pieces of evidence. If someone challenges your claim, what are the two best things you found that support it? You should be able to name them without thinking.

Step 3: Anticipate the push-back. What would someone say against your argument? What's the most obvious objection? Practice a response that acknowledges the objection and holds your position.

Step 4: Prepare for "I don't know." Sometimes someone will ask something you genuinely don't know. The correct answer is: "That's a good question and I don't know the answer — but here's what I do know: [connect it back to your evidence]." Saying "I don't know" honestly is better than bluffing.

docs.title: Deliverable Types and Presentation Notes
docs.text: Deliverable Presentation approach Essay Don't read it aloud. Present your argument — what you argued and what you found. Have the essay available for the audience to read if they want. Podcast Play a 2–3 minute excerpt. Then explain your editorial choices: why did you cover what you covered, in the order you chose? Documentary / video Play a key clip (2–4 min). Then speak to what you learned making it. Website Walk the audience through it live. Narrate your choices as you go. Visual essay / photo essay Walk through it panel by panel. Explain the relationship between image and text. Debate / seminar Your advisor will structure the format. Prepare your opening position and your two best counterargument responses. Research presentation (slides) Maximum 8 slides. Your slides support your talk — they don't replace it.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
After every exhibition, you write a reflection. It goes into your portfolio.

The reflection answers four questions: 1. What was your inquiry question and what did you find? 2. What evidence most changed or shaped your thinking? 3. What would you do differently in your Studio process next time? 4. What connection, if any, do you see between what you studied and something in the present world?

Reflections are not graded separately — they are portfolio artifacts. They are evidence of your growth.

docs.title: Quick Reference — Exhibition Day Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Deliverable is complete and ready to present
  • [ ] I know my claim in one sentence
  • [ ] I know my two strongest pieces of evidence
  • [ ] I have rehearsed at least twice out loud
  • [ ] I've done a tech check if I'm using technology
  • [ ] I've prepared answers for at least three likely questions
  • [ ] I know what C.1 and C.6 are being assessed on
  • [ ] I'm arriving early

Exhibition Prep — SDA Commons Wiki · South High School See also: How to SDA · Studio Archive · ELA Standards — C.1 and C.6

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text: South High School · Self-Directed Academy

docs.title: About the Program
docs.text:
What is SDA? SDA (Self-Directed Academy) is a competency-based program where you earn credit through Studios — six-week learning cycles — instead of traditional seat time and tests. You produce real work and demonstrate that you know academic standards through evidence you choose. See How to SDA for the full explanation.

What classes am I in? In Grade 9, SDA replaces English I and World History with an integrated program. You're enrolled in both — they count as separate credits — but in SDA they run together as one experience. Your writing skills are how you prove what you learned in history.

Do I still have to go to other classes? Yes. SDA is your English and Social Studies credit. You still attend all other classes (math, science, electives, AP, etc.) on your regular schedule.

Is there homework? Not in the traditional sense. There are no assigned homework worksheets. However, Studio work takes time, and if you're not finishing your Studio phases on schedule, you'll need to work outside of class. The Studio is yours to manage — that's the whole point.

docs.title: Studios
docs.text:
What is a Studio? A Studio is a six-week learning cycle. It starts with a shared essential question (e.g., "When is rebellion justified?"), and you investigate your own specific angle within that question. You research, build a deliverable (essay, podcast, video, etc.), and exhibit it publicly in Week 6. See How to SDA for the full lifecycle.

How many Studios do I do per year? Approximately six Studios per year, though the exact count depends on pacing. Year 1 (Grade 9) focuses on World History — Era 1 through Era 4.

Can I choose my topic? Yes, within the Studio's essential question. The class shares one essential question per Studio. You choose your specific inquiry angle — the particular question you'll investigate within that broader question. The more specific your angle, the better your Studio usually goes.

Can I propose my own Studio entirely? Yes, if you're a Phoenix (Level 3). Phoenix students can propose a Studio with their own essential question, not just an angle within the class question. This requires a written proposal and advisor approval. Most students reach Maverick first, then Phoenix.

What if I don't finish a Studio by Week 6? Extensions are available with advisor approval, but they must be requested before the exhibition, not after. If you're falling behind, talk to your advisor by Week 4 at the latest. Don't wait until Week 6 to say you're not ready.

Can I redo a Studio I didn't do well on? Not exactly — you can't redo an entire Studio. But you can resubmit evidence for a specific standard within the same semester if you can show substantially improved or new work. Talk to your advisor about what that looks like for the standard you're working on.

docs.title: Scoring and Grades
docs.text:
How does scoring work? Every standard is scored on a 1.0–4.0 scale: - 4.0 — You exceed the standard (in-depth applications not directly taught) - 3.0 — You meet the standard (no major errors or omissions) - 2.0 — Foundation knowledge only (basic vocabulary and processes, missing complex ideas) - 1.0 — Partial understanding, even with help

Scores in between (3.5, 2.5, 1.5) reflect partial success toward the next level.

What grade does a 3.0 equal? - 4.0 = A (95%) · 3.5 = A- (91%) · 3.0 = B (83%) · 2.5 = B- (78%) · 2.0 = C (73%)

What should I aim for? 3.0 every Studio. That means no major errors or omissions on the standard as written. 4.0 is available if you go further — if you make connections and applications that weren't directly taught. Set your target in your Studio Proposal at the start of each cycle.

What goes into Infinite Campus? Your final proficiency scores for each standard go into Infinite Campus at the end of each semester. You can check your scores in Headrush throughout the semester — that's where your advisor records evidence and scores.

Is there extra credit? Not by that name. But you can always resubmit evidence for a higher score on a standard, and you can always pursue a 4.0 by going deeper than required.

docs.title: Ownership Levels
docs.text:
What are the ownership levels? Trailblazer → Maverick → Phoenix. Your level determines how much independence you have in proposing Studios, choosing topics, and collaborating with other students. Levels are reviewed every two weeks.

How do I move up? Show consistent evidence of independent research, timely completion of Studio phases, and proficiency scores at 2.5 or above (to move from Trailblazer to Maverick) or 3.0+ across multiple Studios (to move from Maverick to Phoenix). You can apply to move up — talk to your advisor.

Can I move down? Yes. If your Studio work is consistently late or incomplete, or if your scores drop, your advisor may move you back to a more structured level. This is not a punishment — it's a reset to get you the support you need.

docs.title: Skinnies
docs.text:
What is a skinny? A skinny is a short, focused mini-lesson that teaches one specific skill. You pull them when you hit a knowledge or skill gap mid-Studio — "I don't know how to write a counterclaim" or "I don't understand what a primary source is." See How to SDA for the full explanation.

Am I required to do skinnies? Not every Studio — only when you have a specific gap blocking your work. Your advisor may recommend one. You can also pull them yourself whenever you need them.

Where do I find skinnies? In this wiki under ELA 9-10 Skinnies and World History Skinnies. The How to SDA guide links to the relevant ones for each skill.

docs.title: Research and Sources
docs.text:
How many sources do I need? At minimum: one primary source and one secondary source per Studio. More is better. The IR bundle (IR.1–IR.5) is assessed every Studio and requires at least two sources, organized in a source log, with source evaluations.

What counts as a primary source? A primary source is a document, artifact, or record created at the time of the event you're studying — a speech, a law, a letter, a photograph, a firsthand account. The Reading Library is full of them. CrashCourse videos are not primary sources — they're secondary sources (someone analyzing history, not living it).

Can I use Wikipedia? Wikipedia is a useful starting point to get oriented — it's not a citable source in your Studio work. Use it to understand a topic, then follow the footnotes to primary or secondary sources you can actually cite.

What citation format do we use? MLA. See the Research Bundle Skinny for format examples.

docs.title: Exhibition
docs.text:
What happens at the exhibition? You present your deliverable to a real audience and then take questions. The exhibition is public — it's not just your class. Depending on the Studio, your audience might include other students, parents, community members, or an expert panel. See Exhibition Prep for the full guide.

What am I graded on at the exhibition? The communication standards: C.1 (your presentation) and C.6 (how you handle questions and discussion). Your proficiency score on the main Studio standard (WH or ELA reading/writing) comes from your Week 5 demonstration of learning — that's already done before the exhibition.

What if I'm nervous? Rehearse. The students who are nervous and rehearse do fine. The students who are nervous and don't rehearse have a harder time. Practice your opening sentence — once you get through that, the rest follows.

docs.title: The Wiki
docs.text:
What is this wiki for? This wiki is your resource library for SDA — standards pages, skinnies, reading library, CrashCourse episode guides, studio packets, and guides like this one. It's meant to be a working tool you use throughout the year, not something you read once at orientation.

Is the wiki always available? The wiki is hosted locally on the school network. It's available on any school device. If you need to access it from home, talk to your advisor about options.

How do I find something specific? Use the navigation on the left side of any page. If you're looking for a specific standard, go to Standards. For texts, go to Reading Library. For skill mini-lessons, go to Skinnies. For CrashCourse episode guides, go to CrashCourse.

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
Who do I ask first? Your advisor (Reese). If you're stuck, say exactly what you're stuck on — "I don't know how to start my research question" is more useful than "I need help."

When should I NOT wait to ask? If you're behind on a Studio phase by more than two days, don't wait — talk to your advisor. Problems that are small in Week 2 become emergencies by Week 5.

What if my advisor isn't available right now? Pull a skinny. Check the reading library. Look at the Studio Archive and see how a past student approached a similar question. The wiki exists precisely for moments when you need to make progress independently.

FAQ — SDA Commons Wiki · South High School See also: How to SDA · Exhibition Prep · Reading Library How-To

docs.title: How to Use the Reading Library
docs.text:
South High School · Self-Directed Academy

The Reading Library is a collection of primary sources and literary texts that appear across SDA Studios. This guide explains what's in it, how it's organized, and how to actually use it during a Studio.

docs.title: What the Reading Library Is
docs.text:
The Reading Library is not a list of books you're assigned to read cover-to-cover. It's a collection of specific texts — speeches, manifestos, letters, memoir excerpts, poems, and chapters — that connect to the history and standards in your Studios.

Each entry in the library has: - The full text (or a significant excerpt) - Context: who wrote it, when, why, and what was happening in the world - Standards connections: which WH standards and ELA standards it addresses - Annotation prompts: questions to guide your reading - Crosswalk links: how this text connects to other texts and Studios

docs.title: How the Library Is Organized
docs.text: The library has two sections:

docs.title: Social Studies Texts
docs.text:
Primary sources organized by historical era. These are real documents from history — written by people who lived through the events in your Studios.

Era Example Texts Era 1 (Ancient) Hammurabi's Code · Analects of Confucius · Pericles' Funeral Oration · Melian Dialogue Era 2 (Middle Ages) Magna Carta · Ibn Battuta's Rihla · Luther's 95 Theses · Las Casas Short Account Era 3 (Revolutions) Common Sense · Declaration of Rights of Man · Wollstonecraft · Haitian Declaration · Communist Manifesto · Douglass · Kipling + H.T. Johnson · The Jungle · Dulce et Decorum Est Era 4 / USH FDR Four Freedoms · MLK Letter from Birmingham · UN Declaration of Human Rights · Mandela Rivonia Speech · Malala UN Speech

docs.title: ELA Texts
docs.text:
Literary works that appear in ELA-focused Studios and can serve as dual-use sources — they're both literature and historical evidence.

  • Night — Elie Wiesel
  • 1984 — George Orwell
  • Fahrenheit 451 — Ray Bradbury
  • Persepolis — Marjane Satrapi
  • Julius Caesar — Shakespeare
  • Warriors Don't Cry — Melba Pattillo Beals
  • Fahrenheit 451 — Ray Bradbury

docs.title: When to Use It
docs.text:
During the Dig (Week 2): The reading library is your first stop for primary sources. Before you go searching the internet for historical documents, check here — most of what you need for Era 1–4 Studios is already in the library with context and annotation guidance already written.

When you hit a knowledge gap: If you're writing about the French Revolution and realize you've never actually read any document from the French Revolution, pull the Declaration of the Rights of Man from the library. Ten minutes in the primary source is better than an hour paraphrasing a summary.

When you need a second source type: If your Studio is primarily research-based but your writing feels thin, a primary source from the reading library can give you direct evidence — a specific quote, a specific claim — that strengthens your argument.

For ELA demonstrations of learning: Many of the ELA reading standards (especially R.9) specifically require you to analyze argumentation in a text. The Social Studies texts in this library are argumentative texts — Paine was arguing for independence, Wollstonecraft was arguing for women's rights, Douglass was arguing against slavery. They're ideal for R.9 work.

docs.title: How to Read a Library Entry
docs.text:
Every entry follows the same structure. Here's what each section does:

The text itself (or excerpt): Read it first. Don't read about it — read it.

Historical context: Read this after your first read-through, when you have questions. The context section answers: who wrote this, to whom, under what circumstances, and what were they trying to accomplish?

Standards alignment table: Shows you exactly which standards this text addresses. Use this during your Studio proposal to decide whether a text is a good fit for your inquiry angle.

Annotation prompts: These are questions to guide a second, slower read. You're not required to answer all of them — choose the ones that connect to your inquiry question.

Crosswalk links: These connect this text to other texts in the library. If you're studying imperialism and you read the Kipling poem, the crosswalk will point you to H.T. Johnson's response — they need to be read together.

Pairs with CrashCourse: Many entries link to a CrashCourse episode that gives you the historical context in video form first. Watch the episode, then read the primary source.

docs.title: Using the Library for Annotation
docs.text:
Annotation is reading with a purpose. When you annotate a library text, you're not just underlining — you're having a conversation with it.

Three basic moves:

  1. Identify the claim. What is this writer arguing? Underline or note the sentence where they state their main position.

  2. Identify the evidence. What reasons or examples do they use to support the claim? Put a bracket around each piece of evidence.

  3. Note your response. Where do you agree, disagree, or have a question? Write a short note in the margin (or in your source log): "This is convincing because..." or "I'm not sure this is true because..." or "This connects to [something else I read]."

The standard this builds: R.9 — analyzing argumentative text. Annotating a primary source from the Reading Library directly builds your R.9 evidence.

docs.title: Citing Library Texts
docs.text:
When you quote or paraphrase a reading library text in your Studio work, cite it like any other source. Use the author, title, and year from the library entry header.

Example MLA format:

Paine, Thomas. Common Sense. 1776.

If you're quoting from the library entry itself (using our context or annotation prompts rather than the original text), note that: "As quoted in SDA Commons Wiki Reading Library."

For full citation guidance: IR.1–5 Research Bundle Skinny

docs.title: Quick Reference — What to Do in the Library
docs.text:
If you need... Do this A primary source for your Dig Browse by Era → find texts that match your inquiry question A text for R.9 annotation Any of the Social Studies argumentative texts work; use the annotation prompts Context before reading Watch the paired CrashCourse episode first, then read the text Related texts Check the crosswalk links at the bottom of each entry Citation format Check the entry header for author, title, and year

How to Use the Reading Library — SDA Commons Wiki · South High School See also: Reading Library Index · How to SDA · ELA-SS Crosswalk

docs.title: Standards Tracker
docs.text:
SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

Your Standards Tracker is the one document that shows everything you've done in SDA — every studio, every standard, every score. You'll use it to plan your next studio, check your progress toward covering all the standards, and show Mr. Reese where your gaps are.

docs.title: Get Your Tracker
docs.text:
Find your grade below. Click the link, then choose File → Make a Copy to save your own version to Google Drive. Rename it: Standards Tracker — [Your Last Name, First Name].

Grade 9 — ELA + World History Open Tracker — Grade 9 (Make a Copy)

Grade 10 — ELA + US History Open Tracker — Grade 10 (Make a Copy)

Once you have your copy, move it to your personal folder in The Commons: 

The Commons → [Grade 9 ELA / Grade 10 ELA] → [Your Last Name, First Name] → Standards Tracker

docs.title: Tab 1 — Start Here
docs.text: Instructions and your name/year field. Read it once at the beginning of the year.

docs.title: Tab 2 — Studio Log
docs.text:
One row per studio. Fill this in the day you get your score from Mr. Reese.

What to fill in Where to find it Essential Question Your studio contract Primary Standard Your studio contract Era or Unit Your studio contract Score Mr. Reese tells you Crosswalk Standard + Score Your studio contract (if applicable) Demo Mode Your studio contract Expo Quarter Which quarter expo your studio was exhibited at Date Done The day you received your score

Use the dropdown arrows for Score, Demo Mode, and Expo Quarter — don't type, just click.

docs.title: Tab 3 — Standards Map
docs.text:
All your ELA and SS standards in one view. Shows how many times you've hit each one.

  • White / "○ Not yet" — you haven't targeted this standard yet
  • Yellow / "➤ Hit once" — you've hit it once; aim for a second
  • Green / "✓ Hit twice" — done; keep this color by end of year

Fill in the Studio # and Score each time you target a standard. The Status column updates automatically. For SS standards, also fill in which Era or Unit your studio was in.

Goal: Hit each standard twice by end of year. That's 18 standards × 2 hits = 36 total.

The 18 standards:

ELA (12 standards) — see the ELA Proficiency Scales for full definitions and score breakdowns.

Code What It Covers C.1 Speaking and listening C.6 Language conventions R.2 Central idea and theme R.4 Word choice and language R.7 Multiple media and text types R.8 Arguments and evidence R.9 Comparing and synthesizing texts W.3 Narrative writing W.4 Writing for task and audience W.6 Producing and publishing L.1 Grammar and mechanics IR.1–5 Research Bundle — five sequential stages: develop a question, gather from multiple sources, synthesize, evaluate credibility (SIFT), cite in MLA. Active in every studio. See the Research Bundle Skinny.

Social Studies (6 standards):

Grade 9 — World History: WH.6_12.1 through WH.6_12.6. One standard per broad historical era. WH Standards →

Grade 10 — US History: USH.1 through USH.6. One standard per unit. USH Standards →

docs.title: Tab 4 — Crosswalk Map
docs.text:
A grid showing which ELA + SS standard pairings you've completed in crosswalk studios. When you finish a crosswalk studio, write the studio number in the cell where your two standards intersect.

Most cells in this grid will stay blank — that's expected. Your goal is to spread your pairings across different rows and columns over the year, not repeat the same combination.

docs.title: The One Rule
docs.text:
Fill in your Studio Log the day you get your score from Mr. Reese.

It takes two minutes. If you wait until later, you'll forget the details. An outdated tracker isn't useful to you or to Mr. Reese.

docs.title: How Mr. Reese Uses This
docs.text:
During studio planning conversations — especially at the start of each new studio — Mr. Reese will look at your Standards Map with you. He'll use it to:

  • Identify which standards you haven't touched yet
  • Notice if you keep targeting the same standard repeatedly
  • Suggest which standard would fill your biggest gap next
  • Check whether you're on track for crosswalk requirements

The tracker isn't a grade — it's a planning tool. The more accurate yours is, the better Mr. Reese can help you design your next studio.

docs.title: Keeping Your Tracker in Google Drive
docs.text:
Your tracker lives in your personal SDA folder in The Commons Google Drive. The folder structure should look like this:

The Commons
  └── Grade 9 ELA / World History
        └── [Your Last Name, First Name]
              ├── Studio Contracts
              ├── Standards Tracker        ← this file
              └── [Studio folders]

If your folder isn't set up yet, ask Mr. Reese.

docs.title: Student Workflow — How to Run a Studio
docs.text:
Self-Directed Academy · South High School

This page tells you what to actually do, week by week. Every studio follows the same six-phase arc. Use this as your map.

docs.title: Phase Overview
docs.text: Phase Week What You're Doing Document You Create Resources to Pull Launch 1 Read the EQ. Pick your inquiry angle. Fill out your Studio Contract. Studio Contract PDF Studio packet, skinnies Dig 2 Research. Primary + secondary sources. Build your source log. Source Log Reading library, IR.1–5 skinnies Build 3 Make the thing. First draft of your deliverable. Your deliverable (draft) W.3 or W.4 skinny Shape 4 Peer review. Advisor conference. Revision plan. Revision notes Advisor, peers Finish 5 Complete your Demo of Learning. Polish the deliverable. Demo of Learning evidence Proficiency scales Publish 6 Exhibition. Reflection. Studio archived. Reflection Exhibition guide

docs.title: Start Here — The Essential Question
docs.text:
Every studio starts with an Essential Question (EQ). The EQ is the shared question the whole class anchors on. Your job is to find your specific angle inside it.

Where to find your EQ:

Go to your studio packet. The EQ is at the top.

Studio Essential Question WH-01 — Who Should Govern? Who should govern, and what gives them the right? WH-02 — Rights Chain When people write down their rights, what changes — and what doesn't? WH-03 — Writing Against Empire When a powerful system is doing harm, what does it take to challenge it — and what does it cost? WH-04 — Reformation and Revolution Can one document change the world — and if so, how? WH-05 — The Cost of War Who pays the cost of war — and who gets to tell the story? WH-06 — The Connected World What connects the world — and who decides who counts as part of it? USH-01 — When the System Fails When an institution fails the people it's supposed to serve, what does it take to fix it — and who has to do the work? USH-02 — Literature of Fear What does a society's fiction reveal about its fears — and whose fears count? USH-03 — When the Floor Falls Out When the economy collapses and millions of people lose everything, what does the government owe them — and what does it cost to find out? USH-04 — The Cost of Victory When a nation believes it is fighting on the right side of history, how does it decide which of its own values it can sacrifice — and who pays the price? USH-05 — What the Government Didn't Say When a government lies to its people about a war, and those lies get exposed — what changes, what doesn't, and who pays for the gap? USH-06 — When the Rules Don't Apply When people in power break the rules they're supposed to enforce, what — if anything — holds them accountable? USH-07 — The Unipolar Moment After winning the Cold War, the United States had more power than any nation in history. What did it do with that power — and who decided?

What is an inquiry angle? The EQ is shared. Your angle is yours. Three students can all be in WH-01 and investigate three completely different civilizations. Your angle is the specific question you will investigate within the EQ.

A good angle: - Connects to the EQ - Is specific enough to research in 6 weeks - Is something you're actually curious about - Can be argued — reasonable people could disagree

docs.title: Week 1 — Launch
docs.text:
Goal: Understand the question. Choose your angle. Commit to a plan.

Step 1 — Read your studio packet Open the studio packet for your current studio. Read:

  • The Essential Question
  • The Inquiry Angle Menu (suggested angles — you can use one or propose your own)
  • The reading list (skim the titles — which ones pull you in?)
  • The active standards (these are what you'll be scored on)

Step 2 — Do a first-pass skim of 2–3 sources Before you commit to an angle, read one passage from two or three texts in the reading library. You're not researching yet — you're figuring out what you're interested in.

Step 3 — Choose your inquiry angle Write your angle as a question. Test it:

  • Can I argue a position on this? → Good
  • Can I Google a single answer in 30 seconds? → Too narrow or too factual — revise it
  • Does it connect to the active standards? → Required

Step 4 — Fill out your Studio Contract Open the Studio Contract (Google Doc). Make a copy — rename it: [Your Name] — [Studio Name] Contract. Fill out all 8 parts. This is your plan for the whole studio.

Studio Contract

Open Studio Contract (Make a Copy) · Download PDF

Parts 1–3 are the most important to get right in Week 1. Part 4 (proficiency targets) requires you to look at the relevant skinny.

Step 5 — Get Reese approval Share your completed contract with Reese before you start Week 2. He'll either approve it or send notes. You don't start digging until it's approved.

What you submit at the end of Week 1: - Completed Studio Contract (shared with Reese via Google Drive)

docs.title: Week 2 — Dig
docs.text:
Goal: Build your evidence base. Find your argument.

Step 1 — Set up your Source Log Open the Source Log (Google Doc or PDF). Make a copy — rename it: [Your Name] — [Studio Name] Source Log.

Source Log

Open Source Log (Make a Copy) · Download PDF

Step 2 — Find your sources You need at minimum:

  • 1 primary source — a document, speech, artifact, or firsthand account from the time period
  • 1 secondary source — a historian, journalist, or expert analyzing the primary source or event

The Reading Library is your first stop. It has primary sources organized by era and unit, with standard connections already mapped.

Step 3 — Annotate each source For each source, record in your Source Log:

  • What it says (summary in your own words)
  • What it means for your research question
  • The author's perspective and purpose
  • Whether it's reliable — and why

Step 4 — Pull any skinnies you need If you hit a knowledge gap, pull a skinny before asking Reese. Common ones for this phase:

  • IR.1–5 Research Bundle — how to write research questions, find and evaluate sources, cite correctly
  • R.9 Informational Text — how to analyze an argument in a primary source

Step 5 — Narrow your research question Based on what you found, sharpen your guiding question. Most good research questions evolve in Week 2. Update your Studio Contract if your angle shifted.

What you submit at the end of Week 2: - Source Log (at least 2 sources annotated) - Updated Studio Contract if your angle changed

docs.title: Week 3 — Build
docs.text:
Goal: Get a first draft down. Done, not perfect.

Step 1 — Choose your demo format and start Look at Part 3 of your Studio Contract — your Demo of Learning mode. That determines what you're making this week:

Demo Mode What You're Building in Week 3 Written Assessment Draft response to your prompt Extended Writing Draft essay — intro, body paragraphs, conclusion Verbal Conversation Outline of your argument + talking points Visual / Creative Rough layout or sketch with annotations Multimedia / Performance Script, outline, or storyboard Portfolio Annotation Curated evidence + draft commentary

Step 2 — Pull writing skinnies if needed - W.4 Argument Writing — claim, evidence, counterclaim - W.3 Explanatory Writing — organizing explanatory evidence - R.4 Main Idea and Theme — if you're analyzing a text's argument

Step 3 — First draft due end of Week 3 It doesn't have to be good. It has to exist. A rough draft you can revise is worth more than a perfect outline you never wrote.

What you submit at the end of Week 3: - First draft of your Demo of Learning (or your deliverable draft if they're the same)

docs.title: Week 4 — Shape
docs.text:
Goal: Get feedback. Make a revision plan. Improve.

This is the most important week. Students who skip the Shape phase rarely reach 3.0.

Step 1 — Peer review Exchange your draft with another student. Use the proficiency scale from the relevant skinny to give specific feedback: - What's at 3.0 already? - What's still at 2.0? - What's missing entirely?

Step 2 — Advisor conference Come to your conference with: - Your draft - A list of 2–3 specific things you're unsure about - Your proficiency targets from Part 4 of your Studio Contract

Step 3 — Write a revision plan After feedback, document: - At least 3 substantive changes you will make - Why each change moves your work closer to 3.0 (or 4.0)

This isn't optional. The revision plan is part of your IR evidence.

What you submit at the end of Week 4: - Peer review notes (yours + theirs) - Written revision plan

docs.title: Week 5 — Finish
docs.text:
Goal: Complete your Demo of Learning. Polish everything.

Step 1 — Complete your Demo of Learning Your Demo evidence must be finalized by the end of Week 5. Reese will give you a preliminary proficiency score before the exhibition.

Review Part 4 of your Studio Contract — do your revisions show 3.0 work on the standard?

Step 2 — Polish your deliverable If your studio has a Week 6 deliverable, finish it. Practice your presentation if applicable.

Step 3 — Fill out your Reflection (first pass) Start the Reflection template now. You'll finalize it after the exhibition, but getting your thoughts down before is easier than starting cold on Week 6.

Reflection Template

Open Reflection (Make a Copy) · Download PDF

What you submit at the end of Week 5: - Completed Demo of Learning evidence - Deliverable (final or near-final)

docs.title: Week 6 — Publish
docs.text:
Goal: Show your work. Reflect. Archive.

Step 1 — Exhibition Present your deliverable to the audience. Your advisor will tell you the format (in-class, parents, community, panel).

Bring: - Your deliverable (printed, displayed, or loaded and ready) - Your Studio Contract (shows your original plan) - Your confidence — you've done the work

Communication standards C.1 and C.6 are assessed here: - C.1 — How you present: format, delivery, preparation - C.6 — How you respond to questions: listening, engaging, defending your ideas

Step 2 — Finalize your Reflection Complete the Reflection template after the exhibition. Answer honestly: - What did you learn about the standard? - What would you do differently? - What are you most proud of?

Step 3 — Submit everything Final checklist:

  • [ ] Demo of Learning evidence (submitted to Reese)
  • [ ] Deliverable (exhibited and/or published)
  • [ ] Completed Reflection
  • [ ] Source Log (final)
  • [ ] Studio Contract (final — filed in your Google Drive folder)

Your studio is now archived. Future SDA students can see what you made.

docs.title: Documents You Need Every Studio
docs.text: Document What It's For Get It Here Studio Contract Your plan — inquiry angle, demo mode, proficiency targets, timeline Make a Copy · PDF Source Log Track and annotate your sources during Dig Make a Copy · PDF Reflection Written reflection after the Week 6 exhibition Make a Copy · PDF

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text:
"Can I change my inquiry angle after Week 1?" Yes, with Reese approval and an updated Studio Contract. Don't change it just because it got hard — that's usually a sign you need to push through.

"What if I can't find good sources?" Check the Reading Library first — it's organized by era and standard. If you still can't find what you need, ask Reese before Week 2 ends.

"What if I don't finish by Week 6?" Extensions are available with advisor approval — but you must request one before the exhibition, not after. Plan backwards from Week 6.

"What's the difference between the Demo and the Deliverable?" Demo = proof you know the standard (scored 1.0–4.0). Deliverable = something you share with an audience (exhibited at Week 6). They can overlap, but they're not the same thing.

"What if I got a 2.0 and want a higher score?" You can resubmit evidence within the same semester. You need to show substantially improved work — not the same thing cleaned up. Talk to Reese.

"Can I work with other students?" Maverick and Phoenix students can collaborate freely. Trailblazers confirm with Reese first. Group deliverables always require individual Demo of Learning evidence.

Student Workflow · SDA Commons Wiki · South High School See also: Home · Reading Library · Skinnies · Studio Archive

docs.title: Studio Contract Template
docs.text:
SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

This is the master template for SDA studio contracts. Every studio a student undertakes in SDA begins with a completed contract — signed by the student and kept in their Google Drive folder. The contract exists so the student knows exactly what they're doing, why, and how they'll be assessed before they begin.

docs.title: How Studio Contracts Work
docs.text:
A studio contract is a 1-page commitment document. It answers: 1. What is my Essential Question? 2. Which standard(s) am I working toward? 3. What will my Demo of Learning look like? 4. What will my Deliverable be (if this is a Week 6 studio)? 5. What does each proficiency score (1.0–4.0) mean for this specific studio? 6. What are my checkpoints?

The contract is created in the student's personal Google Drive folder, signed (by typing their name), and reviewed with Reese before Week 1 work begins.

docs.title: The Template
docs.text:
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SDA STUDIO CONTRACT
South High School · Self-Directed Academy
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STUDENT NAME: ___________________________
DATE STARTED: ___________________________
EXPECTED END DATE: ______________________
COURSE: □ Grade 9 ELA/World History    □ Grade 10 ELA/US History

───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PART 1 — WHAT THIS STUDIO IS ABOUT
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

ESSENTIAL QUESTION:
[Student writes their driving question here — it should be genuinely
arguable, not answerable with yes/no]

WHY THIS QUESTION MATTERS TO ME:
[2–3 sentences. Personal connection or genuine curiosity.]

───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PART 2 — STANDARDS
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PRIMARY STANDARD (required):
□ ELA Standard: ___________________________
□ SS Standard:  ___________________________  + Era/Unit: ___________

CROSSWALK STANDARD (if applicable — circle one):
□ ELA: ___________________________
□ SS: ___________________________  + Era/Unit: ___________

STUDIO TYPE:
□ ELA-primary (demonstrating ELA standard with SS content as context)
□ SS-primary (demonstrating SS standard with ELA skills as method)
□ Crosswalk (both standards equally weighted — Hermes approves these)

───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PART 3 — DEMO OF LEARNING
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DEMO MODE (circle one):
□ Written Assessment
□ Extended Writing
□ Verbal Conversation
□ Visual/Creative
□ Multimedia/Performance
□ Portfolio Annotation

WHAT MY DEMO WILL LOOK LIKE:
[1–3 sentences. Be specific. "I will write a 4-paragraph argumentative
essay..." not "I will write something."]

WHO WILL SCORE MY DEMO:
□ Self-assessment using the rubric
□ Peer review before submission
□ Submitted to Hermes for initial feedback
□ Reviewed by Reese

───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PART 4 — SKINNIES TO INVESTIGATE
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Which skinnies are most relevant to your standard and demo mode?
List them here — you will use them to fill out Part 5.
Find every skinny at the SDA Commons → Skinnies.

1. _______________________________________________
2. _______________________________________________
3. _______________________________________________

───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PART 5 — PROFICIENCY TARGETS (What Each Score Means for THIS Studio)
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Use the skinnies you listed above. Translate each score level
into what it means for YOUR specific demo.

SCORE 1.0 — With help, I can: ________________________________

SCORE 2.0 — On my own, I can: ________________________________

SCORE 3.0 — On my own, I can: ________________________________

SCORE 4.0 — Beyond the standard, I can: _______________________

MY TARGET SCORE FOR THIS STUDIO: _______

───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PART 6 — DELIVERABLE (Week 6 Studios Only)
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

□ This is a Week 6 studio (Deliverable required)
□ This is not a Week 6 studio (Demo only)

IF WEEK 6:

WHAT MY DELIVERABLE WILL BE:
[The publicly exhibited artifact. Different from the Demo — this is what
someone who doesn't know the standard can still understand and engage with.
Examples: poster, podcast, published essay, short film, physical model,
presentation for parents/community.]

WHERE IT WILL BE EXHIBITED:
□ SDA Commons (digital — published to the wiki or Google Drive)
□ Hallway or classroom display
□ Presentation to a specific audience (who?): ___________________
□ Other: _____________________________________________________

───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PART 7 — YOUR TIMELINE
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

WEEK 1 — Launch:
  Checkpoint: _______________________________________________

WEEK 2 — Dig:
  Checkpoint: _______________________________________________

WEEK 3 — Build:
  Checkpoint: _______________________________________________

WEEK 4 — Shape:
  Checkpoint: _______________________________________________

WEEK 5 — Finish:
  Checkpoint: _______________________________________________

WEEK 6 — Publish:
  Demo date: _______________________________________________
  Deliverable exhibition: ___________________________________

───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PART 8 — RESOURCES
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

READING LIBRARY TEXTS I PLAN TO USE:
- [                              ](/reading-library/______________________________.md)
- [                              ](/reading-library/______________________________.md)
- [                              ](/reading-library/______________________________.md)

OTHER SOURCES (at least one you've found yourself):
-
-
-

───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PART 9 — AGREEMENT
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

By signing below, I confirm:
- I chose this Essential Question because I'm genuinely curious about it.
- I understand what score I'm working toward and what it requires.
- I will attend all checkpoints.
- I will ask for help if I get stuck instead of sitting and waiting.
- My Demo of Learning will be my own work.

STUDENT SIGNATURE: _______________________ DATE: _______________

TEACHER REVIEW: □ Approved   □ Needs revision — see notes
NOTES: _________________________________________________________

───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

docs.title: Notes on Filling Out the Contract
Essential Question
docs.text:
A good EQ is genuinely arguable and can't be Googled. Tests: - Can reasonable people disagree? → Good - Can it be answered in one sentence from a Wikipedia search? → Revise it - Does it connect to the standard? → Required - Does the student actually care? → Required

Examples: - Bad: "What caused the French Revolution?" (answerable, not arguable) - Better: "Was the French Revolution a success or a failure?" (arguable, but vague) - Best: "Who did the French Revolution actually liberate — and who did it betray?" (arguable, specific, personally charged)

docs.title: Part 4 — Proficiency Targets
docs.text:
Students should fill this out using the relevant skinny as a reference. The skinny has the 2.0/3.0/4.0 breakdown. The contract should translate that into their specific demo:

For example, if the standard is WH.6_12.4 + Era 3 and the demo is Extended Writing: - 2.0: "I can identify the causes of the French Revolution and name at least 3 key events." - 3.0: "I can write a 4-paragraph argument explaining how Enlightenment ideas caused the French Revolution, with specific evidence." - 4.0: "I can compare the French Revolution to another revolution and argue which better achieved its stated ideals — with evidence and a clear counterargument addressed."

docs.title: The Deliverable vs. The Demo
docs.text:
The biggest confusion students have is between the Demo and the Deliverable.

  • Demo of Learning = proof YOU understand the standard. Scored 1.0–4.0. Hermes and Reese both give feedback. It's assessed.
  • Deliverable = something you share with the world. Not scored on the same rubric. It's how you put the learning to use. A poster, a podcast, a published essay, a presentation to parents. The Demo might be the writing itself; the Deliverable might be the final formatted version published on the Commons.

A student can have a 4.0 Demo and a simple Deliverable. That's fine. The Deliverable isn't scored separately — it's the demonstration that learning lives in the real world, not just in a notebook.

docs.title: Google Drive Filing
docs.text:
When a contract is completed, the Google Doc is saved to:

The Commons → [Grade 9 ELA / Grade 10 ELA / World History / US History]
  → [Student Last Name, First Name]
    → Studio Contracts
      → [Studio Title] Contract — [Start Date].gdoc

Students keep all their contracts. By the end of the year, they have a complete record of every studio they chose, every standard they worked on, and every score they earned.

docs.title: Studio Journal
docs.text: Every Friday · 10 minutes · Three bullets

docs.title: What It Is
docs.text:
The Studio Journal is a short weekly check-in you write at the end of every Friday. It takes about ten minutes. It has three parts — no more, no less.

It is not a summary of everything you did. It is not a reflection essay. It is not graded. It is a quick, honest snapshot of where you are so that you and Mr. Reese stay on the same page — and so that you know exactly where to pick up on Monday.

docs.title: 1. Done
docs.text:
One specific thing you completed this week.

Not "I worked on my studio." That tells nobody anything. Something specific:

"I finished reading the Pericles excerpt and annotated it for R.9 — identified his central claim and two pieces of supporting evidence."

"I wrote a rough draft of my opening paragraph — it has a claim but no counterargument yet."

"I finished my Source Log with four sources — two primary, two secondary."

If you can't write a specific Done, that's important information. It means the week was motion without progress. That's worth knowing.

docs.title: 2. Stuck
docs.text:
One specific thing you haven't gotten past yet.

Again — specific. Not "I'm stuck on my essay." Where exactly?

"I can't figure out how to connect my argument about Hammurabi to the EQ — it feels like two separate things."

"I have three sources but none of them directly address the angle I chose. Not sure if I should change my angle or find better sources."

"My draft exists but it's all summary, no argument. I don't know how to fix that."

This is the most valuable part of the journal — for you and for Mr. Reese. A specific stuck gives Mr. Reese something to address in your Tuesday conference or Thursday's mini-lesson. A vague stuck helps nobody.

docs.title: 3. Plan
docs.text:
The first thing you will do Monday morning.

Not a to-do list. One thing. The next move.

"Monday I'm going to re-read the EQ and write one sentence that answers it directly, then build my draft backward from that."

"Monday I'm swapping out one of my weak sources — I'm going to search the reading library for something on Confucian political philosophy."

"Monday I'm going to ask Mr. Reese to look at my draft opening before I keep going."

Writing this on Friday means you don't waste Monday morning figuring out where you are. You already know. You start.

docs.title: Template
docs.text:
Copy this every Friday and fill it in:

STUDIO JOURNAL — Week [#] — [Date]

DONE: 

STUCK: 

PLAN:

That's it. Three lines. Ten minutes.

docs.title: Where It Lives
docs.text:
Your Studio Journal entries go in your personal Google Drive folder:

The Commons → [Your Grade] → [Your Name] → Studio Journal

Create one Google Doc for the whole studio and add a new entry each Friday at the top (newest first). When the studio ends, the journal stays in your folder as part of your record.

docs.title: Why Mr. Reese Reads It
docs.text:
Mr. Reese reads Studio Journals on Friday evenings or over the weekend. He is not reading to grade you. He is reading to know:

  • Who needs a Tuesday conference
  • What to teach on Thursday
  • Who is doing something remarkable that nobody has noticed yet

If your journal says you're stuck on the same thing two weeks in a row, he will check in with you Monday. If your journal shows a breakthrough, he might ask you to share it in Wednesday's Learning Circle.

The journal is a direct line between you and Mr. Reese that doesn't require either of you to stop and have a conversation in the middle of class.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Good journal entry:

Done: Finished reading Las Casas excerpt. Annotated for R.9 — identified his central claim (Spanish colonialism is morally indefensible) and three pieces of evidence. Strong entry for my source log.

Stuck: My EQ angle is "Did Las Casas's writing actually change anything?" but I can't find evidence that it did. All my sources say the empire continued anyway. Starting to feel like my angle might be wrong.

Plan: Monday — look at the crosswalk page for WH-03 to see if there's a different angle I might have missed. Maybe the question isn't whether it changed the empire, but whether it changed what the empire had to say about itself.

Weak journal entry (don't do this):

Done: Worked on my studio.

Stuck: The writing part.

Plan: Keep working.

The weak version has no information. It doesn't help you, and it doesn't help Mr. Reese.

Studio Journal · SDA Commons Wiki · See also: Student Workflow · Standards Tracker · Build a Studio

docs.title: How to SDA — Complete Student Guide
docs.text:
South High School · Self-Directed Academy · Grades 9–10

This guide explains how SDA actually works — the ideas behind every studio, why the program is built the way it is, and what to expect. Read it at the start of the year. Come back when something isn't clicking.

If you want the week-by-week steps for what to do and when, go to Student Workflow.

docs.title: What Is a Studio?
docs.text:
A Studio is a six-week learning cycle. Every Studio has four components:

1. A Shared Essential Question The whole class starts with the same question — for example: "How did early religions justify political authority?" The question is genuinely arguable. There's no single right answer. Everyone investigates the same question from different angles.

2. Your Individual Inquiry Angle You choose your specific focus within the essential question. Three students might all be in the same Studio but studying completely different civilizations, time periods, or examples. Your inquiry angle is the specific question you will investigate. It's in your Studio Contract.

3. Active Standards Each Studio has 4–5 standards you're working toward — a mix of English and Social Studies. They're posted at the start. Your Studio Contract is built around them.

4. A Public Exhibition At Week 6, your work goes in front of a real audience. Not just your teacher. The audience might be other students, parents, community members, or an expert panel. Your advisor tells you the format.

docs.title: Two Things Every Studio Requires
docs.text: These are completely different. Both are required. Neither replaces the other.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning
docs.text:
Proof that you know the standard. Must be completed by Week 5. Your proficiency score (1.0–4.0) is based on this — not on the deliverable.

You choose one of six modes in Week 1 and commit to it in your Studio Contract:

Mode What It Is 1 — Written Assessment A focused written response to a specific prompt targeting the standard 2 — Extended Writing A standalone essay, analysis, or argument piece as direct evidence 3 — Verbal Conversation An advisor conference where you talk through your understanding; advisor documents the evidence 4 — Visual/Creative A sketchnote, annotated diagram, or infographic showing conceptual understanding 5 — Multimedia/Performance A podcast, video, debate, or presentation demonstrating the standard 6 — Portfolio Annotation You curate existing work and write metacognitive commentary connecting it to the standard using 2.0/3.0/4.0 language

You can request to change your mode, but you'll need to explain why.

docs.title: The Deliverable (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
The PBL artifact. It's what gets exhibited at Week 6 and goes into the Studio Archive. It shows that you took what you know and built something with it.

The relationship: Demonstration of learning = "I know this." Deliverable = "I made something with what I know."

Deliverable types you can propose: essay, podcast episode, documentary, website, visual essay, photo essay, museum exhibit, debate, research presentation, original artwork with artist statement, or something you propose.

docs.title: What Is a Skinny?
docs.text:
A skinny is a mini-lesson. Short, focused, built around one specific skill or concept.

Skinnies aren't required for every studio — they're tools you pull when you hit a gap. When there's something you don't know how to do that's blocking your progress, you pull the skinny for that skill instead of waiting for Reese to explain it.

How to use a skinny: 1. You realize you're stuck: "I don't know how to write a counterclaim" or "I don't understand what a primary source is" 2. Go to the Skinny Library and find the skinny for that skill 3. Work through it — read the 2.0 foundation, understand the 3.0 target, do the scale tasks 4. Apply what you need to your studio work

Your advisor may also recommend specific skinnies. If Reese says "pull the R.9 skinny before your next draft," pull it.

docs.title: What Is a Standard?
docs.text:
A standard is an official skill or knowledge statement from the state of North Dakota. It describes what you should know or be able to do.

Every studio has 4–5 active standards. They're not a surprise — they tell you exactly what you're being assessed on before you start.

Standard codes you'll see: - 9-10.R.9 — Reading standard 9 for grades 9–10 - 9-10.W.4 — Writing standard 4 for grades 9–10 - WH.6_12.2 + Era 1 — World History standard 2, applied to Era 1 content - US.6_12.5 + Unit 3 — US History standard 5, applied to Unit 3 content

For the full breakdown of every standard, see ELA 9-10 Scales.

docs.title: The Proficiency Scale
docs.text:
Every standard is scored 1.0–4.0. Your goal every studio is 3.0.

Score What It Means Grade 4.0 Beyond the standard — inferences and applications that weren't directly taught A (95%) 3.5 Beyond the standard with partial success A- (91%) 3.0 Meeting the standard — no major errors or omissions B (83%) 2.5 Solid 2.0 knowledge + partial 3.0 evidence B- (78%) 2.0 Foundation knowledge — vocabulary and basic processes, missing the complex ideas C (73%) 1.5 Partial 2.0 knowledge D (63%) 1.0 With help, partial 2.0 understanding F (50%)

You set your own target proficiency in Week 1 as part of your Studio Contract. This isn't a formality — your target shapes how you plan your work.

docs.title: Ownership Levels
docs.text: You're always at one of three levels. Your level determines how much structure you get and how much independence you have. Levels are reviewed every two weeks.

docs.title: Trailblazer (Level 1)
docs.text:
New to SDA, or working toward the skills needed for more independence.

  • More structured support from your advisor
  • Pre-assigned or limited inquiry angle options
  • Required advisor approval before you begin a new studio
  • More check-ins throughout the cycle

To move up: Show consistent evidence of independent research, timely completion of studio phases, and quality work at 2.5 or above across at least two studios.

docs.title: Maverick (Level 2)
docs.text:
Growing independence. You can: - Propose your own inquiry angles within the essential question - Collaborate freely with other students - Work with less check-in frequency - Experiment with more ambitious deliverable types

To move up: Consistently demonstrate 3.0 or above across multiple studios. Show that you can self-manage the full six-week cycle without prompting.

docs.title: Phoenix (Level 3)
docs.text:
Full agency. You can: - Propose entirely self-designed studios — your own essential question, not just an angle - Mentor Trailblazer students - Access flex scheduling - Submit more ambitious deliverables (long-form projects, community-facing exhibitions)

Phoenix track requires: A written proposal, advisor approval, and a track record of 3.0+ across at least three studios.

You can move down if the work isn't there. Levels reflect what your work shows, not what you intend.

docs.title: How the Research Standards Work (IR Bundle)
docs.text:
The five Inquiry and Research standards — IR.1 through IR.5 — are always active, every studio, every cycle. They're assessed together as a bundle.

Standard Plain Language When It Shows Up IR.1 Write a focused research question Week 1: your Studio Contract guiding question IR.2 Find and interpret primary and secondary sources Week 2: source log and annotations IR.3 Organize your research so you can use it Weeks 2–4: your notes and source structure IR.4 Evaluate whether a source is credible Week 2: source evaluation in your log IR.5 Cite your sources correctly Weeks 3–5: in-text citations and bibliography

To earn a 3.0 on the IR bundle, you need all five: - A research question that evolved and sharpened through the Dig - At least one primary and one secondary source, interpreted (not just collected) - An organized source log - Written source evaluations noting bias, perspective, and purpose - Correct in-text citations and a complete bibliography

docs.title: How Exhibition Works
docs.text:
Every Studio ends with a public exhibition in Week 6.

The audience: Varies by studio. Other SDA students, parents, community members, or a panel. Your advisor tells you who's coming before Week 5.

What you're presenting: Your deliverable — the artifact you built. You're showing what you made and explaining what you learned.

What you're being scored on: Not the deliverable. The proficiency score is based on your demonstration of learning evidence, which was completed in Week 5. The exhibition makes your work visible — it doesn't change your standard score.

Communication standards assessed at exhibition: - C.1 — Your presentation: format, multimedia choices, delivery, preparation - C.6 — Your discussion: how you listen, respond, and engage with questions

After the exhibition: You write a Reflection. Your studio is archived. Future SDA students can see what you made.

docs.title: Portfolio Defense
docs.text:
Once a semester, you present a portfolio to a panel — your advisor and at least one other person (administrator, community member, or another teacher). You're not defending a grade — you're showing your growth.

The five artifacts you bring: 1. A historical analysis (primary source annotation or bias analysis) 2. An explanatory essay 3. An argument essay 4. A communication artifact (seminar prep, debate outline, or presentation recording) 5. A reflection (growth analysis)

The kinds of questions you'll get: - How did your argument writing improve this semester? - How does a historical system you studied still shape today's world? - What would you do differently in your studio process?

The portfolio defense is about growth across the whole semester, not any single studio. It's your chance to tell the story of how you got better — in your own words, with your own evidence.

docs.title: How to Get Help
docs.text:
Start with a skinny. The Skinny Library exists exactly for this — when you hit a gap in a specific skill, there's a skinny for it. Pull it before asking Reese. Most of the time you'll find what you need.

Then ask Reese. Be specific about what's blocking you: "I don't know how to write a counterclaim for my argument essay" is much more useful than "I need help." The more specific you are, the faster you get unstuck.

The Reading Library. If your research isn't turning up good sources, check the Reading Library before anything else — it's organized by era, unit, and standard.

Other students. Maverick and Phoenix students can collaborate freely. Trailblazers confirm with Reese first before deep collaboration.

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text:
"Can I change my deliverable type after submitting my Studio Contract?" Yes, with advisor approval and a written reason. Don't change it because the original idea got hard — that's usually a sign to push through, not pivot.

"What if I don't finish by Week 6?" Extensions are available with advisor approval. Request before the exhibition, not after. Plan backwards from Week 6 starting in Week 1.

"What if I got a 2.0 and want a higher score?" You can resubmit evidence for a standard within the same semester. You need substantially new or improved work — not the same thing cleaned up. Talk to Reese.

"Can I do my deliverable in a group?" Yes, for Maverick and Phoenix students. Group deliverables require each person's contributions to be clearly documented. The demonstration of learning is always individual.

"What is the Studio Archive?" Every completed studio is logged permanently. Future SDA students can browse it before proposing. Your work becomes part of the institutional memory of the program.

How to SDA · SDA Commons Wiki · South High School See also: Home · Student Workflow · ELA 9-10 Scales · Reading Library

docs.title: CrashCourse Literature
docs.text:
CrashCourse Literature is a YouTube playlist hosted by John Green that offers short, engaging analytical episodes on major literary works taught in high school English classrooms. Each episode focuses on what a text means — its themes, structure, character, and argument — rather than simply summarizing the plot.

In this class, CrashCourse Literature pages work alongside the Reading Library entries. The Reading Library holds full text context, standards alignment, and annotation guides. The CC Lit pages here give you a focused starting point: watch the episode, answer the Watch Prompt, then go deeper with the Reading Library entry and your skinny pages.

How to use these pages:

  1. Watch the linked CrashCourse episode using the YouTube search phrase provided.
  2. Complete the Watch Prompt in your notes before moving on.
  3. Check the Skinny Connection links to see how the text maps to ELA and Social Studies standards.
  4. If you're starting a Studio project, read the Studio Ideas section for concrete deliverable options.
