docs.title: CrashCourse Literature: Romeo and Juliet
docs.text: Author: William Shakespeare · Episode: CrashCourse Literature playlist YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: WH Era 2 — Renaissance Europe ELA Standard: R.8 — Analyze how an author uses literary elements and devices to develop theme SS Connection: WH.6_12.3 — Political and social structures of Renaissance Europe; family, honor, and civic order

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: John Green pushes back on the popular reading of Romeo and Juliet as a simple love story, arguing that Shakespeare wrote it as a critique of impulsive, romanticized emotion — and that the play's body count is Shakespeare's point. The episode analyzes how the sonnet form embedded in Act 1 signals Romeo and Juliet's doomed nature, how the feud functions as a political failure of civic order, and why Friar Lawrence and the Nurse are the moral center of the play. Green situates the play firmly in Renaissance ideas about love, honor, and what happens when private passions collide with public life.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• The sonnet form within the play: Romeo and Juliet's first exchange as a shared sonnet, and what that signals
  • Dramatic irony and foreshadowing: how Shakespeare tells the audience this will end before it begins
  • The feud as a failure of political authority — Verona's Prince and what civil disorder looks like
  • Romeo as a parody of Petrarchan love conventions early in the play (Rosaline scenes)
  • Mercutio's function: wit, realism, and the play's most self-aware character
  • Juliet as the more rational and mature of the two lovers — close textual reading supports this
  • Renaissance honor culture: how insults require violence in a world where reputation is social survival
  • The role of fate vs. human error: who is actually responsible for the deaths?

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: Green argues that Shakespeare is not celebrating Romeo and Juliet's love — he's warning us about it. What specific literary choices (structure, language, character) does Green point to as evidence? Do you agree or disagree with this reading?

Literary choice Green identifies What it suggests about the love story Your position

This connects to R.8: analyzing how Shakespeare uses poetic form and dramatic structure to develop theme, and to WH.6_12.3: understanding how Renaissance political and social order shaped the stakes of private behavior.

docs.title: Full Transcript
docs.text: Transcript pending. This entry is a stub.

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• R.8 Literary Elements — dramatic structure, sonnet form, and theme in Shakespeare
  • WH.6_12.3 Medieval Political Systems — Era 2 — civic order, honor culture, and authority in Renaissance Europe
  • WH.6_12.4 Social & Cultural — Era 2 — Renaissance social structures, family, and gender

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Is It Love or Obsession? After watching the episode, students use Green's textual evidence to write a W.4 argument: is what Romeo and Juliet have actually "love" by any definition worth having? Students must cite at least three specific moments from the text and connect their claim to WH.6_12.3 ideas about what Renaissance society expected from young men and women in terms of social roles.

  2. Mercutio's Eulogy: Students write a eulogy for Mercutio — the character Green identifies as the most self-aware — in a voice that reflects Mercutio's own wit and skepticism. Then they annotate: what literary devices did you use (R.8), and what does Mercutio's death say about what happens when realism collides with romanticism?

  3. Civic Failure Report: Framing themselves as a city council member in Verona, students write a short W.3 report analyzing what went wrong politically in the play and what the Prince should have done differently. This connects R.8 (understanding the play's political themes) to WH.6_12.3 (civic authority and its limits in Renaissance Europe).

Reading Library: Full entry

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Romeo and Juliet is about what happens to individuals when they're trapped inside a conflict they didn't start and can't end — and that structure maps directly onto every contemporary situation in which young people bear the costs of adult choices. The feud between Montagues and Capulets is political, not personal: two factions compete for status and dominance in a city-state, and the price is paid by people who would choose differently if they could. Friar Lawrence's well-intentioned plan that kills everyone is a study in how interventions that make sense in theory create catastrophic outcomes in practice — a lesson relevant to every public policy debate. The play's ending doesn't resolve the feud through understanding or reconciliation but through exhaustion: the families stop fighting because they've destroyed everything they cared about. That's not a happy ending; it's a warning. See WH-02: The Rights We Wrote Down for the long arc of how written rules attempt to contain exactly this kind of factional violence.

docs.title: CrashCourse Literature: Slaughterhouse-Five
docs.text: Author: Kurt Vonnegut · Episode: CrashCourse Literature playlist YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: WH Era 4 — WWII and Its Aftermath / USH Unit 4 — World War II ELA Standard: R.8 — Analyze how an author uses literary elements and devices to develop theme SS Connection: US.6_12.5 — Causes, conduct, and consequences of WWII; WH.6_12.5 — The destruction of WWII and its moral aftermath

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: John Green reads Slaughterhouse-Five as Vonnegut's attempt to write about the firebombing of Dresden — an event Vonnegut survived as a prisoner of war — using a fragmented, non-linear structure because a conventional narrative structure would imply that the war made sense or that the destruction had a clean story to tell. The episode focuses on Billy Pilgrim's "unstuck in time" condition as a literary device (not literally science fiction) for representing trauma, and on Vonnegut's signature phrase "So it goes" as a stylistic choice that accumulates devastating weight across the novel.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• The novel's non-linear structure as a formal argument: trauma doesn't unfold chronologically, and neither should the book
  • Billy Pilgrim "unstuck in time" as a representation of PTSD before the diagnosis existed
  • "So it goes" — appears 106 times, always following a death — what repetition as a literary device accomplishes
  • The Tralfamadorian philosophy ("all moments always have existed, always will exist") as both comfort and critique of passivity
  • The firebombing of Dresden: the historical event, its scale, and why it was largely suppressed in Allied narratives for decades
  • Vonnegut as a character in Chapter 1: the author's presence breaks the fictional frame and insists this is about real events
  • Dark comedy and satire as ways of approaching horrors that resist direct statement
  • The anti-war argument: not through heroism but through showing the chaos, absurdity, and randomness of mass death

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: Vonnegut uses a fragmented, non-linear structure instead of a traditional war narrative. Green argues this is a deliberate choice about what war actually is. What does Vonnegut gain by telling the story this way? What would a conventional war narrative (beginning, middle, end, hero's journey) miss about the experience of Dresden?

Non-linear technique What it captures about war/trauma What a conventional structure would erase

This connects to R.8: analyzing how structure itself is a literary device that creates meaning, and to US.6_12.5 and WH.6_12.5: examining the actual conduct and moral consequences of WWII, including decisions like the strategic bombing of civilian areas.

docs.title: Full Transcript
docs.text: Transcript pending. This entry is a stub.

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• R.8 Literary Elements — structure, narrative voice, repetition, and dark comedy as literary devices
  • US.6_12.5 Causes & Effects — Unit 4 — the conduct and consequences of WWII, including Allied strategic bombing
  • WH.6_12.5 Causes & Effects — Era 4 — WWII's destruction and the moral questions it raised for the postwar world

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "So It Goes" Accumulation: Students find every instance of "So it goes" in a chapter of the novel and chart what each one is responding to. Then they write an R.8 analysis: what does the repetition accomplish that a conventional expression of grief couldn't? They connect to US.6_12.5 by asking: what does it mean that Allied nations rarely discussed the civilian casualties of Dresden?

  2. Unstuck in Time: Students diagram Billy Pilgrim's timeline — what order do the events actually occur in, vs. what order they appear in the novel? Then they write a W.3 explanation connecting the non-linear structure to what we know about PTSD and trauma (teachers can provide a brief background reading). This connects R.8's analysis of structure to the human experience of war (US.6_12.5).

  3. Is "So It Goes" Defeatist or Humanizing? The Tralfamadorian fatalism ("there is no why") could read as Vonnegut saying resistance is pointless. Or it could read as a way of insisting that every death matters, even when the system treats them as acceptable losses. Students write a W.4 argument defending one interpretation, using evidence from the novel and connecting to WH.6_12.5's analysis of the moral debates WWII raised (were the firebombings justified?).

Reading Library: Full entry

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Slaughterhouse-Five was published in 1969, the year after the Tet Offensive, the My Lai massacre, and the assassinations of King and Kennedy — and Vonnegut wrote it because he had survived the firebombing of Dresden in 1945 and felt the moral bankruptcy of treating that kind of destruction as an acceptable war narrative. "So it goes" is a response to a world in which violence is so constant and the suffering so vast that conventional emotional responses have been used up. The novel's formal structure — its refusal of linear narrative, its trauma-inflected time-travel, its insistence that traumatic experience doesn't follow story logic — has become a model for how contemporary literature and film represent war, PTSD, and mass violence. The specific question the novel asks — who gets to decide which civilian deaths are necessary, and what justification survives scrutiny — is being asked in every contemporary armed conflict. See USH-04: The Cost of Victory for the WWII context and the cost-of-victory question.

docs.title: CrashCourse Literature: Their Eyes Were Watching God
docs.text: Author: Zora Neale Hurston · Episode: CrashCourse Literature playlist YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: USH Unit 2 — The Harlem Renaissance and 1930s Black American Experience ELA Standard: R.8 — Analyze how an author uses literary elements and devices to develop theme SS Connection: US.6_12.4 — Social structures and the Black American experience in the interwar period; US.6_12.2 — The Harlem Renaissance as a cultural and ideological movement

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: John Green reads Hurston's novel as a story about self-discovery and the right to define your own identity — and connects it to the Harlem Renaissance's larger project of Black Americans claiming cultural authority and voice on their own terms. The episode pays close attention to Hurston's use of African American vernacular English as a literary choice (not an error or limitation), her depiction of Black Southern life as internally rich and complex, and her protagonist Janie's journey as a feminist narrative decades before the term existed in common use.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• The narrative frame: Janie telling her story to her best friend Pheoby, and what that choice means for voice and audience
  • African American Vernacular English (AAVE) as a literary device — Hurston's deliberate choice to honor the language of her community
  • The horizon as a recurring symbol: Janie's journey as a search for self-actualization
  • Three marriages as a structural device: each relationship as a different kind of oppression or freedom
  • Tea Cake: a complicated figure — what the novel says about love, risk, and the cost of autonomy
  • The Harlem Renaissance context: why Hurston's approach to Black Southern life was controversial among some of her contemporaries (including Richard Wright)
  • Hurston as an anthropologist: how her fieldwork shaped the novel's representation of culture and community
  • The hurricane: natural disaster as both literal plot event and thematic test of character

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: Green says Hurston's choice to write in African American vernacular rather than "standard" English was a political as well as a literary decision. What does he say it accomplishes? What would be lost if the novel were written in standard academic English?

What AAVE does in the novel What a "standard English" version would lose Your assessment

This connects to R.8: analyzing how an author's language choices develop character, voice, and theme, and to US.6_12.2: understanding the Harlem Renaissance as a movement to assert Black cultural identity and challenge racist assumptions about Black intellectual and artistic life.

docs.title: Full Transcript
docs.text: Transcript pending. This entry is a stub.

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• R.8 Literary Elements — voice, symbol, narrative structure, and theme in Hurston
  • US.6_12.2 Ideas & Beliefs — Unit 2 — the Harlem Renaissance, Black cultural movements, and the politics of representation
  • US.6_12.4 Social & Economic — Unit 2 — Black Americans' social and economic conditions in the 1920s–30s South

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Language as Politics: Students select a passage written in AAVE from the novel and rewrite it in standard academic English. Then they write a W.3 reflection: what changed? What was lost? Connect to R.8 (voice and tone as literary elements) and US.6_12.2 (the Harlem Renaissance's cultural politics and what it meant to claim Black artistic authority).

  2. Three Marriages, Three Arguments: Hurston structures Janie's growth through three relationships. Students map each marriage against the novel's central theme of self-discovery, then write a W.4 argument: which marriage is the most important turning point in Janie's development, and why? They connect the social conditions of each relationship to US.6_12.4 (women's roles and Black women's specific constraints in the Jim Crow South).

  3. Hurston vs. Wright: Richard Wright famously criticized this novel as lacking political anger. Hurston believed her celebration of Black life was itself political. Students write a W.4 debate brief taking a side in this argument, using evidence from the novel (R.8) and from US.6_12.2 on competing visions for Black liberation in the Harlem Renaissance era.

Reading Library: Full entry

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Hurston published Their Eyes Were Watching God in 1937, was dismissed by the Black literary establishment for not being political enough, and was rediscovered by Alice Walker in the 1970s as one of the most important American novels ever written. The debate over the novel — is Janie's personal journey toward selfhood and love political enough, or should Black literature focus on struggle and resistance? — is still a live debate about what Black art is supposed to do and for whom. The novel's depiction of an all-Black community in Eatonville, Florida (a real place, the first incorporated all-Black municipality in the U.S.) is also a record of something that rarely gets documented: Black life as it was lived outside the frame of white gaze, white violence, and white judgment. That documentation is itself a political act, and what it documents is largely gone. See USH-01: When the System Fails and the MLK Letter from Birmingham Jail for related context.

docs.title: CrashCourse Literature: Things Fall Apart
docs.text: Author: Chinua Achebe · Episode: CrashCourse Literature playlist YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: WH Era 3 — Imperialism and Colonialism (Africa) ELA Standard: R.8 — Analyze how an author uses literary elements and devices to develop theme SS Connection: WH.6_12.5 — Causes and consequences of European colonialism in Africa; WH.6_12.3 — Political systems and the disruption of pre-colonial African governance

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: John Green reads Things Fall Apart first and foremost as Achebe's deliberate response to the tradition of European fiction about Africa — particularly Conrad's Heart of Darkness — that depicted Africans as primitive, voiceless, and without complex inner lives. The episode traces how Achebe centers an African protagonist and an African community in all their internal complexity, contradiction, and richness before colonialism arrives to dismantle it. Green examines Okonkwo's tragedy as both personal (a man destroyed by his own rigidity) and historical (an entire society's destruction by an external force).

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Achebe's counter-narrative project: writing Things Fall Apart as a correction to Eurocentric representations of Africa
  • Okonkwo as a tragic hero: his fear of weakness (and its roots in his father's failure) as his hamartia
  • Igbo culture depicted from the inside: proverbs, the Oracle, the Week of Peace, the kola nut — richness before the colonizers arrive
  • The novel's three-part structure: Okonkwo's world, his exile, the colonial transformation
  • The missionaries' arrival: how different characters respond, and why (Nwoye's conversion as a political and personal rejection)
  • The District Commissioner and the final page: the shift to a European narrative voice and what Achebe does with it
  • The Yeats epigraph "Things Fall Apart / the center cannot hold": what the poem adds to the novel's argument
  • Colonialism's double destruction: not just political conquest but the dismantling of meaning systems, community bonds, and identity

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: Green says Achebe wrote this novel because African people deserved to see themselves as the protagonists of their own story, not as background characters in a European adventure. What specific narrative choices does Achebe make to accomplish this? How do those choices change what you understand about colonialism?

Achebe's narrative choice What it shows about Igbo society What a European-centered version would miss

This connects to R.8: analyzing how an author's narrative choices shape what readers understand and feel, and to WH.6_12.5: examining the causes and consequences of European colonialism in Africa with attention to what was destroyed and why.

docs.title: Full Transcript
docs.text: Transcript pending. This entry is a stub.

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• R.8 Literary Elements — narrative perspective, tragic structure, and the counter-narrative as a literary form
  • WH.6_12.5 Causes & Effects — Era 3 — causes and consequences of European colonialism in Africa
  • WH.6_12.3 Political Systems — Era 3 — pre-colonial African political systems and the imposition of colonial governance

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Before and After the Missionaries: Students trace the state of one specific aspect of Igbo society (justice system, gender roles, religious practice, or community decision-making) from Part 1 to Part 3 of the novel. They write a W.3 explanatory piece documenting the change and connecting it to WH.6_12.5 on colonialism's mechanisms of disruption. This grounds the historical standard in a specific, humanized example.

  2. Okonkwo or Colonialism: Whose Fault? The novel gives Okonkwo a genuine tragic flaw, but it also makes clear that colonialism would have destroyed him regardless. Students write a W.4 argument: is Okonkwo's tragedy primarily personal or primarily historical? They must use evidence from the novel (R.8) and from WH.6_12.5 (what European colonialism actually did to African societies) to build their case.

  3. The Last Page: Achebe's final page is narrated from the District Commissioner's perspective — a sudden, cold shift. Students write a close reading of that page (R.8), then explain in a W.3 paragraph what Achebe accomplishes by ending the novel this way. How does the Commissioner's tone and his plans for a book about Okonkwo connect to WH.6_12.5's analysis of how colonialism was justified and narrated by Europeans?

Reading Library: Full entry

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Achebe wrote Things Fall Apart in 1958, explicitly as a response to Conrad's Heart of Darkness and to the entire tradition of African literature written by Europeans who never asked Africans about their own experience. The novel is therefore both a work of literature and a political act: it insists on the humanity, complexity, and interiority of a pre-colonial Igbo community and refuses to let colonialism be the main character of the story. That project — of restoring narrative agency to people whose stories were told by those who conquered them — is ongoing in contemporary debates about decolonizing curricula, who gets to write whose history, and whose perspective counts as "objective." Achebe also demonstrates what he wanted to show: that colonialism didn't encounter backward societies in need of civilization — it destroyed functioning ones. See WH-03: Writing Against Empire for the full context of colonial power and resistance.

docs.title: CrashCourse Literature: To Kill a Mockingbird
docs.text: Author: Harper Lee · Episode: CrashCourse Literature playlist YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: USH Unit 6 — The Civil Rights Era ELA Standard: R.8 — Analyze how an author uses literary elements and devices to develop theme SS Connection: US.6_12.3 — Legal and political structures of Jim Crow America; US.6_12.4 — Social conditions and racial inequality in the mid-20th century South

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: John Green takes on both the novel's enduring appeal and one of its central critical problems: To Kill a Mockingbird is a novel about racism narrated by a white child, centered on a white lawyer-hero, that has been used for decades to teach white readers how to feel good about standing up for justice. The episode traces what the novel does brilliantly — Scout's perspective, Atticus's moral clarity, the symbol of the mockingbird — and what its critics (particularly Black scholars) argue it gets wrong or leaves out. This is one of the more honest CC Lit episodes about a text's limitations alongside its strengths.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• The mockingbird as the novel's central symbol: innocence destroyed by a corrupt system (Tom Robinson and Boo Radley as parallel figures)
  • Scout as a first-person child narrator: what we gain (innocence, moral clarity) and what we lose (adult complexity, Black perspectives)
  • Atticus Finch as moral hero and as a "white savior" figure — the critique and why it matters
  • The trial of Tom Robinson: what the legal evidence shows and why it doesn't matter in a segregated court
  • Maycomb as a microcosm of Southern society: class, race, and respectability in a small town
  • The novel's autobiographical roots and the historical context of its 1960 publication (the same year as sit-ins and SNCC)
  • Boo Radley's arc: how the novel uses Gothic mystery to explore fear, empathy, and social exclusion
  • The question of whose story this is — and what that means for how we teach and read it

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: Green raises the "white savior" critique of the novel — the idea that Tom Robinson's story is told from Atticus's perspective rather than Tom's own. What does Green say about this critique? Do you think it's fair? What would be different about the novel if Tom Robinson were the protagonist?

The novel as written A version centered on Tom Robinson What changes and what's lost or gained

This connects to R.8: analyzing narrative perspective as a literary choice with thematic consequences, and to US.6_12.3 and US.6_12.4: understanding how Jim Crow legal structures actually operated and whose experiences were centered and whose were marginalized in mainstream Civil Rights-era culture.

docs.title: Full Transcript
docs.text: Transcript pending. This entry is a stub.

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• R.8 Literary Elements — narrator perspective, symbol, and theme in Lee
  • US.6_12.3 Political Systems — Unit 6 — Jim Crow law, the legal system, and civil rights
  • US.6_12.4 Social & Economic — Unit 6 — racial hierarchy and social conditions in the mid-century South

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Perspective Shift: Students write a brief scene (1 page) from Tom Robinson's first-person perspective, covering a moment the novel describes only through Scout's or Atticus's eyes. Then they write an R.8 reflection: what did you discover about the novel's limitations when you tried to give Tom a voice? What does that tell you about whose perspective shapes the story? Connect to US.6_12.3 on who the legal system was built to serve.

  2. The Mockingbird Argument: Who is the novel's "mockingbird"? Green suggests both Tom Robinson and Boo Radley qualify. Students write a W.4 argument defending their interpretation, using textual evidence and the symbol's definition to build their case. They connect to US.6_12.4 by identifying which historical social conditions made each character's innocence so vulnerable to destruction.

  3. Reading the Critics: Students read a short excerpt from a critique of the novel by a Black scholar (teacher provides — options include passages from Toni Morrison or James Baldwin writing about how white literature handles race). They write a W.3 response: does the critique strengthen or weaken your reading of the novel? How does it change what you notice about Lee's choices? This connects R.8 (literary analysis) to US.6_12.4 (whose experience is centered in Civil Rights-era culture).

Reading Library: Full entry

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
To Kill a Mockingbird is simultaneously one of the most-read and most-banned books in American schools, and the reasons for both tell you everything about what the novel is and why it matters. Atticus Finch is one of the most enduring images of white moral courage in American fiction — but critics have argued that the novel's framing, which puts a white man at the center of a story about Black oppression and positions the white community as the moral arbiter of justice, reproduces exactly the dynamic it claims to critique. That debate — not whether the novel is good but whether it's the right story to use to teach about race — is a live argument in every school district that teaches it. The trial at the novel's center, in which an obviously innocent Black man is convicted because of race, is not historical fiction: the pattern it depicts continued for decades and its consequences (mass incarceration, racial disparities in prosecution and sentencing) are active. See USH-01: When the System Fails and the Civil Rights CrashCourse entry for the broader context.

docs.title: CrashCourse US History: The Black Legend, Native Americans, and Spaniards
docs.text: Episode: USH #1 · Playlist: US History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6E9WU9TGrec Period: Colonial Era (1492–1607) USH Standard Focus: Colonization, first contact, the Black Legend, Spanish Empire in the Americas ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze informational and argumentative texts; evaluate author's purpose and possible biases

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green opens US History by challenging the default Eurocentric framing of American history — the idea that US history starts with the Pilgrims, or even Columbus. Instead he begins with the complexity of Spanish colonization and the "Black Legend": the historical narrative (largely promoted by England) that the Spanish were uniquely brutal colonizers. Green complicates this, arguing that while Spanish colonization was brutal, the Black Legend was partly propaganda — English colonizers were not gentler, just better at winning the narrative war. The episode establishes a core habit for the course: question whose story is being told and why.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• The "Black Legend" — the 16th–18th century English and Dutch narrative portraying Spanish colonizers as exceptionally cruel
  • Origins: Bartolomé de las Casas's A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies — a Spanish priest's own condemnation of Spanish atrocities
  • Why England amplified the Black Legend: commercial and military rivalry with Spain
  • The Encomienda system: forced labor of indigenous peoples in exchange for "Christianization"
  • The actual scale of indigenous population collapse: 50–90% in a century — disease as the primary cause
  • Hernán Cortés and the conquest of the Aztec (Mexica) Empire
  • The complexity of indigenous alliances: not all Native peoples opposed the Spanish — many allied with them against common enemies
  • The Pueblo Revolt (1680): successful indigenous uprising against Spanish rule in New Mexico — 400 Spanish killed, 21 priests killed, Spanish expelled for 12 years
  • Green's argument: US history should start with its true starting point — the full hemisphere, not just the thirteen colonies

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Why start here? The problem with where US history usually begins
  • [1:30] — The Black Legend: what it is and who created it
  • [3:00] — Bartolomé de las Casas and his own evidence against Spain
  • [4:30] — The Encomienda system and forced labor
  • [5:30] — Disease and population collapse — the real devastation
  • [7:00] — Cortés and the Aztec conquest: the alliance question
  • [8:30] — The Pueblo Revolt of 1680
  • [10:00] — Was the Black Legend fair? Comparing English and Spanish colonialism
  • [11:30] — Conclusion: Whose story is US history?

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, use a source analysis tracker:

Green mentions the Black Legend and Bartolomé de las Casas's account. Both are sources about Spanish colonization.

Source Who created it Purpose What it reveals What it might hide Black Legend (English propaganda) De las Casas's Short Account

After watching: In one sentence, what does Green want you to question about how US history is typically taught? Find one piece of evidence from the episode that supports his argument.

This connects to 9-10.R.9a (analyzing author's purpose and possible biases; comparing texts from differing perspectives).

docs.title: Full Transcript
docs.text:
Transcript pending. This entry is a stub.

To generate: On T480 with updated yt-dlp: 

yt-dlp --write-auto-subs --sub-lang en --skip-download \
  "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6E9WU9TGrec" \
  -o "/path/to/us-history/1-black-legend.%(ext)s"
python3 ../vtt_to_markdown.py 1-black-legend.en.vtt -o ep-ush-01-transcript.md

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. The Propaganda Audit: Students read a 1-paragraph excerpt from the Black Legend (English propaganda) alongside a paragraph from de las Casas. They identify: What claim is each making? What evidence do they use? What might each writer be leaving out? Targets 9-10.R.9a and OEC skill strand.

  2. Who Gets to Tell the Story? Students write two 3-sentence summaries of the Spanish conquest: one from the perspective of a Spanish colonizer, one from a Tlaxcalan ally of Cortés. Then they compare: what changes? What stays the same? Forces perspective-taking and builds toward WH.6_12.5 work with primary/secondary sources.

  3. The Pueblo Revolt: Green gives this event about 90 seconds. Students research it for 15 minutes and add three facts Green left out — then discuss: Why do you think he left those out? What does that tell us about how CrashCourse makes choices? Builds critical media literacy alongside historical content.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Black Legend is not just a 16th-century story — it's the blueprint for how nations still frame their rivals today. When one country calls another "barbaric" to justify military or economic pressure, it is using the same rhetorical move English colonizers used to discredit Spain. Modern foreign policy debates are full of this: the language of human rights used selectively, accusations of atrocities deployed against rivals while the accuser's own record goes unexamined. Las Casas — who documented Spanish violence to demand the Church intervene — also shows the other move: using the powerful institution's own standards against it. If you want to go deeper on Las Casas and the question of who gets to tell the story, see the Las Casas reading library entry and WH-03: Writing Against Empire.

docs.title: CrashCourse US History: The Natives and the English
docs.text: Episode: USH #2 · Playlist: US History · Runtime: ~12 min YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XoNSCRsSM5U Period: Colonial Era (1580–1650) USH Standard Focus: English colonization, Native displacement, Jamestown, cultural and economic conflict ELA Connection: R.4 — Determine main idea(s) as they develop over a text and support with textual evidence

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green examines English colonization through the lens of Jamestown and the Powhatan Confederacy, arguing that the standard story — brave English settlers overcoming hardship to build a new world — erases the political sophistication of the indigenous peoples they encountered. The Powhatan Confederacy was a complex political alliance of 30+ tribes; their initial accommodation of English settlers was a calculated strategy, not weakness. Green traces how that relationship soured: English settlers demanded food during famines, introduced tobacco monoculture, and expanded relentlessly onto Native land. The episode asks why we remember John Smith but not Opechancanough.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Roanoke Colony (1585–1590): the "Lost Colony" and what its failure reveals about early English colonization strategies
  • Jamestown (1607): why it was founded (profit, not religious freedom), who settled it (mostly "gentlemen" unaccustomed to labor), and why it nearly failed
  • The Starving Time (1609–1610): 80% of Jamestown's population died
  • Powhatan Confederacy: 30+ tribes, sophisticated political structure, Wahunsenacah (Chief Powhatan) as a political strategist
  • Pocahontas: separating myth from history — she was approximately 10–12 when she "saved" John Smith (if she did); her actual story involves capture, conversion, and use as a political hostage
  • Tobacco as the colony's salvation and catastrophe: economic success required land, which required displacing Native peoples
  • The Anglo-Powhatan Wars (1610–1646): three wars ending in Native defeat and removal
  • Opechancanough: the Powhatan war leader who nearly destroyed Jamestown in 1622 and again in 1644
  • Indentured servitude as the first labor system before slavery

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: What Jamestown was really about (money, not freedom)
  • [1:30] — Roanoke: the colony that disappeared
  • [2:30] — Jamestown's founding and its "gentleman problem"
  • [4:00] — The Starving Time
  • [5:00] — The Powhatan Confederacy: political structure and strategy
  • [6:30] — Pocahontas: myth vs. history
  • [8:00] — Tobacco: economic salvation and ecological/political disaster
  • [9:30] — The Anglo-Powhatan Wars
  • [11:00] — Opechancanough and the 1622 attack
  • [12:00] — Conclusion: Who gets remembered and why

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
Green argues that Jamestown is remembered as a story of English endurance, but it was actually a story of complex conflict between two political systems.

Main idea tracker: At the start of the episode, what does Green say the Jamestown story is usually told as? At the end, what does he say it should be understood as? What evidence does he give for the shift?

Write one sentence identifying Green's main argument, then find two pieces of specific evidence he uses to develop it. This is 9-10.R.4 work: tracking how the main idea develops across the episode.

docs.title: Full Transcript
docs.text: Transcript pending. This entry is a stub.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Pocahontas: The Three Versions: Students compare (1) the Disney version, (2) Green's version, and (3) a brief primary-source-adjacent account of her capture and death in England. They identify: what does each version emphasize? What does each leave out? Who benefits from each version? Targets 9-10.R.9b (multiple accounts in different media).

  2. The Starving Time Math: Jamestown had ~500 settlers in fall 1609; 60–90 survived to spring 1610. Students calculate the mortality rate and then research one explanation for why it happened — was it drought? Conflict? Poor planning? They write a 1-paragraph causal argument using evidence. Builds WH.6_12.5 (causes and effects using sources) + argument writing.

  3. Opechancanough's Strategy: Students read a brief secondary-source description of the 1622 attack (Opechancanough's coordinated strike on 31 English settlements simultaneously). Then they write a paragraph from his strategic perspective: What was the goal? Why did it fail long-term? Forces empathy and analytical thinking about power, WH.6_12.3.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The legal and political questions raised in this episode — who owns land? what makes a treaty binding? what happens when the powerful ignore agreements they made? — are still live cases in U.S. federal courts. Native land rights, tribal sovereignty, and treaty rights to water and fishing grounds are active legal battles from the Dakotas to the Pacific Northwest. The history Green describes is not background context; it's the origin of claims still being contested today. The Standing Rock pipeline protests, the McGirt v. Oklahoma Supreme Court decision, and ongoing debates about federal recognition of tribes are all downstream of the colonial land relationships this episode introduces. For the full arc of that story, see the Era 1 Crosswalk and WH-01: Who Should Govern?.

docs.title: CrashCourse US History: When is Thanksgiving? (Pilgrims and Puritans)
docs.text: Episode: USH #3 · Playlist: US History · Runtime: ~12 min YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qv5DGKeFN7k Period: Colonial Era (1620–1692) USH Standard Focus: Puritan settlement, religious motivation in colonization, myth vs. history ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze author's purpose and evaluate argumentative reasoning in nonfiction text

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green uses the Thanksgiving myth as a lens for examining Puritan colonization — arguing that the story Americans tell about the Pilgrims is almost entirely constructed for political purposes (it became a national holiday during the Civil War). He covers who the Puritans actually were (English Calvinists trying to "purify" the Church of England), why they came (religious freedom for themselves, not tolerance for others), what their relationship with the Wampanoag actually looked like (complex political alliance, not a friendly feast), and how Puritan New England ended in King Philip's War — one of the most devastating wars per capita in American history.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Separatists vs. Puritans: the difference and why it matters
  • The Mayflower Compact (1620): early self-governance document — often cited as a foundation of American democracy, but Green complicates this
  • Squanto/Tisquantum: his actual story (previously kidnapped by English slave traders, spoke English, returned to find his entire village dead of disease) — not the friendly helper of the Thanksgiving myth
  • Massasoit and the Wampanoag alliance with Plymouth: a calculated political decision, not goodwill
  • The Massachusetts Bay Colony and John Winthrop's "City upon a Hill" speech (1630) — the origin of American exceptionalism rhetoric
  • Puritan theology: covenant theology, predestination, the idea that New England was a sacred experiment
  • Roger Williams and Anne Hutchinson: expelled from Massachusetts for disagreeing with Puritan leadership — the limits of Puritan "religious freedom"
  • King Philip's War (1675–1678): Metacom (King Philip) leads a coalition against English expansion; ~600 English colonists and 3,000 Native Americans killed; proportionally one of the deadliest wars in US history
  • When Thanksgiving was actually established: Lincoln's 1863 proclamation during the Civil War — what that context tells us about why the myth was needed

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: When is Thanksgiving? The question behind the question
  • [1:30] — Who were the Pilgrims/Separatists? Why did they leave?
  • [2:30] — The Mayflower Compact
  • [3:30] — Squanto's actual story
  • [5:00] — Massasoit's strategic alliance with Plymouth
  • [6:00] — The Massachusetts Bay Colony and "City upon a Hill"
  • [7:30] — Roger Williams and Anne Hutchinson: internal dissent
  • [9:00] — King Philip's War
  • [10:30] — Lincoln invents the Thanksgiving holiday (1863)
  • [11:30] — Conclusion: Why does this myth persist, and who does it serve?

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
Green is making an argument about why Americans believe a myth about the Pilgrims that isn't historically accurate.

Track the argument: 1. What is the Thanksgiving myth as most Americans know it? 2. What does Green say actually happened? (List 3 specific corrections) 3. Why does Green think the myth was created and when? (Be specific — he gives a date) 4. Whose interests does the myth serve?

After watching: Write one sentence — Is it harmful to teach historical myths, or is there value in national stories even if they're not accurate? You don't have to pick a side — but you have to back up what you say with evidence from the episode. This targets 9-10.R.9c (evaluating how argumentative reasoning supports or undermines the author's purpose).

docs.title: Full Transcript
docs.text: Transcript pending. This entry is a stub.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. The Myth Audit: Students list everything they "know" about Thanksgiving before watching. After watching, they mark each item: accurate / inaccurate / complicated. Then they write a paragraph: which inaccuracy surprised you most, and why does it matter? Builds 9-10.R.9 and forces honest engagement with prior knowledge.

  2. Winthrop's "City upon a Hill" Close Read: Students read the 2-3 most famous lines of Winthrop's 1630 speech and analyze: What is he promising? Who is included in his vision? Who is excluded? Then they find one modern political speech that echoes this language and annotate the connection. Targets 9-10.R.7 (style, syntax, word choices) and WH.6_12.6 (past to present).

  3. King Philip's War — The Numbers: Green mentions the casualty figures. Students contextualize them: if today's population (330 million) lost the same proportion as English colonists did in King Philip's War, how many deaths would that be? Why does scale matter for historical understanding? Then: why do most Americans know about the Pilgrims' "first Thanksgiving" but not King Philip's War? Targets historical thinking and OEC skill strand.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The story of Thanksgiving is one of the clearest examples of how national myths get built and maintained — and what gets left out of them. The Wampanoag perspective on the 1621 harvest has been documented and available for decades; the myth persists not because nobody knows better, but because the simplified story serves a function. That function — producing national unity through a shared founding story — is the same reason historical myths are defended even after they're debunked. Debates about how American history should be taught in schools, which stories count as "divisive," and who gets to define what patriotism looks like are all downstream of this episode's central question: who decides which stories a nation tells about itself? See WH-04: One Idea, 130 Years of War for how documents and narratives shape collective memory.

docs.title: CrashCourse US History: The Quakers, the Dutch, and the Ladies
docs.text: Episode: USH #4 · Playlist: US History · Runtime: ~12 min YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=e09YZRtPNPE Period: Colonial Era (1620–1700) USH Standard Focus: Religious diversity in the colonies, gender in colonial society, Dutch New Amsterdam, Quaker pacifism ELA Connection: 9-10.R.8 — Analyze the development and interaction of literary elements; author's point of view influenced by background, environment, and culture

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green examines the diversity of colonial North America by focusing on three groups whose stories get compressed or erased in standard tellings: the Dutch settlers of New Netherland (later New York), the Quakers of Pennsylvania, and colonial women. Each case challenges a simple narrative. The Dutch were tolerant and commercially minded in ways that shaped New York's culture permanently. The Quakers — radically egalitarian for their era — created a colony explicitly based on pacifism and religious tolerance. And colonial women had a legal status that varied enormously by colony, by race, and by class — far more complex than either the "oppressed housewife" or the "equal partner" story. Green uses all three to argue that colonial America was never a single thing.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• New Netherland and New Amsterdam: Dutch West India Company colony, commercial rather than religious in motivation, remarkably diverse from the start (18+ languages spoken)
  • Peter Stuyvesant: last Dutch governor, tried to expel Jews and Quakers from New Amsterdam — overruled by the Dutch West India Company (bad for business)
  • 1664: English seizure of New Amsterdam (renamed New York) — transition with almost no violence
  • William Penn and Pennsylvania: founded as a "Holy Experiment" — Quaker beliefs in pacifism, equality before God, no clergy hierarchy
  • Quakers and race: more likely than other colonists to question slavery, though Penn himself owned enslaved people — the complexity of principled people in an unprincipled system
  • Pennsylvania's Frame of Government (1682): one of the most liberal colonial constitutions — religious freedom for all monotheists
  • Women in colonial society: coverture (legal erasure of married women), differences between New England (more restrictive) and Chesapeake (more flexible due to demographic imbalance)
  • Anne Bradstreet and Phillis Wheatley: women who wrote and published in a society that did not expect or invite women's intellectual lives
  • The Salem Witch Trials (1692): 200 accused, 19 hanged — Green's argument about what fear and social stress do to communities

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Colonial America was not one thing
  • [1:00] — New Amsterdam: the Dutch commercial experiment
  • [2:30] — Peter Stuyvesant's intolerance and the Company's override
  • [4:00] — The English take New Amsterdam without a fight
  • [5:00] — William Penn and the Holy Experiment
  • [6:30] — Quaker theology: no clergy, no violence, equality before God
  • [7:30] — Women and coverture: legal nonexistence of married women
  • [9:00] — Anne Bradstreet and Phillis Wheatley
  • [10:00] — Salem Witch Trials: what they reveal about colonial society
  • [11:30] — Conclusion: The myth of colonial uniformity

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
Green is arguing that colonial America was more diverse and complicated than standard history suggests.

Evidence tracking task: For each group below, write one sentence about what standard history says vs. what Green's episode adds or corrects:

Group Standard story What Green adds/corrects Dutch colonists Quakers Colonial women

After watching: Which of these three groups do you think gets the most distorted treatment in standard history? Explain with evidence from the episode. This targets 9-10.R.8b — analyzing how an author's background and purpose influence what they include and how they frame it.

docs.title: Full Transcript
docs.text: Transcript pending. This entry is a stub.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Coverture Close Read: Students read a 2-sentence definition of coverture (the legal doctrine that a married woman's legal identity was subsumed by her husband's). Then they find one modern legal right women have that coverture would have prevented — and trace when that right was established. Forces WH.6_12.6 (past to present) and OEC.

  2. The Quaker Contradiction: Penn believed in equality before God and owned enslaved people. Students write a paragraph analyzing this contradiction — not to excuse or condemn Penn, but to explain how a person can hold contradictory beliefs. How does Green handle this contradiction? Does he excuse Penn, condemn him, or something else? Targets 9-10.R.9a (author's purpose and possible biases).

  3. Salem Witch Trials — Cause Analysis: Green argues Salem wasn't really about witches. Students brainstorm: what was it actually about? (Possible answers: gender and power, property disputes, social stress from wars and disease, religious anxiety.) They rank the causes with evidence from the episode and one outside source. Builds WH.6_12.5 causal analysis.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Quakers in this episode aren't just a historical footnote — they established the model for principled civic dissent in America. Conscientious objection, prison reform, anti-slavery activism, and the refusal to participate in war on moral grounds: all of these were Quaker positions before they became broader American debates. New Amsterdam's experiment in commercial religious tolerance — welcome everyone who will trade, whatever they believe — is the other model that keeps reappearing in arguments about pluralism and diversity. Both questions are live: Should religious belief exempt individuals from laws that apply to everyone else? What should government neutrality on religion actually look like? See WH-02: The Rights We Wrote Down for how these arguments evolved into constitutional language.

docs.title: CrashCourse US History: The Seven Years War and the Great Awakening
docs.text: Episode: USH #5 · Playlist: US History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4GdpBMcNqIs Period: Colonial Era / Pre-Revolutionary Era (1730–1763) USH Standard Focus: Seven Years War (French and Indian War), Great Awakening, colonial identity, seeds of revolution ELA Connection: R.4 — Determine main idea(s) as they develop over the course of a text and support with textual evidence

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green covers two developments that seem unrelated but both, he argues, contributed to the conditions for the American Revolution: the Great Awakening (a religious revival that challenged established authority and created a more egalitarian Protestant culture) and the Seven Years War (the first truly global war, which left Britain broke and convinced Parliament that colonists should help pay for their own defense). The episode's main argument: colonial American identity was forged in the mid-18th century by these two forces — one spiritual, one military — and both made the colonists more willing to resist British authority.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• The Great Awakening (1730s–1740s): mass religious revival led by preachers like George Whitefield and Jonathan Edwards
  • "Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God" (Edwards, 1741): the most famous sermon in American history — fire and brimstone, but also deeply democratic (God's wrath applies to everyone equally, regardless of social rank)
  • New Light vs. Old Light: the split between revival-oriented and traditional Calvinist churches
  • Why the Great Awakening mattered politically: it democratized religious authority — if you could have a direct relationship with God, you didn't need a minister, and by extension, maybe you didn't need a king
  • The Seven Years War (1756–1763) — called the French and Indian War in American history, but actually fought on four continents
  • Origins: conflict over the Ohio River Valley, where Virginia land speculators (including George Washington's family) competed with France and its Native allies
  • George Washington's early military career — and early failure (Fort Necessity, 1754)
  • Britain wins the war but goes broke: £122 million in debt
  • The Proclamation of 1763: Britain prohibits colonists from settling west of the Appalachians — meant to prevent more expensive Native wars, experienced by colonists as tyranny
  • Salutary neglect: Britain had largely left the colonies alone for decades; the Seven Years War ended that
  • Green's argument: the Revolution didn't come from nowhere — it came from colonists who had developed their own identity (Great Awakening) and then felt betrayed by a government that suddenly started treating them like subjects instead of partners (Seven Years War aftermath)

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Two events that don't seem connected but are
  • [1:30] — The Great Awakening: who, what, and why it spread
  • [3:00] — Jonathan Edwards and George Whitefield
  • [4:30] — Why revival = political democratization
  • [5:30] — The Seven Years War: a global conflict
  • [7:00] — George Washington's role and his early failure
  • [8:30] — Britain wins but goes bankrupt
  • [9:30] — The Proclamation of 1763
  • [10:30] — Salutary neglect ending — colonists feel the change
  • [11:30] — How these two threads connect: colonial identity + British overreach = Revolution
  • [12:30] — Conclusion: The Revolution was prepared, not spontaneous

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
Green is making a two-part argument: the Great Awakening and the Seven Years War both contributed to American independence.

Main idea development tracker:

Event What Green says it did to colonial identity How it connects to the Revolution Great Awakening Seven Years War

After watching: In 2 sentences, state Green's main argument. Then identify one moment in the episode where the two threads he's tracking come together. This is 9-10.R.4 work — tracking how two parallel ideas develop and converge into a single main claim.

docs.title: Full Transcript
docs.text: Transcript pending. This entry is a stub.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. The Awakening's Political Logic: Students read the key claim: "If you can have a direct relationship with God without a minister, why do you need a king?" They write a paragraph either agreeing or disagreeing with this logical chain — using evidence from the episode and one example from elsewhere. Forces logical analysis (9-10.R.9c) and historical thinking.

  2. The Proclamation of 1763 — Two Perspectives: Students write two 2-sentence reactions to the Proclamation: one from a British Parliament member (we just spent a fortune keeping colonists safe — they owe us) and one from a Virginia land speculator (we fought this war for the right to expand). Then they discuss: Was the British position unreasonable? This builds WH.6_12.5 causal analysis and perspective-taking.

  3. Young George Washington: Green mentions that Washington's early military career included notable failures. Students research Fort Necessity (1754) — Washington's surrender to French forces — and write a paragraph: what does this failure reveal about Washington that the standard "founding father" story doesn't include? Then they connect: how might failure have shaped his later leadership? Targets 9-10.R.8b (how a character's background shapes their actions) applied to historical figure.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Seven Years' War is the clearest historical example of a pattern that repeats: winning a war creates problems that cost more than the war itself. Britain's victory left it with massive debt and a new challenge — how to pay for governing a much larger empire. The answer (taxing the colonies) produced a revolution. This dynamic — military victory producing political crisis — is one historians call "imperial overextension," and it appears in every era of powerful nations' history. The costs of long wars, the challenge of governing territories far from the capital, and the political backlash when those costs are passed down to people who had no say in the decision are all contemporary debates. See USH-07: The Unipolar Moment for how this plays out in the post-Cold War era.
