docs.title: CC USH Ep 06 — The Industrial Revolution
docs.text: CrashCourse US History · Episode 06 (approximate) Era: USH Unit 1 — Industrial Revolution & Progressive Era Standards: US.6_12.4 · US.6_12.5 · ELA R.9 · W.3

docs.title: Watch
docs.text: Watch on YouTube

docs.title: Episode Summary
docs.text: John Green traces the transformation of the American economy between the Civil War and 1900 — from a nation of farms and small workshops to the world's largest industrial power. The episode covers the railroad boom (which stitched the continent together and created a national market), the rise of corporations and trusts, the brutal conditions in mines and factories, and the emergence of organized labor as workers pushed back. Key figures: Andrew Carnegie (steel), John D. Rockefeller (Standard Oil), and the workers — immigrant and native-born — who ran the machines. Green's argument: industrialization created enormous wealth AND enormous inequality simultaneously, and the question of who should benefit from economic growth became the defining political question of the era.

docs.title: Standards Alignment
docs.text: Standard Connection US.6_12.4 Social and economic transformation — class structure, immigration, labor conditions US.6_12.5 Causes and effects — what drove industrialization and what it produced US.6_12.6 Past to present — corporate power, wealth inequality, labor rights still live today ELA R.9 Green makes an argument about industrialization and inequality — analyze his structure ELA W.3 Explain how industrialization changed American life (social, economic, political)

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Vertical and horizontal integration — Carnegie buys everything (ore to rail to steel); Rockefeller buys all competitors
  • Trusts and monopolies — Standard Oil controls 90% of US oil refining by 1880
  • Railroad as infrastructure — creates national market, enables mass production
  • Labor conditions — 12-hour days, no safety regulations, child labor, no job security
  • Immigration and the workforce — waves of Southern/Eastern European immigrants fuel factory growth
  • Knights of Labor → AFL — two different strategies for labor organizing

docs.title: Watch Prompts
docs.text:
Use these while watching:

  1. Green says industrialization created "the modern economy." What does he mean — what specifically changed that hadn't existed before?
  2. What is Green's argument about who benefited from industrialization and who didn't? What evidence does he use?
  3. How did workers try to resist their conditions? What made organizing difficult?
  4. What does this episode tell you about the relationship between technology and inequality?

docs.title: Key Lines
docs.text:
"The railroads didn't just move goods — they moved people, and ideas, and they created the possibility of a national economy."

"Carnegie and Rockefeller were not outlaws. They were doing what the economic system rewarded."

"The workers who built all this wealth saw almost none of it."

docs.title: Pairs With
docs.text:
• The Jungle — Upton Sinclair — muckraker exposé of meatpacking conditions; R.9 + US.6_12.4
  • USH Unit 1 Standards
  • Gr 10 Crosswalk — Units 1–3

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The debates this episode documents — about whether extreme wealth concentration is an acceptable outcome of a free market, what workers are owed by the companies that profit from their labor, and what government's role should be in regulating the economy — are not resolved. Gig workers fighting for benefits, Amazon warehouse employees organizing unions, debates about minimum wage and corporate tax rates, and arguments about billionaire philanthropy vs. democratic redistribution are all continuations of the same arguments being made in the 1890s. Carnegie and Rockefeller believed they were building something great for America; the workers whose labor built their empires believed something different. That argument is still going. For the literary dimension, see The Jungle and USH-01: When the System Fails.

CrashCourse US History Ep 06 — SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

docs.title: CC USH Ep 07 — The Progressive Era
docs.text: CrashCourse US History · Episode 07 (approximate) Era: USH Unit 1 — Industrial Revolution & Progressive Era Standards: US.6_12.3 · US.6_12.4 · US.6_12.6 · ELA R.9 · W.4

docs.title: Watch
docs.text: Watch on YouTube

docs.title: Episode Summary
docs.text: The Progressive Era (roughly 1890–1920) was America's first major attempt to use government power to fix the problems created by industrialization. John Green covers the muckrakers — journalists like Ida Tarbell (who dismantled Standard Oil's reputation with investigative reporting), Jacob Riis (who photographed tenement conditions), and Upton Sinclair (whose The Jungle shocked the nation into demanding food safety laws). He traces the political reforms that followed: antitrust legislation, the FDA, the income tax, direct election of senators, women's suffrage. Green's argument: the Progressives believed that democracy required active government intervention to counteract the power of concentrated wealth — a debate that never went away.

docs.title: Standards Alignment
docs.text: Standard Connection US.6_12.3 Political reform — how democratic systems changed in response to industrial inequality US.6_12.4 Social reform — labor laws, food safety, housing reform US.6_12.5 Causes and effects — what caused the Progressive movement and what it produced US.6_12.6 Past to present — investigative journalism, government regulation still live debates ELA R.9 Muckraker journalism as argument — analyze Sinclair's argumentative strategy ELA W.4 Write your own reform argument using muckraker techniques

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Muckraker journalism — investigative reporting as a tool for political change
  • Ida Tarbell — dismantled Standard Oil through documented reporting; Sherman Antitrust Act enforcement follows
  • Upton Sinclair's The Jungle — aimed for hearts, hit stomachs — led to Pure Food and Drug Act
  • Theodore Roosevelt — "trust-buster" president; uses federal power to break up monopolies
  • Constitutional amendments — 16th (income tax), 17th (direct Senate elections), 18th (Prohibition), 19th (women's suffrage)
  • Women's suffrage movement — culminates in 19th Amendment (1920)

docs.title: Watch Prompts
docs.text:
1. What is Green's argument about what the Progressive movement actually accomplished — and what it failed to do?
  2. How did muckraker journalists use evidence to make arguments? How is that similar to or different from what you do in a W.4 essay?
  3. Green mentions that Sinclair "aimed for the heart but hit the stomach" — what does that mean, and what does it tell us about how arguments actually produce change?
  4. Who was left out of the Progressive Era's reforms? What does that tell us about who had political power?

docs.title: Key Lines
docs.text:
"The muckrakers believed that if you showed people the truth, they would demand change. That turned out to be half right."

"Sinclair wanted to make Americans care about the workers. Instead, they cared about the meat."

"The Progressives didn't agree on much, but they agreed that democracy required limits on concentrated power."

docs.title: Pairs With
docs.text:
• The Jungle — Upton Sinclair — primary source; R.9 + W.4 mentor text
  • USH Unit 1 Standards
  • Gr 10 Crosswalk — Units 1–3

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Progressive Era established the model of using federal regulation and investigative journalism to check corporate power — and every major regulatory agency in the United States traces its philosophical origins to this period. The FDA, FTC, FCC, NLRB, and EPA all exist because Progressive Era reformers argued that markets don't self-correct when the costs of corporate behavior fall on the public rather than on the corporation. Every contemporary debate about whether to regulate a new industry — social media, artificial intelligence, pharmaceutical pricing, platform monopolies — is a debate about whether the Progressive Era model still applies, what it got right, and where it fell short. The muckrakers also established that investigative journalism is a necessary check on power, which is why attacks on press freedom and media credibility are attacks on the Progressive Era's core institutional legacy.

CrashCourse US History Ep 07 — SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

docs.title: CC USH Ep 08 — America and World War I
docs.text: CrashCourse US History · Episode 08 (approximate) Era: USH Unit 2 — Imperialism & WWI Standards: US.6_12.5 · US.6_12.2 · ELA R.9 · W.4

docs.title: Watch
docs.text: Watch on YouTube

docs.title: Episode Summary
docs.text: This episode covers WWI from the American angle — why the US stayed neutral until 1917, what changed (the Zimmermann Telegram, unrestricted submarine warfare, economic ties to Britain), and what the war did to American society once the country entered. Green examines the propaganda machine that turned public opinion, the suppression of dissent through the Espionage and Sedition Acts, and the experience of soldiers who arrived on the Western Front to find a war nothing like what they'd been told. He also covers what the war did at home: the Great Migration begins as Black Americans move north for war industry jobs; women enter the workforce in large numbers. Green's argument: America's entry into WWI reshaped both the war and American society in ways that outlasted the armistice.

docs.title: Standards Alignment
docs.text: Standard Connection US.6_12.5 Causes and effects — why the US entered, what the war produced US.6_12.2 War propaganda as ideology — how governments shape public belief US.6_12.3 Civil liberties under pressure — Espionage Act, Sedition Act US.6_12.4 The Great Migration; women in the workforce ELA R.9 WWI propaganda as argument — analyze how it worked ELA W.4 Argument about whether US entry was justified or avoidable

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• American neutrality 1914–1917 — economic ties to Britain, Wilson's "too proud to fight"
  • Zimmermann Telegram — Germany proposes Mexico attack the US; intercepted by Britain
  • Unrestricted submarine warfare — Germany sinks Lusitania (1915) and resumes sinking US ships (1917)
  • War propaganda — Committee on Public Information; "Four Minute Men"; Liberty Bond posters
  • Espionage Act (1917) / Sedition Act (1918) — criminalizes anti-war speech
  • The Great Migration — 500,000 Black Americans move north for war industry jobs 1916–1920
  • Wilson's Fourteen Points — America's vision for post-war world; Senate rejects League of Nations

docs.title: Watch Prompts
docs.text:
1. Green argues there were multiple causes for American entry into WWI. What does he identify as the most important? Do you agree?
  2. How did the US government use propaganda to build public support for the war? What techniques did it use?
  3. The Espionage and Sedition Acts criminalized anti-war speech. How does Green frame this — as necessary wartime measure or as overreach?
  4. Who benefited from the wartime economy? Who paid the costs?

docs.title: Key Lines
docs.text:
"The Zimmermann Telegram is one of history's great intelligence coups — and one of Germany's great blunders."

"The government didn't just ask Americans to support the war. It made opposition illegal."

"The Great Migration is one of the most significant demographic shifts in American history — and it happened because of a war."

docs.title: Pairs With
docs.text:
• Dulce et Decorum Est — Wilfred Owen — poetic argument against the war's propaganda; R.9
  • CrashCourse WH Ep 38 — WWI — global angle on the same war
  • USH Unit 2 Standards
  • Gr 10 Crosswalk — Units 1–3

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
American entry into WWI established patterns that have repeated in every subsequent war: the transformation of public opinion through propaganda campaigns, the suppression of dissent through wartime legislation (the Espionage Act and Sedition Act — both still on the books and used in prosecutions today), and the gap between stated war aims and actual geopolitical interests. The Espionage Act used to prosecute Daniel Ellsberg for leaking the Pentagon Papers was passed in 1917. The same Act was used in the prosecution of Edward Snowden and Chelsea Manning. Understanding the WWI-era origins of these laws matters for evaluating how they're being applied today and whether the suppression of dissent they enabled then is what their contemporary application is producing now.

CrashCourse US History Ep 08 — SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

docs.title: CC USH Ep 09 — The Great Depression
docs.text: CrashCourse US History · Episode 09 (approximate) Era: USH Unit 3 — Roaring 20s & Great Depression Standards: US.6_12.4 · US.6_12.5 · US.6_12.3 · ELA R.9 · W.3

docs.title: Watch
docs.text: Watch on YouTube

docs.title: Episode Summary
docs.text: John Green covers the crash of 1929 and the decade of Depression that followed — but his argument isn't just about economics. He traces how the Depression changed what Americans thought government was for. Before 1929, most Americans believed the economy was self-correcting and government should stay out. After a decade of 25% unemployment, bank failures, and the Dust Bowl, that belief collapsed. FDR's New Deal didn't end the Depression (WWII did), but it permanently changed the relationship between citizens and government: Social Security, federal bank insurance, labor protections, rural electrification. Green also covers who the New Deal left out — Black Americans and agricultural workers were systematically excluded from most programs. His argument: the Depression didn't just reshape the economy; it reshaped what Americans expected from democracy.

docs.title: Standards Alignment
docs.text: Standard Connection US.6_12.4 Unemployment, poverty, Dust Bowl — social and economic collapse US.6_12.5 Causes — stock speculation, bank failures, Smoot-Hawley tariff; effects — New Deal, WWII US.6_12.3 New Deal as political transformation — what government does expands permanently US.6_12.6 Past to present — Social Security, FDIC, labor law all still active today ELA R.9 FDR's Fireside Chats as argument — how does he rebuild public confidence? ELA W.3 Explain how the Depression changed the role of government in American life

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Black Tuesday (October 29, 1929) — stock market crash; not the sole cause of the Depression but the trigger
  • Bank runs and bank failures — 9,000 banks fail 1930–1933; savings wiped out
  • Hoover's response — believes economy will self-correct; too slow to act
  • The Dust Bowl — drought + overfarming creates environmental catastrophe; 500,000 displaced
  • FDR's New Deal — three Rs: Relief (immediate aid), Recovery (economic stimulus), Reform (prevent future crashes)
  • New Deal exclusions — agricultural and domestic workers excluded from most protections; these were majority-Black occupations in the South
  • Keynesian economics — government spending as stimulus; new idea at the time

docs.title: Watch Prompts
docs.text:
1. What does Green identify as the causes of the Great Depression? Which does he think was most significant?
  2. What is his argument about what the New Deal actually accomplished — and what it failed to do?
  3. Who was systematically excluded from New Deal protections? Why does Green emphasize this?
  4. Green says the Depression "changed what Americans thought government was for." What did they think before? What did they think after?

docs.title: Key Lines
docs.text:
"The Depression didn't just break the economy. It broke the idea that the economy would fix itself."

"The New Deal didn't end the Depression. But it changed what Americans expected their government to do — and that change was permanent."

"Most New Deal programs explicitly excluded Black Americans. That wasn't accidental."

docs.title: Pairs With
docs.text:
• USH Unit 3 Standards
  • Gr 10 Crosswalk — Units 1–3

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Great Depression established the argument — still contested — that government has an obligation to intervene when markets fail everyone at once. The New Deal programs built in response are still operating: Social Security, unemployment insurance, the FDIC, the SEC, the minimum wage. Every debate about the appropriate role of government in the economy is an argument about whether FDR's response was the right model and whether its provisions should be expanded, preserved, or reduced. The 2008 financial crisis and the COVID-19 economic shock were both responses to the same question: are we going to use the Depression playbook? Understanding what the Depression actually was — not just a stock market crash but a collapse of the entire economic system that lasted a decade — is necessary for evaluating those responses and the debates about their adequacy.

CrashCourse US History Ep 09 — SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

docs.title: CC USH Ep 10 — WWII and the American Home Front
docs.text: CrashCourse US History · Episode 10 (approximate) Era: USH Unit 4 — World War II Standards: US.6_12.4 · US.6_12.5 · US.6_12.3 · ELA R.9 · R.8 · W.4

docs.title: Watch
docs.text: Watch on YouTube

docs.title: Episode Summary
docs.text: While CrashCourse World History covers WWII's global scope (see ep-40 and ep-41), this episode focuses on what the war meant for Americans at home. Green covers the economic transformation — the Depression finally ends as war production employs everyone; women enter the industrial workforce in massive numbers ("Rosie the Riveter"); Black Americans migrate north and west in the second wave of the Great Migration. He also covers what the war demanded: rationing, propaganda, war bonds, and the enormous human cost (400,000 American dead). The darkest moment: Japanese American internment — 120,000 US citizens imprisoned by Executive Order 9066 without charges or trial. Green's argument: WWII is remembered as "the good war," but its moral complexity — fighting fascism abroad while practicing racism at home — is essential to understanding what it actually was.

docs.title: Standards Alignment
docs.text: Standard Connection US.6_12.4 Home front transformation — women, Black workers, Japanese American internment US.6_12.5 Causes and effects — Pearl Harbor → mobilization → victory → Cold War US.6_12.3 Japanese American internment as civil liberties crisis US.6_12.2 War propaganda — how government shaped public belief about the war ELA R.9 FDR's Four Freedoms speech — wartime argument about what America is fighting for ELA R.8 Night — literary testimony about what the war cost; W.4 crosswalk on "the good war"

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Pearl Harbor (December 7, 1941) — Japanese attack on US naval base; US enters WWII the next day
  • War production mobilization — US factories retool; GDP doubles; unemployment drops to 1%
  • Women in the workforce — 6 million women take industrial jobs; "Rosie the Riveter" as cultural symbol
  • Second Great Migration — 1.5 million Black Americans move north/west 1940–1970 for war jobs
  • Japanese American internment — Executive Order 9066; Korematsu v. United States upholds it (1944)
  • The Holocaust and American response — US knew about death camps by 1942; chose not to bomb rail lines
  • Atomic bombs — Hiroshima and Nagasaki (August 1945); Japan surrenders; raises moral questions that persist

docs.title: Watch Prompts
docs.text:
1. Green says WWII ended the Depression, not the New Deal. What does he mean, and what does that tell us about how economies work?
  2. What were the contradictions in America "fighting for freedom" during WWII? What examples does Green give?
  3. How did the government use propaganda to build support for the war? Compare to WWI (ep-ush-08).
  4. Green calls Japanese American internment "one of the worst violations of civil liberties in American history." Do you agree? What would justify it — if anything?

docs.title: Key Lines
docs.text:
"The war solved the unemployment crisis that the New Deal couldn't. That tells you something uncomfortable about the relationship between war and capitalism."

"Rosie the Riveter was a symbol — but the women doing the actual riveting were real, and most of them were sent home when the men came back."

"We were fighting against a regime that sorted people by race. And we were doing the same thing at home."

docs.title: Pairs With
docs.text:
• FDR — Four Freedoms Speech — wartime argument; R.9
  • Night — Elie Wiesel — literary testimony; R.8
  • CrashCourse WH Ep 40 — WWII Part 1
  • CrashCourse WH Ep 41 — WWII Part 2
  • USH Unit 4 Standards
  • Gr 10 Crosswalk — Units 4–6

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The WWII home front established what industrial democracy looks like under total war mobilization — and also what civil liberties look like when national security is used to justify suspending them. Japanese American internment was ruled constitutional by the Supreme Court in 1944 (Korematsu v. United States) — a decision not formally overturned until 2018. The military remained racially segregated through a war fought against racial ideology. These contradictions between stated values and actual wartime practice are not historical accidents; they're what happens when security fears override constitutional protections. Every subsequent debate about civil liberties in wartime — detention without trial, surveillance programs, travel bans — engages with the same tension the home front experience revealed.

CrashCourse US History Ep 10 — SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

docs.title: CC USH Ep 11 — The Civil Rights Movement
docs.text: CrashCourse US History · Episode 11 (approximate) Era: USH Unit 6 — Civil Rights Movement Standards: US.6_12.3 · US.6_12.4 · US.6_12.6 · ELA R.9 · R.8 · W.4

docs.title: Watch
docs.text: Watch on YouTube

docs.title: Episode Summary
docs.text: John Green covers the Civil Rights Movement from the legal strategy of the NAACP through the sit-ins, Freedom Rides, March on Washington, and legislative victories of the mid-1960s. He traces how the movement built its strategy — Thurgood Marshall's courtroom approach (Brown v. Board, 1954), MLK's philosophy of nonviolent direct action, SNCC's grassroots organizing, and the role of televised violence (Bull Connor's fire hoses in Birmingham) in shifting public opinion. He covers the major legislation: Civil Rights Act (1964), Voting Rights Act (1965). Green also raises the tensions: Malcolm X's challenge to nonviolence; the limits of legal victory without economic equality; the backlash that followed. His argument: the Civil Rights Movement succeeded at dismantling legal segregation — but the question of whether legal equality produces actual equality remains open.

docs.title: Standards Alignment
docs.text: Standard Connection US.6_12.3 Protest and political change — how the movement used law, demonstration, and media US.6_12.4 Economic and social inequality — why legal victory didn't end the story US.6_12.6 Past to present — Voting Rights Act gutted 2013; racial wealth gap persists ELA R.9 MLK "Letter from Birmingham Jail" — the movement's clearest argumentative text ELA R.8 Warriors Don't Cry — individual experience of desegregation as literary memoir ELA W.4 Argument about whether nonviolent protest is the most effective strategy

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• NAACP legal strategy — incremental courtroom wins leading to Brown v. Board of Education (1954)
  • Montgomery Bus Boycott (1955) — 381 days; Rosa Parks; MLK emerges as leader
  • Sit-ins and Freedom Rides — nonviolent direct action to expose and shame segregationists
  • Birmingham Campaign (1963) — Bull Connor's violence on national television; turns public opinion
  • March on Washington (August 28, 1963) — "I Have a Dream" speech; 250,000 people
  • Civil Rights Act (1964) — prohibits discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, national origin
  • Voting Rights Act (1965) — eliminates literacy tests and other barriers to Black voting
  • Malcolm X and Black Power — challenge to nonviolence and integration as the only path

docs.title: Watch Prompts
docs.text:
1. Green identifies multiple strategies within the Civil Rights Movement. What were they, and what did each accomplish?
  2. What is Green's argument about the relationship between legal equality and actual equality? Do you agree?
  3. How did television change the Civil Rights Movement? What would have been different without it?
  4. Why does Green spend time on Malcolm X's critique of nonviolence? What is he trying to show?

docs.title: Key Lines
docs.text:
"The Civil Rights Movement didn't just change the law. It changed what Americans thought the law was supposed to do."

"Nonviolence wasn't passive. It was a strategy — a calculated choice to expose your opponent's violence by refusing to respond in kind."

"The Voting Rights Act was the movement's greatest legal victory. It was gutted by the Supreme Court in 2013."

docs.title: Pairs With
docs.text:
• MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — R.9 + W.4 mentor text; crosswalk guide
  • Warriors Don't Cry — Melba Beals — R.8; individual experience of desegregation
  • USH Unit 6 Standards
  • Gr 10 Crosswalk — Units 4–6

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Civil Rights movement is living history: the people who were there are still alive, the legal gains are still being contested, and the underlying conditions the movement addressed have not been fully resolved. Voting rights, affirmative action, school integration, police reform, and the racial wealth gap are not historical questions — they are policy debates happening in legislatures and courtrooms right now. The movement is also the most thoroughly studied case in American history of how social change actually happens: what combination of legal strategy, direct action, political pressure, media coverage, and coalition-building is required to move institutions that have strong incentives not to move. That model is studied and applied by every subsequent social movement.

CrashCourse US History Ep 11 — SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

docs.title: CC USH Ep 12 — Vietnam and Watergate
docs.text: CrashCourse US History · Episode 12 (approximate) Era: USH Unit 7 — Vietnam Era & Watergate Standards: US.6_12.5 · US.6_12.2 · US.6_12.3 · ELA R.9 · W.4

docs.title: Watch
docs.text: Watch on YouTube

docs.title: Episode Summary
docs.text: Green covers America's longest war and the political scandal that destroyed a presidency. On Vietnam: why the US got in (containment doctrine, domino theory), why it couldn't get out (political cost of losing), and what the war actually looked like on the ground (guerrilla warfare, civilian casualties, the draft, the credibility gap as the government's public statements diverged from reality). On the home front: massive anti-war protest, Kent State (1970), the Pentagon Papers revealing systematic government deception. Then Watergate: Nixon's 1972 re-election campaign authorizes a break-in at Democratic headquarters; the cover-up unravels over two years; Nixon resigns August 9, 1974. Green's argument: Vietnam and Watergate together produced a collapse in American trust in government that reshaped politics for decades — and that hasn't fully recovered.

docs.title: Standards Alignment
docs.text: Standard Connection US.6_12.5 Causes and effects — why the US entered Vietnam; what the war and Watergate produced US.6_12.2 Domino theory as ideology; government propaganda and the "credibility gap" US.6_12.3 Constitutional crisis — Watergate and the limits of executive power US.6_12.6 Past to present — trust in government collapses; anti-war protest tactics still used today ELA R.9 Anti-war argument — analyze speeches, Pentagon Papers excerpts as argumentative texts ELA W.4 Argument about whether the US should have entered/escalated Vietnam

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Containment doctrine — US foreign policy since Truman: stop Soviet/communist expansion everywhere
  • Domino theory — if Vietnam falls to communism, all of Southeast Asia follows; used to justify intervention
  • Gulf of Tonkin Resolution (1964) — gives LBJ authority to escalate; later revealed as based on exaggerated/false reports
  • Tet Offensive (1968) — North Vietnamese attack across South Vietnam; US claims it's winning; public realizes it isn't
  • The credibility gap — divergence between government statements and reality; fundamental to understanding the era
  • Kent State (May 4, 1970) — National Guard kills 4 student protesters at Ohio university
  • Pentagon Papers (1971) — classified study showing systematic government deception on Vietnam; New York Times publishes; Supreme Court allows it
  • Watergate (1972–1974) — break-in → cover-up → Saturday Night Massacre → tapes → resignation
  • War Powers Act (1973) — Congress tries to limit presidential war-making authority

docs.title: Watch Prompts
docs.text:
1. What is Green's argument about why the US couldn't leave Vietnam once it entered? What kept escalating the commitment?
  2. What is the "credibility gap" and why does Green think it matters so much?
  3. How did Watergate change the relationship between Americans and their government? What evidence does Green give?
  4. Green connects Vietnam and Watergate as a single era of declining trust. Do you think that's fair — or are they separate stories?

docs.title: Key Lines
docs.text:
"The domino theory was never proven. But it was never really about dominoes — it was about the political cost of being the president who lost Vietnam."

"The credibility gap is what happens when a government keeps telling people one thing while doing another. Eventually people stop believing anything."

"Watergate didn't just end a presidency. It ended something Americans had assumed about their government — that it basically played by the rules."

docs.title: Pairs With
docs.text:
• Mandela — Rivonia Trial Speech — resistance argument under state pressure; R.9
  • USH Unit 7 Standards
  • Gr 10 Crosswalk — Units 7–9

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Vietnam and Watergate together produced the collapse of institutional trust that defines American political culture today. The public's default assumption that government officials are not telling the truth, that official statements should be verified rather than believed, and that powerful people routinely abuse their positions — all of that dates to the 1970s and has never fully recovered. The question of whether the institutions that exposed Nixon (the press, the Senate, the special prosecutor, the Supreme Court) would hold in a future crisis has been tested repeatedly since Watergate. Understanding what those institutions actually did in 1972–74, and what conditions made them work, is necessary for evaluating whether they're adequate now and what it would take to strengthen them.

CrashCourse US History Ep 12 — SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: The Agricultural Revolution
docs.text: Episode: 1 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~12 min YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yocja_N5s1I Era: Era 1 — Emergence of Civilizations and Religions Around the Globe WH Standard: WH.6_12.4 — Analyze the influence of social, cultural, and economic development on individuals ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze the development of informational and argumentative elements in nonfiction text

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: John Green opens the CrashCourse World History series by arguing that the Agricultural Revolution — the transition from nomadic foraging to settled farming — may be the single most consequential event in human history, and possibly not in the way students expect. The episode challenges the assumption that farming was obviously "better" than foraging, presenting evidence that early farmers were shorter, sicker, and worked harder than their nomadic predecessors. The central provocation: if agriculture made individual lives worse in the short term, why did it spread everywhere?

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• The Agricultural Revolution (c. 10,000–8,000 BCE) as a global phenomenon, not a single event
  • Fertile Crescent as the earliest documented site of wheat and barley cultivation
  • Simultaneous, independent origins in multiple regions: Yellow River Valley (millet), Mesoamerica (maize), New Guinea (taro)
  • Sedentary lifestyle enabling population growth — more calories per acre, more births per woman
  • The paradox: skeletal evidence shows early farmers had worse nutrition, more disease, and shorter lives than foragers
  • Surplus production as the precondition for social stratification, specialization, and eventually civilization
  • Gender and labor: division of agricultural labor and early hierarchies
  • John Green's argument: "We didn't choose agriculture; agriculture chose us" — population pressure made foraging untenable

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Why start with agriculture? The argument for its importance
  • [1:30] — What is the Agricultural Revolution? When and where?
  • [3:00] — The Fertile Crescent: wheat, barley, goats, sheep
  • [4:30] — Independent origins worldwide: China, Mesoamerica, New Guinea
  • [6:00] — The paradox: archaeology of early farmer skeletons (shorter, more cavities, more disease)
  • [8:00] — Why agriculture spread anyway: caloric density and population growth
  • [9:30] — Surplus → specialization → hierarchy → civilization
  • [11:00] — Conclusion: Was agriculture a choice or a trap?

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: John Green argues that agriculture made individual lives worse but spread anyway. What evidence does he give for each side of this claim? Do you find his explanation convincing? Why or why not?

Use the double-entry format: | What the episode claims | Evidence it gives | Your assessment | |------------------------|-------------------|----------------| | | | |

This connects to WH.6_12.4: analyzing how economic development (agriculture) shaped social structures — even when that development came at a cost to individuals.

docs.text:

Then paste the output into this section.

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"Hi, I'm John Green, this is CrashCourse World History, and today we're going to talk about the Agricultural Revolution, which was arguably the worst mistake in human history."

"Farming enabled humans to produce more calories per acre of land than foraging ever could. More calories meant more people. More people meant you couldn't go back."

"Hunter-gatherers worked maybe four hours a day. Early farmers worked from sunup to sundown. And they were shorter, they had more cavities, they showed signs of nutritional deficiency. Civilization, my friends, came at a cost."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.4 Social & Cultural — Era 1 — economic development, surplus, and hierarchy
  • WH.6_12.1 Science & Tech — Era 1 — agricultural technology as transformative innovation

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. The Trap Argument: After watching, students write a 1-paragraph "What if?" — if humans had somehow maintained foraging as population grew, what would the world look like? Then they identify what evidence from the episode they're using and what they're speculating. This targets WH.6_12.4 (economic development shaping individual lives) and 9-10.R.9 (evaluating an argumentative claim in an informational text).

  2. Skeleton Evidence Gallery: Students are given 3 primary-source-style descriptions of archaeological finds (early farmer skeletons vs. forager skeletons) and must connect the physical evidence to Green's argument. They annotate: "This supports Green's claim because..." — building the textual evidence habit for 9-10.R.2.

  3. Revolution Debate: Is the Agricultural Revolution actually revolutionary? Students receive the definition of a "revolution" from their ELA vocabulary work and must argue whether agriculture qualifies. Forces them to use precise academic language about change and continuity.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Agricultural Revolution produced surplus, which produced hierarchy, which produced civilization, which produced the world we live in — including its inequalities. Industrial agriculture, factory farming, and vertical farming are all attempts to solve the same problem that drove the original shift from foraging to farming: how do you feed more people from limited land? The episode's central provocation — that early farmers had worse nutrition, more disease, and shorter lives than the foragers they replaced, yet agriculture spread everywhere — is a useful frame for every debate about whether technological progress actually makes people's lives better. Once a society is on a path that requires surplus production to sustain its population, the costs of getting off that path may exceed the costs of staying on it even when it's not ideal. That's not ancient history — it's the situation of industrial agriculture today.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: Mesopotamia
docs.text: Episode: 3 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: Era 1 — Emergence of Civilizations and Religions Around the Globe WH Standard: WH.6_12.1 — Analyze the impact of scientific and technological developments on individuals and societies ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze the development of informational and argumentative elements in nonfiction text

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: John Green enters the Fertile Crescent — the Tigris and Euphrates river valley in modern Iraq — to ask what it actually means to build a civilization. Mesopotamia produced the world's first cities (Uruk, Ur, Lagash), the first writing system (cuneiform, invented not for poetry but for accounting grain and livestock), and the first formal law codes. Green's central argument challenges the romantic story: writing didn't emerge to record culture; it emerged because someone needed to track who owed whom three goats. Civilization, in this telling, starts as a logistics problem. He also covers Sargon of Akkad — history's first empire-builder — and the Epic of Gilgamesh, the oldest surviving work of literature, which raises the same question every civilization has raised: what does it mean to be human, and what do we owe each other?

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• The Fertile Crescent: Tigris-Euphrates river valley as the site of the world’s earliest cities
  • Cuneiform writing invented c. 3200 BCE as an accounting tool — not for literature
  • Sumerian city-states: Uruk, Ur, Lagash — governance, temples, surplus agriculture
  • Irrigation technology enabling dense urban populations in a semi-arid environment
  • Sargon of Akkad (c. 2334 BCE): first empire in recorded history
  • The Epic of Gilgamesh: themes of mortality, friendship, and the meaning of civilization
  • Social stratification, craft specialization, and priestly authority
  • The tension between city-state independence and imperial consolidation

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Why start with Mesopotamia?
  • [1:30] — The Fertile Crescent: rivers, agriculture, surplus
  • [3:00] — Cuneiform: writing as accounting, not art
  • [5:00] — Sumerian city-states and temple authority
  • [7:00] — Sargon of Akkad and the first empire
  • [9:00] — The Epic of Gilgamesh: what civilization asks of us
  • [11:00] — Conclusion: civilization as logistics

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: John Green argues that writing was invented to track grain, not to record culture. What evidence does he give? Does this change how you think about what “civilization” means?

Use the double-entry format: | What the episode claims | Evidence it gives | Your assessment | |------------------------|-------------------|----------------| | | | |

This connects to WH.6_12.1: cuneiform, irrigation systems, and early urban planning are all scientific and technological developments that transformed what human social organization was capable of.

docs.title: Full Transcript
docs.text:
Transcript pending. This entry is a stub.

Then paste the output into this section.

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"The first written words weren’t poetry. They were receipts."

"You can’t have civilization without surplus. And you can’t track surplus without writing. So writing, in a very real sense, is the foundation of everything."

"Gilgamesh is the oldest story we have, and it’s already asking the question that all civilization asks: what do we do with the fact that we’re going to die?"

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.1 Science & Tech — Era 1
  • WH.6_12.3 Political Systems — Era 1

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. The Accounting Argument: Students write a 1-paragraph rebuttal or defense of Green’s claim that civilization is fundamentally a logistics problem. Then they identify what evidence from the episode they’re using and what they’re speculating. Targets WH.6_12.1 and 9-10.R.9.

  2. Gilgamesh vs. Hammurabi: After watching, students compare two Mesopotamian texts: a passage from the Epic of Gilgamesh and a selection from Hammurabi’s Code. What does each reveal about Mesopotamian values? What do they disagree on? Targets WH.6_12.3 and R.9.

  3. First Empire Analysis: Students research Sargon of Akkad and write a one-page analysis: Was Sargon’s empire a natural extension of Sumerian city-state logic, or a break from it? What does "the first empire" tell us about how power works? Targets WH.6_12.3 + W.4.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The argument Mesopotamia makes — that complex societies emerge when surplus requires administration, which requires writing, which enables law and hierarchy — is still the basic story of how large organizations work. Every database, every spreadsheet, every inventory system is a descendant of the clay tablets at Uruk. The question of what writing does to power — who controls records, who can read them, who gets to write history — runs from ancient Babylon to debates about algorithmic transparency today. And Gilgamesh’s question — if we’re all going to die, what are we building civilization for? — has not been answered in 4,000 years.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: Alexander the Great and the Situation — the Great?
docs.text: Episode: 8 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: Era 1 — Emergence of Civilizations and Religions Around the Globe WH Standard: WH.6_12.4 — Analyze the influence of social, cultural, and economic factors on individuals and civilizations ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze the development of informational and argumentative elements in nonfiction text

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green opens by asking whether Alexander deserves the title "the Great" — a provocation he never fully settles. By age 32, Alexander had conquered an empire stretching from Greece to modern Pakistan, defeating the Persian Empire, Egypt, and much of Central Asia. Green examines the military genius (the oblique charge, the Companion cavalry, the use of the phalanx) and then asks the harder question: what did Alexander actually leave behind? His answer is Hellenization — the spread of Greek language, architecture, philosophy, and culture across three continents. The episode traces how this cultural diffusion shaped the conditions for early Christianity (the New Testament was written in Greek), Islamic philosophy, and the concept of a "universal" civilization. Green’s central tension: is Alexander great because of what he built, or because of what he destroyed?

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Alexander’s military innovations: the Macedonian phalanx, Companion cavalry, siege engineering
  • Conquest timeline (334–323 BCE): Persia, Egypt, Bactria, northwestern India
  • The founding of Alexandria (Egypt) and 70+ other "Alexandria" cities as nodes of Greek culture
  • Hellenization: the deliberate spread of Greek language, architecture, and intellectual tradition
  • The problem of succession: Alexander died without a named heir, fragmenting the empire into the Diadochi kingdoms
  • Cultural diffusion: how Greek philosophical and artistic traditions merged with Persian, Egyptian, and Indian traditions
  • The Ptolemaic Kingdom (Egypt) as a model of multicultural empire that preserved and transmitted knowledge
  • Whether "greatness" is defined by conquest, cultural legacy, or something else entirely

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Was Alexander actually great?
  • [2:00] — Philip II of Macedon and the phalanx innovation
  • [4:00] — The conquest of Persia: Granicus, Issus, Gaugamela
  • [6:00] — Egypt and the founding of Alexandria
  • [8:00] — The push east: Bactria, the Indus Valley, mutiny
  • [10:00] — Hellenization: what Greek culture did to the world it touched
  • [12:00] — Death, succession, and fragmentation

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: Green never directly answers whether Alexander deserves the title "the Great." By the end of the episode, what is your verdict? Use at least two pieces of evidence from the episode — one for and one against — to support your answer.

Use the double-entry format: | What the episode claims | Evidence it gives | Your assessment | |------------------------|-------------------|----------------| | | | |

This connects to WH.6_12.4: Hellenization is one of history’s most consequential examples of cultural diffusion — how conquest reshapes social, cultural, and intellectual life across entire regions.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:

  2. Hellenization Map Analysis: Using a map of Alexander’s empire and a list of Alexandrias, students trace where Greek cultural influence landed. They annotate: what later civilizations benefited from this diffusion? What was lost? Targets WH.6_12.4 and R.7.

  3. Conquest vs. Legacy Debate: Students debate: should a leader be judged by the method of their rise or by the long-term consequences of their rule? Uses Alexander as the case study. Targets C.6 and WH.6_12.4.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The "great man" question Alexander raises — can one person’s decisions change the trajectory of civilization? — is still how most people understand political leadership. History is full of arguments about whether systems or individuals drive change. Hellenization also raises a question that’s live today: when a powerful culture spreads — through conquest, through trade, through media — what do local cultures preserve and what do they lose? The American cultural export (Hollywood, Silicon Valley platforms, English as global business language) is the 21st-century version of the question Alexander’s conquests first posed.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: The Silk Road and Ancient Trade
docs.text: Episode: 9 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~12 min YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vfe-eNq-Qyg Era: Era 1 — Emergence of Civilizations and Religions Around the Globe WH Standard: WH.6_12.4 — Analyze the influence of social, cultural, and economic development on individuals ELA Connection: R.4 — Determine main idea(s) as they develop over the course of a text and support with textual evidence

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: This episode challenges the romanticized image of the Silk Road as a single continuous highway across Asia traveled by exotic merchants. Green argues that the Silk Road was actually a network of short-hop trade routes, where goods changed hands many times and almost no single trader made the full journey. More important than the silk itself, he argues, was what traveled with it: ideas, religions, diseases, and technologies. The episode sets up how trade networks function as engines of cultural diffusion — a concept that recurs across all four eras.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• The Silk Road as a trade network, not a single road — a series of overlapping routes from China through Central Asia to the Mediterranean
  • Why silk? Chinese monopoly on silk production, its value as currency and status symbol in Rome and Persia
  • Trade goods beyond silk: spices, glassware, cotton, gold, horses (the "blood-sweating" Ferghana horses China traded silk to obtain)
  • Cultural diffusion as the Silk Road's most significant outcome: Buddhism spread east to west to east; Islam spread along trade routes; Christianity traveled the same paths
  • The role of middlemen: Parthians, Sogdians, and the economic logic of controlling the middle
  • Disease transmission: the Antonine Plague (possibly smallpox) arriving in Rome via Silk Road trade
  • The Silk Road's episodic nature — it flourished when Central Asian empires (Mongols, later) guaranteed safe passage, and collapsed when they didn't
  • Green's argument: what matters is not the goods but the "crash" — the collision of cultures, ideas, and microbes

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: The Silk Road myth vs. the Silk Road reality
  • [1:30] — What the Silk Road actually was: network, not highway
  • [3:00] — Why silk? The Chinese monopoly and Roman obsession
  • [4:30] — The horse trade: China's blood-sweating horses and the logic of exchange
  • [6:00] — Buddhism travels west; Christianity travels east
  • [7:30] — The Sogdians: professional middlemen of Central Asia
  • [9:00] — Disease on the road: the Antonine Plague
  • [10:30] — Why the Silk Road opens and closes: imperial stability as prerequisite
  • [11:30] — Conclusion: The Silk Road as a metaphor for globalization

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track the main idea shift in this episode:

Green starts by correcting a misconception (the Silk Road was not what you think) and ends by making a bigger claim (what the Silk Road reveals about how the world works).

Annotation task — use a T-chart: | What Green says the Silk Road was NOT | What Green argues it actually was/did | |--------------------------------------|--------------------------------------| | | |

After watching: In one sentence, what is Green's main argument? Find one specific piece of evidence from the episode that supports it. This targets 9-10.R.4 (main idea development) and WH.6_12.4 (economic development shaping societies and individuals).

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"The Silk Road wasn't a road, and it wasn't really about silk. It was a network of trade routes that connected civilizations — and more importantly, it was a network along which ideas, religions, and diseases traveled."

"Almost no one traveled the entire Silk Road. Merchants would sell goods to the next city, and those merchants would sell them to the next. By the time Roman silk arrived, it had passed through a dozen hands."

"The real cargo of the Silk Road wasn't silk. It was Buddhism. It was Islam. It was plague. The goods were just the excuse for contact."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.4 Social & Cultural — Era 1 — trade networks shaping societies
  • WH.6_12.4 Social & Cultural — Era 2 — Silk Road active into medieval period
  • WH.6_12.6 Past to Present — Era 1 — trade route → globalization connection

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. The Network vs. the Road: Give students a blank map of Eurasia. After watching, they draw what they thought the Silk Road looked like before vs. what Green describes. Then they annotate: what changed in their understanding and why it matters for understanding how ideas spread. Connects to WH.6_12.4 and OEC skill strand.

  2. What Actually Traveled? Students create a ranked list of the five most important things that traveled the Silk Road (can include goods, diseases, religions, technologies). They must justify each ranking using evidence from the episode. Forces argument + evidence practice (9-10.R.9c — evaluating argumentative reasoning).

  3. Silk Road to Now: Students find one modern trade route (shipping lane, internet cable route, pipeline) and write a paragraph comparing it to what Green says about the Silk Road — specifically asking: what else travels along this route besides the stated purpose? Targets WH.6_12.6 (past to present connection).

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Silk Road wasn't primarily about silk — it was about ideas, religions, technologies, and diseases moving through connected trade networks faster than any single government could control. That description fits the internet. China's Belt and Road Initiative is explicitly modeled on the original Silk Road — infrastructure investment across Eurasia to create economic dependency and political influence — and debates about who controls the undersea cables that carry internet traffic have the same strategic logic as debates about who controlled the overland routes in the 13th century. The Black Death also traveled the Silk Road, which is a reminder that connected networks spread pathogens faster than isolated ones — a lesson epidemiologists applied during COVID-19. Understanding the Silk Road is understanding the template for globalization and all its consequences.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: Fall of the Roman Empire… in the 15th Century
docs.text: Episode: 12 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: Era 1 — Emergence of Civilizations and Religions Around the Globe WH Standard: WH.6_12.5 — Analyze the causes and effects of significant events in world history ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze the development of informational and argumentative elements in nonfiction text

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green takes on one of history’s most famous "collapses" and complicates it. He walks through the standard causes of Rome’s fall: the Antonine Plague (165-180 CE) killed 5-10 million people, the "Crisis of the Third Century" produced 26 emperors in 50 years, military overextension required crushing taxation, and Germanic groups steadily encroached on frontier territory. But then Green makes his central argument: Rome didn’t "fall" in 476 CE. The Eastern Roman Empire — what we call the Byzantine Empire — kept going for another 1,000 years, until 1453. What actually fell in 476 was the Western Roman Empire, and even then it transformed rather than collapsed. The episode asks what it means to say a civilization "falls" when its institutions, laws, language, and religion keep shaping the world.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• The Antonine Plague (165-180 CE): estimated 10% population loss, military and agricultural collapse
  • The Crisis of the Third Century (235-284 CE): 26 emperors in 50 years, economic instability
  • Military overextension: the Rhine-Danube frontier required a standing army Rome struggled to fund
  • The split between Western and Eastern Roman Empires (286 CE under Diocletian)
  • Germanic incursions: Visigoths, Vandals, Ostrogoths — not simple "barbarian invasions"
  • 476 CE: Romulus Augustulus deposed — the conventional "end date" of Rome
  • The Byzantine Empire: Eastern Rome survived 1,000 more years, until Constantinople fell in 1453
  • What "collapse" actually means: transformation vs. disappearance

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Why is "fall of Rome" such a big story?
  • [2:00] — The Antonine Plague and its cascading effects
  • [4:00] — The Crisis of the Third Century: political instability
  • [6:00] — Military overextension and economic strain
  • [8:00] — Germanic groups: who were they, and what did they want?
  • [10:00] — 476 CE and what actually happened
  • [12:00] — Byzantium: the empire that didn’t fall

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: Green argues that asking "why did Rome fall?" is the wrong question — we should ask "what does it mean to say a civilization falls?" By the end of the episode, what is your definition of "collapse"? Does Rome fit that definition?

Use the double-entry format: | What the episode claims | Evidence it gives | Your assessment | |------------------------|-------------------|----------------| | | | |

This connects to WH.6_12.5: the fall of the Western Roman Empire is the canonical example of multi-causal historical change — plague, political instability, economic strain, and external pressure combining over centuries.

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"476 CE. That’s when historians say Rome fell. But the Eastern Roman Empire kept going for another 1,000 years. So did Rome fall, or did it just... change addresses?"

"The Antonine Plague killed an estimated 10% of the Roman Empire’s population. Think about what that does to an army. To a tax base. To a supply chain."

"Rome didn’t fall because of the barbarians. Rome fell because it had become too expensive to be Rome."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.5 Causes & Effects — Era 1
  • WH.6_12.3 Political Systems — Era 1

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Was It Inevitable?: Students identify the three causes of Rome’s fall they find most persuasive and write a structured argument: could any of them have been prevented? What would Rome have needed to do differently, and was it actually possible? Targets WH.6_12.5 and W.4.

  2. Defining Collapse: Students write their own definition of "civilizational collapse" and then test it against Rome (476 CE), Byzantium (1453 CE), and one modern example of institutional failure. Targets WH.6_12.5 and R.9.

  3. Plague & Power: Students examine the Antonine Plague’s effects as a case study in how public health crises destabilize political systems. They connect to a modern pandemic (their choice) and ask: what does this comparison reveal? Targets WH.6_12.5 and IR.1-5.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Roman Empire collapsed slowly, over centuries, with warning signs that were visible to contemporaries who largely couldn’t do anything about them. Its fall is the reference point every subsequent civilization uses when it feels unstable — "this is how Rome fell" is a rhetorical move in political arguments from the 5th century to today. The question Green raises — what does it mean for a civilization to "fall" when its legal codes, architectural forms, language (Latin became French, Spanish, Italian, Portuguese, Romanian), and religion survive it? — is actually a question about what civilizations are made of. The institutions outlast the empire. Which raises an uncomfortable question about which of our institutions outlast us.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: Islam, the Quran, and the Five Pillars All Without a Flamewar
docs.text: Episode: 13 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: Era 2 — Expanding Zones of Exchange and Encounter WH Standard: WH.6_12.2 — Analyze the role of belief systems, philosophies, and ideologies in shaping societies ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze the development of informational and argumentative elements in nonfiction text

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green delivers a foundational introduction to Islam without either sanitizing it or sensationalizing it. He covers the life of Muhammad (570-632 CE), the revelation of the Quran in Mecca, the Hijra to Medina, and the early community that formed around the new faith. He explains the Five Pillars (Shahada, Salah, Zakat, Sawm, Hajj) as both spiritual practice and social architecture — the way Islam organized community life. He addresses the Sunni-Shia split (the question of who leads after Muhammad) and traces how Islam spread so rapidly in its first century — not primarily through forced conversion but through trade networks, administrative appeal (lower taxes for converts), and the unifying power of a shared language (Arabic) and law (sharia). The episode also touches on the Islamic Golden Age and what was preserved and transmitted from Greek, Persian, and Indian intellectual traditions.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Muhammad’s life: Mecca, first revelation (610 CE), the Hijra to Medina (622 CE)
  • The Quran: revealed over 23 years, memorized and recited before being compiled in writing
  • The Five Pillars: Shahada (declaration), Salah (prayer), Zakat (almsgiving), Sawm (fasting), Hajj (pilgrimage)
  • The Umma: the community of believers as a social and political unit, not just a religious one
  • The Sunni-Shia split (632 CE): succession dispute — who leads after Muhammad?
  • Rapid spread: 100 years after Muhammad’s death, Islam reached Spain and Central Asia
  • Islamic Golden Age: Baghdad, the House of Wisdom, preservation and translation of Greek texts
  • Sharia: Islamic law as both religious practice and civic governance

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: How to discuss Islam without a flamewar
  • [1:30] — Pre-Islamic Arabia: the context of Muhammad’s world
  • [3:00] — Muhammad’s life, the first revelation, the Hijra
  • [5:00] — The Quran and the Hadith
  • [6:30] — The Five Pillars: what they are and what they do
  • [9:00] — The early caliphate and the Sunni-Shia split
  • [11:00] — How Islam spread so fast
  • [13:00] — The Islamic Golden Age

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: Green argues that the Five Pillars aren’t just religious practices — they’re a social architecture. Choose one Pillar and explain: what social or political function does it serve, beyond its spiritual meaning? Use evidence from the episode.

Use the double-entry format: | What the episode claims | Evidence it gives | Your assessment | |------------------------|-------------------|----------------| | | | |

This connects to WH.6_12.2: Islam is the defining example in Era 2 of how a belief system reshapes governance, social organization, trade networks, and cultural production across an enormous geographic area.

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"In less than 100 years after Muhammad’s death, Islam had spread from Spain to Central Asia. That’s not primarily because of conquest — it’s because of trade, administrative efficiency, and a very compelling message."

"The Five Pillars aren’t just spiritual practices. They’re also a social system. Zakat requires the wealthy to give to the poor. Hajj requires you to stand alongside people from every background on equal terms. The religion literally builds community into its practices."

"Muhammad didn’t see himself as founding a new religion. He saw himself as restoring and completing the revelation that had already been given to Jews and Christians."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.2 Religion & Ideology — Era 2
  • WH.6_12.1 Science & Tech — Era 2

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Five Pillars as Social Architecture: Students choose one Pillar and research its social function: how does this practice build community, establish equality, or organize resources? They write a 1-page analysis connecting the Pillar to WH.6_12.2. Targets R.9 and W.4.

  2. Comparing Spread: Islam vs. Christianity: After watching this episode and reviewing the Crusades episode, students compare how Christianity and Islam each spread in their first 500 years. What role did trade, conquest, language, and law each play? Targets WH.6_12.2 and R.9.

  3. The Golden Age Argument: Students research one specific contribution of the Islamic Golden Age (algebra, astronomy, medicine, philosophy) and write a short argument: why does it matter that Islamic scholars preserved and extended Greek knowledge? Targets WH.6_12.1 and IR.1-5.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Islam is the world’s second-largest religion, with approximately 1.8 billion practitioners. Understanding what the Five Pillars actually are, what the Sunni-Shia divide actually means, and how Islamic civilization contributed to mathematics, medicine, and philosophy is basic civic literacy for anyone who wants to understand contemporary geopolitics in the Middle East, Central Asia, and North Africa. Most American students encounter Islam primarily through post-9/11 news framing, which means they often know what extremist movements claim about the faith while knowing almost nothing about its actual intellectual and spiritual tradition. This episode is a corrective — not because it avoids difficult history, but because it starts with what the faith actually is before engaging with how it has been used and distorted.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: The Dark Ages… How Dark Were They?
docs.text: Episode: 14 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: Era 2 — Expanding Zones of Exchange and Encounter WH Standard: WH.6_12.1 — Analyze the impact of scientific and technological developments on individuals and societies ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze the development of informational and argumentative elements in nonfiction text

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green dismantles the concept of the "Dark Ages" by pointing out that it is a story Western European historians told about themselves. The term was coined by Petrarch in the 14th century to make the Renaissance look impressive by contrast — a rhetorical move, not a historical assessment. Green’s argument: if you lived in the Byzantine Empire, or in the Islamic world during the Golden Age, or in Tang Dynasty China (618-907 CE), or in the Mali Empire, the years 500-1000 CE were not dark at all. They were periods of remarkable intellectual, economic, and artistic flourishing. What the "Dark Ages" actually describes is a narrow regional perspective — Western Europe after Rome. And even there, Green argues, monasteries preserved literacy, Charlemagne attempted to revive learning, and the period laid foundations for the later medieval flowering. The episode is ultimately about who gets to define what counts as "progress."

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• The term "Dark Ages" coined by Petrarch (14th century) as a rhetorical contrast — not a historical category
  • What was actually happening 500-1000 CE: Byzantine Empire, Islamic Golden Age, Tang Dynasty China
  • The Mali Empire and Sub-Saharan African kingdoms — thriving outside the European frame
  • Western European monasticism: preservation of Latin texts, agricultural innovation, literacy centers
  • Charlemagne (768-814 CE): Carolingian Renaissance, standardization of script (Carolingian minuscule)
  • The problem of Eurocentric periodization: calling an era "dark" because one region declined
  • Technology in the supposed Dark Ages: heavy plow, horse collar, water mills, crop rotation
  • What "progress" means — and who defines it

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Are the Dark Ages actually dark?
  • [1:30] — Where did the term come from? (Petrarch)
  • [3:00] — What was happening in the Islamic world 500-1000 CE
  • [5:00] — Byzantine Empire: Rome’s continuation
  • [6:30] — Tang Dynasty China: the most advanced civilization of the era
  • [8:00] — Western Europe: monasteries, Charlemagne, what was actually happening
  • [10:00] — Technology the "Dark Ages" actually produced
  • [12:00] — The problem of Eurocentric history

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: Green argues that the "Dark Ages" is a Eurocentric label. What evidence does he give from outside Europe to support this? Do you find this argument convincing? What would need to be true for "Dark Ages" to be a legitimate historical term?

Use the double-entry format: | What the episode claims | Evidence it gives | Your assessment | |------------------------|-------------------|----------------| | | | |

This connects to WH.6_12.1: the episode shows that scientific and technological development continued during the supposed Dark Ages — just not primarily in Western Europe. Placing intellectual progress in global context is the core skill.

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"The Dark Ages weren’t dark. They were just dark for some people. And those people happened to be the ones writing history."

"While Western Europe was producing Beowulf, Islamic scholars were doing algebra, preserving Aristotle, and building hospitals. The Tang Dynasty was at the peak of its power. It’s a pretty bright dark age."

"Petrarch invented the term ‘Dark Ages’ because he wanted to make himself look good by comparison. That’s not history. That’s marketing."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.1 Science & Tech — Era 2
  • WH.6_12.5 Causes & Effects — Era 2

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Who Gets to Define Progress?: Students write a 1-page argument: is the label "Dark Ages" defensible or misleading? They must use evidence from at least two different regions (Europe AND somewhere else). Targets WH.6_12.1 and W.4.

  2. Non-European 500-1000 CE: Students choose one non-European civilization from this period (Islamic Golden Age, Tang China, Byzantine Empire, Mali Empire) and research one specific intellectual or technological contribution. They present: why should this be in the story we tell about this era? Targets WH.6_12.1 and IR.1-5.

  3. Historiography Audit: Students find a textbook description of the "Dark Ages" and annotate it: what perspective does it center? What does it leave out? What would change if you rewrote it from a non-European vantage point? Targets R.7 and WH.6_12.1.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The "Dark Ages" concept is a lesson in how historical narratives are constructed — who tells the story, from whose vantage point, and what gets called "progress" or "decline." The same move (calling an era dark because your preferred civilization wasn’t dominant) gets made constantly in contemporary political arguments: the framing of immigration, the description of non-Western countries as "developing" or "underdeveloped," the assumption that Western liberal democracy is the endpoint of history. Green’s episode is training for a skill students will use for the rest of their lives: asking whose perspective a historical claim is told from before accepting it.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: The Crusades — Pilgrimage or Holy War?
docs.text: Episode: 15 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X0zudTQelzI Era: Era 2 — Middle Ages, Islamic Golden Age, and the Emergence of Empires WH Standard: WH.6_12.5 — Analyze causes and effects of major world events using primary and secondary sources ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze the development of informational and argumentative elements in nonfiction text; evaluate how argumentative reasoning supports or undermines the author's purpose

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green takes on the Crusades by asking a deceptively simple question: were they a pilgrimage, a holy war, or something more complicated? He argues the Crusades were neither a purely religious movement nor a simple military campaign, but a collision of motivations — papal politics, feudal economics, religious zeal, and naked opportunism — that produced consequences no one intended. The episode is particularly strong on perspective: what the Crusades meant to European Christians, to Muslims defending their cities, and to Jewish communities caught between both armies. Green challenges the assumption that the Crusades were simply Christians versus Muslims, showing how the lines were constantly shifting, with alliances cutting across religious lines. His central argument: the Crusades mattered less because of who won — nobody, really — than because of the contact and exchange they forced between civilizations that had been developing separately.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Pope Urban II's 1095 call at Clermont: the political logic behind a religious appeal
  • The three main explanations for why Crusaders went: salvation, adventure, land; Green's argument that all three operated simultaneously
  • The First Crusade (1096–1099): the People's Crusade's failure and the noblemen's unlikely success; fall of Jerusalem in 1099
  • Crusader States: what Westerners actually built in the Middle East and how they governed
  • Saladin and the Muslim response: the reconquest of Jerusalem in 1187, and why Saladin became a hero in both Muslim and Christian traditions
  • The later Crusades: the Fourth Crusade's sack of Constantinople (1204) — Crusaders attacking a Christian city
  • Jewish communities in the Rhineland: the Crusaders' violence against European Jews before they ever reached the Holy Land
  • Legacy of exchange: mathematics, astronomy, preserved Greek texts, and goods flowing back to Europe via Crusade contact
  • Green's argument: the Crusades failed as military campaigns but succeeded as cultural diffusion events — and that's the more important story

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Why the Crusades are simultaneously more and less than people think
  • [1:30] — Pope Urban II's speech at Clermont: salvation, reconquest, political logic
  • [3:00] — The People's Crusade: what happens when enthusiasm outpaces organization
  • [4:30] — The First Crusade: the noblemen's route; siege of Jerusalem 1099
  • [6:00] — Crusader States: governing the Holy Land
  • [7:30] — Saladin: Muslim reconquest of Jerusalem (1187); why both sides admired him
  • [8:30] — The Fourth Crusade and the sack of Constantinople
  • [9:30] — Violence against Jews in the Rhineland: who bears the cost
  • [10:30] — What the Crusades actually exchanged: ideas, goods, texts
  • [12:00] — Conclusion: the unintended consequences are the real story

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track the multiple motivations argument Green makes:

Green argues that no single explanation accounts for why people went on Crusade. He names religious salvation, adventure, land, and papal politics. But he also shows who paid the price of those mixed motivations.

Annotation task — the motivation matrix: | Who | Why they went (or fought) | What they actually got | |-----|--------------------------|----------------------| | European nobles | | | | Poor Crusaders (People's Crusade) | | | | Jewish communities in Europe | | | | Muslims defending Jerusalem | | | | Pope Urban II | | |

After watching: In one sentence, answer this question — When people claim to be acting for religious reasons, how can we tell what else is motivating them? Find one piece of evidence from the episode that supports your answer.

This connects to WH.6_12.5 (analyzing causes and effects using multiple sources) and 9-10.R.9 (evaluating argumentative reasoning and the author's purpose).

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"Pope Urban II needed the Crusades. He was fighting for supremacy over the secular kings of Europe, and a holy war gave him something no king could claim: the authority to mobilize all of Christendom."

"The People's Crusade was a disaster. Thousands of poor Christians marched east with enthusiasm and no food. They got as far as Hungary before they started eating each other — metaphorically and, some sources suggest, literally."

"The Fourth Crusade never made it to the Holy Land. It sacked Constantinople instead — a Christian city. When your holy war ends with you attacking fellow Christians, it might be time to question the mission."

"What came back from the Crusades mattered more than what was conquered. Mathematics. Astronomy. Aristotle. Sugar. The contact was violent, but the exchange was real."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.5 — Causes and Effects (Era 2)
  • WH.6_12.3 — Medieval Political Systems (Era 2)

docs.title: Pairs With — Reading Library
docs.text:
• Magna Carta (1215) — Written just over a century after the First Crusade; Crusade debts and returning nobles contributed to the baronial pressure on King John that produced Magna Carta
  • Las Casas — A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies — Crusade-era logic of "holy war" against non-Christians reappears in Spanish justifications for conquest; connecting these texts reveals how religious authorization for violence evolves

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Motivation Autopsy: Students take one person from the Crusade narrative (a Pope, a poor knight, a Jewish merchant in the Rhineland) and write a first-person account of their Crusade experience — using evidence from the episode about what motivated people and what they actually experienced. Targets WH.6_12.5 (primary source perspective) and 9-10.R.9a (author's point of view and purpose).

  2. The Fourth Crusade Problem: Green's account of the Fourth Crusade sacking Constantinople raises a genuine ethical question: at what point does a movement betray its own stated principles? Students write a short argument (claim + 2 pieces of evidence from the episode) answering: was the Fourth Crusade still a "Crusade"? Builds W.4 (argument writing) and WH.6_12.5.

  3. What Actually Transferred? Students create an annotated list of the five most significant things Europe got from contact with the Islamic world during and after the Crusades — using Green's claims plus their own prior knowledge. They rank them by long-term impact, justify the ranking, and connect to WH.6_12.6 (past to present). Forces them to think about what "winning" and "losing" actually mean in history.

  4. Crusades → Conquest: After watching, students compare the justifications Green describes (Crusades) to the justifications in the Las Casas reading. What language do both use? What is similar, what is different? This bridges Era 2 to Era 3 and directly targets the "causes and effects" analysis in WH.6_12.5.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Crusades are still being invoked — by jihadist groups recruiting against Western military presence in Muslim-majority countries, by politicians who use the word "crusade" to describe military campaigns (intentionally or carelessly), and by historians trying to explain the deep roots of conflict between the Christian West and the Islamic world. Understanding what the Crusades actually were — complicated, violent, motivated by a mix of religious conviction, political calculation, and land hunger — is necessary for evaluating how they're used rhetorically today. The episode helps students see the difference between what happened and what various modern actors claim happened, which is one of the core skills of historical thinking.
