docs.title: CrashCourse World History: Wait For It… The Mongols!
docs.text: Episode: 17 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: Era 2 — Expanding Zones of Exchange and Encounter WH Standard: WH.6_12.5 — Analyze the causes and effects of significant events in world history ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze the development of informational and argumentative elements in nonfiction text

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green opens with a defense of the Mongols that immediately complicates any simple narrative. He covers Genghis Khan’s rise from nomadic Mongolian tribes to the largest contiguous land empire in history, the military innovations that made the Mongols virtually unstoppable (speed, adaptability, psychological terror), and the staggering death toll of the conquests (estimates range from 10-40 million people). But then Green pivots: the Pax Mongolica — the roughly century-long period of relative stability within Mongol territory — enabled the most extensive trade network Eurasia had yet seen. Goods, people, and ideas moved from China to Europe at a scale not seen since the Roman Empire. Green’s argument: the Mongols were simultaneously the most destructive and most connective force in medieval world history. He also covers the unintended consequence — the Mongol trade network helped carry the Bubonic Plague from Central Asia to Europe.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Genghis Khan (1162-1227): rise from fragmented Mongolian clans to unified empire
  • Military innovations: speed of cavalry, feigned retreat, psychological warfare, siege engineers from conquered peoples
  • Scale of destruction: Baghdad (1258) — the Abbasid Caliphate ended; estimated 800,000 killed
  • The Pax Mongolica: relative stability enabling trade across Eurasia 1250-1350 CE
  • The Silk Road under Mongol control: goods, merchants, and diplomats moving from China to Europe
  • Marco Polo and European discovery of the East via Mongol-secured trade routes
  • The Mongol post system (yam): communication infrastructure across the empire
  • The Black Death connection: Mongol trade routes as vectors for Bubonic Plague spread

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: The Mongol defense Green is about to make
  • [2:00] — Genghis Khan: origins and rise to power
  • [4:00] — Why the Mongols won: military innovation and adaptability
  • [6:00] — The destruction: Baghdad, Persia, Eastern Europe
  • [8:00] — The Pax Mongolica: what peace looks like after conquest
  • [10:00] — Trade, the Silk Road, and Marco Polo
  • [12:00] — The unintended consequence: the Plague

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: Green argues that the Mongols were both history’s greatest destroyers and its greatest connectors. Can both be true simultaneously? Write a response using specific evidence from the episode for each claim.

Use the double-entry format: | What the episode claims | Evidence it gives | Your assessment | |------------------------|-------------------|----------------| | | | |

This connects to WH.6_12.5: the Mongol conquests are among the most consequential events in Era 2 — immediate causes (destruction of the Abbasid Caliphate) leading to long-term effects (Pax Mongolica trade networks, plague diffusion, Eurasian cultural exchange).

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"The Mongols killed more people, as a percentage of world population, than any other event in human history until possibly World War II. And yet… and yet they also created the conditions for the most extensive trade network the world had ever seen."

"Genghis Khan didn’t conquer because he was evil. He conquered because it worked."

"The same roads that carried silk and spices from China to Europe also carried the rats that carried the fleas that carried the Plague. Connection has consequences."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.5 Causes & Effects — Era 2
  • WH.6_12.4 Social & Cultural — Era 2

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. The Destruction-Connection Paradox: Students write a structured argument: was the net effect of Mongol expansion positive or negative for world history? They must engage with BOTH the destruction and the Pax Mongolica. Targets WH.6_12.5 and W.4.

  2. Trade Network Consequences: Students trace one specific item (silk, spices, paper, gunpowder, plague) along the Pax Mongolica trade route and analyze: what changed because this thing moved? What would NOT have happened without Mongol control of the Silk Road? Targets WH.6_12.5 and IR.1-5.

  3. Genghis Khan: Leader Analysis: Students compare Genghis Khan to one other empire-builder (Alexander, Napoleon, Augustus) using the same criteria: military innovation, cultural impact, long-term legacy. What does "great" mean when measured in death tolls? Targets WH.6_12.5 and R.9.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Mongols raise the question that haunts every powerful empire: what is the relationship between destruction and connection? The globalization that shapes the contemporary world — supply chains, internet infrastructure, international trade law — also creates vectors for things nobody wants to spread: financial contagion, misinformation, pandemics. The 2020 COVID-19 pandemic spread along exactly the global trade and travel networks that had made the modern world economically interdependent. The Mongols demonstrate that the infrastructure of connection and the infrastructure of destruction are often the same infrastructure.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: The Renaissance: Was it a Thing?
docs.text: Episode: 22 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: Era 2 — Expanding Zones of Exchange and Encounter WH Standard: WH.6_12.6 — Connect past historical developments to present-day conditions and events ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze the development of informational and argumentative elements in nonfiction text

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green asks the question historians have quietly debated for decades: was the Renaissance a real historical phenomenon or a story that 15th-century Italian elites told about themselves? He examines the intellectual movement centered in Florence — humanism, the recovery of Greek and Roman texts, the emergence of perspective in art, the "Renaissance man" ideal — and then asks who actually had access to it. His answer: a small, wealthy, urban elite. Most people in 15th-century Europe were still medieval peasants whose lives changed very little. Green also traces how the Renaissance’s core claim — that classical antiquity was a golden age worth recovering — was itself a rhetorical construction. The Greek and Roman texts being "rediscovered" had been preserved and transmitted by Islamic scholars throughout the period Europeans were calling the Dark Ages. The Renaissance, in this reading, is partly a story Europeans told to claim ownership of an intellectual inheritance they had actually received through Arabic intermediaries.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Humanism: the philosophical shift placing human beings — not God — at the center of inquiry
  • Florence and the Medici family: merchant wealth funding artistic and intellectual patronage
  • Recovery of Greek and Roman texts — transmitted through Islamic scholars, not "rediscovered"
  • Perspective in painting (Brunelleschi) as a technological/mathematical as well as artistic innovation
  • The "Renaissance man" ideal: Petrarch, Alberti, Leonardo da Vinci as models
  • Printing press (Gutenberg, c. 1450) as a Renaissance accelerant — texts spread at scale
  • Who was excluded: most of Europe’s population experienced no Renaissance
  • The Scientific Revolution connection: Copernicus, Galileo building on humanist foundations

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Was the Renaissance actually a thing?
  • [2:00] — Florence, humanism, and the Medici
  • [4:00] — The "rediscovery" of Greek and Roman texts (and where they actually came from)
  • [6:00] — Perspective, art, and the new visual language
  • [8:00] — The Renaissance man and who got to be one
  • [10:00] — The printing press as accelerant
  • [12:00] — What actually changed — and for whom

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: Green questions whether the Renaissance was a "thing" at all, or a story told by elites. By the end of the episode, what is your verdict? Was the Renaissance a genuine historical turning point, an elite phenomenon, or something in between? Use at least two specific pieces of evidence from the episode.

Use the double-entry format: | What the episode claims | Evidence it gives | Your assessment | |------------------------|-------------------|----------------| | | | |

This connects to WH.6_12.6: the Renaissance is the bridge between Era 2 and Era 3 — connecting medieval intellectual traditions (preserved through Islamic scholarship) to the Scientific Revolution and the Reformation that reshapes the early modern world.

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"The Renaissance was real. But it was real for a very small number of people. For most of Europe, the 15th century looked a lot like the 14th century."

"The Greek texts that fueled the Renaissance weren’t sitting in a vault. They’d been translated, studied, and extended by Islamic scholars for 500 years. The Renaissance was, in part, Europe rediscovering what the Islamic world had preserved."

"Humanism didn’t replace religion. It redirected intellectual energy toward the human — which turns out to have enormous consequences for everything that comes after."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.6 Past to Present — Era 2
  • WH.6_12.1 Science & Tech — Era 3

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Who Owns the Renaissance?: Students research the transmission path of one specific Greek text from Athens → Islamic scholar → Italian humanist. Then they write: who should "get credit" for the Renaissance’s intellectual foundation? Targets WH.6_12.6 and IR.1-5.

  2. Elite vs. Broad Change: Students argue: does a historical movement "count" if only a small elite experiences it? They use the Renaissance as the test case but must also identify a modern parallel. Targets WH.6_12.6 and W.4.

  3. Renaissance to Scientific Revolution: Students trace one idea from humanist philosophy to a specific development in the Scientific Revolution (e.g., Galileo’s methods, Vesalius’s anatomy). How did the Renaissance create the conditions for empirical science? Targets WH.6_12.1 and R.9.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Renaissance is the origin story Western culture tells about itself — a "rebirth" of reason after a dark period, leading to science, democracy, and modernity. Green’s challenge to this narrative matters because the same self-flattering story gets told about the Enlightenment, about liberal democracy, about capitalism. Every era is tempted to call itself a "renaissance" and the era before it "dark." The skill of asking who tells a historical story, who benefits from the telling, and who gets left out is one of the most transferable skills history education produces.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: The Atlantic Slave Trade
docs.text: Episode: 23 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~14 min YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dnDLOSuMqiI Era: Era 2–3 — Early Modern Period WH Standard: WH.6_12.5 — Analyze causes and effects of major world events using primary and secondary sources Secondary Standard: WH.6_12.4 — Analyze the influence of social, cultural, and economic development on individuals ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze argumentative elements and evaluate how reasoning supports or undermines the author's purpose

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green opens by confronting the scale of the Atlantic slave trade directly — 12.5 million Africans transported across the Middle Passage over roughly 400 years — and spends the episode explaining how a system of this magnitude could exist and persist. His argument is not that the slave trade happened because of individual evil, but because of an economic system: sugar. The sugar economy of the Caribbean required intensive labor in conditions where workers died at catastrophic rates, which required a continuous replacement supply, which made the trade self-perpetuating and enormously profitable. Green is careful to discuss African agency in the trade — the role of African states and merchants who sold captives — while being clear that this complexity does not distribute blame equally or excuse the system. He ends by tracing the slave trade's demographic, economic, and political legacies in ways that reach directly to the present.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Scale: 12.5 million Africans transported across the Atlantic; estimated 1.8 million who died during the Middle Passage
  • Why sugar? The demand logic: European consumption of sugar exploded; sugar cultivation required intensive labor in brutal conditions; labor died faster than it could be trained
  • The Triangle Trade: manufactured goods from Europe → enslaved people from Africa → sugar, cotton, tobacco from Americas → back to Europe
  • The Middle Passage: conditions, mortality rates, resistance
  • African states' role: some African kingdoms sold captives; the economic incentives; how this complicates a simple narrative
  • Slave resistance: shipboard rebellions; maroon communities; slow resistance (work slowdowns, sabotage)
  • Where enslaved people went: the Caribbean and South America received far more enslaved people than North America (about 5% of the trade went to British North America)
  • Economic scale: the slave trade funded the Industrial Revolution; profits financed banks, insurers, and manufacturers in Britain
  • Abolition: the Haitian Revolution as the most decisive blow; British abolition in 1807 and 1833; American Civil War
  • Green's argument: the slave trade's legacy is not past — it structured the modern world economy

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: The scale and what it means to take it seriously
  • [1:30] — Why sugar? The plantation economy's labor logic
  • [3:00] — The Triangle Trade: three legs, three profits
  • [4:30] — The Middle Passage: conditions and mortality
  • [5:30] — African states and the slave trade: complexity without absolution
  • [7:00] — Where enslaved people went: Caribbean and South America vs. North America
  • [8:00] — Slave resistance: rebellion, maroon communities, everyday resistance
  • [9:30] — The economics: who got rich and how
  • [11:00] — Abolition: Haiti, Britain, the long fight
  • [12:30] — Conclusion: the world we live in was built on this

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track the system vs. individual argument:

Green consistently explains the slave trade as a system driven by economic logic, not primarily by individual evil. But he also talks about specific choices made by specific people.

Annotation task — the system tracker: | Economic force (the "system") | Human choice made within it | Who benefited? Who was harmed? | |-------------------------------|----------------------------|-------------------------------| | Sugar demand in Europe | | | | African states' participation | | | | Triangle Trade profits | | | | British merchants and insurers | | |

After watching: Write one sentence answering — When an unjust system benefits many people at once, what does it take to end it? Find one piece of evidence from this episode that speaks to how the slave trade was finally challenged.

This connects to WH.6_12.5 (causes and effects, primary source analysis) and 9-10.R.9 (evaluating argumentative reasoning across a complex topic).

docs.text:

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"12.5 million people were transported across the Atlantic Ocean as slaves. That's not a fact to be noted and moved on from. That's the foundation of the modern world economy."

"The slave trade wasn't driven by individual wickedness. It was driven by sugar. Europeans wanted it. Growing it killed workers. Workers had to be replaced. The logic was economic, and that made it harder to stop, not easier."

"Some African kingdoms participated in the slave trade. I want to be clear about that. But I also want to be clear that acknowledging African participation doesn't distribute blame equally. The system was created by European demand, enforced by European ships and guns, and its profits went to European investors."

"The Haitian Revolution was the most important event in the abolition of slavery. Not the speeches of abolitionists. A successful slave rebellion. The enslaved people of Haiti ended slavery there by winning."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.5 — Causes and Effects (Era 3)
  • WH.6_12.4 — Revolution, Social & Economic Development (Era 3)

docs.title: Pairs With — Reading Library
docs.text:
• Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass (1845) — Douglass's autobiography puts a first-person account against Green's structural analysis; together they show the system and the person caught inside it
  • Bartolomé de las Casas — A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies (1542) — Las Casas witnessed earlier forced labor systems in the Americas; students can trace how extractive labor systems evolved

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Douglass + Green: Students read a passage from the Douglass narrative (reading library), then write a paragraph that uses both the episode and the text: what does Green explain that Douglass can't? What does Douglass convey that Green can't? Builds R.9 (comparing argumentative and narrative texts) and WH.6_12.5.

  2. The Economic Logic: Green argues that the slave trade persisted because it was profitable. Students work through the economic chain: sugar demand → plantation labor → slave trade → Triangle Trade profits → European industrialization. They must answer: if the economic logic drove the system, what kind of force could actually stop it? Forces W.4 (argument writing) and systems thinking.

  3. Scale Exercise: 12.5 million people. Students put this in context: How many people live in your city? In your state? How long would it take to count to 12.5 million? This is a numeracy + empathy exercise that precedes deeper analysis — students can't engage with the history if the numbers remain abstract.

  4. Who Ended It? Green says the Haitian Revolution was the most decisive blow against Atlantic slavery. Students assess this claim using evidence from the episode and the Haitian Revolution episode (ep-31). Was abolition achieved primarily by moral argument, by political pressure, or by the enslaved themselves? Builds W.4 and WH.6_12.6 (past to present).

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The transatlantic slave trade is the direct ancestor of the racial wealth gap that shapes American life today. The wealth built on enslaved labor — in cotton, tobacco, sugar, and the financial systems that insured and financed slave trading — capitalized the Industrial Revolution. The legal and social structures built to maintain slavery didn't disappear when it ended; they were reconstructed in new forms (Black Codes, Jim Crow, redlining, mass incarceration). Understanding the scale, mechanics, and economic logic of the slave trade is necessary context for any serious engagement with debates about racial wealth gaps, reparations, housing segregation, and the persistence of racial disparities in health, education, and the criminal justice system. The numbers in this episode are not historical curiosities. They are the foundation of the present.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: The Columbian Exchange
docs.text: Episode: 24 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~11 min YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AT9h9rx8OZk Era: Era 2–3 — Age of Exploration and Early Globalization WH Standard: WH.6_12.4 — Analyze the influence of social, cultural, and economic development on individuals Secondary Standard: WH.6_12.5 — Analyze causes and effects of major world events ELA Connection: R.4 — Determine main idea(s) as they develop over the course of a text and support with textual evidence

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green argues that the Columbian Exchange — the biological transfer of plants, animals, and diseases between the Old World and New World after 1492 — was the single most transformative event in world history, and that most people massively underestimate it. The episode leads with disease: 90% of the indigenous population of the Americas may have died within a century of contact, not from conquest but from epidemic illness against which they had no immunity. Then Green pivots to food: the potato, corn, tomatoes, and cacao transformed European diets and economies; in return, horses, pigs, and wheat transformed the Americas. His argument is that the Columbian Exchange matters not because of what Columbus did, but because of what microscopic organisms and plants did when they crossed an ocean — often faster and with more lasting consequence than any army.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• The biological context of 1492: Old World and New World had been isolated for 10,000+ years, developing separate disease environments
  • Disease as the primary agent of depopulation: smallpox, measles, typhus arriving ahead of conquistadors; the 90% mortality estimate
  • Why indigenous peoples had no immunity and Europeans did: domesticated animals as disease reservoirs; zoonotic illness
  • Plants going east: potato (feeding Europe's poor, enabling population growth), corn, tomatoes, chocolate, tobacco, rubber
  • Animals going west: horses (transformed Plains cultures), pigs, cattle, sheep; ecological transformation
  • Plants going west: wheat, rice, sugar (with its plantation system and slave labor implications)
  • Alfred Crosby's term "Columbian Exchange" and why naming matters
  • The demographic collapse and its political consequence: depopulation made European colonization easier than conquest could have
  • Green's argument: the most important agents of the Columbian Exchange were not people but diseases and plants

docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Why 1492 matters more than Columbus
  • [1:30] — The isolation premise: two worlds developing separately for millennia
  • [2:30] — Disease transmission: the 90% figure and why it happened
  • [4:00] — Zoonotic disease logic: why Europeans had immunity
  • [5:00] — New World plants going east: potato, corn, tomatoes
  • [6:30] — The potato's role in European population growth
  • [7:30] — Old World going west: horses, pigs, wheat, sugar
  • [9:00] — Sugar, slavery, and the plantation system
  • [10:00] — Conclusion: the Exchange as the real story of 1492

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track the what actually caused what argument:

Green's main claim is that the most important consequences of 1492 were biological, not political or military. He presents a chain of cause and effect:

Annotation task — the chain: | Event/factor | Direct consequence | Long-term consequence | |-------------|-------------------|----------------------| | 10,000 years of isolation | | | | European contact with Americas (1492) | | | | Disease (smallpox, measles) | | | | Potato arrives in Europe | | | | Horses arrive in Americas | | | | Sugar + plantation system | | |

After watching: In one sentence, what is Green's main argument? Then: do you think the evidence he gives is sufficient to support it? Where is it strong? Where might it be incomplete?

This connects to WH.6_12.4 (economic and social development shaping individuals) and 9-10.R.4 (tracking how main ideas develop through a text).

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"Within a century of contact, 90% of the indigenous population of the Americas may have died. Not from conquest. From disease. The Spanish didn't have to conquer a civilization — they just had to survive long enough to claim the ruins."

"The potato changed everything in Europe. A peasant family could grow enough calories to survive on a single acre of potatoes. Population exploded. The Industrial Revolution had its labor force because the potato fed them."

"Columbus did not discover America. He connected two worlds that had been developing separately for ten thousand years. What happened next — the disease, the food, the animals, the plants — was bigger than anything any individual could have planned or intended."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.4 — Revolution, Social & Economic Development (Era 3)
  • WH.6_12.5 — Causes and Effects (Era 3)

docs.title: Pairs With — Reading Library
docs.text: • Bartolomé de las Casas — A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies (1542) — Las Casas witnessed the human cost of colonization; this episode gives the biological context for why the indigenous population was so devastated; reading both together forces students to see the same events from structural and personal perspectives

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. The Unintended Consequences Argument: Green claims no one "planned" the Columbian Exchange — it happened because of biological forces larger than any human decision. Students write a paragraph arguing for or against this claim: Was the suffering caused by the Columbian Exchange anyone's "fault"? They must use at least two pieces of evidence from the episode. Builds W.4 and forces engagement with WH.6_12.5.

  2. What's on Your Plate? Students inventory a meal they eat regularly and trace the origins of its ingredients — which came from the Old World? Which from the New? Then they write a short reflection: what does this meal reveal about the Columbian Exchange? This is a personal entry point into WH.6_12.4 (economic and cultural development affecting individuals).

  3. The 90% Figure: Green cites the 90% mortality estimate. Students examine: what kind of evidence would support this number? What kind of source could tell us this? They practice the WH.6_12.5 skill of evaluating primary and secondary sources by asking: how do historians know what they know about populations that left few written records?

  4. Las Casas + Green: Students read the Las Casas excerpt (reading library) alongside a recap of this episode. Assignment: Green explains what happened structurally (disease, biology). Las Casas tells you what it looked like on the ground. Write a paragraph that uses both sources to answer: What was lost in the Columbian Exchange that Green's framework doesn't fully capture? Builds R.9 (argumentative reasoning across texts).

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Columbian Exchange is the most consequential ecological event since the ice age, and it is still happening. The potato that came from the Americas to Europe fed the Industrial Revolution's workforce. The corn that came from the Americas now feeds more humans than any other crop on earth. The diseases that went the other way killed an estimated 90% of Indigenous populations in the Americas — the largest demographic collapse in human history. The contemporary version of this exchange is ongoing: invasive species spread along global trade routes, COVID-19 traveled the modern Columbian Exchange routes, and debates about GMOs and food security are debates about who controls the crops that came from this exchange. 1492 did not end — it is the beginning of the world most people reading this were born into.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: The Protestant Reformation
docs.text: Episode: 25 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: Era 2–3 — Transition from Middle Ages to Age of Revolutions WH Standard: WH.6_12.2 — Analyze the impact of major religions and ideologies on political, social, and cultural development Secondary Standard: WH.6_12.5 — Analyze causes and effects using primary and secondary sources ELA Connection: R.9 — Evaluate how argumentative reasoning supports or undermines the author's purpose

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green argues that the Protestant Reformation was not primarily a theological dispute but a political and economic revolution wearing religious clothing. Luther's 95 Theses created a crisis for the Catholic Church not because the arguments were new — critics of Church corruption had been making them for centuries — but because of a new technology: the printing press. The episode traces how Luther's ideas went viral, how the German princes used religion as cover for a power grab against Rome, and how the Reformation shattered European Christendom into competing factions that would fight each other for the next century and a half. Green's central provocation: the Reformation produced religious freedom almost entirely by accident, as an unintended consequence of political fragmentation — nobody planned for pluralism.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Pre-Reformation Church: indulgences, simony, clerical corruption; Johann Tetzel and the indulgence trade
  • Martin Luther's 95 Theses (1517): the specific complaints, why they resonated, and how printing spread them across Germany in weeks
  • Luther's core theological breaks: faith alone (sola fide), scripture alone (sola scriptura), the priesthood of all believers
  • The Diet of Worms (1521): Luther refusing to recant; "Here I stand"
  • German princes and why they backed Luther: seizure of Church lands, independence from Rome, political advantage
  • Zwingli and Calvin: Reformed Protestantism; predestination and the elect
  • The English Reformation: Henry VIII's divorce, the break with Rome, and how personal desire created national religion
  • The Wars of Religion (1562–1648): 80+ years of conflict between Protestant and Catholic powers
  • The Peace of Augsburg (1555) and Westphalia (1648): cuius regio, eius religio — whose region, his religion
  • Green's argument: religious freedom emerged from exhaustion, not from anyone believing in it

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Why the Reformation still shapes the world
  • [1:30] — The Church before Luther: indulgences, Tetzel, corruption
  • [3:00] — Luther's 95 Theses and the printing press
  • [4:30] — Luther's core theological arguments: sola fide, sola scriptura
  • [5:30] — Diet of Worms: confrontation with the Emperor
  • [6:30] — German princes seize the moment — and Church land
  • [7:30] — Zwingli, Calvin, and the Reformed tradition
  • [8:30] — The English Reformation: Henry VIII's divorce problem
  • [9:30] — Wars of Religion: what fragmentation actually looked like
  • [11:00] — Peace of Augsburg and Westphalia: tolerance by exhaustion
  • [12:00] — Conclusion: The Reformation's accidental legacy

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track the technology + power argument:

Green claims the Reformation happened when it did — not earlier, when critics of the Church existed — because of the printing press. He also claims it spread partly because powerful people (princes, kings) found it useful.

Annotation task — the "why now?" tracker: | Why couldn't earlier critics of the Church succeed? | What did the printing press change? | What did princes gain from supporting Luther? | |-----------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------| | | | |

After watching: Write one sentence answering — Can an idea change the world on its own, or does it always need political power behind it? Find one piece of evidence from this episode and one from any other source you know that addresses this question.

This connects to WH.6_12.2 (ideologies shaping political and cultural development) and 9-10.R.9 (evaluating argumentative reasoning).

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"Luther's ideas weren't new. Church critics had been making the same arguments for a hundred years. What was new was the printing press. Within two weeks of Luther posting his theses, they were all over Germany."

"The German princes didn't care that much about indulgences. They cared about land. The Church owned about a third of German territory. Backing Luther was the best land grab of the 16th century."

"Henry VIII didn't want to reform the Church. He wanted a divorce. The English Reformation happened because one man needed a son and a Pope said no."

"Religious tolerance wasn't the goal. It was the conclusion the survivors reached after 130 years of killing each other. Peace of Westphalia, 1648: you can believe what you want, as long as you stop shooting."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.2 — Religion and Ideology (Era 2)
  • WH.6_12.5 — Causes and Effects (Era 2)

docs.title: Pairs With — Reading Library
docs.text: • Martin Luther — 95 Theses (1517) — The primary source that started it; students reading the actual theses after watching Green's episode can identify which arguments Green emphasizes and which he omits

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. The Printing Press as Social Media: Students draw an analogy between the printing press's role in spreading Luther's ideas and a modern platform of their choice. They must identify: what made both capable of spreading ideas quickly? What made both dangerous? What couldn't be controlled? Targets WH.6_12.2 (ideologies) and WH.6_12.6 (past to present).

  2. Luther's 95 Theses — Close Read: Students read 5–6 of the actual theses (selected from the reading library entry) and annotate: What specific complaint is this? Who benefits if this practice stops? Is this primarily theological, economic, or political? Builds R.9a (author's point of view) and WH.6_12.5 (primary source analysis).

  3. The Accidental Reformer: Green argues no one planned for religious pluralism — it emerged from exhaustion. Students write a short argumentative paragraph: Was the Reformation's most important legacy intentional or accidental? They must use two pieces of evidence from the episode. Builds W.4 (argument) and analytical thinking about cause/effect.

  4. Reformation Scorecard: Students assess: did the Reformation achieve Luther's stated goals? They use evidence from the episode to score: (a) end to Church corruption, (b) return to scripture-only authority, (c) freedom of conscience. Then they answer: Who actually won the Reformation? Targets WH.6_12.5 and forces students to distinguish between intentions and outcomes.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Reformation's most important lesson for today is not theological — it's about what happens when a new communications technology (the printing press) combines with existing social divisions to produce change that no one fully anticipated or controlled. Luther didn't intend to start 130 years of religious war; he intended to reform the church. Social media is the second media revolution with the same structure: new technology, existing divisions, change faster than institutions can manage. Every debate about misinformation, platform authority, and who decides what counts as true online has the same basic shape as the debate Luther started. The episode also shows how quickly a local controversy can become a continental crisis when the infrastructure for rapid information spread suddenly exists.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: The Black Death and the Printing Press
docs.text: Episode: 28 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: Era 2 — Expanding Zones of Exchange and Encounter WH Standard: WH.6_12.4 — Analyze the influence of social, cultural, and economic factors on individuals and civilizations ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze the development of informational and argumentative elements in nonfiction text

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green covers two transformative forces that remade European society within a century of each other. The Black Death arrived in Europe via Mongol trade routes in 1347 and killed an estimated 30-60% of the European population within five years — the most catastrophic mortality event in recorded history. Green examines not just the death toll but the social consequences: labor scarcity gave surviving peasants bargaining power that hadn’t existed under feudalism; the plague's apparent randomness — striking the pious alongside the sinful — undermined the Church’s authority as an explanatory system; and the "dance of death" tradition in art signals a cultural obsession with mortality that reshaped how Europeans thought about individual life. The second transformation: Gutenberg’s printing press (c. 1450) didn’t just make books cheaper — it shifted who controlled information. Green connects both transformations to the conditions that made the Reformation possible.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Bubonic Plague origins: Yersinia pestis bacterium, rat-flea transmission, origins in Central Asia
  • Mongol trade route transmission: the Silk Road carrying plague from Central Asia to Crimea to Europe
  • Death toll: estimated 75-200 million globally, 30-60% of European population 1347-1353
  • Social consequences: labor scarcity, peasant bargaining power, feudal system strain
  • Cultural consequences: memento mori tradition, danse macabre, obsession with individual mortality
  • Church authority undermined: if prayer didn’t stop the plague, what was prayer for?
  • Gutenberg’s printing press (c. 1450): movable type enabling mass production of texts
  • Information control shift: from Church scriptoria to commercial printers serving a literate public

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Two things that remade Europe
  • [2:00] — The Plague arrives: 1347, transmission route
  • [4:00] — The death toll and what it felt like
  • [6:00] — Social consequences: labor, feudalism, the Church
  • [8:00] — Cultural consequences: art, death obsession, memento mori
  • [10:00] — The printing press: Gutenberg and movable type
  • [12:00] — What the press did to information control
  • [14:00] — Connecting both: conditions for the Reformation

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: Green connects the Black Death and the printing press as two forces that together made the Reformation possible. What is his argument for how each contributed? Do you think either alone would have been sufficient?

Use the double-entry format: | What the episode claims | Evidence it gives | Your assessment | |------------------------|-------------------|----------------| | | | |

This connects to WH.6_12.4: the Black Death is the defining example of how a catastrophic event reshapes social structure — by killing people unevenly, it redistributed power between peasants and nobility in ways that centuries of protest could not achieve.

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"In five years, the plague killed 30 to 60 percent of Europe’s population. That’s not a statistic. That’s everyone you know."

"The plague didn’t just kill people. It killed the certainty that God rewarded the righteous. If prayer stopped the plague, why were the monks dying too?"

"The printing press didn’t create the Reformation. But it made the Reformation impossible to stop. Ideas that used to take decades to spread across Europe now took months."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.4 Social & Cultural — Era 2
  • WH.6_12.6 Past to Present — Era 2

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Catastrophe as Catalyst: Students argue: did the Black Death ultimately make European society better or worse? They must engage with both the death toll and the social changes (labor power, Church authority) it triggered. Targets WH.6_12.4 and W.4.

  2. Information Control Then and Now: Students compare the printing press to one modern information technology (the internet, social media) using the same criteria: who controlled information before, who controls it after, what changed about power? Targets WH.6_12.4 and R.9.

  3. The Plague and the Church: Students examine primary sources from survivors of the Black Death and analyze: how did the plague change how ordinary people related to the Church? What does this reveal about the relationship between catastrophe and belief? Targets WH.6_12.4 + WH.6_12.2 and R.9.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The printing press argument Green makes — that a technology shifting who controls information reshapes political power — is the argument being made about the internet right now. The same disruption: institutions that controlled information (the Church, then newspapers, then broadcast television) lost the monopoly on narrative that made their authority possible. What fills the vacuum is unpredictable. In the 16th century, it was the Protestant Reformation — which produced both religious liberty and a century of religious war. The contemporary version of this disruption is still in progress.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: The Enlightenment: Crash Course European History #4
docs.text: Episode: 30 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: Era 3 — The Emergence of the First Global Age WH Standard: WH.6_12.2 — Analyze the role of belief systems, philosophies, and ideologies in shaping societies ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze the development of informational and argumentative elements in nonfiction text

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green covers the 18th-century Enlightenment as a philosophical revolution that asked whether human reason — rather than divine authority or tradition — could ground political and social institutions. He traces the major thinkers: Locke (natural rights, government by consent), Voltaire (religious toleration, anticlerical satire), Rousseau (the social contract, the "noble savage"), and Montesquieu (separation of powers). Green’s argument is that Enlightenment ideas didn’t just circulate as abstract philosophy — they provided the vocabulary for three revolutions: American, French, and Haitian. But he also asks the uncomfortable question: if Enlightenment thinkers argued for human equality grounded in reason, why did they simultaneously defend or ignore slavery? The answer reveals that "reason" in the Enlightenment was often understood as a characteristic of propertied, educated, white, European men — and its limits matter as much as its achievements.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Enlightenment premises: reason, empiricism, human progress, individual rights
  • John Locke: natural rights (life, liberty, property), government by consent, right of revolution
  • Voltaire: religious toleration, criticism of Church and monarchy, "Crush the Infamous Thing"
  • Rousseau: the social contract, general will, the "noble savage" and corruption of civilization
  • Montesquieu: separation of powers, checks and balances, The Spirit of the Laws
  • The Encyclopedia (Diderot & D’Alembert): systematizing Enlightenment knowledge
  • Enlightenment ideas fueling the American Revolution (1776), French Revolution (1789), Haitian Revolution (1804)
  • The Enlightenment’s limits: who counts as "rational"? The exclusion of women, enslaved people, colonized peoples

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: What is the Enlightenment?
  • [2:00] — Locke: natural rights and government by consent
  • [4:00] — Voltaire: satire, toleration, anticlerical politics
  • [6:00] — Rousseau: the social contract and its complications
  • [8:00] — Montesquieu: separating powers
  • [10:00] — The Enlightenment in action: three revolutions
  • [12:00] — The exclusions: who gets to be rational?

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: Green identifies a central contradiction in Enlightenment thought: its claims about universal human reason coexisted with the defense or acceptance of slavery. How did Enlightenment thinkers resolve (or avoid) this contradiction? What does their answer reveal about who they thought "human reason" applied to?

Use the double-entry format: | What the episode claims | Evidence it gives | Your assessment | |------------------------|-------------------|----------------| | | | |

This connects to WH.6_12.2: the Enlightenment is the defining example in Era 3 of an ideology reshaping political structures — natural rights philosophy doesn’t just describe governments, it provides the vocabulary for overthrowing them.

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"The Enlightenment said: reason, not God, is the basis of authority. That is a genuinely revolutionary idea. It means that any institution — any king, any church, any law — can be questioned. That’s terrifying if you’re in charge."

"Locke said governments derive their just powers from the consent of the governed. Jefferson copied that almost word for word. The American Revolution was, in large part, a pamphlet war between Enlightenment philosophers."

"The Enlightenment was the first major intellectual movement to argue for universal human rights. And also to leave out women. And enslaved people. And colonized peoples. Universal is a strong word."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.2 Religion & Ideology — Era 3
  • WH.6_12.3 Political Systems — Era 3

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Who Counts as Rational?: Students examine one Enlightenment thinker’s specific exclusion of a group (women, enslaved people, colonized peoples) and analyze: what argument did the thinker use to justify this? Is the argument internally consistent with Enlightenment premises? Targets WH.6_12.2 and R.9.

  2. Enlightenment to Revolution: Students trace one specific Enlightenment idea from philosopher to revolution (e.g., Locke’s consent → Declaration of Independence, or Rousseau’s general will → French Revolutionary rhetoric). How was the idea transformed in application? Targets WH.6_12.2 + WH.6_12.3 and IR.1-5.

  3. Is the Enlightenment Over?: Students research one contemporary political debate that uses Enlightenment language (natural rights, consent of the governed, free speech) and argue: is Enlightenment thinking still our operating framework, or have we moved past it? Targets WH.6_12.2 and W.4.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Every major political argument in the contemporary American landscape draws on Enlightenment vocabulary: natural rights, consent of the governed, separation of powers, freedom of speech and religion. The Constitution is an Enlightenment document. So is the Declaration of Independence. So is the Universal Declaration of Human Rights. When politicians argue about what the Founders intended, they are arguing about which parts of Enlightenment philosophy to apply and to whom. Green’s question — who counted as "rational" enough to have rights? — is the question underneath every debate about voting rights, immigration, criminal justice reform, and what equal citizenship actually means.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: Haitian Revolutions
docs.text: Episode: 31 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5A6lYHyYe6w Era: Era 3 — Age of Revolutions WH Standard: WH.6_12.3 — Analyze the effects of different political systems on people's lives Secondary Standard: WH.6_12.5 — Analyze causes and effects using primary and secondary sources ELA Connection: R.9 — Evaluate how argumentative reasoning supports or undermines the author's purpose

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green argues that the Haitian Revolution was the most radical of all the Atlantic revolutions — more radical than the American or French Revolutions — and that it has been systematically underweighted in world history. Where the American Revolution preserved slavery and the French Revolution produced a decade of elite-dominated chaos, the Haitian Revolution was carried out by enslaved people against their enslavers, produced the first Black republic in the world, and sent shock waves through every slave society in the Atlantic world. The episode traces the complex layering of Haitian society (white colonists, free people of color, and enslaved Africans all in conflict) and shows how Toussaint L'Ouverture navigated those divisions. Green's central argument: Haiti's revolution was treated as a threat by every surrounding slaveholding power precisely because it succeeded, and that deliberate marginalization explains why most students have never heard of it.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Saint-Domingue (colonial Haiti) as the most profitable colony in the world by the 1780s: sugar, coffee, the brutal plantation system
  • Three-tier social structure: grand blancs (white planters), affranchis (free people of color, some slaveholders themselves), enslaved Africans — each group with different grievances
  • The French Revolution's impact: liberty and equality claims resonating in the colony; Vincent Ogé leading the free people of color uprising (1790); its failure
  • The slave uprising of 1791: Bois Caïman ceremony; Boukman Dutty; the scale of the revolt
  • Toussaint L'Ouverture: formerly enslaved, military genius, strategic diplomat; navigating Spanish, British, and French pressure
  • The ideological contradiction: France abolished slavery in 1794 (partly to gain Saint-Domingue's allegiance) but Napoleon reinstated it
  • Napoleon's expedition (1802): capturing Toussaint by deception; yellow fever defeating the French army
  • Jean-Jacques Dessalines declaring Haitian independence: January 1, 1804
  • Haiti's isolation: France demanding reparations (the "independence debt"); U.S. refusing recognition until 1862
  • Green's argument: Haiti's marginalization in history is itself evidence of the revolution's threat to the Atlantic order

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Why you've probably never heard enough about Haiti
  • [1:30] — Saint-Domingue: the most profitable colony in the world
  • [2:30] — Three tiers: who had what to fight for
  • [3:30] — The French Revolution arrives; free people of color demand rights; Ogé's uprising
  • [5:00] — The slave revolt of 1791: Bois Caïman; Boukman
  • [6:30] — Toussaint L'Ouverture: rise, strategy, and ideology
  • [8:00] — France abolishes slavery (1794); Napoleon reinstates it
  • [9:00] — Napoleon's expedition; yellow fever; Toussaint's capture
  • [10:30] — Dessalines declares independence: January 1, 1804
  • [11:30] — The aftermath: isolation, reparations, and deliberate forgetting
  • [12:30] — Conclusion: why this is the most radical revolution

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track the who gets to claim "freedom" argument:

Green compares the Haitian Revolution to the American and French Revolutions and argues it was more radical. He also shows how it was treated differently by the outside world.

Annotation task — the revolution comparison: | | American Revolution | French Revolution | Haitian Revolution | |--|----|----|---| | Who led it? | | | | | What did they want? | | | | | Who was left out? | | | | | How did surrounding powers react? | | | | | What was the outcome for the most oppressed group? | | | |

After watching: Write one sentence answering — Why do some revolutions get celebrated in world history and others get forgotten? Find evidence from this episode to support your answer.

This connects to WH.6_12.3 (effects of political systems on people) and 9-10.R.9 (evaluating the author's argument and reasoning).

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"Saint-Domingue produced 40% of Europe's sugar and more than half of its coffee. It was the most profitable colony on earth. And it was built entirely on the backs of enslaved people who died so fast they had to be constantly replaced."

"Toussaint L'Ouverture was born into slavery and taught himself to read. He read Enlightenment texts — the same texts the French revolutionaries were reading. He took them more seriously than the French did."

"Napoleon sent his brother-in-law to reconquer Haiti. Yellow fever killed 80% of the French force. Nature accomplished what the Haitian army couldn't do alone."

"Haiti had to pay France reparations for its own freedom. The 'independence debt' — compensation to French slaveholders for their 'lost property' — took Haiti 122 years to pay off. The last payment was in 1947."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.3 — Political Systems (Era 3)
  • WH.6_12.5 — Causes and Effects (Era 3)

docs.title: Pairs With — Reading Library
docs.text: • Haitian Declaration of Independence (1804) — The primary source produced by the revolution itself; students can read the declaration after watching Green's episode to see what Dessalines and the revolutionaries chose to emphasize in their founding document

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. The Comparison Essay: After watching this and the French Revolution episode (ep-33), students write a paragraph arguing: which revolution was more radical? They must define "radical" first (a vocabulary exercise), then use evidence from at least one episode. Builds W.4 (argument) and WH.6_12.3 (political systems comparison).

  2. The Reparations Problem: Haiti paid France for 122 years. Students research: what was the total amount? In today's dollars, what would that be? Then they write a short response: was this just? They must make a claim and support it with evidence from the episode. Directly targets WH.6_12.6 (past to present) — Haiti's 20th century poverty is directly connected to this.

  3. Toussaint's Reading: Green notes that Toussaint read Enlightenment texts and took them seriously. Students select one Enlightenment idea (natural rights, popular sovereignty, etc.) and write a paragraph showing how Toussaint applied it — using evidence from the episode. Forces close reading and ideological analysis (WH.6_12.2 + WH.6_12.3).

  4. Why Forgotten? Green argues Haiti has been deliberately marginalized. Students generate a list of reasons (using evidence from the episode) why slaveholding nations didn't want this story told, then compare: what do we celebrate? What do we leave out? Connects to WH.6_12.6 and the historian's craft.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Haitian Revolution is the most radical revolution in modern history — the only successful revolution by enslaved people, who took the Enlightenment's claims about universal liberty seriously enough to apply them without exception. The world's response proved what the revolutionary powers actually meant: France demanded reparations for the "lost property" (enslaved people who had freed themselves), and Haiti paid that debt until 1947. The United States didn't recognize Haitian independence until 1862. Haiti's ongoing economic and political challenges cannot be understood without the two-century context of isolation, debt, and foreign intervention that followed its independence. The Haitian Revolution is also the most complete test of whether the ideals of the Enlightenment were ever meant to be universal — and the answer it produced is still relevant to every debate about who universal rights actually apply to.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: Tea, Taxes, and the American Revolution
docs.text: Episode: 32 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: Era 3 — The Emergence of the First Global Age WH Standard: WH.6_12.3 — Analyze the development and impact of democratic ideas and political revolutions ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze the development of informational and argumentative elements in nonfiction text

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green examines the American Revolution not as a heroic founding myth but as a specific argument about taxation, representation, and imperial authority. He walks through the sequence of British taxation acts following the Seven Years’ War (Stamp Act, Townshend Acts, Tea Act), the colonial response (Sons of Liberty, the Boston Massacre, the Tea Party), and the philosophical framework the colonists used: the Lockean argument that government derives authority from the consent of the governed and that taxation without representation is illegitimate. Green’s central challenge to the founding myth: the American Revolution was also a civil war. An estimated one-third of colonists were Loyalists. The "Founding Fathers" were mostly wealthy white landowners protecting their economic interests. And the revolution’s most radical claim — that all men are created equal — was written by a slaveholder who knew the contradiction and left it for future generations to resolve.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Seven Years’ War (1756-1763): Britain in debt, colonies expected to help pay
  • Taxation without representation: the colonial argument grounded in English legal tradition
  • Key legislation: Stamp Act (1765), Townshend Acts (1767), Intolerable Acts (1774)
  • Colonial response: boycotts, Sons of Liberty, Boston Massacre (1770), Boston Tea Party (1773)
  • Common Sense (Paine, 1776): making the case for independence to ordinary colonists
  • Declaration of Independence (1776): Lockean natural rights in political document form
  • The revolution as civil war: Loyalists (estimated one-third of colonists)
  • The contradiction: "all men are created equal" — who counts?

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: What kind of revolution was this?
  • [2:00] — The Seven Years’ War and British debt
  • [4:00] — Taxation without representation: the argument
  • [6:00] — The sequence: Stamp Act to Boston Tea Party
  • [8:00] — Paine’s Common Sense and the independence argument
  • [10:00] — The Declaration of Independence: Locke in political form
  • [12:00] — Who was on the other side: Loyalists and the civil war dimension
  • [14:00] — The contradiction at the heart of it all

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: Green calls the American Revolution "the most conservative revolution in history." What evidence does he give for this characterization? Do you agree with this framing? What would the American Revolution need to have done differently to be "radical" by Green’s definition?

Use the double-entry format: | What the episode claims | Evidence it gives | Your assessment | |------------------------|-------------------|----------------| | | | |

This connects to WH.6_12.3: the American Revolution is the case study for Enlightenment political theory becoming political practice — and for the gap between revolutionary ideals and the realities of who benefits.

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"The colonists’ argument was basically: we have the rights of Englishmen, and one of those rights is not being taxed without our consent. Which is a very English argument. This wasn’t a revolution against English political philosophy. It was a demand that it be applied."

"About one-third of colonists were Loyalists. The American Revolution was, in part, a civil war. We don’t talk about that much."

"Thomas Jefferson wrote ‘all men are created equal’ while owning 600 enslaved people. He knew the contradiction. He left it for us to figure out."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.3 Political Systems — Era 3
  • WH.6_12.6 Past to Present — Era 3

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Was it Revolutionary?: Students apply a definition of "revolution" to the American Revolution and argue: does it qualify? They must engage with both what changed (British rule ended, new constitutional system) and what didn’t change (slavery, property restrictions on voting, Indigenous land). Targets WH.6_12.3 and W.4.

  2. The Loyalist Perspective: Students read a primary source from a Loyalist and write a 1-page analysis: what was their argument? Is it coherent? Why did history mostly forget them? Targets WH.6_12.3 and R.9.

  3. "All Men Are Created Equal": Students trace the phrase through history: what did Jefferson mean? What did abolitionists say it meant? What did suffragettes say? What do contemporary activists say? How does meaning change when different groups claim the same language? Targets WH.6_12.3 + WH.6_12.6 and IR.1-5.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The American founding documents are the most cited and most contested texts in American political life. Every argument about rights — gun rights, abortion, free speech, voting rights, immigration — eventually reaches back to the Declaration or the Constitution. Understanding those documents as products of a specific historical moment, written by specific people with specific interests and specific contradictions, is the difference between civic literacy and civic mythology. Green’s "conservative revolution" argument is not cynicism about the founding — it is realism about what revolutions actually produce and what work remains to be done.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: The French Revolution
docs.text: Episode: 33 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lTTvKwCylFY Era: Era 3 — Age of Revolutions WH Standard: WH.6_12.3 — Analyze the effects of different political systems on people's lives ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze the development and interaction of informational and argumentative elements in nonfiction text; evaluate how argumentative reasoning supports or undermines the author's purpose

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green covers the French Revolution from its origins in fiscal crisis and Enlightenment ideology through the Reign of Terror and Napoleon's rise — arguing that it represents the moment when political power became permanently detached from inherited birth and attached instead to "the people," however violently that idea was contested. He treats the Revolution not as a single event but as a decade-long process that kept eating its own leaders. The episode is particularly strong on why the Revolution turned violent: not because revolutionaries were inherently bloodthirsty, but because the logic of popular sovereignty creates a problem — who speaks for "the people" when the people disagree?

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Causes: fiscal crisis from American Revolution debts, grain shortage, Estates system (First/Second/Third Estate), Enlightenment ideas about natural rights
  • The Three Estates and the tax problem: clergy (1%) and nobility (2%) exempted from taxation while the Third Estate (everyone else) bore the burden
  • The Tennis Court Oath (1789): the National Assembly refusing to disband
  • The storming of the Bastille (July 14, 1789) as symbol vs. as event — only 7 prisoners freed
  • The Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen (1789) — influenced by American Declaration, extended (in theory) to all men
  • Olympe de Gouges and the Declaration of the Rights of Woman (1791) — the revolution's limits
  • The execution of Louis XVI (1793) — regicide as political statement
  • The Reign of Terror under Robespierre: 17,000 official executions, 40,000 total deaths
  • Robespierre's logic: the Republic of Virtue required eliminating its enemies — the logic of revolutionary purity
  • Thermidorian Reaction: Robespierre's own execution; the Revolution devouring itself
  • Napoleon as heir and betrayer of revolutionary ideals
  • Green's argument: the French Revolution established the modern political vocabulary — left/right, citizen, nationalism — that still structures global politics

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Why the French Revolution matters more than you think
  • [1:00] — The Estates system and the tax crisis
  • [2:30] — Enlightenment ideology meets bread riots
  • [4:00] — The National Assembly and Tennis Court Oath
  • [5:00] — The Bastille: symbol vs. substance
  • [6:00] — The Declaration of the Rights of Man; Olympe de Gouges
  • [7:30] — The execution of the king; the logic of regicide
  • [8:30] — Robespierre and the Reign of Terror
  • [10:00] — The guillotine as democratic equalizer (and horror)
  • [11:00] — Thermidorian Reaction: Robespierre guillotined
  • [12:00] — Napoleon and the legacy question: continuity or betrayal?
  • [12:45] — Conclusion: The Revolution's vocabulary is still ours

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track the central tension of this episode:

The French Revolution was supposed to be about liberty, equality, and the rights of all people. But it produced the Reign of Terror — mass executions in the name of those same ideals.

Annotation task — the paradox tracker: | Revolutionary ideal stated | How it was violated or distorted | Who paid the price | |---------------------------|----------------------------------|-------------------| | Liberty | | | | Equality | | | | Popular sovereignty | | |

After watching: Write one sentence answering this question — Is it possible to create a free society by force? Then find one piece of evidence from the episode that either supports or complicates your answer.

This connects to WH.6_12.3 (analyzing effects of political systems on people) and 9-10.R.9c (evaluating whether argumentative reasoning supports the author's purpose — Green argues a specific thesis about the Revolution's legacy).

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"The French Revolution gave us the political vocabulary we still use today. Left and right, as political terms, come from where people sat in the National Assembly in 1789."

"Robespierre believed that the Republic of Virtue was only possible if its enemies — internal and external — were eliminated. The problem is that list of enemies kept growing."

"The guillotine was, in a perverse way, an egalitarian instrument. Before it, aristocrats were beheaded and commoners were hanged. The guillotine gave everyone the same death. Whether that's progress is another question."

"The French Revolution didn't end with liberty, equality, and fraternity. It ended with Napoleon. But it changed what political power meant. After 1789, you had to at least claim to rule in the name of the people."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.3 Political Systems — Era 3 — political power, revolution, and popular sovereignty
  • WH.6_12.2 Religion & Ideology — Era 3 — Enlightenment ideology driving the revolution
  • WH.6_12.5 Causes & Effects — Era 3 — fiscal crisis → revolution → Terror causal chain

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. The Purity Spiral: Students read a 1-paragraph secondary source description of Robespierre's logic (the Republic of Virtue required eliminating enemies) and connect it to Green's argument. Then they find one modern historical parallel — any case where a movement's internal purges destroyed the movement. This targets WH.6_12.6 (past to present) and 9-10.R.9 (evaluating argumentative reasoning).

  2. Olympe de Gouges Witness Account: Students write a first-person paragraph from de Gouges's perspective responding to the Declaration of the Rights of Man — using evidence from the episode about what the Revolution promised and who it excluded. Builds 9-10.R.9a (author's POV and purpose) while doing historical empathy work.

  3. Revolution Scorecard: Students create a simple rubric (Did this revolution achieve its stated goals? Partially? Not at all?) and score the French Revolution on liberty, equality, women's rights, and economic justice — using evidence from the episode. Then they compare it to the American Revolution (ep. 32) using the same rubric. Forces comparative historical analysis, WH.6_12.3.

  4. Left and Right Origins: The political vocabulary claim Green makes at the end (left/right seating in the National Assembly) is a strong "past to present" hook. Students research one current political debate framed as "left vs. right" and ask: does this framing come from 1789 thinking? What gets lost when we use this framework? Targets WH.6_12.6 and OEC skill strand.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The French Revolution produced a template that has repeated itself across two centuries: popular uprising → declaration of universal rights → radicalization and factional infighting → state terror justified as necessary → conservative reaction → strongman who "restores order" while keeping the revolution's rhetoric. You can trace that arc through the Russian Revolution, the Iranian Revolution, and many others. Green's observation that the French Revolution invented our political vocabulary — "left" and "right" come from where delegates sat in the National Assembly — is a reminder that we're still arguing in the categories the Revolution created. Every time someone invokes "liberty, equality, fraternity" to justify a crackdown, or asks who the revolution is actually for when it arrives, they're repeating 1789's central argument. If you want to see how the revolution's promises played out elsewhere, compare this episode to ep-31: The Haitian Revolution — the most radical test of whether those promises were universal.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: Napoleon Bonaparte and the Napoleonic Wars
docs.text: Episode: 34 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: Era 3 — The Emergence of the First Global Age WH Standard: WH.6_12.3 — Analyze the development and impact of democratic ideas and political revolutions ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze the development of informational and argumentative elements in nonfiction text

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green examines Napoleon as both the product and the destroyer of the French Revolution. Born in Corsica, Napoleon rose through the Revolutionary army to become First Consul and then Emperor — technically restoring the monarchical form he had risen to power fighting against. Green covers the military campaigns (from Egypt to Moscow), the Napoleonic Code that spread legal equality across Europe, and the contradiction at the center of Napoleon’s legacy: he carried Enlightenment ideals about law and citizenship into every country he conquered, while simultaneously making himself emperor and reinstating slavery in Haiti. Green’s argument: Napoleon is the first modern strongman — a leader who uses the language of popular sovereignty and national liberation while consolidating personal power. His defeat at Waterloo produced a century of relative European peace (the Concert of Europe) and simultaneously planted the seeds of nationalism that would explode in 1914.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Napoleon’s rise: Corsican origins, Revolutionary army, coup of 18 Brumaire (1799)
  • The Napoleonic Code (1804): legal equality, property rights, religious toleration across Europe
  • Military campaigns: Egyptian expedition, Italian campaigns, the Moscow disaster (1812)
  • The Continental System: economic war against Britain, its failure and consequences
  • The contradiction: Emperor who spread revolutionary ideals; restored slavery in Haiti (1802)
  • Nationalism as unintended consequence: occupied peoples developed national identity against Napoleon
  • The Hundred Days and Waterloo (1815)
  • The Concert of Europe: Vienna settlement attempting to restore pre-revolutionary order

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Napoleon as the first modern strongman
  • [2:00] — Rise: from Corsica to the coup of 1799
  • [4:00] — The Napoleonic Code: revolution in law
  • [6:00] — Military campaigns: Egypt, Italy, Prussia
  • [8:00] — The Moscow disaster and the beginning of the end
  • [10:00] — The contradiction: Emperor of the Revolution who enslaved Haiti
  • [12:00] — Waterloo and the Concert of Europe
  • [14:00] — Legacy: nationalism, legal reform, the strongman template

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: Green argues Napoleon is the "first modern strongman" — someone who used revolutionary language to consolidate personal power. What evidence does he give? What features of Napoleon’s rule fit this pattern, and are there features that don’t?

Use the double-entry format: | What the episode claims | Evidence it gives | Your assessment | |------------------------|-------------------|----------------| | | | |

This connects to WH.6_12.3: Napoleon’s career is the pivot point in Era 3 — the moment when revolutionary ideals (liberty, equality, popular sovereignty) met the reality of how power actually gets consolidated and exercised.

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"Napoleon didn’t restore the monarchy. He invented something new: a dictatorship in the name of the people. He is, in a very real sense, the template for every 20th-century strongman."

"The Napoleonic Code is still the basis of law in Louisiana. And Quebec. And most of continental Europe. Napoleon’s armies were defeated. His law codes were not."

"He spread revolutionary ideas with one hand and restored slavery in Haiti with the other. Napoleon believed in liberty for some people. Which is to say, he didn’t actually believe in liberty."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.3 Political Systems — Era 3
  • WH.6_12.5 Causes & Effects — Era 3

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. The Strongman Template: Students identify three specific features of Napoleon’s rule and argue: does this pattern recur in later history? They find one 20th-century leader who fits the template and one who doesn’t. Targets WH.6_12.3 and IR.1-5.

  2. Legal Legacy vs. Military Legacy: Students argue: should Napoleon be judged more by the Napoleonic Code (which is still in effect) or by his military campaigns (which caused approximately 3.5 million military deaths)? Targets WH.6_12.3 + WH.6_12.5 and W.4.

  3. Haiti and the Revolution: Students examine Napoleon’s decision to restore slavery in Haiti and trace its consequences: the Haitian Revolution, the Louisiana Purchase, Napoleon’s retreat from the Americas. How did his Haiti decision change the history of the Western Hemisphere? Targets WH.6_12.3 and R.9.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Napoleon’s legacy is directly visible in contemporary politics. The Napoleonic Code is the foundation of civil law in approximately 75 countries. The pattern he established — a charismatic leader claiming to represent the popular will while concentrating power in himself — is the template for 20th and 21st-century authoritarianism. Contemporary debates about democratic backsliding, executive overreach, and the fragility of republican institutions are essentially debates about Napoleonic political innovation. When political scientists warn that democracies die from within, Napoleon is the case study they are pointing at.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: The Industrial Revolution
docs.text: Episode: 35 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: Era 3 — The Emergence of the First Global Age WH Standard: WH.6_12.1 — Analyze the impact of scientific and technological developments on individuals and societies ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze the development of informational and argumentative elements in nonfiction text

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green asks why the Industrial Revolution started in Britain, in the late 18th century, and not somewhere else, earlier. His answer involves multiple converging factors: coal deposits close to the surface and navigable rivers to transport it; the Enclosure Acts that drove peasants off common land and into cities (creating a labor supply); colonial capital that funded early factories; patent law protecting inventors’ profits; and a culture that, unusually for the time, celebrated commercial wealth. He then echoes his Agricultural Revolution argument: the Industrial Revolution made Britain vastly wealthier in aggregate while making many individual workers’ lives worse. Early factory workers faced 14-hour days, child labor, and conditions that shortened lives. Surplus was extracted upward. This is the contradiction that Karl Marx was watching when he wrote the Communist Manifesto — a text that will not make sense without this episode.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Why Britain? Coal, geography, Enclosure Acts, colonial capital, patent law
  • The Enclosure Acts: removing peasants from common land → forced urbanization → factory labor supply
  • Textile industry first: the spinning jenny, the water frame, the power loom
  • James Watt’s steam engine (1769): converting coal energy to mechanical work at scale
  • Factory system replacing cottage industry: discipline, schedules, child labor
  • The aggregate/individual gap: national wealth increased while workers’ conditions worsened
  • Urbanization: Manchester, Birmingham, Leeds — cities growing faster than infrastructure
  • Setting up for Marx: the class structure industrialization creates

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Why Britain? Why then?
  • [2:00] — Coal, geography, and energy
  • [4:00] — The Enclosure Acts and labor supply
  • [6:00] — Textiles: the first factory industry
  • [8:00] — Watt’s steam engine and what it changed
  • [10:00] — Factory conditions: the lives of workers
  • [12:00] — The wealth gap: aggregate vs. individual
  • [14:00] — Setting up for Marx

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: Green argues that the Industrial Revolution, like the Agricultural Revolution, made society richer in aggregate while making many individuals’ lives worse. What evidence does he give for each side of this claim? Is this pattern — aggregate progress, individual cost — inevitable in major economic transitions?

Use the double-entry format: | What the episode claims | Evidence it gives | Your assessment | |------------------------|-------------------|----------------| | | | |

This connects to WH.6_12.1: the Industrial Revolution is the defining technological transformation of Era 3 — not just machines, but a reorganization of labor, time, energy, and social structure that remakes what societies are capable of producing.

docs.text:
