docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"The Industrial Revolution made Britain rich. It also made British workers miserable. These two facts are not unrelated."

"The Enclosure Acts took land that peasants had farmed for generations and gave it to private landlords. The peasants who lost their land moved to cities, where they worked in factories. This was called ‘progress.’"

"Karl Marx was watching all of this. And he was taking notes."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.1 Science & Tech — Era 3
  • WH.6_12.4 Social & Economic — Era 3

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. The Aggregate/Individual Gap: Students argue: when a technology increases national wealth while worsening conditions for many individuals, is it progress? They use the Industrial Revolution as evidence but must also identify a contemporary parallel. Targets WH.6_12.1 and W.4.

  2. Why Britain? Causal Analysis: Students rank Green’s five factors explaining why industrialization started in Britain (coal, Enclosure Acts, colonial capital, patent law, culture) and argue: which is most important? Which could have been replicated elsewhere? Targets WH.6_12.1 and IR.1-5.

  3. Child Labor Primary Sources: Students examine 2-3 primary sources from child factory workers in 19th-century Britain and write a structured response: what argument does the evidence make about the costs of industrialization? Who was bearing those costs? Targets WH.6_12.1 + R.9.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The factory system the Industrial Revolution created is still the operating model for most manufacturing. The questions it raised — who owns the surplus produced by workers, what conditions are acceptable, what obligations does an employer have to employees — are the questions behind every contemporary debate about minimum wage, labor unions, gig economy classification, and Amazon warehouse conditions. The specific argument that Green makes — that aggregate GDP growth can mask widespread individual decline — is the argument economists make about contemporary inequality. The Industrial Revolution didn’t end. It moved.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: Coal, Steam, and the Industrial Revolution
docs.text: Episode: 36 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: Era 3 — The Emergence of the First Global Age WH Standard: WH.6_12.1 — Analyze the impact of scientific and technological developments on individuals and societies ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze the development of informational and argumentative elements in nonfiction text

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: The second Industrial Revolution episode focuses on energy and infrastructure. Green examines coal extraction and the steam engine as the twin engines of industrialization, then traces how steam power reorganized not just production but time, space, and social life. Railroads compressed distance: a journey that took weeks took hours. Telegraphs compressed time: information moved at the speed of electrical signals rather than horse and rider. Green argues that these technologies didn’t just make existing things faster — they changed what was possible to think about organizing. They enabled the mass coordination of capital, labor, and resources that made the modern corporation possible. He also traces the dark side: coal mining mortality, the emerging labor movement, and the way industrial capitalism created the class structure that Marx named. This episode is the bridge from the Industrial Revolution to imperialism — industrial powers needed raw materials their home territories couldn’t supply.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Coal as the energy source of industrialization: extraction, transportation, conversion
  • Watt’s steam engine improvements: from pumping mines to powering factories
  • Railroads: compressing distance, enabling mass movement of goods and people
  • The telegraph: information moving faster than physical transport for the first time
  • The modern corporation: enabled by mass coordination across distance
  • Labor movement emergence: Luddites (1811), Chartists, early trade unions
  • Time discipline: factory schedules imposing clock-time on agricultural populations
  • Bridge to imperialism: industrial economies needing raw materials from colonies

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Energy and infrastructure as the second phase
  • [2:00] — Coal: extraction, danger, and density as energy
  • [4:00] — Steam engine applications: from mines to railroads
  • [6:00] — Railroads and the compression of space
  • [8:00] — The telegraph: information infrastructure
  • [10:00] — The modern corporation as organizational technology
  • [12:00] — Labor movement: Luddites, Chartists, unions
  • [14:00] — Bridge to imperialism: the resource problem

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: Green argues that railroads and telegraphs didn’t just make things faster — they changed what could be organized. What specific organizational capacities did these technologies enable? Can you think of a contemporary technology that changed what could be organized in the same way?

Use the double-entry format: | What the episode claims | Evidence it gives | Your assessment | |------------------------|-------------------|----------------| | | | |

This connects to WH.6_12.1: steam power, railroads, and telegraph infrastructure are the technological foundation of the modern world — and understanding how infrastructure enables social and political organization is the core skill this standard builds.

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"Coal is ancient sunlight. Millions of years of photosynthesis compressed into rock. When you burn coal, you’re releasing energy that was captured before there were dinosaurs. We are running the modern world on geological time."

"The railroad didn’t just move people faster. It made national markets possible. A factory in Manchester could sell to customers in Edinburgh in the same week. That had never been true before."

"The Luddites didn’t hate technology. They hated being replaced by machines whose profits went to owners while they starved. There’s a difference. We often forget that."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.1 Science & Tech — Era 3
  • WH.6_12.4 Social & Economic — Era 3

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Infrastructure as Power: Students argue: who controls the infrastructure controls the economy. They use railroads as the 19th-century example and identify a contemporary infrastructure analogy (internet backbone, cloud computing, port access). Targets WH.6_12.1 and W.4.

  2. The Luddites Were Right (or Wrong): Students research the Luddite movement and write a structured argument: was their response to industrial machinery rational? Do their concerns apply to contemporary automation? Targets WH.6_12.1 + R.9.

  3. Industrial to Imperial: Students trace the causal chain from industrial resource needs to imperial expansion: what specific raw materials did British industries need, where were they located, and what did Britain do to access them? Targets WH.6_12.1 + WH.6_12.5 and IR.1-5.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The infrastructure questions the Industrial Revolution raised are the questions of the contemporary technology economy: who owns the rails (or the fiber optic cables, or the satellite constellations), who gets access, and on what terms? Every debate about net neutrality, platform monopoly, and who controls digital infrastructure is downstream of the 19th-century debate about who owned the railroads. The Luddite question — when automation eliminates jobs, whose responsibility is the displaced worker? — is the question being asked about AI right now. Coal’s legacy is the most literal: the carbon dioxide from 200 years of burning ancient sunlight is still accumulating in the atmosphere.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: Imperialism: Crash Course World History
docs.text: Episode: 37 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~14 min YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=alJaltUmrGo Era: Era 3–4 — Age of Imperialism WH Standard: WH.6_12.5 — Analyze causes and effects of major world events using primary and secondary sources Secondary Standard: WH.6_12.4 — Analyze the influence of social, cultural, and economic development on individuals ELA Connection: R.9 — Evaluate how argumentative reasoning supports or undermines the author's purpose; analyze how an author uses evidence

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green examines European imperialism in the 19th century by asking: how did a handful of European nations come to control most of the world's surface by 1900, and what were the actual arguments made to justify it? He presents three types of imperialism apologists — the economic exploiters (colonies as markets and resource extraction), the "civilizing mission" advocates (Europeans genuinely believed they were doing good), and the plain Social Darwinists (conquest as proof of superior fitness). Then he presents the counterargument: what the colonized actually experienced. Green's central argument is that imperialism's most enduring damage was not economic extraction but cultural destruction — the dismantling of existing political structures, languages, and self-concepts in colonized populations, effects that outlasted formal colonialism by generations.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• The scale of European imperialism: by 1900, European powers controlled roughly 85% of the world's land surface
  • Industrial Revolution enabling imperialism: steam ships, repeating rifles, telegraphs, quinine
  • Economic arguments: markets for manufactured goods, raw materials (rubber, cotton, diamonds, oil), investment opportunities
  • The "civilizing mission" (mission civilisatrice): genuine belief among some Europeans that colonialism was a humanitarian project
  • Social Darwinism applied to nations: Herbert Spencer's "survival of the fittest" borrowed by empire advocates
  • Kipling's "White Man's Burden": the poem as primary source for colonial ideology
  • Specific case studies: the Berlin Conference (1884-85), the Scramble for Africa, the Congo under Leopold II
  • Indian resistance to British rule: the 1857 rebellion ("Sepoy Mutiny" vs. "Indian Rebellion" — naming as argument)
  • The economics of colonialism: who actually benefited; the drain theory (Indian economists arguing colonialism impoverished India)
  • Green's argument: the cultural and political damage outlasted economic extraction; colonialism's deepest effects were psychological and structural

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: The scale — 85% of the world's surface
  • [1:30] — Technology enabling conquest: rifles, quinine, steam ships
  • [2:30] — Three types of imperial apologists
  • [3:30] — Economic argument: markets and resources
  • [4:30] — The "civilizing mission": true belief or convenient cover?
  • [5:30] — Social Darwinism and Herbert Spencer
  • [6:30] — Kipling's "White Man's Burden" as ideology in verse
  • [7:30] — The Berlin Conference: Europe divides Africa
  • [9:00] — Leopold II's Congo: the worst case
  • [10:00] — Indian responses to British colonialism
  • [11:30] — The drain theory: was colonialism economically rational?
  • [12:30] — Conclusion: what outlasted the empire

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track the argument vs. counterargument structure:

Green presents the justifications imperialists gave for their actions, then challenges each one.

Annotation task — the argument tracker: | Imperial justification | Evidence Green gives FOR it | Evidence Green gives AGAINST it | |------------------------|----------------------------|---------------------------------| | Economic benefit | | | | "Civilizing mission" | | | | Social Darwinism | | |

After watching: Which justification do you think was most powerful — and most dishonest? Write one sentence explaining your choice, and find one piece of evidence from the episode to support it.

This connects to WH.6_12.5 (evaluating arguments from multiple perspectives) and 9-10.R.9c (evaluating whether argumentative reasoning supports the author's purpose).

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"By 1900, European powers controlled about 85% of the world's land surface. Let that number sink in. One continent decided it owned the rest of the planet."

"Some imperialists genuinely believed in the civilizing mission. I'm not saying they were lying. I'm saying that genuine belief in your own good intentions does not make your actions good."

"Kipling said it was a burden to rule over 'half-devil and half-child.' The colonized people Kipling described had universities, legal codes, and literature before Britain showed up. Kipling just didn't look."

"The Congo under Leopold II is the most extreme example of what colonialism looked like without the ideological cover. Hands cut off for not meeting rubber quotas. Between 1 and 10 million people dead. Leopold personally. This wasn't a side effect. It was the system."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.5 — Causes and Effects (Era 3)
  • WH.6_12.4 — Revolution, Social & Economic Development (Era 3)

docs.title: Pairs With — Reading Library
docs.text: • Rudyard Kipling — "The White Man's Burden" + H.T. Johnson — "The Black Man's Burden" (1899) — The two poems together show imperialism's ideology and its direct rebuttal; this episode provides the historical context for both poems

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Kipling + Green: Students read the Kipling poem (reading library) after watching this episode. Assignment: Identify three specific claims Kipling makes about empire. For each, write a sentence showing what evidence from the episode supports or refutes it. Builds R.9 (evaluating argumentative reasoning in primary sources) and WH.6_12.5.

  2. The Genuine Believer Problem: Green says some imperialists genuinely believed they were helping. Students discuss: does genuine belief in your own good intentions matter morally? They must use evidence from the episode (Leopold vs. "civilizing mission" advocates) to support their position. Forces engagement with the ethics of historical judgment.

  3. Drain Theory Economics: Green mentions the drain theory — Indian economists arguing colonialism impoverished India. Students work through a simple version: if Britain extracted resources and taxes from India for 200 years, what would the economic math look like? Then they argue: was colonialism economically beneficial to colonized people or only to colonizers? Builds WH.6_12.4 and WH.6_12.6.

  4. Naming as Argument: Green notes that what the British called the "Sepoy Mutiny" the Indians called the "First War of Independence." Students collect three other examples from the episode where naming reveals perspective (Berlin Conference, "civilizing mission," "White Man's Burden"). Then they write: what names in current events reveal whose perspective the name-giver holds? Targets WH.6_12.6 and critical vocabulary analysis.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Almost every major conflict in the Middle East, Africa, and South Asia unfolds inside borders drawn by European colonial powers at conferences where the people living there were not invited. The Berlin Conference of 1884–85 divided Africa into 50+ countries by drawing straight lines through ethnic, linguistic, and political communities — and those lines became the borders of independent states after decolonization. The wars those borders produce aren't "ancient tribal hatreds"; they're the predictable result of 19th-century administrative decisions made for European convenience. The "drain theory" — the argument that colonialism systematically transferred wealth from colonized to colonizing nations — is still being debated in economics and international development, with direct implications for arguments about debt relief, reparations, and foreign aid. And neo-imperialism arguments — that wealthy nations exert colonial-style control over poorer nations through debt, trade terms, and military presence rather than formal occupation — are central to contemporary international relations debates. For more on how imperialism's legacy shapes the texts in this course, see WH-03: Writing Against Empire.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: Archdukes, Cynicism, and World War I
docs.text: Episode: 38 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: Era 4 — Global War and Depression WH Standard: WH.6_12.5 — Analyze causes and effects of major world events using primary and secondary sources Secondary Standard: WH.6_12.2 — Analyze the impact of major ideologies on political and social development ELA Connection: R.9 — Evaluate how argumentative reasoning supports or undermines the author's purpose

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green uses WWI to make an argument about causation: how do historians decide what "caused" an event? He walks through the standard MAIN causes (Militarism, Alliances, Imperialism, Nationalism) while pushing back on the "one thing caused it" narrative. His central argument: WWI is the case study in unintended consequences — every major power entered the war expecting it to be short, manageable, and politically useful. None of them anticipated four years of industrial-scale slaughter. Green is particularly good on the human cost: trench warfare, chemical weapons, the futility of sending men across no man's land. He ends with the question the war forces: when political leaders make decisions that lead to millions of deaths, and no one wanted that outcome, who is responsible?

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• The assassination of Franz Ferdinand (June 28, 1914): the spark and why it was not sufficient cause on its own
  • MAIN causes: Militarism (arms race, Germany's military build-up), Alliances (Triple Alliance vs. Triple Entente), Imperialism (European competition for colonies), Nationalism (Serbian nationalism, Austro-Hungarian anxiety)
  • The mobilization logic: why each country's calculation made war feel safe — and why all of them were wrong
  • Schlieffen Plan: Germany's two-front war plan; why it required invading Belgium; how Belgium's neutrality brought Britain in
  • The Western Front: trench warfare; the stalemate; the logic of attrition
  • Technology of mass killing: machine guns, barbed wire, artillery, chemical weapons (chlorine, mustard gas)
  • The Battle of the Somme: 60,000 British casualties on the first day; 1 million total casualties
  • The home front: women in manufacturing, propaganda, civilian hardship; the war changing who does what work
  • Colonial troops: Africans, South Asians, and Australians fighting in a war over European ambitions
  • Green's argument: WWI destroyed the Enlightenment faith that reason and progress would prevent catastrophe

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: the assassination and the question of causation
  • [1:30] — MAIN causes: militarism, alliances, imperialism, nationalism
  • [3:00] — The mobilization calculation: why each country thought war would be short
  • [4:30] — The Schlieffen Plan and Belgium; Britain enters
  • [5:30] — The Western Front: trenches, stalemate, attrition
  • [6:30] — Technology: machine guns, gas, artillery
  • [7:30] — The Battle of the Somme
  • [8:30] — The home front: women, propaganda, industrial production
  • [9:30] — Colonial troops: the world pays for Europe's war
  • [10:30] — The Eastern Front: Russia's collapse; revolution
  • [11:30] — Conclusion: what WWI destroyed

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track the causation question Green is really asking:

Green presents the MAIN causes but then complicates them. He's interested in why historians argue about causes and what that argument means.

Annotation task — the cause chain: | Cause (MAIN) | How it contributed to WWI starting | Was this cause necessary? Could WWI have happened without it? | |-------------|-----------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------| | Militarism | | | | Alliances | | | | Imperialism | | | | Nationalism | | | | The assassination | | |

After watching: Write one sentence answering — If every country entered WWI expecting a short, manageable war, and instead got four years of catastrophe, what does that tell us about how political leaders make decisions? Find one piece of evidence from this episode to support your answer.

This connects to WH.6_12.5 (causes and effects analysis) and 9-10.R.9 (evaluating argumentative reasoning).

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"Historians don't agree on what caused World War I. This is not a failure. This is historians doing their job — recognizing that events with millions of causes don't reduce to one."

"Every major power entered World War I thinking it would be over by Christmas. The Germans had a timetable. The French had a plan. The British thought it would be a naval war. They were all catastrophically wrong."

"On the first day of the Battle of the Somme, the British suffered 60,000 casualties. 60,000. In one day. And then they did it again the next day."

"World War I destroyed the 19th century faith in progress. Reason, science, technology — all those things that were supposed to make the world better — they made the killing more efficient. And then what do you believe in?"

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.5 — Causes and Effects (Era 4)
  • WH.6_12.2 — Religion and Ideology (Era 4)

docs.title: Pairs With — Reading Library
docs.text: • Wilfred Owen — "Dulce et Decorum Est" (1917) — Owen's poem is written from the trench; this episode gives students the historical context to understand exactly what Owen witnessed; reading the poem after watching the episode forces the contrast between the "glorious war" narrative and the reality

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Owen + Green: Students read "Dulce et Decorum Est" (reading library) after watching this episode. Assignment: What does Green explain that Owen cannot (structural, political)? What does Owen convey that Green cannot (experience, moral weight)? Write a paragraph using both sources to answer: what is the truth of WWI? Builds R.9 (comparing texts) and WH.6_12.5.

  2. The Causation Debate: Is the assassination of Franz Ferdinand the "real" cause of WWI or just a trigger? Students take a position (claim), use two pieces of evidence from the episode to support it, and then identify the strongest counterargument. Builds W.4 (argument writing) and forces the cause/effect analysis central to WH.6_12.5.

  3. Technology and Killing: Green traces how industrial technology made WWI's killing different from previous wars. Students list the technologies he names and for each write: what was this designed for, and what did it actually do? Then: does technology change what war is? Builds WH.6_12.1 (science and technology) and WH.6_12.6 (past to present).

  4. Who Fought Europe's War? Green mentions colonial troops from Africa, South Asia, and Australia. Students research: approximately how many colonial troops fought in WWI? What did they fight for? What did they get in return? Then they argue: was it the same war for everyone who fought it? Builds WH.6_12.5 (perspectives) and WH.6_12.6.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The alliance system that turned a regional assassination into a world war is a structural problem that hasn't been solved — it's been rebuilt. NATO's Article 5 (an attack on one is an attack on all) is a direct descendant of the 1914 alliance logic: the commitment that makes the alliance credible is also the commitment that makes a small conflict capable of pulling everyone in. Nuclear deterrence adds another layer: the weapons are designed to make war too costly to start, but the same design makes any conflict that starts enormously dangerous to stop. Green's episode also raises the question of decision-making speed: in 1914, mobilization orders moved faster than diplomatic communication, and leaders found themselves locked into sequences they couldn't reverse. That gap between military momentum and political decision-making is, if anything, larger today — modern weapons can escalate a conflict in hours. And the question of who fights empires' wars — colonial troops, working-class conscripts, populations with no say in the decision — is still the question being asked about every war. See also WH-05: The Cost of War for how the consequences of WWI played out in literature and politics.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: Communists, Nationalists, and China's Revolutions
docs.text: Episode: 39 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: Era 4 — The Age of Global War and the Pursuit of Peace WH Standard: WH.6_12.3 — Analyze the development and impact of democratic ideas and political revolutions ELA Connection: R.9 — Analyze the development of informational and argumentative elements in nonfiction text

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green covers China’s turbulent transformation from imperial dynasty to communist state in one of the most consequential political stories of the 20th century. He begins with the fall of the Qing Dynasty (1912) — the last imperial dynasty, weakened by Western imperialism and internal rebellion — and the founding of the Republic of China under Sun Yat-sen. He traces the split between the Nationalist Party (Kuomintang, led by Chiang Kai-shek) and the Communist Party (led by Mao Zedong), their brief alliance against Japan during World War II, and the eventual Communist victory in 1949. Key moments: the Shanghai Massacre (1927), the Long March (1934-35), the Japanese invasion and the Nanjing Massacre (1937), and the People’s Republic founded on October 1, 1949. Green’s argument: China’s revolutions were about more than ideology — they were about who would modernize China, on whose terms, and at what cost to the Chinese people.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Fall of the Qing Dynasty (1912): Western pressure, Opium Wars legacy, internal rebellion (Taiping Rebellion)
  • Republic of China: Sun Yat-sen, Three Principles (nationalism, democracy, people’s livelihood)
  • The Communist Party of China: founded 1921, influenced by Russian Revolution
  • Nationalist-Communist split: Shanghai Massacre (1927), Chiang Kai-shek’s purge
  • The Long March (1934-35): 6,000-mile Communist retreat, Mao consolidating leadership
  • Japanese invasion (1937): Second Sino-Japanese War, Nanjing Massacre
  • Nationalist-Communist United Front against Japan (1937-1945)
  • Chinese Civil War outcome: Mao’s victory, People’s Republic of China (October 1, 1949)

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Why China’s revolutions matter
  • [2:00] — The fall of the Qing Dynasty (1912)
  • [4:00] — Sun Yat-sen and the Republic of China
  • [6:00] — The Communist Party and the Nationalist-Communist alliance
  • [8:00] — The Shanghai Massacre and the split
  • [10:00] — The Long March
  • [12:00] — Japanese invasion and the United Front
  • [14:00] — Civil war outcome: 1949 and the People’s Republic

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track this question in your notes: Green describes multiple competing visions for China in the early 20th century: Qing conservatism, Sun Yat-sen’s nationalism, Chiang Kai-shek’s Nationalist order, and Mao’s communism. What did each vision promise the Chinese people? Why, in Green’s telling, did Mao win?

Use the double-entry format: | What the episode claims | Evidence it gives | Your assessment | |------------------------|-------------------|----------------| | | | |

This connects to WH.6_12.3: China’s 20th-century revolutions are the Era 4 case study for how political ideology, military conflict, and foreign intervention combine to reshape state structure — and who gets to define what a "revolution" is for.

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"China had four major political crises in 50 years: the fall of the Qing, the warlord era, the Japanese invasion, and the civil war. That’s not instability. That’s a country trying to figure out what it is."

"The Long March is to Chinese Communism what Valley Forge is to American independence: the moment of suffering that proves the cause is worth dying for."

"Mao won in 1949 not primarily because of ideology. He won because his army was in the countryside and his party organized the peasants. Chiang Kai-shek’s Nationalists held the cities and forgot about everyone else."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.3 Political Systems — Era 4
  • WH.6_12.5 Causes & Effects — Era 4

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Why Did Mao Win?: Students write a structured causal argument: identify the three factors most responsible for the Communist Party’s victory in 1949 and rank them by importance. They must engage with both internal Chinese dynamics and external factors (Japan, Soviet support). Targets WH.6_12.3 and W.4.

  2. Competing Visions of Modernization: Students compare Sun Yat-sen’s Three Principles and Mao’s communist program: what did each promise Chinese peasants? What did each deliver? Targets WH.6_12.3 and R.9.

  3. The Long March as Mythology: Students examine how the Long March is depicted in Chinese Communist Party history and ask: what is the difference between a historical event and a founding myth? How do states use mythology? Targets WH.6_12.3 + IR.1-5.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
China is the world’s second-largest economy, the United States’ primary strategic competitor, and a country that most American students know almost nothing about in historical depth. The People’s Republic of China’s self-understanding — the Communist Party’s legitimacy, the "century of humiliation" framework, the meaning of Taiwan — all flow directly from the events of 1912-1949. Contemporary American foreign policy debates about China, trade, Taiwan, and the South China Sea cannot be understood without understanding why and how the Communist Party came to power. This episode is basic preparation for being an informed citizen of a country in strategic competition with China.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: World War II Part 1
docs.text:
Episode: 40 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~13 min YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q78COTwT7nE Era: Era 4 — Global War and Depression WH Standard: WH.6_12.5 — Analyze causes and effects of major world events using primary and secondary sources Secondary Standard: WH.6_12.2 — Analyze the impact of major ideologies on political and social development ELA Connection: R.9 — Evaluate how argumentative reasoning supports or undermines the author's purpose

Note: See also Episode 41 — WWII Part 2 which covers the European theater and Holocaust.

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green opens by rejecting the "Hitler was a madman" explanation for WWII, arguing that this framing lets everyone else off the hook — the German voters who elected him, the Western powers who appeased him, the economic system whose collapse created his opportunity. This episode focuses on origins and the Pacific theater: the rise of fascism in Germany, Italy, and Japan; the Great Depression's political consequences; the failure of the League of Nations; Japan's expansionism in Asia; and the attack on Pearl Harbor. Green's central argument: WWII was not a surprise. It was the predictable result of WWI's unresolved grievances, the Depression's political desperation, and democratic nations' unwillingness to stop what they could see coming.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• WWI's unresolved aftermath: the Treaty of Versailles's war guilt clause, reparations, territorial losses; German resentment as political fuel
  • The Great Depression (1929): how economic collapse destroyed democratic legitimacy and created space for authoritarian alternatives
  • Fascism: what it is (nationalism + authoritarianism + anti-communism + charismatic leader), why it appealed to people experiencing chaos
  • Hitler's rise: 1923 Beer Hall Putsch failure; 1933 election; enabling act; legal seizure of power
  • Mussolini's Italy: fascism's original model
  • Imperial Japan: expansion into Manchuria (1931) and China; the logic of resource scarcity driving militarism
  • The League of Nations' failure: why it couldn't stop Japan or Germany; U.S. never joining; lack of enforcement mechanism
  • Appeasement: Munich Agreement (1938); Neville Chamberlain; the logic and the failure
  • The Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact (1939): Nazi-Soviet non-aggression; why both sides signed it
  • Germany's blitzkrieg: fall of France in six weeks; what shocked everyone
  • Pearl Harbor (December 7, 1941) and U.S. entry

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: Why "Hitler was a madman" is the wrong explanation
  • [1:30] — WWI's aftermath: Versailles, reparations, German resentment
  • [2:30] — The Great Depression and democratic collapse
  • [3:30] — Fascism: ideology, appeal, and the Italian model
  • [4:30] — Hitler's rise: the Beer Hall Putsch to the enabling act
  • [5:30] — Japan's expansion: Manchuria, China, and the resource logic
  • [6:30] — The League of Nations and why appeasement made sense (and failed)
  • [7:30] — Munich and the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact
  • [8:30] — Blitzkrieg: the fall of France
  • [10:00] — The Battle of Britain and Germany's limits
  • [11:00] — Pearl Harbor and U.S. entry
  • [12:00] — Conclusion: a preventable war

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track the structural causes vs. individual blame argument:

Green argues that blaming Hitler as an individual misses the structural conditions that made him possible. But he also describes specific choices made by specific people (German voters, Chamberlain, Western powers).

Annotation task — the conditions tracker: | Structural condition | How it contributed | Who made a choice within it, and what? | |---------------------|-------------------|---------------------------------------| | WWI's unresolved grievances | | | | Great Depression | | | | Fascism's appeal | | | | League of Nations weakness | | | | Western appeasement | | |

After watching: Write one sentence answering — When a catastrophe unfolds slowly enough that people can see it coming, what prevents them from stopping it? Use one piece of evidence from this episode.

This connects to WH.6_12.5 (causes and effects) and 9-10.R.9 (evaluating argumentative reasoning).

docs.title: Full Transcript
docs.text:
Transcript pending. This entry is a stub.

python3 ../vtt_to_markdown.py 40-wwii-1.en.vtt \
  -o ep-40-wwii-1-transcript.md
Then paste the output into this section.

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"Calling Hitler a madman is comforting. It means this was an aberration, a freak of history. But Hitler was elected. He was supported by millions of ordinary Germans who thought he would fix their economy and restore their pride. That's not madness. That's politics."

"The Great Depression didn't just make people poor. It made democracy look like it couldn't deliver. When democracy fails to feed people, they start looking for alternatives."

"Appeasement gets a bad name now, but at the time it seemed rational. Britain couldn't fight another war like WWI. Giving Hitler the Sudetenland seemed like a small price for peace. Chamberlain wasn't stupid. He was wrong."

"Japan's problem was resources. It had none. It wanted a Pacific empire to supply its industry. The logic was brutal but coherent. The Americans cut off their oil. Japan attacked Pearl Harbor. You can trace the causation."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.5 — Causes and Effects (Era 4)
  • WH.6_12.2 — Religion and Ideology (Era 4)

docs.title: Pairs With — Reading Library
docs.text:
• Night by Elie Wiesel — This episode covers the political and ideological origins of WWII; Wiesel's memoir shows what those origins produced for individuals; used together across episodes 40 and 41
  • FDR — The Four Freedoms Speech (1941) — FDR's articulation of what the Allies were fighting for; delivered as America was entering the war this episode describes

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. The Conditions Argument: Green says Hitler's rise required specific conditions (depression, Versailles, democratic failure). Students write a paragraph arguing: are these conditions unique to Germany in 1933, or could they occur elsewhere? They must use at least two pieces of evidence from the episode. Builds W.4 and WH.6_12.6 (past to present).

  2. Appeasement Simulation: Students receive a simplified briefing on Munich 1938 (Britain's options: fight now, appease, sanction). They make a decision as Chamberlain, then assess: given what you knew in 1938 (not what we know now), was appeasement rational? After the debrief, they discuss why knowing the outcome changes our judgment of decisions. Targets WH.6_12.5.

  3. Fascism's Appeal: Green argues fascism appealed to people experiencing chaos. Students identify three specific things fascism offered (security, national pride, a scapegoat) using evidence from the episode. Then: are any of these appeals still visible in contemporary politics? Builds WH.6_12.2 and WH.6_12.6 — and requires the kind of careful, evidence-based claim-making that guards against both under-claiming and over-claiming.

  4. Night + Episode 40: Students read the opening chapter of Night (reading library) after watching this episode. Assignment: Green describes the political conditions that made the Holocaust possible. Wiesel describes a family that didn't believe it could happen to them. Write a paragraph explaining: what does Wiesel's experience reveal about how ordinary people experience political extremism from the inside? Builds R.9 and WH.6_12.5.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The conditions Green describes for fascism's rise — economic crisis, national humiliation, weak democratic institutions, a scapegoated minority, a charismatic leader who promises to restore national greatness — aren't unique to 1930s Europe. Political scientists use precisely these factors as indicators of "democratic backsliding," and they track them in contemporary democracies worldwide. The question this episode forces is not "could that happen again?" (it can and does) but "how does it happen from the inside?" — because Wiesel's account in Night shows that ordinary people experiencing totalitarianism don't experience it as a sudden catastrophe. They experience it as a series of steps, each of which seemed manageable until it wasn't. Green's episode is best understood as the political and structural context for Wiesel's personal account — watch them together if you can. For the institutional and normative question ("what holds democracy together?"), see USH-06: When the Rules Don't Apply.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: World War II Part 2 — The War in Europe
docs.text:
Episode: 41 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~14 min YouTube: Watch on YouTube Era: Era 4 — Global War and Depression WH Standard: WH.6_12.5 — Analyze causes and effects of major world events using primary and secondary sources Secondary Standard: WH.6_12.2 — Analyze the impact of major ideologies on political and social development ELA Connection: R.9 — Evaluate how argumentative reasoning supports or undermines the author's purpose

Note: See also Episode 40 — WWII Part 1 which covers the origins and Pacific theater.

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green focuses this episode on the European theater: Operation Barbarossa (Germany's invasion of the Soviet Union), the Holocaust, D-Day, the collapse of Nazi Germany, and the moral questions that the war's conduct forces. He argues that understanding WWII means taking the Holocaust seriously as the ideological endpoint of Nazi ideology — not a side effect of war, but its deliberate program. Green is honest about the difficulty: Allied powers knew about the Holocaust earlier than they acted; the Soviet Union was a partner in defeating Nazism while simultaneously running its own terror state. The war raises the question of whether there is a version of this where the "good guys" are uncomplicated — and Green's answer is no. He ends with the war's immediate consequences: 70–85 million dead, the United Nations, the Cold War beginning, and the decolonization movements the war accelerated.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Operation Barbarossa (June 1941): the largest military invasion in history; Germany attacking the Soviet Union; why Hitler violated the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact
  • The Eastern Front: casualty scale — an estimated 30 million Soviet dead; the Battle of Stalingrad as turning point
  • The Holocaust: the ideological origins (Nazi racial ideology, Social Darwinism, antisemitism); the progression from discrimination to genocide
  • Nuremberg Laws (1935) → Kristallnacht (1938) → ghettos → Einsatzgruppen mobile killing units → death camps (Auschwitz, Treblinka, Sobibor)
  • Six million Jewish dead; Romani, disabled, LGBTQ+ communities, political prisoners also targeted
  • Allied response: when did they know? What did they do? The bombing of railways debate
  • D-Day (June 6, 1944): Operation Overlord; the scale; why it succeeded
  • The fall of Berlin (May 1945): Hitler's death; German surrender
  • The atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki (August 1945): the debate over justification
  • Immediate aftermath: 70–85 million dead; the Nuremberg Trials; the United Nations; the Universal Declaration of Human Rights
  • Decolonization accelerated: colonial subjects who fought for empires now demanding independence
  • The Cold War beginning: Soviet and American spheres emerging from the Allied victory

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: the moral complexity of a "good war"
  • [1:30] — Operation Barbarossa: the Eastern Front's scale
  • [2:30] — The Battle of Stalingrad: turning point
  • [3:30] — The Holocaust: ideology, escalation, death camps
  • [5:30] — What the Allies knew and when
  • [6:30] — D-Day: Operation Overlord
  • [8:00] — The fall of Berlin; German surrender
  • [8:30] — The Pacific: island hopping; atomic bombs
  • [10:00] — The debate over Hiroshima and Nagasaki
  • [11:00] — Aftermath: 70–85 million dead; the UN; Nuremberg
  • [12:00] — Decolonization and the Cold War beginning
  • [13:00] — Conclusion: is there a "good war"?

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track the moral complexity Green is building:

Green argues that WWII is often called "the good war" but resists making it simple. He shows how the "good guys" were also complicated.

Annotation task — the complexity tracker: | "Good war" claim | Complication Green raises | |-----------------|--------------------------| | The Allies fought Hitler | | | Allies liberated Europe | | | The Holocaust had to be stopped | | | The atomic bombs ended the war | | | The UN was created to prevent future wars | |

After watching: Write one sentence answering — Is it possible for a war to be both necessary and morally wrong? Use one piece of evidence from this episode. This is genuinely hard — do not look for an easy answer.

This connects to WH.6_12.5 (historical analysis of causes and effects) and 9-10.R.9 (evaluating argumentative complexity).

docs.text:

  -o ep-41-wwii-2-transcript.md
Then paste the output into this section.

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"The Holocaust was not a side effect of the war. It was the ideological goal. The Nazis believed they were solving what they called the 'Jewish question.' The war gave them the cover and the infrastructure to attempt it."

"The Eastern Front killed more people than any other campaign in any war in history. 30 million Soviet dead. Not Americans. Not British. Soviets. When we remember WWII, we often forget who bore the most cost."

"The Allies knew about the death camps earlier than they acted. This is uncomfortable. It doesn't change who was right and who was wrong. But it complicates the story of the good war."

"The atomic bomb ended the war faster. It also killed 140,000 people in Hiroshima and 70,000 in Nagasaki — mostly civilians. Both things are true. The question of whether it was justified is not resolved by either fact alone."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.5 — Causes and Effects (Era 4)
  • WH.6_12.2 — Religion and Ideology (Era 4)

docs.title: Pairs With — Reading Library
docs.text:
• Night by Elie Wiesel — Wiesel's memoir is the first-person account of what this episode describes structurally; students who read Night after watching this episode understand the institutional machinery that produced Wiesel's experience
  • FDR — The Four Freedoms Speech (1941) — FDR's articulation of what the Allies said they were fighting for; after watching this episode, students can evaluate whether the Four Freedoms were actually achieved by the war's end

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Night + Episode 41: Students read a passage from Night (reading library) and then write a paragraph that uses both sources: What does the episode explain (institutional, political, ideological)? What does Wiesel convey (experiential, emotional, moral)? What can you only understand by reading both? Builds R.9 (comparing argumentative and narrative texts) and WH.6_12.5.

  2. The Hiroshima Debate: Green presents the atomic bomb as morally contested. Students take a position (was the bombing of Hiroshima justified?), use two pieces of evidence from the episode to support it, and identify the strongest counterargument. The goal is not to "win" but to hold two true things in tension. Builds W.4 and WH.6_12.5.

  3. FDR's Four Freedoms — Did the War Deliver? Students read FDR's speech (reading library) and use evidence from this episode to assess: at war's end, were the Four Freedoms more or less achieved? For whom? They must consider colonial subjects, Holocaust survivors, Soviet citizens, and American veterans separately. Builds R.9 and WH.6_12.6.

  4. The "Good War" Test: Green resists calling WWII uncomplicated. Students create a rubric: what would make a war "good"? (Just cause, proportional means, non-combatant protection, achieves stated goals?) Then they score WWII on each dimension — using evidence from both WWII episodes. Builds analytical thinking and WH.6_12.5.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The postwar international order Green describes — the United Nations, NATO, the Bretton Woods institutions, the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, nuclear deterrence — is the order we still live in, and it is under more stress now than at any point since its creation. The UN was designed to prevent great-power war by giving great powers a veto; that same veto now prevents the UN from responding to great-power aggression. NATO was designed around the assumption that the U.S. would lead it; that assumption is now contested. The nuclear deterrence logic — Mutually Assured Destruction as stability — requires both sides to believe the other will actually use the weapons, which means the stability depends on maintaining credible threats of civilizational destruction. The Universal Declaration of Human Rights was adopted in 1948 as the world's answer to what had just happened — and every subsequent debate about genocide, refugee rights, and international law tests whether that answer was sufficient. The postwar order isn't background history; it's the architecture of the world students are inheriting.

docs.title: CrashCourse World History: USA vs USSR Fight! The Cold War
docs.text: Episode: 42 · Playlist: World History · Runtime: ~14 min YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=y9HjvHZfCUI Era: Era 4 — Global War and Depression (into Contemporary) WH Standard: WH.6_12.2 — Analyze the impact of major religions and ideologies on political, social, and cultural development Secondary Standard: WH.6_12.6 — Connect historical events and developments to current situations ELA Connection: R.9 — Evaluate how argumentative reasoning supports or undermines the author's purpose

docs.title: What This Episode Is About
docs.text: Green argues that the Cold War is misunderstood as a military confrontation when it was primarily an ideological one — a competition between two competing claims about how human society should be organized, with proxy wars, propaganda, and nuclear brinkmanship replacing direct combat. He traces the Cold War from its origins in the WWII alliance's mutual suspicion through to the late 1980s, covering the major flashpoints (Berlin blockade, Korean War, Cuban Missile Crisis, Vietnam) while keeping his focus on the central question: what was each side actually fighting for? Green is particularly good on the Cold War's costs outside the U.S. and USSR — the proxy wars fought in Korea, Vietnam, Angola, and Latin America, where millions died while the superpowers avoided direct confrontation. He ends by asking what the Cold War's outcome tells us about the 21st century.

docs.title: Key Concepts Covered
docs.text:
• Origins: WWII alliance's mutual suspicion; the Yalta and Potsdam conferences; Soviet expansion in Eastern Europe; Churchill's "iron curtain" speech
  • Ideological clash: American liberal democracy + capitalism vs. Soviet communism; why both sides saw this as total and global
  • The Truman Doctrine (1947) and Marshall Plan: containment strategy; economic reconstruction as anti-communist strategy
  • NATO vs. Warsaw Pact: the alliance systems
  • The Berlin Crisis and Blockade (1948–49): Berlin as Cold War symbol
  • Nuclear arms race: why MAD (Mutually Assured Destruction) paradoxically prevented direct conflict
  • Korean War (1950–53): hot war within cold war; the forgotten war
  • McCarthyism: the Cold War at home; the domestic cost of the ideological struggle
  • Cuban Missile Crisis (1962): the closest moment; what actually happened
  • Vietnam: Cold War logic applied to nationalist revolution
  • Non-Aligned Movement: nations refusing to choose sides
  • Détente and the end: Reagan, Gorbachev, the fall of the Berlin Wall (1989), USSR dissolution (1991)
  • Green's argument: the Cold War was won not by military force but by the Soviet economic system's internal collapse — and its legacy shapes every major 21st-century conflict

docs.title: Key Timestamps
docs.text:
• [0:00] — Intro: ideology vs. military — what the Cold War actually was
  • [1:30] — Origins: WWII alliance → mutual suspicion → 1946–47
  • [2:30] — Truman Doctrine, Marshall Plan, containment
  • [3:30] — Nuclear weapons and MAD: why direct war never happened
  • [4:30] — Berlin: the symbolic city
  • [5:30] — Korean War: hot conflict in cold war
  • [6:30] — McCarthyism: the domestic Cold War
  • [7:30] — Cuban Missile Crisis: 13 days and what they revealed
  • [8:30] — Vietnam: Cold War logic meets anti-colonial revolution
  • [9:30] — The Non-Aligned Movement: the world outside the binary
  • [10:30] — Détente; the Soviet economic crisis
  • [11:30] — The fall of the Berlin Wall; USSR collapse
  • [12:30] — Conclusion: what did the Cold War settle — and what didn't it?

Note: Timestamps are approximate. Verify against actual video when transcript is available.

docs.title: Watch Prompt
docs.text:
As you watch, track the ideology as war argument:

Green says the Cold War was primarily an ideological conflict. Track the evidence he gives for this claim, and the evidence that complicates it.

Annotation task — the ideology tracker: | Moment/event | Primarily ideological? | Primarily political/economic/military? | Evidence | |-------------|----------------------|---------------------------------------|---------| | Truman Doctrine | | | | | Korean War | | | | | Cuban Missile Crisis | | | | | Vietnam War | | | | | Non-Aligned Movement | | | | | USSR collapse | | | |

After watching: Write one sentence answering — If the Cold War was about ideas, what does it mean that it ended not with a military victory but with one side's economic collapse? Find evidence from the episode.

This connects to WH.6_12.2 (ideologies shaping political development) and WH.6_12.6 (connecting past to present).

docs.text:
Transcript pending. This entry is a stub.

docs.title: Key Lines (from memory / known content)
docs.text:
"The United States and the Soviet Union didn't fight each other directly. But they fought each other everywhere else. Korea, Vietnam, Angola, Afghanistan — millions died in proxy wars so the superpowers could stay cold."

"MAD — Mutually Assured Destruction — sounds insane. And it is. But it worked. Both sides had enough nuclear weapons to destroy the other several times over. That made starting a nuclear war suicide. So neither side did."

"McCarthyism showed what happens when the Cold War comes home. Fear of communism became a tool to silence dissent, destroy careers, and enforce conformity. The external threat justified internal repression."

"The Cold War didn't end because the United States defeated the Soviet Union militarily. It ended because the Soviet economic system couldn't compete. Communism failed to deliver the living standards capitalism could. That's not a military argument — it's an economic one."

docs.title: Skinny Connection
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.2 — Religion and Ideology (Era 4)
  • WH.6_12.6 — Past to Present (Era 4)

docs.title: Pairs With — Reading Library
docs.text:
• George Orwell — 1984 (1949) — Written at the Cold War's opening; Orwell's totalitarian Oceania was a critique of Stalinist communism and surveillance states; students who read 1984 alongside this episode see exactly what Orwell was responding to
  • Ray Bradbury — Fahrenheit 451 (1953) — Written during McCarthyism; Bradbury's fear of book-burning was specifically about Cold War conformism and the suppression of dissent; this episode's section on McCarthyism gives the historical context

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. Orwell + the Cold War: Students read a passage from 1984 (reading library) after watching this episode. Assignment: What specific Cold War conditions is Orwell responding to? Identify two claims Orwell makes about totalitarianism and connect each to historical evidence from the episode. Builds R.9 (text in historical context) and WH.6_12.2.

  2. Fahrenheit 451 + McCarthyism: Students read the McCarthyism section recap from this episode and a passage from Fahrenheit 451 (reading library). Assignment: What is Bradbury afraid of? How does the historical context of McCarthyism change your reading of the novel? Builds R.9 and WH.6_12.2.

  3. MAD Logic: Green explains Mutually Assured Destruction as paradoxically stabilizing. Students evaluate: was MAD a rational strategy? They must define "rational," use two pieces of evidence from the episode, and identify the strongest argument against their position. Builds W.4 and systems-level thinking.

  4. The Proxy War Map: Green says millions died in proxy wars while the superpowers stayed cold. Students map the proxy conflicts he mentions (Korea, Vietnam, Angola, Afghanistan) and for each write: who was actually fighting, what were they fighting about, and what did the U.S./USSR have at stake? Then they argue: were proxy wars "cold"? Targets WH.6_12.5 and WH.6_12.6 — and directly addresses whose lives the Cold War cost most.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Cold War ended in 1991, but most of its infrastructure did not. Nuclear weapons still exist in the quantities built for mutual deterrence — the U.S. and Russia together hold over 90% of the world's roughly 12,000 warheads. NATO still exists and has expanded eastward. The proxy war pattern — great powers fighting each other through smaller states — continues in contemporary conflicts. The intelligence agencies, surveillance systems, and intervention doctrines built for Cold War competition didn't disappear; they were repurposed. And the ideological framing — that the world is divided between systems that are fundamentally incompatible, and that the competition between them will eventually be decided — has returned in debates about U.S.-China competition. Green's episode is the foundation for USH-07: The Unipolar Moment, which asks what the U.S. did with its power after "winning" — and whether the Cold War's end was really an ending at all.

docs.title: Grade 10 — ELA & US History Crosswalk
docs.text:
South High School · Self-Directed Academy · Grade 10

This tab is the integration hub for Grade 10 SDA. Every Studio in Year 2 combines an English standard with a US History standard. This section organizes all wiki resources — reading library texts, CrashCourse episodes, skinnies, and studio packets — by unit group, so you can see everything relevant to one Studio in one place.

How to use this: Find the unit your Studio is in. Everything on that page connects to each other. Each unit page has a Start here box at the top — that's the highest-leverage path if you're not sure where to begin.

docs.title: How Grade 10 Works
docs.text:
In Grade 10, you are simultaneously enrolled in English II and US History. The integration works the same way as Grade 9 — one Studio, two standards, one body of evidence. The content shifts to American history (Reconstruction to present) but the ELA skills you built in Grade 9 carry forward and deepen.

The standard pairing pattern every Studio:

Slot Standard Notes US History One USH standard (US.6_12.1–6) + one Unit The content anchor Reading R.2, R.4, R.7, R.8, or R.9 Primary source + text analysis Writing W.3 (explanatory) or W.4 (argument) Alternates each Studio Research IR.1–5 bundle Active every Studio Communication C.1 and/or C.6 Assessed at Week 6 exhibition

docs.title: Year 2 Unit Sequence
docs.text:
Navigate to the unit group your Studio is in to find all resources assembled in one place:

Units Content Focus Crosswalk Page Units 1–3 Industrial Revolution · Imperialism & WWI · Roaring 20s & Depression Units 1–3 Crosswalk Units 4–6 WWII · Cold War · Civil Rights Units 4–6 Crosswalk Units 7–9 Vietnam · 1970s–80s · 1990s–2000s Units 7–9 Crosswalk

docs.title: US History Standards
docs.text: Code Standard When Active US.6_12.1 Science, technology, and economic development Units 1–3 especially US.6_12.2 Religions and ideologies Units 3–5 (fascism, communism) US.6_12.3 Effects of political systems on people Units 4–7 especially US.6_12.4 Social, cultural, and economic development All units US.6_12.5 Causes and effects using primary sources All units US.6_12.6 Connecting past to present Units 7–9 especially

docs.title: ELA Standards Active in Grade 10
docs.text:
The same standards from Grade 9 continue, with increased complexity expected:

Code Standard Best USH Pairing R.9 Informational and argumentative text analysis Every unit — strongest crosswalk R.8 Literary elements and author's craft Units 5–6 — Night, Warriors Don't Cry, Persepolis R.2 Comprehension and inference Every unit — primary source reading W.4 Argument writing Units 2, 5–7 especially W.3 Explanatory writing Units 1, 3–4 IR.1–5 Research bundle Every Studio

docs.title: Studio Packets Available
docs.text:
Code Essential Question Units USH-01 When an institution fails the people it's supposed to serve, what does it take to fix it? 1–2, 6 USH-02 What does a society's fiction reveal about its fears — and whose fears count? 5 + WH Era 4

USH Skinnies are now available — 54 skinnies across 9 units and 6 standards. Browse via the Skinnies tab above, or use the unit crosswalk pages below for standard × unit pairings.

docs.title: Units 1–3 Crosswalk — Industrial Revolution, Imperialism & WWI, Roaring 20s
docs.text:
Grade 10 · US History Units 1–3 + ELA 9-10 Span: 1870s–1930s · Industrialization · Progressive Era · Imperialism · WWI · Boom and Bust

Start here — one Demo, two standards

US.6_12.4–5 + R.9 + W.4 — Read The Jungle. Analyze Sinclair's argument about labor conditions (R.9 Skinny). Write your own reform argument (W.4 Skinny). That's US.6_12.4 content + two ELA standards in one Demo. Use the US.4 Unit 1 Skinny to find your argument move.

docs.title: 1. "When a system produces suffering at scale, who's responsible?"
docs.text: Essential question: When industrialization creates widespread poverty and dangerous conditions, is anyone to blame — or is it just the system? USH Standard: US.6_12.4–5 + Unit 1 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 Source anchor: The Jungle (Sinclair) Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing Possible deliverables: Argument essay · Investigative journalism piece (written in Sinclair's tradition) · Podcast The argument: Sinclair wrote The Jungle as an argument for reform. Students analyze his argumentative strategy (R.9 — who is his audience? what does he want them to do? what evidence does he use?) and write their own argument about whether systemic harm requires systemic solutions or individual accountability. W.4 + US.6_12.4.

docs.title: 2. "What justified American imperialism — and does that justification hold up?"
docs.text: Essential question: When a powerful country takes control of a weaker one "for its own good," how do we evaluate that claim? USH Standard: US.6_12.4 + Unit 2 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 Source anchors: Kipling — "White Man's Burden" + H.T. Johnson — "Black Man's Burden" Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Verbal Conversation Possible deliverables: Comparative essay · Debate · Podcast The argument: Students read both poems as arguments (R.9), identify the claims, evidence, and intended audiences of each, then write their own argument (W.4) evaluating the "civilizing mission" claim. See also CrashCourse Ep 37 — Imperialism for historical context.

docs.title: 3. "Who entered WWI knowing what they were getting into?"
docs.text: Essential question: If every country entered WWI expecting a short, winnable war, what does that tell us about how leaders make decisions? USH Standard: US.6_12.5 + Unit 2 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 or W.3 Source anchor: Dulce et Decorum Est + CrashCourse ep-38 (WWI) Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Multimedia Possible deliverables: Essay · Documentary · Annotated comparison The argument: Green's episode shows the strategic miscalculations; Owen's poem shows what the reality looked like. Students read both (R.9 for Owen's poetic argument, the episode for historical argument structure) and write either an explanatory piece (W.3) on what the leaders got wrong, or an argument (W.4) about who bears responsibility.

docs.title: 4. "Can a story change a law?"
docs.text: Essential question: The Jungle led to the Pure Food and Drug Act. What does it take for a piece of writing to produce real policy change? USH Standard: US.6_12.4–5 + Unit 1 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.3 Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Multimedia Possible deliverables: Explanatory essay · Documentary · Research presentation The argument: Students research the connection between The Jungle and the 1906 Pure Food and Drug Act, analyzing Sinclair's argument strategy (R.9) and then explaining (W.3) what made his writing capable of producing legislative action. Bonus: they find a contemporary example of journalism or creative work that influenced policy — connecting to US.6_12.6.

docs.title: Standards Active These Units
docs.text:
Subject Standard Focus US History Unit 1 Industrial Revolution, labor, immigration, Progressive Era US History Unit 2 American imperialism, Spanish-American War, WWI US History Unit 3 1920s boom, Harlem Renaissance, Great Depression ELA — Reading R.9 Muckraker journalism, political speeches, propaganda analysis ELA — Reading R.2 Comprehension in complex historical and journalistic texts ELA — Writing W.4 Argument writing — labor rights, imperialism, reform ELA — Writing W.3 Explanatory writing — how industrialization changed American life

Strongest crosswalk pairing Units 1–3: US.6_12.4–5 + R.9 + W.4 — The muckraker tradition (Sinclair, Ida Tarbell, Jacob Riis) is investigative argument. These texts are both primary sources and W.4 mentor texts simultaneously. A student can analyze Sinclair's argument structure (R.9) and write their own reform argument (W.4) while demonstrating US.6_12.4 content knowledge about labor conditions.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Units 1–3 cover the decades when the United States industrialized, built a global empire, fought its first world war, and then watched the economy collapse — and every pattern from that era is recognizable today. The debate between Booker T. Washington and W.E.B. Du Bois about how to achieve civil rights never ended; it's still happening. The question of whether the United States should use military force to spread its values abroad — debated during the Spanish-American War and WWI — is asked about every conflict since. The Great Depression revealed that an unregulated economy can fail everyone at once, and the argument about what the government owes people when it does is the argument behind every debate about the social safety net. The muckrakers — Sinclair, Tarbell, Riis — invented the model of investigative journalism that is still the main check on corporate and government power. If you care about labor rights, press freedom, economic inequality, or when the U.S. should go to war, this is the era where those arguments started. See USH-01: When the System Fails and USH-03: When the Floor Falls Out.

Units 1–3 Crosswalk — SDA Commons Wiki · South High School See also: Unit 1 · Unit 2 · Unit 3 · Units 4–6 Crosswalk

docs.title: Units 4–6 Crosswalk — WWII, Cold War, Civil Rights
docs.text:
Grade 10 · US History Units 4–6 + ELA 9-10 Span: 1930s–1970s · WWII · Cold War · Civil Rights Movement · Great Society

Start here — one Demo, two standards

US.6_12.3 + R.9 + W.4 — Read MLK's Letter from Birmingham Jail. Analyze his argument for civil disobedience (R.9 Skinny). Write your own argument about protest strategy (W.4 Skinny). That's US.6_12.3 + two ELA standards in one Demo. See the Letter from Birmingham + W.4 Crosswalk for a step-by-step guide. Use the US.3 Unit 6 Skinny to find your argument move.

docs.title: 1. "What did 'the good war' cost — and who paid?"
docs.text: Essential question: WWII is called America's "good war." Was it — and for whom? USH Standard: US.6_12.5 + Unit 4 ELA Standard: R.8 + W.4 Source anchors: Night (Wiesel) + CrashCourse ep-41 + FDR Four Freedoms Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Verbal Conversation Possible deliverables: Argument essay · Podcast · Documentary The argument: FDR says the war is for "four freedoms." Green shows the WWII's moral complexity (Holocaust response, atomic bomb). Wiesel shows what one person's experience cost. Students read across all three (R.9 for FDR, R.8 for Wiesel) and write an argument about whether calling WWII "good" is accurate, for whom, and why it matters what we call it. Strong W.4 + US.6_12.5 crosswalk.

docs.title: 2. "Does propaganda work — and how would you know if it was working on you?"
docs.text: Essential question: How does a government shape what its citizens believe — and what's the difference between education and propaganda? USH Standard: US.6_12.2–3 + Unit 5 ELA Standard: R.8 + W.4 Source anchors: Fahrenheit 451 or 1984 + CrashCourse ep-42 (McCarthyism section) Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Multimedia Possible deliverables: Essay · Video essay · Annotated text analysis + argument The argument: Both Bradbury and Orwell wrote about the Cold War fear of conformism. Students use R.8 to analyze how each author literarily embodies that fear (character, setting, symbolism) and W.4 to write an argument about whether the fear was justified and whether it's still relevant. WH.6_12.2 and US.6_12.2 content throughout. See studio packet: USH-02 Literature of Fear

docs.title: 3. "What makes a protest strategy work?"
docs.text: Essential question: The Civil Rights Movement used nonviolent resistance — was that a moral choice, a strategic one, or both? USH Standard: US.6_12.3–4 + Unit 6 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 Source anchor: MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Verbal Conversation Possible deliverables: Argument essay · Debate · Podcast The argument: MLK writes the "Letter from Birmingham Jail" while in jail, defending the strategy of nonviolent civil disobedience to skeptics. Students analyze his argument (R.9 — who is his audience? what is his claim? what evidence does he use?) and write their own argument about whether nonviolent protest is a more effective strategy than other forms of resistance. See: Letter from Birmingham + W.4 Crosswalk.

docs.title: 4. "What does integration cost the person being integrated?"
docs.text: Essential question: When Melba Pattillo Beals walked into Central High School, she was making history — but what was she giving up? USH Standard: US.6_12.3 + Unit 6 ELA Standard: R.8 + W.3 or W.4 Source anchor: Warriors Don't Cry (Beals) Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Verbal Conversation Possible deliverables: Essay · Podcast · Interview-style documentary The argument: Students read Beals's memoir (R.8 — perspective, voice, memoir conventions) and write either an explanatory piece (W.3) on what integration demanded of individual Black students, or an argument (W.4) on whether the burden of desegregation was fairly distributed. US.6_12.3 content throughout.

Subject Standard Focus US History Unit 4 WWII — causes, home front, Holocaust, atomic bomb US History Unit 5 Cold War — ideology, McCarthyism, proxy conflicts US History Unit 6 Civil Rights Movement — law, protest, resistance ELA — Reading R.8 Literary elements — Night, Warriors Don't Cry, 1984, Fahrenheit 451, Persepolis ELA — Reading R.9 Argumentative analysis — FDR, MLK, political speeches, propaganda ELA — Reading R.2 Comprehension and inference in historical literary texts ELA — Writing W.4 Argument writing — the richest unit group for W.4 ELA — Writing W.3 Explanatory writing — Cold War structure, civil rights strategy

Strongest crosswalk pairing Units 4–6: US.6_12.3 + R.9 + W.4 — The Civil Rights era is the richest crosswalk zone in US History. MLK's "Letter from Birmingham Jail" is simultaneously a primary source document AND the ideal W.4 mentor text. See: Letter from Birmingham + W.4 Crosswalk.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Units 4–6 cover WWII, the Cold War, and the Civil Rights movement — three events that created the world students are growing up in. The nuclear weapons built to end WWII are still here, and the question of how to prevent their use is more urgent than it's been in decades. The Cold War framework — U.S. vs. an authoritarian rival, allies and enemies, proxy wars and intelligence operations — never fully ended; it's the template for current U.S.-China and U.S.-Russia tensions. The Civil Rights movement established the legal foundation for every equality argument made since: Title IX, disability rights, LGBTQ rights, immigration rights — all use the framework built by the movement. And the question at the center of Unit 4 — what does a country fighting for freedom owe the people it's fighting alongside, or the civilians it's killing? — is asked about every military action since Hiroshima. If you're trying to understand why racial inequality persists, why nuclear deterrence is still the main reason major powers don't go to war, or why the U.S. still stations troops in 80 countries, Units 4–6 are where the answers start. See USH-04: The Cost of Victory and USH-01: When the System Fails.

Units 4–6 Crosswalk — SDA Commons Wiki · South High School See also: Unit 4 · Unit 5 · Unit 6 · Units 7–9 Crosswalk

docs.title: Units 7–9 Crosswalk — Vietnam, 1970s–80s, 1990s–2000s
docs.text:
Grade 10 · US History Units 7–9 + ELA 9-10 Span: 1960s–2000s · Vietnam · Watergate · Reagan Era · End of Cold War · 9/11 and After

Start here — one Demo, two standards

US.6_12.6 + R.9 + W.4 — These units are the closest to your lived experience. Read Malala's UN Speech or Mandela's Rivonia Speech. Analyze the argument (R.9 Skinny). Write your own answer to: What does the past owe the present? (W.4 Skinny). Use the US.6 Unit 9 Skinny — it's the capstone of the whole two-year arc.

docs.title: 1. "When does protest become history?"
docs.text: Essential question: How does a protest movement decide it's winning — and how does it decide to keep going when it isn't? USH Standard: US.6_12.3–4 + Unit 7 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 Source anchors: Mandela Rivonia Speech + Vietnam-era protest context Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Verbal Conversation Possible deliverables: Argument essay · Podcast · Research presentation The argument: Mandela argued for justice from a defendant's chair, knowing he would likely be imprisoned for life. Students analyze his argumentative strategy (R.9 — what is his claim? who is his real audience?) and write their own argument (W.4) about what makes a protest movement capable of achieving its goals across time. Connects US.6_12.3 with WH.6_12.6.

docs.title: 2. "Who defines what counts as progress?"
docs.text: Essential question: The US has changed dramatically over the 20th century — but changed toward what, and for whom? USH Standard: US.6_12.6 + Units 7–9 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 Source anchor: UN UDHR + Malala UN Speech + student-selected current events source Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Verbal Conversation Possible deliverables: Argument essay · Research presentation · Podcast The argument: The UN UDHR (1948) describes what the world committed to after WWII. Malala (2013) shows what that commitment still requires 65 years later. Students use both to argue: by the standards the world set for itself, how much progress has actually been made — and who gets to define the answer? Strong US.6_12.6 + W.4 crosswalk.

docs.title: 3. "What does it feel like to live inside a political revolution?"
docs.text: Essential question: When a political system changes rapidly around you, how do ordinary people experience it — and what do they lose and gain? USH Standard: US.6_12.3 + Units 7–8 context ELA Standard: R.8 + W.3 Source anchor: Persepolis (Satrapi) Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Verbal Conversation Possible deliverables: Essay · Visual essay (inspired by graphic memoir format) · Podcast The argument: Satrapi tells the story of Iran's 1979 revolution through her own childhood. Students use R.8 to analyze how she uses the graphic memoir form (image, caption, perspective, irony) to show political change, then write a W.3 explanatory piece on what Persepolis reveals about how political change is experienced differently by different people. US.6_12.3 + comparative framework with any US political shift studied in Units 7–8.

docs.title: 4. "How do we know what we know about recent history?"
docs.text: Essential question: The further in the past an event, the more we rely on historians. For recent events, we have everything — does that make it easier or harder to know the truth? USH Standard: US.6_12.5–6 + Unit 9 ELA Standard: R.9 + IR.4 (source credibility) Suggested Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation or Written Assessment Possible deliverables: Annotated source log · Research presentation · Podcast on media literacy The argument: Unit 9 covers events students have some memory of (9/11, social media, 2008 financial crisis). This Studio uses that proximity to interrogate how we know what we know — practicing IR.4 (source credibility) at the highest level, while using R.9 to analyze how contemporary journalism and government documents argue. Strong capstone Studio for the full-year US.6_12.5 research skill strand.

docs.title: 5. "What does the past owe the present?"
docs.text: Essential question: When historical injustice produced ongoing inequality, what — if anything — do later generations owe? USH Standard: US.6_12.6 + Unit 9 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 Source anchors: Malala UN Speech + UN UDHR + student-selected contemporary source Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Verbal Conversation Possible deliverables: Argument essay · Structured debate · Podcast The argument: This is the capstone question of the full two-year SDA arc. Students use the whole year's reading library — from Hammurabi to Malala — to write the most ambitious argument they've attempted: what does history owe the present? What obligations does understanding injustice create? This Studio rewards Phoenix-track students who can propose their own angle. US.6_12.6 + W.4 at full extension.
