docs.title: Standards Active These Units
docs.text:
Subject Standard Focus US History Unit 7 Vietnam War — causes, protest, aftermath; Watergate US History Unit 8 1970s stagflation · Reagan conservatism · Cold War end US History Unit 9 Globalization · 9/11 · War on Terror · contemporary America ELA — Reading R.9 Contemporary argument analysis — speeches, journalism, manifestos ELA — Reading R.2 Comprehension and inference in contemporary texts ELA — Reading R.7 Style, word choice, and author's craft in contemporary writing ELA — Writing W.4 Argument writing — the most directly relevant units for student lives ELA — Writing W.3 Explanatory writing — contemporary systems and their origins

Strongest crosswalk pairing Units 7–9: US.6_12.6 + R.9 + W.4 — these units are the closest to students' lived experience, which makes the "past to present" connection (US.6_12.6) most powerful here. The Malala speech, Mandela, and UN UDHR all connect contemporary global issues to the history students have studied all year.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Units 7–9 are the recent past — close enough that many students' parents lived through parts of it, and close enough that its consequences are still unfolding. The distrust of government that Vietnam and Watergate created didn't heal; it's the baseline for American political culture today. The deregulation and wealth concentration of the Reagan era set the conditions for the inequality students are currently living with. And the War on Terror — the invasion of Afghanistan, the invasion of Iraq based on weapons that didn't exist, the PATRIOT Act, the surveillance infrastructure, the moral and strategic cost of two decades of conflict — is not over. Soldiers who deployed to Afghanistan were not yet born when the Twin Towers fell. The questions Units 7–9 raise are not historical questions: When does a government have the right to deceive its people? What holds powerful people accountable? What does the most powerful country in the world owe the countries it has military relationships with? There are no final answers here — just better and worse arguments. See USH-05: What the Government Didn't Say, USH-06: When the Rules Don't Apply, and USH-07: The Unipolar Moment.

Units 7–9 Crosswalk — SDA Commons Wiki · South High School See also: Unit 7 · Unit 8 · Unit 9 · Units 4–6 Crosswalk · Gr 9 Crosswalk

docs.title: Grade 9 — ELA & World History Crosswalk
docs.text:
South High School · Self-Directed Academy · Grade 9

This tab is the integration hub for Grade 9 SDA. Every Studio in Year 1 combines an English standard with a World History standard. This section organizes all wiki resources — reading library texts, CrashCourse episodes, skinnies, and studio packets — by era, so you can see everything relevant to one Studio in one place.

How to use this: Find the era your Studio is in. Everything on that page connects to each other. Each era page has a Start here box at the top — that's the highest-leverage path if you're not sure where to begin.

docs.title: How Grade 9 Works
docs.text:
In Grade 9, you are simultaneously enrolled in English I and World History. In SDA these are not separate — every Studio produces evidence for both. A single essay, podcast, or documentary can satisfy an ELA writing standard and a World History standard at once. That's the crosswalk.

The standard pairing pattern every Studio:

Slot Standard Notes World History One WH standard (WH.6_12.1–6) + one Era The content anchor Reading R.2, R.4, R.7, R.8, or R.9 Primary source + text analysis Writing W.3 (explanatory) or W.4 (argument) Alternates each Studio Research IR.1–5 bundle Active every Studio Communication C.1 and/or C.6 Assessed at Week 6 exhibition

docs.title: Year 1 Era Sequence
docs.text:
Navigate to the era your Studio is in to find all resources assembled in one place:

docs.title: Era 1 Crosswalk — Ancient Civilizations
docs.text:
Grade 9 · World History Era 1 + ELA 9-10 Span: Prehistory through 600 CE · Earliest civilizations, first religions, ancient empires

Start here — one Demo, two standards

WH.6_12.3 + R.9 + W.4 — Read Hammurabi's Code. Analyze its claims about who the law protects (R.9 Skinny). Write your own argument about who should govern (W.4 Skinny). That's WH.6_12.3 content + two ELA standards in one Demo. Use the WH.6_12.3 Skinny to find your argument move.

docs.title: Project Ideas — Era 1
docs.text: These are proposed Studio inquiry angles. Any can be adapted for your own inquiry question.

docs.title: 1. "Who gets to make the rules?"
docs.text: Essential question: When someone writes down a law, who benefits — and who is left out? WH Standard: WH.6_12.3 + Era 1 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 Source anchor: Hammurabi's Code + Pericles' Funeral Oration Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing (argument essay) or Written Assessment Possible deliverables: Comparative essay · Annotated primary source analysis · Podcast episode The argument: Students take a position on what Hammurabi's Code reveals about who the law was designed to protect — using R.9 to analyze the text's claims and W.4 to write their own argument.

docs.title: 2. "Is the powerful person always right?"
docs.text: Essential question: When a stronger power tells a weaker one that might makes right, what can the weaker power do? WH Standard: WH.6_12.3 + Era 1 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 Source anchor: The Melian Dialogue Suggested Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation or Extended Writing Possible deliverables: Essay · Staged debate using Melian Dialogue format · Visual essay mapping the power argument The argument: Thucydides' Melian Dialogue is one of the oldest recorded arguments about power. Students analyze the Athenian argument structure (R.9) and write a response from the Melian position (W.4), connecting to WH.6_12.3.

docs.title: 3. "How did belief become government?"
docs.text: Essential question: How did early societies use religion to justify political authority — and did it work? WH Standard: WH.6_12.2 + Era 1 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.3 Source anchors: Analects of Confucius + Pericles' Funeral Oration Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Verbal Conversation Possible deliverables: Explanatory essay · Documentary script · Annotated comparison chart The argument: Students compare how Confucius and Pericles each used their ideology to justify political organization — using W.3 to explain the mechanism, and WH.6_12.2 as content evidence.

docs.title: 4. "What did ancient people actually know?"
docs.text: Essential question: How advanced were ancient civilizations — and what does the answer reveal about how we define 'advanced'? WH Standard: WH.6_12.1 + Era 1 ELA Standard: W.3 + R.4 Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Multimedia Possible deliverables: Documentary · Museum-style exhibit · Research presentation The argument: Students examine a specific ancient technology or scientific achievement (irrigation, medicine, the arch, astronomical calendars) and write an explanatory piece arguing for its significance. Challenges presentism.

docs.title: 5. "What do laws reveal about values?"
docs.text: Essential question: Can you tell what a society cared about just by reading its laws? WH Standard: WH.6_12.3 + WH.6_12.6 + Era 1 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 Source anchor: Hammurabi's Code Suggested Demo Mode: Written Assessment or Portfolio Annotation Possible deliverables: Analysis essay · Comparative chart + written reflection · Podcast The argument: Students read selected laws from Hammurabi's Code and identify the values embedded in them (property, family, class), then connect to a modern law that reveals contemporary values. Direct WH.6_12.6 (past to present) entry point.

Highest-leverage crosswalk text this era: Hammurabi's Code — short, dense, analytically rich. The code is an argument about governance. Perfect for R.9 (analyze claims and evidence) + WH.6_12.3 (political systems) in one Demo.

docs.title: Standards Active This Era
docs.text:
Subject Standard Focus World History WH.6_12.1 Science & technology achievements of ancient world World History WH.6_12.2 How early belief systems shaped civilization World History WH.6_12.3 Effects of Greek, Roman, and early political systems World History WH.6_12.4 Social, cultural, and economic development World History WH.6_12.5 Causes and effects using primary sources World History WH.6_12.6 Ancient foundations connecting to the present ELA — Reading R.9 Analyzing argumentative structure in ancient texts ELA — Reading R.2 Comprehension and inference in complex primary sources ELA — Reading R.4 Main idea development in historical argument ELA — Writing W.4 Argument writing — "Who should govern?" ELA — Writing W.3 Explanatory writing — "How did belief shape empire?"

Strongest crosswalk pairing this era: WH.6_12.3 + R.9 + W.4 — ancient political texts are argumentative by nature (Hammurabi, Pericles, Melian Dialogue). A student can analyze the argument structure (R.9), write their own position on political authority (W.4), and simultaneously demonstrate WH.6_12.3 content knowledge. One Demo, two standards.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The questions ancient civilizations asked — who should hold power, what makes a law just, whose life counts — are not settled. They're the same arguments happening in courtrooms, legislatures, and social media feeds right now. When the U.S. Supreme Court debates equal protection under the law, it's arguing with Hammurabi. When a democracy asks whether it has the right to intervene militarily in a weaker country, it's arguing with Thucydides. When a government claims that order requires inequality, it's making Hammurabi's argument. Every Era 1 text was written by someone trying to answer an urgent question about power and justice — and none of those questions have been fully answered yet. If you want to follow this thread into the present, start with WH-01: Who Should Govern? or the ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk.

Era 1 Crosswalk — SDA Commons Wiki · South High School See also: Era 1 Standards Page · Era 2 Crosswalk · Master Crosswalk

docs.title: Era 2 Crosswalk — Middle Ages
docs.text:
Grade 9 · World History Era 2 + ELA 9-10 Span: 500–1600 CE · Islamic Golden Age, Crusades, Mongol Empire, Renaissance, early Reformation

Start here — one Demo, two standards

WH.6_12.3 + R.9 + W.4 — Read Magna Carta. Analyze its claims about limiting government (R.9 Skinny). Argue whether limiting power is always justified (W.4 Skinny). That's WH.6_12.3 + two ELA standards in one Demo. Use the WH.6_12.3 Skinny to find your argument move.

docs.title: 1. "Is order worth obedience?"
docs.text: Essential question: When a political system keeps order by demanding obedience, is the trade worth it? WH Standard: WH.6_12.3 + Era 2 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 Source anchor: Magna Carta + feudal system context Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Verbal Conversation Possible deliverables: Argument essay · Structured debate · Podcast comparing feudalism to another system The argument: Students read the Magna Carta (what rights were demanded, by whom, from whom?) and write an argument about whether limits on government power require people with power to demand them. Direct W.4 + WH.6_12.3 crosswalk.

docs.title: 2. "What did the Crusades actually exchange?"
docs.text: Essential question: If the Crusades failed as military campaigns, why do they still matter? WH Standard: WH.6_12.5 + Era 2 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.3 Source anchor: CrashCourse ep-15 (Crusades) Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Multimedia Possible deliverables: Explanatory essay · Documentary · Annotated map with written analysis The argument: John Green argues the Crusades mattered less because of who won than what was exchanged (mathematics, texts, trade goods). Students analyze his argument (R.9) and write an explanatory piece on why unintended consequences matter in history (W.3 + WH.6_12.5).

docs.title: 3. "Can an idea spread faster than an army?"
docs.text: Essential question: When does an idea become powerful enough to threaten an empire? WH Standard: WH.6_12.2 + Era 2 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 Source anchor: Luther's 95 Theses + CrashCourse ep-25 (Reformation) Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Multimedia Possible deliverables: Argument essay · Podcast · Video essay The argument: Students analyze the 95 Theses as an argument (R.9 — what is Luther's claim? what evidence does he give?) and write their own argument about whether technology (printing press) or ideas (theology) drove the Reformation. WH.6_12.2 content + W.4 argument.

docs.title: 4. "When is bearing witness an act of resistance?"
docs.text: Essential question: What does it cost someone to document an atrocity — and why does it matter? WH Standard: WH.6_12.4–5 + Era 2–3 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 Source anchor: Las Casas — A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Verbal Conversation Possible deliverables: Essay · Podcast · Annotated primary source analysis The argument: Las Casas witnessed Spanish colonial violence and wrote about it explicitly to change policy. Students analyze his argumentative strategy (R.9 — who is he writing to? what is his evidence? what does he want the reader to do?) and write their own argument about whether witnessing and documenting harm is a moral obligation. WH.6_12.4–5 + W.4.

WH.6_12.4–5 + R.9 + W.4 WH-04 Can one document change the world — and if so, how? WH.6_12.5 + W.3 WH-06 What connects the world — and who decides who counts? docs.text:
Text WH Standard ELA Crosswalk Link Magna Carta (1215) WH.6_12.3 R.9 — argument; W.4 — limits on government Open Ibn Battuta — Rihla (1355) WH.6_12.4 R.2 — comprehension of travel narrative; W.3 — explain trade networks Open Martin Luther — 95 Theses (1517) WH.6_12.2 R.9 — analyze a reform argument; W.4 — argument for institutional change Open Las Casas — Short Account (1542) WH.6_12.4–5 R.9 — eyewitness argument; W.4 — moral argument Open Romeo and Juliet — Shakespeare (c. 1597) WH.6_12.3 + WH.6_12.4 R.8 — dramatic structure, honor culture; W.4 — who is responsible? Open Hamlet — Shakespeare (c. 1600) WH.6_12.3 + WH.6_12.2 R.8 — soliloquy, dramatic irony; R.9 — embedded argument; W.4 Open

Highest-leverage crosswalk text this era: Magna Carta — compact, dense with argument, and directly tied to WH.6_12.3. Excellent for R.9 analysis (what is the barons' argument? what evidence do they give?) and W.4 writing ("Should there be limits on government authority?").

docs.title: Standards Active This Era
docs.text:
Subject Standard Focus World History WH.6_12.1 Medieval science, technology, and Islamic Golden Age World History WH.6_12.2 Religion and ideology — Islam, Christianity, feudal church World History WH.6_12.3 Medieval political systems — feudalism, Magna Carta, Ottoman Empire World History WH.6_12.4 Trade, cultural exchange, and social development World History WH.6_12.5 Causes and effects — Crusades, Black Death, fall of Constantinople World History WH.6_12.6 Medieval foundations connecting to the present ELA — Reading R.9 Analyzing argumentative structure in medieval texts ELA — Reading R.2 Comprehension and inference in complex historical documents ELA — Writing W.4 Argument writing — authority, obedience, religious power ELA — Writing W.3 Explanatory writing — how medieval systems worked

Strongest crosswalk pairing this era: WH.6_12.3 + R.9 + W.4 — the Magna Carta is one of the clearest argument texts in world history. A student can analyze its claims (R.9), argue whether limiting government power is always justified (W.4), and demonstrate WH.6_12.3 political systems knowledge simultaneously.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Middle Ages built the systems that still govern modern life — and then shook them to pieces. The Magna Carta's argument that rulers must follow laws, not just make them, is the foundation of every constitution in the world. The Crusades introduced a template for religiously justified violence that didn't end in 1291. The Islamic Golden Age preserved the mathematics, medicine, and philosophy that made the European Renaissance possible — and then was written out of the story when Europe told it back. The Black Death killed one-third of Europe and rewired labor markets, social hierarchy, and the relationship between people and institutions in ways that took centuries to settle. If you want to understand why international law exists, why church and state are separate, or why questions about religious authority in government keep coming back — this era is where to look. Start with WH-04: One Idea, 130 Years of War or WH-06: The Connected World to see how this era's ideas played out.

Era 2 Crosswalk — SDA Commons Wiki · South High School See also: Era 2 Standards Page · Era 1 Crosswalk · Era 3 Crosswalk

docs.title: Era 3 Crosswalk — Age of Revolutions
docs.text:
Grade 9 · World History Era 3 + ELA 9-10 Span: 1400–1900 · Exploration, Enlightenment, Atlantic Revolutions, Industrialization, Imperialism

Start here — one Demo, two standards

WH.6_12.3 + R.9 + W.4 — Era 3 is the richest crosswalk era. Pick any text from the Reading Library table below — Common Sense, Wollstonecraft, Douglass, or Kipling+Johnson. Analyze the argument (R.9). Write your own position (W.4). Each one already is WH.6_12.3 primary source evidence. Use the WH.6_12.3 Skinny or the Common Sense + W.4 Crosswalk to scaffold.

docs.title: 1. "When is rebellion justified?"
docs.text: Essential question: What conditions have to exist before revolution is a legitimate response to injustice? WH Standard: WH.6_12.3 + Era 3 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 Source anchors: Common Sense + Declaration of Rights of Man + Haitian Declaration Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Verbal Conversation Possible deliverables: Argument essay · Structured debate · Comparative analysis The argument: Students compare what Common Sense, the DROM, and the Haitian Declaration each argue justifies revolution. Using R.9, they identify the argumentative structure of at least two. Using W.4, they write their own position on when rebellion is justified. Three revolutions, one essay.

docs.title: 2. "Who gets left out of 'liberty and equality'?"
docs.text: Essential question: Every revolution claims to fight for freedom — so why do some people always end up excluded from it? WH Standard: WH.6_12.3–4 + Era 3 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 Source anchors: Wollstonecraft + Haitian Declaration + CrashCourse ep-31 Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Multimedia Possible deliverables: Essay · Podcast · Documentary The argument: Wollstonecraft asks why the French Revolution's "rights of man" excluded women. The Haitian Revolution asks why the same revolution's rhetoric didn't end slavery. Students read both arguments (R.9) and write their own (W.4): what does the pattern reveal about how political systems treat those they were not built for?

docs.title: 3. "How do you write a nation into existence?"
docs.text: Essential question: Can a document actually create a country — and what makes some documents more powerful than others? WH Standard: WH.6_12.5 + Era 3 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.3 Source anchors: Common Sense + Declaration of Rights of Man Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing Possible deliverables: Explanatory essay · Comparative analysis · Documentary The argument: Students analyze Common Sense and the DROM as rhetorical objects — what makes them persuasive? Using R.9 to analyze argument structure and W.3 to explain, they produce a piece on how language creates political legitimacy. See: Common Sense + W.4 Crosswalk.

docs.title: 4. "Who pays the cost of empire?"
docs.text: Essential question: When one group benefits from a global system, who bears the cost — and does the beneficiary know? WH Standard: WH.6_12.4–5 + Era 3 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 Source anchors: Kipling + H.T. Johnson + CrashCourse ep-37 (Imperialism) Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Multimedia Possible deliverables: Comparative essay · Podcast · Photo essay with argument The argument: Students read Kipling's "White Man's Burden" alongside H.T. Johnson's direct rebuttal. They analyze both as arguments (R.9) — what is each writer claiming? who is their audience? what evidence do they use? — then write their own position (W.4) on who is right. WH.6_12.4 content + strongest W.4 model this era.

docs.title: 5. "Why did some people know slavery was wrong and do nothing?"
docs.text: Essential question: What does it take for moral knowledge to become moral action — and what gets in the way? WH Standard: WH.6_12.4–5 + Era 3 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 Source anchor: Narrative of Frederick Douglass + CrashCourse ep-23 (Slave Trade) Suggested Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation or Extended Writing Possible deliverables: Essay · Podcast · Debate The argument: Douglass writes explicitly about this — he knows his enslavers know it's wrong. Students use his argument (R.9) alongside Green's structural analysis of the slave trade (ep-23) to write an argument about the gap between moral understanding and political action. Rich W.4 entry point.

docs.title: Standards Active This Era
docs.text:
Subject Standard Focus World History WH.6_12.1 Industrial technology, steam power, scientific revolution World History WH.6_12.2 Enlightenment ideologies, nationalism, abolitionism World History WH.6_12.3 Revolutions and their effects on political systems World History WH.6_12.4 Atlantic slave trade, colonialism, social transformation World History WH.6_12.5 Causes and effects — Columbian Exchange, revolutions, imperialism World History WH.6_12.6 Revolutionary era connecting to the present ELA — Reading R.9 Analyzing Enlightenment and revolutionary arguments ELA — Reading R.2 Comprehension and inference in argumentative primary sources ELA — Writing W.4 Argument writing — the richest era for W.4 ELA — Writing W.3 Explanatory writing — causes of revolution, effects of trade

Strongest crosswalk pairing this era: Era 3 is the richest crosswalk era — nearly every reading library text here is an argument. WH.6_12.3 + R.9 + W.4 can be satisfied by Common Sense, the Declaration of Rights of Man, Wollstonecraft, Douglass, or Kipling+Johnson — any one of which works as a W.4 mentor text while simultaneously being primary source evidence for the WH standard.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Era 3 is the era when humans decided that individuals had rights governments couldn't take away — and then immediately started arguing about who counted as an individual. The American Revolution, French Revolution, and Haitian Revolution all claimed universal human freedom. All three drew lines around who that freedom actually applied to. Those lines are still being redrawn. Arguments about who deserves citizenship, who the law protects, whether economic inequality can coexist with political equality, and whether revolutionary violence is justified — these are Era 3 arguments. When people today debate immigration, civil rights, labor rights, or the legitimacy of protest, they're using the vocabulary of this era. Thomas Paine, Mary Wollstonecraft, Toussaint Louverture, and Karl Marx were all trying to answer questions that haven't been answered yet. See WH-02: The Rights We Wrote Down or WH-03: Writing Against Empire for studios built around these questions.

Era 3 Crosswalk — SDA Commons Wiki · South High School See also: Era 3 Standards Page · Era 2 Crosswalk · Era 4 Crosswalk

docs.title: Era 4 Crosswalk — Global War and Contemporary
docs.text:
Grade 9 · World History Era 4 + ELA 9-10 Span: 1900–present · WWI, WWII, Cold War, Decolonization, Globalization

Start here — one Demo, two standards

Two paths depending on your inquiry:

  • Literary angle → WH.6_12.2 + R.8 — Read Night. Analyze Wiesel's use of literary elements (R.8 Skinny). That's Holocaust evidence (WH.6_12.2) + R.8 in a single Demo.
  • Argument angle → WH.6_12.6 + R.9 + W.4 — Read Malala's UN Speech. Analyze the argument (R.9 Skinny). Write your own position on what history requires of us now (W.4 Skinny).

docs.title: 1. "What does a society's fiction reveal about its fears?"
docs.text: Essential question: When a society produces a specific kind of story over and over, what does that tell us about what it's afraid of? WH Standard: WH.6_12.2 + Era 4 ELA Standard: R.8 + W.4 Source anchors: 1984 + Fahrenheit 451 + CrashCourse ep-42 (Cold War / McCarthyism) Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Multimedia Possible deliverables: Analytical essay · Podcast episode · Video essay comparing both novels The argument: Orwell and Bradbury were both writing in the early Cold War, both afraid of the same thing (totalitarian conformism). Students use R.8 to analyze the literary elements each author uses to embody that fear, and W.4 to argue which fear was more prescient — or to argue something about fear and fiction itself. WH.6_12.2 content throughout. See studio packet: USH-02 Literature of Fear

docs.title: 2. "What did 'never again' actually require?"
docs.text: Essential question: After the Holocaust, the world said "never again" — what would that actually have required, and did anyone do it? WH Standard: WH.6_12.5–6 + Era 4 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 Source anchors: Night (Wiesel) + UN UDHR + Malala UN Speech Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Verbal Conversation Possible deliverables: Argument essay · Debate · Documentary The argument: Wiesel's memoir shows what the Holocaust cost one person. The UDHR shows what the world promised in response. Malala shows what "never again" required 65 years later. Students read all three (R.9 + R.8) and write an argument about whether "never again" has worked — and what it actually demands.

docs.title: 3. "Whose stories get told after a war?"
docs.text: Essential question: History is written by those who survive — but who among the survivors gets to write it? WH Standard: WH.6_12.5 + Era 4 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.4 or W.3 Source anchors: Night (Wiesel) + CrashCourse ep-41 (WWII Part 2) + Dulce et Decorum Est Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Multimedia Possible deliverables: Essay · Documentary · Podcast The argument: Green's WWII episode tells the structural story. Owen's poem tells it from the trench. Wiesel's memoir tells it from a camp. Students compare all three narrative positions (R.9 for the argumentative texts, R.8 for the literary ones) and write an argument about what gets lost when we rely on a single kind of narrator for history.

docs.title: 4. "Is it possible to resist a totalitarian state?"
docs.text: Essential question: When a government controls everything — information, movement, who is human — what can an ordinary person actually do? WH Standard: WH.6_12.2–3 + Era 4 ELA Standard: R.8 + W.4 Source anchors: 1984 or Persepolis + Mandela Rivonia Speech Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Verbal Conversation Possible deliverables: Essay · Podcast · Research presentation The argument: Winston Smith (fictional) and Mandela (historical) both resist totalitarian states. Students compare their strategies and contexts using R.8 (for 1984 or Persepolis) and R.9 (for Mandela's speech), then write an argument about what conditions make resistance possible. WH.6_12.2–3 throughout.

docs.title: 5. "What did WWI destroy that never came back?"
docs.text: Essential question: Some losses from a war are physical. What about the losses that can't be rebuilt? WH Standard: WH.6_12.5 + Era 4 ELA Standard: R.9 + W.3 or W.4 Source anchors: Dulce et Decorum Est + CrashCourse ep-38 (WWI) Suggested Demo Mode: Extended Writing or Multimedia Possible deliverables: Essay · Annotated poem analysis + historical context piece · Podcast The argument: Green argues WWI destroyed the Enlightenment faith in progress. Owen's poem documents what the killing looked like from inside. Students read both (R.9 for the argumentative essay framework, R.8 elements for the poem) and write either an explanatory piece (W.3) on what the war destroyed culturally, or an argument (W.4) on whether that loss was necessary.

docs.title: Studio Packets Available
docs.text: Code Essential Question Standards WH-05 Who pays the cost of war — and who gets to tell the story? WH.6_12.5 + R.9 + W.4 USH-02 What does a society's fiction reveal about its fears — and whose fears count? WH.6_12.2 + R.8 + W.4

Open Slaughterhouse-Five — Kurt Vonnegut (1969) WH.6_12.5 R.8 — non-linear narrative, dark satire; W.4 — anti-war argument Open

Highest-leverage crosswalk texts this era: - Night — Literary analysis AND Holocaust evidence simultaneously. R.8 + WH.6_12.2. See the ELA ↔ SS Master Crosswalk. - Malala UN Speech — Contemporary, short, and analytically rich. R.9 + WH.6_12.6. Perfect for students who need a modern anchor point.

docs.title: Standards Active This Era
docs.text:
Subject Standard Focus World History WH.6_12.1 20th century technology — atomic weapons, communication, medicine World History WH.6_12.2 Fascism, communism, human rights ideology World History WH.6_12.3 Totalitarianism, democracy, decolonization movements World History WH.6_12.4 Social and cultural effects of global conflict World History WH.6_12.5 Causes and effects of WWI, WWII, Cold War World History WH.6_12.6 20th century events connecting directly to the present ELA — Reading R.9 Analyzing argumentative speeches, manifestos, and human rights documents ELA — Reading R.8 Literary elements — Night, 1984, Fahrenheit 451, Persepolis ELA — Reading R.2 Comprehension and inference in literary and historical texts ELA — Writing W.4 Argument writing — the most contested era ELA — Writing W.3 Explanatory writing — causes of war, Cold War structure

Strongest crosswalk pairing this era: WH.6_12.2 + R.8 — Era 4 is the era where ELA literary texts and WH content are most directly fused. Night (Wiesel) is both literature and Holocaust evidence. 1984 is both a novel and a document of Cold War fear. Fahrenheit 451 is both fiction and a primary source on McCarthyism. A student reading any of these is doing R.8 and WH.6_12.2 simultaneously.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Era 4 is the era we're still living in. The global order built after World War II — the United Nations, the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, the International Criminal Court, NATO, the nuclear deterrence framework — is the world students are inheriting. And it is under more stress right now than at any point since it was built. The questions this era raises aren't history: What do you do when a nuclear-armed country invades a neighbor? What happens when democratic governments start using authoritarian tactics? Who enforces human rights when the countries that wrote the rules stop following them? Elie Wiesel wrote Night because he believed that if enough people understood what happened, it could never happen again. The ongoing debates about genocide prevention, refugee rights, and international accountability are the test of whether he was right. See WH-05: The Cost of War and the ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk for the deepest connections between this era's texts and today.

docs.title: Reading Library
docs.text:
SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

This is where you find texts for your studio. Every entry has the full passage, plain-language context, vocabulary, and connections to your standards — everything you need to start reading and building an argument.

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text:
What week are you in?

  • Week 1 — Launch: Browse the table below by era or text type. Pick one passage from each of your studio's four texts. You don't need to read everything — just enough to write a first reaction.
  • Week 2 — Dig: Come back to the 1–2 texts that grabbed you. Use the full entry — read the key passage, check the vocabulary, note the standard connections.
  • Week 3–5 — Build, Shape, Finish: Use the "Studio Ideas" section of each entry to sharpen your argument. Check the crosswalk entries if your studio spans both ELA and SS.
  • Stuck? If you don't know which texts fit your studio, go to your Studio Packet page first — it lists the four recommended texts and why they matter.

docs.title: I'm in a World History studio
docs.text: Era What You're Studying Go here Era 1 Ancient civilizations — law, religion, government, philosophy Era 1 texts Era 2 Middle Ages — feudalism, the Church, early trade, colonization Era 2 texts Era 3 Revolutions — political, industrial, anti-colonial Era 3 texts Era 4 20th century — world wars, civil rights, globalization Era 4 texts

docs.text: That's a Crosswalk text. See the Dual-Use texts section and the Crosswalk pages.

docs.title: How to Read an Entry
docs.text:
Every text in this library has the same structure — once you know it, you can navigate any entry in about 30 seconds:

Section What it tells you What This Text Is About Two sentences. Plain language. Read this first. Read It The actual passage — a key excerpt embedded directly on the page ELA Standards This Connects To Which reading/writing standards this text helps you demonstrate Social Studies Standards Which SS standard + era this connects to Key Vocabulary Words you need to know before you read Studio Ideas Ready-made Essential Questions built around this text Why This Is Relevant Today? How this old text connects to something happening right now

docs.title: Era 1 — Ancient Civilizations (c. 3000 BCE – 500 CE)
docs.text:
These texts ask: Who gets power, and why? How do societies decide what is just?

Text Author What it's about Egyptian Book of the Dead Ancient Egyptian scribes What ancient Egyptians believed about death, judgment, and the soul Hammurabi's Code Hammurabi One of the world's first written legal codes — 282 laws carved in stone The Dhammapada Attributed to the Buddha Core Buddhist teachings on the mind, suffering, and how to live The Twelve Tables of Rome Roman Decemviri Rome's first public law code — written so citizens could know their rights Polybius — On the Roman Republic Polybius A Greek historian explains why Rome's mixed government made it so powerful The Analects Confucius Short teachings on leadership, virtue, and how to run a just society Pericles' Funeral Oration Thucydides Athens' most famous speech defending democracy — and what it cost The Melian Dialogue Thucydides Athens tells a small island: surrender or be destroyed. The island refuses.

Read these two together: Pericles' Funeral Oration and the Melian Dialogue are by the same author, set 15 years apart. One celebrates Athenian democracy. The other shows what that democracy did when nobody was watching.

docs.title: Era 2 — Middle Ages and Early Modern (500–1750 CE)
docs.text:
These texts ask: Who has authority — king, church, or law? What happens when that breaks down?

Text Author What it's about Feudal Obligations Various The actual contracts that held medieval society together — land for loyalty Pope Gregory VII / Emperor Henry IV Pope Gregory VII; Henry IV A showdown between the Pope and the Holy Roman Emperor over who has ultimate power Magna Carta English barons / King John The document that said even kings have to follow rules — 1215 Rihla (Travels) Ibn Battuta A 14th-century Muslim traveler's account of the world as it actually was The 95 Theses Martin Luther The document that started the Protestant Reformation — posted to a church door A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies Bartolomé de las Casas A Spanish priest witnesses the genocide of Indigenous people and refuses to stay silent

docs.title: Era 3 — Age of Revolutions and Industry (1750–1914)
docs.text:
These texts ask: What makes a revolution legitimate? Who gets left out?

Text Author What it's about Common Sense Thomas Paine The pamphlet that convinced a generation of colonists that independence was obvious Declaration of the Rights of Man National Assembly of France France's version of universal rights — written during one of history's bloodiest revolutions Declaration of the Rights of Woman Olympe de Gouges Written in response to the Declaration above — because it didn't include women A Vindication of the Rights of Woman Mary Wollstonecraft The argument that women's subjugation is taught, not natural — 1792 Haitian Declaration of Independence Dessalines / Boisrond-Tonnerre The only successful slave revolt in history announces itself to the world The Communist Manifesto Marx & Engels The argument that history is a story of class conflict — and that workers will win Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass Frederick Douglass A formerly enslaved man describes what slavery actually was — and dismantles every argument for it "The White Man's Burden" + H.T. Johnson response Kipling / Johnson A pro-empire poem and a direct rebuttal — read them together The Jungle Upton Sinclair A novel about immigrant meatpacking workers that accidentally exposed the entire food industry "Dulce et Decorum Est" Wilfred Owen A WWI soldier's poem about what it actually looks like when someone dies from a gas attack

docs.title: Era 4 — Global War and Globalization (1914–Present)
docs.text:
These texts ask: What do we owe each other? Who decides what freedom means?

Text Author What it's about FDR — Four Freedoms Speech Franklin D. Roosevelt FDR defines what America is fighting for in WWII — including freedom from fear and want UN Universal Declaration of Human Rights United Nations The world's attempt, after WWII, to say: these rights belong to every human Letter from Birmingham Jail Martin Luther King Jr. MLK responds to white clergymen who told him to slow down — one of the great arguments in American history Rivonia Trial Speech Nelson Mandela Mandela speaks at his own trial for fighting apartheid — knowing he might be sentenced to death UN Speech — "One Child, One Teacher" Malala Yousafzai A teenager shot for going to school addresses the United Nations

Rights chain: If you're tracing the argument for universal human rights across history, read these in order: Hammurabi's Code → Magna Carta → Common Sense → Declaration of Rights of Man → Haitian Declaration → FDR Four Freedoms → UN UDHR → Malala. Each one builds on — or challenges — the one before it.

docs.title: ELA Literary Texts
docs.text:
These texts are organized by what they teach best. Most also connect to a historical era — see the History Connection column.

Text Author Best for History Connection The Odyssey Homer Epic narrative, character arc, heroism and its cost WH Era 1 — Greek civilization Oedipus Rex Sophocles Fate vs. free will, dramatic irony, tragedy WH Era 1 — Greek drama and politics Romeo & Juliet Shakespeare Dramatic structure, language, tragic flaw WH Era 2 — Renaissance Julius Caesar Shakespeare Political rhetoric, manipulation, persuasion WH Era 1 (Rome) Hamlet Shakespeare Moral ambiguity, revenge, action vs. inaction WH Era 2 — Early modern Europe Moby Dick Melville Obsession, symbolism, the American frontier WH Era 3 — 19th century America Narrative of Frederick Douglass Douglass Argument, ethos, evidence — and what slavery was USH — Civil War era The Jungle Sinclair Naturalism, investigative writing, social reform USH — Progressive Era The Great Gatsby Fitzgerald Symbolism, the American Dream, class and illusion USH — 1920s Their Eyes Were Watching God Hurston Voice, dialect, identity, women and autonomy USH — Harlem Renaissance Things Fall Apart Achebe Colonialism from the inside, cultural identity WH Era 3–4 — Africa under empire Lord of the Flies Golding Civilization vs. savagery, allegory, WWII context WH Era 4 Night Wiesel Memoir, witness, the Holocaust from inside it WH Era 4 — Holocaust "Politics and the English Language" Orwell How language is used to manipulate — still true today WH Era 4 1984 Orwell Totalitarianism, surveillance, propaganda WH Era 4 — Cold War Fahrenheit 451 Bradbury Censorship, conformity, what books are for WH Era 4 A Raisin in the Sun Hansberry Dreams, race, family, mid-century America USH — Civil Rights era To Kill a Mockingbird Lee Justice, moral courage, race in the American South USH — Civil Rights era I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings Angelou Memoir, trauma, resilience, Black American experience USH — Civil Rights era Warriors Don't Cry Beals Primary source memoir — Little Rock integration, 1957 USH — Civil Rights Persepolis Satrapi Graphic memoir — growing up in the Iranian Revolution WH Era 4 The Handmaid's Tale Atwood Dystopia, gender, power, political allegory Contemporary The Kite Runner Hosseini Guilt, redemption, Afghanistan — history through personal story WH Era 4 Beloved Morrison Slavery's legacy, memory, trauma — told from the inside USH — Reconstruction

Public domain text (short): Paste full text into ## Read It as blockquotes. Public domain text (long): Embed key chapters; link to Project Gutenberg at top of section. PDF from Internet Archive: Run pdftotext file.pdf file.txt, clean OCR artifacts, paste into ## Read It. docs.title: Declaration of the Rights of Woman and of the Female Citizen (1791)
docs.text: Author: Olympe de Gouges Type: Primary Source · Political Manifesto Source: Public domain — Wikisource translation Era/Period: Era 3 — Age of Revolutions WH Standards: WH.6_12.4 · WH.6_12.3 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.9 · 9-10.W.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
The "Declaration of the Rights of Woman and of the Female Citizen" is a political manifesto published in 1791 by Olympe de Gouges, a French playwright and political activist who sought to extend the revolutionary principles of liberty, equality, and fraternity to women. De Gouges' declaration mirrors the famous "Declaration of the Rights of Man," but it specifically addresses the rights of women, challenging the gender inequalities that persisted in post-Revolutionary France. This document is a cornerstone in the history of feminism and human rights advocacy, advocating for women's political, social, and economic equality.

This text is crucial for high school students to understand because it introduces them to the foundational concepts of civil rights and feminist philosophy during the Age of Revolutions. It also encourages critical thinking about the role of gender in society and its impact on individual freedoms and collective progress.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Source: Declaration of the Rights of Woman and of the Female Citizen by Olympe de Gouges, 1791

docs.title: Preamble
docs.text:
Women, wake up! A revolution is being prepared in France but are we to be excluded from its benefits? No. Take courage then, a day is coming when it will be announced: "Woman, you are no longer enslaved; woman, learn to know yourself and to arm yourself with the knowledge of your rights."

The natural rights of women and of men are liberty, equality, property, and resistance to oppression.

docs.title: Articles 1–6 — Core Rights
docs.text:
Article I. Women are born free and remain equal to men in rights.

Article II. Distinctions based on sex are contrary to nature; they should be abolished by law.

Article III. The right to participate in the making of laws is a fundamental guarantee of women's freedom.

Article IV. Liberty consists in being able to do anything which does not harm others; thus, the exercise of the natural rights of every woman has no bounds other than those that ensure to the community at large the enjoyment of its own liberty.

Article V. The law has only the right to prohibit such actions as are hurtful to society. Everything else is beyond the pale of its authority because it would be prejudicial to personal freedom.

docs.title: Articles 10–11 — Freedom of Speech
docs.text:
Article X: "Woman has the right to mount the scaffold; she must equally have the right to mount the rostrum."

[This article emphasizes that if women are subject to punishment under law, they should also be afforded the same opportunities as men to express their opinions and engage in public debate.]

docs.title: The Social Contract — Postscript
docs.text: A society where half of its members suffer from a lack of freedom cannot be considered free. Women must have an equal voice in shaping laws and participating in governance because this is essential for the realization of true liberty and equality.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• 9-10.R.9: Analyze seminal U.S. documents of historical and literary significance (e.g., Washington’s Farewell Address, the Gettysburg Address, Roosevelt’s Four Freedoms speech, King’s “Letter from Birmingham Jail”), including how they address related themes and concepts.
  • 9-10.W.4: Produce clear and coherent writing in which the development, organization, and style are appropriate to task, purpose, and audience.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.4: Analyze multiple causal factors that shaped major outcomes of World War II.
  • WH.6_12.3: Use questions generated about individuals and groups to analyze primary and secondary sources for accuracy and sufficiency in addressing the historical question.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Suffrage The right to vote Enfranchisement The process of granting a group the rights of voting Equality The state of being equal in status, rights, and opportunities Citizenry The body of citizens of a country or region Liberty Freedom from arbitrary or despotic control Fraternity A feeling of close association with others based on shared interests or ideals

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. EQ: How does the Declaration challenge societal norms and expectations for women?
  2. Standard: 9-10.R.9
  3. Mode: Demo Mode (students present their analysis to peers)
  4. EQ: What are some historical and contemporary examples of movements that aimed at achieving similar rights as outlined in the Declaration?
  5. Standard: WH.6_12.3
  6. Mode: Studio Creation Mode (collaborative presentation or research project)
  7. EQ: How can women's political participation contribute to societal progress and change?
  8. Standard: 9-10.W.4
  9. Mode: Workshop Mode (peer review of written arguments)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Olympe de Gouges wrote her Declaration of the Rights of Woman in 1791 by simply taking the Declaration of the Rights of Man and replacing "man" with "woman" — to show that the logic of universal rights either applies to everyone or it isn't universal. She was guillotined two years later. The argument she made — that if rights are truly inherent to persons, denying them to women is a logical contradiction — has been made by every feminist movement since, in every country where women have fought for rights they were told they didn't deserve. The specific rights she demanded (to vote, to own property, to speak publicly, to participate in government) are still contested in parts of the world today, and the argument that women's equality threatens the natural social order is still made. Her fate is also instructive: she challenged a revolutionary government that proclaimed universal freedom and was executed for doing so. The gap between a movement's stated values and who it actually includes is never closed automatically.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:

  • Wollstonecraft — Vindication of the Rights of Woman
  • Era 3 — Age of Revolutions
  • WH.6_12.4 Revolution Skinny

docs.title: Crosswalk: Common Sense as Argumentative Mentor Text
docs.text: Text: Common Sense by Thomas Paine (1776) Primary Entry: Common Sense — Paine Crosswalk Type: ELA ↔ Social Studies ELA Standards: 9-10.R.9 · 9-10.W.4 SS Standards: WH.6_12.4 + Era 3 · WH.6_12.5 + Era 3

docs.title: Why This Text Works for Both
docs.text:
Common Sense is the ideal crosswalk text because it was written as an argument. Its purpose is to persuade. This means it can be used simultaneously to:

  1. Hit W.4 (ELA) — by studying how Paine constructs an argument
  2. Hit WH.6_12.4 (SS) — by studying what the argument was about and how it caused the Revolution

Most crosswalk texts require a workaround — using a historical document for ELA or using a literary text for SS. Common Sense is genuinely both at once.

docs.title: ELA Use (W.4 Argument Writing — Reading as a Writer)
docs.text: When using Common Sense for ELA, the question is: How does Paine build his argument?

docs.title: Structural Map
docs.text: Section Move Paine Makes What Students Learn Introduction Distinguishes society (good) from government (necessary evil) Setting up a framework before the claim Part I Uses the Bible against monarchy — a source his audience trusts Using audience values as evidence Part II Traces the history of monarchy — all began in conquest Historical evidence to undermine legitimacy Part III Argues American independence is both possible and necessary Two-part argument (feasibility + morality) Part IV Addresses the logistics of a navy and government Counterargument preemption

docs.title: W.4 Mentor Text Questions
docs.text:
• What is Paine's main claim? Where does he state it explicitly?
  • What types of evidence does he use? (Biblical, historical, economic, logical)
  • How does he address counterarguments? Find a specific example.
  • Why does he write the way he does — short punchy sentences mixed with long catalogs?
  • What does he assume his reader already believes? How does he use those beliefs?

docs.title: Social Studies Use (WH.6_12.4 + Era 3)
docs.text: When using Common Sense for SS, the question is: How did this document contribute to the Revolution?

docs.title: Historical Context Questions
docs.text:
• Why was anonymity important when Paine first published it?
  • Who was the intended audience? Who was actually reading it?
  • How did Enlightenment ideas (Locke, Rousseau) appear in Paine's argument?
  • Why did the argument resonate with ordinary colonists more than elite political writing?

docs.title: Connections to Era 3 Standards
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.4 — Common Sense is a direct cause document: it shifted public opinion in favor of independence. The pamphlet sold 500,000 copies in a population of 2.5 million — roughly equivalent to 60 million copies today.
  • WH.6_12.5 — Paine's economic argument (British trade restrictions, the cost of being part of the empire) is as central as his political argument. Revolution had economic as well as ideological causes.

docs.title: Combined Studio: "Paine's Argument Then and Now"
docs.text:
Essential Question: What makes an argument powerful enough to change history?

Standards: WH.6_12.4 + Era 3 · 9-10.W.4 (ELA)

Six-Week Arc: - Weeks 1–2: Read and annotate Common Sense (R.9 analysis of argument structure) - Week 3: Research the context (what was happening in 1776 that made this land?) - Week 4: Draft an argumentative essay analyzing Paine's most effective move - Week 5: Revision using peer feedback + Hermes feedback - Week 6: Demo of Learning (Extended Writing) + Deliverable (exhibit-ready essay with historical context)

Demo: Extended Writing scored on W.4 rubric (WH.6_12.4 + Era 3 content embedded in the argument) Deliverable: Published argumentative essay for the SDA Commons Reading Library

docs.title: Comparative Pairing
docs.text: Common Sense pairs well with: - Declaration of Rights of Man — French Revolution parallel, same Enlightenment ideas, different outcomes - Letter from Birmingham Jail — Argumentative structure comparison across 200 years: What makes each persuasive for its moment?

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Common Sense is the most influential pamphlet in American history — Paine published it in January 1776 and within months the colonial position shifted from "reform" to "independence." Paine was not a politician or a general; he was a writer, and what he wrote changed what people believed was possible. That is the argument for what good W.4 writing actually does: it doesn't just describe the world, it changes the world by changing minds. Using Common Sense as a mentor text means learning from one of history's most effective arguers — studying the specific choices (direct address, plain language, emotional appeal grounded in evidence) that made his argument land. For the full historical text, see Common Sense in the Reading Library; for the studio that uses it most heavily, see WH-04: One Idea, 130 Years of War.

Reading Library Crosswalk · SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

docs.title: ELA ↔ Social Studies Crosswalk
docs.text:
SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

This page maps texts that simultaneously serve both ELA and Social Studies standards. Crosswalk texts are the highest-leverage studio resources — one text, two standards addressed at once.

docs.title: How to Use a Crosswalk Text
docs.text:
A crosswalk text lets a studio session count toward both the ELA and SS standard on the same student's record. This works when:

  1. The text is genuinely analytical from both an ELA and a historical perspective
  2. The Demo of Learning assesses both standards explicitly
  3. The studio contract names both standards in the "Standards" section

docs.title: Highest-Leverage Texts (Use These First)
docs.text:
These are the texts where the ELA-SS crosswalk is most natural — the SS content is the argument, making ELA work feel authentic rather than forced:

  1. MLK "Letter from Birmingham Jail" — Argues a historical case. The argument structure IS the history lesson. Perfect W.4 mentor text.
  2. Common Sense (Paine) — The Revolution's most effective argument. W.4 structure + WH.6_12.4 cause of revolution.
  3. Declaration of the Rights of Man — Compare/contrast with US Declaration for both R.9 (ELA) and WH.6_12.3 (political systems).
  4. Night (Wiesel) — Literary analysis AND Holocaust evidence simultaneously. R.8 + WH.6_12.2.
  5. Hammurabi's Code — Short, dense, analyzable. R.9 argument analysis + WH.6_12.3 political systems.

docs.title: Full Library Crosswalk Entries
docs.text:
• Common Sense + W.4 Crosswalk — Paine as W.4 mentor text + WH.6_12.4 Revolution cause
  • Letter from Birmingham + W.4 Crosswalk — MLK as W.4 mentor text + US.6_12.3 Civil Rights

docs.title: How Crosswalk Standards Appear on a Studio Contract
docs.text:
When a studio uses a crosswalk text, the contract lists:

Standards Addressed:
- ELA: 9-10.W.4 (Extended Argumentative Writing)
- SS: WH.6_12.4 + Era 3 (Causes and Effects of Revolution)

Demo of Learning: Extended Writing (scored on both W.4 rubric and WH content accuracy)

Both standards get credit in Headrush. The student has one Demo, two standards satisfied.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The crosswalk is built on a simple observation: the best historical texts are also works of rhetoric, and analyzing them as arguments (ELA R.9) is exactly the same skill as analyzing them as historical evidence (WH.6_12.2–5). A student who can explain how Paine constructs his argument in Common Sense — what evidence he uses, what assumptions he makes, what counterarguments he dismisses — has simultaneously done the ELA work and the history work. This isn't a shortcut; it's a recognition that real-world thinking doesn't divide neatly into subject-area boxes. The most civic skill there is — evaluating whether a text's reasoning actually supports its claims — applies whether you're reading a primary source in history class or a news article on your phone. For the studio that uses crosswalk texts most directly, see WH-04: One Idea, 130 Years of War.

ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk · SDA Commons Wiki · South High School See also: Reading Library · ELA 9-10 Scales · WH Standards Overview

docs.title: Crosswalk: "Letter from Birmingham Jail" as Argumentative Mentor Text
docs.text: Text: "Letter from Birmingham Jail" by Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. (1963) Primary Entry: Letter from Birmingham Jail Crosswalk Type: ELA ↔ Social Studies ELA Standards: 9-10.R.9 · 9-10.W.4 SS Standards: US.6_12.3 + Unit 6 · US.6_12.4 + Unit 6

docs.title: Why This Text Works for Both
docs.text:
The Letter from Birmingham Jail is, structurally, a formal argumentative essay. King is responding to eight specific claims made by white Alabama clergy. His letter models every element of the W.4 argument writing standard:

  • Claim — explicitly stated ("...direct action is necessary")
  • Evidence — historical examples, philosophical authorities (Aquinas, Socrates, Jefferson)
  • Reasoning — logical development from premise to conclusion
  • Counterargument — direct quotation of the clergymen's position before refutation
  • Acknowledgment of complexity — King distinguishes between just and unjust laws without oversimplifying

At the same time, the letter is a primary source documenting: - The strategy of the Birmingham Campaign (US.6_12.3) - The political and economic structures that maintained segregation (US.6_12.4)

docs.title: Argument Map (for Student Use)
docs.text: Clergymen's Claim King's Response Evidence King Uses "This is not the time" "Justice too long delayed is justice denied" History of Black Americans waiting; Gandhi; Reinhold Niebuhr "Your actions are extreme" "Was not Jesus an extremist for love?" Jefferson, Lincoln, Jesus, Paul — all called extremists "You are outsiders here" Interconnectedness of all communities "Injustice anywhere is a threat to justice everywhere" "Obey the law" Just vs. unjust law distinction Augustine, Aquinas, natural law theory "Negotiations, not demonstrations" Demonstrations force negotiation History of Birmingham's broken promises

docs.title: ELA Use (W.4 — Reading as a Writer)
docs.text:
When using this for W.4, the driving question is:

How does King build an argument that his opponents can't easily dismiss?

docs.title: Mentor Text Analysis Activities
docs.text:
1. Claim Identification — Where does King state his central claim? Is it in the opening? Middle? End? Why?

  2. Evidence Catalog — King uses at least 4 types of evidence: philosophical authority, historical examples, biblical reference, and personal/experiential testimony. Have students catalog each type and evaluate which is most effective.

  3. Counterargument Technique — King quotes his opponents before refuting them. Why does this work? (It shows he's read them; it demonstrates fairness; it lets him reframe their argument on his terms.)

  4. Tone Shifts — The letter shifts from scholarly to personal to prophetic. Map where each shift occurs and why King makes it at that moment.

  5. Apply the Model — Students write their own "letter to someone who agrees with your goals but not your methods" on a contemporary issue they care about, using at least 3 of King's rhetorical techniques.

docs.title: Social Studies Use (US.6_12.3-4 + Unit 6)
docs.text:
When using this for SS, the driving question is:

What does this letter reveal about the strategy and philosophy of the Civil Rights Movement?

docs.title: Historical Analysis Activities
docs.text:
1. Mapping the Birmingham Campaign — Why Birmingham? Who was Bull Connor? What was the role of the SCLC? The letter was written in the context of Project C (Confrontation) — the deliberate strategy of provoking visible injustice.

  2. Economic Pressure — King explicitly mentions the role of economic boycotts in Birmingham. US.6_12.4 asks students to analyze economic dimensions of historical change. How did Black consumer power function as political leverage?

  3. The "White Moderate" Section — This is King's most politically pointed argument: that the white moderate "who prefers the absence of tension to the presence of justice" is more of an obstacle than the open racist. Students discuss: Is this still true in contemporary contexts?

  4. The Just/Unjust Law Framework — Have students apply King's framework (a just law lifts human personality; an unjust law degrades it) to other historical and contemporary laws.

docs.title: Combined Studio: "Letters That Changed History"
docs.text:
Essential Question: What does it take to change a mind that's made up?

Standards: US.6_12.3 + Unit 6 · 9-10.W.4 (ELA)

Studio Arc: - Week 1: Read letter; identify argumentative structure; map King's moves - Week 2: Historical context research — Birmingham Campaign, Project C, Bull Connor - Week 3: Analyze King's most effective argument (W.4 analytical essay draft) - Week 4: Compare with a second historical open letter (Emile Zola's "J'Accuse," or Wollstonecraft's Vindication) - Week 5: Draft original argumentative letter (contemporary issue, King's structure) - Week 6: Demo + Deliverable

Demo of Learning: Extended Writing — W.4 scored on both argument quality and historical content accuracy Deliverable: Published argumentative letter in SDA Commons

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Letter from Birmingham Jail
  • Common Sense + W.4 Crosswalk
  • Writing Standards
  • Unit 6 — Civil Rights

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
MLK wrote the Letter from Birmingham Jail in the margins of a newspaper, on scraps of paper, in a jail cell, responding to white clergymen who told him to wait. It is arguably the most precisely argued document in American civil rights history — and it was written under maximum constraint with zero resources. Using it as a W.4 mentor text teaches students that argument is constructed under constraint, which is the condition most real-world writing happens in. The letter's central distinction between just and unjust laws — and its argument that individuals have a moral obligation to disobey unjust ones — is still the governing framework for every civil disobedience debate from climate protests to campus activism. For the full text and historical context, see Letter from Birmingham Jail in the Reading Library; for the studio that engages most directly with the Civil Rights Movement, see USH-01: When the System Fails.

Reading Library Crosswalk · SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

docs.title: Beloved (1987)
docs.text: Author: Toni Morrison Type: Literary Text · Novel Source: Copyright © 1987 Toni Morrison — school library copy required Full Text: Available via Open Library — free digital borrowing · school library copy required for extended reading Era/Period: USH Unit 1 — Reconstruction era (set 1873 Cincinnati, Ohio; based on events of 1856) USH Standards: US.6_12.4 · US.6_12.5 · US.6_12.3 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.8 · 9-10.W.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Beloved is Toni Morrison's 1987 Pulitzer Prize–winning novel about Sethe, an enslaved woman who escaped to Ohio, and the ghost of her daughter Beloved — whom Sethe killed as a baby rather than allow her to be taken back into slavery. The novel is set in 1873 Cincinnati, with extensive memories of the Kentucky plantation called Sweet Home. It is simultaneously a ghost story, a historical novel about slavery's psychological legacy, and a meditation on what slavery required of those who survived it.

Morrison wrote Beloved to fill a silence. She has said that slavery's horrors are "unspeakable" — that American culture had not fully reckoned with what slavery was and what it cost the people inside it. The novel is her attempt to make the reader sit with that cost, to refuse the historical abstraction ("millions of enslaved people") and insist on the human particular: one woman, one baby, one impossible choice. Beloved is dedicated "To the Sixty Million and more" — those who died in the Middle Passage and in slavery.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Beloved is copyrighted. Key passages below are provided for study and analysis. For the full novel, see the access options at the bottom of this section.

docs.title: The Sweet Home Men — What Slavery Looked Like
docs.text:
Part One

"Sad as it was that she did not know where her children were buried or what they looked like if alive, fact was she knew more about them than she knew about herself, having watched them every one of her days. Had she been able to see them scattered and grown, she might have felt the pain more. Instead she felt the muffled shout of being where they were not... In the place where long grass spread, and trees that were not trees at all but the shape of trees."

Paul D arrives at 124 Bluestone Road and finds Sethe. Their reunion opens with the weight of Sweet Home — the Kentucky plantation where they were both enslaved. Morrison's description of the "Sweet Home men" (the enslaved workers there) is deliberately ironic: the place was called Sweet Home; it was not.

docs.title: Rememory — The Past That Cannot Be Left Behind
docs.text:
Part One — Sethe and Denver

"I was talking about time. It's so hard for me to believe in it. Some things go. Pass on. Some things just stay. I used to think it was my rememory. You know. Some things you forget. Other things you never do. But it's not. Places, places are still there. If a house burns down, it's gone, but the place — the picture of it — stays, and not just in my rememory, but out there, in the world."

Denver asks if that means the picture is still there even if Sethe is not.

"Oh yes. Oh yes, yes, yes. Someday you be walking down the road and you hear something or see something going on. So clear. And you think it's you thinking it up. A thought picture. But no. It's when you bump into a rememory that belongs to somebody else."

This passage is the heart of the novel's argument about trauma. "Rememory" is Morrison's word for the way the past exists as a physical presence in the world — not just as personal memory but as a shared historical reality that can be stumbled into by anyone. Students connect this to US.6_12.5: the consequences of slavery did not end when slavery ended. The past is still out there.

docs.title: The Woodshed — Sethe's Choice
docs.text:
Part One — the central crisis of the novel

Morrison does not narrate the woodshed scene abstractly. Baby Suggs arrives. Stamp Paid recounts what happened. Schoolteacher's nephews arrive to reclaim Sethe under the Fugitive Slave Act. What Sethe does — what she is found doing — is presented fully, not summarized. Morrison refuses to make the reader comfortable.

The passage cannot be excerpted here without removing the weight the novel places on it by approaching it slowly, through multiple perspectives. This is one of the passages that must be read in the full text. What you need to know to engage it: Sethe chose to kill her infant daughter rather than allow her to be taken back into slavery. The novel asks the reader to sit with this choice without resolving it into simple condemnation or simple justification.

"She just flew. Collected every bit of life she had made, all the parts of her that were precious and fine and beautiful, and carried, pushed, dragged them through the veil, out, away, over there where no one could hurt them."

This is how Paul D processes what Sethe did. Morrison makes both the horror and the love fully present at once — which is the novel's central achievement.

Get the full text: - Open Library — free digital borrowing (free account, borrow like a library) - No account? Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.8 — Morrison's narrative structure is fragmented and non-linear because the characters themselves cannot bear to remember in sequence. Students analyze how form mirrors content: the novel's structural fragmentation is itself a portrait of trauma. The ghost Beloved functions as a literary device: she embodies the past that will not stay buried, the children who were not free, the violence that haunts the present. Morrison's prose is lyrical and dense — close reading of individual passages is essential.
  • W.4 — "Sethe's choice was an act of love, not a crime." "The novel argues that slavery's true horror is what it required of the enslaved, not just what it did to them." Both are strong W.4 arguments requiring careful textual evidence and engagement with USH historical context.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• US.6_12.4 — Beloved is set in the immediate aftermath of slavery, in the Reconstruction period (1873). Students encounter the social and economic conditions of Black life after emancipation: the psychological legacy of enslavement, the threat of re-enslavement under the Fugitive Slave Act, the fragility of freedom in a still-hostile North. US.6_12.4 asks how social development shapes individual lives — Morrison answers with searing specificity.
  • US.6_12.5 — The novel is built on the causal chain of slavery: the Middle Passage → chattel slavery → the Fugitive Slave Act → Sethe's escape → her impossible choice → the haunting. Students trace causes and consequences at the individual level, connecting to the historical record.
  • US.6_12.3 — The Fugitive Slave Act (1850) is a political system acting on a person's life in the most extreme way imaginable. Sethe's choice is a direct response to the political system that denied enslaved people any legal personhood. Students analyze US.6_12.3 (political systems and their effects on people) through this specific, documented case.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Beloved The name inscribed on the baby's gravestone; also the name of the ghost/young woman who appears in the novel Sweet Home The Kentucky plantation where Sethe was enslaved — a name Morrison uses with full irony; the description of the "Sweet Home men" makes clear what the place actually was The Fugitive Slave Act (1850) Federal law requiring all citizens, including those in free states, to assist in capturing and returning escaped enslaved people to their owners — the law Sethe was running from Margaret Garner The real historical person on whom Sethe is based; Garner escaped slavery in 1856 and killed her daughter rather than allow her to be returned to the enslaver Reconstruction The period from 1865–1877 following the Civil War during which Congress attempted to reorganize the South and integrate formerly enslaved people into citizenship; the novel is set in its aftermath Trauma Psychological damage resulting from severe experiences; the novel depicts how slavery's trauma persisted in survivors long after physical escape

docs.title: Key Passages
docs.text:
The dedication:

"Sixty Million and more"

Morrison's dedication is itself an argument: the novel is not just about one woman. It is testimony for all those who did not survive.

On the nature of memory:

"She had to do something with her hands... So she sat in the chair... and did what she had not done in eighteen years: thought about the best bedding plan... Her brain was not interested in the future. Loaded with the past and hungry for more, it left her no room to imagine, let alone plan for, the next day."

Morrison depicts trauma not as dramatic flashback but as the constant occupation of the present moment by the past — students connect this to US.6_12.4 (what slavery's aftermath cost individuals).

On the choice: The novel requires students to sit with the scene in the woodshed — Morrison does not describe it abstractly. What Sethe did, and why, is not explained away but presented in all its horror and love. Students must read it and form their own judgment.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "What Slavery Required" — Students identify 3–4 specific moments in the novel where the enslaved characters are forced to make choices that no free person would ever face. They write a W.3 explanatory piece: what does each moment reveal about what slavery required of people beyond the physical — psychologically, morally, relationally? They connect to US.6_12.4: how does this deepen the historical understanding of slavery's social impact?

  2. "The Fugitive Slave Act and Sethe's Choice" — Students research the Fugitive Slave Act and the case of Margaret Garner (IR.1–5) and then write a W.4 argument: given the legal and political conditions Sethe faced (US.6_12.3), how should we judge her choice? This requires students to read R.8 carefully (what does Morrison want the reader to feel and think?) and to engage the historical record.

  3. "The Past That Won't Stay Dead" — Beloved is a ghost story, but Morrison has said every element of Beloved's haunting is rooted in historical fact, not the supernatural. Students write a W.4 argument: what does it mean that the past haunts the present in this novel? How does Morrison's use of the supernatural connect to US.6_12.5's framework for understanding historical causes and effects? How does slavery's legacy continue to "haunt" American life?

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Beloved is about the specific, unresolved trauma of American slavery — how it haunts not just survivors but their children, their communities, and the national memory. Morrison's central argument is that you cannot heal what you refuse to remember. Sethe cannot move forward until she confronts what she did and why, and the community cannot heal until it stops trying to protect itself from the ghost of what happened. That argument applies to every conversation about racial injustice, historical trauma, and collective memory: the question of whether acknowledging the full truth of what happened is necessary for any real reconciliation, or whether moving forward requires setting the past aside. Beloved says the past doesn't cooperate with being set aside — it comes back as a ghost and disrupts everything until it's dealt with. The debate about reparations, about teaching the history of slavery in schools, about monuments and memorials — all of it is an argument about whether Sethe's choice (confront the ghost) or Denver's initial choice (pretend it isn't there) is the right model.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:

  • W.4 Argument Writing Skinny
  • Narrative of Frederick Douglass — Primary source memoir of slavery from the same era; compare documentary testimony with Morrison's fictional reckoning
  • CrashCourse Literature — Beloved
