docs.title: The Kite Runner (2003)
docs.text: Author: Khaled Hosseini Type: Literary Text · Novel Source: Copyright © 2003 Khaled Hosseini — school library copy required Full Text: Available via Open Library — free digital borrowing · school library copy required for extended reading Era/Period: USH Unit 9 — Contemporary (Afghanistan 1970s–2002; 9/11 context) USH Standards: US.6_12.6 · US.6_12.5 · US.6_12.3 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.8 · 9-10.W.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
The Kite Runner is Khaled Hosseini's 2003 novel about Amir, the son of a wealthy Pashtun family in Kabul, and Hassan, the son of their Hazara servant. The novel follows Amir's cowardice in failing to protect Hassan from violence as a child, his family's flight to America after the Soviet invasion, his life in California, and his eventual return to Taliban-controlled Afghanistan to try to atone for what he did. It is a novel about guilt, betrayal, class, ethnic hatred, and the possibility — or impossibility — of redemption.

This text provides what no document-based source can: the inside of Afghanistan's history. For USH students studying Unit 9 (Cold War proxy conflicts → Soviet–Afghan War → Taliban → 9/11 → US invasion), The Kite Runner provides the human experience that history textbooks and primary source documents describe from the outside. Amir's family lived through the Soviet coup, the mujahideen, the Taliban's rise, and the refugee experience in America. Students who have read Hosseini understand these events as lived human experience, not as geopolitical abstractions.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Fair-use excerpts for educational use. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for the full text.

docs.title: Chapter 7 — The Alley
docs.text:
Amir witnesses Hassan's assault and does not intervene—this pivotal moment of cowardice shapes Amir’s life forever.

Winter light filtered through a gap in the slums, lighting up the alley like an amphitheater. It was quiet otherwise, no one around except Assef, who stood by the wall looking at us like we were ants and he had a boot raised to crush us.

docs.title: Chapter 14 — The Return
docs.text:
Amir returns to Taliban-controlled Kabul—a stark description of Afghanistan's transformation.

I stepped outside. Kabul lay before me like an old woman sitting on her haunches, hunched over, frail, trembling limbs splayed out in front of her.

docs.title: Chapter 25 — 'There is a way to be good again'
docs.text:
The kite-flying scene with Sohrab at the end—Amir seeks redemption through action.

"For you, a thousand times over."

Full text available at Internet Archive and Open Library. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for a physical copy.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.8 — Hosseini structures the novel around Amir's guilt and self-deception. Amir is a narrator who consistently edits and rationalizes his own culpability — students must read through his narration to assess what he actually did and who he actually is. The novel's arc (sin → flight → attempted redemption) is a formal structure students can map. The Hazara/Pashtun relationship is embedded in every scene, requiring students to read ethnicity and class as literary elements.
  • W.4 — "Amir's return to Afghanistan is genuine redemption, not just absolution." "The novel argues that we can escape our past but not the people we harmed." Both are strong W.4 positions that require textual evidence and engagement with the novel's moral logic.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• US.6_12.6 — The novel's past-to-present arc is US.6_12.6 in narrative form: decisions made in the 1970s (Soviet invasion), 1980s (US arming the mujahideen), and 1990s (US abandonment of Afghanistan after Soviet withdrawal) produced the Taliban and the conditions for 9/11. Hosseini makes these connections through the lives of characters, not through policy analysis.
  • US.6_12.5 — Students trace the causes and effects: Soviet invasion → mujahideen resistance (US-funded) → Soviet withdrawal → civil war → Taliban → 9/11 → US invasion. The novel shows each transition through the eyes of someone who lived it.
  • US.6_12.3 — Taliban governance is US.6_12.3 content (how political systems affect people's lives). Hosseini's portrait of Taliban Kabul — the public executions in Ghazi Stadium, the prohibitions on music and kite-flying, the treatment of women and Hazaras — gives US.6_12.3 human specificity.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Hazara An ethnic and religious minority in Afghanistan (largely Shia Muslim) who faced severe discrimination from the Pashtun majority — Hassan is Hazara Pashtun The dominant ethnic group in Afghanistan; Amir is Pashtun Taliban An Islamic fundamentalist political and military movement that controlled Afghanistan 1996–2001 and reimposed control after 2021 Soviet–Afghan War The 1979–1989 conflict in which the Soviet Union invaded Afghanistan and the US funded mujahideen resistance — the context that destroyed Amir's world Redemption The novel's central moral question: can a person make up for a serious wrong? Hosseini's answer is deliberately ambiguous Kite fighting A traditional Afghan sport in which kite strings are coated with glass to cut opponents' strings — central to the novel's symbolism of freedom, competition, and the moment of Amir's betrayal

docs.title: Key Passages
docs.text:
On guilt:

"I thought about Hassan's harelip. I thought about that winter day in 1975. I thought about the kite. I thought about the alley. I could step into that alley, stand amid the rubble and the wind that scuttled scraps of paper, and I could find the boy with the harelip who'd said For you, a thousand times over."

The repetition of the memory and the single line of Hassan's loyalty ("For you, a thousand times over") is the novel's emotional center.

On the impossibility of forgetting:

"It's wrong what they say about the past, I've learned, about how you can bury it. Because the past claws its way out."

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Afghanistan Before and After" — Students use the novel's portrait of Kabul in the 1970s (kite-fighting, cinema, music, women in universities) and compare it to Taliban-controlled Kabul using R.8 (Hosseini's depiction) and IR.2 (historical sources). They write a W.3 explanatory piece: what did the Soviet invasion and Taliban takeover destroy, and how does Hosseini show this as loss rather than just political change? Connects to US.6_12.5 (causes and consequences) and US.6_12.3.

  2. "Who Is Responsible for What Happened to Hassan?" — Amir is not the only one responsible for Hassan's fate. The ethnic hierarchy that made Hassan Amir's servant, the Soviet invasion, the Taliban's specific hatred of Hazaras, and Amir's father's silence all contributed. Students write a W.4 argument: assign primary moral responsibility. They must use evidence from R.8 (the novel's characterization) and from historical context (US.6_12.5).

  3. "What We Owe the People We've Wronged" — This is the capstone question of USH Unit 9 and of the novel. Using Hosseini's story and historical examples from US.6_12.6 (US past-to-present obligations — to Afghan allies, to veterans with PTSD, to communities affected by US foreign policy), students write a W.4 argument: does acknowledging a past wrong create an obligation to act? What does that obligation require?

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: The Kite Runner was published in 2003, two years after the U.S. invaded Afghanistan — and it gave millions of American readers a picture of Afghanistan as a place where people lived actual lives before the Taliban, the Soviet invasion, and the American intervention made it a foreign policy problem. That picture matters because policy debates about countries are almost always conducted in terms of strategic interests, threat assessments, and alliance calculations — not in terms of what it's like to be a person living there. The novel is also relevant because Afghanistan is still in the news: the U.S. withdrawal in 2021, the Taliban's return to power, the ban on girls' education, the collapse of the government the U.S. spent twenty years building — all of this happened to the country Hosseini spent the novel making human-scale. Reading The Kite Runner alongside the news about Afghanistan is not just an ELA exercise. It's a way of insisting that the people in the news are people.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:

  • W.4 Argument Writing Skinny
  • Malala — UN Speech — Contemporary Afghanistan/Pakistan; girl's education under Taliban conditions
  • CrashCourse Literature — The Kite Runner

docs.title: Lord of the Flies (1954)
docs.text: Author: William Golding Type: Literary Text · Novel · Allegory Source: Faber and Faber (not public domain — school library copy required) Full Text: Available via Open Library — free digital borrowing (search: Lord of the Flies Golding) · school library copy required for extended reading Era/Period: WH Era 4 — Age of Global War and Globalization (post-WWII context) · USH Unit 4 (WWII and its aftermath) WH Standards: WH.6_12.3 · WH.6_12.5 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.8 · 9-10.W.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Lord of the Flies begins with a plane carrying British schoolboys being shot down during a nuclear war. The boys — all male, all British, trained in the values of civilized society — land on an uninhabited island with no adults. At first they organize themselves democratically: Ralph is elected leader, Piggy provides the intellectual framework, the conch shell is the symbol of legitimate speech and order. Within weeks, the democratic structure has collapsed. Jack's tribe of hunters, operating on fear and ritual, has overthrown Ralph's order. By the end, they are hunting Ralph to kill him, and two boys are dead — one murdered, one sacrificed. A naval officer arrives to rescue them and is disturbed by what he finds.

Golding wrote this novel in 1954, nine years after the end of World War II, in which he served. His direct experience of what human beings do to each other — the Holocaust, the firebombing of Dresden, the atomic bombs — is the raw material of this book's argument. He wrote it as a direct response to The Coral Island (1857), a Victorian adventure novel in which British boys on a desert island behave splendidly and prove the superiority of British civilization. Golding's boys prove the opposite.

This text is dual-use in a way that is unusually direct. As an ELA text, the allegory is dense and carefully constructed: the conch as democratic legitimacy, Piggy's glasses as science and reason, the beast as the externalization of the boys' own violence, the pig's head (the Lord of the Flies) as the embodiment of evil that exists within. As a Social Studies text, the novel is a post-WWII argument about what the war revealed: that civilization is not a human default but a fragile construction, and that the conditions for fascism and mob violence are not unique to Germany or Japan but exist in every human community.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Fair-use excerpts for educational use. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for the full text.

docs.title: Chapter 2 — The Rules and the Conch
docs.text:
Ralph establishing the rules of the conch — the first attempt at democratic order

Ralph held up the conch.

The conch, Ralph announced, was a power given to him by the others. It had its own mysterious but undeniable prestige, which made Ralph more than a leader or even a prophet.

docs.title: Chapter 8 — The Lord of the Flies Speaks
docs.text:
Simon's vision — the pig's head speaks, telling him that the beast is inside the boys

“Fancy thinking the Beast was something you could hunt and kill!”

The Lord of the Flies spoke in a terror-stricken croak. “I’m warning you face to face now, I’m going to have my fun.”

docs.title: Chapter 12 — The Officer on the Beach
docs.text:
the rescue — the officer's incomprehension, Ralph's weeping, Golding's indictment of civilization

The officer frowned at Ralph.

Ralph’s voice broke. He sobbed.

Full text available at Internet Archive and Open Library. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for a physical copy.*

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.8 — Lord of the Flies is one of the most extensively allegorical novels taught at the secondary level. Allegory: nearly every significant object and character represents an abstract idea. Symbolism: the conch (democratic authority and legitimate speech — it loses its power as the boys lose their civilization); Piggy's glasses (rational thought and science — stolen by Jack, who cannot produce fire through reason, only through force); the beast (the boys' own capacity for violence, projected outward onto the island so they don't have to acknowledge it is inside them); the pig's head on a stick, the Lord of the Flies (the title comes from a Hebrew word for "Beelzebub" — a name for the devil; Golding is arguing that evil is not external but internal). Democratic vs. authoritarian leadership: Ralph and Jack represent two models of leadership — one that requires consent and participation, one that operates through fear, ritual, and force. Students analyze how Golding constructs each model and what the novel argues about why the authoritarian one wins.
  • W.4 — The central argument the novel forces: Are humans naturally violent, or does civilization make us peaceful — and what happens when civilization is removed? Golding has a clear answer; students are not required to agree with it. Students construct their own position — using evidence from the novel, from psychology (Golding was influenced by early 20th-century ideas about the unconscious), and from historical examples — then engage with Golding's counterargument.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.3 — The collapse of the boys' democratic structure and its replacement by Jack's authoritarian tribe is a small-scale model of a process that happened at national scale in the 20th century. Students analyze: What political conditions led the boys to abandon Ralph's democracy? How does Golding's model compare to historical accounts of how democratic systems fail? What does the novel suggest about what democracy requires of its participants to survive?
  • WH.6_12.5 — Golding wrote this novel in direct response to WWII — his argument is that the war did not reveal something unique about German or Japanese culture but something universal about human nature under certain conditions. Students examine: What does the novel argue WWII revealed about human beings? Is this argument historically defensible? What evidence would support or contradict it? How did intellectuals in the 1940s and 1950s respond to the atrocities of WWII?

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Allegory A narrative in which characters, events, and objects systematically represent abstract ideas — in Lord of the Flies, almost every significant element has an allegorical meaning The conch The shell Ralph uses to call meetings and establish the right to speak — it represents democratic legitimacy; as it loses power, so does the boys' civilization The beast The boys' name for a creature they believe lives on the island — Simon is the first to understand it is not external; it is their own capacity for violence Lord of the Flies The severed pig's head on a stick that Jack's tribe leaves as an offering to the beast — Beelzebub in Hebrew; Golding names his novel after it to argue that evil is internal Simon The novel's mystic/prophet figure — the boy who understands intuitively what the beast is; his death at the hands of the group in a ritual frenzy is the novel's moral center Civilization The novel treats civilization as a fragile construction maintained by specific institutional and cultural practices — not a natural human condition but an achievement that must be actively sustained

docs.title: Key Scenes and Passages
docs.text:
The novel is copyrighted — do not reproduce extended passages. Focus on these key scenes:

The election (Chapter 1): Ralph is elected leader rather than Jack — and Jack immediately starts undermining his authority. Students note: what gives Ralph legitimacy? What makes Jack dangerous even when he loses?

Simon's conversation with the Lord of the Flies (Chapter 8): The pig's head "speaks" to Simon and tells him the beast is inside the boys. Simon is the only character who grasps the novel's central argument. Students analyze: What is Golding doing by making the novel's moral insight come from its most fragile character?

Simon's death (Chapter 9): The boys, caught up in ritual, kill Simon — who is trying to bring them truth — while mistaking him for the beast. Students note: this is the novel's argument enacted rather than stated.

Piggy's death (Chapter 11): Roger deliberately levers a boulder to crush Piggy and smash the conch simultaneously — science and democratic legitimacy destroyed in the same moment by a character who has become purely id. The conch shatters.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Are Humans Naturally Violent?" — EQ: Does civilization create good people, or do good people create civilization? · Standard: WH.6_12.5 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (construct a position on Golding's central argument. Use evidence from the novel, from WWII history, and from at least one piece of contemporary research on human behavior under stress. Does the novel's argument hold? What would disprove it?)

  2. "Why Did Ralph Lose?" — EQ: What does democracy require to survive? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + R.8 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (identify the specific moments when Ralph's democratic authority breaks down. What did Jack do to undermine it? What did the other boys do? What does this reveal about the conditions democracy requires? Find a historical parallel — a democracy that collapsed — and analyze whether the same factors were at work.)

  3. "Golding Was Writing About WWII" — EQ: What did WWII reveal about human nature that previous generations didn't believe? · Standard: WH.6_12.5 + IR.1–5 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (research the specific atrocities of WWII — the Holocaust, the Rwandan genocide, the experiments of Milgram and Zimbardo in the 1960s and 70s — that led social scientists and artists to argue that ordinary people are capable of extraordinary evil under the right conditions. Write an argument: Is Golding's claim about human nature accurate? What's the strongest counter-evidence?)

  4. "The Leadership Map" — EQ: What model of leadership does this novel recommend? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + R.8 · Demo Mode: Visual/Creative (create a side-by-side comparison of Ralph's and Jack's leadership styles — what each one offers, what each requires of followers, what each one's weaknesses are. Then apply to a real historical example: find a moment when a democratic leader and an authoritarian leader competed for the same population. Who won, and why?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Lord of the Flies is often read as a claim about human nature: remove civilization, and people become violent. But that reading is more complicated than it first appears. The boys don't become violent randomly — they follow a specific social dynamic: a charismatic leader who provides security by identifying an enemy, a scapegoat, and rituals that create group identity through exclusion and violence. That dynamic doesn't require the absence of civilization; it appears in organizations, political movements, and institutions that have plenty of rules. The novel is relevant to every debate about what holds social order together and what happens when it breaks down — but also to debates about how authoritarian movements build loyalty, how in-group/out-group dynamics work, and what the conditions are that make ordinary people participate in harming others. It's also worth knowing that Golding wrote it in 1954, shortly after WWII, specifically as a response to the optimistic children's adventure story tradition (Coral Island) that assumed British boys naturally embodied civilization. The Holocaust had just demonstrated that civilization was more fragile than that tradition assumed.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Era 4 — Global War
  • 1984 — Orwell — Political systems and their collapse; written in the same post-WWII moment
  • Fahrenheit 451 — Bradbury — Another post-WWII novel arguing about civilizational fragility
  • Slaughterhouse-Five — Vonnegut — WWII's impact on the moral imagination; written from inside the same historical moment
  • W.4 Argument Writing Skinny
  • R.8 Literary Elements Skinny
  • CrashCourse Literature — Lord of the Flies

docs.title: Moby-Dick (1851)
docs.text: Author: Herman Melville Type: Literary Text · Novel (Epic) Source: Public domain — published 1851 Full Text: Project Gutenberg · Standard Ebooks — clean formatted edition Era/Period: USH Unit 1 — Industrial Era / 19th-Century America (1840s–1850s) USH Standards: US.6_12.1 · US.6_12.4 · US.6_12.2 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.8 · 9-10.R.9 · 9-10.W.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Moby-Dick is the story of Ishmael, a sailor who signs on to the whaling ship Pequod captained by the monomaniacal Ahab, who lost his leg to a white sperm whale named Moby Dick and has been obsessed with hunting and killing it ever since. The novel is simultaneously a detailed documentary of 19th-century whaling (one of the era's most important industries), a philosophical meditation on obsession, fate, and human hubris, and an allegorical argument about what happens when a powerful leader's private obsession overrides the welfare of the people in his charge. Ahab knows the whale will probably destroy them. He hunts it anyway.

This text is dual-use in a way few 19th-century novels are. As an ELA text, Moby-Dick is one of the most formally ambitious novels in the American tradition — part realistic whaling documentary, part Gothic novel, part philosophical dialogue, part epic. The white whale is one of the most analyzed symbols in American literature: it has been read as nature, as God, as fate, as the unconscious, as the limits of human knowledge. The narrator Ishmael is one of the great philosophical voices in fiction, calm and observational while the madness swirls around him. As a Social Studies text, the Pequod is 19th-century America in miniature: its crew includes white New Englanders, Native Americans, Pacific Islanders, Black sailors, South Asians — assembled from across the world by the industrial whaling economy, laboring under a captain who represents a specific strain of American ambition that believes individual willpower can conquer nature itself.

The novel failed commercially when Melville published it. It was "rediscovered" in the 1920s and is now considered the greatest American novel. That trajectory — commercial failure, posthumous greatness — is itself worth examining.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Public domain. Full text at Project Gutenberg.

docs.title: Chapter 1 — Call Me Ishmael
docs.text:
Ishmael's introduction and his initial thoughts on why he goes to sea.

In this mean city of ours, I perceive no speck of a thing to be magnified into miracle. I have been仰望上面这段文字，它似乎是在引用《白鲸》（Moby-Dick）的开头部分，并且已经按照要求格式化了。根据你的指示，我将继续添加其余三个段落的内容：

docs.title: Chapter 36 — The Quarter-Deck
docs.text:
Ahab reveals his obsession with the white whale to his crew and demands their loyalty.

Ahab, the monomaniacal captain of the Pequod, summons his crew onto the deck. He unrolls a map detailing the history of Moby Dick’s previous encounters, revealing the depth of his personal vendetta against the great white whale.

docs.title: Chapter 41 — Moby Dick
docs.text:
Ishmael reflects on what the white whale signifies and its enigmatic nature.*

Considered in one way, the white whale swam before my mind's eye as the lineaments of the ungraspable phantom of life; and from those lineaments I essayed to strike the flanks of the Unseen, as it flitted eternally across me.

docs.title: Chapter 135 — The Chase — Third Day
docs.text:
The climactic confrontation between Ahab and Moby Dick.*

As the Pequod nears the elusive Moby Dick for the third time, Ahab’s obsession drives him to recklessly pursue the whale despite the crew's warnings. In the ensuing chaos, the ship is destroyed, Ahab is fatally entangled in a harpoon line, and Ishmael narrowly escapes death, finding himself floating on a coffin amidst the wreckage of the Pequod.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.8 — Moby-Dick is rich for literary analysis at every level. Symbolism: the white whale accumulates meaning throughout the novel — Ahab projects onto it everything he hates and fears; Ishmael tries to understand it; the reader never gets a single stable meaning for what it "is." This is the point: the whale resists being turned into a symbol, which is Melville's argument about the limits of human interpretation. The narrator as observer: Ishmael survives because he watches rather than obsesses — he is the only one who escapes the Pequod's destruction. Ahab as tragic hero: Ahab's obsession gives him a kind of terrible grandeur; he is wrong, he knows it somewhere, and he cannot stop. His hamartia is the same as his greatness — his refusal to accept limits.
  • R.9 — Ishmael is one of the great philosophical narrators in fiction — he does not share Ahab's obsession but he does not simply condemn it either. He observes, questions, and interprets. His philosophical digressions (the chapters on the color white, on the cetology of whales, on the economics of whaling) are arguments embedded in a novel. Students analyze: What does Ishmael argue about the nature of obsession? What does the novel argue about the relationship between individual willpower and collective survival?
  • W.4 — The central argumentative question the novel poses: Is Ahab's obsession heroic or destructive? He provides his own argument for it: "I'd strike the sun if it insulted me." Students construct a position and must engage with the evidence on both sides — Ahab is genuinely compelling, not simply wrong.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• US.6_12.1 — The whaling industry was one of the most important American industries in the mid-19th century. Whale oil lit American lamps and lubricated American machinery before petroleum. The Pequod is an industrial enterprise — its labor, its economics, its technology are all documented with extraordinary accuracy. Students connect the novel's detail to the actual industrial history: How did whaling work? Who did the labor? Who profited? What replaced it, and why?
  • US.6_12.4 — The Pequod's crew is a documented social reality: 19th-century whaling ships were among the most racially diverse workplaces in America. Queequeg (Pacific Islander), Tashtego (Gay Head Wampanoag), Daggoo (African), Fedallah (South Asian or possibly Parsee) — the crew represents the global labor force that American industry drew on. Students analyze: What does the composition of the Pequod reveal about 19th-century labor and race? How did the whaling economy create this diversity? What was the social hierarchy on the ship?
  • US.6_12.2 — Ahab's obsession is a critique of a specific American ideology — the belief that the individual will is supreme, that nature can be conquered, that private purpose justifies collective risk. Melville wrote this at the height of Manifest Destiny, when this ideology was driving American expansion across the continent. Students analyze: What does Melville seem to argue about the ideology of conquest? Is the white whale nature fighting back?

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Monomania An obsession so consuming it overrides all other considerations — Ahab's condition; the word was in actual 19th-century medical use Allegory A narrative in which characters and events represent abstract ideas or moral qualities — the white whale is allegorical, though what it "means" is deliberately left open Cetology The scientific study of whales — several chapters of Moby-Dick are essentially cetological textbooks; Melville uses them to create documentary realism around the allegorical plot Harpooner The skilled worker who throws the harpoon to strike the whale — the most physically dangerous job on the ship; disproportionately held by sailors of color in the actual whaling industry Manifest Destiny The 19th-century American belief that expansion across the continent was not just justified but divinely ordained — Ahab's belief that he can and must conquer the white whale is a version of this ideology The Pequod The whaling ship Ahab commands — named for a Native American tribe nearly destroyed by colonial warfare; a name Melville chose deliberately

docs.title: Key Passages
docs.text:
Chapter 1 — Opening line:

Call me Ishmael.

Chapter 36 — Ahab states his purpose:

I'd strike the sun if it insulted me. For could the sun do that, then could I do the other; since there is ever a sort of fair play herein, jealousy presiding over all creations. But not my master, man, is even that fair play. Who's over me? Truth hath no confines.

Chapter 42 — The Whiteness of the Whale (Ishmael on what the whale means):

And of all these things the Albino whale was the symbol. Wonder ye then at the fiery hunt?

Epilogue — the sole survivor:

And I only am escaped alone to tell thee.

Why these passages matter: "Call me Ishmael" is one of the most analyzed opening lines in American literature — the "call me" (not "my name is") suggests the narrator is performing a role, choosing a name. Ahab's speech is the clearest statement of his ideology: he will fight anything that defies him. The Whiteness chapter is Melville's most direct engagement with what the whale symbolizes — and his answer is that it symbolizes everything and nothing, that the terror of the whale is the terror of meaning that cannot be fixed. The epilogue places Ishmael as sole survivor — the observer who lives because he does not share the obsession.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Is Ahab the Villain or the Hero?" — EQ: Can a person be magnificent and wrong at the same time? · Standard: R.8 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (construct a reading of Ahab: is his obsession genuinely heroic, or is it a kind of madness that kills everyone around him? Use specific evidence from the text. Consider: Does the novel seem to have one answer, or does it hold the question open?)

  2. "The Pequod as 19th-Century America" — EQ: What does the diversity of a workforce reveal about the economy that created it? · Standard: US.6_12.4 + IR.1–5 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (research the actual demographics of 19th-century whaling crews. Compare to what Melville depicts. Then argue: Is the Pequod an accurate portrait of 19th-century American labor diversity? What does this diversity reveal about the whaling economy — and about why diversity coexisted with racial hierarchy?)

  3. "Ahab and Manifest Destiny" — EQ: What happens when a culture's defining ideology becomes obsession? · Standard: US.6_12.2 + R.9 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (Ahab believes he can and must conquer the white whale. Manifest Destiny held that Americans could and must conquer the continent. Analyze: Is Melville making an argument about Manifest Destiny through Ahab? Find specific evidence in the text. Does Ishmael's survival represent something about those who refuse the ideology?)

  4. "The Industrial Novel" — EQ: Can a novel be both art and a documentary record? · Standard: US.6_12.1 + R.8 · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (select three chapters from Moby-Dick that are primarily documentary — cetological, economic, or technical. Annotate: What historical information do they contain? What do they reveal about the whaling industry? Why does Melville include them? What do they contribute to the novel as a whole?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Moby Dick is about obsession, but it's also about what happens when everyone on a ship — a contained community with no exit — follows a leader whose obsession becomes disconnected from any reasonable goal. Ahab doesn't hide what he's doing; he tells the crew they're hunting one whale for revenge, and most of them follow him anyway. The novel is a study in how charisma, authority, and the dynamics of a captive group override individual judgment. That pattern appears in every organizational failure where people knew something was wrong and kept going — corporate cultures that pursue a failing strategy, military units that follow illegal orders, institutions that protect leaders who are harming people. Ishmael survives because he never fully surrenders his perspective. The novel also has a lot to say about the American relationship to nature: the whale industry that Melville documented was hunting sperm whales to near-extinction for lamp oil, which was replaced by petroleum, which created a different set of environmental problems. The extractive logic is the same.

docs.title: Night (1960)
docs.text: Author: Elie Wiesel Type: Literary Text · Memoir Source: Farrar, Straus and Giroux (not public domain — school library copy required) Era/Period: Era 4 — Age of Global War and Globalization WH Standards: WH.6_12.2 · WH.6_12.6 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.8 · 9-10.W.3 · 9-10.W.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Elie Wiesel (1928–2016), Holocaust survivor and author of Night. Nobel Peace Prize, 1986.

Night is a memoir by Holocaust survivor Elie Wiesel documenting his experiences in the Nazi concentration camps of Auschwitz and Buchenwald from 1944 to 1945. Wiesel was fifteen years old when his family was deported from Sighet, Romania. The book traces the systematic destruction of Jewish life, his father's death, and his own spiritual crisis — a wrestling with faith, silence, and God's presence or absence in the face of genocide. It is one of the most widely read Holocaust memoirs in the world.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Fair-use excerpts for educational use. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for the full text.

docs.title: Chapter 1 — Arrival at Birkenau
docs.text: It was about four o'clock in the afternoon. A dark sky cast a grey pall over everything around us. It was then that I heard the first question, the one Auschwitz would repeat throughout the years: Where is God now? And I heard the reply: Here He is—He is hanging here on this gallows...

docs.title: Chapter 4 — The Hanging of the Child
docs.text:
His face was pale and his lips were blue. His eyes stared vacantly straight ahead; they never saw us, never felt our presence... When he was pushed back by an SS man, he looked at me with those two big, empty eyes. It was such a terrible night.

Where is God now? Someone behind me asked the question. So as not to meet his eye, I gazed toward the distant sea where the first rays of sun painted the horizon red.

docs.title: Chapter 9 — Liberation
docs.text:
I saw myself. That was all. But it was enough. I recoiled in horror and anguish... I looked at my face. A young boy's face was peering into mine from a distance.

Full text available at Internet Archive and Open Library. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for a physical copy.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• 9-10.R.8 — Night is a literary text that relies heavily on symbol (fire, night, silence), motif (father-son relationship, the hanging of a child), and tone shifts from naive faith to dark despair. Students analyze how Wiesel uses literary elements to convey meaning beyond the literal events.
  • W.3 — Informational writing: Explain how Wiesel uses the motif of silence throughout Night and what it represents. Or: Explain the historical context of the Holocaust and how Night documents it.
  • W.4 — Argumentative writing prompt: "Is Night primarily a historical document or a work of literature? Use evidence from the text."
  • R.7 — Wiesel's style choices: fragmented sentences during trauma, longer sentences during memory and reflection. The decision to write in French (not Hungarian or Yiddish) and for a European audience.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.2 — The Holocaust is the most extreme example in the 20th century of how ideology (Nazi racial theory) can be institutionalized as state religion/policy and used to justify mass murder. The belief system at the center of the Holocaust — racial hierarchy — connects directly to WH.6_12.2 (how belief systems shape societies and cause conflict).
  • WH.6_12.6 — Night raises direct questions about human rights: What obligations do nations and individuals have when genocide is occurring? The UN Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) was adopted directly in response to the Holocaust.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Genocide The deliberate and systematic destruction of a racial, ethnic, national, or religious group Dehumanization The process of denying humanity to a group of people — the first step in enabling mass violence Memoir A first-person account of lived experience — different from autobiography in its focus on a specific period or theme Motif A recurring element in a literary work that develops meaning over the course of the text Complicity Participating in or permitting wrongdoing through action or inaction

docs.title: Key Passage
docs.text:
Never shall I forget that night, the first night in camp, which has turned my life into one long night, seven times cursed and seven times sealed. Never shall I forget that smoke. Never shall I forget the little faces of the children, whose bodies I saw turned into wreaths of smoke beneath a silent blue sky.

Never shall I forget those flames which consumed my faith forever.

Never shall I forget that nocturnal silence which deprived me, for all eternity, of the desire to live. Never shall I forget those moments which murdered my God and my soul and turned my dreams to dust. Never shall I forget these things, even if I am condemned to live as long as God Himself. Never. — Night, Chapter 3

This is the structural and thematic center of the book. The anaphoric repetition of "Never shall I forget" is a direct inversion of the language of prayer and covenant. It is simultaneously a literary device and a moral statement — Wiesel refuses to let these events become forgettable or abstract.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "What Does It Mean to Bear Witness?" — EQ: Do we have an obligation to know about atrocities that happened before we were born? · Standard: WH.6_12.6 + Era 4 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (W.4 argument) or Verbal Conversation

  2. "Silence as Symbol" — EQ: What does Wiesel's silence mean, and why does it matter? · Standard: R.8 (ELA) · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (annotate 5 passages where silence appears and build an interpretation of what it represents)

  3. "Ideology into Genocide" — EQ: How does a society go from prejudice to mass murder? · Standard: WH.6_12.2 + Era 4 · Demo Mode: Visual/Creative (timeline or cause-and-effect map tracing how Nazi racial ideology became state-sanctioned genocide)

  4. "Night and the Universal Declaration" — EQ: What rights were violated in the Holocaust, and how did the world respond? · Standard: WH.6_12.6 + Era 4 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (match specific events from Night to specific articles of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights that were violated)

docs.title: Sensitive Content Note
docs.text: Night contains descriptions of violence, death, and dehumanization that are historically accurate and necessary to the text's purpose. These topics should be framed with appropriate context. Students who are processing personal trauma related to genocide, mass violence, or loss may need additional support. See Reese before assigning.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Elie Wiesel wrote Night because he believed that if enough people understood exactly what happened — not as statistics but as lived experience — it could never happen again. That belief is being tested. Genocide has happened repeatedly since 1945: Cambodia, Bosnia, Rwanda, Darfur, the Uyghur detention camps in China. In each case, the world had the information before the killing reached its worst, and in each case the response was inadequate. Night matters today not as a completed historical lesson but as an ongoing argument: Wiesel's "never again" was a promise, and the question of whether it's been kept is not rhetorical. The Holocaust is also the central case in the argument about whether understanding evil prevents it — and the evidence on that question is genuinely mixed. Reading Night is not sufficient to prevent genocide, but Wiesel's argument was that it is necessary — that a world that could look clearly at what was done to his people and still do nothing would be making a different and worse choice than one that never knew.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:

  • Un Declaration Human Rights
  • 1984 — Orwell

docs.title: The Odyssey (~800 BCE)
docs.text: Author: Homer (attributed) Type: Literary Text · Epic Poem (Translation) Source: Public domain (ancient text) — modern translations by Emily Wilson (2017) and Robert Fagles (1996) recommended; older translations via Project Gutenberg Full Text: Project Gutenberg — older translations · Modern translations (Wilson or Fagles) require purchase but excerpts are widely available in anthologies and educational resources. The Emily Wilson translation is particularly recommended for classroom use — it is the first English translation of the Odyssey by a woman, and Wilson's choices make the text more accessible and honest about its treatment of women and enslaved people. Era/Period: WH Era 1 — Ancient Civilizations (Ancient Greece, ~800 BCE) WH Standards: WH.6_12.3 · WH.6_12.4 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.8 · 9-10.R.9

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Odysseus tied to the mast as the ship passes the Sirens. Attic red-figure stamnos, c. 480 BCE. British Museum.

The Odyssey is the foundational Western epic — the story of Odysseus (Ulysses in Latin), king of Ithaca, and his ten-year journey home after the fall of Troy. He fights monsters, outwits gods, resists temptation, loses his entire crew, and eventually returns to reclaim his household from suitors who have invaded his home and are consuming his wealth while pursuing his wife Penelope. The poem is structured around a simple question — will Odysseus make it home? — but it is really a meditation on intelligence, loyalty, hospitality, and what it means to be human in a world governed by forces larger than you.

This text is dual-use in a way few others are. As an ELA text, the Odyssey is the template for virtually every adventure narrative in Western literature — the hero who leaves home, faces trials, and returns transformed. Every hero's journey, from Star Wars to The Hunger Games, descends from this structure. Students reading the Odyssey are reading the source code of Western storytelling. As a Social Studies text, it is a primary source for ancient Greek values: what Greeks believed about hospitality (xenia), the relationship between mortals and gods, loyalty and cunning as heroic virtues, and the political structure of the heroic age — small kingdoms ruled by warrior-kings whose legitimacy rested on personal prowess rather than institutional authority.

The Emily Wilson translation is specifically recommended because her choices raise important questions: she translates the Greek word polytropos (traditionally "crafty" or "cunning") as "complicated" — a deliberately ambiguous word for a complicated man. She also translates words for enslaved people as "enslaved," rather than "servants" — forcing readers to confront what the Odyssey actually describes. These translation choices are themselves an argument about how we read ancient texts, making this an ideal text for R.9 (reading texts as arguments).

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Public domain — multiple translations. Full text at Project Gutenberg.

docs.title: Book 1 — The Opening Invocation and Telemachus
docs.text:
At the beginning of the epic...

Sing to me of the man, Muse, the man of twists and turns driven time and again off course, once he had plundered the sacred realm of the hallowed one of the dark clouds, wild-hearted, far from his friends, but still he fought beyond his own desperate homecoming.

When it is evening in Ithaca, and Telemachus’ heart is sick with sorrow...

docs.title: Book 9 — The Cyclops
docs.text:
From Odysseus' encounter with the giant Polyphemus...

Polyphemus asks who they are and why they have come.

"Strangers," we answered him, "we are Achaeans driven in our ships by adverse winds from far away. Pity us, O mighty one, and show guest-friendship in your heart..."

Odysseus then pours into Polyphemus' wine the strong wine of the gods that they had brought...

docs.title: Book 12 — Scylla and Charybdis
docs.text:
When Odysseus must navigate between two fearsome monsters...

The cliffs are sheer, and hard by there is another cliff where rests a fig tree in full leaf; but around the root of it is a cave, deep within, with dark entrance, for dread Scylla lives there.

They were all in one mind about turning back...

docs.title: Book 23 — The Recognition
docs.text:
When Odysseus and Penelope finally reunite...

Penelope comes to the hall and sees him. She is dumbfounded, and a great wave of grief takes hold on her heart, and she utters her prayer, calling Zeus among the gods by name...

And now they have it ready, the test that will confirm the truth of all he has said; the bed with its secret...

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.8 — The Odyssey is the origin point of several key literary conventions students encounter throughout their reading lives. Epic conventions: invocation of the Muse, in medias res opening (the story begins ten years in; we learn the earlier events through flashback and stories), epithets (repeated descriptive phrases like "grey-eyed Athena" and "rosy-fingered Dawn" — these are oral composition devices), the epic simile (extended comparisons that slow the narrative for effect). Divine intervention: the gods are characters who act on human events — students analyze how divine involvement changes moral responsibility. The hero: Odysseus is not a conventional hero — he is small, cunning, frequently lying, and ruthlessly practical. Analyzing what kind of hero he is reveals what Greek culture valued.
  • R.9 — The Odyssey makes an implicit argument about what intelligence is and why it matters more than raw strength. Odysseus defeats opponents who are physically stronger (the Cyclops, the suitors) through cleverness — he tells the Cyclops his name is "Nobody," so when the Cyclops calls for help he says "Nobody is hurting me." Students analyze: What is Homer arguing about the relationship between intelligence and power? What does the translation you're reading argue about the original Greek? Wilson's translation specifically invites this analysis.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.3 — The political world of the Odyssey is the heroic age — small kingdoms ruled by warrior-kings whose legitimacy is personal, not institutional. Odysseus's kingdom is nearly lost because he is absent; there is no government mechanism to maintain it. The suitors believe that if Odysseus is dead, any one of them can seize Penelope and with her the kingdom. Students analyze: What does this reveal about political systems before stable institutions? How is heroic kingship different from democracy or the Roman Republic? How does political legitimacy work when it depends entirely on the person rather than the office?
  • WH.6_12.4 — The Odyssey is saturated with Greek cultural values. Xenia (guest-friendship/hospitality): Zeus himself protects guests, and the suitors' violation of hospitality laws is part of what makes them deserving of death. Nostos (homecoming): the Greeks considered returning home the highest good, which is why Odysseus refuses immortality from Calypso. Cunning vs. strength: that Odysseus is defined by intelligence, not just force, reveals something specific about what Greeks admired. Students connect these values to what they learn about Greek culture more broadly.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Epic A long narrative poem about a hero's journey, featuring divine involvement, battles, and cultural values — the Odyssey is the template for this genre In medias res "In the middle of things" — the Odyssey opens ten years into events; the backstory is told through flashbacks and characters' stories Epithet A repeated descriptive phrase attached to a character or object ("grey-eyed Athena," "swift-footed Achilles") — these are oral composition devices that helped bards remember the poem Xenia The ancient Greek concept of guest-friendship and hospitality — hosts were obligated to shelter guests and guests were obligated to behave well; violation of xenia is a serious moral offense in the poem Nostos The Greek word for homecoming — the driving goal of the Odyssey and the root of the word "nostalgia" Hubris Excessive pride that challenges or defies the gods — Odysseus's crew's actions (eating the Cattle of the Sun) and the suitors' behavior are examples; hubris leads to punishment Polytropos The Greek word used to describe Odysseus in the first line — usually translated "crafty" or "many-turned"; Wilson translates it "complicated." This single word opens the question of what kind of hero he is

docs.title: Key Passages
docs.text:
Book 1, opening invocation (Fagles translation):

Sing to me of the man, Muse, the man of twists and turns driven time and again off course, once he had plundered the hallowed heights of Troy. Many cities of men he saw and learned their minds, many pains he suffered, heartsick on the open sea, fighting to save his life and bring his comrades home.

Book 9 — Odysseus to the Cyclops (Fagles translation):

Nobody — that's my name. Nobody — so my mother and father call me, all my friends.

Book 23 — Penelope tests Odysseus (the bed, Fagles translation):

You have not won me yet. If you are truly Odysseus home at last, tell me why our bed is hard to move — it has a secret.

Why these passages matter: The opening invocation establishes the epic conventions and the nature of the hero — a man of "twists and turns," not simply strength. The Cyclops scene shows Odysseus's characteristic method: he uses language itself as a weapon. The bed scene is the poem's emotional climax — Penelope's test of the bed's secret is as clever as anything Odysseus does, establishing that she too is a hero of intelligence.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "What Kind of Hero Is This?" — EQ: Does what a culture makes heroic reveal what that culture values? · Standard: WH.6_12.4 + R.8 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (compare Odysseus as hero to a hero from another culture or time period — what does each hero's defining quality reveal about the society that created them? Use the text as evidence.)

  2. "Translation Is an Argument" — EQ: When you translate a text, are you making creative choices or factual ones? · Standard: R.9 + R.8 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (compare two translations of the same passage — Wilson and Fagles, or Wilson and an older Gutenberg translation. What specific word choices differ? What does each translator seem to believe about the original? What does the choice of words reveal about the translator's argument?)

  3. "Hospitality as Law" — EQ: What happens to a society when informal rules (like hospitality) replace formal laws? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + WH.6_12.4 · Demo Mode: Visual/Creative (map the violations of xenia in the Odyssey — who violates it? Who upholds it? What are the consequences? What does this reveal about how ancient Greek society maintained order without modern institutions?)

  4. "The Hero's Journey Then and Now" — EQ: Why do humans keep telling the same story? · Standard: R.8 + IR.1–5 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (identify a modern story — film, novel, video game — that follows the hero's journey structure. Map its stages onto the Odyssey. Then argue: Is this structure universal, or is it a cultural choice that reflects specific values?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: The Odyssey is 2,800 years old and every culture in the world tells a version of it — departure, trials, return — because the pattern maps onto something real about how life works. The returning veteran who finds the world has moved on without him, the person who has been away so long they don't know who they are anymore, the question of what loyalty means across a long separation: these are not ancient problems. American veterans returning from Afghanistan and Iraq frequently describe an "Odyssey problem" — coming home to find that home is no longer what they left, that they've changed in ways the people at home can't see, and that the journey back to ordinary life is its own kind of odyssey. The poem's other thread — Penelope's twenty-year wait, using wit and deception to hold power while being technically powerless — is also a contemporary story about how people without formal power navigate systems that constrain them. Homer's world is gone. His characters aren't.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:

  • Oedipus Rex — Sophocles — Another foundational Greek text, same era
  • Julius Caesar — Shakespeare — Later use of classical world as political mirror

docs.title: Oedipus Rex (~429 BCE)
docs.text: Author: Sophocles Type: Literary Text · Play · Greek Tragedy Source: Public domain (ancient text) Full Text: Project Gutenberg · Internet Classics Archive Era/Period: WH Era 1 — Ancient Civilizations (Ancient Greece, ~429 BCE) WH Standards: WH.6_12.3 · WH.6_12.4 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.8 · 9-10.W.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Oedipus Rex is one of the most analyzed plays ever written. The story is deceptively simple: Oedipus, king of Thebes, investigates a plague afflicting his city and discovers that the gods sent the plague because the murderer of the previous king, Laius, has never been brought to justice. The investigation gradually reveals that Oedipus himself is Laius's killer — and, more devastatingly, that Laius was his biological father and that his wife Jocasta is his biological mother. The prophecy the play opens with has already come true before the play begins. The audience knows this from Greek mythology; Oedipus does not. The entire play is built on that gap.

This text is dual-use in the most direct sense. As an ELA text, Oedipus Rex is the origin of concepts that define Western drama: dramatic irony, the tragic hero, hamartia (the fatal flaw), catharsis. Aristotle used this play as his primary example in the Poetics when he defined tragedy — which means this play invented most of the vocabulary students use to analyze literary texts. As a Social Studies text, it documents ancient Greek political and religious thought: what is the relationship between political authority and divine will? What is the relationship between fate and human choice? How did Greeks understand the role of oracles and prophecy in political decision-making? These are not abstract questions — real Greek rulers consulted the Oracle at Delphi before major decisions.

Aristotle called Oedipus Rex the greatest play ever written. It is worth asking why: the answer lies in how perfectly it constructs the dramatic irony that makes every line mean two things at once.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Public domain — Dudley Fitts and Robert Fitzgerald translation. Full text at Project Gutenberg.

docs.title: Prologue — Oedipus and the Plague
docs.text:
Oedipus’s pledge to find the cause of the plague, his certainty and confidence before the investigation begins.

Oedipus: "I myself, who bear the burdens of the State And have at other times relieved it of them, I will not stop now that this new evil Comes upon you. I have sent already To Delphi, to the kindly lord Apollo, In hope that he would teach me what disease Is wasting Thebes."

docs.title: Scene 2 — Oedipus vs. Teiresias
docs.text:
The blind prophet tells Oedipus the truth; Oedipus refuses to believe it.

Oedipus: "You have come from your cave To corrupt my city, you and all your wizardry."

Teiresias: "I came because I pitied you And those who share your sufferings."

docs.title: Ode 2 — The Chorus on Fate
docs.text:
The Chorus reflects on the limits of human knowledge and the inevitability of fate.

The Chorus: When one man is marked out for disaster, His ruin rolls in from every quarter.

docs.title: Exodus — The Discovery and Its Consequences
docs.text:
Oedipus learns the truth, blinds himself, the final confrontation with Creon — the last lines.

Oedipus: "I am blind now to all but evil. I grope alone Through the dark world."

Creon: "Come then, take my hand."

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.8 — Oedipus Rex is the foundational text for three of the most important literary concepts students will use throughout their education. Dramatic irony: the audience knows the truth about Oedipus's parentage and crimes; Oedipus does not. Every line he speaks that expresses confidence in his investigation is darkly ironic — he is hunting himself. Sophocles constructs this irony with extraordinary precision. Hamartia: the "fatal flaw" — Oedipus's hamartia is not that he killed his father or married his mother (he did not know), but that his pride and certainty in his own intelligence prevent him from accepting warning signs. His strength (determination to know the truth) becomes his destruction. Tragic structure: Aristotle's model — rise, reversal (peripeteia), recognition (anagnorisis), fall — is on full display. Students can map each stage.
  • W.4 — The central argument the play forces is one of the deepest in philosophy: Is Oedipus responsible for his fate? He was prophesied before birth. He tried to escape the prophecy. He acted in ignorance. And yet the oracle came true, and he punishes himself with blinding and exile. Students construct a position on this question using textual evidence — and then consider what the Greek answer to this question reveals about how Greeks understood free will, divine power, and human responsibility.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.3 — The political structure of Thebes in Oedipus Rex is instructive: a king whose legitimacy is entirely personal, whose authority depends on his ability to solve problems and protect the city, and who loses everything when his identity is revealed. Students analyze: How is kingship in this play different from other political systems? What happens when a ruler's legitimacy is destroyed? How did Greek city-states actually govern themselves, and how does the play reflect or distort that reality?
  • WH.6_12.4 — The Oracle at Delphi was a real institution that real Greek leaders consulted. The concept that fate is determined by the gods and that humans cannot escape divine will was not just a literary convention — it was a live religious and philosophical belief. Students examine: What does Greek religion actually hold about fate and human agency? How did the Oracle function politically? How did belief in oracles shape Greek decision-making — in the play and in history?

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Dramatic irony A literary device in which the audience knows something crucial that the character does not — in Oedipus Rex, the audience knows from mythology that Oedipus killed his father and married his mother before the play begins Hamartia The "fatal flaw" of a tragic hero — not necessarily a moral failing but a characteristic that, in combination with circumstances, leads to the hero's destruction; Oedipus's is his relentless, proud pursuit of the truth Tragic hero A protagonist of high status who falls from prosperity to misery through a combination of fate, flaw, and choice — the model Aristotle derived from this play Catharsis Aristotle's term for the emotional release an audience experiences at the end of a tragedy — the pity and fear provoked by watching Oedipus's fall Peripeteia The reversal — the moment in a tragedy when the situation flips from good to bad (or apparent good to devastating truth); in this play, it is the moment Oedipus realizes who he is Oracle A person or institution believed to communicate divine prophecy — the Oracle at Delphi was a real Greek institution consulted by political leaders; the oracle's prophecy drives this entire plot Anagnorisis The recognition — the moment of discovery when the character (and audience) finally understands the full truth; in this play, it is Oedipus's realization that he is the murderer he has been hunting

docs.title: Key Passages
docs.text:
Opening — Oedipus's confidence (Fagles translation):

I know you are all sick, yet there is not one of you, sick as you are, who is as sick as I myself. My spirit groans for the city, for myself, and for you all.

The shepherd reveals the truth:

He is the child of Laius' house. But she within, your wife, could tell you best of all his birth.

Oedipus's recognition:

I who first saw the light bred of a match accursed, and accursed in my living with them I lived with, cursed in my killing.

Why these passages matter: The opening lines establish Oedipus's defining quality — he suffers more than others because he cares more than others, which is both his greatness and his flaw. The shepherd's revelation is the play's hinge point. The recognition speech is one of the most compact expressions of tragic ruin in Western literature — every element of the prophecy (birth, marriage, murder) is named in sequence.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Oedipus on Trial" — EQ: Can a person be guilty of something they did not know they were doing? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (stage a mock trial of Oedipus — the charge is murder of Laius. What is the prosecution's case? What is the defense? What does the question of guilt reveal about Greek vs. modern concepts of responsibility and justice?)

  2. "How Dramatic Irony Works" — EQ: How does knowing the ending change the experience of watching a story? · Standard: R.8 · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (select 5 moments in the play where dramatic irony is operating — where the audience knows what the character doesn't. For each, annotate: What does Oedipus believe? What does the audience know? What does Sophocles achieve by putting them in the same scene?)

  3. "The Oracle Was Real" — EQ: What happens to political decision-making when leaders believe the future is knowable? · Standard: WH.6_12.4 + IR.1–5 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (research the Oracle at Delphi — how did it actually work? Who consulted it? Find two documented cases where Greek leaders made political or military decisions based on oracular prophecy. What does this reveal about how religion and politics interacted in ancient Greece?)

  4. "Fate vs. Choice — Still Unresolved" — EQ: If the outcome is determined, does how you got there matter? · Standard: W.4 + WH.6_12.2 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (construct your position on whether Oedipus is responsible for his fate. Use evidence from the play. Then connect to a real historical case: find a historical figure who made decisions that led to disaster — were they fated or did they choose? Is the distinction meaningful?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Oedipus Rex is the original story about a person who does everything right — investigates, follows the evidence, refuses to stop when warned — and ends up proving the thing he was trying to disprove. Sophocles is asking whether it's possible to escape the consequences of who you are or where you come from, and whether knowledge is always better than ignorance. Those questions don't resolve. The play's investigation structure (Oedipus pursuing truth until it destroys him) maps onto every story about whistleblowers, investigators, and journalists who uncover something that ruins their own position in the process. It also maps onto every argument about genetic determinism, fate vs. agency, and whether knowing your diagnosis is always better than not knowing. The concept of "tragic flaw" — hamartia — that Aristotle derived from Oedipus is still the framework therapists, writers, and coaches use to describe how someone's greatest strength becomes their greatest weakness under pressure.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Era 1 — Ancient Civilizations
  • The Odyssey — Homer — Same era, same cultural world; different genre
  • Julius Caesar — Shakespeare — Political tragedy; fate vs. choice in a different tradition
  • Hamlet — Shakespeare — Shakespearean tragedy explicitly compared to Oedipus by critics

Author: George Orwell Type: Literary Text · Dystopian Novel Source: Secker & Warburg (not public domain in US — school library copy or licensed edition required) Era/Period: Era 4 — Age of Global War and Globalization WH Standards: WH.6_12.2 · WH.6_12.3 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.8 · 9-10.W.3 · 9-10.W.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
George Orwell (1903–1950). This press photograph was taken around 1943, six years before Nineteen Eighty-Four was published.

1984 is a dystopian novel set in a totalitarian superstate called Oceania, where the Party controls all information, language, history, and thought. The protagonist Winston Smith works at the Ministry of Truth rewriting historical records to match the Party's current version of events. Orwell wrote it in 1948 (published 1949) as a warning about Stalinist totalitarianism and the mechanics of political control — surveillance, propaganda, the destruction of language as a means of controlling thought. It remains the defining literary portrait of authoritarianism.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Fair-use excerpts for educational use. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for the full text.

docs.title: Part 1, Chapter 1 — 'It was a bright cold day in April'
docs.text:
This passage sets the dystopian scene of Airstrip One (formerly Britain), where posters featuring Big Brother’s face are ubiquitous and citizens live under constant surveillance.

It was a bright cold day in April, and the clocks were striking thirteen. Winston Smith, his chin nuzzled into his breast in an effort to escape the vile wind, slipped quickly through the glass doors of Victory Mansions...

docs.title: Part 2, Chapter 5 — The Principles of Newspeak
docs.text:
Syme explains the concept of doublethink and the reductionist nature of Newspeak aimed at limiting freedom of thought by controlling language.

'Don't you see that the whole aim of Newspeak is to narrow the range of thought? In the end we shall make thoughtcrime literally impossible, because there will be no words in which to express it.'

docs.title: Part 3, Chapter 2 — Room 101
docs.text:
In this harrowing scene, O'Brien reveals the true motive behind the Party's oppressive regime—power for its own sake.

The object of power is power... We know that no one ever seizes power with the intention of relinquishing it. Power is not a means; it is an end.'

docs.title: Appendix — The Principles of Newspeak
docs.text:
This section provides an academic overview of Newspeak’s design, emphasizing its purpose to control and ultimately eradicate free thought by restricting language.

Newspeak was designed to meet the ideological needs of Ingsoc (English Socialism). Its vocabulary was deliberately reduced in size, with synonyms eliminated...

Full text available at Internet Archive and Open Library. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for a physical copy.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• 9-10.R.8 — 1984 is one of the richest texts for literary analysis at this level: symbol (the telescreen, Room 101, the glass paperweight), extended metaphor (the Party's rewriting of history as memory-control), irony (Ministry of Truth lies; Ministry of Love tortures; Ministry of Peace makes war), and character development under systematic oppression.
  • R.7 — Orwell's prose style is deliberate and clean — in direct contrast to "Newspeak," the fictional language the Party creates to narrow thought. Students can analyze how Orwell's prose style demonstrates his thesis about language and power.
  • W.4 — High-leverage argumentative prompt: "Is surveillance for public safety a slippery slope toward the kind of control Orwell describes? Use 1984 and at least one piece of current evidence."
  • W.3 — Informational writing: Explain how the Party uses "doublethink" to maintain control of the population. What historical examples from the 20th century does Orwell seem to be drawing from?

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.2 — The Party is a belief system as much as a government — it demands total ideological loyalty, including the rewriting of memory. Students can analyze it as a fictional model of how totalitarian regimes operate, connected to actual historical examples (Stalinism, Nazism, Maoism).
  • WH.6_12.3 — 1984 depicts a political system at its most extreme — total state control of citizens. It functions as a theoretical opposite to democracy: What does maximum authoritarianism look like, and how does it sustain itself?

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Totalitarianism A political system in which the state controls every aspect of public and private life Doublethink Holding two contradictory beliefs simultaneously and believing both are true — a mechanism of the Party's control Newspeak The Party's fictional constructed language, designed to make certain thoughts impossible by eliminating the words needed to express them Surveillance Systematic observation and monitoring of citizens — a central mechanism of control in the novel Propaganda Information, especially biased or misleading, used to promote a political cause or point of view

docs.title: Key Passage
docs.text:
Don't you see that the whole aim of Newspeak is to narrow the range of thought? In the end we shall make thoughtcrime literally impossible, because there will be no words in which to express it. — 1984, Part 1, Chapter 5 (Syme speaking to Winston)

This is the thesis of Orwell's argument about language and control: if you eliminate the words for freedom, resistance, or doubt, you eliminate the thoughts themselves. This connects directly to Orwell's contemporaneous essay "Politics and the English Language" and to any discussion of how political language shapes public consciousness.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Language as Control" — EQ: Can you think a thought if you don't have words for it? · Standard: WH.6_12.2 + Era 4 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation or Portfolio Annotation (compare Newspeak with actual propaganda language from 20th-century totalitarian regimes)

  2. "Surveillance Then and Now" — EQ: At what point does monitoring citizens become tyranny? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + Era 4 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (W.4 argument using 1984 + contemporary surveillance examples — NSA, social media algorithms, etc.)

  3. "The Ministry of Truth and Social Media" — EQ: What happens to truth when everyone controls the narrative? · Standard: R.8 (ELA) · Demo Mode: Multimedia/Performance (create a "Ministry of Truth"-style analysis of a contemporary misinformation case)

  4. "Reading 1984 as History" — EQ: What historical events was Orwell warning against? · Standard: WH.6_12.2 + Era 4 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (research the historical context — Stalinism, WWII propaganda — and identify 3 specific events that Orwell seems to be drawing from)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: 1984 sold more copies in January 2017 than in any month since its publication in 1949. People reach for it whenever political language starts to feel disconnected from reality, when official statements contradict documented evidence, when the term "alternative facts" enters the news cycle. Orwell's specific predictions about the mechanisms of a surveillance state — the telescreen that watches you, the memory hole that destroys inconvenient records, the Ministry of Truth that rewrites history, the Two Minutes Hate that channels grievance into loyalty — are not literal descriptions of contemporary life. They are conceptual tools for analyzing tendencies that exist in every political system. The surveillance infrastructure built after 9/11, the algorithmic curation of what you see online, the use of social media to generate outrage and loyalty simultaneously — these are not 1984, but Orwell's framework helps you see what they are. The novel is also a warning about language: when political speech is designed to make thought impossible rather than to convey meaning, democracy cannot function. That warning is relevant in any era when political language is used to obscure rather than illuminate.
