docs.title: "Politics and the English Language" (1946)
docs.text: Author: George Orwell Type: Nonfiction Essay Source: Fair-use excerpts — full essay available at Orwell Foundation and Internet Archive Era/Period: WH Era 4 — Age of Global War and Globalization ELA Standards: 9-10.R.4 · 9-10.R.8 · 9-10.W.4 WH Standards: WH.6_12.5

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text: George Orwell argues that political language is often deliberately vague and ugly, hiding the truth from people. He targets bad writers in government and journalism who use complex phrases to make simple ideas seem more important or mysterious. It matters for students because learning to spot this kind of writing can help you be a better critic and argument writer.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Fair-use excerpts for educational use. Full essay at links above.

docs.title: The Central Problem
docs.text: Orwell argues that political and economic writing has become deliberately vague, using long words and complex sentences to hide meaning rather than clarify it. This vagueness often serves the purpose of hiding inconvenient facts or truth from the public.

docs.title: Five Symptoms of Bad Writing
docs.text:
Dying metaphors: "He is an old hand" instead of a clear statement

Operators or verbal false limbs: "This represents a new departure in our policy"

Pretentious diction: "Utilize" instead of "use", "phenomenon" instead of "thing"

Meaningless words: "A seamless web of deception" where simpler alternatives would suffice

docs.title: Orwell's Six Rules
docs.text:
These are the six rules Orwell gives for clear writing:

  1. Never use a metaphor, simile, or other figure of speech which you are used to seeing in print.
  2. Never use a long word where a short one will do.
  3. If it is possible to cut a word out, always cut it out.
  4. Never use the passive where you can use the active.
  5. Never use a foreign phrase, a scientific word, or a jargon word if you can think of an everyday English equivalent.
  6. Break any of these rules sooner than say anything outright barbarous.

docs.title: Politics and Language
docs.text:
Orwell argues that in our time political speech is often used to defend the indefensible, that vague language enables political dishonesty, and that clarity in writing forces clarity in thinking. He warns against how language can corrupt thought and vice versa.

Get the full text: - Internet Archive — free access - Orwell Foundation — complete essay online - No library card? Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• 9-10.R.4 — Orwell makes an argument by building a case from specific examples of poor writing in political texts, showing how they obscure meaning and hide truth. Students should trace how Orwell uses these examples to support his broader claim about the need for clear language.
  • 9-10.R.8 — Orwell uses rhetorical strategies including naming specific bad examples (like "incontrovertible"), appeals to practicality (by suggesting rules for better writing), and giving concrete rules at the end to help readers see how to apply his ideas.
  • 9-10.W.4 — Orwell's six rules function as a direct writing checklist that students can use in their own drafts, making W.4 criteria concrete and memorable by providing clear guidelines on style and clarity.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text: • WH.6_12.5 — This essay is a product of 1946, right after WWII when Orwell had seen how totalitarian governments used language to control people's thoughts (like Nazi propaganda or Soviet doublespeak). Understanding the historical context makes this essay a primary source on how language functions in political power.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Euphemism A mild or vague word used in place of a blunt or harsh one — often to hide what is actually happening Jargon Specialized vocabulary used by a group, often used to sound authoritative while obscuring meaning Abstraction A word that refers to a concept rather than a specific thing — often used to avoid being precise Passive voice A sentence structure where the subject receives the action instead of doing it — "mistakes were made" instead of "I made mistakes" Rhetoric The art of persuasion — the techniques a writer or speaker uses to convince an audience

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Diagnose the Disease" — EQ: How do politicians use language to hide what they are actually doing? · Standard: 9-10.R.4 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (find a political speech or statement; apply Orwell's five symptoms to diagnose its evasions; write an argument about what the speaker is actually saying vs. what the words literally say)
  2. "Apply the Six Rules" — EQ: What does it actually mean to write clearly? · Standard: 9-10.W.4 · Demo Mode: Workshop (take a paragraph from a recent student draft; apply Orwell's six rules as a revision checklist; compare before and after; explain which changes made the biggest difference and why)
  3. "Then and Now" — EQ: Has political language gotten better or worse since Orwell wrote this in 1946? · Standard: WH.6_12.5 + 9-10.R.8 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (select a political speech from 1946 and one from today; apply Orwell's diagnostic framework to both; make an argument about whether Orwell's warning has been heeded)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Orwell published "Politics and the English Language" in 1946, and it reads like it was written about the current news cycle. His central argument — that bad politics and bad writing reinforce each other, that vague, abstract, euphemistic language is used to make unacceptable things acceptable — is a user manual for analyzing political speech in any era. "Collateral damage" instead of "civilian deaths." "Enhanced interrogation" instead of "torture." "Undocumented" vs. "illegal." "Urban unrest" instead of "uprising." Every euphemism debate, every argument about what to call something and why the name matters, is an Orwell argument. His six rules for clear writing — including "never use a long word where a short one will do" and "if it is possible to cut a word out, always cut it out" — are still the best concise writing advice available. Media literacy education, political rhetoric courses, and journalism ethics programs all draw on this essay. It's short enough to read in one class period and useful enough to return to every time you encounter political language that feels designed to prevent thought.

docs.title: Persepolis (2000)
docs.text: Author: Marjane Satrapi Type: Literary Text · Graphic Memoir · Primary Source (Iranian Revolution) Source: Pantheon Books (not public domain — school library copy required) Full Text: Available via school library; Internet Archive lending copy Era/Period: Era 4 — Age of Global War and Globalization WH Standards: WH.6_12.2 · WH.6_12.3 · WH.6_12.4 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.8 · 9-10.R.9 · 9-10.W.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Persepolis is Marjane Satrapi's autobiographical graphic novel about growing up in Iran during and after the 1979 Islamic Revolution. Satrapi was 9 years old when the Revolution brought the Ayatollah Khomeini to power, replacing the U.S.-backed Shah's authoritarian secular regime with a theocratic authoritarian regime. The memoir covers her childhood (Volume 1) and young adulthood (Volume 2) — including the Iran-Iraq War, the suppression of dissidents (including members of her own family), the imposition of the hijab and other restrictions on women, her parents' decision to send her to Vienna at 14 for her safety, and her eventual emigration.

This text is dual-use. As ELA, it is one of the most studied graphic memoirs in the curriculum — rich for literary analysis of the graphic novel form, complex characterization, and multiple POV. As history, it is a first-person primary source account of the Iranian Revolution from inside it — the only text in the reading library that covers the Islamic world in Era 4, the Iranian Revolution, or political Islam as an ideology.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Fair-use excerpts for educational use. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for the full text.

docs.title: 'The Veil' — The Islamic Revolution Changes Everything
docs.text:
Chapter 4: The veil is introduced as a mandatory requirement in Marji's school, leading to a moment of confusion and resistance.

Marji stands outside her classroom door, watching her classmates. Some girls wear headscarves while others don't, creating an immediate divide among them. When the teacher enters, she instructs all students to cover their heads with scarves or veils. Marji feels lost as she tries to understand why such a simple item of clothing has suddenly become so significant. She decides not to follow the rule, but her parents later sit her down for a conversation about the importance of compliance and safety in these tumultuous times.

docs.title: 'The Letter' — Marji's Uncle Anoosh
docs.text:
Chapter 9: In a letter to Marji, Anoosh recounts his experience as a political prisoner.

Anoosh writes to Marji from prison, sharing stories of his detention under the Shah and how he continued to fight for freedom after the revolution. He describes being beaten and tortured but also finding hope in the solidarity among prisoners who shared their dreams and struggles. His letter serves as a bridge between generations, instilling in young Marji a deeper understanding of political activism and its consequences.

docs.title: 'The Dowry' — Leaving Iran
docs.text:
Chapter 12: As Marji prepares to leave for Europe with her mother.

The scene shifts to the airport where Marji's departure is bittersweet. She looks back at her family, especially her mother who collapses in grief as they board their flight. The emotional farewell highlights the weight of exile and the sacrifices made for a better future elsewhere. In this poignant moment, Marji reflects on the cost of leaving home.

Full text available at Internet Archive and Open Library. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for a physical copy.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.8 — The graphic novel form creates unique literary analysis opportunities: Satrapi's visual style is deliberately simple (black and white, bold shapes) in contrast to the complexity of what she depicts. Students analyze how the visual choices — what is shown vs. described in text, how the same events appear differently in panels — create meaning that prose alone couldn't achieve. The child narrator vs. the adult author is a central character complexity.
  • R.9 — Satrapi's memoir makes an implicit argument: that Western caricatures of Iran — as either the Shah's secular "progress" or Khomeini's religious reaction — miss the lived complexity that people inside the country experienced. Students identify this argument and trace how she constructs it through specific scenes rather than direct statement.
  • W.4 — Use as a W.4 case study for argument made through narrative rather than direct claim. How does Satrapi argue without arguing? What is the difference between a story and an argument?
  • R.2 — The memoir rewards inference throughout: what does young Marji understand and not understand? Where does the reader know more than she does? What does the gap between her childhood understanding and the reader's adult knowledge create?

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.2 — Persepolis is a primary source document of political Islam as an ideology — how it came to power, how it exercised control, how it defined itself against both Western secularism and Marxist alternatives. Satrapi's family were Marxists and secularists; her uncle was executed by the Islamic Republic. Students understand the Iranian Revolution as a collision of three ideological forces (the Shah's secular authoritarianism, Marxist opposition, and Islamic fundamentalism) rather than a simple religious uprising.
  • WH.6_12.3 — The memoir documents how the Islamic Republic functioned as a political system: the Revolutionary Guard, the dress code enforcement, the suppression of political opposition, the use of religion to justify state power. Students compare this to other authoritarian systems they've studied (Nazi Germany, Stalinist USSR) — what does theocratic authoritarianism share with secular authoritarianism? What is different?
  • WH.6_12.4 — The Iran-Iraq War (1980–1988) — which killed an estimated 500,000–1,000,000 people — is documented through Satrapi's childhood experience. Students understand how a revolution that promised liberation quickly mobilized an entire generation for a war that served the regime's survival interests.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Iranian Revolution (1979) The overthrow of Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi (backed by the U.S.) and the establishment of the Islamic Republic under Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini Hijab Islamic head covering; its mandatory use was one of the first acts of the new Islamic Republic and a central symbol of the regime's control over women's bodies SAVAK The Shah's secret police — responsible for torture and political suppression; one reason many Iranians initially supported the Revolution against him Revolutionary Guard The paramilitary force established by the Islamic Republic to enforce the regime's ideology — distinct from the regular army Theocracy Government by religious authority — Iran under Khomeini replaced the Shah's secular state with a government explicitly based on Islamic law

docs.title: Key Scenes
docs.text:
Chapter 1 — "The Veil": Nine-year-old Marji and her classmates are suddenly required to wear the hijab. The girls treat it as a game, not understanding what it means. Adult Satrapi frames the panel showing girls wearing veils over their faces like masks — the visual comment is sharper than any explanation.

Chapter 3 — "The Water Cell": Marji's uncle Anoosh is arrested by the Shah, released, then rearrested and executed by the Islamic Republic. He represents the Marxist generation that supported the Revolution against the Shah and was then destroyed by it. His execution is the memoir's emotional center.

Chapter 9 — "The F-14s": The Iran-Iraq War arrives. Marji begins to understand that the regime is deliberately extending the war for political reasons — using 14-year-olds as "human mine-sweepers" with plastic keys to paradise — and her faith in the Revolution dies completely.

Why these scenes matter: Each scene is historically documented: the mandatory hijab, the purge of the left after 1979, the child soldiers of the Iran-Iraq War. Satrapi's memoir makes historical events legible through personal experience.

docs.title: Chapter Guide
docs.text: Chapter(s) Historical Content ELA Focus 1–4 Islamic Revolution; initial changes Child narrator vs. adult author; dramatic irony 5–8 Shah's history; SAVAK; why the Revolution happened R.9: implicit argument about Western responsibility 9–12 Iran-Iraq War begins; family survival Setting-character interaction; loss of innocence theme 13–16 Life under the Islamic Republic; Marji's resistance Complex character development; R.8 symbol analysis 17–20 Decision to leave; departure for Vienna WH.6_12.4: individual vs. historical forces

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "What Revolution Promises vs. What It Delivers" — EQ: Why do revolutions so often produce the opposite of what they promised? · Standard: WH.6_12.2 + WH.6_12.3 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (compare the Iranian Revolution to one other revolution studied in class — French, Russian, Haitian. What pattern do you see? Write an argument about what causes revolutionary movements to turn authoritarian)

  2. "The West's Role" — EQ: Does a country bear responsibility for the governments its foreign policy creates? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (research the CIA's 1953 coup that restored the Shah — Satrapi references this. Argue: did U.S. foreign policy in Iran contribute to the 1979 Revolution? Use evidence from Persepolis and at least one external source)

  3. "How Graphic Novels Argue" — EQ: Can a picture make an argument that words can't? · Standard: R.8 + R.9 · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (select 5 panels from Persepolis where the visual choice does something the words don't or can't. Annotate each: what is Satrapi showing? What argument is the image making that she doesn't state in the text?)

  4. "Persepolis and 1984" — EQ: Is theocratic authoritarianism different from secular authoritarianism? · Standard: WH.6_12.2 + R.8 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (compare the surveillance and social control systems in Persepolis and 1984 — what is the same? What is different? Does the regime's justification — religion vs. ideology — change how it operates or only how it presents itself?)

docs.title: Sensitive Content Note
docs.text: Persepolis contains depictions of political violence, torture (described), child death, and the psychological effects of living under authoritarian rule. These are historically documented events presented through a child's perspective. Appropriate historical framing is essential. Students from Iranian American or Muslim communities may have complex personal relationships with this text.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Persepolis is banned in Iran. It depicts the country's people — Satrapi's family, her friends, the ordinary Iranians who lived through the Islamic Revolution — as fully human, politically complex, and frequently in disagreement with the government that claims to represent them. That distinction (a government vs. its people) is one the novel insists on, and it's relevant whenever Iran appears in the news as a foreign policy abstraction. The graphic memoir form is also significant: Satrapi chose it specifically because it allowed her to show what couldn't be photographed under the Islamic Republic's censorship — the interior life, the private conversations, the small acts of resistance that don't make the news. The novel's argument that art can show you something about a country that policy documents and news coverage cannot is a claim about the purpose of literature that is worth taking seriously as a general principle. Persepolis also bridges World History and US History: the Iranian Revolution it depicts was shaped in part by U.S. policy toward Iran, and the novel's account of that history from inside Iran is a perspective that rarely makes it into American textbooks.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Era 4 — Global War
  • 1984 — Orwell — Secular authoritarianism; powerful comparison text
  • Night — Wiesel — Another memoir of surviving an ideological state
  • Communist Manifesto — The ideology Satrapi's family believed in before the Revolution
  • UN Universal Declaration of Human Rights — The rights the Islamic Republic violated

docs.title: A Raisin in the Sun (1959)
docs.text: Author: Lorraine Hansberry Type: Literary Text · Play Source: Random House (not public domain — school library copy required) Full Text: Available via Open Library — free digital borrowing (search: A Raisin in the Sun Hansberry) · school library copy required for extended reading Era/Period: USH Unit 6 — Civil Rights Era (1950s–60s); housing discrimination context USH Standards: US.6_12.3 · US.6_12.4 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.8 · 9-10.W.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Lorraine Hansberry (1930–1965), playwright and author of A Raisin in the Sun (1959). Photo: New York World-Telegram and Sun.

The Younger family — grandmother Lena, her son Walter Lee, his wife Ruth, their son Travis, and Walter Lee's sister Beneatha — live crowded into a small apartment on Chicago's South Side in the late 1950s. They are waiting for a $10,000 life insurance check from the death of Lena's husband, Walter Senior. The play is about what the family does with this money — and what each character believes the money represents. Walter Lee wants to invest in a liquor store. Beneatha wants to go to medical school. Lena wants to buy a house. The house she buys is in Clybourne Park, a white neighborhood whose residents' association sends a representative to pay the family not to move in.

Hansberry was the first Black playwright produced on Broadway. She wrote A Raisin in the Sun from direct personal experience: her own father, Carl Hansberry, had fought a legal battle — Hansberry v. Lee, which went to the Supreme Court in 1940 — against Chicago's restrictive housing covenants that prevented Black families from buying homes in white neighborhoods. The Younger family's choice is the choice her family made. The title comes from Langston Hughes's poem "Harlem" (1951): "What happens to a dream deferred? / Does it dry up / like a raisin in the sun?" — the question the play poses and refuses to simply answer.

This text is dual-use in the most direct historical sense. As an ELA text, the play is one of the finest American examples of dramatic structure built around character foils — Walter Lee and Beneatha represent two visions of Black aspiration in direct tension with each other. The setting (a cramped apartment) functions as a character: physical space represents social constraint. The play's final decision — to move to Clybourne Park — is left deliberately ambiguous. As a Social Studies text, the play is a primary source for the specific mechanisms of housing segregation: restrictive covenants, "block busting," and the concentrated poverty that resulted from confining Black families to specific neighborhoods. These mechanisms created the geography of American cities — including Fargo — that students may inhabit today.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Fair-use excerpts for educational use. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for the full text.

docs.title: Act 1, Scene 1 — The Morning Routine
docs.text:
The setting introduces the Younger family's crowded apartment, highlighting their living conditions as a symbol of social inequality.

As Lena Younger prepares breakfast in her small kitchen, she speaks to Ruth about how tired Walter is from his job and the strain it puts on their family. This dialogue sets up the physical constraints of their space and its symbolic weight in societal oppression.

docs.title: Act 2, Scene 2 — Walter and the Dream
docs.text:
Walter's speech reveals his aspirations for himself and his son, emphasizing the internal conflict between ambition and reality.

“I want so many things that they are driving me kind of crazy. I want colored Cadillac's, and I want night clubs, and I want to move into a fine house and have people say, ‘That’s Walter Lee Younger’s house’ when they see it.” This monologue captures Walter’s ambition amidst the struggle for economic freedom.

docs.title: Act 3 — 'We have decided to move'
docs.text:
The climax of the play where the family decides on a course of action that prioritizes their dignity.

When Lindner offers the Youngers money not to move into an all-white neighborhood, Walter refuses and proclaims, “You hear me? We don’t need you or your offer! . . . You just want us to be grateful and go away and leave you alone.” This moment is a pivotal declaration of the family’s determination to pursue their dreams with dignity.

Full text available at Internet Archive and Open Library. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for a physical copy.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.8 — A Raisin in the Sun demonstrates several dramatic structures with unusual clarity. Character foils: Walter Lee and Beneatha are the play's central foil pair — Walter Lee's dream is economic (ownership, business, "making it" in capitalist terms); Beneatha's dream is professional and intellectual (medical school, identity, African heritage). They represent two competing visions of what Black liberation looks like in 1950s America, and the play does not fully resolve the tension. Setting as character: the apartment's smallness, its shared bathroom with neighbors, its single bedroom — these are not background detail. Hansberry uses the physical space to represent the social constraint the family lives under; the house in Clybourne Park represents freedom partly because it is larger. Dramatic structure: the play follows a clear five-act arc (complication, revelation, crisis, decision, departure) that rewards structural analysis.
  • W.4 — The play ends with the family deciding to move to Clybourne Park knowing the white residents will resist them. The question the play leaves open: What does this decision mean — hope or delusion? Students construct an argument about the ending using evidence from the play. This requires engaging with the specific historical context (the real violence that accompanied housing integration) and with Hansberry's own biography — her family fought this battle and won, but her father died young and embittered.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• US.6_12.3 — The play's central conflict is legal and political: restrictive housing covenants were real legal instruments that bound white homeowners not to sell to Black buyers. The Supreme Court ruled restrictive covenants unenforceable in Shelley v. Kraemer (1948) — but the play's events occur after that ruling, and Mr. Lindner's visit makes clear that informal community enforcement continued. Students examine: How did housing segregation work legally? What did the Supreme Court ruling actually change? What does the gap between legal change and social reality reveal about how racial inequality is maintained?
  • US.6_12.4 — The Great Migration brought millions of Black Southerners to Northern cities in the early 20th century. The Younger family is part of this migration's second generation. The specific economic conditions of Chicago's South Side — concentrated in a small area by housing restrictions — created the poverty that Lena Younger's insurance check represents an escape from. Students analyze: What were the economic effects of housing segregation? What did it mean economically for a family to be confined to a specific neighborhood? How did this shape the geography of American cities?

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Restrictive covenant A legal clause in a property deed that restricted who could buy or occupy the property — commonly used to prevent Black families from purchasing homes in white neighborhoods; ruled unenforceable by the Supreme Court in 1948 but socially enforced for decades after The Great Migration The movement of millions of Black Americans from the Jim Crow South to Northern and Midwestern cities between 1910 and 1970 — the Younger family is part of this migration's second generation in Chicago Blockbusting A real estate practice in which agents deliberately sold homes in white neighborhoods to Black families to trigger white panic-selling, then bought the remaining properties cheaply — a mechanism of housing segregation Langston Hughes — "Harlem" The 1951 poem that gives the play its title; Hughes asks what happens to a deferred dream — "Does it dry up / like a raisin in the sun?" — the question the play dramatizes Clybourne Park The fictional white Chicago neighborhood where the Youngers buy a house — in 2011, playwright Bruce Norris wrote Clybourne Park, a play set in the same house showing what happened to the white residents before and after the Youngers arrived Assimilationism The belief that Black Americans should adopt the dominant white cultural norms to achieve equality — Beneatha rejects this in her confrontation with George Murchison, who represents the assimilationist view

docs.title: Key Scenes and Passages
docs.text:
The play is copyrighted — do not reproduce extended passages. Focus on these key scenes:

The opening (the alarm clock): The play opens before dawn with Ruth getting Walter Lee up for work. The physical reality of the apartment and the exhaustion of its inhabitants is established before a word of dialogue. Students note: What does Hansberry communicate in staging alone, before anyone speaks?

Walter Lee's dream speech: Walter Lee explains to Travis what he envisions for their future — a vision built on business ownership and property. Students analyze: What does Walter Lee believe money represents? What values does his dream reflect? Why does Ruth find it painful rather than inspiring?

Beneatha and the African identity (Asagai scenes): Beneatha's relationship with the Nigerian student Joseph Asagai raises questions about Black identity, African heritage, and assimilation that are specific to the late 1950s context. Students analyze: What is the debate between Asagai's vision and George Murchison's assimilationist vision? What does Hansberry seem to argue?

Mr. Lindner's visit (Act II, Scene 3): The representative from the Clybourne Park Improvement Association offers to pay the Youngers not to move in — framing it as "the happiness of all concerned." This is the play's most politically explicit scene. Students note: What rhetoric does Lindner use? How does it compare to the explicit racism of segregation?

The final decision (Act III): Walter Lee's decision about whether to accept or refuse Lindner's second offer is the play's climax. Students analyze: What does he decide, and why? What does Lena's reaction reveal about what she values? What does the ending leave unresolved?

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "The Deferred Dream" — EQ: What happens to people whose aspirations are blocked by forces outside their control? · Standard: US.6_12.4 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (connect the play's characters to the historical reality of housing segregation. Research what housing discrimination actually cost Black families economically — in wealth accumulation, educational access, and opportunity. Argue: Does the Younger family's situation represent individual failure or structural constraint? What evidence from history and the play supports your position?)

  2. "What Does Mr. Lindner's Visit Mean?" — EQ: How does polite racism differ from explicit racism — and which is more dangerous? · Standard: US.6_12.3 + R.8 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (analyze Lindner's language in Act II, Scene 3. What does he say explicitly? What does he imply? What rhetoric does he use to avoid sounding racist while making a racist argument? Compare to the rhetoric of actual housing covenants from this period — find primary source language.)

  3. "Walter Lee vs. Beneatha: Two Visions of Freedom" — EQ: Can people who want the same thing want it for incompatible reasons? · Standard: R.8 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (create a character analysis of Walter Lee and Beneatha as foils. What does each want? What does each believe money or education represents? What does each sacrifice to get it? What does the tension between them reveal about the competing philosophies of Black advancement in the 1950s?)

  4. "Hughes to Hansberry — The Deferred Dream in History" — EQ: What does it mean that a poem and a play written ten years apart are asking the same question? · Standard: US.6_12.4 + IR.1–5 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (read Langston Hughes's poem "Harlem" (1951). Analyze its central question. Then trace how Hansberry uses that question as the organizing frame of the play. Research: What were the specific historical conditions — in housing, employment, education — that made the dream "deferred" for Black Americans in the 1950s? Write an analysis connecting the two texts to the historical reality they describe.)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: A Raisin in the Sun premiered in 1959, three years before the Fair Housing Act existed and in a city where banks, real estate companies, and neighborhood associations collaborated to keep Black families out of white neighborhoods. The practice was called redlining — the systematic denial of mortgages and insurance to residents of Black neighborhoods, combined with racial covenants that prevented Black families from buying homes in white ones. Those policies were outlawed but not reversed: the wealth gap they created (homeownership is the primary way American families build wealth) persists today. The neighborhood the Youngers are trying to move into — and the white homeowner's association trying to buy them out — is not a historical artifact. Housing segregation in American cities is nearly as severe today as it was when Hansberry wrote the play. The specific argument Walter Lee and Mama have — about dignity, economic pragmatism, assimilation, and what the family owes itself — is also still being had in every community navigating the tension between economic survival and cultural identity.

docs.title: Romeo and Juliet (~1597)
docs.text: Author: William Shakespeare Type: Literary Text · Play · Tragedy Source: Public domain — written ~1594–1596, published 1597 Full Text: Project Gutenberg · Folger Shakespeare Library — annotated Era/Period: WH Era 2 — Renaissance (set in Renaissance Verona; written in Elizabethan England) WH Standards: WH.6_12.3 · WH.6_12.4 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.8 · 9-10.W.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Romeo and Juliet is Shakespeare's tragedy of two young people from feuding noble families in Verona — the Montagues and the Capulets — who fall in love, secretly marry, and die within the span of four days because private family war and public civic authority cannot solve a conflict they have made everyone else's problem. Romeo kills Juliet's cousin Tybalt in a street fight; Juliet's parents arrange her marriage to someone else; a message fails to arrive on time. The tragedy is not just about two unlucky individuals — it is about what happens when private violence (the family feud) operates in the middle of a public city and the public authority (Prince Escalus) lacks the power to stop it.

This text is dual-use in ways that are easy to overlook. As an ELA text, Romeo and Juliet is one of the most formally sophisticated plays in the Shakespeare canon — the prologue is a perfect sonnet; the play contains another sonnet hidden in Romeo and Juliet's first meeting; the verse structure tracks social status; the foil characters (Romeo vs. Mercutio, Juliet vs. the Nurse) are precisely constructed. As a Social Studies text, the play dramatizes the political reality of Renaissance Italy: the survival of private family power (the feudal clan structure) within a city trying to establish civic authority. Prince Escalus is repeatedly unable to enforce the peace because the families are more powerful than the state. This is a documented feature of Renaissance Italian political life — the struggle between emerging civic governments and entrenched noble families.

Shakespeare's audience in 1597 would have read the feud as the problem — not the love story. The modern tendency to focus entirely on the romance inverts what the play actually argues.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Public domain. Full text at Project Gutenberg.

docs.title: Act I, Scene 5 — 'Did my heart love till now?'
docs.text:
Did my heart love till now? Forswear it, sight! For I ne'er saw true beauty till this night.

— Romeo sees Juliet for the first time at the Capulet ball, and Tybalt recognizes Romeo, feeling a deep hatred towards him.

docs.title: Act II, Scene 2 — The Balcony Scene
docs.text:
But soft, what light through yonder window breaks? It is the east, and Juliet is the sun. Arise, fair sun, and kill the envious moon, Who is already sick and pale with grief...

Two hours of love's precious dawnings will shine Away eight long nights, having little strength With which to keep on our perpetual night.

— Romeo stands below Juliet's balcony in a famous declaration of his love for her.

docs.title: Act III, Scene 1 — The Turning Point
docs.text:
This day’s black fate on more days doth depend; This but begins the woe other days predict. The worm i’ th’ bud! O, woeful day!

Romeo kills Tybalt in a duel and is banished by Prince Escalus.

— Mercutio's death at the hands of Tybalt and subsequent revenge killing of Tybalt by Romeo mark the pivotal moment where the play shifts from romance to tragedy.

docs.title: Act V, Scene 3 — The Final Scene
docs.text:
Here’s to my love! O true apothecary, thy bare neelds Are sharp enough to sheathe another man. — Romeo in the tomb, having taken poison to be with Juliet and about to die.

For never was a story of more woe Than this of Juliet and her Romeo.

— The Prince's final speech upon discovering the tragic deaths of both young lovers, highlighting the devastating cost of the long-standing feud between their families.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.8 — Romeo and Juliet offers one of the richest sets of literary structures available in a single text. Sonnet form: the Prologue is a perfect Shakespearean sonnet (14 lines, ABAB CDCD EFEF GG); Romeo and Juliet's first conversation (Act I, Scene 5) is a hidden sonnet split between their dialogue — Elizabethan audiences would have recognized this as a signal of romantic destiny. Foil characters: Mercutio is everything Romeo is not — cynical, realistic, funny, unwilling to be absorbed into romantic fantasy; Juliet is more rational than Romeo almost from the start (the balcony scene, which is actually Juliet's scene, not Romeo's). Dramatic structure: students map the classic five-act tragic structure. Dramatic irony: the audience is told in the Prologue that both die — every hopeful moment is shadowed by this knowledge.
  • W.4 — The central argument the play invites is: Who is responsible for Romeo and Juliet's deaths? Shakespeare provides multiple candidates — the feuding families, the Friar's plan, the failed message, fate, the lovers' own haste, Prince Escalus's inability to enforce the peace. Students construct a position using evidence from the text, which requires weighing competing causal claims — exactly what W.4 argument writing demands.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.3 — The feud between the Montagues and Capulets is not just personal grudge — it represents the political structure of Renaissance Italian city-states, where noble families wielded private military and judicial power within cities theoretically governed by civic authorities. Prince Escalus's repeated failures to end the feud document this tension. Students analyze: What does the play reveal about the relationship between private power (noble families) and public authority (the Prince) in Renaissance Italy? How is this different from modern government? How does this connect to the broader Renaissance political context (Machiavelli's The Prince was written in 1513 for exactly this political world)?
  • WH.6_12.4 — The play dramatizes Renaissance social norms around gender, honor, and marriage that are specific to this era and culture. Juliet is 13; her parents arrange a marriage without asking her. Honor violence (Tybalt's challenge, Romeo's revenge killing) is presented as a social norm that men are obligated to participate in, even when they don't want to. Students examine: What does the play reveal about gender roles and family structure in Renaissance society? What does the honor culture require of its participants?

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Sonnet A 14-line poem with a specific rhyme scheme — Shakespeare used the form ABAB CDCD EFEF GG; the Prologue is a sonnet, and Romeo and Juliet's first meeting contains a hidden sonnet split between their dialogue Foil A character whose qualities deliberately contrast with another character's to highlight both — Mercutio is Romeo's foil (cynical vs. romantic); the Nurse is Juliet's foil (practical vs. idealistic) Dramatic irony The audience knows the outcome (announced in the Prologue) before the characters do — every hopeful scene is shadowed by knowledge of the ending Honor culture A social system in which men are required to defend their personal and family reputation through violence — central to the play's conflict and to Renaissance Italian and Elizabethan social reality Prologue The opening speech that announces the play's story — in Romeo and Juliet, it is a sonnet that tells the audience both characters die, creating dramatic irony from the first scene Feudal clan A noble family that maintains private military power and private courts of justice — the Montagues and Capulets are Renaissance versions of this medieval institution, operating inside a city trying to establish civic government

docs.title: Key Passages
docs.text:
Prologue — the sonnet (Shakespeare's framing of the whole play):

Two households, both alike in dignity, In fair Verona, where we lay our scene, From ancient grudge break to new mutiny, Where civil blood makes civil hands unclean. From forth the fatal loins of these two foes A pair of star-cross'd lovers take their life; Whose misadventured piteous overthrows Do with their death bury their parents' strife.

Act II, Scene 2 — The balcony scene (Juliet, not Romeo, is the rational one):

What's in a name? that which we call a rose By any other name would smell as sweet; So Romeo would, were he not Romeo call'd, Retain that dear perfection which he owes Without that title.

Act V, Scene 3 — Prince Escalus's final verdict:

See, what a scourge is laid upon your hate, That heaven finds means to kill your joys with love. And I for winking at your discords too Have lost a brace of kinsmen: all are punish'd.

Why these passages matter: The Prologue announces that the lovers are "star-cross'd" — but it also frames their deaths as the result of the "ancient grudge." Shakespeare is telling us from the start what this play is actually about. Juliet's balcony speech is the play's most philosophical moment — she is arguing that identity is not fixed by name or family, which is the argument the feud refuses to accept. Escalus's final lines acknowledge his own complicity — he "winked at" the violence and paid for it.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Who Killed Romeo and Juliet?" — EQ: Can a tragedy have more than one cause? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (identify at least four contributing causes to the lovers' deaths — structural, personal, political, and chance. Construct a ranked argument: which cause bears the most responsibility? Use specific evidence from the play. Consider: Is the play's answer the same as yours?)

  2. "Honor Culture Then and Now" — EQ: What does a culture's concept of honor require its members to do? · Standard: WH.6_12.4 + R.8 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (identify every moment in the play where a character acts because honor requires it. What would have happened if Tybalt had refused to fight? If Romeo had walked away? What does this reveal about how honor cultures operate? Are there modern equivalents?)

  3. "The Hidden Sonnet" — EQ: What does it mean that their first conversation is a sonnet? · Standard: R.8 · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (find Romeo and Juliet's first conversation in Act I, Scene 5. Identify the 14 lines that form a sonnet between them. Annotate: Why would Shakespeare structure their first meeting as a sonnet? What does the form signal? What happens to the sonnet when Juliet's mother interrupts them at line 14?)

  4. "Private War in a Public City" — EQ: What happens when powerful private groups don't follow public law? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + WH.6_12.4 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (research one real Renaissance Italian city — Florence, Venice, or Verona itself — and find evidence of noble family violence within the city. How did civic authorities respond? Write a comparison: How does the real history compare to what Shakespeare depicts?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Romeo and Juliet is not primarily a love story — it's a story about what inherited conflict costs people who didn't choose it. Romeo and Juliet don't hate each other's families; they were born into a feud they had no part in starting and die because neither family could stop it in time. That pattern — young people destroyed by conflicts the older generation won't resolve — is recognizable in every context where inherited enmity outlasts its original cause: gang violence, political sectarianism, ethnic conflict, family feuds. Shakespeare also shows how quickly escalation happens once it starts: Mercutio's death, then Tybalt's, then the plan that goes wrong — each step following logically from the last, none of them necessary. The play is a tragedy not because of fate but because of choices that could have been made differently at every step. That's still how most tragedies work.

docs.title: Slaughterhouse-Five (1969)
docs.text: Author: Kurt Vonnegut Type: Literary Text · Novel · Anti-War Satire Source: Delacorte (not public domain — school library copy required) Full Text: Available via Open Library — free digital borrowing (search: Slaughterhouse-Five Vonnegut) · school library copy required for extended reading Era/Period: WH Era 4 — Age of Global War and Globalization (WWII) · USH Unit 4 (WWII; also relevant to Unit 9 — Vietnam context of the novel's publication) WH Standards: WH.6_12.5 · WH.6_12.2 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.8 · 9-10.R.9 · 9-10.W.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
On the night of February 13–14, 1945, Allied bombers firebombed the German city of Dresden, killing an estimated 25,000 civilians in a city of no significant military value. Among the American prisoners of war who survived the firebombing sheltering in a slaughterhouse (Schlachthof Fünf — Slaughterhouse-Five) was Kurt Vonnegut, age 22. He spent decades trying to write about what he witnessed, and Slaughterhouse-Five is what came out: a novel that refuses to be organized, refuses to make sense of events that have no sense to make, and says "So it goes" every time someone or something dies — which happens 106 times.

The novel's protagonist, Billy Pilgrim, has "come unstuck in time." He experiences his life out of sequence: his WWII imprisonment and the Dresden firebombing, his post-war suburban life as an optometrist in Ilium, New York, and his abduction by aliens from the planet Tralfamadore, who experience all moments of time simultaneously and therefore believe there is no such thing as death — only moments that always will have been. Vonnegut opens the novel by saying he wrote it, explaining why it is the way it is, and warning readers that it is "a failure" because there is nothing intelligent to say about a massacre.

This text is dual-use in ways that reward careful examination. As an ELA text, Slaughterhouse-Five is one of the clearest available examples of non-linear narrative structure, dark humor as a literary mode, and the unreliable narrator as a response to trauma. The structural choices are not random — they are the form of Billy Pilgrim's broken mind, and of Vonnegut's own. As a Social Studies text, the novel raises historical questions that are still debated: Was the Dresden firebombing a war crime? What distinguishes legitimate military action from atrocity? The novel doesn't answer these questions — it insists they cannot be answered — which is itself an argument about how war creates moral situations that conventional reasoning cannot resolve.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Fair-use excerpts for educational use. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for the full text.

docs.title: Chapter 1 — The Author's Note
docs.text: Kurt Vonnegut introduces the novel as a confession about what happened to him in Dresden during World War II, explaining that he has struggled for 23 years to write this story.

docs.title: Chapter 2 — 'So it goes'
docs.text: Billy Pilgrim is introduced as an optometrist who has come unstuck in time. After describing an event in his life, Vonnegut writes "so it goes," a refrain used throughout the book as a way of coping with and accepting death.

docs.title: Chapter 10 — After Dresden
docs.text:
In the final chapter, Vonnegut returns to Dresden in 1967, finding birds singing and wondering, "'Poo-tee-weet?'" as an expression of confusion about why things happen the way they do.

Full text available at Internet Archive and Open Library. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for a physical copy.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.8 — Slaughterhouse-Five is one of the most structurally distinctive novels students are likely to encounter. Non-linear narrative: events are not presented in chronological order; the novel jumps between Billy's wartime past, his post-war present, and his Tralfamadorian "abduction." Students analyze: Why does Vonnegut structure the novel this way? What does a non-linear narrative do that a conventional chronological one cannot? The answer is that the non-linear structure is the trauma itself — Billy (and Vonnegut) cannot organize these events into a story because they refuse to be storified. Dark humor/satire: "So it goes" — repeated every time anything dies — begins as a darkly funny refrain and becomes, by the end, devastating. Students trace how Vonnegut uses humor as a way of bearing witness to things that cannot be borne directly. Billy Pilgrim's unreliable consciousness: the Tralfamadore episodes may be fantasy, delusion, or PTSD hallucination — Vonnegut never clarifies. Students analyze: What does the Tralfamadorian worldview (all moments exist simultaneously; there is no death) offer Billy? What does it cost him?
  • R.9 — Vonnegut's pacifist argument is embedded in the novel's structure rather than stated in its content. He never argues explicitly that Dresden was wrong; he simply refuses to let the reader experience it as anything other than the death of 25,000 civilians. The form is the argument: non-linearity, black humor, "So it goes," and the deliberate refusal to make the bombing heroic or meaningful are all argumentative choices. Students analyze: What argument does the structure of this novel make? What would be lost if it were told chronologically with a conventional war narrative arc?
  • W.4 — The historical question the novel raises: Was the Dresden firebombing justified? Students construct an argument using historical evidence — the military rationale, the casualty counts, the context of the war in February 1945 — and engage with the counterarguments. Then consider: Does Vonnegut seem to want this question answered, or does he think the question itself is wrong?

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.5 — The Dresden firebombing is a documented historical event — one of the most debated of WWII. Students research it as history: What was the strategic rationale? What was the casualty count? How did postwar historians and governments characterize it? The novel is the cultural record of what this event did to the people who survived it — which is a different kind of historical evidence than the strategic documents.
  • WH.6_12.2 — The Tralfamadorian philosophy — that all moments exist simultaneously, that death is only a temporary condition in the context of eternal time, that "So it goes" is the only appropriate response — is a belief system Vonnegut constructs to embody a specific form of fatalism. Students analyze: What does this belief system offer to someone who has survived what Vonnegut survived? What does it cost? Is fatalism a coping mechanism or a worldview? Compare to actual religious and philosophical traditions that developed similar responses to mass death — Buddhist impermanence, Stoic acceptance, Jewish mourning practices.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Non-linear narrative A story told out of chronological order — Vonnegut uses this structure not as a stylistic flourish but as the formal expression of traumatic memory, which does not organize itself chronologically Satire A literary mode that uses humor, irony, or exaggeration to criticize or expose — Vonnegut's dark humor about death is both a coping mechanism and an argument about how we narrate war Fatalism The belief that events are determined by fate and cannot be changed — the Tralfamadorian philosophy Billy adopts is a form of fatalism; the novel questions whether this is wisdom or damage Dresden The German city firebombed by Allied forces in February 1945 — Vonnegut survived the attack as a POW; he spent decades processing it before writing this novel "So it goes" Vonnegut's refrain, repeated 106 times whenever something dies — it begins as dark humor and accumulates into one of the most affecting literary devices in American fiction PTSD Post-traumatic stress disorder — the clinical term for what Billy Pilgrim (and Vonnegut) exhibit; the novel's non-linear structure can be read as a formal representation of traumatic memory

docs.title: Key Scenes and Passages
docs.text:
The novel is copyrighted — do not reproduce extended passages. Focus on these key scenes:

Chapter 1 — Vonnegut's opening: The novel opens with Vonnegut himself explaining why the book is the way it is: "I would hate to tell you what this lousy little book cost me in money and anxiety and time. When I got home from the Second World War twenty-three years ago, I thought it would be easy for me to write about the destruction of Dresden, since all I would have to do would be to report what I had seen." Students note: What does this framing do? Why does Vonnegut begin with himself rather than Billy Pilgrim?

Billy "unstuck in time": Billy's time-travel episodes — from war to suburban present to Tralfamadore — are narrated with the same flat, "So it goes" affect. Students analyze: How does Vonnegut's prose style create the feeling of dissociation? What does the flatness of his narration communicate about Billy's psychological state?

The firebombing (Chapter 9): When Dresden is finally described, it is done in the same flat tone that characterizes the whole novel — the horror is registered by what is not said. Students note: What does Vonnegut's restraint in this scene accomplish that a dramatic description would not?

"You were just babies" (Chapter 1): A woman Vonnegut contacts while researching the book says: "You were just babies in the war." The phrase haunts the novel. Students analyze: What does it mean that the people doing the killing in WWII were teenagers?

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Was Dresden a War Crime?" — EQ: Is there a moral line in warfare, and who gets to say where it is? · Standard: WH.6_12.5 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (research the Dresden firebombing: the Allied rationale, the casualty estimates, the historical debates. Then construct an argument: Was Dresden a war crime? Use historical evidence, not just the novel. Then consider: Does the question of justification matter to the people who were burned? What does Vonnegut seem to think about the question?)

  2. "Why Is the Novel Structured Like This?" — EQ: Can the form of a story be its argument? · Standard: R.8 + R.9 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (map the novel's structure: What happens in chronological order? What order does Vonnegut present events in? Why? Then argue: What does the non-linear structure do that a conventional WWII narrative could not? What would be lost if Vonnegut told this story chronologically, with a hero's arc and a meaningful ending?)

  3. "So It Goes — What Repetition Does" — EQ: How can a repeated phrase change meaning over the course of a novel? · Standard: R.8 · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (track every use of "So it goes" through the novel — or a significant sample. Annotate: What died? How does Vonnegut's tone feel in this instance? Does the phrase feel funny, numb, sad? How does the feeling change as the novel progresses? What does Vonnegut achieve by using the exact same phrase every time?)

  4. "Vonnegut and Vietnam" — EQ: Why was an anti-WWII novel published in 1969 about the Vietnam War? · Standard: WH.6_12.5 + IR.1–5 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (Slaughterhouse-Five was published in 1969, at the peak of the Vietnam War. Research the anti-war movement of 1969 and the My Lai massacre (1968). Write an analysis: Why did a novel about WWII become an anti-Vietnam War text? What did the two wars have in common, from the perspective of 1969? What does this reveal about how literature functions in political contexts?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Vonnegut was a POW in Dresden when the Allied firebombing killed somewhere between 22,000 and 25,000 people — mostly civilians — in February 1945. He spent 23 years trying to write about it before finding the form of Slaughterhouse-Five, and the book's fractured, time-unstuck structure is itself the argument: trauma doesn't organize itself chronologically, and writing about atrocity in a straight narrative would be a lie about what atrocity feels like from the inside. The novel is relevant today for several reasons. The question it raises — what is the honest way to represent mass civilian death? — is asked every time a journalist decides how to photograph a war, every time a filmmaker decides how graphic to make a combat scene, every time a government decides what casualty numbers to release. Billy Pilgrim's PTSD (before the term existed) is also a portrait of what war does to the people who fight it that is still more honest than most official accounts. And Vonnegut's dark humor — "So it goes" — is a model for how to process horror without either sanitizing it or being destroyed by it.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:

  • Lord of the Flies — Golding — Written in the same post-WWII disillusionment; different formal response
  • 1984 — Orwell — Another post-WWII novel about what the war revealed about power
  • The Handmaid's Tale — Atwood — Dystopian fiction as political argument; structural experiment

docs.title: Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937)
docs.text: Author: Zora Neale Hurston Type: Literary Text · Novel Source: J.B. Lippincott (not public domain — school library copy required) Full Text: Available via Open Library — free digital borrowing (search: Their Eyes Were Watching God Hurston) · school library copy required for extended reading Era/Period: USH Unit 2 — 1930s America; Harlem Renaissance; Black life in the Jim Crow South USH Standards: US.6_12.4 · US.6_12.3 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.8 · 9-10.W.3

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Zora Neale Hurston (1891–1960), photographed by Carl Van Vechten in 1938, the year after Their Eyes Were Watching God was published.

Their Eyes Were Watching God follows Janie Crawford, a Black woman in early 20th-century Florida, across three marriages and her search for a life and voice of her own. The novel opens with Janie returning to Eatonville, Florida — a real, historically Black-governed town — after an absence that the community regards with scandal. She tells her story to her friend Pheoby, and the novel is structured as that telling. Janie's journey takes her from a marriage arranged by her grandmother (who wants security above all), to a prosperous but emotionally stifling second marriage to the mayor of Eatonville, to a third marriage with Tea Cake — a younger man with no money and great joy — that ends in tragedy and genuine love.

Hurston was a trained anthropologist who grew up in Eatonville and studied under Franz Boas, the founder of American cultural anthropology. She collected Black folklore across the South, and Their Eyes Were Watching God is saturated with that folklore — in the dialect of its characters, in its narrative structures, and in its insistence that Black Southern vernacular speech is not broken English but a living, creative, philosophically rich language in its own right. This is not incidental to the novel — it is its central argument.

This text is dual-use in ways that deepen with attention. As an ELA text, Their Eyes Were Watching God is one of the few major American novels of its era written by a Black woman about a Black woman's interiority — her desires, her voice, her choices. Hurston's dialect is a deliberate literary choice that carries the novel's argument: that Black Southern vernacular is not inferior to "standard" English but expresses truths that standard English cannot. The frame narrative (Janie telling her story to Pheoby) locates the novel in the oral storytelling tradition Hurston studied as an anthropologist. As a Social Studies text, the novel documents the specific social geography of Black life in the Jim Crow South in the 1920s–30s: the reality of all-Black towns (Eatonville was real), the social hierarchies within Black communities, the way legal segregation shaped even the spaces of Black freedom.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Fair-use excerpts for educational use. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for the full text.

docs.title: Chapter 1 — Ships at a Distance
docs.text:
the opening paragraph — 'Ships at a distance have every man's wish on board' — the distinction between men's and women's dreams

Ships at a distance have every man’s wish on board. For some they come in with the tide. For others they sail forever on the horizon, never out of sight, never landing.

Janie watches the world around her and reflects on how people hold onto their hopes and dreams.

docs.title: Chapter 6 — The Porch
docs.text:
Janie silenced by Joe at the store — the mule's funeral as social commentary, Janie's inner voice vs. her outer silence

"You know Ah’m uh woman brung up in de house uh Gawd. Don’t matter what you say to me."

Joe's control over Janie is evident when he silences her at the store, highlighting the oppressive nature of his rule.

docs.title: Chapter 20 — The Return
docs.text:
Janie returning to Eatonville, telling Pheoby her story — 'you got tuh go there tuh know there'

"De nigger dat come out here wid de sun will go in wid it. In a way yuh oughta be sorry for me but in udduh ways yuh oughta tell mahself dat you can’t never know uh thing till after yuh live it."

Janie shares her experiences with Pheoby, emphasizing the importance of personal experience.

Full text available at Internet Archive and Open Library. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for a physical copy.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.8 — Their Eyes Were Watching God rewards literary analysis at multiple levels. Dialect and voice as literary choice: Hurston's use of Black Southern vernacular is deliberate and argued — she wrote about this choice in her essays, including "How It Feels to Be Colored Me." Students analyze specific passages in dialect and consider: What does this language do that standard English cannot? What would be lost in translation? This is one of the most powerful demonstrations available of how a novelist's language choices carry meaning. Janie's character arc: the novel tracks a specific developmental journey — Janie begins without voice (she cannot speak her own desires) and ends with it (she can tell her own story). Students trace this arc through the three marriages. Frame narrative: the novel's structure — Janie telling her story to Pheoby in the present — is drawn from oral storytelling traditions. Students analyze how this structure changes the reader's relationship to the story.
  • W.3 — The primary W.3 (explanatory writing) application: How does Hurston use dialect to make an argument about authenticity, community, and the value of Black vernacular culture? Students explain the choice, trace specific examples, and explain what the argument means in the context of 1937 — when the dominant view was that "proper" English was superior to vernacular forms.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• US.6_12.4 — The novel documents the specific social and economic reality of Black life in the Jim Crow South with unusual precision. Eatonville, Florida was (and is) a real all-Black incorporated town — one of the first in American history. Its existence represented a specific form of Black freedom within the constraints of segregation: a space where Black people governed themselves. The social hierarchies within Eatonville (Joe Starks as mayor and bourgeois gatekeeper; Tea Cake as working-class joy) document the class structures within Black communities that are often erased in broader historical accounts.
  • US.6_12.3 — Legal racial inequality in the Jim Crow South shaped the space within which the novel's events occur. Janie's marriages, her mobility, her legal status as a widow — all are constrained by the specific legal structures of Florida in the 1920s–30s. The muck (the agricultural labor camp where Janie and Tea Cake live and work) documents the economic position of Black agricultural workers in the South. Students connect the novel's specific details to the legal and economic structures of Jim Crow.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Dialect A variety of a language specific to a region, community, or social group — Hurston's use of Black Southern vernacular is not an error but a deliberate literary and political choice Vernacular The everyday spoken language of a community, as opposed to the formal or "standard" written language — Hurston argued that Black vernacular was philosophically and artistically rich Frame narrative A story within a story — Janie tells her life story to Pheoby; the novel is structured as that telling; this form is drawn from oral storytelling traditions Eatonville A real all-Black incorporated town in Florida where Hurston grew up — the novel's central setting; one of the first self-governing Black communities in American history The Harlem Renaissance A flourishing of Black artistic, literary, and intellectual life centered in Harlem, New York in the 1920s — Hurston was a major figure; the movement celebrated Black cultural heritage and pushed back against assimilationist pressure Porch In the novel, the porch of Joe Starks's store is the community's center of storytelling and social life — Janie is excluded from it, which represents her silencing; reclaiming the porch means reclaiming voice

docs.title: Key Scenes and Passages
docs.text:
The novel is copyrighted — do not reproduce extended passages. Focus on these key scenes:

Janie's pear tree vision (Chapter 2): Young Janie watches a bee pollinating a pear tree and forms her vision of what love should be — an image of mutual completion. This is the novel's romantic ideal that all three marriages are measured against. Students note: How does Hurston use this natural image to establish Janie's inner life before she has any social power?

The porch and Janie's silencing (Chapters 4–7): Joe Starks forbids Janie from participating in porch storytelling, insisting a mayor's wife does not "sit around on porches and laugh." Students analyze: What does Joe's silencing of Janie reveal about the relationship between social status and voice? What does it mean that his form of liberation (as a Black man building power) requires her oppression?

Janie speaks back (Chapter 7): After years of silence, Janie publicly humiliates Joe Starks in front of the store's male community — using the porch language he tried to deny her. Students note: What makes this moment the novel's political and personal turning point?

The muck (Chapters 14–18): Janie and Tea Cake work and live in the agricultural labor camps of the Everglades — a world defined by music, storytelling, and community among Black workers. Students connect to the economic history of Black agricultural labor in the South.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "What Hurston's Dialect Is Doing" — EQ: Can the way a story is told carry a political argument? · Standard: R.8 + W.3 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (select a key dialogue passage from the novel. Rewrite it in "standard" English. Then explain: What is lost in the rewrite? What does the original dialect carry that the standard English cannot? What is Hurston arguing by choosing dialect over "standard" English? What does that argument mean in the context of 1937?)

  2. "Three Marriages, One Arc" — EQ: What does it mean to find your own voice? · Standard: R.8 · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (create a chart tracking Janie across her three marriages. For each: What does Janie have? What is she denied? What does she discover? How does her voice change? Annotate with textual evidence. Then write: What does Hurston argue about the relationship between love and selfhood?)

  3. "Eatonville Was Real" — EQ: What does a self-governing Black community in the Jim Crow South tell us about what freedom meant under segregation? · Standard: US.6_12.3 + US.6_12.4 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (research the history of Eatonville, Florida. What conditions made it possible? What constraints surrounded it? Write an analysis: What does the existence of Eatonville reveal about the possibilities and limits of Black freedom in the Jim Crow South? How does the novel use Eatonville as a social document?)

  4. "Hurston vs. Wright" — EQ: Can two Black writers of the same era have fundamentally different visions of what Black literature should do? · Standard: R.9 + IR.1–5 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (Richard Wright reviewed Their Eyes Were Watching God harshly in 1937, arguing it was minstrelsy that avoided confronting racial injustice directly. Hurston argued Black life had value and complexity beyond its relationship to white oppression. Research this debate. Take a position: Whose vision of Black literature do you find more compelling? What does the disagreement reveal about the pressures on Black artists in this era?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Their Eyes Were Watching God is about a Black woman in the 1930s American South finding her own voice and defining her own life on her own terms — and how hard that is when every institution around you has already decided who you are. Janie's journey is not just about romantic love; it's about the difference between living inside someone else's story about you and finding your own. That question — how do you build an authentic self when the world keeps trying to assign you one? — is live for every person navigating identities they didn't choose. Hurston was also doing something politically significant that went unrecognized for decades: she was writing Black Southern life as fully human, complex, and worth literary attention at a time when the dominant literary tradition either ignored Black characters or depicted them as symbols. Richard Wright criticized the novel for not being angry enough about racism. Hurston's response (implicitly) was that Black joy, interiority, and full humanity are themselves a political act in a world that denies them. That debate is still happening.
