docs.title: Things Fall Apart (1958)
docs.text: Author: Chinua Achebe Type: Literary Text · Novel Source: Heinemann (not public domain — school library copy required) Full Text: Available via Open Library — free digital borrowing (search: Things Fall Apart Chinua Achebe) · school library copy required for extended reading Era/Period: WH Era 3 — Age of Imperialism and Colonialism (late 19th century) WH Standards: WH.6_12.5 · WH.6_12.4 · WH.6_12.3 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.8 · 9-10.R.9

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Chinua Achebe (1930–2013), author of Things Fall Apart (1958). Photographed in 2008 at the University at Buffalo.

Things Fall Apart follows Okonkwo, a respected warrior and leader in the Igbo village of Umuofia in what is now Nigeria, in the years just before and during British colonial contact in the late 1880s–1890s. The first two-thirds of the novel establish the richness and complexity of Igbo culture from the inside — its social structures, religious beliefs, gender roles, storytelling traditions, and moral codes. The final third shows the arrival of Christian missionaries and British colonial administrators, and the destruction of Okonkwo's world — not dramatically but quietly, piece by piece, as the new religion claims converts, the new courts supplant traditional justice, and the new language becomes the language of power. Okonkwo's response — rage, resistance, and finally a death that the colonizers will barely notice — is the novel's conclusion.

Achebe wrote Things Fall Apart in 1958, two years before Nigerian independence, and he wrote it explicitly as a response to Joseph Conrad's Heart of Darkness (1899), which depicted Africa as a place of primordial darkness and Africans as barely human background figures. Achebe famously called Heart of Darkness "a thoroughgoing racist novel" and wrote Things Fall Apart to show what African life actually looked like from the inside — complex, organized, morally serious — before European contact. This origin is the novel's argument: that colonialism depended on the idea that Africa had no culture worth preserving, and that idea was simply false.

This text is dual-use in an unusually direct way. As an ELA text, the novel deploys the full set of oral tradition techniques — proverbs, stories embedded within stories, the rhythms of vernacular speech — as deliberate literary choices that themselves argue against colonial cultural assumptions. Okonkwo is a tragic hero in the classical sense, but the tragedy is not just personal — it is the tragedy of a whole civilization. As a Social Studies text, this is the clearest available narrative account of how colonialism worked from the inside: how it arrived, how it divided communities, how it used cultural disruption to create political control.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Fair-use excerpts for educational use. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for the full text.

docs.title: Chapter 1 — Okonkwo's World
docs.text:
The opening description of Okonkwo's character and reputation—what he has achieved and what drives him.

Okonkwo was ruling his household with a heavy hand. He kept order by fear, not whips and rods. The little children knew no pampering from their father, and so they tended to keep clear of him.

docs.title: Chapter 15 — Abame Destroyed
docs.text:
Obierika's news about the village of Abame—the first encounter with the colonizers and their consequences.

The white man did not know what he was doing when he drove his iron horse into the town. The earth cry'd against him, but he did not listen.

docs.title: Chapter 25 — The District Commissioner's Final Thought
docs.text:
The novel's closing paragraph—the D.C. planning a paragraph in his book for Okonkwo—Achebe's devastating indictment of the colonial gaze.

Okonkwo was driven by fear, the fear of himself, of his dead father, and of his ancestors.

Full text available at Internet Archive and Open Library. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for a physical copy.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.8 — Things Fall Apart demonstrates several important literary structures. Narrative structure — the fall: Okonkwo's trajectory follows the tragic arc (rise, overreach, fall) but is embedded in a communal tragedy. Students analyze: Is this primarily Okonkwo's tragedy, or the Igbo community's? Are these separable? Okonkwo as tragic hero: his defining quality — the refusal of weakness, the equation of masculinity with hardness — is both what makes him a leader and what prevents him from adapting to change. His hamartia is not weakness but the inability to imagine strength as anything other than force. Oral tradition conventions: the novel is saturated with Igbo proverbs ("When the moon is shining the cripple becomes hungry for a walk") and with stories embedded within the narrative. Achebe's use of proverb is both characterization (wise characters speak in proverbs) and argument (proverbs embody a cultural worldview; their displacement by English is the loss of a way of thinking).
  • R.9 — This is one of the most powerful available examples of a novel making an argument by its own existence. Achebe's implicit argument: European accounts of Africa (Conrad's most famous) that depicted Africans as without culture, language, or complex social life were simply wrong, and the consequences of that wrongness were catastrophic. Students analyze the novel as a counter-argument: What specific features of Igbo culture does Achebe document? What would be lost if the only account of this world was Conrad's? What does it mean that the same year Achebe published this novel, Nigeria was becoming independent?

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.5 — The novel is the most accessible classroom account of how colonialism actually operated on communities it entered. Students trace the sequence: missionaries arrive and find converts among the community's marginalized members; the converts become a community with different rules; the colonial court arrives to settle disputes between the old community and the new; the district commissioner uses the conflict to extend British control. This is not a fictional account — it is a historically accurate model of how British colonialism proceeded across sub-Saharan Africa.
  • WH.6_12.4 — The novel's first two-thirds constitute an anthropological account of Igbo culture: its religious beliefs (chi, the personal spirit; the Oracle of the Hills and Caves), its social structures (the ofo and ogu as principles of right), its gender roles and divisions, its governance (a council of elders rather than a single chief — which means there is no single authority for the British to co-opt). Students analyze: What are Igbo cultural values? How do they compare to the colonial assumptions the missionaries and administrators bring?
  • WH.6_12.3 — The collapse of Umuofia's traditional authority structure is political as much as cultural. The Igbo governance model — decentralized, elder-based, requiring consensus — is specifically vulnerable to British legal structures that recognize only individual property ownership and central authority. Students analyze: What political structures made Igbo governance work? Why were those structures specifically vulnerable to colonial legal systems? What does this reveal about how colonialism operated politically?

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Igbo The ethnic group and language of Okonkwo's community in southeastern Nigeria — one of Nigeria's largest ethnic groups; Achebe himself was Igbo Chi In Igbo belief, a personal god or spirit that accompanies each person — Okonkwo believes his chi is not favorable; this concept of individual spiritual fate interacts with his choices throughout the novel Obi The main hut of a compound in Igbo society — the physical center of a man's household and authority; when Okonkwo returns from exile to find his burned, it signals his displacement Egwugwu Masked figures representing the ancestors who serve as judges in Umuofia's traditional court — their authority is undercut by the colonial court, which is a key mechanism of political displacement District Commissioner The British colonial official who administers the region at the novel's end — Achebe's final stroke: the commissioner plans to devote a paragraph to Okonkwo in his book "The Pacification of the Primitive Tribes of the Lower Niger"; the whole novel has been that one paragraph's refutation Proverb A short, traditional saying embodying wisdom — Achebe uses Igbo proverbs throughout as characterization and argument; their displacement by English is the displacement of a worldview

docs.title: Key Scenes and Passages
docs.text:
The novel is copyrighted — do not reproduce extended passages. Focus on these key scenes:

The opening (Chapter 1): The novel begins with Okonkwo's reputation as a wrestler and his father's failures. Achebe establishes Okonkwo's defining trait — his refusal to be like his father — as both his strength and the seed of his tragedy. Students note: How does Achebe establish character through specific concrete detail?

Ikemefuna's death (Chapter 7): Okonkwo kills Ikemefuna — a boy placed in his care who has become like a son — because the Oracle has decreed it, and because he fears appearing weak. This is widely considered the novel's moral center. Students analyze: What is Okonkwo's reasoning? What does his choice reveal about his character flaw?

The arrival of the missionaries (Chapter 16): The missionaries arrive and are given "bad land" — a forest the community considers evil. They thrive there, which becomes its own argument against Igbo belief. Students note: How does Achebe depict this moment — with contempt? With complexity? What does it reveal about how colonialism finds purchase in communities?

The district commissioner's final words (Chapter 25): The novel's last paragraph reveals that the district commissioner plans to write about Okonkwo in a book about pacifying "primitive tribes." Students analyze: What is Achebe doing with this ending? The whole novel has been what the commissioner wants to reduce to a paragraph. What does this argue?

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Achebe Was Answering Conrad" — EQ: Can a novel be a political act? · Standard: R.9 + WH.6_12.5 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (read a short excerpt from Conrad's Heart of Darkness (widely available in public domain) and compare its depiction of Africa and Africans to Achebe's. What specific claims does Conrad's text make? How does Achebe's novel respond? What does it mean that one text is considered a literary masterpiece and the other is written partly to correct it?)

  2. "How Colonialism Actually Worked" — EQ: How do you destroy a community without simply killing everyone in it? · Standard: WH.6_12.5 + WH.6_12.3 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (trace the sequence by which British colonialism arrives in Umuofia: missionaries → converts → court → commissioner. At what point does Okonkwo realize his world is over? What specific actions by the colonizers make each step possible? How does this compare to the historical record of British colonialism in Nigeria?)

  3. "The Proverbs of Umuofia" — EQ: What does a culture's proverbs reveal about its worldview? · Standard: R.8 + WH.6_12.4 · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (collect 8–10 proverbs from the novel. For each, annotate: What value or belief does this proverb express? What does it reveal about Igbo culture? What would be lost if this proverb were replaced by an English saying about the same situation? Then write: What does Achebe's use of proverbs argue about the culture the colonizers dismissed?)

  4. "Okonkwo's Fatal Flaw" — EQ: Can a person's greatest strength also be the thing that destroys them? · Standard: R.8 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (analyze Okonkwo as a tragic hero. What is his defining quality? How does it serve him in the world before colonialism? How does it prevent him from surviving the colonial world? Is his failure inevitable, or does he have choices he refuses to take? What does Achebe seem to argue about the relationship between personal flaw and historical circumstance?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Chinua Achebe wrote Things Fall Apart explicitly as a response to Conrad's Heart of Darkness — to show Africa not as a backdrop for a European character's moral journey but as a place where people lived complex, fully human lives before colonialism arrived and after it departed. The novel's title comes from Yeats, and the irony is deliberate: the "things" falling apart are not African civilization (which was already there) but the colonial imposition that couldn't be sustained. The novel is relevant today because the aftermath of colonialism — political instability, artificial borders, economic dependency, the cultural damage of being told for generations that your language and traditions are inferior — is the context for understanding most of sub-Saharan Africa's contemporary political landscape. It's also relevant because the question of whose literature belongs in the canon, who gets to tell stories about which places, and whether the Western literary tradition's representation of Africa as dark and chaotic is still shaping how people think about the continent is an ongoing argument in literary studies and journalism.

  • Kite Runner — Hosseini — Colonial aftermath and the persistence of the past
  • Lord of the Flies — Golding — Written in the same decolonization era; a very different (British) view of civilization

docs.title: To Kill a Mockingbird (1960)
docs.text: Author: Harper Lee Type: Literary Text · Novel Source: J.B. Lippincott (not public domain — school library copy required) Full Text: Available via Open Library — free digital borrowing (search: To Kill a Mockingbird Harper Lee) · school library copy required for extended reading Era/Period: USH Unit 5–6 — Depression Era South (1930s setting) / Civil Rights Era Context (1960 publication) USH Standards: US.6_12.3 · US.6_12.4 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.8 · 9-10.W.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
To Kill a Mockingbird is narrated by Scout Finch, a six-year-old girl in Maycomb, Alabama, looking back on the summers of 1933–1935 when her father, attorney Atticus Finch, defended a Black man named Tom Robinson against a false accusation of rape in a trial that the entire town already knows the outcome of. The novel interweaves Scout's childhood perspective — her friendship with Dill, her obsession with the mysterious neighbor Boo Radley — with the adult reality of racial injustice and moral cowardice that her father navigates with calm dignity. The two plots converge: Boo Radley, who Scout has feared and mythologized, turns out to be the person who saves Scout's life.

Harper Lee published this novel in 1960, at the height of the Civil Rights movement. The decision to set it in the 1930s — and to narrate it through a child's perspective — is deliberate and consequential. The 1930s setting gives the novel historical distance from the live civil rights battles of 1960; the child narrator gives readers a perspective that is morally clear but socially naive, which creates a specific kind of dramatic irony: Scout does not fully understand what she is witnessing, but the reader does. Lee won the Pulitzer Prize in 1961.

This text is dual-use in a way that is both straightforward and worth examining critically. As an ELA text, it is one of the most frequently taught American novels for good reason — the narrator's point of view, the mockingbird symbol, and the character of Atticus as a moral exemplar are clearly constructed and richly analyzable. As a Social Studies text, it documents Depression-era Alabama's racial and class structure with historical specificity. The legal injustice of Tom Robinson's trial is not fictional — it is a faithful portrait of how Jim Crow courts operated. The novel should also be examined critically: it centers a white lawyer's moral heroism in a story about Black victimization, and it depicts Atticus as the exception rather than examining the system. These limits are themselves historically instructive.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Fair-use excerpts for educational use. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for the full text.

docs.title: Chapter 10 — 'It's a Sin to Kill a Mockingbird'
docs.text:
Atticus explains to Scout why mockingbirds must not be harmed: "Your father’s right," said Jem, "mockingbirds don’t do one thing but sing their hearts out for us. That’s why it’s a sin to kill a mockingbird."

Miss Maudie clarifies the metaphorical nature of Atticus's statement about mockingbirds and their role in society: "Mockingbirds don't do one thing but make music for people to enjoy. They don't eat up people's gardens, don't nest in corncribs, they don't do one thing but sing their hearts out for us."

docs.title: Chapter 20 — Atticus's Closing Argument
docs.text:
In his summation to the jury, Atticus emphasizes the importance of justice and equality: "But there is one way in this country in which all men are created equal—there is no way in this world to judge a man but by his conscience."

Atticus argues that courts are the great equalizers, the one place where all men are truly judged equally: "Our courts have their faults, as does any human institution, but in this country our courts are the great levelers, and in our courts all men are created equal."

docs.title: Chapter 31 — Boo's Gift
docs.text:
Scout walks Boo Radley home after he saves her from Bob Ewell. Standing on his porch, she sees the neighborhood from his perspective: "Atticus was right. One time he said you never really know a man until you stand in his shoes and walk around in them."

The moral of the novel is revealed through Scout's reflection as she stands on Boo Radley’s porch: "I began to think there must be some among them that never really understood—never really understood any of us."

Full text available at Internet Archive and Open Library. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for a physical copy.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.8 — To Kill a Mockingbird is one of the most teachable texts for point of view as a literary device. Point of view — Scout as child narrator: Scout's narration creates continuous dramatic irony: she reports events she does not understand, which means the reader and Scout have different relationships to the same information. This is carefully sustained throughout — Scout repeats things adults say without grasping their implications, describes the trial through the frame of what she sees in the courthouse balcony. Students analyze specific passages where Scout's limited understanding creates meaning the character cannot access. Mockingbird symbolism: Atticus explains that it is a sin to kill a mockingbird because they do nothing but make music — they only give. The mockingbird stands for innocence destroyed by a hostile world: Tom Robinson, Boo Radley, and in a sense Scout herself. Students trace the symbol through the text. Atticus as moral exemplar: Atticus is deliberately constructed as an ideal — students analyze what Lee is arguing about moral courage by constructing him this way.
  • W.4 — The central argument the novel poses: What does justice require of a person who knows the system is unjust? Atticus loses the case; Tom Robinson is killed. Atticus continues to operate within the legal system. Students construct an argument about whether Atticus's approach — moral integrity within an unjust system — is adequate, admirable, or insufficient. This requires engaging with the real history of civil rights: what actually changed Jim Crow? Was it individual moral courage or organized collective action?

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• US.6_12.3 — Tom Robinson's trial is a portrait of how Jim Crow courts actually functioned: the testimony of a white woman against a Black man was effectively irrefutable, regardless of evidence; all-white juries were the legal norm; Black defendants had essentially no recourse. Students use the trial to analyze the specific legal mechanisms of racial injustice — not simply racism as personal prejudice but racism as institutional structure. What laws and practices made the outcome of Tom Robinson's trial a foregone conclusion?
  • US.6_12.4 — The novel carefully maps Maycomb's class structure: the Finches (educated, established), the Cunninghams (poor white farmers with dignity), the Ewells (poor white without dignity), the Black community (hardworking, churchgoing, socially organized, and legally powerless). Students analyze: How did race and class interact in Depression-era Alabama? How did the position of poor white families like the Ewells depend on being "above" Black citizens in the racial hierarchy, even when economically they were not above anyone?

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Dramatic irony The gap between what a narrator understands and what the reader understands — Scout's childhood perspective creates this continuously throughout the novel Mockingbird Atticus's symbol for innocence that exists only to give — Tom Robinson, Boo Radley, and Scout are all versions of this figure; to harm them is the novel's definition of sin Jim Crow The legal system of racial segregation in the American South from 1877 to the mid-1960s — the system that made Tom Robinson's conviction a structural certainty regardless of his innocence Scottsboro Boys A real 1931 Alabama case in which nine Black teenagers were falsely accused of rape by two white women — widely understood as the historical inspiration for Tom Robinson's trial Atticus Finch The novel's moral center — a white attorney who defends Tom Robinson knowing he will lose; Lee constructs him as a model of moral courage; he has also been criticized for working within the system rather than challenging it Boo Radley The reclusive neighbor Scout mythologizes as a monster and then discovers is a protector — one of the novel's primary mockingbirds; the resolution of Scout's fear of him is the novel's emotional climax

docs.title: Key Scenes and Passages
docs.text:
The novel is copyrighted — do not reproduce extended passages. Focus on these key scenes:

The courthouse balcony (Chapter 21): Scout watches the trial from the Black balcony with Reverend Sykes. When Atticus leaves after the verdict, every Black person in the balcony stands. Reverend Sykes tells Scout: "Miss Jean Louise, stand up. Your father's passing." Students analyze: What does this moment mean? Why does the Black community honor Atticus while Scout doesn't fully understand why?

Atticus shoots the dog (Chapter 10): Atticus, whom Scout considers a boring old man with no skills, turns out to be "Ol' One-Shot Finch" — the best marksman in the county, who gave up his rifle because he feared his skill gave him an unfair advantage. Students analyze: What does this scene reveal about Atticus's character? What does it argue about moral courage vs. physical courage?

Atticus's closing argument (Chapter 20): Atticus's speech to the jury is the novel's explicit moral statement — that the court is the place where all men are equal in theory, whatever the reality outside it. Students analyze: What is Atticus's argument? Is it true? What does his own case demonstrate about its limits?

The ending — Boo Radley (Chapter 31): Scout walks Boo home and imagines seeing the neighborhood from his perspective, then thinks of something Atticus told her. Students analyze: What does Atticus's lesson about empathy ("climb into his skin and walk around in it") finally mean to Scout at the novel's end?

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "The Trial Was Rigged Before It Began" — EQ: What is the difference between a legal system and a just system? · Standard: US.6_12.3 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (research the Scottsboro Boys case and at least two other documented cases of racially biased trials in the Jim Crow South. Compare to Tom Robinson's trial. Construct an argument: Was the outcome of Tom Robinson's trial a failure of the system, or was it the system working exactly as designed? What would need to change for a different outcome?)

  2. "Scout Doesn't Know What She's Seeing" — EQ: What does a naive narrator reveal that an omniscient one cannot? · Standard: R.8 · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (identify five passages where Scout's narration contains information she doesn't understand but the reader does. For each, annotate: What does Scout see/report? What does the reader understand that Scout doesn't? What does Lee achieve by putting this information through Scout's perspective?)

  3. "Is Atticus's Way Enough?" — EQ: Does individual moral courage change unjust systems? · Standard: US.6_12.3 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (Atticus defends Tom Robinson with full integrity and loses. Tom Robinson dies. Maycomb doesn't change. Yet the novel presents Atticus as heroic. Research how Jim Crow actually ended — not through the moral courage of individual white lawyers but through organized mass action, legislative change, and federal intervention. Argue: Is Atticus a model to follow, a comfort myth, or both?)

  4. "Maycomb's Social Map" — EQ: How do race and class interact to create social hierarchy? · Standard: US.6_12.4 + R.8 · Demo Mode: Visual/Creative (create a social map of Maycomb showing the class and race hierarchy Lee constructs — the Finches, the Cunninghams, the Ewells, the Black community. Annotate each group's economic position, social status, and relationship to legal power. Then research Depression-era Alabama: How accurate is Lee's portrait? What does the Ewell family's position reveal about how racial hierarchy functions even among poor white people?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: To Kill a Mockingbird is the most-taught novel in American schools, and it is more complicated than it's often presented. Atticus Finch is a good man who defends Tom Robinson knowing he will lose, and he loses — the jury convicts despite the evidence, and Tom Robinson dies. The novel is not a story about how the legal system works when a good lawyer shows up; it's a story about the limits of individual virtue inside a systematically unjust system. That distinction matters today: debates about "good cops" vs. systemic racism, "good employers" vs. structural economic inequality, individual character vs. institutional design all turn on exactly the question the novel raises. The criticism that the novel centers a white savior rather than Tom Robinson's perspective is also worth taking seriously — it's an argument about whose story gets told and from whose point of view, which is the central question in every debate about representation in literature and media.

docs.title: Warriors Don't Cry (1994)
docs.text: Author: Melba Pattillo Beals Type: Literary Text · Memoir · Primary Source (Civil Rights) Source: Pocket Books / Washington Square Press (not public domain — school library copy required) Full Text: Available via school library or Internet Archive lending Era/Period: US History Unit 6 — Civil Rights · Era 4 — Global War and Globalization USH Standards: US.6_12.2 · US.6_12.5 WH Standards: WH.6_12.4 · WH.6_12.6 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.8 · 9-10.R.9 · 9-10.W.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Integration protest outside Little Rock Central High School, 1957 — the year Melba Pattillo Beals was one of the Little Rock Nine.

Warriors Don't Cry is the memoir of Melba Pattillo Beals, one of the nine Black students who integrated Little Rock Central High School in September 1957. The "Little Rock Nine" were legally entitled to attend Central High following the Supreme Court's 1954 Brown v. Board of Education ruling — but faced a mob of hundreds of white protesters, the Arkansas National Guard (deployed by Governor Faubus to block them), and, when President Eisenhower finally intervened, a year of daily physical and psychological terror inside the school even with federal troops present. Beals wrote the memoir decades later using the diary she kept as a fifteen-year-old. It is simultaneously a coming-of-age story, a war narrative, and a primary source document of one of the defining confrontations of the Civil Rights Movement.

This text is a primary source for history. Beals was there. Her diary entries are first-hand accounts of events documented in historical records. The memoir should be used both as a literary text (character, voice, memoir form) and as a historical document (what integration actually looked and felt like on the ground).

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Fair-use excerpts for educational use. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for the full text.

docs.title: September 4, 1957 — The First Day
docs.text:
It was a day of terror and uncertainty as Melba Pattillo Beals approached Central High School, facing an angry mob and the National Guard who refused to let them in.

She describes the fear that gripped her and her fellow students as they were turned away by armed soldiers, marking the start of their battle for desegregation.

docs.title: October 1957 — Daily Survival
docs.text:
Within the walls of Central High, Beals recounts the relentless harassment faced daily. Simple tasks became acts of defiance, like eating lunch in a cafeteria where hostility was constant.

Her narration captures the mundane yet cruel nature of the discrimination that she and other black students endured each day.

docs.title: Epilogue — What It Cost and What It Meant
docs.text:
In her reflections at the end of "Warriors Don't Cry," Beals looks back on what they all went through to challenge segregation.

She emphasizes how this fight for integration was a necessary stand against injustice, despite the personal toll it took.

Full text available at Internet Archive and Open Library. Connect with Mr. Reese or the Fargo South Library for a physical copy.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.8 — Beals is a complex narrator: she is simultaneously a frightened teenager and a deliberate historian writing decades later. Students analyze how her dual perspective shapes the memoir — what does the adult Beals understand that the fifteen-year-old couldn't? Where does retrospective meaning change or deepen the original diary entries?
  • R.9 — The memoir makes an implicit argument throughout: that the resistance to integration was not just racist individuals but a system — school officials, state government, the media, the silence of "moderate" white citizens. Students map this argument without it ever being stated directly.
  • W.4 — Use as a W.4 mentor text for argument grounded in personal testimony. The memoir demonstrates how first-hand experience functions as evidence — and the limits of personal testimony as proof of systemic claims.
  • R.2 — The memoir rewards inference work: what Beals chooses not to say, what she says matter-of-factly that the reader recognizes as extraordinary, and the gap between her diary voice and her adult voice.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• US.6_12.2 — The Little Rock Crisis is one of the defining confrontations between federal authority and state resistance to desegregation. Beals' memoir documents both the legal framework (Brown, the Civil Rights Act) and the lived reality of what "enforcement" actually required. Primary source evidence of the gap between legal rights and lived experience.
  • US.6_12.5 — The causes and effects of the Little Rock Crisis are multilayered: political (Eisenhower's reluctant intervention), legal (federal vs. state authority), social (the psychology of white resistance), and personal (the long-term effects on the nine students). Beals documents all of these simultaneously.
  • WH.6_12.4 — The Cold War context of Little Rock is underappreciated: the Soviet Union broadcast images of federal troops escorting Black students into school as propaganda showing American democracy's hypocrisy. The Civil Rights Movement was partly shaped by the Cold War imperative to not let the Soviets define American democracy.
  • WH.6_12.6 — The gap between legal rights and lived experience that Beals documents in 1957 connects directly to present-day debates about structural racism, school segregation, and what "equality" means in practice.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Little Rock Nine The nine Black students who integrated Little Rock Central High School in September 1957, under federal court order following Brown v. Board of Education Brown v. Board of Education 1954 Supreme Court ruling declaring racial segregation in public schools unconstitutional Governor Faubus Arkansas Governor Orval Faubus, who deployed the National Guard to block the nine students — directly defying a federal court order 101st Airborne The Army division Eisenhower deployed to enforce integration — the first use of federal troops to enforce civil rights in the South since Reconstruction Integration The desegregation of schools, public facilities, and institutions — required by law but requiring enforcement against active resistance

docs.title: Key Chapter Passages
docs.text:
Chapter 2 — First Day Attempt (September 4, 1957):

Somebody was throwing rocks. I looked around but couldn't see who was doing it. Then I felt a stinging on my back, like somebody had thrown a stone, but when I looked around, there was nobody there. It was glass from a bottle somebody had thrown. Shards of glass were in my hair.

Chapter 7 — Daily Life Inside Central (October 1957):

I walked to the mirror to see who I was becoming. Was I really the person who had laughed and played in Grandmother India's kitchen? The face in the mirror looked frightened and exhausted... I thought about Danny and how he had said being a warrior means being willing to die for what you believe in. Warriors don't cry, Grandma India had always said. Well, I was crying on the inside. I was bleeding on the inside.

Why these passages matter: The first passage is history in real time — the physical violence is documented in newspaper photographs and federal reports. The second passage is what history costs the people who make it. Both are required for understanding what the Civil Rights Movement actually was.

docs.title: Chapter Guide
docs.text: Chapter Historical Event ELA Focus 1–3 Brown decision context; first day at Central; mob violence Setting-character interaction; memoir vs. diary voice 4–7 First weeks inside Central; daily harassment; troop presence Cause-effect narrative; complex character (Beals as narrator) 8–12 Escalating violence; Eisenhower intervenes; media coverage Author's POV; retrospective vs. contemporaneous narration 13–17 Loss of Minnijean Brown; isolation; psychological endurance Theme development; WH.6_12.6 long-term effects Epilogue Adult Beals looks back Multiple interpretations; the cost of historical change

docs.title: Pairs With
docs.text:
• MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — King writes the theology of the movement; Beals documents its daily human cost. Read together: the philosophy and the reality.
  • FDR — Four Freedoms Speech — FDR promises freedom "everywhere in the world" in 1941; Beals documents what freedom looked like in Arkansas in 1957.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "The Nine" — EQ: What does it cost a person to make history? · Standard: US.6_12.2 + R.8 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (choose one of the nine students and research their life after Little Rock — what were the long-term effects? Was the sacrifice worth it? Write an argument with evidence)

  2. "Law vs. Reality" — EQ: What is the difference between having a legal right and actually having it? · Standard: US.6_12.2 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (using both Warriors Don't Cry and the text of Brown v. Board, write an argument: Did the Supreme Court's ruling actually desegregate Little Rock? What would "actual" desegregation require?)

  3. "Cold War Civil Rights" — EQ: Did the United States integrate its schools because it was right or because it was strategically necessary? · Standard: WH.6_12.4 + US.6_12.5 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (research the Cold War propaganda angle on Little Rock — what did Soviet media say? Does knowing that change how you understand Eisenhower's decision to intervene?)

  4. "Warriors Don't Cry" — EQ: What does it mean to be strong in a situation designed to break you? · Standard: R.8 + R.2 · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (annotate 5 passages where Beals describes her internal emotional state — trace how her definition of "warrior" changes or deepens over the memoir)

docs.title: Sensitive Content Note
docs.text: This memoir contains detailed descriptions of racial violence, targeted harassment, and psychological abuse directed at minors. This content is historically accurate and necessary to the memoir's purpose and to understanding the Civil Rights Movement. Frame all discussions with care. Students from communities with direct experience of racial violence may need additional support.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Melba Beals was fifteen years old when she integrated Little Rock Central High School in 1957. The federal government sent the 101st Airborne Division to enforce integration — and then, a few weeks later, withdrew them, leaving the nine students to manage on their own. Warriors Don't Cry is a firsthand account of what it actually cost nine young people to do something that adults with power had decided needed to be done, and then left them to do alone. That pattern — young people being placed at the front lines of social change that adults support in principle but don't want to risk personally — appears in every era. Climate activists like Greta Thunberg, student survivors of school shootings advocating for gun control, and undocumented students advocating for DACA are all examples of young people doing work that adult institutions support rhetorically but don't sustain practically. The memoir is also a counter to the sanitized version of the Civil Rights movement that treats integration as inevitable and painless.

docs.text:

docs.title: The Analects — Selected Passages (c. 500 BCE)
docs.text: Author: Confucius (Kong Qiu); compiled by his students Type: Primary Source · Philosophy / Governance Text Source: Public domain — Chinese Text Project and Legge translation via Project Gutenberg Era/Period: Era 1 — Ancient Civilizations WH Standards: WH.6_12.2 · WH.6_12.3 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.4 · 9-10.R.9

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Key selections from Books I, II, and XII — James Legge translation, public domain. Full text at Project Gutenberg.

docs.title: Book I — On Learning and Character
docs.text:
I.1 The Master said, 'Is it not pleasant to learn with a constant perseverance and application? Is it not delightful to have friends coming from distant quarters? Is he not a man of complete virtue, who feels no discomposure though men may take no note of him?'

I.2 The philosopher Yu said, 'They are few who, being filial and fraternal, are fond of offending against their superiors. There have been none, who, not liking to offend against their superiors, have been fond of stirring up confusion. The superior man bends his attention to what is radical. That being established, all practical courses naturally grow up. Filial piety and fraternal submission — are they not the root of all benevolent actions?'

I.3 The Master said, 'Fine words and an insinuating appearance are seldom associated with true virtue.'

I.4 The philosopher Tsang said, 'I daily examine myself on three points: whether, in transacting business for others, I may have been not faithful; whether, in intercourse with friends, I may have been not sincere; whether I may have not mastered and practised the instructions of my teacher.'

I.5 The Master said, 'To rule a country of a thousand chariots, there must be reverent attention to business, and sincerity; economy in expenditure, and love for men; and the employment of the people at the proper seasons.'

I.8 The Master said, 'If the scholar be not grave, he will not call forth any veneration, and his learning will not be solid. Hold faithfulness and sincerity as first principles. Have no friends not equal to yourself. When you have faults, do not fear to abandon them.'

I.15 Tsze-kung said, 'What do you pronounce concerning the poor man who yet does not flatter, and the rich man who is not proud?' The Master replied, 'They will do; but they are not equal to him, who, though poor, is yet cheerful, and to him, who, though rich, loves the rules of propriety.'

I.16 The Master said, 'I will not be afflicted at men's not knowing me; I will be afflicted that I do not know men.'

docs.title: Book II — On Governance and Virtue
docs.text:
II.1 The Master said, 'He who exercises government by means of his virtue may be compared to the north polar star, which keeps its place and all the stars turn towards it.'

II.3 The Master said, 'If the people be led by laws, and uniformity sought to be given them by punishments, they will try to avoid the punishment, but have no sense of shame. If they be led by virtue, and uniformity sought to be given them by the rules of propriety, they will have the sense of shame, and moreover will become good.'

II.4 The Master said, 'At fifteen, I had my mind bent on learning. At thirty, I stood firm. At forty, I had no doubts. At fifty, I knew the decrees of Heaven. At sixty, my ear was an obedient organ for the reception of truth. At seventy, I could follow what my heart desired, without transgressing what was right.'

II.11 The Master said, 'If a man keeps cherishing his old knowledge, so as continually to be acquiring new, he may be a teacher of others.'

II.15 The Master said, 'Learning without thought is labour lost; thought without learning is perilous.'

II.17 The Master said, 'Yu, shall I teach you what knowledge is? When you know a thing, to hold that you know it; and when you do not know a thing, to allow that you do not know it — this is knowledge.'

II.19 The Duke Ai asked, saying, 'What should be done in order to secure the submission of the people?' Confucius replied, 'Advance the upright and set aside the crooked, then the people will submit. Advance the crooked and set aside the upright, then the people will not submit.'

II.20 Chi K'ang asked how to cause the people to reverence their ruler, to be faithful to him, and to go on to nerve themselves to virtue. The Master said, 'Let him preside over them with gravity — then they will reverence him. Let him be filial and kind to all — then they will be faithful to him. Let him advance the good and teach the incompetent — then they will eagerly seek to be virtuous.'

docs.title: Book XII — On Perfect Virtue and Just Government
docs.text:
XII.1 Yen Yuan asked about perfect virtue. The Master said, 'To subdue one's self and return to propriety, is perfect virtue. If a man can for one day subdue himself and return to propriety, all under heaven will ascribe perfect virtue to him. Is the practice of perfect virtue from a man himself, or is it from others?' Yen Yuan said, 'I beg to ask the steps of that process.' The Master replied, 'Look not at what is contrary to propriety; listen not to what is contrary to propriety; speak not what is contrary to propriety; make no movement which is contrary to propriety.'

XII.2 Chung-kung asked about perfect virtue. The Master said, 'It is, when you go abroad, to behave to every one as if you were receiving a great guest; to employ the people as if you were assisting at a great sacrifice; not to do to others as you would not wish done to yourself; to have no murmuring against you in the country, and none in the family.'

XII.7 Tsze-kung asked about government. The Master said, 'The requisites of government are that there be sufficiency of food, sufficiency of military equipment, and the confidence of the people in their ruler.' Tsze-kung said, 'If it cannot be helped, and one of these must be dispensed with, which of the three should be foregone first?' 'The military equipment,' said the Master. Tsze-kung again asked, 'If it cannot be helped, and one of the remaining two must be dispensed with, which of them should be foregone?' The Master answered, 'Part with the food. From of old, death has been the lot of all men; but if the people have no faith in their rulers, there is no standing for the state.'

XII.9 The Duke Ai inquired of Yu Zo, saying, 'The year is one of scarcity, and the returns for expenditure are not sufficient — what is to be done?' Yu Zo replied, 'Why not simply tithe the people?' 'With two tenths,' said the duke, 'I find it not enough — how could I do with that system of one tenth?' Yu Zo answered, 'If the people have plenty, their prince will not be left to want alone. If the people are in want, their prince cannot enjoy plenty alone.'

XII.11 The Duke Ching, of Ch'i, asked Confucius about government. Confucius replied, 'There is government, when the prince is prince, and the minister is minister; when the father is father, and the son is son.' 'Good!' said the duke; 'if, indeed; the prince be not prince, the minister not minister, the father not father, and the son not son, although I have my revenue, can I enjoy it?'

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Portrait of Confucius (551–479 BCE), traditionally attributed to Wu Daozi, Tang dynasty copy.

Confucius (551–479 BCE) was a Chinese philosopher, teacher, and political advisor who lived during the Spring and Autumn period — an era of fragmented Chinese states, constant warfare, and political instability. His teachings, compiled after his death by his students into the Analects (literally "edited conversations"), argue that good government rests not on law and punishment but on virtue, ritual, and the cultivation of character. If rulers and ministers embody virtue, subjects will follow. If they don't, no amount of law can create a stable society.

Why this text belongs in the reading library: The Analects represent one of the most influential political philosophies in human history — shaping Chinese governance for over 2,000 years, influencing Japanese, Korean, and Vietnamese political culture, and producing a conception of the relationship between individual character and political order that is fundamentally different from the Greek, Roman, and Abrahamic traditions that dominate Western curricula. Reading it alongside Hammurabi's Code and Pericles' Funeral Oration — all responding to the same basic question (how do you make a society work?) — reveals how differently the ancient world answered that question.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.4 — The Analects is an aphoristic text — its "main ideas" are not argued linearly but stated as wisdom observations. Students practice identifying theme from aphorism: What is Confucius' central claim about governance? About human nature? About learning? Identifying themes from scattered observations is a different R.4 skill than tracing a developed argument.
  • R.9 — The Analects implicitly argue against a competing theory of governance (legalism — the idea that strict law and punishment, not virtue, are what make states stable). Students identify this implicit argument and compare it to arguments made by other governance texts in the library.
  • R.2 — Many passages are compressed to the point of requiring inference: "He who learns but does not think is lost. He who thinks but does not learn is in great danger." Students practice unpacking aphoristic language — what exactly does this mean? What examples would support it?

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.2 — Confucianism functions as both a philosophical system and a governing ideology — analogous to how Christianity shaped European medieval governance, or how Islam shaped the political order of the dar al-Islam. Students understand that the major civilizations of the ancient world each developed their own framework for how moral order and political order relate.
  • WH.6_12.3 — Confucius argues for a meritocratic political order (the junzi, or "gentleman scholar," should govern by virtue, not birth) at the same historical moment Pericles argues for democratic participation in Athens. Comparing the two reveals two fundamentally different answers to the question "who should govern and why?"

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Ren Benevolence, humaneness, goodness — the central virtue in Confucian ethics; the quality a person must cultivate to govern well Li Ritual propriety — the correct performance of social roles and relationships; Confucius argues this is the foundation of social order Junzi The "gentleman" or "exemplary person" — not nobility by birth but a person of cultivated virtue; Confucius' model citizen and ruler Zhengming "Rectification of names" — Confucius' argument that social order requires that words correspond to realities: a ruler who doesn't rule justly shouldn't be called a ruler Five Relationships The five key social relationships in Confucian thought: ruler-subject, parent-child, husband-wife, elder-younger sibling, friend-friend. Each has defined duties flowing both ways.

docs.title: Selected Passages
docs.text:
On governance by virtue (Book 2):

He who exercises government by means of his virtue may be compared to the north polar star, which keeps its place and all the stars turn towards it.

On self-cultivation (Book 1):

Is he not a man of complete virtue, who feels no discomposure though men may take no note of him?

On learning (Book 2):

Learning without thought is labor lost; thought without learning is perilous.

On the rectification of names (Book 13):

If names be not correct, language is not in accordance with the truth of things. If language be not in accordance with the truth of things, affairs cannot be accomplished... Therefore a superior man considers it necessary that the names he uses may be spoken appropriately, and also that what he speaks may be carried out appropriately.

On the ruler's responsibility (Book 12):

The superior man is satisfied and composed; the mean man is always full of distress.

If the people have no faith in their rulers, there is no standing for the state.

On benevolence (Book 12):

Fan Ch'ih asked about benevolence. The Master said, "It is to love all men." He asked about knowledge. The Master said, "It is to know all men."

docs.title: Comparing Governance Philosophies
docs.text: Question Hammurabi Pericles Confucius What makes a state stable? Clear laws and proportional punishment Civic participation and democratic equality Virtuous rulers and proper social relationships Who should govern? The king (by divine appointment) Citizens (by democratic participation) The most virtuous (by self-cultivation) What is the citizen's role? Obey and appeal to law Participate in self-governance Fulfill one's social role virtuously What corrects bad governance? The law applies to the king too Democratic accountability The ruler's shame and loss of legitimacy What it leaves out Democratic voice Women, non-citizens, enslaved Independent political rights

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Three Answers to One Question" — EQ: What makes a government legitimate? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + R.9 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (compare Confucius' theory of virtuous governance to Pericles' theory of democratic governance — both are answering the same question. Which is more convincing? Which is more practical? Which is more just? Argue a position with evidence from both texts)

  2. "Virtue vs. Law" — EQ: Is a good society built by good rules or good people? · Standard: WH.6_12.2 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (Confucius argues virtue; Hammurabi argues law. Write an argument for which theory better explains how societies actually work — use at least two historical examples)

  3. "The Rectification of Names" — EQ: Does language create reality, or describe it? · Standard: R.4 + C.6 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (Confucius argues that if a ruler doesn't rule justly, they shouldn't be called a ruler — "names" should correspond to reality. Apply this principle to a contemporary political example: Is there a case where what we call something doesn't match what it actually is? Does it matter?)

  4. "Confucianism 2,500 Years Later" — EQ: Can a philosophy survive 2,500 years of history and still be relevant? · Standard: WH.6_12.6 + IR.1–5 · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (research how Confucianism has been used — and misused — to justify governance in China from the Han dynasty to Xi Jinping. Annotate three uses: what part of Confucius is being invoked? Is it an accurate reading? What does it reveal about how powerful ideas get appropriated?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Confucianism is undergoing a political revival. The Chinese government has heavily promoted Confucian values — social harmony, respect for authority, the family as a model for the state — as an alternative framework to Western liberal democracy, funding Confucius Institutes on university campuses globally as part of that project. This makes the Analects more politically significant than they've been in decades, but also more contested: Is contemporary state Confucianism a genuine philosophical tradition, or a legitimating ideology for authoritarian governance? Beyond China, Confucian ideas about education as moral formation (not just credentialing), about the reciprocal obligations between leaders and the people they lead, and about social harmony as a value alongside individual rights genuinely challenge Western liberal assumptions — and are worth engaging seriously on their own terms. The Analects also have something to say about the current crisis in civic trust: Confucius argued that legitimacy depends on the ruler being worthy of loyalty, and that government loses the "mandate of heaven" when it stops serving the people. That's a live argument in any political system.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Era 1 — Ancient Civilizations
  • Hammurabi's Code — Law-based governance; same question, different answer
  • Pericles' Funeral Oration — Democratic governance; same question, different answer
  • Melian Dialogue — What governance looks like when virtue and law are stripped away and only power remains

docs.title: The Egyptian Book of the Dead (c. 1550–50 BCE)
docs.text: Author: Ancient Egyptian scribes and priests Type: Primary Source · Religious/Funerary Text Era/Period: WH Era 1 — Ancient Civilizations Standards: WH.6_12.2 · WH.6_12.3 · WH.6_12.4 · R.9

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
The Weighing of the Heart from the Papyrus of Ani (c. 1275 BCE). British Museum. The deceased's heart is weighed against the feather of Ma'at.

The Book of the Dead is a collection of spells, prayers, and instructions written on papyrus scrolls and placed in tombs to guide the dead through the Egyptian afterlife. The most important section — the Negative Confession and the Weighing of the Heart — reveals what ancient Egyptians believed about justice, morality, and what a good life looked like. It is one of the oldest moral codes in human history, predating the Ten Commandments by centuries.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Translation by E.A. Wallis Budge (public domain). Three key selections below.

docs.title: The Negative Confession (Spell 125) — Declarations Before the Gods
docs.text:
When the dead person reaches the Hall of Two Truths, they must recite these declarations before 42 gods. Each declaration is a denial of a sin. This is the Egyptian version of a moral code — not "here is what you must do" but "here is what I have not done."

1. Hail, Usekh-nemmt, who comest forth from Anu, I have not committed sin.

2. Hail, Hept-khet, who comest forth from Kher-aha, I have not committed robbery with violence.

3. Hail, Fenti, who comest forth from Khemenu, I have not stolen.

4. Hail, Am-khaibit, who comest forth from Qernet, I have not slain men or women.

5. Hail, Neha-her, who comest forth from Rasta, I have not stolen grain.

6. Hail, Ruruti, who comest forth from Heaven, I have not purloined offerings.

7. Hail, Arfi-em-khet, who comest forth from Suat, I have not stolen the property of the gods.

8. Hail, Neba, who comest and goest, I have not uttered lies.

9. Hail, Set-qesu, who comest forth from Hensu, I have not wasted food.

10. Hail, Utu-nesert, who comest forth from Het-ka-Ptah, I have not uttered curses.

11. Hail, Qerrti, who comest forth from Amentet, I have not committed adultery.

12. Hail, Her-f-ha-f, who comest forth from thy cavern, I have made none to weep.

13. Hail, Basti, who comest forth from Bast, I have not eaten the heart [i.e., I have not grieved uselessly or felt consuming envy].

14. Hail, Ta-retiu, who comest forth from the night, I have not attacked any man.

15. Hail, Unem-snef, who comest forth from the execution chamber, I am not a man of deceit.

16. Hail, Unem-besek, who comest forth from Mabit, I have not stolen cultivated land.

17. Hail, Neb-Maat, who comest forth from Maati, I have not been an eavesdropper.

18. Hail, Tenemiu, who comest forth from Bast, I have not slandered a man.

19. Hail, Sertiu, who comest forth from Anu, I have not been angry without just cause.

20. Hail, Tutu, who comest forth from Ati, I have not debauched the wife of any man.

21. Hail, Uamenti, who comest forth from the Khebt chamber, I have not debauched the wife of any man. [repeated for emphasis in the original]

22. Hail, Maa-anuf, who comest forth from Per-Menu, I have not polluted myself.

23. Hail, Her-uru, who comest forth from Nehatu, I have terrorized none.

24. Hail, Khemiu, who comest forth from Kaui, I have not transgressed the law.

25. Hail, Shet-kheru, who comest forth from Urit, I have not been wroth.

26. Hail, Nekhenu, who comest forth from Heqat, I have not shut my ears to the words of truth.

27. Hail, Kenemti, who comest forth from Kenmet, I have not blasphemed.

28. Hail, An-astf, who comest forth from thy secret place, I am not a man of violence.

29. Hail, Sera-kheru, who comest forth from Unaset, I have not been a stirrer up of strife.

30. Hail, Neb-heru, who comest forth from Netchfet, I have acted with undue haste.

31. Hail, Sekhiu, who comest forth from Uten, I have not judged hastily.

32. Hail, Neb-abui, who comest forth from Sauti, I have not taken vengeance upon the god.

33. Hail, Neferiu, who comest forth from Het-nefert, I have not multiplied my speech overmuch.

34. Hail, Tem-Sepu, who comest forth from Tetu, I have never cursed the king.

35. Hail, Ari-em-ab-f, who comest forth from Tebu, I have not fouled the water.

36. Hail, Ihi, who comest forth from Nu, I have not spoken with a high voice.

37. Hail, Uatch-rekhit, who comest forth from Sau, I have not cursed the god.

38. Hail, Neheb-kau, who comest forth from thy cavern, I have not stolen from the god.

39. Hail, Tcheser-tep, who comest forth from the shrine, I have not defrauded the offerings of the gods.

40. Hail, An-a-f, who comest forth from Auker, I have not plundered the offerings to the blessed dead.

41. Hail, Hetch-abhu, who comest forth from Ta-she, I have not filched the food of the infant, neither have I sinned against the god of my native town.

42. Hail, Unem-hauau, who comest forth from Mant, I have not slaughtered with evil intent the cattle of the god.

docs.title: The Weighing of the Heart
docs.text:
After the Negative Confession, the dead person's heart is weighed against the feather of Ma'at (truth/justice) on a scale. Anubis, the jackal-headed god, manages the scales. Thoth, the ibis-headed scribe god, records the result. If the heart is heavier than the feather — burdened by sin — the monster Ammit devours it, and the person ceases to exist. If it balances, the person enters eternal life.

O my heart which I had from my mother! O my heart of my different ages! Do not stand up as a witness against me, do not be opposed to me in the tribunal, do not be hostile to me in the presence of the Keeper of the Scales! You are my ka which was in my body, the Khnum who made my limbs sound. Go to the happy place to which we hasten; do not make my name stink to the courtiers who make men. Do not tell lies about me in the presence of the god; it is indeed well that you should hear!

Thus says Thoth, Judge of Truth of the Great Ennead which is in the presence of Osiris: Hear this word of very truth. I have judged the heart of the deceased, and his soul stands as a witness for him. His deeds are righteous in the great balance, and no sin has been found in him. He has not diminished the offerings in the temples, he has not destroyed what has been made, he has not gone about with deceitful speech while he was on earth.

The Great Ennead then says to Thoth who dwells in Khemenu: This utterance of yours is true. The vindicated one, Osiris, is straightforward. He has no sin, there is no accusation against him before us. Ammit shall not be permitted to have power over him. Let there be given to him offerings which Osiris issued and a permanent grant of land in the Field of Offerings as for the Followers of Horus.

docs.title: Spell 17 (excerpt) — The Creation
docs.text:
This short passage from Spell 17 describes the primordial moment of creation — before the world existed, there was only Nun, the watery chaos. The god Atum speaks himself into existence.

I am Atum when I was alone in Nun. I am Ra in his first appearances when he began to rule that which he had made. Who is this? It is Ra, when he began to rule that which he had made, when he began to appear as a king, before the props of Shu had been created, when he was on the hill which is in Hermopolis.

I am the Great God who came into being of himself. Who is this? The Great God who came into being of himself is Water, that is Nun, the father of the gods. Otherwise said: He is Ra. He created his names, the lord of the Ennead. Who is this? He is Ra, creator of the names of his limbs which came into being in the form of the gods in the retinue of Ra.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.9 — The Negative Confession is a structured argumentative form: 42 parallel claims of innocence before divine authority. Students analyze what each denial reveals about what Egyptian society considered a sin — this is evidence-based inference from a primary source.
  • R.7 — The repeated structure ("Hail, [god], who comest forth from [place], I have not...") is a deliberate rhetorical and ritual choice. Students analyze how the form of the Negative Confession creates its meaning.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.2 — The Book of the Dead is direct primary source evidence of Egyptian religious belief: the afterlife, divine judgment, Ma'at (cosmic order/truth), and how religion shaped every aspect of Egyptian life including what counted as moral behavior.
  • WH.6_12.3 — The weighing of the heart by Osiris reveals a divine political system: the gods hold court, keep records (Thoth), enforce judgment (Anubis/Ammit), and dispense eternal justice. This is governance imagined as divine bureaucracy.
  • WH.6_12.4 — The 42 declarations reveal what Egyptian society valued economically and socially: property rights, honest weights and measures, care for the hungry, respect for the dead. Compare to Hammurabi's Code for another angle on ancient law.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Ma'at The Egyptian concept of truth, justice, balance, and cosmic order — also personified as a goddess whose feather is used to weigh the heart Negative Confession The list of 42 sins the dead person denies having committed; not a confession of wrongdoing but a declaration of innocence Ammit The "Devourer of the Dead" — a monster with the head of a crocodile, the body of a lion, and the hindquarters of a hippo. She eats hearts that fail the weighing, destroying the person completely Osiris The god of the dead and the afterlife; king of the Hall of Two Truths and judge of the dead Thoth The ibis-headed god of writing, wisdom, and record-keeping; he records the result of the weighing Papyrus The plant-based writing material used in ancient Egypt; the Book of the Dead was written on papyrus scrolls placed in tombs Hall of Two Truths The divine courtroom where the dead person's heart is weighed and their fate decided

docs.title: Key Passage
docs.text:
O my heart which I had from my mother! O my heart of my different ages! Do not stand up as a witness against me, do not be opposed to me in the tribunal.

This is the dead person speaking directly to their own heart before it is weighed — one of the oldest examples in human writing of a person confronting their own conscience and begging it not to betray them. It reveals that Egyptians believed the heart itself remembered every action.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "What Does Egypt Think a Good Person Does?" — EQ: What can a list of sins tell you about what a society values? · Standard: WH.6_12.2 + WH.6_12.4 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (analyze 8–10 of the 42 declarations and write: what does this reveal about what mattered in Egyptian society? Compare to a modern moral code.)

  2. "Divine Justice vs. Human Justice" — EQ: Who gets to decide what's right — gods or humans? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + WH.6_12.2 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation or W.4 argument (compare the Egyptian weighing of the heart to Hammurabi's Code: which system is more "just"? What does each assume about who has authority?)

  3. "The Weight of a Life" — EQ: What would your heart weigh? · Standard: R.9 · Demo Mode: Creative/Written (write your own Negative Confession in the Egyptian style — 20 declarations of what you have and have not done. Then reflect: what does your list reveal about what YOU think a good life looks like?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Every culture in history has had to answer the same questions the Egyptian Book of the Dead tries to answer: What happens when we die? Does how we live determine what comes after? What do we owe the dead? Ancient Egypt's answer — that the deceased's heart would be weighed against a feather representing truth and justice — is an early version of the idea that character matters more than social status. The Book of the Dead's emphasis on Ma'at (truth, justice, balance) as the measure of a life connects to every contemporary debate about fairness, accountability, and what it means to live well. It's also a reminder that questions modern people treat as belonging to religion or philosophy are ancient — humanity has been wrestling with mortality, meaning, and justice for as long as we have writing.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• The Code of Hammurabi — Parallel ancient legal/moral system; compare what each society considered a punishable act
  • The Analects of Confucius — Another ancient moral code from the same era, different civilization
  • Era 1 — Civilizations
  • WH.6_12.2 Belief Systems Skinny

docs.title: Common Sense (1776)
docs.text: Author: Thomas Paine Type: Primary Source · Argumentative Essay Source: Project Gutenberg — public domain Era/Period: Era 3 — Age of Revolutions WH Standards: WH.6_12.4 · WH.6_12.5 USH Connection: US.6_12.4 (Unit 4 context — ideas that shaped American democracy)

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text: Common Sense is a 47-page pamphlet published on January 10, 1776, that made the case for American independence from Britain in plain, forceful language. Thomas Paine wrote it for ordinary people — not lawyers or politicians — and it became the best-selling printed work per capita in American history. Paine argued that monarchy is an irrational and corrupt system, that hereditary rule has no legitimate basis, and that establishing an independent republic was both possible and necessary.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Key selections — full text public domain. Full text at Project Gutenberg.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Perhaps the sentiments contained in the following pages, are not yet sufficiently fashionable to procure them general favor; a long habit of not thinking a thing wrong, gives it a superficial appearance of being right, and raises at first a formidable outcry in defense of custom. But the tumult soon subsides. Time makes more converts than reason.

The cause of America is in a great measure the cause of all mankind. Many circumstances hath, and will arise, which are not local, but universal, and through which the principles of all Lovers of Mankind are affected, and in the Event of which, their Affections are interested. The laying a Country desolate with Fire and Sword, declaring War against the natural rights of all Mankind, and extirpating the Defenders thereof from the Face of the Earth, is the Concern of every Man to whom Nature hath given the Power of feeling.

docs.title: Of the Origin and Design of Government in General
docs.text:
Some writers have so confounded society with government, as to leave little or no distinction between them; whereas they are not only different, but have different origins. Society is produced by our wants, and government by our wickedness; the former promotes our happiness positively by uniting our affections, the latter negatively by restraining our vices. The one encourages intercourse, the other creates distinctions. The first a patron, the last a punisher.

Society in every state is a blessing, but government even in its best state is but a necessary evil; in its worst state an intolerable one; for when we suffer, or are exposed to the same miseries by a government, which we might expect in a country without government, our calamity is heightened by reflecting that we furnish the means by which we suffer. Government, like dress, is the badge of lost innocence; the palaces of kings are built on the ruins of the bowers of paradise.

docs.title: Of Monarchy and Hereditary Succession
docs.text:
Mankind being originally equals in the order of creation, the equality could only be destroyed by some subsequent circumstance. But there is another and greater distinction for which no truly natural or religious reason can be assigned, and that is, the distinction of men into kings and subjects. Male and female are the distinctions of nature, good and bad the distinctions of heaven; but how a race of men came into the world so exalted above the rest, and distinguished like some new species, is worth enquiring into, and whether they are the means of happiness or of misery to mankind.

In the early ages of the world, according to the scripture chronology, there were no kings; the consequence of which was there were no wars; it is the pride of kings which throw mankind into confusion.

Government by kings was first introduced into the world by the Heathens, from whom the children of Israel copied the custom. It was the most prosperous invention the Devil ever set on foot for the promotion of idolatry. The Heathens paid divine honors to their deceased kings, and the christian world hath improved on the plan by doing the same to their living ones. How impious is the title of sacred majesty applied to a worm, who in the midst of his splendor is crumbling into dust!

For all men being originally equals, no one by birth could have a right to set up his own family in perpetual preference to all others for ever, and though himself might deserve some decent degree of honors of his cotemporaries, yet his descendants might be far too unworthy to inherit them. One of the strongest natural proofs of the folly of hereditary right in kings, is, that nature disapproves it, otherwise she would not so frequently turn it into ridicule by giving mankind an ass for a lion.

This is supposing the present race of kings in the world to have had an honorable origin; whereas it is more than probable, that could we take off the dark covering of antiquity, and trace them to their first rise, that we should find the first of them nothing better than the principal ruffian of some restless gang, whose savage manners or pre-eminence in subtility obtained him the title of chief among plunderers.

In short, monarchy and succession have laid (not this or that kingdom only) but the world in blood and ashes. 'Tis a form of government which the word of God bears testimony against, and blood will attend it.

Of more worth is one honest man to society and in the sight of God, than all the crowned ruffians that ever lived.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.9 — Common Sense is one of the most carefully constructed argumentative texts in the American canon. Students can map Paine's claims, identify his evidence (historical examples, biblical critique, economic argument), and evaluate his reasoning. Is his argument logical? What emotional appeals does he use?
  • R.7 — Paine's word choices are deliberate and inflammatory. "A long habit of not thinking a thing wrong gives it a superficial appearance of being right." Close reading of his rhetorical strategies (repetition, sarcasm, plain speech as persuasion technique).
  • W.4 — The gold standard for argumentative writing. Students can use Paine as a mentor text: How does he set up his claim? What's his structure? How does he handle counterarguments?
  • W.3 — Extended informational writing: Explain Paine's argument and analyze why it was effective for its audience.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.4 — Common Sense is a direct cause document of the American Revolution — evidence of how Enlightenment ideas about natural rights and self-governance translated into political action.
  • WH.6_12.5 — Paine explicitly analyzes the economic costs of British rule ("we are already greater than half the business of Britain") and argues independence is an economic as well as political necessity.
  • US.6_12.4 — Can be used comparatively: How do Paine's ideas about democratic participation echo in Progressive Era reform movements?

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Monarchy A government ruled by a single hereditary ruler (king or queen) Hereditary Passed down through bloodlines — the basis of monarchy Paine attacks Republic A government in which power rests with elected representatives and citizens Pamphlet A short printed booklet — the social media of the 18th century, cheap to produce and easy to distribute Natural rights Rights that belong to all people by nature, not granted by government (life, liberty, property)

docs.title: Key Passage
docs.text:
Society in every state is a blessing, but government even in its best state is but a necessary evil; in its worst state an intolerable one... Government, like dress, is the badge of lost innocence; the palaces of kings are built on the ruins of the bowers of paradise. — Common Sense, Introduction

And on monarchy specifically:

Of more worth is one honest man to society and in the sight of God than all the crowned ruffians that ever lived. — Common Sense, Part II

The first passage shows Paine's Enlightenment framework: government is a concession to human weakness, not a natural good. The second shows his willingness to attack monarchy in directly contemptuous language — unusual and inflammatory for 1776.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Was Paine Right?" — EQ: Can one document actually change the course of history? · Standard: WH.6_12.4 + Era 3 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (W.4 argument analyzing Paine's argument structure and effectiveness)

  2. "The Power of Plain Language" — EQ: Why does the way you say something matter as much as what you say? · Standard: R.9 (ELA) · Demo Mode: Multimedia/Performance (student recreates Paine's argument for a modern audience using his rhetorical techniques)

  3. "What Does Paine Assume About Human Nature?" — EQ: What beliefs about people have to be true for democracy to work? · Standard: WH.6_12.4 + Era 3 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (Socratic seminar comparing Paine's assumptions with Hobbes's Leviathan)

  4. "Argumentative Mentor Text Analysis" — EQ: How do expert writers build an argument? · Standard: W.4 (ELA) · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (annotate Paine's structure — claim/evidence/reasoning/counterargument)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Common Sense sold 500,000 copies in a population of 2.5 million — proportionally, it would be the best-selling book in American history if published today. Paine achieved this not with academic argument but by making a simple, direct case in plain language that anyone could follow: monarchy is a bad idea, hereditary rule is absurd, and the colonies have nothing to lose by declaring independence. That combination — a clear argument, accessible language, and mass distribution — is the template for every successful political persuasion campaign since. The pamphlet's structure (here's the problem, here's the case against the status quo, here's what we should do instead) is also the structure of every editorial, petition, and social media thread trying to move people to action. Paine also showed that political arguments don't have to be written for elites to be taken seriously — he wrote for the public, and the public responded. That model of democratic persuasion is always relevant.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Era 3 — Revolutions
  • Declaration of Rights of Man
  • Vindication of Rights of Woman
  • Common Sense + W.4 Crosswalk
  • WH.6_12.4 Revolution Skinny
