docs.title: The Communist Manifesto — Excerpts (1848)
docs.text: Author: Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels Type: Primary Source · Political Manifesto Source: Public domain — 1848 original; Marxists Internet Archive recommended Era/Period: Era 3 — Revolution and Industrialization · Era 4 — Age of Global War and Globalization WH Standards: WH.6_12.2 · WH.6_12.3 · WH.6_12.5 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.9 · 9-10.W.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Karl Marx (1818–1883), co-author of the Communist Manifesto (1848). This photograph was taken in 1875.

Published in February 1848 — weeks before revolutions broke out across Europe — The Communist Manifesto is a short political pamphlet by German philosophers Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels, written as the founding document of the Communist League. It diagnoses capitalism as a system that concentrates wealth, alienates workers from their labor, and inevitably produces class conflict. It argues that history is the history of class struggle, that the industrial working class (the proletariat) is the agent of historical change, and that the overthrow of capitalism and the establishment of collective ownership of production is both historically inevitable and morally necessary.

Why this text belongs in the reading library: The Communist Manifesto directly shaped the ideologies behind the Russian Revolution (1917), the Chinese Revolution (1949), the Cuban Revolution (1959), and dozens of 20th-century independence and liberation movements. It also shaped every major anti-communist political movement of the same period — the New Deal, McCarthyism, NATO, the Cold War — as a reaction to what it proposed. You cannot understand the 20th century without understanding this document. It is also one of the most rhetorically effective political arguments ever written, and one of the most-banned books in American history.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Key selections from Part I — full text public domain. Full text at Marxists Internet Archive or Project Gutenberg.

docs.title: Preamble
docs.text:
A spectre is haunting Europe—the spectre of Communism. All the Powers of old Europe have entered into a holy alliance to exorcise this spectre: Pope and Czar, Metternich and Guizot, French Radicals and German police-spies.

Where is the party in opposition that has not been decried as Communistic by its opponents in power? Where is the Opposition that has not hurled back the branding reproach of Communism, against the more advanced opposition parties, as well as against its reactionary adversaries?

Two things result from this fact. I. Communism is already acknowledged by all European Powers to be itself a Power. II. It is high time that Communists should openly, in the face of the whole world, publish their views, their aims, their tendencies, and meet this nursery tale of the Spectre of Communism with a Manifesto of the party itself.

docs.title: Part I — Bourgeois and Proletarians
docs.text:
The history of all hitherto existing societies is the history of class struggles.

Freeman and slave, patrician and plebeian, lord and serf, guild-master and journeyman, in a word, oppressor and oppressed, stood in constant opposition to one another, carried on an uninterrupted, now hidden, now open fight, a fight that each time ended, either in a revolutionary re-constitution of society at large, or in the common ruin of the contending classes.

The modern bourgeois society that has sprouted from the ruins of feudal society has not done away with class antagonisms. It has but established new classes, new conditions of oppression, new forms of struggle in place of the old ones. Our epoch, the epoch of the bourgeoisie, possesses, however, this distinctive feature: it has simplified the class antagonisms. Society as a whole is more and more splitting up into two great hostile camps, into two great classes, directly facing each other: Bourgeoisie and Proletariat.

The bourgeoisie, wherever it has got the upper hand, has put an end to all feudal, patriarchal, idyllic relations. It has pitilessly torn asunder the motley feudal ties that bound man to his "natural superiors," and has left remaining no other nexus between man and man than naked self-interest, than callous "cash payment." It has drowned the most heavenly ecstasies of religious fervour, of chivalrous enthusiasm, of philistine sentimentalism, in the icy water of egotistical calculation. It has resolved personal worth into exchange value, and in place of the numberless and indefeasible chartered freedoms, has set up that single, unconscionable freedom—Free Trade. In one word, for exploitation, veiled by religious and political illusions, naked, shameless, direct, brutal exploitation.

The bourgeoisie has stripped of its halo every occupation hitherto honoured and looked up to with reverent awe. It has converted the physician, the lawyer, the priest, the poet, the man of science, into its paid wage labourers.

The bourgeoisie cannot exist without constantly revolutionising the instruments of production, and thereby the relations of production, and with them the whole relations of society. All fixed, fast-frozen relations, with their train of ancient and venerable prejudices and opinions, are swept away, all new-formed ones become antiquated before they can ossify. All that is solid melts into air, all that is holy is profaned, and man is at last compelled to face with sober senses, his real conditions of life, and his relations with his kind.

The weapons with which the bourgeoisie felled feudalism to the ground are now turned against the bourgeoisie itself.

But not only has the bourgeoisie forged the weapons that bring death to itself; it has also called into existence the men who are to wield those weapons—the modern working class—the proletarians.

In proportion as the bourgeoisie, i.e., capital, is developed, in the same proportion is the proletariat, the modern working class, developed—a class of labourers, who live only so long as they find work, and who find work only so long as their labour increases capital. These labourers, who must sell themselves piece-meal, are a commodity, like every other article of commerce, and are consequently exposed to all the vicissitudes of competition, to all the fluctuations of the market.

Owing to the extensive use of machinery and to division of labour, the work of the proletarians has lost all individual character, and consequently, all charm for the workman. He becomes an appendage of the machine, and it is only the most simple, most monotonous, and most easily acquired knack, that is required of him.

Modern industry has converted the little workshop of the patriarchal master into the great factory of the industrial capitalist. Masses of labourers, crowded into the factory, are organised like soldiers. As privates of the industrial army they are placed under the command of a perfect hierarchy of officers and sergeants. Not only are they slaves of the bourgeois class, and of the bourgeois State; they are daily and hourly enslaved by the machine, by the over-looker, and, above all, by the individual bourgeois manufacturer himself.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.9 — The Manifesto is a formal argument: it has a thesis (history is the history of class struggle), a mechanism (capitalism produces its own destruction by creating the proletariat), and a call to action (workers of the world, unite). Students map the argumentative structure and evaluate: Is the logic internally consistent? What assumptions does it depend on? What does it fail to predict?
  • W.4 — The Manifesto is a masterclass in argument by historical narrative: rather than asserting "capitalism is unjust," Marx argues "capitalism will be overthrown because this is how history works" — making inevitability the argument. Use as a W.4 case study: How does framing change what kind of argument you're making?
  • R.7 — The Manifesto's rhetoric is deliberately designed to be memorable, urgent, and mobilizing. The opening sentence ("A spectre is haunting Europe — the spectre of communism") and the closing sentence ("Workers of the world, unite!") are two of the most studied rhetoric moves in political writing.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.2 — The Manifesto is the founding document of Marxist ideology — one of the three major secular ideological frameworks of the modern era (alongside liberalism and fascism). Students understand what Marxism actually argued, rather than the caricature versions taught in Cold War contexts.
  • WH.6_12.3 — The Manifesto argues for a specific political transition: from bourgeois democracy to communist collective ownership. Students compare this model of political change to the democratic revolutions they've studied (French, American, Haitian) — same era, very different theory of who should hold power.
  • WH.6_12.5 — The Manifesto's publication in 1848 was both a cause and a symptom of the revolutions that swept Europe that year. Its influence then spread through the 20th century as a cause of the Russian Revolution, Cold War, decolonization movements, and McCarthy-era anti-communism in the U.S. The causal chain from this pamphlet to the 20th century is one of the most traceable in modern history.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Proletariat The industrial working class — those who own no means of production and must sell their labor to survive Bourgeoisie The owning/capitalist class — those who own the means of production (factories, land, capital) and profit from others' labor Means of production The tools, factories, land, and resources required to produce goods — who owns them is the central political question Class struggle Marx's argument that all of history is shaped by conflict between classes defined by their relationship to the means of production Historical materialism Marx's theory that the driving force of history is economic conditions and class conflict — not ideas, individuals, or divine will

docs.title: Key Passages
docs.text:
Opening:

A spectre is haunting Europe — the spectre of communism. All the powers of old Europe have entered into a holy alliance to exorcise this spectre: Pope and Tsar, Metternich and Guizot, French Radicals and German police-spies.

On class struggle:

The history of all hitherto existing society is the history of class struggles.

Freeman and slave, patrician and plebeian, lord and serf, guild-master and journeyman, in a word, oppressor and oppressed, stood in constant opposition to one another, carried on an uninterrupted, now hidden, now open fight, a fight that each time ended, either in a revolutionary re-constitution of society at large, or in the common ruin of the contending classes.

On capitalism's self-destruction:

The bourgeoisie, during its rule of scarce one hundred years, has created more massive and more colossal productive forces than have all preceding generations together... But not only has the bourgeoisie forged the weapons that bring death to itself; it has also called into existence the men who are to wield those weapons — the modern working class — the proletarians.

Closing:

The proletarians have nothing to lose but their chains. They have a world to win. WORKERS OF THE WORLD, UNITE!

Why these passages matter: The opening establishes the rhetorical frame: communism is already present, already feared, already being fought — this document is naming it. The "class struggle" passage reframes all of history as a single pattern. The self-destruction passage is Marx's most original and controversial claim. The closing is one of the most famous sentences in political history.

docs.title: What the Manifesto Got Right (and Wrong)
docs.text:
Students should engage with the Manifesto critically, not as gospel or as propaganda:

What held up: The prediction that industrial capitalism would produce extreme inequality; the observation that political systems tend to reflect the interests of the economically powerful; the identification of alienated labor as a real phenomenon.

What didn't: The prediction that revolution would come first in the most industrialized countries (it came in agrarian Russia and China); the assumption that a workers' state would be temporary (it became permanent authoritarianism); the underestimation of nationalism as a force that would prevent international worker solidarity.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Read the Document, Not the Caricature" — EQ: What does the Communist Manifesto actually argue? · Standard: WH.6_12.2 + R.9 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (read the key excerpts and summarize Marx's actual argument in your own words — without using "evil" or "good." Then evaluate: Which parts of his analysis hold up? Which don't?)

  2. "The History of Class Struggle" — EQ: Is Marx right that all history is the history of class conflict? · Standard: WH.6_12.5 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (test Marx's thesis against two historical events you've studied — does the class-struggle framework explain them well? Better than other explanations? Write an argument for or against Marx's thesis with historical evidence)

  3. "Why Did the 20th Century Fear This Pamphlet?" — EQ: Why would a small book threaten governments enough to ban it? · Standard: WH.6_12.2 + WH.6_12.5 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (the Manifesto was banned in the U.S. during the Cold War and is still banned in some countries. Research: What specifically in the text was threatening? Compare to Fahrenheit 451 — is there a connection between what a society bans and what it fears?)

  4. "From Manifesto to Revolution" — EQ: Can a text cause a revolution? · Standard: WH.6_12.5 + IR.1–5 · Demo Mode: Visual/Creative (trace the Manifesto's influence from 1848 → 1917 Russian Revolution → Chinese Revolution → Cold War → decolonization movements → today. At each step: did the revolution use Marx's ideas, or did it transform them?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Marx and Engels predicted in 1848 that capitalism would produce accelerating inequality, periodic crises, and eventually its own destruction through class conflict. The inequality part is difficult to dispute: wealth concentration in the United States and globally is at levels not seen since the Gilded Age. The periodic crises part produced the Great Depression, the 2008 financial crisis, and the COVID-19 economic shock. Whether the rest of the prediction is accurate is one of the central contested questions in contemporary politics. Arguments about minimum wage, union rights, gig economy classification, wealth taxes, and the role of government in the economy are all, at root, arguments about the relationship between capital and labor that Marx analyzed. You don't have to accept his conclusions to find his analysis useful: the Manifesto's description of how capitalism works — commodification of labor, global expansion, displacement of traditional structures — reads like a description of the gig economy written in 1848.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Era 3 — Revolution and Industrialization
  • Era 4 — Global War
  • Haitian Declaration of Independence — Another 19th-century document arguing that the current order must be overthrown
  • Fahrenheit 451 — Written in direct response to Cold War anti-communism; Marx's book would be burned in Bradbury's world
  • 1984 — Orwell — Orwell was a socialist who hated Stalinism; 1984 is partly an argument about what Marxism became

docs.title: Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen (1789)
docs.text: Author: National Constituent Assembly of France (principal drafters: Lafayette, Sieyès) Type: Primary Source · Historical Document Source: Avalon Project, Yale Law School — public domain Era/Period: Era 3 — Age of Revolutions WH Standards: WH.6_12.4 · WH.6_12.3

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Displayed at the Assemblée Nationale. Adopted by the National Constituent Assembly of France, August 26, 1789.

The Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen was adopted by France's National Constituent Assembly on August 26, 1789, during the early phase of the French Revolution. It declared that all men are born free and equal in rights, that sovereignty belongs to the nation (not the king), and that government exists only to protect natural rights. It drew directly from Enlightenment philosophy and the American Declaration of Independence — but went further in applying these principles to a monarchy already in crisis.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text:
Full text — public domain. Adopted by the National Constituent Assembly of France, August 26, 1789.

Preamble

The representatives of the French people, organized as a National Assembly, believing that ignorance, neglect, or contempt of the rights of man are the sole cause of public calamities and of the corruption of governments, have determined to set forth in a solemn declaration the natural, unalienable, and sacred rights of man, in order that this declaration, being constantly before all the members of the Social body, shall remind them continually of their rights and duties; in order that the acts of the legislative power, as well as those of the executive power, may be compared at any moment with the objects and purposes of all political institutions and may thus be more respected; and, lastly, in order that the grievances of the citizens, based hereafter upon simple and incontestable principles, shall tend to the maintenance of the constitution and redound to the happiness of all.

Therefore the National Assembly recognizes and proclaims, in the presence and under the auspices of the Supreme Being, the following rights of man and of the citizen:

Article 1. Men are born and remain free and equal in rights. Social distinctions may be founded only upon the general good.

Article 2. The aim of all political association is the preservation of the natural and imprescriptible rights of man. These rights are liberty, property, security, and resistance to oppression.

Article 3. The principle of all sovereignty resides essentially in the nation. No body nor individual may exercise any authority which does not proceed directly from the nation.

Article 4. Liberty consists in the freedom to do everything which injures no one else; hence the exercise of the natural rights of each man has no limits except those which assure to the other members of the society the enjoyment of the same rights. These limits can only be determined by law.

Article 5. Law can only prohibit such actions as are hurtful to society. Nothing may be prevented which is not forbidden by law, and no one may be forced to do anything not provided for by law.

Article 6. Law is the expression of the general will. Every citizen has a right to participate personally, or through his representative, in its foundation. It must be the same for all, whether it protects or punishes. All citizens, being equal in the eyes of the law, are equally eligible to all dignities and to all public positions and occupations, according to their abilities, and without distinction except that of their virtues and talents.

Article 7. No person shall be accused, arrested, or imprisoned except in the cases and according to the forms prescribed by law. Any one soliciting, transmitting, executing, or causing to be executed, any arbitrary order, shall be punished. But any citizen summoned or arrested in virtue of the law shall submit without delay, as resistance constitutes an offense.

Article 8. The law shall provide for such punishments only as are strictly and obviously necessary, and no one shall suffer punishment except it be legally inflicted in virtue of a law passed and promulgated before the commission of the offense.

Article 9. As all persons are held innocent until they shall have been declared guilty, if arrest shall be deemed indispensable, all harshness not essential to the securing of the prisoner's person shall be severely repressed by law.

Article 10. No one shall be disquieted on account of his opinions, including his religious views, provided their manifestation does not disturb the public order established by law.

Article 11. The free communication of ideas and opinions is one of the most precious of the rights of man. Every citizen may, accordingly, speak, write, and print with freedom, but shall be responsible for such abuses of this freedom as shall be defined by law.

Article 12. The security of the rights of man and of the citizen requires public military forces. These forces are, therefore, established for the good of all and not for the personal advantage of those to whom they shall be intrusted.

Article 13. A common contribution is essential for the maintenance of the public forces and for the cost of administration. This should be equitably distributed among all the citizens in proportion to their means.

Article 14. All the citizens have a right to decide, either personally or by their representatives, as to the necessity of the public contribution; to grant this freely; to know to what uses it is put; and to fix the proportion, the mode of assessment and of collection and the duration of the taxes.

Article 15. Society has the right to require of every public agent an account of his administration.

Article 16. A society in which the observance of the law is not assured, nor the separation of powers defined, has no constitution at all.

Article 17. Since property is an inviolable and sacred right, no one shall be deprived thereof except where public necessity, legally determined, shall clearly demand it, and then only on condition that the owner shall have been previously and equitably indemnified.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.9 — Students can compare this document directly with the U.S. Declaration of Independence, analyzing how two documents with similar arguments structure their claims differently for different audiences and political contexts.
  • R.7 — The phrase "liberty, property, security, and resistance to oppression" vs. Jefferson's "life, liberty, and pursuit of happiness" — close reading of word choice reveals political philosophy differences.
  • W.4 — Argumentative writing prompt: "Which declaration — American or French — more fully embodied Enlightenment ideals?"

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.4 — The Declaration is a founding document of the French Revolution. It represents the most direct application of Enlightenment natural rights philosophy to political action in European history.
  • WH.6_12.3 — Article 3 ("The principle of all sovereignty resides essentially in the nation") directly redefines political legitimacy from divine right to popular sovereignty — a fundamental shift in how political systems justify themselves.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Natural rights Rights that all humans have by nature, regardless of government — life, liberty, property Popular sovereignty The idea that political power comes from the people, not from a king or god Inalienable Cannot be taken away or given up Estate One of the three social classes of pre-Revolutionary France: clergy (First), nobility (Second), everyone else (Third) Enlightenment 18th-century intellectual movement that emphasized reason, science, and individual rights over tradition and religious authority

docs.title: Key Passage
docs.text:
The principle of all sovereignty resides essentially in the nation. No body nor individual may exercise any authority which does not proceed directly from the nation. — Article 3

Liberty consists in the freedom to do everything which injures no one else; hence the exercise of the natural rights of each man has no limits except those which assure to the other members of the society the enjoyment of the same rights. These limits can only be determined by law. — Article 4

Article 3 ends divine right monarchy as a legitimate political theory. Article 4 defines liberty as bounded by others' liberty — the classic liberal political framework that still underlies modern democratic theory.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "From Versailles to Revolution" — EQ: What ideas are powerful enough to topple a monarchy? · Standard: WH.6_12.4 + Era 3 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (analyze how the Declaration's ideas caused the French Revolution to radicalize)

  2. "Liberty, Property, or Security — What Matters Most?" — EQ: If rights conflict, which takes priority? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + Era 3 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (structured debate using the Declaration's article framework)

  3. "Two Declarations, Two Revolutions" — EQ: Why did the French Revolution end so differently from the American Revolution? · Standard: WH.6_12.4 + Era 3, R.9 (ELA) · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (W.4 comparative argument using both documents as primary sources)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: The Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen proclaimed universal rights — and then defined "citizen" in ways that excluded women, enslaved people, Jews, and colonized peoples. That gap between "universal" and the actual scope of who counted was the political work of the next two centuries, and it is not finished. Every argument about who deserves citizenship, who the law protects, and whose rights are taken seriously at the border is an argument about where the line gets drawn on "universal." The Declaration is also the source of the concept that rights are inherent to persons, not granted by rulers — which is the philosophical foundation of every human rights framework since. When the Universal Declaration of Human Rights was written in 1948, it was explicitly building on this tradition. And when governments today argue that their sovereignty gives them the right to treat their own citizens however they choose, they're arguing against this tradition. The debate between state sovereignty and universal human rights is still the central tension in international law.

docs.text:

docs.title: The Dhammapada (c. 3rd century BCE)
docs.text: Author: Attributed to the Buddha; compiled by his disciples Type: Primary Source · Religious/Philosophical Text Era/Period: WH Era 1 — Ancient Civilizations Standards: WH.6_12.2 · WH.6_12.4 · R.4 · R.9

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Emperor Ashoka's pillar (3rd century BCE), inscribing Buddhist teachings in public stone — the same tradition that preserved the Dhammapada.

The Dhammapada is a collection of 423 sayings attributed to the Buddha, organized into 26 short chapters. Written in the Pali language around the 3rd century BCE, it is one of the most widely read texts in all of Buddhism and one of the most translated books in world literature. The name means roughly "the path of the Dhamma" — Dhamma being the Buddhist word for truth, law, and the proper way of living. Where Hammurabi's Code tells you what the state will do to you if you sin, the Dhammapada tells you what will happen inside you.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Translation by F. Max Müller (public domain). Key chapters selected for SDA studio use.

docs.title: Chapter 1 — The Twin Verses (Yamakavagga)
docs.text:
1. All that we are is the result of what we have thought: it is founded on our thoughts, it is made up of our thoughts. If a man speaks or acts with an evil thought, pain follows him, as the wheel follows the foot of the ox that draws the carriage.

2. All that we are is the result of what we have thought: it is founded on our thoughts, it is made up of our thoughts. If a man speaks or acts with a pure thought, happiness follows him, like a shadow that never departs.

3. "He abused me, he beat me, he defeated me, he robbed me" — in those who harbor such thoughts hatred will never cease.

4. "He abused me, he beat me, he defeated me, he robbed me" — in those who do not harbor such thoughts hatred will quickly cease.

5. Hatred does not ever cease in this world by hating, but by not hating; this is an eternal rule.

6. Others do not understand that we must all come to an end here; — but those who understand this, their quarrels cease at once.

7. He who lives looking for pleasures only, his senses uncontrolled, immoderate in his food, idle, and weak, Mara (the tempter) will certainly overthrow him, as the wind throws down a weak tree.

8. He who lives without looking for pleasures, his senses well-controlled, moderate in his food, faithful and strong, him Mara will certainly not overthrow, any more than the wind throws down a rocky mountain.

9. He who wishes to put on the yellow robe without having cleansed himself from sin, who disregards also temperance and truth, is unworthy of the yellow robe.

10. But he who has cleansed himself from sin, is well grounded in all virtues, and regards also temperance and truth; he is indeed worthy of the yellow robe.

11. They who imagine truth in untruth, and see untruth in truth, never arrive at truth, but follow vain desires.

12. They who know truth in truth, and untruth in untruth, arrive at truth, and follow true desires.

13. As rain breaks through an ill-thatched house, passion will break through an unreflecting mind.

14. As rain does not break through a well-thatched house, passion will not break through a well-reflecting mind.

15. The evil-doer mourns in this world, and he mourns in the next; he mourns in both. He mourns and suffers when he sees the evil of his own work.

16. The virtuous man delights in this world, and he delights in the next; he delights in both. He delights and rejoices, when he sees the purity of his own work.

17. The evil-doer suffers in this world, and he suffers in the next; he suffers in both. He suffers when he thinks of the evil he has done; he suffers more when going on the evil path.

18. The virtuous man is happy in this world, and he is happy in the next; he is happy in both. He is happy when he thinks of the good he has done; he is still more happy when going on the good path.

19. The thoughtless man, even if he can recite a large portion of the law, but is not a doer of it, has no share in the priesthood, but is like a cowherd counting the cows of others.

20. The follower of the law, even if he can recite only a small portion of the law, but, having forsaken passion and hatred and foolishness, possesses true knowledge and serenity of mind, he, caring for nothing in this world or that to come, has indeed a share in the priesthood.

docs.title: Chapter 2 — On Earnestness (Appamadavagga)
docs.text:
21. Earnestness is the path of immortality, thoughtlessness the path of death. Those who are in earnest do not die, those who are thoughtless are as if dead already.

22. Those who are advanced in earnestness, having understood this clearly, delight in earnestness, and rejoice in the knowledge of the Ariyas (the elect).

23. These wise people, meditative, steady, always possessed of strong powers, attain to Nirvana, the highest happiness.

24. If an earnest person has roused himself, if he is not forgetful, if his deeds are pure, if he acts with consideration, if he restrains himself, and lives according to law — then his glory will increase.

25. By rousing himself, by earnestness, by restraint and control, the wise man may make for himself an island which no flood can overwhelm.

26. Fools follow after vanity, men of evil wisdom. The wise man keeps earnestness as his best jewel.

27. Follow not after vanity, nor after the enjoyment of love and lust! He who is earnest and meditative obtains ample joy.

28. When the learned man drives away vanity by earnestness, he, the wise, climbing the terraced heights of wisdom, looks down upon the fools, serene he looks upon the toiling crowd, as one that stands on a mountain looks down upon them that stand upon the plain.

29. Earnest among the thoughtless, awake among the sleepers, the wise man advances like a racer, leaving behind the hack.

30. By earnestness did Maghavan (Indra) rise to the lordship of the gods. People praise earnestness; thoughtlessness is always blamed.

31. A Bhikshu (mendicant) who delights in earnestness, who looks with fear on thoughtlessness, moves about like fire, burning all his fetters, small or large.

32. A Bhikshu (mendicant) who delights in reflection, who looks with fear on thoughtlessness, cannot fall away from his perfect state — he is close upon Nirvana.

docs.title: Chapter 5 — The Fool (Balavagga)
docs.text:
60. Long is the night to him who is awake; long is a mile to him who is tired; long is life to the foolish who do not know the true law.

61. If a traveller does not meet with one who is his better, or his equal, let him firmly keep to his solitary journey; there is no companionship with a fool.

62. "These sons belong to me, and this wealth belongs to me" — with such thoughts a fool is tormented. He himself does not belong to himself; how much less sons and wealth?

63. The fool who knows his foolishness is wise at least so far. But a fool who thinks himself wise, he is called a fool indeed.

64. If a fool be associated with a wise man even all his life, he will perceive the truth as little as a spoon perceives the taste of soup.

65. If an intelligent man be associated for one minute only with a wise man, he will soon perceive the truth, as the tongue perceives the taste of soup.

66. Fools of poor understanding have themselves for their greatest enemies, for they do evil deeds which must bear bitter fruits.

67. That deed is not well done of which a man must repent, and the reward of which he receives crying and with a tearful face.

68. No, that deed is well done of which a man does not repent, and the reward of which he receives gladly and cheerfully.

69. As long as the evil deed done does not bear fruit, the fool thinks it is like honey; but when it ripens, then the fool suffers grief.

70. Let a fool month after month eat his food (like an ascetic) with the tip of a blade of kuśa grass, yet he is not worth the sixteenth particle of those who have well weighed the law.

71. An evil deed, like newly-drawn milk, does not turn suddenly; smouldering, like fire covered by ashes, it follows the fool.

72. And when the evil deed, after it has become known, brings sorrow to the fool, then it destroys his bright lot, nay, it cleaves his head.

73. Let the fool wish for a false reputation, for precedence among the Bhikshus, for lordship in the convents, for worship among other people!

74. "May both the layman and he who has left the world think that this is done by me; may they be subject to me in everything which is to be done or is not to be done" — thus is the mind of the fool, and his desire and pride increase.

75. "One is the road that leads to wealth, another the road that leads to Nirvana" — if the Bhikshu, the disciple of Buddha, has learnt this, he will not yearn for honour, he will strive after separation from the world.

docs.title: Chapter 8 — The Thousands (Sahassavagga)
docs.text:
100. Even though a speech be a thousand (of words), but made up of senseless words, one word of sense is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.

101. Even though a Gatha (poem) be a thousand (of words), but made up of senseless words, one word of a Gatha is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.

102. Though a man recite a hundred Gathas made up of senseless words, one word of the law is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.

103. If one man conquer in battle a thousand times thousand men, and if another conquer himself, he is the greatest of conquerors.

104. One's own self conquered is better than all other people; not even a god, a Gandharva, not Mara with Brahma could change into defeat the victory of a man who has vanquished himself, and always lives under restraint.

105. If a man for a hundred years sacrifice month after month with a thousand, and if he but for one moment pay homage to a man whose soul is grounded in true knowledge, better is that homage than sacrifice for a hundred years.

106. If a man for a hundred years worship Agni (fire) in the forest, and if he but for one moment pay homage to a man whose soul is grounded in true knowledge, better is that homage than sacrifice for a hundred years.

107. Whatever a man sacrifice in this world as an offering or as an oblation for a whole year in order to gain merit, the whole of it is not worth a quarter of a farthing; reverence shown to the righteous is better.

108. Four things does a wreckless man gain who covets his neighbour's wife — a bad reputation, an uncomfortable bed, thirdly, punishment, and lastly, hell.

109. There is bad reputation, and the evil way (to hell), there is the short pleasure of the frightened in the arms of the frightened, and the king imposes heavy punishment; therefore let no man think of his neighbour's wife.

110. As a grass-blade, if badly grasped, cuts the arm, badly-practised asceticism leads to hell.

111. An act carelessly performed, a broken vow, and lukewarm service of a great soul, does not bring much reward.

112. If anything is to be done, let a man do it, let him attack it vigorously! A careless pilgrim only scatters the dust of his passions more widely.

113. An evil deed is better left undone, for a man repents of it afterwards; a good deed is better done, for having done it, one does not repent.

114. Like a well-guarded frontier fort, with defences within and without, so let a man guard himself. Not a moment should escape, for they who allow the right moment to pass, suffer pain when they are in hell.

115. They who are ashamed of what they ought not to be ashamed of, and are not ashamed of what they ought to be ashamed of, such men, embracing false doctrines, enter the evil path.

116. They who fear when they ought not to fear, and fear not when they ought to fear, such men, embracing false doctrines, enter the evil path.

117. They who forbid when there is nothing to be forbidden, and forbid not when there is something to be forbidden, such men, embracing false doctrines, enter the evil path.

118. They who know what is forbidden as forbidden, and what is not forbidden as not forbidden, such men, embracing the true doctrine, enter the good path.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.4 — Each verse states a main idea directly and develops it through comparison (verses 1–2 are the clearest parallel structure in ancient literature). Students track main idea and supporting development across chapters.
  • R.9 — The Dhammapada makes sustained philosophical claims about the nature of the mind, the consequences of action, and what a good life looks like. Students analyze the argument: what is the Buddha claiming, and what is his evidence?
  • R.7 — The verse form, parallelism, and concrete comparisons (spoon and soup, well-thatched house and rain) are deliberate stylistic choices. Students analyze how form creates meaning.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.2 — The Dhammapada is direct primary source evidence of Buddhist belief: karma, the path to Nirvana, the role of the mind in shaping experience. Compare to the Egyptian Book of the Dead and the Analects for how three civilizations approached ethics at roughly the same historical moment.
  • WH.6_12.4 — Buddhism spread from India throughout Asia along trade routes, shaping social development across an enormous geographic area. The Dhammapada is the text that traveled — studying it reveals why Buddhist values spread so widely.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Dhamma (Pali) / Dharma (Sanskrit) The teachings of the Buddha; also the cosmic law or truth that underlies all existence; "the right way" Nirvana The Buddhist concept of the end of suffering — not a heaven exactly, but the extinguishing of desire, hatred, and delusion Karma The idea that actions have consequences that follow you — good actions lead to good results, bad actions to bad results Bhikshu A Buddhist monk or wandering mendicant who has renounced worldly possessions Mara The Buddhist figure of temptation and death — the force that tries to keep people attached to the world and away from enlightenment Pali The ancient Indian language in which the earliest Buddhist texts were written Earnestness The Dhammapada's word for diligence, mindfulness, and disciplined attention — the opposite of carelessness or thoughtlessness

docs.title: Key Passage
docs.text:
If a man speaks or acts with an evil thought, pain follows him, as the wheel follows the foot of the ox that draws the carriage. If a man speaks or acts with a pure thought, happiness follows him, like a shadow that never departs. — Verses 1–2

These first two verses set up the entire logic of the Dhammapada: what happens to you is a result of what you think and do. This is the core of Buddhist ethics and the concept of karma — not a mystical belief but a psychological claim: your mind shapes your experience.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Mind First" — EQ: Do your thoughts control your life, or do your circumstances? · Standard: WH.6_12.2 + R.9 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (Socratic seminar: do you agree with verses 1–2? What evidence supports or challenges the idea that "all we are is the result of what we have thought"?)

  2. "Three Moral Codes" — EQ: What do ancient civilizations agree about what makes a good person? · Standard: WH.6_12.2 + WH.6_12.4 · Demo Mode: Visual/Creative (create a comparison chart: Hammurabi's Code, the Negative Confession, and the Dhammapada — what does each say about theft, violence, honesty, and self-discipline? Where do they agree? Disagree?)

  3. "The Conqueror of Himself" — EQ: Is self-control more valuable than military victory? · Standard: WH.6_12.2 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (verse 103: "If one man conquer in battle a thousand times thousand men, and if another conquer himself, he is the greatest of conquerors." Write a W.4 argument: do you agree? Use at least one historical example to support your claim.)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: The Dhammapada's core teaching — that suffering comes from craving, and that freedom comes from training the mind — is now the philosophical basis of a multi-billion dollar wellness industry. Mindfulness meditation, apps like Headspace and Calm, secular mindfulness in schools and corporations — all of these draw on Buddhist frameworks originally articulated in texts like the Dhammapada. Buddhism is the fastest-growing religion in the Western world, and its influence extends far beyond formal conversion: Buddhist ideas about impermanence, compassion, and the relationship between mind and suffering have shaped Western psychology, therapy, and how people think about mental health. Reading the Dhammapada lets you see where those ideas came from, what they originally meant, and what gets preserved or lost when a 2,500-year-old spiritual tradition gets turned into a productivity app.

  • The Code of Hammurabi — Legal moral code from the same era; compare systems of accountability
  • The Analects of Confucius — Chinese philosophical text from the same era; comparison of Eastern ethical systems

docs.title: "Dulce et Decorum Est" (1920)
docs.text: Author: Wilfred Owen Type: Primary Source · War Poetry Source: Public domain — first published posthumously, 1920 Full Text: Poetry Foundation Era/Period: Era 4 — Age of Global War and Globalization WH Standards: WH.6_12.2 · WH.6_12.5 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.4 · 9-10.R.7 · 9-10.R.8

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Wilfred Owen was a British Army officer who served in the trenches of World War I and was killed in action on November 4, 1918 — one week before the Armistice. He was 25. "Dulce et Decorum Est" was drafted in 1917 and published posthumously in 1920. It is widely considered the greatest anti-war poem in the English language.

The poem describes a gas attack on Owen's trench unit. In vivid, deliberately ugly language, Owen shows a soldier who fails to get his gas mask on in time — dying slowly while Owen can do nothing but watch. The title and final lines are a direct attack on the Latin phrase Dulce et decorum est pro patria mori — "It is sweet and fitting to die for one's country" — a quote from Horace used extensively in WWI-era propaganda and recruitment materials. Owen calls it "the old Lie."

This poem is a primary source for understanding how WWI was experienced by combatants, how WWI propaganda worked, and why the disillusionment of the 1920s ran so deep. It is also one of the best short texts in the library for R.7 (style and word choice) and R.8 (literary elements).

docs.title: Read It
docs.text:
Full text — public domain. First published posthumously 1920.

Bent double, like old beggars under sacks, Knock-kneed, coughing like hags, we cursed through sludge, Till on the haunting flares we turned our backs, And towards our distant rest began to trudge. Men marched asleep. Many had lost their boots, But limped on, blood-shod. All went lame; all blind; Drunk with fatigue; deaf even to the hoots Of gas-shells dropping softly behind.

Gas! GAS! Quick, boys!—An ecstasy of fumbling Fitting the clumsy helmets just in time, But someone still was yelling out and stumbling And flound'ring like a man in fire or lime.— Dim through the misty panes and thick green light, As under a green sea, I saw him drowning.

In all my dreams before my helpless sight, He plunges at me, guttering, choking, drowning.

If in some smothering dreams, you too could pace Behind the wagon that we flung him in, And watch the white eyes writhing in his face, His hanging face, like a devil's face of sin; If you could hear, at every jolt, the blood Come gargling from the froth-corrupted lungs, Obscene as cancer, bitter as the cud Of vile, incurable sores on innocent tongues,— My friend, you would not tell with such high zest To children ardent for some desperate glory, The old Lie: Dulce et decorum est Pro patria mori.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.7 — Owen's word choices are deliberate and precise. He uses clinical medical vocabulary ("guttering, choking, drowning"), archaic words that evoke heroic poetry ("guttering" echoes the sound of dying candles in old literature), and visceral physical language side by side — to force readers to experience the physical reality of gas warfare while aware they're in a poem. Students trace specific word choices and explain why Owen chose them.
  • R.8 — The poem's extended simile ("as under a green sea") transforms the gas cloud into something vast and drowning — the soldier is not just dying from poison but being swallowed by an unnatural ocean. The nightmare vision in lines 17–24 introduces the speaker as the poem's most complex character: a witness who can neither help nor stop watching. Students analyze the interaction of imagery, speaker's POV, and tone.
  • R.4 — The poem's claim (stated explicitly in the final lines) is that "the old Lie" of glorious death for one's country is a lie — and that anyone who knew what combat actually looked like would not tell it to children. Students identify the claim and trace how every earlier image in the poem builds toward it.
  • R.9 — Owen is arguing against a specific text: the Horace quote used in propaganda. The poem is a direct rhetorical rebuttal — matching the propaganda's register (Latin, classical allusion) and then destroying it with what the speaker has actually seen. Use alongside the Kipling/Johnson pair for a sequence on propaganda vs. counter-testimony.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.2 — WWI was accompanied by the most extensive state propaganda campaign in history to that point. Governments used classical education — Horace, patriotic verse, heroic imagery — to construct the ideology of glorious sacrifice. Owen's poem directly attacks this ideology using the same classical tools. Students understand how ideology is constructed and how it can be dismantled.
  • WH.6_12.5 — The poem documents a specific military technology (mustard gas) and its effects — one of the causes of WWI's unprecedented casualty rates and one of the reasons combatants' experience was so different from civilian understanding of the war. The gap between the home front's understanding and the trenches' reality is a cause of the post-war disillusionment that shaped the 1920s and contributed to WWII's ideological context.

docs.title: Full Text
docs.text:
Bent double, like old beggars under sacks, Knock-kneed, coughing like hags, we cursed through sludge, Till on the haunting flares we turned our backs, And towards our distant rest began to trudge. Men marched asleep. Many had lost their boots, But limped on, blood-shod. All went lame; all blind; Drunk with fatigue; deaf even to the hoots Of gas-shells dropping softly behind.

Gas! GAS! Quick, boys!—An ecstasy of fumbling Fitting the clumsy helmets just in time, But someone still was yelling out and stumbling And flound'ring like a man in fire or lime.— Dim through the misty panes and thick green light, As under a green sea, I saw him drowning.

In all my dreams before my helpless sight, He plunges at me, guttering, choking, drowning.

If in some smothering dreams, you too could pace Behind the wagon that we flung him in, And watch the white eyes writhing in his face, His hanging face, like a devil's face of sin; If you could hear, at every jolt, the blood Come gargling from the froth-corrupted lungs, Obscene as cancer, bitter as the cud Of vile, incurable sores on innocent tongues,— My friend, you would not tell with such high zest To children ardent for some desperate glory, The old Lie: Dulce et decorum est Pro patria mori.

docs.title: Analysis Notes
docs.text:
Structure: The poem is in two parts — the march (exhaustion, dehumanization), then the gas attack (panic, death, aftermath) — followed by a two-line bridge and a final stanza that turns outward to address the reader directly.

The most important move in the poem: Lines 17–18. "If in some smothering dreams, you too could pace / Behind the wagon that we flung him in..." — Owen breaks the fourth wall. The poem stops being a personal memory and becomes a direct challenge: if you could see what I saw, you would not repeat the lie. The "you" addressed in the final stanza is the reader. This is how the poem transforms a witness account into a political argument.

"The old Lie": Horace's phrase was quoted in British classrooms, recruitment posters, newspaper editorials, and letters home from officers. It was the specific ideological claim Owen was writing against. By naming it at the end — in Latin, italicized — he exposes it as a lie in the same register it was told as truth.

docs.title: Pairs With
docs.text: Text What the Comparison Reveals Kipling — "The White Man's Burden" Both are poems used ideologically to support imperial/military projects; Owen's poem is the direct counter-tradition FDR — Four Freedoms Speech FDR revives the idea of glorious sacrifice for universal values 24 years after Owen. Does Owen's critique apply? Pericles' Funeral Oration Pericles uses a funeral to argue that death in service of the city is honorable — the same argument Owen destroys

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Counter the Lie" — EQ: Is it ever right to tell young people that dying for their country is glorious? · Standard: WH.6_12.2 + R.9 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (analyze Owen's argument against "the old Lie" — is it convincing? What would a recruiter in 1917 say in response? Write Owen's argument and the strongest counterargument, then evaluate which is more honest)

  2. "What War Actually Looks Like" — EQ: Why does the reality of war keep being hidden from people who haven't experienced it? · Standard: WH.6_12.5 + R.7 · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (annotate the poem line by line for word choice — for each image, explain: what does Owen choose to show? What does he choose to say explicitly vs. leave as image? Why?)

  3. "Propaganda vs. Testimony" — EQ: Whose version of an event should we trust — the institution that organized it or the person who survived it? · Standard: WH.6_12.2 + IR.4 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (find a WWI-era British recruitment poster that uses the Horace quote or similar language. Compare the poster's claim to Owen's poem — same event, completely different account. Which is a more reliable source? For what question?)

  4. "The Gap Between Home and Front" — EQ: How did WWI create a generation that couldn't talk to civilians? · Standard: WH.6_12.5 + R.8 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (research the concept of "shell shock" and the post-war silence about combat experience. Using Owen's poem as evidence, write an argument: What does the gap between combatant experience and civilian understanding cost a society?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Owen wrote "Dulce et Decorum Est" to counter the propaganda being used to recruit young men into a war that was killing them in industrial quantities — the Latin phrase "it is sweet and fitting to die for one's country" is the lie he is calling out. The poem matters today because the gap between how war is depicted in political speech, recruitment materials, and patriotic narrative versus what soldiers actually experience is still the same gap. Veterans' suicide rates, PTSD rates, and the long-term physical and psychological costs of service are poorly represented in the rhetoric used to justify sending people to war. Owen is not anti-soldier — he is pro-honesty about what you are asking soldiers to do and what it costs them. Every debate about military intervention, veteran care, the use of war as a foreign policy tool, and the moral obligations of a society to the people it sends into combat runs through the question Owen raised: are we being told the truth about what this costs?

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:

  • "The White Man's Burden" — Kipling/Johnson — Another poem weaponized as ideology
  • Night — Wiesel — WWII equivalent: witness testimony that destroys ideology's abstractions
  • FDR — Four Freedoms Speech — The ideological argument that follows Owen's disillusionment

docs.title: "Four Freedoms" Speech (1941)
docs.text: Author: Franklin D. Roosevelt Type: Primary Source · Political Speech Source: Miller Center, University of Virginia — public domain Era/Period: Era 4 — Age of Global War · US History Unit 4 (WWII) WH Standards: WH.6_12.2 · WH.6_12.3 USH Standards: US.6_12.1 · US.6_12.2 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.9 · 9-10.W.4 · 9-10.C.1

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Franklin D. Roosevelt (1882–1945). This color portrait was taken in 1944, the year of D-Day and his final presidential election.

Delivered as FDR's State of the Union address on January 6, 1941 — nearly eleven months before Pearl Harbor — this speech is one of the most consequential arguments in American political history. With Europe already at war and American public opinion deeply divided between intervention and isolationism, Roosevelt reframed the question of U.S. involvement not as a military choice but as a moral and ideological one. The speech's climax articulates "four essential human freedoms" that the U.S. should be willing to defend worldwide: freedom of speech, freedom of worship, freedom from want, and freedom from fear. These four freedoms directly shaped the 1941 Atlantic Charter, the 1945 United Nations Charter, and the 1948 Universal Declaration of Human Rights — making this speech a pivot point between WWI-era isolationism and the post-WWII international rights framework.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Public domain — U.S. government work. Full text at Miller Center.

docs.title: The World Situation
docs.text: The future and safety of our country are more immediately involved now than at any other time in its history. As a nation, we are passing through a period which will find its place in history as a struggle for the indefinite extension of democratic free government.

docs.title: The Four Freedoms
docs.text:
In the future days, which we seek to make secure, and which we intend to make prosperous, as we have made our land safe, democracy alone can supply the answer.

Fighting for survival, endangered by new technical developments, assaulted by internal enemies of degree, quality and ability, the democratic philosophy has not grown old. It has newly attained its full and greatest age. It has not decayed. It has newly prospered. It is not dead. It cannot perish so long as faith endures that is the final objective of our age.

Let the word go forth, to friend and foe alike, that the United States expects to continue in the lead in offering liberation by new methods to new generations.

In the future days, which we seek to make secure, and which we intend to make prosperous, as we have made our land safe, democracy alone can supply the answer.

We look forward to a world founded upon four essential human freedoms.

docs.title: The Call to American Action
docs.text:
America has no strategy save the strategy of friendship; she seeks no objectives except the objective of justice; and her purpose is nothing less than the elimination of fear and want from the lives of all men everywhere.

In any nation, a duly constituted self-perpetuating body such as Congress can be relied upon to respond in a way which will best serve the welfare of that nation.

It is for us now—in unhasting but unremitting pace—to pass on the torch of vigilance and faith. We may then, with confidence in the justness of our cause, speed forward into the dark valley that forms the threshold to a better world.

In the face of great perils never before encountered, our inherent national trait teaches us that grave threats and challenges are mastered not by passing them from leader to leader or from generation to generation. They are mastered by the determination of each individual American to meet whatever comes with fortitude and resolution.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.9 — The speech is a masterclass in argumentative structure calibrated to a hostile-leaning audience (isolationist Congress and public). FDR acknowledges the opposing view ("Many citizens favor... isolationism"), then systematically dismantles it using ethos, logos, and a reframing of self-interest. Students map how he moves the audience from "stay out" to "we must act."
  • W.4 — The "four freedoms" framework is a masterful argumentative move: by defining freedom as a universal human condition rather than an American possession, FDR changes the terms of the debate entirely. Use as a W.4 model for reframing a question.
  • R.7 — FDR's speech style: plain language, parallel structure, and the deliberate escalation from practical ("our democracy is threatened") to universal ("everywhere in the world"). The anaphoric final section ("The first is...The second is...The third is...The fourth is...") is one of the most analyzed rhetorical structures in American political speech.
  • C.1 — The Four Freedoms speech is the model for a formal argument delivered to a resistant audience. Use for C.1 preparation: how do you present an unpopular position compellingly?

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.2 — The speech directly confronts fascism and Nazism as ideological threats — not just military ones. FDR frames WWII as a war of ideas: democratic freedoms vs. totalitarian control. This is the strongest short primary source for teaching how WWII was understood ideologically by its participants.
  • WH.6_12.3 — The four freedoms framework becomes the ideological foundation for the post-war international order (UN Charter, UDHR). Students trace the direct line: 1941 speech → 1945 UN Charter → 1948 UDHR.
  • US.6_12.1 — The speech documents the ideological and political context of U.S. entry into WWII — the shift from isolationism to engagement, and the specific argument FDR made to a divided nation.
  • US.6_12.2 — FDR's "freedom from want" (economic security) and "freedom from fear" (national security) extend democratic ideals into economic and international policy — connecting to the New Deal and the post-war welfare state.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Isolationism The foreign policy position that the U.S. should avoid entanglement in other nations' conflicts Totalitarianism A political system in which the state holds total authority over all aspects of public and private life Atlantic Charter A 1941 joint declaration by FDR and Churchill stating the Allies' post-war goals — directly built on the Four Freedoms Freedom from want FDR's term for economic security — freedom from poverty, unemployment, and deprivation Freedom from fear FDR's term for national security — freedom from the threat of military aggression

docs.title: Key Passages
docs.text:
In the future days, which we seek to make secure, we look forward to a world founded upon four essential human freedoms.

The first is freedom of speech and expression — everywhere in the world.

The second is freedom of every person to worship God in his own way — everywhere in the world.

The third is freedom from want — which, translated into world terms, means economic understandings which will secure to every nation a healthy peacetime life for its inhabitants — everywhere in the world.

The fourth is freedom from fear — which, translated into world terms, means a world-wide reduction of armaments to such a point and in such a thorough fashion that no nation will be in a position to commit an act of physical aggression against any neighbor — anywhere in the world. — State of the Union, January 6, 1941

Why this passage matters: The phrase "everywhere in the world" repeated four times is not accidental — it universalizes American democratic values as the basis for a new global order. This move is rhetorically brilliant and politically controversial: FDR is arguing that American self-interest and universal human rights are the same thing.

This nation has placed its destiny in the hands and heads and hearts of its millions of free men and women, and its faith in freedom under the guidance of God. Freedom means the supremacy of human rights everywhere. Our support goes to those who struggle to gain those rights and keep them. Our strength is our unity of purpose. — State of the Union, January 6, 1941

docs.title: Lineage of the Four Freedoms
docs.text:
The speech created a chain of documents students can trace:

  1. Four Freedoms Speech (Jan 1941) → defined the ideological stakes of WWII
  2. Atlantic Charter (Aug 1941, FDR + Churchill) → first multilateral commitment to post-war rights
  3. UN Charter (Jun 1945) → institutionalized the rights framework
  4. Universal Declaration of Human Rights (Dec 1948) → codified specific rights into international law

Each document in the chain is in the SDA Reading Library. Use all four for IR.1–5 research projects.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Why Should America Care?" — EQ: When does another nation's injustice become your problem? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + US.6_12.1 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (analyze FDR's argument for intervention — is it convincing? What assumptions does it make? Would an isolationist be persuaded?)

  2. "Four Freedoms Then and Now" — EQ: Are FDR's four freedoms still secure today — everywhere in the world? · Standard: WH.6_12.6 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (choose one of the four freedoms; write an argument about whether it is adequately protected in one country today, using current evidence)

  3. "Reframing the Debate" — EQ: How do you change someone's mind when they think you're asking the wrong question? · Standard: R.9 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (annotate 3 moments in the speech where FDR reframes the isolationist argument on his own terms — explain the move each time)

  4. "The Chain of Rights" — EQ: How does a speech become international law? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + IR.1–5 · Demo Mode: Visual/Creative (create a visual tracing the Four Freedoms → Atlantic Charter → UN Charter → UDHR, showing what each document added or changed)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: FDR's Four Freedoms — freedom of speech, freedom of worship, freedom from want, freedom from fear — are relevant today because three of them remain contested. Freedom of speech is contested in every debate about social media content moderation and disinformation. Freedom from want (the idea that people have a right to basic material security) is the philosophical foundation of every argument about the social safety net: Social Security, Medicare, unemployment insurance, SNAP, Medicaid. FDR was arguing in 1941 that freedom from poverty and freedom from fear are as real as freedom of speech — and that a democracy that doesn't secure them isn't fully free. That argument is still contested: the debate about whether economic security is a right or a personal responsibility is one of the most fundamental divides in American politics. Freedom from fear — as FDR framed it, freedom from the threat of aggressive international violence — is also live in every debate about nuclear deterrence, arms control, and the conditions under which the use of force is legitimate.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:

  • MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — Rights in practice, 22 years later
  • Nelson Mandela — Rivonia Trial Speech

docs.title: Feudal Obligations — Selected Primary Sources (c. 900–1300 CE)
docs.text: Author: Various Type: Primary Source · Legal/Social Documents Era/Period: WH Era 2 — Middle Ages and Early Modern Standards: WH.6_12.2 · WH.6_12.3 · WH.6_12.4 · R.9

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text: The feudal system organized medieval European society around a hierarchy of obligations: kings granted land to lords, lords granted land to knights, and at the bottom of the chain, serfs worked the land and owed labor, goods, and loyalty to their lord. This entry collects three short primary sources that show feudalism from multiple angles — an oath ceremony, a manor survey, and a list of serf obligations — so students can see how the system actually worked in practice, not just in theory.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: All three documents are public domain, drawn from the Fordham Internet Medieval Sourcebook and similar archives.

docs.title: Document 1 — Oath of Fealty and Homage (c. 1127)
Recorded by Galbert of Bruges, De Multro (On the Murder of Charles the Good)
docs.text:
Galbert of Bruges was a notary who witnessed and recorded the ceremonies of homage at the court of William of Clito, Count of Flanders, in 1127. This is one of the most detailed surviving accounts of how the feudal ceremony actually worked.

First they did their homage thus. The count asked if he was willing to become completely his man, and the other replied, "I am willing"; and with clasped hands, surrounded by the hands of the count, they were bound together by a kiss.

Second, he who had done homage gave his fealty to the representative of the count in these words: "I promise on my faith that I will in future be faithful to count William, and will loyally observe my homage to him completely against all persons in good faith and without deceit."

Third, he took this oath upon the relics of the saints. Afterward, with a little rod which the count held in his hand, he gave investiture to all those who by this agreement had given their security and homage and accompanying oath.

docs.title: A Manor Survey from Sussex, England
docs.text:
The "Extent" was an official survey of what a manor owed and what its people were required to do. This excerpt records the specific obligations of a villein (serf) named John of Cayworth at Bernehorne manor. Note how precisely each obligation is recorded — this was a legal document, not a description.

John of Cayworth holds one house and thirty acres of land, and owes yearly 2 shillings at Easter and Michaelmas.

And he ought to harrow for two days at the Lenten sowing with one man and his own horse and his own harrow, the value of the work being 4d; and he is to receive from the lord on each day 3 meals, of the value of 5d, and then the lord will be at a loss of 1d. Thus his harrowing is of no value to the service of the lord.

And he ought to carry the manure of the lord for two days with one cart, with his own two oxen, the value of the work being 8d; and he is to receive from the lord each day 3 meals of the price as above; and thus the service is worth 3d clear.

And he ought to carry wood from the woods of the lord to the manor house for two days in summer, with a cart and three animals of his own, the value of the work being 9d. And he receives from the lord each day 3 meals of the above price; and thus the work is worth 4d clear.

And he ought to find one man for two days to mow the meadow of the lord, who can mow, by estimation, one acre and a half; the value of mowing an acre being 6d; and thus the total value of this mowing is 9d. And he is to receive each day 3 meals of the above price; and thus the mowing is worth 4d clear.

And he ought to gather and carry that same hay for one day, the value of the work being 3d. And he has 3 meals of the above price.

And he ought to carry the hay of the lord for one day with a cart and three animals of his own, the price of the work being 6d. And he receives from the lord 3 meals of the price of 5d. And thus the work is worth 1d clear.

And he ought to help to thresh in the autumn for two days, with two men, the value of the work being 10d. And he is to receive from the lord 3 meals of the above price for each man. And thus the threshing is worth no more than 2d clear.

The total of his works is... 2 shillings, 11 pence.

docs.title: From an English Manor Survey
docs.text:
This document lists what a serf named Richard, son of Anketill, owed his lord at a typical English manor. Unlike the precise accounting of Bernehorne above, this reads more as a general description of yearly obligations.

Richard, son of Anketill, holds a half-virgate of land and owes for it yearly:

He shall plow one acre in winter, and one in Lent, and one in summer.

He shall reap for the lord in August for three days with his entire family, except his wife; and each day he shall receive a loaf of bread from the lord.

He shall carry the lord's grain to market on his cart twice a year.

He shall pay at Christmas 2 hens. At Easter, one egg for every hen he keeps, or a halfpenny.

When he marries off a daughter, he shall pay the lord merchet — a fee for permission.

When he dies, the lord shall have his best beast as heriot.

He may not leave the land without the lord's permission. He may not sell his ox, his horse, or his cow without permission. He may not send his son to school or to be ordained as a priest without the lord's license.

All these things he owes, and he holds his land at the will of the lord.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.9 — Each of these documents has a clear purpose and an implicit argument: what the system of feudal obligation looks like from the perspective of those who administered it. Students analyze the author's purpose (who wrote this? For whom? Why?), identify the claims made, and evaluate what is left unsaid.
  • R.7 — The shift in register between a ceremonial oath ("with clasped hands, surrounded by the hands of the count") and a legal survey ("he shall plow one acre in winter") is deliberate. Students analyze how the form of each document shapes what we can learn from it.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.2 — The oath of fealty is saturated with religious meaning: the kiss, the relics of the saints, the word "faith." The feudal system was inseparable from the Catholic Church's authority and worldview.
  • WH.6_12.3 — These documents show how the feudal system actually operated as a political and legal structure — not just as an abstract hierarchy but as a web of specific, enforceable obligations. Compare to Hammurabi's Code and the Twelve Tables: all three show political systems using law to organize a society.
  • WH.6_12.4 — The manor survey reveals the economic foundation of medieval life: the serf as a unit of agricultural labor, the precise accounting of every day of work, every egg, every animal. Students connect this to the question of who benefited from the feudal system and who did not.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Fealty The loyalty sworn by a vassal to his lord; the promise to be faithful and not to betray Homage The formal ceremony in which a vassal kneels before his lord, places his hands in the lord's hands, and swears allegiance Investiture The formal granting of a fief (land or office) by a lord to a vassal, symbolized by a rod or staff Villein (serf) A peasant legally bound to the land; not a slave (they could not be sold), but not free — they could not leave the land, marry, or educate their children without the lord's permission Virgate A unit of land roughly equal to 30 acres — the standard holding for a serf family Heriot The "death tax" — when a serf died, the lord took his best animal as payment Merchet A fee paid to the lord when a serf's daughter married; the lord's acknowledgment that even marriage required his permission Manor The lord's estate — the basic economic and social unit of medieval Europe, including the lord's house, the village, the fields, and the church

docs.title: Key Passage
docs.text:
He may not leave the land without the lord's permission. He may not sell his ox, his horse, or his cow without permission. He may not send his son to school or to be ordained as a priest without the lord's license. He holds his land at the will of the lord. — Document 3

This is the most important line in the collection: "he holds his land at the will of the lord." The serf does not own the land. He is not a slave — but his freedom is entirely conditional on a lord who could, in theory, remove him at any time. The phrase captures the fundamental insecurity of medieval peasant life.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Who Does the Feudal System Work For?" — EQ: When is a system of mutual obligation actually just? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + WH.6_12.4 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (the lord and the serf both owe each other things — does that make the relationship fair? Use Documents 2 and 3 as evidence. Write a W.4 argument: was the feudal system a reasonable social contract, or exploitation disguised as obligation?)

  2. "What the Ceremony Means" — EQ: Why do humans use ritual to create political bonds? · Standard: WH.6_12.2 + WH.6_12.3 · Demo Mode: Visual/Creative or Verbal Conversation (Document 1 describes a very specific ceremony with specific actions — clasped hands, a kiss, relics of saints. Why? What does each element do? What modern ceremonies serve similar purposes — inaugurations, weddings, naturalization ceremonies? What do they have in common?)

  3. "The Serf's Perspective" — EQ: What does power look like from the bottom of a hierarchy? · Standard: WH.6_12.4 + WH.6_12.6 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (Document 3 is written by a lord's administrator. Write the same document from Richard's point of view — not the lord's list of what Richard owes, but Richard's account of what his year actually looks like. What would he say about "holding his land at the will of the lord"? Use W.3 explanatory or W.5 narrative format.)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Feudal obligations look strange from the outside — a web of personal loyalty, labor, and protection binding peasants to lords and lords to kings. But the underlying structure (you provide labor and loyalty; in exchange you receive security and a share of the surplus) is recognizable in every employment contract ever written. The gig economy has made this comparison sharper: when platform workers argue they're employees rather than independent contractors, they're arguing about the nature of the obligation between a person who does the work and the entity that controls the conditions. Uber, DoorDash, and Amazon Mechanical Turk have all been described as "digital feudalism" — not as a metaphor but as an analysis of power asymmetry. Feudalism was also the first major system built to manage agricultural surplus at scale, which means understanding it helps explain why land ownership, inheritance rights, and the right to tax became the central conflicts of the Middle Ages — and why versions of those conflicts (property rights, wealth concentration, estate taxes) remain live political debates today.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Pope Gregory VII and Emperor Henry IV — The Investiture Controversy: when church and feudal lord competed for authority
  • The Magna Carta — What happens when barons push back against their king; limits on feudal power from above
  • Era 2 — Middle Ages
  • WH.6_12.3 Medieval Political Skinny
