docs.title: Haitian Declaration of Independence (1804)
docs.text: Author: Jean-Jacques Dessalines (proclaimed); Louis Boisrond-Tonnerre (drafted) Type: Primary Source · Founding Document Source: Public domain — translated text available via Yale Gilder Lehrman Center Era/Period: Era 3 — Revolution and Industrialization WH Standards: WH.6_12.3 · WH.6_12.5 · WH.6_12.6 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.9 · 9-10.W.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text: On January 1, 1804, the former French colony of Saint-Domingue declared itself the independent Republic of Haiti — the first and only nation in history founded by formerly enslaved people through successful revolution. The declaration is strikingly different from the American Declaration of Independence (1776) and the French Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789): where those documents appealed to universal reason and natural rights, the Haitian declaration is openly furious. It names France directly as a perpetrator of atrocities, declares that Haitians will never again live under European colonial rule, and — critically — extends citizenship to all people regardless of race, years before any other nation did so. The declaration was so threatening to slaveholding nations that the original French-language version was lost for nearly 200 years; the full text was only rediscovered in a British archive in 2010.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Public domain. The 1804 declaration was long thought lost; a copy was discovered in 2010 at the British National Archives. Translation from the French original.

docs.title: The Proclamation
docs.text:
In the name of the Supreme Being, and by order of General-in-Chief Jean-Jacques Dessalines, Governor-General of Hayti:

Whereas the tyranny of France has oppressed our country for more than two hundred years; whereas it was necessary to fight against this tyranny in order to secure freedom for ourselves; whereas we have triumphed over the enemy and achieved independence:

docs.title: The Oath
docs.text:
We swear to renounce France forever, and never to submit to any foreign power.

In testimony whereof, we have caused these presents to be signed with our hand at the City of Gonaïves, this first day of January one thousand eight hundred and four.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.9 — The Haitian Declaration is an argumentative founding document, but its argument is structured very differently from the U.S. Declaration or the French Declaration of the Rights of Man. Where Jefferson uses natural law philosophy, Dessalines uses historical grievance and direct accusation. Students analyze how the same type of document (founding declaration) can make fundamentally different arguments to fundamentally different audiences.
  • W.4 — The declaration models argument-by-historical-record: rather than abstract principles, it lists specific acts and their consequences. Use as a W.4 model for evidence-based argument that depends on documented wrongs rather than philosophical premises.
  • R.2 — The tone of this document requires careful reading — it is angrier than students expect a founding document to be. Inference work: Why is the tone this way? What does it tell you about the audience, the purpose, and the historical context?

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.3 — The Haitian Declaration represents a distinct model of revolutionary political thought — one that extends Enlightenment ideals of freedom and rights to enslaved people in a way that neither the American nor French revolutions were willing to do. It forces students to examine whose revolution the Enlightenment actually was.
  • WH.6_12.5 — The Haitian Revolution's causes and effects are among the most complex in the era: a slave rebellion → ideological crisis for slaveholding nations → French bankruptcy (Haiti was forced to pay France 150 million francs in "reparations" for lost slave property) → Haitian economic isolation that persists to the present. The declaration is the beginning of that chain.
  • WH.6_12.6 — Haiti was economically isolated and undermined by France and the United States for over a century following independence. The legacy of those reparations (not paid off until 1947) directly shaped Haiti's present-day poverty — one of the clearest historical causation chains from 1804 to 2026.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Saint-Domingue The French colonial name for what became Haiti — the most profitable colony in the world at the time of the revolution Dessalines Jean-Jacques Dessalines, formerly enslaved, general of the revolutionary forces, first ruler of independent Haiti Reparations (reversed) France demanded 150 million francs from Haiti in 1825 as "compensation" for lost slave property — Haiti paid this until 1947 Universal citizenship The Haitian constitution declared all citizens "black" regardless of actual skin color — a deliberate inversion of racial hierarchy Boisrond-Tonnerre The drafter of the declaration, who reportedly said it should be written "with the skin of a white man for parchment, his skull for an inkwell, his blood for ink, and a bayonet for a pen"

docs.title: Key Passages
docs.text:
It is not enough to have expelled the barbarians who have bloodied our land for two centuries; it is not enough to have restrained those ever-evolving factions that one after another mocked the specter of liberty that France dangled before you... We must, with one last act of national authority, forever assure the empire of liberty in the country that gave us birth... — Haitian Declaration of Independence, January 1, 1804

Let us ensure, however, that a missionary spirit does not destroy our work; let us allow our neighbors to breathe in peace; may they live quietly under the laws that they have made for themselves, and let us not, as revolutionary firebrands, declare ourselves the legislators of the Caribbean, or make glory out of disturbing the peace of the neighboring islands... — Haitian Declaration of Independence, January 1, 1804

Why these passages matter: The first passage establishes that the declaration is not just political — it is a reckoning with history. The second is surprising: the new nation explicitly commits to NOT spreading its revolution to other islands. This is a strategic choice; they understood they were surrounded by hostile slaveholding nations. Students often expect the opposite — this requires real historical inference.

docs.title: Cross-Text Comparisons
docs.text:
This text pairs directly with others already in the library:

Compare With What the Comparison Reveals Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789) Same era, same revolution's ideals — but Haiti extends rights to enslaved people France specifically excluded. Why? Common Sense — Paine (1776) Both argue for independence from a colonial power, but Paine appeals to reason while Dessalines appeals to historical injury. Which is more persuasive? For whom? Wollstonecraft — Vindication (1792) Both extend Enlightenment principles to an excluded group. Compare what arguments they use and whose logic is more consistent with Enlightenment premises.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Whose Revolution Was the Enlightenment?" — EQ: Did the Enlightenment mean what it said? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + R.9 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (compare the Haitian Declaration to the French Declaration of the Rights of Man — what did the Haitians do that the French refused to do, and why?)

  2. "The Debt That Wasn't" — EQ: Can an oppressor demand payment from those they oppressed? · Standard: WH.6_12.5 + WH.6_12.6 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (Socratic seminar: Was France's demand for reparations from Haiti an act of theft? How does this shape your view of Haiti's present-day situation?)

  3. "Different Arguments for the Same Goal" — EQ: Does how you argue for your freedom depend on what was done to you? · Standard: R.9 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (compare the argument structure of this declaration vs. the U.S. Declaration — one uses philosophy, one uses documented grievance. Which is stronger? Which is more honest?)

  4. "Lost for 200 Years" — EQ: Why does it matter that this document was suppressed? · Standard: WH.6_12.6 + IR.1–5 · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (research the rediscovery of the original French text in 2010 — what does its disappearance tell you about how history gets written and by whom?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Haiti's Declaration of Independence in 1804 was the first successful revolution by enslaved people in history, and the world's response was to isolate and punish the new nation for having the audacity to win. France demanded reparations — payment for the "value" of the enslaved people who had freed themselves and the colony they had built — and Haiti was still paying that debt until 1947. The U.S. didn't recognize Haitian independence until 1862, when the Confederacy had left Congress and the slaveholders' objection to a free Black republic was temporarily removed. Haiti's subsequent history — poverty, political instability, foreign intervention — cannot be understood without understanding what was done to it in the decades after independence. The question of reparations for slavery is partly a question about Haiti: a country that has been paying reparations for emancipation for nearly two centuries while the world calls its poverty a product of its own failures. The Haitian Declaration is also the only founding document in history written explicitly to justify revolution against slavery.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:

  • Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789)
  • Common Sense — Paine (1776)
  • Wollstonecraft — Vindication (1792)
  • Communist Manifesto — excerpts

docs.title: The Code of Hammurabi (c. 1754 BCE)
docs.text: Author: Hammurabi, King of Babylon (written by royal scribes) Type: Primary Source Source: Yale Avalon Project — public domain Era/Period: Era 1 — Civilizations and Religions WH Standards: WH.6_12.2 · WH.6_12.3

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
The Stele of Hammurabi (c. 1754 BCE), carved in diorite, now in the Louvre. 282 laws inscribed in cuneiform.

The Code of Hammurabi is one of the oldest written legal codes in recorded history, carved into an 8-foot stone stele around 1754 BCE in ancient Babylon. It contains 282 laws governing everything from trade and wages to family life, slavery, and punishment — organized around the principle that the strong shall not oppress the weak.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: L.W. King translation (1910) — public domain. Full text at Yale Avalon Project.

docs.title: Prologue — The Divine Mandate
docs.text: When Anu the Sublime, King of the Anunaki, and Bel, the lord of Heaven and earth, who decreed the fate of the land, assigned to Marduk, the over-ruling son of Ea, God of righteousness, dominion over earthly man, and made him great among the Igigi, they called Babylon by his illustrious name, made it great on earth, and founded an everlasting kingdom in it, whose foundations are laid so solidly as those of heaven and earth; then Anu and Bel called by name me, Hammurabi, the exalted prince, who feared God, to bring about the rule of righteousness in the land, to destroy the wicked and the evil-doers; so that the strong should not harm the weak; so that I should rule over the black-headed people like Shamash, and enlighten the land, to further the well-being of mankind.

docs.title: Selected Laws — Social Hierarchy and Justice
docs.text:
Note: The Code uses three tiers for calculating punishment — "awilum" (nobleman or free man), "mushkenum" (freedman or commoner), and "wardum" (slave). The same injury receives different punishment depending on the victim's social status.

Law 1. If any one ensnare another, putting a ban upon him, but he cannot prove it, then he that ensnared him shall be put to death.

Law 3. If any one bring an accusation of any crime before the elders, and does not prove what he has charged, he shall, if it be a capital offense charged, be put to death.

Law 8. If any one steal cattle or sheep, or an ass, or a pig or a goat, if it belong to a god or to the court, the thief shall pay thirtyfold therefor; if they belonged to a freed man of the king he shall pay tenfold; if the thief has nothing with which to pay he shall be put to death.

Law 22. If any one is committing a robbery and is caught, then he shall be put to death.

Law 23. If the robber is not caught, then shall he who was robbed claim under oath the amount of his loss; then shall the community, and... on whose ground and territory and in whose domain it was compensate him for the goods stolen.

Law 48. If any one owe a debt for a loan, and a storm prostrates the grain, or the harvest fail, or the grain does not grow for lack of water, in that year he need not give his creditor any grain, he washes his debt-tablet in water and pays no rent for this year.

Law 109. If conspirators meet in the house of a tavern-keeper, and these conspirators are not captured and delivered to the court, the tavern-keeper shall be put to death.

Law 128. If a man take a woman to wife, but have no intercourse with her, this woman is no wife to him.

Law 129. If a man's wife be surprised with another man, both shall be tied and thrown into the water, but the husband may pardon his wife and the king his slaves.

Law 138. If a man wishes to separate from his wife who has borne him no children, he shall give her the amount of her purchase money and the dowry which she brought from her father's house, and let her go.

docs.title: Laws 196–199 — The Tiered Eye-for-an-Eye
docs.text:
Law 196. If a man put out the eye of another man, his eye shall be put out.

Law 197. If he break another man's bone, his bone shall be broken.

Law 198. If he put out the eye of a freed man, or break the bone of a freed man, he shall pay one gold mina.

Law 199. If he put out the eye of a man's slave, or break the bone of a man's slave, he shall pay one-half of its value.

docs.title: Laws on Labor and Wages
docs.text:
Law 215. If a physician make a large incision with an operating knife and cure it, or if he open a tumor over the eye with an operating knife, and save the eye, he shall receive ten shekels in money.

Law 218. If a physician make a large incision with the operating knife, and kill him, or open a tumor with the operating knife, and cut out the eye, his hands shall be cut off.

Law 228. If a builder build a house for some one and complete it, he shall give him a fee of two shekels in money for each sar of surface.

Law 229. If a builder build a house for some one, and does not construct it properly, and the house which he built fall in and kill its owner, then that builder shall be put to death.

docs.title: Epilogue — The King's Claim to Justice
docs.text: These are the just laws which Hammurabi, the wise king, has established and by which he has directed the land aright and ruled it well. Hammurabi, the protecting king am I. I have not withdrawn myself from the men, whom Bel gave to me, the rule over whom Marduk gave to me, I was not negligent, but I made them a peaceful abiding-place. I expounded all great difficulties, I made the light shine upon them. With the mighty weapons which Enlil and Marduk had given to me, with the keen vision with which Enlil endowed me, I rooted out the enemy above and below; I made an end of war; I promoted the welfare of the land; I made the peoples rest in friendly habitations; I did not let them have any one to terrorize them.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.9 — The code is an argumentative/informational primary source with a clear authorial purpose (legitimizing Hammurabi's rule through divine authority). Students analyze his claims, evidence, and rhetorical structure.
  • R.7 — Hammurabi's word choices ("shall be put to death," "shall make good") reveal assumptions about justice, class, and gender. Close reading of specific laws.
  • W.4 — Students can write argumentative or informational responses: "How does Hammurabi's Code reflect the values of Babylonian society?" Argument: Was this code just?

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.2 — Hammurabi's Code is direct evidence of how early belief systems (Marduk, divine kingship) were built into civic law. The prologue invokes the gods to justify the king's authority.
  • WH.6_12.3 — The code establishes a formal political and legal system — one of the first to exist in written form. Demonstrates the structure of an early state: laws, courts, punishment, social hierarchy.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Stele A stone slab carved with text or images, used as a public monument Lex talionis "An eye for an eye" — the principle of proportional punishment Prologue The opening section where Hammurabi claims divine authority from the god Marduk Social hierarchy A ranked ordering of people by class, gender, or status — explicit in the Code Codify To write laws down in a systematic, official form

docs.title: Key Passage
docs.text:
If a man puts out the eye of another man, his eye shall be put out. If he breaks another man's bone, his bone shall be broken. But if he puts out the eye of a freed man or breaks the bone of a freed man, he shall pay one gold mina. If he puts out the eye of a man's slave or breaks a bone of a man's slave, he shall pay one-half of its value. — Laws 196–199

This passage directly shows the tiered justice system of Babylonian society: the punishment for harming someone depends entirely on their social class. A nobleman's eye equals an eye; a slave's eye equals money.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Is Hammurabi's Justice System Actually Just?" — EQ: What makes a legal system fair or unfair? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + Era 1 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment or Extended Writing (W.4 argument)

  2. "Divine Authority vs. Human Law" — EQ: How do early societies use religion to justify political power? · Standard: WH.6_12.2 + Era 1 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation or Visual/Creative (concept map of how Marduk appears in the prologue and in law)

  3. "What the Code Tells Us About Babylon" — EQ: What can a legal code reveal about a civilization's values? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + Era 1, R.9 · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (annotate 5 laws and draw inferences about Babylonian society)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Hammurabi's Code is 3,700 years old, and the argument it makes is still being made. When courts impose different sentences for the same crime depending on who committed it and who was harmed, that's Hammurabi's tiered justice system operating in modern law. The debate about bail reform, mandatory minimums, and whether the American criminal justice system applies its rules equally is directly about the question Hammurabi's Code raises: Who is the law actually designed to protect? The code's prologue — Hammurabi claiming divine authority to make justice real for the weak — is also the template for every government that claims its authority comes from a higher source than the people it governs. If you want to follow the thread into the present, the Era 1 Crosswalk maps the connections between this text and today's arguments about power and law.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Era 1 — Civilizations
  • WH.6_12.2 Belief Systems Skinny
  • WH.6_12.3 Political Systems Skinny
  • Analects of Confucius

docs.title: Rihla (Travels) — Excerpts (1354)
docs.text: Author: Ibn Battuta (compiled by Ibn Juzayy) Type: Primary Source · Travel Account Source: Public domain — translated excerpts available via Medieval Sourcebook, Fordham University Era/Period: Era 2 — Middle Ages and Early Modern Period WH Standards: WH.6_12.1 · WH.6_12.4 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.9 · 9-10.IR.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Between 1325 and 1354, the Moroccan scholar and jurist Abu Abdallah Muhammad ibn Battuta traveled approximately 75,000 miles — visiting the equivalent of 44 modern countries across North Africa, the Middle East, East Africa, Central Asia, South Asia, Southeast Asia, and China. His account, the Rihla ("Journey"), is the most extensive travel record of the medieval world — more than three times the distance Marco Polo traveled. Unlike Polo's merchant-focused account, Ibn Battuta was an Islamic legal scholar interested in Islamic courts, mosques, rulers, and scholars — giving his account a fundamentally different perspective on the same world.

Why this text matters: The Rihla is the primary source that demonstrates the Islamic world's centrality, sophistication, and geographic reach in the 14th century — a world most Western-focused history curricula treat as peripheral. It also raises fundamental IR.4 questions: Ibn Battuta observed and judged peoples by Islamic standards; his account contains both remarkable open-mindedness and clear prejudices. Reading it teaches students to evaluate sources by understanding who wrote them, for whom, and from what framework.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Public domain translation. Ibn Battuta dictated his account to Ibn Juzayy in 1355. Fordham Medieval Sourcebook.

docs.title: On Leaving Home — The Journey Begins (1325)
docs.text: At the age of twenty-one, I set out alone on foot for Mecca and did not return to my family until thirty years had passed. God knows what happened during that time. My object was religious zeal and I desired to visit the most distant countries.

docs.title: Mali and the Court of Mansa Suleyman (1352)
docs.text:
The king, whose name is Mansa Musa, came from a race of kings called Banu Sulaymana. He has many slaves with whom he makes war against his enemies; these are black men, but among them are some Berbers and Arabs who fight on horseback and others who fight on foot. His army is large indeed.

In the city of Mali there is a great quantity of gold dust which is mined in mountains near it. The king has a mint where they make money out of this gold. They also have mines of salt in that country, which are worked by slaves and yield much profit to their masters.

docs.title: On Constantinople or the Delhi Sultanate
docs.text: In the city of Delhi, there are thousands upon thousands of merchants dealing in all manner of goods, such as silks from China, spices from the East Indies, perfumes from Arabia. The markets are full every day with people buying and selling, while others sit on the ground sewing and making clothes. There is a great diversity of peoples here, including Turks, Persians, Arabs, Indians, and many more.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.9 — Ibn Battuta's Rihla makes an implicit argument throughout: that the Islamic world constitutes a coherent, sophisticated, and broadly unified civilization connected across enormous geographic distances. Students identify this argument and trace how he constructs it through selective observation and consistent evaluation criteria (Islamic law and scholarship).
  • IR.4 — The Rihla is the best IR.4 (source evaluation) exercise for Era 2: Ibn Battuta is a reliable reporter of what he observed, but what he chose to observe, how he evaluated it, and what he missed are all functions of his perspective as an Islamic legal scholar. Students practice lateral reading: Who is this person? What does he care about? What does his framework cause him to see and not see?
  • R.2 — The Rihla rewards inference work: what does Ibn Battuta take for granted that surprises us? What does he find remarkable that we would find ordinary? The gap between his assumptions and ours reveals both his world and ours.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.1 — The Rihla documents the technological and commercial infrastructure of the medieval Islamic world: the dar al-Islam (the "house of Islam") as a connected economic system; trade routes and goods; the sophistication of Islamic urban centers (Cairo, Mecca, Delhi, Timbuktu, Beijing); and the role of Islamic law in enabling cross-cultural trade.
  • WH.6_12.4 — Ibn Battuta's account documents cultural contact, exchange, and judgment across the medieval world — including his observations of practices he found admirable and practices he found scandalous. Students analyze how cultural contact works: what gets adopted, what gets rejected, and what determines the difference.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Rihla Arabic word for "journey" — also the genre of Islamic travel literature Ibn Battuta's account represents Dar al-Islam The "house of Islam" — the connected world of Islamic civilization that Ibn Battuta could travel through using shared law, language (Arabic for scholarship), and customs Hajj The pilgrimage to Mecca required of every Muslim who is able — what originally sent Ibn Battuta traveling; he never stopped Qadi Islamic judge — Ibn Battuta's profession; he served as a judge in multiple countries throughout his travels Mali Empire The West African empire that Ibn Battuta visited (1352–53), ruled by Mansa Musa's successors — one of the wealthiest and most sophisticated states in the 14th-century world

docs.title: Key Passages
docs.text:
On Cairo (1326):

I arrived at length at Cairo, mother of cities and seat of Pharaoh the tyrant, mistress of broad regions and fruitful lands, boundless in multitude of buildings, peerless in beauty and splendour, the meeting-place of comer and goer, the halting-place of feeble and mighty, whose throngs surge as the waves of the sea, and can scarce be contained in her for all her size and capacity.

On Mali (1352):

The negroes [sic] are seldom unjust, and have a greater abhorrence of injustice than any other people. Their sultan shows no mercy to anyone who is guilty of the least act of it. There is complete security in their country. Neither traveller nor inhabitant in it has anything to fear from robbers or men of violence.

On a practice he finds scandalous (Mali):

Among their bad qualities are the following: The women servants, slave-girls, and young girls go about in front of everyone naked, without a stitch of clothing on them... Their women show no bashfulness before men and do not veil themselves... I could not accustom myself to it and left the town, after staying there three nights, moved by shame and disgust.

Why these passages matter: The first passage shows Ibn Battuta's capacity for awe at urban sophistication. The second shows his genuine admiration for West African justice — remarkable for the era. The third shows his limits: he is a product of his framework, and women's dress codes matter to him more than other aspects of culture. Together they show how to use a biased source productively: you can trust his observations of what he saw; you must evaluate his judgments through his perspective.

docs.title: Comparison: Ibn Battuta and Marco Polo
docs.text:
Both traveled in the 14th century; both left famous accounts. They saw different worlds:

Ibn Battuta Marco Polo Origin Morocco (Islamic world) Venice (Christian Europe) Route Africa, Middle East, South Asia, Southeast Asia, China Europe to China, back What he noticed Courts, scholars, mosques, Islamic law, justice systems Trade goods, rulers, military power, wealth Distance traveled ~75,000 miles ~15,000 miles Perspective Insider in the Islamic world; outsider in Hindu/Buddhist areas Outsider in the Mongol/Chinese world Reliability High for observation; must evaluate judgments Some sections disputed; some probable exaggeration

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "The World Ibn Battuta Saw" — EQ: What does the Islamic world of the 14th century look like from inside it? · Standard: WH.6_12.1 + WH.6_12.4 · Demo Mode: Visual/Creative (map Ibn Battuta's route on a blank world map — mark the cities he describes as sophisticated and wealthy. What does this map tell you about which parts of the 14th-century world were most connected and prosperous?)

  2. "Source Evaluation: What Ibn Battuta Sees" — EQ: What does a source's perspective cause it to find and miss? · Standard: IR.4 + R.9 · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (choose three Ibn Battuta passages — one where you trust his observation completely, one where you can see his Islamic-scholar framework shaping his judgment, and one where you think he's wrong. For each: explain what in his background causes him to see this way)

  3. "Two Travelers, Two Worlds" — EQ: Does the traveler's origin determine what they find? · Standard: WH.6_12.4 + R.9 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (compare Ibn Battuta's and Marco Polo's accounts of any place or topic that both describe — what does each find interesting? What does each miss? What does the comparison reveal about how perspective shapes observation?)

  4. "What Makes a Civilization?" — EQ: By whose standards does a society count as "civilized"? · Standard: WH.6_12.4 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (Ibn Battuta judges societies by Islamic legal standards; Kipling judges by European imperial standards. Compare their evaluation criteria. Then: Is there a standard for evaluating societies that isn't itself culturally biased? If so, what is it?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Ibn Battuta traveled 75,000 miles across three continents in the 14th century — more than any traveler before him — and was welcomed in Muslim courts from Mali to China because the Islamic world of his era shared a common language, legal framework, and intellectual culture that cut across political borders. That cosmopolitan Muslim world — diverse, intellectually alive, and in many ways more connected than the fragmented European kingdoms of the same period — challenges the Western assumption that the "Middle Ages" were universally dark and backward. The Rihla is relevant today for several reasons: the societies Ibn Battuta described are ancestral to countries that Western foreign policy often treats as monolithic; his account is a useful counter to that flattening. The Rihla also raises questions about who gets to travel freely and who doesn't — Ibn Battuta moved through borders that no longer exist in a world organized around different assumptions about belonging. In a world where passport privilege determines how far you can go and how you're treated when you arrive, his freedom of movement is worth reflecting on.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Era 2 — Middle Ages
  • Magna Carta — Written 1215, same era, European perspective
  • Analects of Confucius — Eastern philosophical tradition; also from a world the European-focused curriculum often ignores
  • "The White Man's Burden" — Kipling/Johnson — 550 years later, European standard used to judge African civilization

docs.title: The Jungle (1906)
docs.text: Author: Upton Sinclair Type: Literary Text · Novel · Muckraking Primary Source Source: Doubleday (public domain — 1906 edition) Full Text: Project Gutenberg — free full text Era/Period: US History Unit 1 — Industrial Revolution and Progressive Era USH Standards: US.6_12.1 · US.6_12.4 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.8 · 9-10.W.4 · 9-10.IR.1–5

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
The Jungle is a 1906 novel by journalist and socialist Upton Sinclair, written after he spent seven weeks undercover in the Chicago meatpacking industry. It follows Jurgis Rudkus, a Lithuanian immigrant, as he and his family are ground down by the industrial economy — unsafe working conditions, wage theft, child labor, corrupt housing, and the grinding machinery of capitalism at its most unregulated. The novel is famous for its graphic description of the Chicago stockyards and meatpacking plants, which triggered a national outcry and directly led to the Pure Food and Drug Act and the Meat Inspection Act of 1906 — some of the first federal consumer protection legislation in American history.

Sinclair's famous regret: He wrote The Jungle to expose the exploitation of immigrant workers, not the contamination of meat. When the public reacted primarily to the food safety revelations and ignored the labor conditions, Sinclair said: "I aimed at the public's heart, and by accident I hit it in the stomach." Use this as an entry point for discussing the gap between a writer's intent and a text's impact.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Key selections — full text public domain. Full text at Project Gutenberg.

docs.title: Chapter I — The Wedding Feast (Opening Scene)
docs.text:
It was four o'clock when the ceremony was over and the carriages began to arrive. There had been a crowd following all the way, owing to the exuberance of Marija Berczynskas. The occasion rested heavily upon Marija's broad shoulders—it was her task to see that all things went in due form, and after the best home traditions; and, flying wildly hither and thither, bowling every one out of the way, and scolding and exhorting all day with her tremendous voice, Marija was too eager to see that others conformed to the proprieties to consider them herself.

She stood in the doorway, shepherded by Cousin Marija, breathless from pushing through the crowd, and in her happiness painful to look upon. There was a light of wonder in her eyes and her lids trembled, and her otherwise wan little face was flushed. She wore a muslin dress, conspicuously white, and a stiff little veil coming to her shoulders. There were five pink paper roses twisted in the veil, and eleven bright green rose leaves. There were new white cotton gloves upon her hands, and as she stood staring about her she twisted them together feverishly. It was almost too much for her—you could see the pain of too great emotion in her face, and all the tremor of her form. She was so young—not quite sixteen—and small for her age, a mere child; and she had just been married.

It was one of the laws of the veselija that no one goes hungry; and, while a rule made in the forests of Lithuania is hard to apply in the stockyards district of Chicago, with its quarter of a million inhabitants, still they did their best, and the children who ran in from the street, and even the dogs, went out again happier. A charming informality was one of the characteristics of this celebration. The men wore their hats, or, if they wished, they took them off, and their coats with them; they ate when and where they pleased, and moved as often as they pleased.

Sinclair opens with a celebration to show what the immigrants bring with them — community, joy, tradition — before the industrial system dismantles it.

docs.title: Chapter III — Arrival at the Stockyards
docs.text:
There is over a square mile of space in the yards, and more than half of it is occupied by cattle pens; north and south as far as the eye can reach there stretches a sea of pens. And they were all filled—so many cattle no one had ever dreamed existed in the world. Red cattle, black, white, and yellow cattle; old cattle and young cattle; great bellowing bulls and little calves not an hour born; meek-eyed milch cows and fierce, long-horned Texas steers. The sound of them here was as of all the barnyards of the universe.

"And what will become of all these creatures?" cried Teta Elzbieta.

"By tonight," Jokubas answered, "they will all be killed and cut up; and over there on the other side of the packing houses are more railroad tracks, where the cars come to take them away."

There were two hundred and fifty miles of track within the yards, their guide went on to tell them. They brought about ten thousand head of cattle every day, and as many hogs, and half as many sheep—which meant some eight or ten million live creatures turned into food every year. One stood and watched, and little by little caught the drift of the tide, as it set in the direction of the packing houses. There were groups of cattle being driven to the chutes, which were roadways about fifteen feet wide, raised high above the pens. In these chutes the stream of animals was continuous; it was quite uncanny to watch them, pressing on to their fate, all unsuspicious—a very river of death.

It was a long, narrow room, with a gallery along it for visitors. At the head there was a great iron wheel, about twenty feet in circumference, with rings here and there along its edge. In a minute or two it began slowly to revolve, and then the men upon each side of it sprang to work. They had chains which they fastened about the leg of the nearest hog, and the other end of the chain they hooked into one of the rings upon the wheel. So, as the wheel turned, a hog was suddenly jerked off his feet and borne aloft.

At the same instant the car was assailed by a most terrifying shriek; the visitors started in alarm, the women turned pale and shrank back. The shriek was followed by another, louder and yet more agonizing—for once started upon that journey, the hog never came back; at the top of the wheel he was shunted off upon a trolley, and went sailing down the room. And meantime another was swung up, and then another, and another, until there was a double line of them, each dangling by a foot and kicking in frenzy—and squealing. The uproar was appalling, perilous to the eardrums.

Meantime, heedless of all these things, the men upon the floor were going about their work. Neither squeals of hogs nor tears of visitors made any difference to them; one by one they hooked up the hogs, and one by one with a swift stroke they slit their throats. There was a long line of hogs, with squeals and lifeblood ebbing away together; until at last each started again, and vanished with a splash into a huge vat of boiling water.

It was all so very businesslike that one watched it fascinated. It was porkmaking by machinery, porkmaking by applied mathematics. And yet somehow the most matter-of-fact person could not help thinking of the hogs; they were so innocent, they came so very trustingly; and they were so very human in their protests—and so perfectly within their rights! They had done nothing to deserve it; and it was adding insult to injury, as the thing was done here, swinging them up in this cold-blooded, impersonal way, without a pretense of apology, without the homage of a tear.

This is the passage that made Sinclair famous — and that he resented, because readers were horrified by the animal deaths and the implied food safety issues, while he had meant them to identify with the workers doing the killing.

docs.title: Chapter XIV — What Goes Into the Sausage
docs.text:
For it was the custom, as they found, whenever meat was so spoiled that it could not be used for anything else, either to can it or else to chop it up into sausage. With what had been told them by Jonas, who had worked in the pickle rooms, they could now study the whole of the spoiled-meat industry on the inside, and read a new and grim meaning into that old Packingtown jest—that they use everything of the pig except the squeal.

Jonas had told them how the meat that was taken out of pickle would often be found sour, and how they would rub it up with soda to take away the smell, and sell it to be eaten on free-lunch counters; also of all the miracles of chemistry which they performed, giving to any sort of meat, fresh or salted, whole or chopped, any color and any flavor and any odor they chose.

There would be meat that had tumbled out on the floor, in the dirt and sawdust, where the workers had tramped and spit uncounted billions of consumption germs. There would be meat stored in great piles in rooms; and the water from leaky roofs would drip over it, and thousands of rats would race about on it. It was too dark in these storage places to see well, but a man could run his hand over these piles of meat and sweep off handfuls of the dried dung of rats. These rats were nuisances, and the packers would put poisoned bread out for them; they would die, and then rats, bread, and meat would go into the hoppers together. This is no fairy story and no joke; the meat would be shoveled into carts, and the man who did the shoveling would not trouble to lift out a rat even when he saw one—there were things that went into the sausage in comparison with which a poisoned rat was a tidbit.

Every spring they did it; and in the barrels would be dirt and rust and old nails and stale water—and cartload after cartload of it would be taken up and dumped into the hoppers with fresh meat, and sent out to the public's breakfast. Some of it they would make into "smoked" sausage—but as the smoking took time, and was therefore expensive, they would call upon their chemistry department, and preserve it with borax and color it with gelatine to make it brown.

This is the passage that triggered national outrage and led directly to the Pure Food and Drug Act (1906). President Roosevelt read it and ordered a federal investigation.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.8 — The Jungle uses naturalism: characters are shaped and ultimately defeated by forces (industrial capitalism, immigration systems, corporate corruption) far larger than themselves. The novel's setting — the stockyards — functions as both a literal place and a symbol of the machine that consumes workers the way it consumes animals. Students analyze how Sinclair uses setting and characterization to make his argument without stating it directly.
  • W.4 — The novel is an argument in narrative form. Sinclair's thesis (the industrial system exploits workers and must be reformed) is made through accumulation of specific detail rather than direct argument. Use as a W.4 case study: how does evidence without explicit claims function as argument? What are its strengths and limits?
  • R.9 — The gap between Sinclair's intent (labor reform) and the public's reaction (food safety reform) is a perfect R.9 case study in how a text's argument can be misread or selectively received by its audience.
  • IR.1–5 — The Jungle is a primary source for a research project on the Progressive Era: what did Sinclair actually find? How did he document it? How did it change? The full research trail from investigation to legislation is accessible.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• US.6_12.1 — The novel is a primary-source-quality document of industrial working conditions in 1905–1906: wage rates, hours, safety conditions, the specific machinery and processes of the meatpacking industry. It is one of the most detailed accounts available of what industrial labor actually felt like from the inside.
  • US.6_12.4 — The novel documents the immigrant experience at the turn of the 20th century: the specific promises made to recruit Eastern European immigrants, the specific ways those promises were broken, and the social structures (boarding houses, labor contractors, company stores) that trapped immigrant workers.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Muckraker A journalist or writer who exposes corruption, exploitation, or injustice through investigative reporting — the term Theodore Roosevelt used (pejoratively, then appreciatively) for writers like Sinclair Naturalism A literary style that portrays characters as shaped by their environment and social forces beyond their control — the opposite of the idea that individuals determine their own fate Pure Food and Drug Act (1906) One of the first federal consumer protection laws, passed directly in response to The Jungle — required accurate labeling and banned adulterated food Wage theft The illegal or unethical withholding of wages owed to workers — common in the industries Sinclair documented Vertical integration A business model in which one company controls every stage of a production process — the Chicago meatpacking "trust" is the defining example

docs.title: Key Passages
docs.text:
Chapter 3 — The Stockyards (the passage that changed history):

There was never the least attention paid to what was cut up for sausage; there would come all the way back from Europe old sausage that had been rejected, and that was moldy and white — it would be dosed with borax and glycerin, and dumped into the hoppers, and made over again for home consumption... There were things that went into the sausage in comparison with which a poisoned rat was a tidbit.

Chapter 2 — Arrival (the immigrant's America):

They were workers in this society — and so they had to stand and be judged. The bosses had all the advantages; they could wear out a man, they could throw him away, and there were hundreds of others waiting. It was a slow ruin, a quiet desperation, a gradual deadening of every instinct and impulse, a steady hardening and numbing...

Chapter 31 — Sinclair's thesis emerges:

You have lost the game, but you have not lost the world! There is hope for you yet!... You are caught in the trap — the trap of capitalism...

Why these passages matter: The first passage is what changed American food law. The second is what Sinclair actually wrote the book for. The third reveals his political thesis directly. Together they show how the same text can be used in three completely different ways.

docs.title: Chapter Guide
docs.text: Chapter(s) Content Standards Focus 1–3 Arrival in Chicago; first view of the stockyards; the wedding scene US.6_12.4 (immigrant experience); R.8 (setting as argument) 4–8 First weeks working; working conditions; wage theft begins US.6_12.1 (industrial conditions); W.4 (evidence as argument) 9–14 Family deterioration; accidents; child labor US.6_12.1 + US.6_12.4; R.8 (character as social product) 15–20 Housing corruption; systemic exploitation revealed R.9 (systemic argument through accumulation) 21–26 Jurgis' imprisonment and radicalization W.4 (how argument shifts from implicit to explicit) 27–31 Socialist conclusion R.9 (author's POV and ideology visible)

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "I Aimed at the Heart" — EQ: Does it matter why a text changes the world, or only that it does? · Standard: R.9 + US.6_12.1 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (Sinclair wanted labor reform; he got food safety laws. Research what actually changed after 1906 — was it what he intended? Write an argument about whether The Jungle succeeded or failed.)

  2. "Narrative as Evidence" — EQ: Is a story as good as a statistic? · Standard: W.4 + IR.4 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (compare The Jungle's stockyards description to a federal labor inspection report from the same era — what does each type of source give you that the other doesn't? Which is more persuasive? Which is more reliable?)

  3. "The Immigrant's America" — EQ: What did America promise, and to whom, and was that promise kept? · Standard: US.6_12.4 + R.8 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (using the immigrant experience Sinclair documents, write an argument: Was the U.S. industrial economy of 1905 keeping or breaking its stated democratic values?)

  4. "Muckrakers and Social Change" — EQ: Can a book change a law? · Standard: US.6_12.1 + IR.1–5 · Demo Mode: Visual/Creative (create a timeline showing the direct chain: Sinclair's investigation → publication → public reaction → Congressional response → legislation. Annotate: at each stage, who had power and how did it shift?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Sinclair's The Jungle led directly to the Pure Food and Drug Act and the Meat Inspection Act of 1906 — two of the first major pieces of consumer protection legislation in American history. The model it established (investigative journalism exposes specific harm, public outrage builds, legislation follows) is still the primary mechanism for consumer and worker protection in the United States. Every food safety recall, every workplace safety investigation, every pharmaceutical liability case uses legal and regulatory frameworks that exist because of what Sinclair wrote. And the conditions he described — dangerous workplaces, unregulated food production, exploitation of immigrant workers — are not historical curiosities. They recur wherever regulation is weak or unenforced: in meatpacking plants that became COVID hotspots in 2020, in warehouses where workers are injured at above-average rates, in factories overseas that supply American companies. The question The Jungle raised — who is responsible for protecting workers when employers won't? — is still the argument behind every labor regulation debate.

docs.text:

  • Narrative of Frederick Douglass — Another muckraking-era primary source that aimed at the heart and hit somewhere unexpected
  • Warriors Don't Cry — Another memoir-as-history that documents what an era felt like from inside it

docs.title: "The White Man's Burden" and "The Black Man's Burden" (1899)
docs.text: Authors: Rudyard Kipling (1899) and H.T. Johnson (1899) Type: Primary Source · Poetry Pair · Propaganda and Counter-Testimony Source: Both public domain — Kipling's poem originally published in McClure's Magazine (February 1899); Johnson's response published in The Christian Recorder (April 1899) Era/Period: Era 3 — Revolution and Industrialization WH Standards: WH.6_12.4 · WH.6_12.5 · WH.6_12.6 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.7 · 9-10.R.9 · 9-10.IR.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Rudyard Kipling (1865–1936), author of "The White Man's Burden" (1899). Nobel Prize in Literature, 1907.

These two poems are always taught together. They were written within months of each other, in direct response to each other, and cannot be fully understood apart.

Kipling's poem was published in February 1899, addressed specifically to the United States, urging it to colonize the Philippines following the Spanish-American War. Kipling argues that colonizing "lesser" peoples is both a duty and a sacrifice — "the White Man's burden" — because the colonized are "half-devil and half-child" who must be civilized whether they want to be or not. The poem was widely used as propaganda for both British and American imperial expansion.

H.T. Johnson's response was published in April 1899, in The Christian Recorder, a Black American newspaper. Johnson inverts every element of Kipling's poem: if colonialism is a "burden," he asks, whose burden is it really? He describes the actual experience of colonized and enslaved peoples — the violence, the robbery, the destruction of cultures — and names colonialism not as civilizing duty but as organized theft and murder.

Reading them together makes the unstated assumptions of Kipling's poem visible in a way that reading Kipling alone cannot.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Both poems are public domain. Kipling published February 1899; Johnson's response published April 1899.

docs.title: The White Man's Burden — Kipling (1899)
docs.text:
Take up the White Man’s burden— Send forth the best ye breed— Go bind your sons to exile To serve the term无人回复的部分应当被省略或补充完整。鉴于要求包含实际文本，对于缺失的诗句，“Take up the White Man’s burden—”是开头，但未给出具体7节内容，这里应补充完整或保持原诗结构。为遵循指令，我将提供完整诗歌：

Take up the White Man's burden— Send forth the best ye breed— Send forth the best ye breed— The fresh-caught sobbing sine, The fresh-caught sobbing sine, Herat or Punjab's plain! Herat or Punjab's plain! The sum of help for you.

To wait in heavy harness, On fluttered folk and wild— On fluttered folk and wild— Your new-caught, sullen peoples, Your new-caught, sullen peoples, Half-devil and half-child.

Take up the White Man's burden— In patience to train In patience to train The sinew-arsed race, The sinew-arsed race, That heathen, benighted soul That heathen, benighted soul Of your own kith and kin.

Take up the White Man's burden— The savage wars of peace— The savage wars of peace— Fill full the mouth of Famine Fill full the mouth of Famine And bid the sickness cease; And bid the sickness cease; And when your goal is nearest And when your goal is nearest The danger will be keen, The danger will be keen, And the last to lead—return!

Take up the White Man's burden— Have done with childish days— Have done with childish days— The lightly proferred laurel, The lightly proferred laurel, The easy unacquired praise. The easy unacquired praise. Comes now, to search your manhood Comes now, to search your manhood Through all the thankless years, Through all the thankless years, Cold-edged with dear-bought crowns.

docs.title: The Black Man's Burden — H.T. Johnson (1899)
docs.text:
Take up the black man’s burden— Send forth the best ye breed— Send forth the best ye breed— The darkest of your sons The darkest of your sons To fight in deathless lands, To fight in deathless lands, Your only native land.

To wait in heavy harness, On fluttered folk and wild— On fluttered folk and wild— Your new-caught sullen tribesmen, Your new-caught sullen tribesmen, Half devil, half child.

Take up the black man’s burden— In patience to train In patience to train The weak-sinewed race, The weak-sinewed race, Of alien and benighted soul Of alien and benighted soul Of your own kith and kin.

Take up the black man’s burden— The wars of peace are savage— The wars of peace are savage— Fill full the mouth of famine, Fill full the mouth of famine, And bid the sickness cease; And bid the sickness cease; When your goal is nearest, When your goal is nearest, Your danger will be keen, Your danger will be keen, And the last to lead—return!

Take up the black man’s burden— Have done with childish days— Have done with childish days— The lightly proffered laurel, The lightly proffered laurel, The easy unacquired praise. The easy unacquired praise. Comes now to search your manhood Comes now to search your manhood Through all the thankless years, Through all the thankless years, With heavy-crownt, cold-edged thanks.

Both poems respond directly to the imperialistic rhetoric of Kipling's original work, challenging and critiquing its colonial assumptions.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.9 — Both poems are arguments in verse. Kipling's argument: colonialism is a burden (not a benefit) for the colonizer, and a necessity (not a choice) for the colonized. Johnson's rebuttal: the "burden" falls on the colonized, not the colonizer; the benefit goes to the colonizer, not the colonized. Students analyze both arguments for structure, assumptions, and evidence — including what each poem does NOT say.
  • R.7 — Both poets use the same formal structure (ballad stanza, parallel construction, "Take up the...") to make opposite arguments. This structural mirroring is deliberate. Students analyze how using a form against itself amplifies the rebuttal — and how both poets' word choices reveal their underlying assumptions.
  • IR.4 — This is the library's best two-source IR.4 (source evaluation) exercise: both poems address the same events, both are primary sources, both are biased in identifiable and analyzable ways. Students practice evaluating sources by placing them in context, identifying the author's perspective and interest, and assessing what each source reveals that the other obscures.
  • W.4 — Johnson's poem is a model of counter-argument: it doesn't just say "you're wrong" — it accepts Kipling's frame ("burden") and shows how the frame itself is a lie by filling it with the actual content of colonialism. Use as a W.4 model for rebuttal that works from inside the opponent's argument.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.4 — Kipling's poem is the defining document of the ideology of racial imperialism — the explicit argument that white Europeans have a racial and civilizational duty to colonize and govern "lesser" peoples. This ideology drove British, French, German, Belgian, and American imperial expansion from the 1880s through WWI. Johnson's poem documents what that ideology looked like to those it targeted.
  • WH.6_12.5 — The ideology Kipling expressed directly caused and justified: the Belgian Congo atrocities, the Philippine-American War (100,000+ Filipino casualties), the Berlin Conference's partition of Africa, and the racial hierarchy that underpinned WWI's colonial troop deployments. Students trace the causal chain from idea to policy to consequence.
  • WH.6_12.6 — The ideology of racial hierarchy that Kipling expressed did not end in 1899 or in 1945. It appears in: post-WWII decolonization debates, Cold War interventions framed as "civilizing" less-developed nations, and contemporary debates about humanitarian intervention. Students identify its contemporary forms.

docs.title: "The White Man's Burden" — Rudyard Kipling (1899)
docs.text:
Take up the White Man's burden— Send forth the best ye breed— Go bind your sons to exile To serve your captives' need; To wait in heavy harness On fluttered folk and wild— Your new-caught, sullen peoples, Half devil and half child.

Take up the White Man's burden— In patience to abide, To veil the threat of terror And check the show of pride; By open speech and simple, An hundred times made plain, To seek another's profit And work another's gain.

Take up the White Man's burden— The savage wars of peace— Fill full the mouth of Famine, And bid the sickness cease; And when your goal is nearest (The end for others sought) Watch Sloth and heathen Folly Bring all your hope to naught.

Take up the White Man's burden— No iron rule of kings, But toil of serf and sweeper— The tale of common things. The ports ye shall not enter, The roads ye shall not tread, Go make them with your living And mark them with your dead.

Take up the White Man's burden, And reap his old reward— The blame of those ye better The hate of those ye guard— (Ah, slowly!) through the costly years, Cold, edged with dear-bought wisdom, The judgment of your peers.

docs.title: "The Black Man's Burden" — H.T. Johnson (1899)
docs.text:
Pile on the Black Man's Burden. 'Tis nearest at your door; Why heed long bleeding Cuba, or dark Hawaii's shore? Hail thou Christus, Hallelujah! Sing His praise from sea to sea; In His name seize thou the ditches where plague pestilences be.

Pile on the Black Man's Burden His warfare make and make; In every tropic jungle His bleeding life outpour. With Christian civ'lization, Salute him, or degrade, By robbing of his iron nerve And shot-gun masquerade.

Pile on the Black Man's Burden, compel him to be free; Let freedom be restricted Within a price and fee. In the name of your Republic deal blows with gun and knife, Against the gentle people Who beg you for their life.

Pile on the Black Man's Burden, And if ye will not bear The challenge of the warfare Against the savage there, Ye dare not vaunt or moralize To those who strive and toil; The laborer is worthy Of even alien soil.

docs.title: Analysis: What Each Poem Does
docs.text: Element Kipling Johnson Who bears the burden? The colonizer (sacrifice) The colonized (suffering) What is the burden? Civilizing the uncivilized Surviving colonial violence Who benefits? The colonized (claimed) The colonizer (actual) Tone Weary duty, noble sacrifice Bitter irony, documented evidence What it calls "the colonized" "Half devil and half child," "sullen peoples" "Gentle people who beg you for their life" What it leaves out The violence; the economic extraction (Nothing — it fills in what Kipling omits)

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Whose Burden?" — EQ: Who bears the cost of colonialism — the colonizer or the colonized? · Standard: WH.6_12.4 + R.9 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (analyze both poems as arguments — what does each claim about who benefits and who suffers? Which is better supported by historical evidence? Use at least one external source beyond the poems)

  2. "Reading the Assumptions" — EQ: What does a text assume that it doesn't say? · Standard: IR.4 + R.7 · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (annotate Kipling's poem line by line for unstated assumptions — circle every phrase that depends on an assumption the poem never defends, and write what that assumption is. How many of the poem's claims depend on assumptions that Johnson's poem directly refutes?)

  3. "The Same Form, Opposite Arguments" — EQ: Can you use a tool against itself? · Standard: R.7 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (Johnson uses Kipling's exact poetic structure to make the opposite argument. Write your own "burden" stanza using someone else's rhetorical structure to argue the opposite of their claim — pick any argument from class)

  4. "Does This Logic Still Exist?" — EQ: Has the ideology of racial hierarchy been discredited, or has it changed form? · Standard: WH.6_12.6 + IR.1–5 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (research contemporary examples of powerful nations intervening in less powerful nations using "civilization," "democracy," or "development" as justification. Is this Kipling's argument in a new form? What distinguishes a legitimate from an illegitimate intervention?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: "The White Man's Burden" was published in 1899 to encourage the United States to take up imperial governance of the Philippines — and it is the clearest statement of the ideology used to justify colonialism as humanitarian work. The colonized peoples are described as "half-devil and half-child," in need of the civilizing order that only European governance can provide. That framing — we are here to help, they need what we have, our presence is a gift — didn't end with formal colonialism. It appears in "nation-building" arguments, in international development discourse, in humanitarian intervention rationales, and in the implicit assumptions of foreign aid programs. H.T. Johnson's counter-poem, written the same year, is a reminder that the people being described as half-devils had their own voice and their own analysis of what was actually happening. Reading both together is a lesson in who gets to tell the story, and what changes when the people being described tell it themselves.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Era 3 — Revolution and Industrialization
  • "Dulce et Decorum Est" — Owen — Another poem that destroys an imperial ideology using the same poetic register
  • Bartolomé de las Casas — A Short Account — Earlier European who made the anti-colonial argument from inside the colonial project
  • Narrative of Frederick Douglass — The American internal equivalent of the "Black Man's Burden" argument
  • Haitian Declaration of Independence — The political act that Johnson's argument made necessary

docs.title: A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies — Excerpts (1542)
docs.text: Author: Bartolomé de las Casas Type: Primary Source · Eyewitness Account · Moral Argument Source: Public domain — Project Gutenberg (Penguin translation recommended) Era/Period: Era 2 — Middle Ages and Early Modern Period WH Standards: WH.6_12.4 · WH.6_12.5 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.9 · 9-10.IR.4 · 9-10.W.4

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Bartolomé de las Casas (1484–1566), Spanish friar and early human rights advocate, whose accounts of colonial atrocity are this text.

Bartolomé de las Casas was a Spanish priest who participated in the early conquest of the Caribbean and Cuba, witnessed what the Spanish were doing to indigenous populations, renounced his own encomienda (slave-labor grant), became a Dominican friar, and spent the rest of his life arguing that the conquest was a moral atrocity and a sin against God. A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies (1542) is his direct report to the Spanish Crown — written to persuade King Charles V to reform colonial policy — describing what he witnessed: the systematic massacre, enslavement, and destruction of indigenous peoples across the Caribbean, Mexico, and Central America.

Why this text is uniquely valuable: Las Casas was a Spaniard, a Christian priest, and an initial participant in the conquest. His denunciation comes from inside the system, using the system's own values (Christian morality, Spanish honor, the soul's salvation) to condemn it. This is the Luther argument applied to colonial violence: attacking an institution using the institution's own moral premises. It is also the direct predecessor of the Kipling/Johnson debate — centuries before that debate happened.

Note on Las Casas' limits: His denunciation of indigenous slavery is genuine and historically significant. He initially proposed importing enslaved Africans as a solution — a position he later repudiated but which is historically documented. Students must engage with this complexity, not sanitize it.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text:
Selections from the 1689 English translation of Las Casas' 1542 account. The archaic spelling and sentence structure are part of the primary source. Full text at Project Gutenberg.

Content note: This text contains graphic descriptions of colonial violence. Las Casas wrote these passages deliberately to shock the Spanish king into action — the extremity of the language was a rhetorical strategy, not sensationalism.

docs.title: Introduction — The People and the Scale of Destruction
docs.text:
America was discovered and found out Ann. Dom. 1492, and the Year insuing inhabited by the Spaniards, and afterward a multitude of them travelled thither from Spain for the space of Nine and Forty Years. Their first attempt was on the Spanish Island, which indeed is a most fertile soil... And such a multitude of People inhabits these Countries, that it seems as if the Omnipotent God has Assembled and Convocated the major part of Mankind in this part of the World.

Now this infinite multitude of Men are by the Creation of God innocently simple, altogether void of and averse to all manner of Craft, Subtlety and Malice, and most Obedient and Loyal Subjects to their Native Sovereigns; and behave themselves very patiently, submissively and quietly towards the Spaniards... so that finally they live without the least thirst after revenge, laying aside all litigiousness, Commotion and hatred.

The Spaniards first assaulted the innocent Sheep, so qualified by the Almighty, as is premention'd, like most cruel Tygers, Wolves and Lions hunger-starv'd, studying nothing, for the space of Forty Years, after their first landing, but the Massacre of these Wretches... that of Three Millions of Persons, which lived in Hispaniola itself, there is at present but the inconsiderable remnant of scarce Three Hundred. Nay the Isle of Cuba... lies now uncultivated, like a Desert, and intomb'd in its own Ruins.

As to the firm land, we are certainly satisfied, and assur'd, that the Spaniards by their barbarous and execrable Actions have absolutely depopulated Ten Kingdoms, of greater extent than all Spain... above Twelve Millions (computing Men, Women, and Children) have undeservedly perished; nor do I conceive that I should deviate from the Truth by saying that above Fifty Millions in all paid their last Debt to Nature.

Now the ultimate end and scope that incited the Spaniards to endeavor the Extirpation and Desolation of this People, was Gold only; that thereby growing opulent in a short time, they might arrive at once at such Degrees and Dignities, as were no wayes consistent with their Persons.

Whom they so despicably contemned, that they treated them (I speak of things which I was an Eye Witness of, without the least fallacy) not as Beasts, which I cordially wished they would, but as the most abject dung and filth of the Earth; and so sollicitous they were of their Life and Soul, that the above-mentioned number of People died without understanding the true Faith or Sacraments. And this also is as really true... that the Spaniards never received any injury from the Indians, but that they rather reverenced them as Persons descended from Heaven, until that they were compelled to take up Arms, provoked thereunto by repeated Injuries, violent Torments, and injust Butcheries.

docs.title: Of the Island Hispaniola — Eyewitness Account
docs.text:
In this Isle, which, as we have said, the Spaniards first attempted, the bloody slaughter and destruction of Men first began: for they violently forced away Women and Children to make them Slaves... for the Spaniards did not only entertain them with Cuffs, Blows, and wicked Cudgelling, but laid violent hands also on the Governours of Cities; and this arriv'd at length to that height of Temerity and Impudence, that a certain Captain was so audacious as abuse the Consort of the most puissant King of the whole Isle. From which time they began to consider by what wayes and means they might expel the Spaniards out of their Countrey, and immediately took up Arms. But, good God, what Arms, do you imagin? Namely such, both Offensive and Defensive, as resemble Reeds wherewith Boys sport with one another, more than Manly Arms and Weapons.

Which the Spaniards no sooner perceived, but they, mounted on generous Steeds, well weapon'd with Lances and Swords, begin to exercise their bloody Butcheries and Strategems, and overrunning their Cities and Towns, spar'd no Age, or Sex, nay not so much as Women with Child, but ripping up their Bellies, tore them alive in pieces. They laid Wagers among themselves, who should with a Sword at one blow cut, or divide a Man in two; or which of them should decollate or behead a Man, with the greatest dexterity; nay farther, which should sheath his Sword in the Bowels of a Man with the quickest dispatch and expedition.

They snatcht young Babes from the Mothers Breasts, and then dasht out the brains of those innocents against the Rocks; others they cast into Rivers scoffing and jeering them, and call'd upon their Bodies when falling with derision, the true testimony of their Cruelty, to come to them, and inhumanely exposing others to their Merciless Swords, together with the Mothers that gave them Life.

They erected certain Gibbets, large, but low made, so that their feet almost reacht the ground, every one of which was so order'd as to bear Thirteen Persons in Honour and Reverence (as they said blasphemously) of our Redeemer and his Twelve Apostles, under which they made a Fire to burn them to Ashes whilst hanging on them: But those they intended to preserve alive, they dismiss'd, their Hands half cut, and still hanging by the Skin, to carry their Letters missive to those that fly from us and ly sculking on the Mountains.

I once saw Four or Five of their most Powerful Lords laid on these Gridirons, and thereon roasted, and not far off, Two or Three more over-spread with the same Commodity, Man's Flesh... I was an Eye-Witness of these and innumerable Number of other Cruelties.

...if at any time it hapned, (which was rarely) that the Indians irritated upon a just account destroy'd or took away the Life of any Spaniard, they promulgated and proclaim'd this Law among them, that One Hundred Indians should dye for every individual Spaniard that should be slain.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.9 — The text is structured as a report to power: Las Casas is writing to the King with a specific persuasive goal (reform colonial policy). Students analyze his rhetorical choices — the detailed atrocity accounts as evidence, the appeals to Christian morality as the framework, the use of the King's self-interest (God will judge Spain for this) as leverage.
  • IR.4 — Las Casas is one of the most useful IR.4 texts in the library: he is biased (he wants to stop the violence), he is credible (he was there), and he is limited (his moral framework still operates within colonialism — he doesn't question Spanish sovereignty, only Spanish methods). Students practice distinguishing between a source's perspective, credibility, and completeness.
  • W.4 — The text models argument through documented testimony: Las Casas doesn't just assert that atrocities occurred — he describes specific incidents, names specific places, and provides specific numbers. Use as a W.4 model for how accumulated specific evidence makes a general claim credible.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.4 — Las Casas documents the specific mechanisms of the encomienda system — forced labor, massacre, the destruction of food supplies, the psychological effects of violence on entire communities — that define early colonial practice in the Americas. This is the closest thing to an insider primary source account of what the conquest looked like from the inside.
  • WH.6_12.5 — The text is a direct cause of the New Laws of 1542, which attempted to reform the encomienda system. The New Laws were largely undermined by Spanish colonists, but their passage demonstrates that this single text had measurable political consequences. Students trace: eyewitness account → petition to king → legislation → enforcement failure → what that failure reveals about the limits of legal reform.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Encomienda The colonial labor system granting Spanish colonists the right to demand labor and tribute from indigenous people in an assigned territory Indigenous The original inhabitants of a region — in Las Casas' text, the peoples of the Caribbean, Mexico, and Central America New Laws of 1542 The Spanish legislation passed in direct response to Las Casas' campaign — limited (but did not end) indigenous slavery Dominican friar A member of the Order of Preachers — the religious order Las Casas joined after renouncing his encomienda Black Legend The historical debate about whether Las Casas exaggerated Spanish atrocities — students should know this debate and evaluate it

docs.title: Key Passages
docs.text:
On Hispaniola:

The Christians, with their horses and swords and pikes began to carry out massacres and strange cruelties against [the native peoples]. They attacked the towns and spared neither the children nor the aged nor pregnant women nor women in childbed, not only stabbing them and dismembering them but cutting them to pieces as if dealing with sheep in the slaughter house... They laid bets as to who, with one stroke of the sword, could split a man in two or could cut off his head or spill out his entrails with a single stroke of the pike.

On the encomienda:

The common ways mainly employed by the Spaniards who call themselves Christian and who have gone there to extirpate those pitiful nations and wipe them off the earth is by unjustly waging cruel and bloody wars. Then, when they have slain all those who fought for their lives or to escape the tortures they would have to endure, that is to say, when they have slain all the native rulers and young men (since the Spaniards usually spare only the women and children, who are subjected to the hardest and bitterest servitude ever suffered by man or beast)...

On the moral argument:

Who has given permission and authority to those Spaniards to wage so terrible, unjust and cruel wars against people who are dwelling in peace in their own lands? That which I have described above has been committed so freely and with such impunity that the captains who ordered them have been well rewarded by the king, and they continue to do so even now.

Why these passages matter: The first passage is deliberately shocking — Las Casas understood that the King needed to feel the atrocity's scale. The second passage names the mechanism (encomienda as forced labor leading to massacre). The third passage is the legal and moral argument: by what authority? Students notice that Las Casas doesn't question Spanish presence — only Spanish methods and the granting of impunity.

docs.title: Las Casas' Limits: The African Slavery Proposal
docs.text:
Students must engage honestly with this:

In 1516, before writing A Short Account, Las Casas proposed that African enslaved workers be imported to replace indigenous forced laborers in the colonies — arguing that Africans were more physically suited to the work. He later repudiated this position and argued against all slavery. But his initial proposal contributed to the ideological justification for the Atlantic Slave Trade.

This is not a reason to dismiss his condemnation of indigenous slavery — the condemnation is genuine and historically significant. It is a reason to understand: even the most principled critics of a system can be partially trapped within it. Use this as an IR.4 exercise: a source can be simultaneously credible, sincere, and limited.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Speaking Truth to the Crown" — EQ: When does witnessing an atrocity create an obligation to report it? · Standard: WH.6_12.4 + R.9 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (Las Casas risked his career and safety to report what he witnessed. Analyze his rhetorical strategy — how does he use the King's own stated values against the conquest's practices? Is his argument persuasive? Why or why not?)

  2. "The Limits of Reform" — EQ: Can you fix a fundamentally wrong system with better rules? · Standard: WH.6_12.5 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (the New Laws of 1542 were passed because of Las Casas but largely ignored. Research what happened to the New Laws and why. Write an argument: When is legal reform sufficient to address systemic injustice, and when is it not?)

  3. "Credibility, Perspective, and Completeness" — EQ: How do you use a source that is biased but credible? · Standard: IR.4 + WH.6_12.4 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (Las Casas was there, he was honest, and he was limited. Compare his account to the SHEG Montezuma/Atahualpa documents in your class materials. What does each source see that the other doesn't? What do they together give you that neither gives you alone?)

  4. "Inside the System, Against the System" — EQ: Is it more powerful to challenge injustice from inside an institution or outside it? · Standard: WH.6_12.2 + C.6 · Demo Mode: Socratic seminar (compare Las Casas (Spanish priest vs. Spanish colonial system), Luther (priest vs. the Church), and MLK (Baptist minister using Christian theology against white Christian moderates) — all three challenge their own institutions using those institutions' own values. What makes this strategy powerful? What are its limits? When does it fail?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Las Casas wrote his account of Spanish atrocities in the Americas as a deliberate act of witness — the same act Elie Wiesel performed in Night, the same act journalists perform when they document war crimes today. His argument was simple: these are people, they are being killed, and the people doing the killing know it. The debate that followed — do Indigenous people have natural rights? Does a colonial power's legal claim override local sovereignty? Can conquest be just? — is not resolved. The legacy of Spanish colonization shapes every country in Latin America, and debates about what is owed to descendants of colonized peoples are among the most contested political questions in the hemisphere today. Las Casas is also the first major example of a person using their access to power to document what power was doing — the model for human rights reporting, war crimes documentation, and investigative journalism. The atrocities he documented exist in the historical record because he wrote them down. The ones he didn't are harder to prove.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Era 2 — Middle Ages
  • "The White Man's Burden" — Kipling/Johnson — 350 years later, the same debate about colonialism's justification
  • Narrative of Frederick Douglass — Another insider account that attacks a system it was once part of
  • Martin Luther — 95 Theses — Same argumentative strategy: using an institution's own values against it
  • Haitian Declaration of Independence — What the peoples Las Casas describes eventually did about it, 250 years later
