docs.title: The Twelve Tables of Rome (c. 450 BCE)
docs.text: Author: The Decemviri (a Roman commission of ten men) Type: Primary Source · Legal Document Era/Period: WH Era 1 — Ancient Civilizations Standards: WH.6_12.2 · WH.6_12.3 · WH.6_12.4 · R.9

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text: The Twelve Tables were Rome's first written law code, produced around 450 BCE after plebeians (common citizens) demanded that laws be written down so that patricians (aristocrats) could no longer interpret them secretly. The laws were engraved on twelve bronze tablets and displayed in the Roman Forum for all citizens to read. They governed daily life: debt, property, family, contracts, and criminal punishment. They are not the first law code in history (Hammurabi's predates them by 1,300 years), but they are the foundation of Roman law — and through Rome, of modern Western legal systems.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Standard public domain translation. The original Latin text was lost — these laws are reconstructed from later Roman legal writings that quoted them.

docs.title: Table I — Concerning the Summons to Court
docs.text:
1. If anyone summons another before the magistrate, he must go. If he does not go, the one who summons him shall call witnesses to this. Then he shall seize him.

2. If he refuses or tries to flee, the summoner shall lay hands on him.

3. If illness or old age prevents the summoned man from appearing, the summoner shall furnish him a beast of burden. He is not obligated to furnish a covered carriage, unless he chooses.

4. If the defendant has a surety, the plaintiff shall proceed against the surety, not the defendant.

5. If the parties agree, they may settle the case themselves. If not, they shall state their case in the assembly or the court before noon. They shall appear together.

6. After noon, if only one party has appeared, the magistrate shall decide in his favor.

7. If both parties are present at sunset, the magistrate shall adjourn the case.

docs.title: Table II — Concerning Trials
docs.text:
1. A lawsuit for which no specific penalty is fixed shall be assessed at three hundred asses if the matter involves a broken bone; one hundred fifty asses in other cases.

2. If a man is unable to appear due to serious illness or if he is engaged in the service of the state, the day for his appearance shall be postponed.

3. The witness who refuses to give testimony shall be publicly called out as infamous, and shall be barred from giving testimony and from calling others as witnesses.

docs.title: Table III — Concerning Debts
docs.text:
1. After a debt has been acknowledged, or judgment has been pronounced in court, there shall be thirty days of grace.

2. After that, the debtor may be arrested. The creditor shall bring him to court.

3. If the debtor does not satisfy the judgment, and no one offers surety for him, the creditor shall take him home and put him in chains. He shall bind him with not less than fifteen pounds of chains, or with more if he chooses.

4. The debtor in chains shall live on his own food if he has it. If not, the creditor who holds him in chains shall give him a pound of grain per day, or more.

5. Unless the debt is discharged, the creditor shall hold him in chains for sixty days. During that time, on three successive market days, he shall bring him to the magistrate in the assembly and the amount of the debt shall be publicly announced.

6. If no one pays the debt, the creditor may sell the debtor as a slave beyond the Tiber River, or put him to death.

7. If there are several creditors, they may divide the debtor's body among them. If any creditor takes more or less than his share, there shall be no penalty.

docs.title: Table IV — Concerning the Rights of Fathers (Patria Potestas)
docs.text:
1. A notably deformed child shall be killed immediately.

2. If a father sells his son three times, the son shall be free from his father.

3. To repudiate a wife, her husband shall order her to have her own property for her own use, shall take the keys, and shall expel her.

4. A child born ten months after the father's death shall not be admitted to a legal inheritance.

docs.title: Table V — Concerning Inheritance and Guardianship
docs.text:
1. As a man has disposed of his household and his money in his will, so shall it be binding. If there is no will, the nearest male relation shall be heir. If there is no near relation, the clansmen shall have possession.

2. If a man is raving mad and has no guardian, his person and property shall be in the care of his clansmen.

3. An improvident person shall be placed under the guardianship of his clansmen or patrons. They shall have power over his property.

4. A child born after the father's will was drawn up shall receive the same share as if born before.

5. The debts of the deceased shall be divided in proportion among his heirs.

docs.title: Table VI — Concerning Acquisition and Possession
docs.text:
1. When a man makes a contract or conveyance, whatever he states verbally, that shall be binding.

2. Usucaption of moveable things requires one year's possession for its completion; but usucaption of an estate and buildings, two years.

3. A woman who does not wish to be subjected to her husband's control in this manner shall absent herself three nights in succession in every year, and so interrupt the usucaption of each year.

4. If a man wishes to alienate a slave or an animal and the slave or animal has a defect, the seller shall disclose it. He is responsible for concealed defects for two years.

5. A beam built into a house or a vineyard trellis may not be removed. Action may be taken against one who has built in another's beam for double its value.

6. Timber felled and made into boards shall be provided to the owner, if he desires, to enable him to claim his stolen property.

docs.title: Table VII — Concerning Land Rights
docs.text:
1. Let a space of two feet and a half be left between neighboring buildings.

2. Where the road is not paved, animals may be driven in any direction, as the driver wishes.

3. If the rainwater of a neighbor does injury to a man's property, he may bring an action. But if it is a natural watercourse, he may not.

4. A man must trim back his trees to a height of fifteen feet, so that neighbors' property receives light. If the tree extends over the neighbor's property, the neighbor may lop it himself.

5. A man may gather any fruit that falls from his tree onto a neighbor's property.

6. The right of passage over another's land may not be acquired by long use.

docs.title: Table VIII — Concerning Torts and Delicts (Injuries)
docs.text:
1. If anyone sings or composes a song that slanders or dishonors another, he shall be clubbed to death.

2. If anyone has broken the limb of another and does not settle with him, let there be retaliation in kind.

3. If anyone has broken a bone of a freeman with his hand or with a club, he shall pay a penalty of three hundred asses; or one hundred fifty asses if the bone is of a slave.

4. If anyone has committed a personal injury upon another, the penalty shall be twenty-five asses.

5. If a dog savages a man, the owner shall either restrain the dog or compensate the victim.

6. If anyone commits robbery or theft by night and if the owner kills him in the act, he shall be held to have killed him lawfully.

7. It is forbidden for a thief to be killed by daylight, unless he defends himself with a weapon; even then, the owner must call out before killing him.

8. In the case of a thief caught red-handed, if he is a slave, he shall be flogged and thrown from the Tarpeian Rock. If he is a free man, he shall be flogged and delivered as a bondsman to the person he robbed.

9. If anyone sets fire to a building, or to a heap of grain left near a farmhouse, he shall be bound, flogged, and put to death by fire; provided always that he has committed this act knowingly and with malice aforethought. If it was an accident, the law provides that he shall make good the damage; or if he has not the means, he shall receive a lighter punishment.

10. If anyone destroys by grazing or cuts another's crops harvested by night, he shall be hanged and put to death as a sacrifice to Ceres.

11. If a patron defrauds his client, he shall be declared an outcast.

12. Any person who incites an enemy against the Roman state, or hands over a citizen to the enemy, shall be put to death.

13. It shall be a capital offense for a judge or an arbiter to have accepted money in the course of a trial.

14. Testimony of a witness who has been found guilty of false witness shall never be received again.

docs.title: Table IX — Concerning Public Law
docs.text:
1. No privileges or statutes shall be enacted in favor of private persons, to the injury of others contrary to the law common to all citizens, and which individuals, no matter of what rank, have a right to make use of.

2. The penalty shall be death for a judge or arbiter legally appointed who has been found guilty of receiving a bribe for giving a decision.

3. It is forbidden to put to death any man unconvicted. The sentence must be that of the Roman people.

4. If anyone should stir up war against his country or deliver a citizen to the enemy, he shall suffer capital punishment.

5. Whoever shall have caused a tumult by night in the city, or whoever shall have killed another person shall be condemned.

docs.title: Table X — Concerning Sacred Law
docs.text:
1. No person shall bury or burn a corpse within the city.

2. One shall not do more than this in the preparation of a funeral: not more than three mourning garments, a small purple tunic, and ten flute players.

3. Women shall not tear their faces nor wail at funerals.

4. No person shall collect the bones of a deceased in order to hold a second funeral. This law does not apply to soldiers who have died in war or abroad.

5. Gold shall not be placed with the dead except in the following case: if the dead man's teeth are bound with gold, it shall be lawful to bury or burn that gold with him.

6. Let no newly made grave come within sixty feet of a house or a planted field.

docs.title: Table XI — Supplement I
docs.text:
1. Marriage between patricians and plebeians is hereby forbidden.

(Note: This prohibition was abolished shortly after the Tables were published, by the Lex Canuleia of 445 BCE — one of the earliest examples of a Roman law being successfully challenged by popular protest.)

docs.title: Table XII — Supplement II
docs.text:
1. If a slave commits a theft or injury in accordance with his master's order, the master is responsible.

2. If anyone knowingly commits fraud in dedicating property to the gods, he shall be judged guilty of a capital offense.

3. Whatever the people orders and enacts last shall be held as established law.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.9 — The Twelve Tables are a structured legal argument: each provision states a rule and implies the reasoning behind it. Students analyze what each law reveals about Roman values and what problems it was trying to solve.
  • R.7 — The terse, declarative style ("If anyone… he shall…") is a deliberate legal register. Compare to the narrative style of the Egyptian Book of the Dead or the philosophical verse of the Dhammapada — three different ways of encoding moral rules.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.2 — The Tables reveal Roman values: family authority (patria potestas), property rights, the sacred obligation to honor the dead, the horror of treason. What Rome believed shows in what Rome punished.
  • WH.6_12.3 — The Twelve Tables are the foundation of Roman law — and Roman law is the foundation of most Western legal systems today. They established the principle that laws must be written and public. The demand for written laws by plebeians is one of the first examples of citizens using political pressure to limit the power of an aristocracy.
  • WH.6_12.4 — Tables III (debt slavery) and IV (patria potestas) reveal the economic and social structure of early Rome: extreme inequality between creditors and debtors, near-absolute power of fathers over families, and legal distinctions between free citizens, freed persons, and slaves.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Patrician A member of the Roman aristocracy — the class that originally controlled law, politics, and religion Plebeian A common citizen of Rome — the class that demanded the Twelve Tables so they could know the laws that governed them Decemviri The committee of ten men appointed to write the Twelve Tables Patria potestas "Power of the father" — the legal authority of a Roman father over his entire household, including the power of life and death over his children Usucaption Acquiring legal ownership of property by possessing it for a long enough time without the owner objecting Surety A person who guarantees to pay a debt or fulfill an obligation if the primary party cannot Tarpeian Rock A cliff on the Capitoline Hill in Rome from which criminals and traitors were thrown to their deaths

docs.title: Key Passage
docs.text:
If a father sells his son three times, the son shall be free from his father. — Table IV, Law 2

This law reveals both the extreme power of Roman fathers (they could legally sell their sons into slavery) and a limit on that power — the son could be freed after three sales. It shows a legal system still treating people as property while beginning to recognize limits on that treatment.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Who Did Roman Law Protect?" — EQ: When a law is written down, who benefits? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + WH.6_12.4 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (read Tables III, IV, and VIII carefully. Who is protected by these laws? Who is not? Write a W.4 argument: were the Twelve Tables a step toward justice, or a codification of inequality?)

  2. "Three Legal Codes" — EQ: What does a civilization's law reveal about its values? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 · Demo Mode: Visual/Creative (create a comparison chart of Hammurabi's Code, the Twelve Tables, and one modern law — what problem does each address? What does each assume about human nature? What punishments does each allow?)

  3. "The People Demand to See the Law" — EQ: Why does it matter whether laws are written down and public? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (the Twelve Tables were created because plebeians demanded that laws be written down. Why? What happens when only some people know what the laws are? Can you think of modern examples where access to legal knowledge creates inequality?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: The Twelve Tables were created because ordinary Romans demanded that the laws be written down and posted publicly — before that, patrician magistrates could apply the law however they chose because only they knew what it said. That demand (make the rules visible to the people they govern) is the foundation of due process, the rule of law, and the legal right to know what you're accused of. When people argue about mandatory sentencing guidelines, judicial transparency, or the right to see the government data collected about you, they're making the same argument the plebs made in 450 BCE. The Twelve Tables also established that law should be fixed and predictable — not whatever the magistrate feels like today — which is still the argument in every debate about judicial discretion vs. mandatory minimums. Ignorance of the law is no excuse, as every law student learns — but that principle only makes sense when the law is publicly posted.

docs.title: Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948)
docs.text: Author: United Nations General Assembly (drafted by a committee chaired by Eleanor Roosevelt) Type: Primary Source · Founding International Document Source: United Nations — public domain Era/Period: Era 4 — Age of Global War and Globalization WH Standards: WH.6_12.3 · WH.6_12.6 USH Standards: US.6_12.1 · US.6_12.4 ELA Standards: 9-10.R.9 · 9-10.IR.1–5

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
On December 10, 1948 — three years after the liberation of the Nazi concentration camps and the Nuremberg trials — the United Nations General Assembly adopted the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR). It was the first international document to assert that all human beings possess inherent rights regardless of nationality, ethnicity, religion, or sex — and that these rights are not granted by governments but are universal and inalienable. The UDHR contains 30 articles covering civil, political, economic, social, and cultural rights. It was not a treaty (legally binding) but a declaration (statement of shared principle) — a distinction that has defined both its influence and its limits ever since.

The UDHR was drafted by representatives from countries across the ideological spectrum of the early Cold War, including the United States, the Soviet Union, France, China, and Lebanon. It passed 48–0, with 8 abstentions (including the Soviet bloc and Saudi Arabia). It has since become the foundation for more than 70 international human rights treaties and is the most translated document in human history.

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Public domain — UN document. Full text at UN.org.

docs.title: Preamble
docs.text:
The General Assembly,

Proclaims this Universal Declaration of Human Rights as a common standard of achievement for all peoples and all nations, to the end that every individual and every organ of society, keeping this Declaration constantly in mind, shall strive by teaching and education to promote respect for these rights and freedoms and by progressive measures, national and international, to secure their universal and effective recognition and observance, both among the peoples of Member States themselves and among the peoples of territories under their jurisdiction.

docs.title: Articles 1–10 — Core Rights
docs.text:
Article 1. All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. They are endowed with reason and conscience and should act towards one another in a spirit of brotherhood.

Article 2. Everyone is entitled to all the rights and freedoms set forth in this Declaration, without distinction of any kind, such as race, colour, sex, language, religion, political or other opinion, national or social origin, property, birth or other status.

Article 3. Everyone has the right to life, liberty and security of person.

Article 4. No one shall be held in slavery or servitude; slavery and the slave trade shall be prohibited in all their forms.

Article 5. No one shall be subjected to torture or to cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment.

Article 6. Everyone has the right to recognition everywhere as a person before the law.

Article 7. All are equal before the law and are entitled without any discrimination to equal protection of the law. All are entitled to equal protection against any discrimination in violation of this Declaration and against any incitement to such discrimination.

Article 8. Everyone has the right to an effective remedy by the competent national tribunals for acts violating the fundamental rights granted him by the constitution or by law.

Article 9. No one shall be subjected to arbitrary arrest, detention or exile.

docs.title: Articles 18–21 — Rights of Conscience and Political Life
docs.text:
Article 18. Everyone has the right to freedom of thought, conscience and religion; this right includes freedom to change his religion or belief, and freedom, either alone or in community with others and in public or private, to manifest his religion or belief in teaching, practice, worship and observance.

Article 19. Everyone has the right to freedom of opinion and expression; this right includes freedom to hold opinions without interference and to seek, receive and impart information and ideas through any media and regardless of frontiers.

Article 20. (1) Everyone has the right to peaceful assembly and association. (2) No one may be compelled to belong to an association.

Article 21. (1) Everyone has the right to take part in the government of his country, directly or through freely chosen representatives. (2) Everyone has the right of equal access to public service in his country. (3) The will of the people shall be the basis of the authority of government; this will shall be expressed in periodic and genuine elections which shall be by universal and equal suffrage and shall be held by secret vote or by equivalent free voting procedures.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.9 — The UDHR is an argumentative document structured as a declaration: its Preamble makes the case for why universal rights are necessary; its articles specify what those rights are. Students analyze the argument structure of the Preamble — what assumptions does it make? What historical events is it responding to? Compare the rhetorical approach to the French Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789) and the Haitian Declaration (1804).
  • IR.1–5 — The UDHR is the natural anchor document for any research project on human rights, international law, decolonization, or Cold War politics. Every article is a potential research question: How well has Article X been protected? Which countries have violated it? What enforcement mechanisms exist?
  • W.4 — The Preamble is a model of argument-by-historical-premise: each "Whereas" clause establishes a premise; the conclusion follows from the accumulated premises. Students use this structure as a W.4 model.

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.3 — The UDHR represents the post-WWII attempt to build an international order based on rights rather than power alone — the direct institutional response to the Melian logic that enabled fascism, imperialism, and genocide. Students trace the lineage: Four Freedoms Speech → Atlantic Charter → UN Charter → UDHR.
  • WH.6_12.6 — The UDHR is the living document most directly relevant to the present. Every contemporary human rights debate — refugee rights, LGBTQ+ rights, economic rights, climate displacement — involves specific UDHR articles. Students research which articles are honored, which are violated, and what enforcement looks like (or doesn't).
  • US.6_12.1 — The UDHR was partly a Cold War document: the U.S. pushed civil and political rights; the Soviet Union pushed economic and social rights. This debate shaped the Cold War ideological competition and explains why the UDHR covers both.
  • US.6_12.4 — Civil rights activists, including MLK, explicitly invoked the UDHR in arguing that the U.S. was violating its stated international commitments. The UDHR was a weapon in the domestic civil rights argument.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Inalienable rights Rights that cannot be taken away or transferred — not granted by governments but inherent in human personhood Declaration vs. treaty A declaration is a statement of principle; a treaty is legally binding. The UDHR is a declaration — it carries moral authority but no enforcement mechanism Eleanor Roosevelt Chair of the UN Human Rights Commission that drafted the UDHR; instrumental in its scope and passage Abstention Choosing not to vote — the 8 nations that abstained (Soviet bloc, Saudi Arabia, South Africa) did not oppose but refused to endorse Ratification The formal adoption of an international treaty by a national government — subsequent human rights treaties (ICCPR, ICESCR) were treaties requiring ratification

docs.title: Selected Articles
docs.text:
Preamble:

Whereas recognition of the inherent dignity and of the equal and inalienable rights of all members of the human family is the foundation of freedom, justice and peace in the world...

Whereas disregard and contempt for human rights have resulted in barbarous acts which have outraged the conscience of mankind, and the advent of a world in which human beings shall enjoy freedom of speech and belief and freedom from fear and want has been proclaimed as the highest aspiration of the common people...

Article 1:

All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. They are endowed with reason and conscience and should act towards one another in a spirit of brotherhood.

Article 3:

Everyone has the right to life, liberty and security of person.

Article 25:

Everyone has the right to a standard of living adequate for the health and well-being of himself and of his family, including food, clothing, housing and medical care and necessary social services, and the right to security in the event of unemployment, sickness, disability, widowhood, old age or other lack of livelihood in circumstances beyond his control.

Article 26:

Everyone has the right to education. Education shall be free, at least in the elementary and fundamental stages...

Why these articles matter: Article 1 is the philosophical foundation. Article 3 is the minimum protection that every other article builds on. Articles 25 and 26 are the most contested: they claim economic and social rights — which the U.S. has historically resisted as properly governmental obligations. These are still live arguments.

docs.title: The Rights Chain in the Reading Library
docs.text:
The UDHR is the culmination of a chain of rights documents traceable through the library:

Document Year What it contributed Hammurabi's Code c. 1754 BCE Law as protection against arbitrary power Pericles' Funeral Oration 431 BCE Civic rights within democracy Magna Carta 1215 Limits on sovereign power; due process Common Sense 1776 Self-governance as a right Declaration of Rights of Man 1789 Universal rights as political foundation Haitian Declaration 1804 Rights extended to formerly enslaved people FDR Four Freedoms 1941 Freedom framework for international order UDHR 1948 Universal codification of all prior frameworks

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Which Rights Matter Most?" — EQ: If you could only protect three of the 30 UDHR articles, which would you choose and why? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + W.4 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (make an argument for your selection — what principle guides your choices? What does your selection reveal about your values?)

  2. "The Rights the U.S. Didn't Sign" — EQ: Can a country claim to support human rights while refusing to legally commit to protecting them? · Standard: US.6_12.1 + R.9 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (research which international human rights treaties the U.S. has ratified and which it hasn't — construct an argument about what this pattern reveals)

  3. "Night Meets the UDHR" — EQ: What rights were violated in the Holocaust, and did the world respond adequately? · Standard: WH.6_12.6 + IR.1–5 · Demo Mode: Visual/Creative (match specific events from Night to the UDHR articles violated — then research: did any of the subsequent enforcement mechanisms actually prevent another genocide?)

  4. "The Rights Chain" — EQ: How does an idea about human dignity become international law? · Standard: WH.6_12.6 + IR.1–5 · Demo Mode: Portfolio Annotation (trace the chain from Magna Carta through the UDHR using documents in the reading library — annotate at each step: what problem was this document trying to solve? What did it leave unresolved?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights was adopted in 1948 and has no enforcement mechanism. That gap — between what the Declaration says and what governments do — is a live political question every day. When refugees are turned back at borders, when political prisoners are held without trial, when journalists are jailed for reporting, when ethnic minorities are persecuted by their own governments — in each case, the human rights framework created by the UDHR is the vocabulary used to condemn the action, and the lack of enforcement is why the condemnation often changes nothing. The Declaration is also the source of the framework that every human rights organization, international court, and humanitarian intervention uses to justify its actions. Understanding the UDHR means understanding where the language of international human rights law comes from, why it has the authority it has, and why it has the limits it has. Article 25 (the right to a standard of living adequate for health and wellbeing, including food, housing, and medical care) is also the foundation of every argument about economic rights — and is one of the most contested articles in the document.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Era 4 — Global War
  • FDR — Four Freedoms Speech — Direct predecessor
  • Nelson Mandela — Rivonia Trial Speech — Rights claimed in practice
  • MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — Rights invoked domestically
  • Melian Dialogue — The logic the UDHR was designed to overcome
  • Night — Wiesel — The events that made the UDHR necessary

Reading Library · SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

docs.title: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792)
docs.text: Author: Mary Wollstonecraft Type: Primary Source · Argumentative Essay Source: Project Gutenberg — public domain Era/Period: Era 3 — Age of Revolutions WH Standards: WH.6_12.4 · WH.6_12.3

docs.title: Read It
docs.text: Key selections — full text public domain. Full text at Project Gutenberg.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
After considering the historic page, and viewing the living world with anxious solicitude, the most melancholy emotions of sorrowful indignation have depressed my spirits, and I have sighed when obliged to confess, that either nature has made a great difference between man and man, or that the civilization, which has hitherto taken place in the world, has been very partial. I have turned over various books written on the subject of education, and patiently observed the conduct of parents and the management of schools; but what has been the result? a profound conviction, that the neglected education of my fellow creatures is the grand source of the misery I deplore; and that women in particular, are rendered weak and wretched by a variety of concurring causes, originating from one hasty conclusion.

Men neglect the duties incumbent on man, yet are treated like demi-gods; religion is also separated from morality by a ceremonial veil, yet men wonder that the world is almost, literally speaking, a den of sharpers or oppressors.

My own sex, I hope, will excuse me, if I treat them like rational creatures, instead of flattering their FASCINATING graces, and viewing them as if they were in a state of perpetual childhood, unable to stand alone. I earnestly wish to point out in what true dignity and human happiness consists — I wish to persuade women to endeavour to acquire strength, both of mind and body, and to convince them, that the soft phrases, susceptibility of heart, delicacy of sentiment, and refinement of taste, are almost synonymous with epithets of weakness, and that those beings who are only the objects of pity and that kind of love, which has been termed its sister, will soon become objects of contempt.

Dismissing then those pretty feminine phrases, which the men condescendingly use to soften our slavish dependence, and despising that weak elegancy of mind, exquisite sensibility, and sweet docility of manners, supposed to be the sexual characteristics of the weaker vessel, I wish to show that elegance is inferior to virtue, that the first object of laudable ambition is to obtain a character as a human being, regardless of the distinction of sex; and that secondary views should be brought to this simple touchstone.

The education of women has, of late, been more attended to than formerly; yet they are still reckoned a frivolous sex, and ridiculed or pitied by the writers who endeavour by satire or instruction to improve them. It is acknowledged that they spend many of the first years of their lives in acquiring a smattering of accomplishments: meanwhile, strength of body and mind are sacrificed to libertine notions of beauty, to the desire of establishing themselves, the only way women can rise in the world — by marriage. And this desire making mere animals of them, when they marry, they act as such children may be expected to act: they dress; they paint, and nickname God's creatures. Surely these weak beings are only fit for the seraglio! Can they govern a family, or take care of the poor babes whom they bring into the world?

docs.title: Chapter 9 — Of the Pernicious Effects Which Arise from the Unnatural Distinctions Established in Society
docs.text:
From the respect paid to property flow, as from a poisoned fountain, most of the evils and vices which render this world such a dreary scene to the contemplative mind. For it is in the most polished society that noisome reptiles and venomous serpents lurk under the rank herbage; and there is voluptuousness pampered by the still sultry air, which relaxes every good disposition before it ripens into virtue.

One class presses on another; for all are aiming to procure respect on account of their property: and property, once gained, will procure the respect due only to talents and virtue.

There must be more equality established in society, or morality will never gain ground, and this virtuous equality will not rest firmly even when founded on a rock, if one half of mankind are chained to its bottom by fate, for they will be continually undermining it through ignorance or pride. It is vain to expect virtue from women till they are, in some degree, independent of men; nay, it is vain to expect that strength of natural affection, which would make them good wives and good mothers. Whilst they are absolutely dependent on their husbands, they will be cunning, mean, and selfish, and the men who can be gratified by the fawning fondness, of spaniel-like affection, have not much delicacy, for love is not to be bought, in any sense of the word, its silken wings are instantly shrivelled up when any thing beside a return in kind is sought.

Destructive, however, as riches and inherited honours are to the human character, women are more debased and cramped, if possible by them, than men, because men may still, in some degree, unfold their faculties by becoming soldiers and statesmen.

But, to have done with these episodical observations, let me return to the more specious slavery which chains the very soul of woman, keeping her for ever under the bondage of ignorance.

The preposterous distinctions of rank, which render civilization a curse, by dividing the world between voluptuous tyrants, and cunning envious dependents, corrupt, almost equally, every class of people, because respectability is not attached to the discharge of the relative duties of life, but to the station, and when the duties are not fulfilled, the affections cannot gain sufficient strength to fortify the virtue of which they are the natural reward. Still there are some loop-holes out of which a man may creep, and dare to think and act for himself; but for a woman it is an herculean task, because she has difficulties peculiar to her sex to overcome, which require almost super-human powers.

A truly benevolent legislator always endeavours to make it the interest of each individual to be virtuous; and thus private virtue becoming the cement of public happiness, an orderly whole is consolidated by the tendency of all the parts towards a common centre.

docs.title: What This Text Is About
docs.text:
Mary Wollstonecraft (1759–1797), author of A Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792). Portrait by John Opie.

A Vindication of the Rights of Woman is one of the earliest and most forceful arguments for women's equality, written by Mary Wollstonecraft in 1792. Wollstonecraft argued that women are not naturally inferior to men — they appear so only because they are denied education and treated as ornamental objects rather than rational citizens. She wrote directly in response to the Declaration of the Rights of Man, which proclaimed universal rights while explicitly excluding women from political participation. The book is both a philosophical argument and a direct critique of the Enlightenment's failure to follow its own logic.

docs.title: ELA Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• R.9 — Wollstonecraft constructs a rigorous argument: Women seem inferior because they lack education. Education would prove they're rational equals. Therefore, denying women education is circular — it creates the inferiority it claims to observe. Students trace her logical structure.
  • R.7 — Her tone is unusually direct and combative for a woman writing in 1792. She uses words like "degraded" and "tyranny" to describe women's social position. Close reading of word choice reveals the risks she was taking.
  • W.4 — Argumentative writing: "Was the Declaration of the Rights of Man truly a universal document? Use Wollstonecraft as evidence." Or: "Which did more to expand rights — the Declaration of the Rights of Man or Wollstonecraft's Vindication?"

docs.title: Social Studies Standards This Connects To
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.4 — Vindication is a revolutionary document in the literal sense — it demanded a revolution in women's legal and social status. It demonstrates that the Age of Revolutions generated radical challenges to all forms of traditional hierarchy, not just monarchy.
  • WH.6_12.3 — Wollstonecraft directly challenges the political systems of her era (constitutional monarchy, republican government) for excluding women from citizenship. She exposes the gap between Enlightenment ideals and Enlightenment practice.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary
docs.text: Term Definition Rational Based on reason and logic — the Enlightenment's highest value, which Wollstonecraft claims women have equally Virtue Moral excellence — Wollstonecraft argues women are taught to value beauty over virtue, which corrupts their character Sensibility Emotional sensitivity and aesthetic refinement — the "feminine" qualities Wollstonecraft critiques as substitute for rational development Tyranny Cruel and oppressive rule — Wollstonecraft applies this word, typically used for kings, to the power men hold over women Subjugation The state of being under the control or authority of another — what Wollstonecraft argues is women's current condition

docs.title: Key Passage
docs.text:
I do not wish [women] to have power over men; but over themselves. — A Vindication of the Rights of Woman, Chapter 4

And:

Taught from their infancy that beauty is woman's sceptre, the mind shapes itself to the body, and roaming round its gilt cage, only seeks to adorn its prison. — A Vindication of the Rights of Woman, Chapter 3

The first passage states the argument precisely: not power over others, but self-determination. The second is one of the most memorable metaphors in political writing — women's education trains them to be beautiful prisoners. Students can analyze how Wollstonecraft uses this metaphor structurally throughout the text.

docs.title: Studio Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Who Did the Enlightenment Leave Out?" — EQ: Can a movement for freedom also be a movement for oppression? · Standard: WH.6_12.4 + Era 3 · Demo Mode: Written Assessment (analyze the contradiction between the Declaration of the Rights of Man and Wollstonecraft's critique)

  2. "Wollstonecraft vs. Rousseau" — EQ: Who controls what women should be? · Standard: WH.6_12.3 + Era 3 · Demo Mode: Verbal Conversation (structured discussion comparing Rousseau's Emile view of female education with Wollstonecraft's argument against it)

  3. "The Argument That Changed Nothing (At First)" — EQ: Why do powerful ideas sometimes fail to produce immediate change? · Standard: WH.6_12.4 + Era 3 · Demo Mode: Extended Writing (W.4 argument: Wollstonecraft published in 1792. Women in Britain didn't get the vote until 1928. What happened in between? What does this tell us about how social change works?)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Wollstonecraft's core argument — that women appear inferior to men not because they are, but because they've been denied the education and opportunity that would develop their capacities — is the founding argument of every subsequent debate about gender equality in education, employment, and public life. Her target was Rousseau, who argued that women should be educated to please men rather than to think for themselves. The contemporary version of that argument (that gender gaps in certain fields reflect natural differences rather than socialization and opportunity) is still made, still contested, and still requires exactly the kind of evidence-based rebuttal Wollstonecraft provided. Her argument that rational capacity is the basis of citizenship, and that if women have rational capacity they have the same claim to rights as men, is the logic used in every legal challenge to gender-based discrimination since. A Vindication of the Rights of Woman is 230 years old. The argument it makes hasn't been fully settled yet.

docs.title: Resource Bank
docs.text:
SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

The Resource Bank is the raw materials library for SDA. Everything here is a source document — PDFs, framers, unit plans, student samples, primary sources — organized by subject and ready to be processed into wiki entries, skinnies, or reading library pages.

docs.title: How This Works
docs.text:
The Resource Bank is modeled on the same pattern as the Continental Philosophy Wiki's raw library. Drop a file in the right folder, and Hermes can process it into a full wiki entry:

  1. Place a PDF or document in the appropriate subfolder below
  2. Tell Hermes: "Process RAW_INTAKE/[folder]/[filename] into a reading library entry"
  3. Hermes reads the file, generates a structured wiki page, and commits it

docs.title: Primary Sources
docs.text: Primary Sources — Historical documents, speeches, legal texts, manifestos. The raw texts that become reading library entries.

docs.title: FPS Framers
docs.text: FPS Framers — South High ELA and Social Studies unit framers. The official curriculum documents that define what students are expected to know.

docs.title: Student Samples
docs.text: Index — Anonymized student work at each proficiency level (1.0–4.0). Used to calibrate rubrics and train Hermes to score accurately.

docs.title: Studio Archives
docs.text: Index — Completed studio documentation from previous years. Essential Questions, Demos, Deliverables, and outcomes.

docs.title: Unit Plans
docs.text: Index — World History and US History unit plans, era guides, and pacing documents.

docs.title: Misc
docs.text: Index — Everything else that doesn't fit cleanly above.

docs.title: Processing Queue
docs.text:
Files dropped in ~/sda-wiki/RAW_INTAKE/ are the processing queue. Subfolders:

Folder What Goes Here RAW_INTAKE/reading_library/ PDFs to become reading library entries RAW_INTAKE/skinnies_raw/ Raw standard descriptions to become skinnies RAW_INTAKE/fps_frames/ FPS framer documents RAW_INTAKE/standards/ Standards documents RAW_INTAKE/crashcourse/ CrashCourse transcripts RAW_INTAKE/misc/ Everything else

docs.title: Ingest System
docs.text:
SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

The ingest system converts raw source documents into structured wiki entries. Every wiki entry links back to its original source document.

docs.title: How to Ingest a Document
docs.text:
Option 1 — Tell Hermes via Telegram: 

Process this PDF into a reading library entry: [drop file or paste URL]

Option 2 — Drop in RAW_INTAKE and ask Hermes: 

Process RAW_INTAKE/reading_library/[filename.pdf] into a reading library entry for Era 3.

Option 3 — Project Gutenberg (public domain texts): 

Add to reading library: Common Sense by Thomas Paine (Project Gutenberg)

Hermes will fetch the plain text, generate the structured entry, and commit it to the wiki.

docs.title: Source Document Storage
docs.text:
All source documents are stored in ~/sda-wiki/RAW_INTAKE/ and linked from wiki entries. Every reading library entry must include a Source field pointing to either:

  1. A file path in RAW_INTAKE (for PDFs you own)
  2. A public URL (Project Gutenberg, Avalon Project, etc.)
  3. A note that a school library copy is required (copyrighted texts)

docs.text:

docs.text:
Every reading library entry must have:

**Source:** [URL or file path — always a real link to the original document]

If it's a copyrighted text (Night, 1984, etc.): 

**Source:** School library copy required — not available digitally

docs.title: Studio Onboarding — Google Forms + Apps Script
docs.text:
SDA Commons · South High School

Every studio cycle begins with a student submitting a Google Form. The form triggers an Apps Script that generates a Studio Contract Google Doc and saves it to Drive.

docs.title: Step 1 — Create the Google Form
docs.text:
Before adding any questions: go to Form Settings → Responses tab → turn OFF "Collect email addresses." Google Forms enables this by default. If it's on, student Google account emails are logged — that's a FERPA problem. Turn it off first.

Also turn OFF "Limit to 1 response" — it requires sign-in and ties the response to a Google account.

Create a new Google Form with these exact fields (in order):

Question Type Required Student ID (your school ID number — no names) Short answer Yes Select your inquiry angle Multiple choice (options added per studio) Yes How will you demonstrate your learning? Multiple choice (6 options below) Yes Describe your deliverable Paragraph Yes What is your guiding research question? Paragraph Yes Target proficiency score Multiple choice: 2.0 / 3.0 / 4.0 Yes

Demo mode options (copy these exactly): 1. Written Assessment — focused written response to a prompt 2. Extended Writing — standalone essay or analytical piece 3. Verbal Conversation — talk through your understanding with your advisor 4. Visual/Creative — sketchnote, diagram, or infographic 5. Multimedia/Performance — podcast, video, or formal presentation 6. Portfolio Annotation — curate existing work with written reflection

docs.title: Step 2 — Set Up the Apps Script
docs.text:
In the Google Form: ⋮ menu → Script editor

Paste this code, then fill in the constants at the top:

// ============================================================
// SDA Studio Contract Generator
// Paste into Google Form Script Editor
// ============================================================

// FILL THESE IN before deploying:
const STUDIO_TITLE = "Power & Belief";           // current studio name
const ESSENTIAL_QUESTION = "How did early religions justify political authority?";
const WH_STANDARD = "WH.6_12.2";
const ERA_UNIT = "Era 1 — Emergence of Civilizations";
const ELA_STANDARD_1 = "9-10.R.9";
const ELA_STANDARD_2 = "9-10.W.3";
const WRITING_TYPE = "Explanatory (W.3)";
const DRIVE_FOLDER_ID = "YOUR_FOLDER_ID_HERE";  // ID from Drive folder URL
const NOTIFY_EMAIL = "YOUR_EMAIL_HERE";          // Reese's email for digest

// ============================================================

function onFormSubmit(e) {
  const responses = e.values;
  // e.values[0] = timestamp (no email column — email collection must be OFF)
  // If email collection is accidentally on, indices shift by 1 and email leaks into the doc.
  // Double-check Form Settings → Responses → Collect email addresses = OFF
  const timestamp   = responses[0];
  const studentId   = responses[1].trim().replace(/[^a-zA-Z0-9_-]/g, ''); // sanitize — IDs only
  const angle       = responses[2];
  const demoMode    = responses[3];
  const deliverable = responses[4];
  const researchQ   = responses[5];
  const targetScore = responses[6];

  // Hard stop: if studentId looks like an email address, something is misconfigured
  if (studentId.includes('@')) {
    MailApp.sendEmail(NOTIFY_EMAIL, '[SDA] FORM CONFIG ERROR',
      'A submission contained an email address in the Student ID field. Check that "Collect email addresses" is OFF in Form Settings.');
    return;
  }

  // Create the contract doc
  const doc = DocumentApp.create(`SDA Contract — ${studentId} — ${STUDIO_TITLE}`);
  const body = doc.getBody();

  body.appendParagraph("STUDIO CONTRACT")
    .setHeading(DocumentApp.ParagraphHeading.HEADING1);

  body.appendParagraph(`Student ID: ${studentId}`);
  body.appendParagraph(`Date: ${new Date(timestamp).toLocaleDateString()}`);
  body.appendParagraph(`Studio: ${STUDIO_TITLE}`);
  body.appendParagraph(`Essential Question: ${ESSENTIAL_QUESTION}`);

  body.appendParagraph("").appendHorizontalRule();

  body.appendParagraph("STANDARDS")
    .setHeading(DocumentApp.ParagraphHeading.HEADING2);
  body.appendParagraph(`Social Studies: ${WH_STANDARD} + ${ERA_UNIT}`);
  body.appendParagraph(`ELA Reading: ${ELA_STANDARD_1}`);
  body.appendParagraph(`ELA Writing: ${ELA_STANDARD_2} — ${WRITING_TYPE}`);
  body.appendParagraph(`Research: 9-10.IR.1–5 (active every studio)`);

  body.appendParagraph("").appendHorizontalRule();

  body.appendParagraph("MY STUDIO PLAN")
    .setHeading(DocumentApp.ParagraphHeading.HEADING2);
  body.appendParagraph(`Inquiry Angle: ${angle}`);
  body.appendParagraph(`Guiding Research Question: ${researchQ}`);
  body.appendParagraph(`Demonstration of Learning: ${demoMode}`);
  body.appendParagraph(`Deliverable: ${deliverable}`);
  body.appendParagraph(`Target Proficiency Score: ${targetScore}`);

  body.appendParagraph("").appendHorizontalRule();

  body.appendParagraph("PACE SHEET — 6 WEEKS")
    .setHeading(DocumentApp.ParagraphHeading.HEADING2);

  const paceData = [
    ["Week", "Phase", "My Goal This Week", "Done?"],
    ["1", "LAUNCH", "Submit this contract. Select sources. Write first draft of research question.", ""],
    ["2", "DIG",    "Annotate 3+ sources. Pull skinnies for gaps. Start evidence log.", ""],
    ["3", "BUILD",  "First draft of deliverable. Demonstration of learning work begins.", ""],
    ["4", "SHAPE",  "Peer feedback. Revision. Advisor check-in.", ""],
    ["5", "FINISH", "Complete demonstration of learning. Finalize deliverable.", ""],
    ["6", "PUBLISH","Exhibition. Reflection. Archive submission.", ""],
  ];

  const table = body.appendTable(paceData);
  table.getRow(0).editAsText().setBold(true);

  body.appendParagraph("").appendHorizontalRule();
  body.appendParagraph("Student signature (type your ID to confirm): _______________");
  body.appendParagraph("Advisor review: _______________");

  doc.saveAndClose();

  // Move to the designated Drive folder
  const file = DriveApp.getFileById(doc.getId());
  const folder = DriveApp.getFolderById(DRIVE_FOLDER_ID);
  folder.addFile(file);
  DriveApp.getRootFolder().removeFile(file);

  // Email digest to Reese
  MailApp.sendEmail({
    to: NOTIFY_EMAIL,
    subject: `[SDA] New contract: ${studentId} — ${STUDIO_TITLE}`,
    body: `Student ID: ${studentId}\nAngle: ${angle}\nDemo mode: ${demoMode}\nDeliverable: ${deliverable}\nResearch Q: ${researchQ}\nTarget: ${targetScore}\n\nContract saved to Drive.`
  });
}

docs.title: Updating for Each New Studio
docs.text:
At the start of each cycle, open Script Editor and update the constants at the top: - STUDIO_TITLE - ESSENTIAL_QUESTION - WH_STANDARD / ERA_UNIT - ELA_STANDARD_1 / ELA_STANDARD_2 - WRITING_TYPE - DRIVE_FOLDER_ID (if saving to a new subfolder)

Also update the inquiry angle options in the form itself (the multiple choice question).

docs.title: Skinny Library
docs.text:
South High School · Self-Directed Academy

Skinnies are short, focused reference sheets — one for each standard and era. Each one breaks down what the standard looks like at score levels 1.0 through 4.0, in plain language specific to that content.

Mr. Reese may assign skinnies as part of your studio plan. He uses them to make sure your studio demonstrates the full standard — not just the parts that come easily — and to confirm that crosswalk studios cover both the ELA and Social Studies sides completely. Think of your assigned skinnies as the specific expectations your studio is being held to.

You'll also pull skinnies on your own when filling out Part 5 (Proficiency Targets) of your Studio Contract, or whenever you hit a knowledge or skill gap mid-studio and need to know exactly what the standard is asking for.

docs.title: Formal Presentation — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: 9-10.C.1 — Give formal presentations that present ideas clearly, using appropriate language, eye contact, speaking rate, volume, and relevant multimedia support Applies across: All studios — any studio exhibition, research presentation, or public demonstration of learning Integrated with: C.6 (discussion skills), W.3 (explanatory), W.4 (argument), IR.1–5 (research) NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 15–30 min prep + presentation time Subject: ELA — Communication

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
A formal presentation is not a casual conversation — it is a prepared communication designed to inform or persuade a specific audience in a structured format. At the 2.0 level, you can deliver a basic presentation that covers the content. At the 3.0 level, you can calibrate every element — language, delivery, visuals, structure — to the specific audience and purpose.

  • Formal Register: Language appropriate to an academic or professional context — complete sentences, standard grammar, vocabulary suited to the topic, no filler words ("um," "like," "you know"). Formal register is different from your conversational voice, but it should still be natural.
  • Informal Register: Conversational language — contractions, slang, incomplete sentences — appropriate among friends but not in formal academic presentations. Using informal register in a formal presentation signals to the audience that you haven't prepared.
  • Eye Contact: Looking at your audience rather than reading from notes or a screen. Eye contact signals confidence, invites engagement, and shows you know your material well enough to not need to read it.
  • Speaking Rate: How fast or slowly you speak. Too fast: the audience can't follow; too slow: they disengage. Pausing before major points emphasizes importance.
  • Volume: Projecting enough that everyone can hear, even at the back. Volume also conveys confidence. Dropping volume at the end of sentences is a common habit that signals uncertainty.
  • Multimedia Support: Visual aids (slides, images, video clips, artifacts) that enhance the presentation — illustrating a point that's hard to say verbally, or showing evidence that supports your claim. Multimedia should add to the presentation, not just repeat what you're saying.
  • Claim: What you're arguing or explaining — the central idea you want the audience to walk away with. In a formal presentation, the claim should be stated explicitly early.
  • Organization: A presentation should have a clear structure: opening (state your topic and claim), body (develop with evidence and examples), closing (summarize and land the main takeaway). The audience should always know where they are.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Prepare an opening that states your topic and central claim. - [ ] Prepare a closing that restates the main takeaway. - [ ] Use notes (not a full script) — bullet points only; you should know your material. - [ ] Practice at least once aloud before presenting. - [ ] Choose any multimedia for what it adds, not just because it looks good.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who reads from their slides — covering the content but with their back to the audience, no eye contact, a flat delivery that doesn't make the audience feel the material matters.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
9-10.C.1 — Give formal presentations that present ideas clearly, using appropriate language, eye contact, speaking rate, volume, and relevant multimedia support

To meet this standard, your presentation must be clearly organized, audienced appropriately, delivered with confidence (no reading from a script), and supported by multimedia that enhances rather than replaces your words.

3.0 Example: A student presenting research on the Versailles Treaty's role in WWII opens by stating the central claim directly: "Today I'm arguing that the Versailles Treaty's terms did more to create World War II than Hitler's ideology — and I'll show you exactly how." The student speaks in full sentences without reading, makes eye contact with different parts of the room, and pauses after key statistics to let them land. The slides contain images (a political cartoon of Versailles from 1919, a graph of German unemployment 1930–1933), not text blocks — the student explains the visuals verbally. When reaching the counterclaim, the student explicitly signals: "Now, here's the strongest argument against what I'm saying — and why I don't think it holds." The presentation structure is clear: the audience always knows what claim is being developed and where in the argument they are.

The Difference: A 2.0 student covers the same information while reading from notes, with slides full of bullet points that duplicate what they're saying. A 3.0 student delivers with eye contact and pacing, uses multimedia that adds to rather than replaces the spoken content, and structures the presentation so the audience follows the argument.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, your presentation must demonstrate real-time audience awareness — adapting to your audience's questions, confusion, or engagement rather than just delivering a prepared script.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Q&A handling: A presentation that falls apart in the Q&A isn't fully mastered. Prepare to field at least three likely questions — and during the presentation, invite the hardest question you can anticipate: "The strongest counterargument here is probably X — let me address that directly." 2. Audience calibration: Present the same information to two different audiences (peers vs. teachers vs. younger students). Identify specifically what you changed for each — vocabulary, assumed knowledge, examples used, how much you explained vs. how much you asserted. 3. Use of evidence in real-time: Rather than just presenting your argument, demonstrate your research live — pull up a primary source and walk the audience through your reading of it. Show the process, not just the conclusion.

4.0 Example: A student presenting research on the Haitian Revolution ends their presentation by inviting the class to challenge the most contested claim — "Was the Haitian Revolution a universal model for liberation, or is it inseparable from its specific historical conditions?" The student is genuinely uncertain about one aspect and says so: "I haven't fully resolved whether the Haitian case proves Enlightenment ideals work for everyone or whether it's actually a refutation of them — and I'd like to hear what you think." This is not confusion — it is the mark of a student who has thought deeply enough about the topic to understand where the genuine uncertainty lies, and who is using the presentation as a real intellectual exchange, not just a performance.

docs.title: Multimedia That Adds Value
docs.text:
Multimedia should do something your words alone can't — or can't do as well: - Primary source images (political cartoons, photos, maps) — the audience can see the source you're analyzing rather than just hearing your description of it - Data visualizations (graphs, timelines) — showing a trend is clearer than describing it - Audio/video clips (30–60 seconds max) — hearing MLK's cadence rather than just reading his words - Artifacts or handouts — primary sources the audience can examine during discussion

Multimedia that does NOT add value: - Slides that repeat your words — you are the presentation, the slide is the support - Decorative images with no connection to the content - Long video clips that do the presenting for you - Slides with 10+ bullet points (the audience reads instead of listens)

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Not the primary demonstration mode for C.1 — but you can write a detailed preparation document: outline, slides plan, anticipated questions and responses. Submit alongside a brief reflection on how you would deliver the presentation. 2 Extended Writing Write a "Presentation Transcript + Commentary": the script of a presentation you would give (using formal register throughout), with marginal annotations explaining your delivery choices — where you'd pause, what multimedia you'd use, how you'd handle the Q&A. 3 Verbal Conversation Deliver a 3–5 minute formal presentation on a topic from your current studio. Must include: stated claim, organized argument with evidence, at least one multimedia element, and a Q&A portion (at least 2 questions). 4 Visual/Creative Create a complete presentation deck (8–12 slides) for a topic from your current studio. Slides should contain only visuals and key data — no text blocks. Write a one-sentence "speaker note" for each slide explaining what you would say. 5 Multimedia/Performance Record a 3–5 minute formal presentation to camera. Must use: formal register, no script-reading, at least one piece of multimedia support (shown on camera), and a clear organization (opening claim, body, closing takeaway). 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous presentation or speech you delivered. Write a 200-word reflection using C.1 criteria: Was the claim clear? Was the language appropriately formal? Was there eye contact / no script-reading? Did multimedia add value? What specifically would you change?

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Write an opening for a presentation (3–4 sentences). State: (1) your topic, (2) your central claim, (3) your structure ("I'll show X in three parts: ..."). Read it aloud twice — check for formal register.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Record a 2-minute practice run of one section of your presentation. Watch it back. Note: How many times did you look at your notes instead of the camera/audience? Were there filler words? Did you explain your multimedia or just point at it?
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Invite a peer to ask you the hardest question they can think of about your presentation topic. Give an answer on the spot — without notes. Then reflect: What did you know well enough to answer? Where did you realize you have a gap?

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• C.1 Scale — Full NDSBL scale for this standard
  • C.6 Discussion and Debate — Closely related: formal discussion shares delivery and register skills
  • W.4 Argument Writing — A strong presentation often argues a claim
  • W.3 Explanatory Writing — Some presentations explain rather than argue
  • IR.1–5 Research Bundle — Research supports what you'll present

How to use this skinny: 1. Start with the 2.0 vocabulary and processes. 2. Read the 3.0 example carefully. That's the target. 3. Choose your demonstration mode and begin gathering evidence. 4. Pull additional skinnies if you hit a gap. 5. Check your scale tasks as you complete them.

Skinny v1.0 — SDA Commons Wiki · June 2026 For the full anatomy of a skinny, see Skinny Anatomy

docs.title: Discussion and Debate — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: 9-10.C.6 — Participate in collaborative discussions and formal debates to explore ideas, analyze evidence, and reach conclusions, demonstrating active listening, idea building, and civil discourse Applies across: All studios — Socratic seminars, project critiques, panel discussions, debate formats Integrated with: C.1 (formal presentation), W.4 (argument), R.9 (argument analysis), W.6 (revision through peer feedback) NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: Ongoing — applies to all collaborative discussion formats Subject: ELA — Communication

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Collaborative discussion and debate are not the same as arguing or talking. They are structured academic activities with specific purposes: to explore ideas, analyze evidence together, and reach more nuanced conclusions than anyone could reach alone. At the 2.0 level, you can participate — contribute ideas, respond to others. At the 3.0 level, you can use discussion to actually build understanding collaboratively.

  • Collaborative Discussion: A structured conversation in which participants work toward shared understanding of a complex question, not just trading opinions. Everyone's goal is to advance the group's thinking, not just express their own.
  • Socratic Seminar: A specific discussion format: participants sit in a circle, the teacher facilitates minimally, and the group interrogates a text or question together. The standard: contribute by either asking a genuine question, building on someone's idea, or offering evidence-based pushback.
  • Formal Debate: A structured format in which two positions are argued. Each side presents a claim, evidence, and rebuttal. Unlike Socratic seminar, a debate has winners and losers (by the quality of argument, not by who is "right").
  • Active Listening: Genuinely attending to what someone else is saying — not just waiting to talk. Evidenced by: building on specific points they made ("You said X, and I think that connects to..."), asking follow-up questions about their specific claim, or acknowledging where their argument was stronger than expected.
  • Idea Building: The practice of extending, refining, or connecting someone else's contribution before adding your own: "Building on what [X] said..." or "I think that's right, and also..." or "I'd push back on that a little — if that's true, then doesn't that mean..."
  • Civil Discourse: Engaging with opposing views respectfully and substantively — critiquing the argument, not the person. Civil discourse is not the same as agreement or niceness: you can strongly disagree and still be civil. Incivility is attacking the person, dismissing without engagement, or talking over others.
  • Evidence-Based Contribution: Making a point that is supported by a specific text, source, or piece of reasoning — not just assertion. "I think the French Revolution was more about economics than ideology" is an assertion; "The 1789 cahiers show that two-thirds of peasant grievances were about taxes and debt rather than political rights" is an evidence-based contribution.
  • Rebuttal: A direct response to someone else's claim — explaining specifically why you disagree or what they've missed.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Prepare at least 2 evidence-based points before entering any Socratic seminar. - [ ] Listen for specific claims (not just general positions) so you can build on or rebut them specifically. - [ ] Distinguish between building (extending an idea) and pivoting (changing the subject to what you wanted to say). - [ ] Attribute ideas: "Building on what [X] said..." or "I'd push back on [X's] point about..." - [ ] Identify when you've changed your mind during discussion — that's the goal.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who participates in seminar by contributing ideas and responding generally to others — but whose contributions don't specifically reference what others have said, and whose positions don't change over the course of the discussion regardless of what evidence emerges.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
9-10.C.6 — Participate in collaborative discussions and formal debates demonstrating active listening, idea building, and civil discourse

To meet this standard, your participation must show that the discussion is actually changing your thinking — or that you are changing others' thinking by building on their specific contributions rather than just rotating through prepared points.

3.0 Example: In a Socratic seminar on whether the Haitian Revolution was a success or failure, a student enters with the prepared position that it was a success. Midway through, another student argues: "The revolution ended the slave trade but left Haiti economically isolated — France imposed crippling reparations that the Haitian government was paying until 1947. So the political liberation was real but it was immediately weaponized economically." The 3.0 student: (1) Actively listens — doesn't respond until the full argument is made; (2) Builds on it specifically: "That's a point I hadn't considered — but I'd push back on the framing. If success requires economic freedom, then does any post-colonial state qualify as a 'success'? Because France extracted wealth from its colonies for decades; the Haitian reparations are more brutal but not categorically different. The question is whether our standard of 'success' is fair or whether we're holding Haiti to a standard we'd never apply to the U.S." (3) The student's final position is more qualified than their starting position — not because they caved to social pressure, but because the argument improved their thinking.

The Difference: A 2.0 student contributes 3 prepared points and responds to others generally ("I agree with what they said"). A 3.0 student builds on specific claims, traces their own thinking aloud as it develops, and acknowledges when an opposing argument has genuinely changed or complicated their position.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must advance the seminar itself — asking the question that opens a new dimension, identifying where the group is talking past each other, or synthesizing multiple contributions into a clearer framing of the problem.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Metacommentary: During or after a discussion, comment on the discussion itself: "I notice we've been debating whether the revolution was a success without agreeing on what 'success' means. Can we define terms first?" This kind of move — identifying a shared confusion and naming it — is a 4.0 contribution. 2. Steel-manning the opposition in debate: In a formal debate, present the strongest possible version of the opposing argument before presenting your own case. "The strongest argument against my position is X. Here's why I still disagree." This is both civil discourse and high-level argument strategy. 3. Synthesis: Toward the end of a seminar, offer a synthesis of the most important competing claims: "I think we've established X, and most of us agree on Y, but the real disagreement is between [student A's position] and [student B's position] about Z. Can we focus there?" This moves the group from scattered discussion to targeted argument.

4.0 Example: In a seminar on Wollstonecraft, a student notices that two classmates are arguing past each other: one says Wollstonecraft was a feminist ahead of her time; the other says she reinforced class biases. The 4.0 student offers: "I think you're both right and both missing something. Wollstonecraft was ahead of her time on gender within her class — she was extending Enlightenment principles to women. But she never applied the same principles to class or race. So the question isn't whether she was a feminist; it's whether we can call a feminism that only includes educated middle-class white women 'ahead of its time.' That's the actual disagreement here." This contribution doesn't just build on the existing discussion — it restructures the question so the group can actually address the underlying disagreement.

docs.title: Formats You'll Encounter in SDA
docs.text:
Socratic Seminar: Open discussion; all students responsible for advancing the conversation; use evidence from texts; teacher doesn't evaluate you for having the right answer — only for the quality of your contribution.

Panel Discussion: Small group presents multiple perspectives on a contested question; rest of the class can ask questions; each panelist represents a defensible position (not necessarily their own).

Structured Academic Controversy (SAC): In pairs, each person argues a position they were assigned — not necessarily their own view. Then sides switch. Then the pair works together to find a nuanced position. Forces genuine engagement with the opposing argument.

Formal Debate: Two sides; each side has an opening statement, cross-examination, and closing. Judged on the quality of argument, not who is right.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Write a 200-word "Discussion Reflection" after any Socratic seminar: (1) What was the most compelling contribution someone else made? (2) How did it change or complicate your thinking? (3) What was the most important thing you contributed? (4) Where did the group get stuck, and why? 2 Extended Writing Write a "Structured Academic Controversy" document: present the strongest case for one position on a contested historical question, then the strongest case for the opposing position, then your own nuanced view after considering both. Show that you can steelman both sides. 3 Verbal Conversation Participate in a 15-minute Socratic seminar on a contested question from your current studio. Demonstrate: at least 3 contributions that build specifically on other students' ideas; at least one rebuttal with evidence; at least one moment where you acknowledge the opposing argument has strengthened or complicated your position. 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Discussion Map" for a seminar: draw the flow of ideas (who said what, how it connected to what came before, where the key turning points were). Annotate the moments where the group's understanding deepened or where someone's position changed. 5 Multimedia/Performance Record a 3–4 minute "Structured Debate" with a partner on a contested historical question: each side gives an opening statement (1 min), rebuttal (30 sec), and closing (30 sec). Evaluate: Was the rebuttal responsive to the specific argument made, or to a straw man? 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a project that involved group discussion. Write a 200-word reflection: Did your contributions build on specific ideas from others, or were they independent of what others said? Did your position change during the discussion? If not — why not? What does that tell you?

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Before a seminar, prepare three evidence-based contributions — specific claims with specific sources. Write them out: "I'll argue [X] based on [specific evidence from Y source]."
  • [ ] 3.0 task: After a seminar, write three "building" statements you made or could have made: "Building on what [X] said about [specific claim], I'd add/push back by..." Focus on specific claims, not general positions.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: After a seminar, identify one place where the group was talking past each other. Write the metacommentary you could have offered: "I think the actual disagreement here is between [position A] and [position B] about [specific question]. Let me try to name it..."

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• C.6 Scale — Full NDSBL scale for this standard
  • C.1 Formal Presentation — Formal presentations share delivery and register skills
  • W.4 Argument Writing — Discussion argument uses the same claim/evidence/reasoning structure as written argument
  • R.9 Informational/Argumentative — Analyzing others' arguments in text prepares you to analyze them in discussion
  • W.6 Writing Process — Peer feedback in discussion is a form of the writing process
  • Wollstonecraft

How to use this skinny: 1. Start with the 2.0 vocabulary and processes. 2. Read the 3.0 example carefully. That's the target. 3. Choose your demonstration mode and begin gathering evidence. 4. Pull additional skinnies if you hit a gap. 5. Check your scale tasks as you complete them.
