docs.title: Research Bundle — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: 9-10.IR.1–5 — Conduct short and extended research projects by developing a focused question; gathering, synthesizing, and evaluating information from multiple sources; citing sources correctly; and presenting findings Applies across: All studios — every project involving primary or secondary sources Integrated with: W.3 (explanatory), W.4 (argument), R.9 (argument/source analysis), IR standards (1–5 individually scaffolded below) NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: Varies by project scope (short research: 1–2 hours; extended research: multiple sessions) Subject: ELA — Independent Research

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text: Research is not "looking things up." Research is a process of asking a question, finding relevant and reliable information, evaluating that information critically, synthesizing it into a coherent understanding, and communicating the findings clearly. The IR.1–5 standards are bundled because they describe the five stages of the research process — each depends on the others.

docs.title: IR.1 — Develop a Research Question
docs.text:
• Research Question: A specific, focused, answerable question that drives a research project. A good research question is: (1) focused enough to actually answer with available evidence, (2) open enough that the answer isn't already obvious, (3) significant — the answer matters.
  • Too broad: "What caused WWI?" (a dissertation could barely answer this)
  • Too narrow: "What was the date of the Archduke's assassination?" (a single Google search answers this; it's a lookup, not research)
  • Researchable: "Was the alliance system or nationalist movements more responsible for WWI escalating from a regional conflict to a world war?"
  • Subtopics: Breaking a big research question into smaller component questions you can actually investigate.

docs.title: IR.2 — Gather Information from Multiple Sources
docs.text:
• Primary Source: A first-hand account or direct evidence from the historical period — original documents, letters, speeches, photographs, data. What happened vs. what someone says happened. (MLK's Letter is a primary source; a historian's analysis of the Letter is secondary.)
  • Secondary Source: A source that analyzes, interprets, or comments on primary sources or events. Most history textbooks, academic articles, and encyclopedia entries are secondary sources.
  • Multiple Sources: Research requires triangulation — finding information from at least 2–3 different sources so you can identify agreements and contradictions.

docs.title: IR.3 — Synthesize Information
docs.text:
• Synthesis: Combining information from multiple sources into a coherent, original understanding — not summarizing each source separately, but weaving them together. The result of synthesis is a claim supported by evidence from multiple sources, not a summary of what each source says.
  • Paraphrase: Restating someone else's idea in your own words, completely — not just swapping out a few words. Paraphrase + citation is how you use sources without over-quoting.

docs.title: IR.4 — Evaluate Sources for Credibility
docs.text:
• Credibility: Whether a source can be trusted — based on: the author's expertise, the publication's standards, the evidence provided, and whether the claims can be verified elsewhere.
  • Relevance: Whether the source actually addresses your research question — not just vaguely related to the topic.
  • Bias: A systematic tendency to favor certain perspectives or conclusions. Every source has a perspective — the question is whether the bias distorts the information or is acknowledged and accounted for.
  • SIFT Framework: A quick credibility check:
  • Stop — don't share/use before evaluating
  • Investigate the source — who wrote this, why, for whom?
  • Find better coverage — is there a more authoritative source?
  • Trace claims — follow the evidence; does it actually say what the source claims?
  • Lateral Reading: Rather than reading a source deeply to evaluate it, quickly check what other sources say about this source. What do others say about this publication, organization, or author?

docs.title: IR.5 — Cite Sources Correctly
docs.text:
• Citation: Giving credit to the source of information you used — required for direct quotes, paraphrases, and specific data or claims.
  • MLA Format: Modern Language Association format — the citation standard in ELA. A basic MLA in-text citation: (Author's Last Name, Page or Paragraph #) or (Author's Last Name). A Works Cited entry includes: Author. "Title." Publication, Year. URL or DOI if digital.
  • Why Cite? Three reasons: (1) Academic honesty — claiming others' ideas as your own is plagiarism; (2) Credibility — showing you have evidence makes your claims stronger; (3) Traceability — your reader can follow your evidence to verify and extend it.
  • Plagiarism: Using someone else's words or ideas without attribution — whether intentional or not. Accidental plagiarism is still plagiarism.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Narrow a broad topic into a specific, focused research question. - [ ] Find at least two sources on your question — one primary, one secondary. - [ ] Read each source and identify: the author's perspective and any potential bias. - [ ] Write a paragraph that synthesizes information from both sources — don't summarize each separately. - [ ] Cite both sources in MLA format (in-text citation + Works Cited entry).

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who finds relevant sources and summarizes them accurately — but whose "synthesis" is a series of paragraphs about each source separately, rather than a unified explanation that weaves the sources together around a central claim.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
9-10.IR.1–5 — Conduct research by developing a focused question; gathering, evaluating, and synthesizing information; citing correctly; and presenting findings

To meet this standard at 3.0, your research must demonstrate all five stages of the process working together: a focused question that drives the research; sources gathered and evaluated for credibility and bias; information synthesized (not just summarized) into a clear claim; and findings presented with correct citations.

3.0 Example: A student researching whether the Haitian Revolution was ideologically independent or derived from European Enlightenment ideas develops the question: "Did the Haitian revolutionary leadership apply Enlightenment principles borrowed from Europe, or did they develop an independent ideological framework that the Enlightenment failed to supply?" Sources gathered: (1) Primary: excerpts from the Haitian Declaration of Independence (1804) — language, word choice, what was borrowed from French Revolution vs. what was different; (2) Secondary: a historian's analysis of Toussaint Louverture's speeches vs. the Declaration des droits de l'homme; (3) Secondary: a historian arguing for the Haitian Revolution's ideological originality. The student evaluates each for credibility (peer-reviewed vs. popular press), notes the historians' different interpretations, and synthesizes: "The Haitian revolutionary leaders borrowed the Enlightenment's language of rights but extended it to enslaved people in a way the Enlightenment never did — which made the Haitian Declaration of Independence both derivative and genuinely original." Citations are in MLA format throughout.

The Difference: A 2.0 student summarizes three sources on the Haitian Revolution with in-text citations. A 3.0 student uses a focused research question to drive source selection, evaluates sources for credibility and bias, synthesizes into an original claim, and presents with correct citations.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, your research must account for the limits of your sources — acknowledging where the evidence is incomplete, contested, or absent — and adjust your claims accordingly.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Source gap analysis: For any research project, explicitly identify: What would I need to find to be more confident in my claim? What kind of source would answer that? Did you look for that source and not find it, or did you not look? This kind of reflection on your own research is part of the research process. 2. Conflicting sources: Find two credible sources that reach different conclusions about the same question. Analyze: Why do they disagree? Is it different evidence, different framing, different values? Which do you find more convincing, and why? This is how historians actually work. 3. Primary source analysis as research: Rather than using a primary source just as evidence for a claim, make the primary source itself the subject of analysis. What does this source tell us about who wrote it, for whom, and under what conditions? What doesn't it tell us?

4.0 Example: A student researching Nazis' use of propaganda analyzes both Goebbels' own writing on propaganda techniques (primary source) and a historian's analysis of how ordinary Germans responded to Nazi propaganda (secondary source). The student identifies a gap: the historian's evidence is based on post-war survivor testimony, which is subject to hindsight bias — people who survived and were willing to speak may have been different from the population being studied. The student acknowledges this limit: "My claim that ordinary Germans were active participants rather than passive recipients of propaganda is supported by the evidence available, but that evidence is incomplete — we don't have access to what the majority who didn't survive or testify believed." This is not hedging; it is intellectual honesty about the limits of historical knowledge.

docs.title: Reading Library Research Opportunities
docs.text: These texts model research-worthy questions and provide primary source material: - Hammurabi's Code — Primary source; compare legal principles to modern law; research question: "Does Hammurabi's Code represent justice or control?" - MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — Primary source; rich for rhetorical analysis and historical context research - Common Sense — Paine — Primary source; research question: "How did Paine's argument change public opinion on independence, and can we measure that?" - Declaration of the Rights of Man — Primary source; compare to Haitian Declaration; research question: "What did the French Revolution exclude that the Haitian Revolution did not?" - Wollstonecraft — Vindication — Primary source; research question: "What did Wollstonecraft's argument assume that she never examined?" - Night — Wiesel — Primary source memoir; research question: "How did Wiesel's account of the Holocaust differ from historical records, and what does that difference tell us?"

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Complete a short research project: (1) Write a focused research question, (2) gather 2–3 sources and evaluate each for credibility/bias in 2–3 sentences, (3) write a synthesis paragraph that weaves information from all sources around your central claim, (4) include a Works Cited in MLA format. 2 Extended Writing Complete an extended research piece (400–600 words) on a question from your studio. Must include: a focused research question stated in the intro, evidence from at least 3 sources synthesized (not summarized), in-text citations throughout, Works Cited page, and a 100-word reflection on your source evaluation process. 3 Verbal Conversation Present your research process in a 3-minute "Research Talk": (1) What was your focused question? (2) How did you find and evaluate your sources? (3) What did each source contribute? (4) What is your synthesized claim? (5) What would you need to find to be more confident? 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Research Map": a visual showing your research question at the center, your sources branching out (labeled by type and credibility), your synthesis claim at the end, and your Works Cited list. Annotate each source with: What it contributed. Why you trust it. Any bias you identified. 5 Multimedia/Performance Record a 3–4 minute "Source Evaluation" video: show a source on screen, walk through the SIFT framework, explain why you trust or question it, and explain how it supports (or complicates) your research claim. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project that involved research. Write a 200-word reflection: Was your research question focused enough to drive the project? Did you evaluate sources for credibility and bias? Did you synthesize information or summarize it separately? What would you do differently?

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Narrow a broad topic ("The Holocaust") into a focused research question (not "What caused the Holocaust?" but "How did the Nazi bureaucracy use legal processes to normalize genocide before the Final Solution?"). Test it: Is it focused? Is it answerable? Is it significant?
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a synthesis paragraph using at least two sources. Check: Does the paragraph argue a claim, or does it summarize each source separately? Are all sources cited? Is the synthesis your original claim supported by the sources, or just a summary?
  • [ ] 4.0 task: For one source you used, write 100 words identifying: What is its perspective? What might it miss? What would I need to find to verify its key claim? Does any part of what it says rely on assumptions I should examine?

docs.title: MLA Quick Reference
docs.text:
In-text citation: (Author Last Name page/paragraph #) or (Author Last Name) - Quote: MLK writes, "Injustice anywhere is a threat to justice everywhere" (King 1). - Paraphrase: King argues that local injustice has national implications (King 1).

Works Cited entry — Book: Last, First. Title of Book. Publisher, Year.

Works Cited entry — Website/Online Source: Last, First. "Title of Page." Name of Site, Date. URL.

Works Cited entry — Reading Library text: Wiesel, Elie. Night. Hill and Wang, 1960. SDA Commons Wiki Reading Library, 2026.

docs.text:

  • W.4 Argument Writing — Research provides the evidence for argument
  • R.9 Informational/Argumentative — Source evaluation (IR.4) closely relates to R.9 bias analysis
  • ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk — Research crosswalk examples
  • Haitian Declaration

How to use this skinny: 1. Start with the 2.0 vocabulary and processes. 2. Read the 3.0 example carefully. That's the target. 3. Choose your demonstration mode and begin gathering evidence. 4. Pull additional skinnies if you hit a gap. 5. Check your scale tasks as you complete them.

docs.title: Language Usage — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: 9-10.L.1 — Demonstrate command of the conventions of standard English grammar and usage when writing or speaking; use language choices appropriate to task, purpose, and audience Applies across: All studios — the baseline standard active in every piece of writing and every presentation Integrated with: W.1 (clarity), W.3 (explanatory), W.4 (argument), C.1 (formal presentation) NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: Applied throughout all writing — not a standalone task Subject: ELA — Language

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text: Language usage is not just about following grammar rules — it is about controlling the impression your writing makes. Errors signal carelessness; appropriate register signals that you know your audience and purpose. At the 2.0 level, you can apply standard conventions correctly. At the 3.0 level, you use language actively as a tool — choosing constructions, register, and phrasing for their effect, not just their correctness.

docs.title: Grammar and Usage Conventions
docs.text:
• Subject-Verb Agreement: The subject and verb must agree in number. The evidence [suggests], not suggest. The causes [are], not is.
  • Pronoun-Antecedent Agreement: A pronoun must agree with the noun it replaces. Each student must complete [their] work (singular "their" is acceptable in standard usage for gender-neutral reference). Watch for: "The government changed [its] policy" (not "their" — government is singular).
  • Verb Tense Consistency: Don't shift tenses without a reason. Historical present is acceptable for literary analysis ("MLK [argues] in paragraph 3...") but must be used consistently throughout a piece.
  • Comma Splice: Joining two independent clauses with only a comma is an error. Fix with: a period, a semicolon, or a coordinating conjunction (for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so).
  • Splice: "The treaty failed, no one enforced it."
  • Fixed: "The treaty failed; no one enforced it." OR "The treaty failed because no one enforced it."
  • Fragment: An incomplete sentence — missing a subject, a verb, or both. Sometimes used intentionally for effect; usually an error when unintentional.
  • Fragment: "A policy that many argued was too harsh and that historians now consider a turning point."
  • Fixed: "Many argued the policy was too harsh — a turning point historians still debate."
  • Modifier Placement: Modifying phrases should be placed near what they modify. Dangling modifier: "Having read the treaty, its flaws were obvious." (Who read it? The flaws?) Fixed: "Having read the treaty, I found its flaws obvious."
  • Parallel Structure: Items in a list or comparison should use the same grammatical form. Broken: "The causes were economic instability, rising nationalism, and governments had miscalculated." Fixed: "The causes were economic instability, rising nationalism, and government miscalculation."

docs.title: Register and Audience
docs.text:
• Register: The level of formality in your language. Academic writing uses formal register; casual conversation uses informal. The wrong register for the context is not just stylistic — it undermines credibility.
  • Informal: "Basically, the Weimar Republic was a mess and it basically fell apart pretty fast."
  • Formal: "The Weimar Republic's structural weaknesses — proportional representation, Article 48's emergency powers, and the crushing economic impact of reparations — made it vulnerable to collapse under sustained pressure."
  • Appropriate Vocabulary: Using words precisely and appropriately for your audience. In academic writing: define technical terms the first time you use them; use field-specific vocabulary when it's more precise, not just to sound sophisticated.
  • Sentence Variety: Varying sentence length and structure — long complex sentences for developed ideas, short sentences for emphasis. Monotonous sentence structure (all sentences the same length and structure) is harder to read.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Read your final draft aloud — you will hear errors you miss reading silently. - [ ] Check every sentence for subject-verb agreement, especially in long sentences where the subject and verb are far apart. - [ ] Check for comma splices wherever you have two independent clauses. - [ ] Before editing, identify your audience and register — then check whether your language is appropriate. - [ ] Use the grammar/spell-checker as a first pass, not a final pass — it misses many errors and flags correct constructions.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student whose writing demonstrates control of basic grammar conventions in short passages, but whose longer pieces show inconsistent tense, comma splices, and occasional register shifts — and who hasn't yet learned to read their own writing with the reader's eye rather than the writer's.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
9-10.L.1 — Command of standard English grammar and usage; language choices appropriate to task, purpose, and audience

To meet this standard, your writing must demonstrate not just rule-following but intentional language choice — using grammar as a tool, varying register deliberately, and showing that you know when a rule applies and when a stylistic choice is appropriate.

3.0 Example: A student writing a formal argument essay about the Versailles Treaty uses: - Consistent historical present for analysis ("the treaty [imposes] reparations that Germany [is] structurally unable to pay") and simple past for historical narrative ("Clemenceau [insisted] on punitive terms"); - Formal register throughout, with vocabulary precision: "punitive" rather than "mean," "reparations" rather than "payment," "structural instability" rather than "problems"; - Sentence variety: "The Weimar Republic was structurally vulnerable. Its proportional representation system fragmented power among competing parties, none of which had the majority needed to govern effectively. This fragmentation, combined with Article 48's emergency powers provision, made authoritarian takeover not just possible but likely." (Short declarative → long complex explanation → synthesis sentence) - When the student's argument shifts from explanation to evaluation, the register adjusts slightly — less neutral, more direct — to signal the shift in purpose.

The Difference: A 2.0 student produces grammatically correct writing with consistent register. A 3.0 student uses grammar and register as tools — varying sentence structure for effect, choosing vocabulary precisely, and matching language to the specific purpose and audience.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must demonstrate conscious stylistic choice — not just using grammar correctly, but using language to create specific effects, and being able to explain why specific choices serve your purpose.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Style analysis → style application: Pick a paragraph from any reading library text and analyze the grammar and style choices: What kinds of sentences does the author use? What is the register? What is the effect of specific word choices? Then write a paragraph of your own in a similar style on a different topic — demonstrating that you can adopt and adapt another writer's techniques. 2. Register-switching exercise: Take a paragraph from your most recent essay and rewrite it for a completely different audience (a younger student, a policymaker, a skeptic who disagrees with your argument). What changes? What stays the same? This reveals which choices are about register and which are about content. 3. Deliberate fragment or short sentence: Find a place in your writing where a grammatically unconventional choice (a fragment, a one-word sentence, a very long complex sentence) would serve the rhetorical purpose better than the conventional choice. Use it deliberately and explain why.

4.0 Example: A student revising an essay on the Holocaust notices that their writing becomes passive and distanced when describing the bureaucratic processes: "The victims were transported to the camps. Records were maintained. Selections were performed." The student recognizes that passive voice here is actually a problem — it mirrors the language of the perpetrators who used passive constructions to depersonalize their actions. The student revises to active voice with named subjects: "SS guards separated arriving prisoners into two groups. Administrators recorded names and numbers. Officers decided who would work and who would die immediately." The revision is not just grammatically more active — it is ethically more honest. The student can explain the choice.

docs.title: Language Patterns to Know
docs.text:
Passive vs. Active Voice: - Passive: "The war was started by the Austro-Hungarian government's ultimatum." (what happened to whom) - Active: "The Austro-Hungarian government started the war with an ultimatum designed to be rejected." (who did what) Active voice is usually clearer and more direct. Passive voice is appropriate when the actor is unknown, unimportant, or when the thing acted upon is the important element ("Millions were killed by the Nazi regime" — the victims are the emphasis, not the perpetrators).

Subordination and Coordination: - Coordination (equal ideas): "The revolution succeeded, and its legacy shaped future liberation movements." - Subordination (one idea depends on another): "Although the revolution succeeded politically, its economic legacy was shaped by the reparations France imposed." Subordination shows relationships between ideas; overusing coordination flattens everything to equal importance.

Word Precision: - Affect (verb: to influence) vs. Effect (noun: a result): "The famine [affected] migration patterns"; "The [effect] of the famine was mass emigration." - Then (sequence) vs. Than (comparison): "First the siege, [then] the surrender." "The French army was larger [than] expected." - Which (non-restrictive, adds information) vs. That (restrictive, identifies): "The treaty[, which] was signed in 1919[,] contained 440 articles." (removable) vs. "The treaty [that] ended WWI contained 440 articles." (identifies which treaty)

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Submit a final draft of any piece of writing alongside a "Grammar Annotation": mark every sentence where you made a deliberate grammatical or stylistic choice (not just following default rules), and write a brief note explaining the choice and its intended effect. 2 Extended Writing Write a 300-word analysis of the language choices in any reading library text. Focus on: register, sentence variety, specific word choices and their connotations, one grammatical construction used for effect. Then write a paragraph of your own in a similar style on a different topic. 3 Verbal Conversation Read a 2-minute passage from any reading library text aloud, then analyze: What is the register? What kind of sentence structures does the author use? What specific word choices create the strongest effect? How do these choices serve the purpose? 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Language Toolkit" for a writing project: a visual document showing your vocabulary choices (alternatives you considered and why you chose what you did), your sentence structure decisions (variety map), and your register check (formal? does it match the audience?). 5 Multimedia/Performance Record a 2-minute "Grammar Commentary" for a piece of your own writing: read a paragraph aloud, then explain three specific language choices and why you made them — including at least one sentence structure choice and one vocabulary choice. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select any piece of writing from your portfolio. Write a 200-word reflection: What register did you use, and was it appropriate? Where did you make deliberate sentence structure choices? What word choices would you revise, and what would you replace them with? Why?

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Take any paragraph from your writing and do a grammar check: mark every verb and check tense consistency; mark every place you join two independent clauses and check for comma splices; identify the register (formal or informal).
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Rewrite one paragraph from your writing with deliberate sentence variety: (1) a short sentence for emphasis, (2) a long complex sentence with subordination, (3) a sentence that begins with a subordinating clause. Compare your revised version to the original — what effect does the variety create?
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Choose one word in your writing that you used for precision (a technical term or a word with specific connotations). Write 50 words explaining: What alternatives did you consider? Why did you choose this word? What would be different if you had used a more common synonym?

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• L.1 Scale — Full NDSBL scale for this standard
  • W.4 Argument Writing — Argument writing benefits most from precise language and strong register
  • C.1 Formal Presentation — Register and usage apply equally to spoken formal communication
  • R.7 Style and Word Choice — Analyzing others' style choices develops your own

How to use this skinny: 1. Start with the 2.0 vocabulary and processes. 2. Read the 3.0 example carefully. That's the target. 3. Choose your demonstration mode and begin gathering evidence. 4. Pull additional skinnies if you hit a gap. 5. Check your scale tasks as you complete them.

docs.title: Reading Complex Texts — Comprehension and Inference — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: 9-10.R.2 — Comprehend a variety of texts with multiple levels of complexity while developing inferences and providing relevant textual evidence and reasoning Applies across: All studios — active whenever reading a primary source or literary text Integrated with: WH.6_12.1–6 (primary/secondary source analysis), W.3 (explanatory), W.4 (argument) NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 45–60 minutes Subject: ELA — Reading Comprehension

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Reading at the 9-10 level means more than understanding what a text says on the surface. You must understand what the text implies — the meaning that's present but not stated directly.

  • Comprehension: The ability to understand what a text says — the literal content, the events, the facts.
  • Inference: A conclusion you draw from evidence in the text, not stated directly. Example: If a character in a story never speaks up in conversations, you might infer they are anxious or afraid — the text doesn't say so, but the evidence supports the conclusion.
  • Textual Evidence: A direct quote or specific paraphrase from the text that supports your inference. Without textual evidence, an inference is just a guess.
  • Relevant Evidence: Evidence that actually connects to the inference you're making — not just any quote from the text.
  • Levels of Complexity: Texts can be complex in different ways: vocabulary complexity (unusual or technical words), syntactic complexity (long, difficult sentences), conceptual complexity (ideas that require background knowledge to fully understand), and ambiguity (the text can mean more than one thing).
  • Context Clues: Information in the surrounding text that helps you figure out the meaning of an unfamiliar word or phrase without looking it up.
  • Text Structure: How a text is organized — chronological, cause-and-effect, compare-contrast, problem-solution, argument. Identifying structure helps comprehension.
  • Paraphrase: Restating a text's meaning in your own words — not quoting, not summarizing, but capturing the meaning in new language. A strong paraphrase shows you understood.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Identify what a difficult passage says literally before drawing any inferences. - [ ] Distinguish between what the text states directly and what you are inferring. - [ ] Find and quote the specific textual evidence that supports your inference. - [ ] Explain the reasoning connecting your evidence to your inference. - [ ] Identify the type of complexity in a difficult passage (vocabulary, syntax, concept, ambiguity).

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can correctly summarize what Elie Wiesel describes in Chapter 3 of Night — the arrival at Birkenau — without yet drawing an inference about what the scene reveals about how the Nazi system used dehumanization as a deliberate method, supported by specific textual evidence.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
9-10.R.2 — Comprehend a variety of texts with multiple levels of complexity while developing inferences and providing relevant textual evidence and reasoning

To meet this standard, you must demonstrate that you understood the text AND drew a valid inference from it, backed by relevant evidence and explained reasoning. The three-part structure — inference + evidence + reasoning — is the core.

3.0 Example: Reading MLK's "Letter from Birmingham Jail," a student achieves a 3.0 by going beyond summary (King wrote a letter from jail defending civil disobedience) to make an inference: King's use of biblical and historical allusions strategically repositions him from "troublemaker" to "tradition-bearer." Evidence: "Just as the Apostle Paul left his village of Tarsus...so am I compelled to carry the gospel of freedom beyond my own home town." Reasoning: By comparing himself to Paul — a figure white clergy revere — King makes it harder for them to dismiss him as an outsider agitator. He's not breaking with tradition; he's fulfilling it. This is an inference because the text never explicitly states King's rhetorical strategy — but the evidence and reasoning make it the most logical conclusion.

The Difference: A 2.0 student summarizes what King said in the letter. A 3.0 student infers what King was doing rhetorically — and backs it with specific evidence and explained reasoning.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate competing inferences — explaining why yours is better supported than an alternative, or drawing a synthesis inference that connects evidence across a whole text rather than a single passage.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Competing inferences: Someone reading 1984 might infer that Orwell's message is "totalitarianism is inevitable" — while another reader might infer "totalitarianism can be resisted but wasn't." Find evidence that could support each inference and evaluate which is better supported by the full text. 2. Cross-text inference: Both Night and the "Letter from Birmingham Jail" involve people facing unjust systems. What does comparing them allow you to infer about what individuals can do against systemic evil — and what they cannot? Support this synthesis inference with evidence from both texts. 3. Ambiguity: Some texts are deliberately ambiguous. Identify a passage in one of the reading library texts where two different inferences are equally well-supported by the evidence. Explain what this ambiguity tells you about the author's purpose.

4.0 Example: A student reading Wollstonecraft's Vindication argues that the deepest inference from the text is that Wollstonecraft does not argue for equality with men — she argues that women who act the way men expect them to act (vain, emotional, dependent) have been made that way by their education and circumstances. This is a different argument than "women are equal" — it's "women are the products of an unjust system, and the system must change." The student supports this with multiple passages across the text, shows why the simpler "she argued for equality" inference is incomplete, and explains why the fuller inference changes how you read the whole text.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
CrashCourse Literature episodes — coming soon in reading library

Practice approach until CrashCourse is available: Use the "inference triangle" for any difficult text: 1. Read the passage closely 2. Write: "I notice [specific evidence]..." 3. Write: "This makes me think [inference]..." 4. Write: "Because [reasoning connecting evidence to inference]..."

docs.title: Reading Library Texts for Practice
docs.text:
These texts have multiple levels of complexity — all are good for R.2 practice:

  • Night — Elie Wiesel — Syntactic and conceptual complexity; indirect communication of trauma; inference about what is NOT said
  • 1984 — George Orwell — Conceptual complexity; requires inference about political allegory and what Orwell implies about his own era
  • MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — Multiple layers of meaning; King's stated arguments AND implied rhetorical strategy
  • Common Sense — Thomas Paine — Rhetorical complexity; Paine's audience assumptions embedded in his argument
  • Wollstonecraft — Vindication — Conceptual complexity; feminist argument operating within Enlightenment framework it also challenges

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose a 2–3 paragraph passage from any reading library text. Write: (1) a summary of what it literally says, (2) an inference about what it implies, (3) at least two pieces of textual evidence with quotation, (4) your reasoning connecting evidence to inference. 2 Extended Writing Write a 300–400 word inference-based analysis of one text from the reading library. Identify the inference you're making (NOT just summary), provide multiple pieces of evidence, and explain your reasoning. Address the most obvious alternative inference and explain why yours is better supported. 3 Verbal Conversation Record yourself doing a close read of a passage. Say aloud: what you notice, what inference you're drawing, what evidence supports it, and what your reasoning is. Show the difference between summary and inference. 4 Visual/Creative Create an "Inference Map" for one text. In the center: your inference about the text's deeper meaning. Branching out: the evidence (specific quotes). Connecting arrows annotated with: the reasoning. On the side: the alternative inference you considered and why you rejected it. 5 Multimedia/Performance Record a 2-minute "Close Read" video. Open to a specific passage, read it aloud, then explain: (1) what it says literally, (2) your inference, (3) specific evidence, (4) your reasoning. Show the thinking process, not just the conclusion. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project where you read a primary source or literary text. Write a 150-word reflection: What inferences did you draw? What evidence did you use? If you were to revise, what inference would you make now that you didn't make then?

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Choose a passage from any reading library text. Write a 2–3 sentence summary of what it says literally. Identify one word or phrase you needed to use context clues to understand.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write an inference-evidence-reasoning response: "I infer [X] because [evidence] and the reasoning connecting them is [Y]." Make sure the inference is not stated directly in the text.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write 150 words identifying a competing inference to the one you made, explaining what evidence could support it, and defending why your inference is better supported.

  • R.7 Style and Word Choice — Related: style analysis builds on inference about author's purpose
  • R.9 Informational/Argumentative — Related: argument analysis requires inferring author's strategy

  • MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — Practice text

docs.title: Main Idea, Claim, and Theme — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: 9-10.R.4 — Determine main idea(s), claim(s), or theme(s) as they develop over the course of the text and support with textual evidence Applies across: All studios — active whenever reading a full text or extended primary source Integrated with: W.3 (explanatory), W.4 (argument), R.2 (comprehension/inference) NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 45–60 minutes Subject: ELA — Reading

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Main idea, claim, and theme are three related but different concepts. Getting the distinction right is essential for reading accurately — the type of text determines which term applies.

  • Main Idea: The central point of a nonfiction informational text — what the author most wants you to understand. Example: The main idea of Hammurabi's Code is that a just ruler establishes consistent, written laws that apply to everyone.
  • Claim: The central argument being made in a persuasive or argumentative text — the position the author wants you to accept. Example: MLK's central claim in "Letter from Birmingham Jail" is that nonviolent direct action is the morally correct and strategically necessary response to injustice.
  • Theme: The central insight or truth about human experience explored in a literary text — not the plot, but the underlying idea. Example: A theme of Night is that systematic dehumanization can erode an individual's sense of self even while they resist it.
  • Supporting Evidence: The specific passages, examples, or details the author uses to develop the main idea, claim, or theme over the course of the text.
  • Development: How the main idea, claim, or theme changes, deepens, complicates, or resolves over the full arc of the text. Development means the central idea isn't stated once and repeated — it grows.
  • Central vs. Supporting: Not every idea in a text is the main one. Distinguishing between the central idea and supporting ideas is a core reading skill.
  • Explicit vs. Implicit: Some texts state their main idea/claim directly ("I am writing to argue that..."). Others (especially literary texts) present a theme through what happens to characters — you must infer it.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Identify whether a text is informational (→ main idea), argumentative (→ claim), or literary (→ theme). - [ ] State the main idea/claim/theme in one precise sentence — not just a topic ("justice") but a complete idea ("MLK argues that justice delayed is justice denied"). - [ ] Identify at least three specific passages where the author develops the central idea. - [ ] Explain how the central idea has deepened or changed by the end of the text compared to its beginning. - [ ] Distinguish the central idea from the supporting details.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can state that the theme of Night is "the Holocaust was terrible" — instead of identifying a specific theme about human experience ("the systematic destruction of identity precedes the destruction of the body") and tracing how that theme develops across the text with specific evidence.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
9-10.R.4 — Determine main idea(s)/claim(s)/theme(s) as they develop over the course of the text and support with textual evidence

To meet this standard, you must identify the central idea AND show how it develops — tracing it through specific passages across the full text, from beginning to end.

3.0 Example: Reading Wollstonecraft's A Vindication of the Rights of Woman, a student achieves a 3.0 by identifying the central claim: Women's apparent inferiority to men is not natural — it is produced by an education system that deliberately keeps women ignorant, vain, and dependent. The student then traces how this claim develops: early in the text, Wollstonecraft establishes that "femininity" as currently defined is a set of cultivated weaknesses, not natural traits. In the middle sections, she analyzes how education specifically suppresses women's rational development. In the conclusion, she argues for a complete restructuring of education to allow women to develop reason and virtue. The student shows how the claim deepens — from "women are treated unjustly" to "the educational system is the mechanism of that injustice" to "the solution requires structural change."

The Difference: A 2.0 student states: "Wollstonecraft argues that women deserve equal rights." A 3.0 student identifies the specific mechanism in the claim (education produces inferiority) and traces how that argument develops across the full text.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate how the author develops the central idea — not just identify and trace it, but assess the choices the author made and what effect those choices have on meaning.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Multiple themes in one text: Night has more than one theme — identify at least two and show how they are both developed across the text. Are they in tension with each other, or do they reinforce each other? 2. Claim that complicates itself: MLK's "Letter from Birmingham Jail" makes a central claim about nonviolent direct action — but the letter also anticipates and addresses counterarguments, which complicates the simple version of the claim. Trace the full argument: What is the central claim, how does it develop, and how do MLK's responses to objections deepen or complicate it? 3. Theme vs. message: There's a difference between a literary theme ("identity can survive extreme dehumanization") and a moral message ("resist oppression"). Analyze a text to distinguish the two: What is Orwell showing (theme), and what is Orwell saying we should do (message)?

4.0 Example: A student analyzes how Paine's central claim in Common Sense evolves across the pamphlet. The early sections make a relatively modest claim: monarchy is an irrational form of government. The middle section deepens it: the British constitutional monarchy in particular is illegitimate because it violates natural rights. The final sections radicalize it: independence is not just justified but morally obligatory — delay is betrayal. The student argues that this progressive intensification of the claim is Paine's deliberate rhetorical strategy, designed to bring a reader from "well, monarchy is flawed..." to "we must declare independence immediately" by escalating logical and emotional pressure. The student evaluates whether this strategy is intellectually honest or manipulative.

docs.title: Reading Library Texts for Practice
docs.text:
• Night — Elie Wiesel — Multiple developing themes; practice distinguishing theme from plot summary
  • 1984 — George Orwell — Theme vs. allegory vs. political message; complex layering
  • MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — Central argumentative claim that develops and deepens over 18 sections
  • Common Sense — Thomas Paine — Argumentative claim that escalates in intensity across the pamphlet
  • Wollstonecraft — Vindication — Central claim about education and inferiority that develops through analysis

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose one text from the reading library. Write: (1) one sentence stating the central main idea, claim, or theme precisely, (2) a brief description of how it appears at the beginning, (3) how it develops or deepens in the middle, (4) where it arrives by the end. Use at least three specific passages as evidence. 2 Extended Writing Write a 300–400 word analysis of how a central theme or claim develops across one full text. Show how the idea changes, deepens, or complicates from beginning to end. Avoid summary — focus only on the development of the central idea. 3 Verbal Conversation Record yourself explaining: (1) What type of text it is and what central concept applies (main idea, claim, or theme), (2) the central idea stated precisely, (3) three moments in the text where it develops, (4) how the idea has changed from beginning to end. 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Development Arc" visual for one text. Show the central idea at the beginning, key development moments, and where it arrives at the end. Annotate each moment with a specific passage from the text. 5 Multimedia/Performance Record a 2-minute "Literary Commentary" video. Open the text, read a passage from the beginning, middle, and end, and explain how the central idea you've identified is different (deeper, more complex, more urgent) at each point. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project where you wrote about a text's main argument or theme. Write a 150-word reflection: Did you identify the central idea precisely (one sentence, complete thought) or vaguely (one topic word)? Did you trace its development, or just state it and move on? What would you add?

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Choose a text. Identify whether its central concept is a main idea, claim, or theme — and explain why. State it in one complete sentence (not a topic word).
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph tracing how the central idea develops from beginning to middle to end in one text. Use at least three specific passages as evidence.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write 150 words evaluating the author's choices in developing the central idea. What strategy did they use? Was it effective? Was it intellectually honest?

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:

  • R.2 Comprehension and Inference — Foundation for theme/main idea identification
  • R.9 Informational/Argumentative — Argument analysis closely related to claim development
  • W.4 Argument Writing — Your writing needs a claim that develops — same skill in reverse
  • MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — Best single text for R.4 practice
  • Hammurabi

How to use this skinny: 1. Start with the 2.0 vocabulary and processes. 2. Read the 3.0 example carefully. That's the target. 3. Choose your demonstration mode and begin gathering evidence. 4. Pull additional skinnies if you hit a gap. 5. Check your scale tasks as you complete them.

docs.title: Style, Word Choice, and Author's Craft — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: 9-10.R.7 — Analyze the impact of specific style, syntax, and word choices on meaning, mood, and tone, including figurative and ambiguous language Applies across: All studios — especially active with literary texts and political speeches/primary sources Integrated with: R.2 (comprehension), R.4 (theme/claim), R.8 (literary elements), W.1 (style in your own writing) NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 45–60 minutes Subject: ELA — Text Analysis

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Authors don't choose words randomly. Every word choice, sentence length, and structural decision shapes what the reader feels, what they understand, and what they believe. Analyzing style means asking: Why did the author choose these words, and what do they do to me as a reader?

  • Style: The distinctive way a writer uses language — sentence length, vocabulary level, tone, figurative language, rhythm. Style is how something is said, as distinct from what is said.
  • Syntax: The structure and arrangement of words in a sentence. Long, complex sentences vs. short, simple ones create different effects. Fragments can create urgency or emphasis. Parallel structure (repeating the same grammatical pattern) creates rhythm and emphasis.
  • Word Choice (Diction): The specific words an author selects. Every word has a denotation (dictionary meaning) and connotation (emotional associations). "Thin" and "gaunt" have similar denotations but different connotations — "gaunt" implies illness, deprivation, or suffering.
  • Tone: The author's attitude toward the subject or audience, communicated through word choice, syntax, and what they choose to include or omit. Example: MLK's tone in "Letter from Birmingham Jail" is controlled, formal, and respectful — despite writing from jail, he never sounds defeated or bitter.
  • Mood: The emotional atmosphere a text creates in the reader. Mood is what you feel as you read; tone is what the author feels toward the subject.
  • Figurative Language: Language that means something beyond its literal meaning.
  • Metaphor: A direct comparison without "like" or "as" ("Life is a journey")
  • Simile: A comparison using "like" or "as" ("Life is like a journey")
  • Allusion: A reference to another text, person, or event ("I am not the first man to be jailed for justice" — allusion to Paul, Socrates, etc.)
  • Personification: Giving human qualities to non-human things
  • Anaphora: Repetition of a word or phrase at the beginning of successive clauses ("I have a dream...I have a dream...")
  • Ambiguous Language: Language that can be interpreted in more than one way — sometimes intentionally, to allow readers to bring their own meaning; sometimes accidentally.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Identify a specific word or phrase and explain its connotation (not just definition). - [ ] Identify a figurative language device (metaphor, simile, allusion, anaphora) and name what is being compared or referenced. - [ ] Describe the tone of a passage in one precise adjective + explanation. - [ ] Identify how sentence length or structure affects the pace and feeling of a passage. - [ ] Distinguish between the literal meaning of a phrase and its figurative meaning.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can identify that MLK uses a simile in "Injustice anywhere is a threat to justice everywhere" — actually, that's not a simile, which reveals the 2.0 problem: ability to spot a device but not to accurately identify it or explain what it does to meaning.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
9-10.R.7 — Analyze the impact of specific style, syntax, and word choices on meaning, mood, and tone

To meet this standard, you must explain not just WHAT a stylistic device is, but WHAT EFFECT it has — how it shapes meaning, creates mood, or establishes tone. The word is not "identify" — it's "analyze the impact."

3.0 Example: Reading MLK's "Letter from Birmingham Jail," a student achieves a 3.0 by analyzing his use of allusion in the passage: "Just as the Apostle Paul left his village of Tarsus and carried the gospel of freedom beyond his particular hometown, and just as the Apostle Paul answered the Macedonian call for aid, so am I compelled to carry the gospel of freedom beyond my own home town." The student explains the impact: By alluding to Paul — a figure the white clergy who are criticizing him deeply revere — MLK reframes the question. Instead of being asked "why are you in Birmingham causing trouble," he positions himself as following in the footsteps of a revered Christian missionary. The allusion doesn't just make the letter sound more educated; it changes the moral terms of the entire argument. It makes dismissing King as an outside agitator equivalent to dismissing Paul as an outside agitator.

The Difference: A 2.0 student identifies that MLK uses allusion to Paul in the letter. A 3.0 student analyzes what that allusion does — how it reshapes the moral argument and makes a specific rhetorical move.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate patterns of style across a text — not analyzing single devices in isolation but showing how an author's stylistic choices work together to create a sustained effect.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Pattern analysis: In Night, Wiesel uses very short, declarative sentences throughout — especially when describing the most traumatic moments. Analyze this pattern: What is the effect of the short, simple syntax at moments of extreme horror? How does it differ from what a long, complex sentence at the same moment would have done? 2. Tone shift: Find a moment in any reading library text where the tone shifts — where the author's attitude toward the subject or audience changes. Analyze what creates the shift (word choice, syntax, a specific device) and what it reveals about the author's purpose. 3. Ambiguity as choice: The title 1984 is somewhat arbitrary (Orwell originally considered other years). But the ending of 1984 is genuinely ambiguous — is Winston's final "He loved Big Brother" defeat, survival strategy, or something else? Analyze the ambiguity using the text's final passages.

4.0 Example: A student analyzes Paine's style in Common Sense as a sustained pattern of democratization — deliberately replacing elevated, abstract language with concrete, accessible language to reach an audience of ordinary colonists rather than educated elites. The student identifies three specific stylistic patterns: (1) Paine rarely uses Latin-derived vocabulary when Anglo-Saxon words are available ("kingly" not "monarchical"; "common sense" not "rational apprehension"); (2) his metaphors are domestic (kings compared to robbers; government compared to clothing, worn when needed); (3) his sentences, while not short, use mainly coordinating conjunctions ("and," "but") rather than subordinating ones ("although," "notwithstanding"), making his logic feel like common sense rather than formal argument. The student argues that Paine's style is as revolutionary as his content.

docs.title: Reading Library Texts for Practice
docs.text:
• MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — Rich allusion, anaphora, parallel structure, controlled formal tone despite extreme circumstances
  • Common Sense — Thomas Paine — Deliberate democratization of political writing; metaphor, accessible diction
  • Night — Elie Wiesel — Spare, declarative syntax as expression of trauma; the effect of what is NOT said
  • Declaration of the Rights of Man — Formal declarative style; effect of the enumerated list structure; "universal" language that implies specific audience
  • Wollstonecraft — Vindication — Argumentative tone blending formal analysis and passionate indignation; effect of direct address to readers

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose one 1–2 paragraph passage from any reading library text. Write a close analysis: (1) identify 2–3 specific word choices, syntax decisions, or figurative language devices, (2) explain the impact of each on meaning, mood, or tone, (3) analyze how they work together — not in isolation. 2 Extended Writing Write a 300–400 word style analysis of one author from the reading library. Identify a pattern in their style (not just one device), explain what effect the pattern creates, and evaluate whether it is effective for the author's purpose. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 2-minute close reading. Open to a specific passage and read it aloud. Then explain: What specific words or structures are doing work here? What is their effect on tone or meaning? What would be different if the author had made a different word choice? 4 Visual/Creative Choose one passage (8–10 lines) and annotate it completely: mark every figurative device, every significant word choice, every structural decision. For each annotation, write one sentence explaining the impact. Then write a 2–3 sentence synthesis: how do all these choices work together? 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2-minute "Rewrite" video. Take a passage from one reading library text and rewrite it in a completely different style (e.g., rewrite a passage from Night in Paine's argumentative style, or rewrite MLK in plain bureaucratic language). Then explain: What is lost? What does the difference reveal about the original author's choices? 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project where you quoted from a text. Write a 150-word reflection: Did you analyze the author's word choices, or just use the quotes as evidence for your argument? What would a genuine style analysis of those passages have added?

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Find three examples of figurative language in any reading library text. For each, identify the device (metaphor, allusion, simile, anaphora, etc.) and explain the literal meaning vs. the figurative meaning.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph analyzing ONE word choice or stylistic device in a reading library text. Identify what it is, explain its connotation or effect, and argue why the author chose it rather than a simpler alternative.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write 150 words analyzing a pattern of stylistic choices in one author's work — not a single device, but a recurring approach. Evaluate: what effect does this pattern create, and is it effective?

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• R.7 Scale — Full NDSBL scale for this standard
  • R.8 Literary Elements — Related: literary elements (character, symbolism, setting) are shaped by and expressed through style
  • R.2 Comprehension — Close reading foundation for style analysis
  • MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — Best single text for R.7 practice
  • ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk — Common Sense + W.4 crosswalk available
  • 1984

How to use this skinny: 1. Start with the 2.0 vocabulary and processes. 2. Read the 3.0 example carefully. That's the target. 3. Choose your demonstration mode and begin gathering evidence. 4. Pull additional skinnies if you hit a gap. 5. Check your scale tasks as you complete them.

docs.title: Literary Elements and Author's Craft — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: 9-10.R.8 — Analyze the development and interaction of literary elements and determine how they impact meaning, using strong textual evidence (includes: complex characters, symbolism, mood, setting; author's POV influenced by background/culture; multiple interpretations of the same scene) Applies across: All studios — active with literary texts (Night, 1984) and when analyzing author's perspective in primary sources Integrated with: R.2 (inference), R.4 (theme), R.7 (style), W.3 (explanatory) NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 45–60 minutes Subject: ELA — Literary Text Analysis

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Literary elements are the building blocks authors use to construct meaning in fiction and narrative nonfiction. At the 2.0 level, you need to identify these elements. At the 3.0 level, you need to analyze how they work together to create meaning.

  • Character: A person (or being) in a literary text. Complex characters have contradictory qualities, change over time, and resist simple categorization as "good" or "bad." A dynamic character changes significantly during the story; a static character stays the same.
  • Characterization: How an author reveals character — through direct description, dialogue, actions, other characters' reactions, or the character's own thoughts. Showing (letting actions reveal character) vs. telling (directly stating what a character is like).
  • Symbolism: A concrete object, person, place, or event in the text that represents something beyond itself — an idea, quality, or concept. In Night, the flames of the crematoriums function symbolically as both literal destruction and the annihilation of Wiesel's faith.
  • Mood: The emotional atmosphere the text creates for the reader. Created through setting, word choice, pacing, and imagery. Distinct from tone (the author's attitude).
  • Setting: The time and place of a narrative — but at the literary analysis level, setting is also about how place and time shape character and theme. The physical environment of a narrative is never just background; it interacts with plot and character.
  • Author's Point of View (POV): The perspective from which the narrative is told. Also: how the author's own background, culture, experiences, and historical moment shaped what they chose to write and how.
  • Multiple Interpretations: Many literary scenes or passages can be read in more than one way — different readers, bringing different backgrounds and questions, will find different meaning in the same text. Strong literary analysis acknowledges this.
  • Irony: A gap between what is stated and what is meant, or between expectation and reality. Dramatic irony: the reader knows something a character doesn't. Situational irony: what happens is the opposite of what was expected. Verbal irony: saying the opposite of what you mean.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Identify whether a character is complex or simple, dynamic or static — with textual evidence. - [ ] Identify a symbol and explain what it represents, using evidence from the text. - [ ] Describe the mood of a passage and identify which specific elements (setting, word choice, imagery) create it. - [ ] Identify the narrative POV and explain one way it shapes what the reader knows and how. - [ ] Find a moment in the text that supports more than one interpretation.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can identify that Eliezer in Night is the protagonist, that the flames at Birkenau are symbolic, and that the mood of Chapter 3 is "dark" or "sad" — without yet analyzing how the specific fire imagery interacts with Wiesel's characterization of his own faith, or how the author's survivor POV shapes what the memoir includes and omits.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
9-10.R.8 — Analyze the development and interaction of literary elements and determine how they impact meaning

To meet this standard, you must show how literary elements work together — not analyzing character in isolation, then setting in isolation, but analyzing how character and setting interact to create meaning, or how symbolism reinforces theme.

3.0 Example: Reading Night, a student achieves a 3.0 by analyzing how the interaction of setting and characterization in Chapter 3 (arrival at Birkenau) creates one of the memoir's central meanings. The physical setting — the flames, the selection, the smell — is described with spare, almost toneless language. Eliezer's emotional state is shown primarily through what he notices and doesn't notice: he describes the selection process in a matter-of-fact tone that, the student argues, is not emotional numbness but the beginning of the dissociation the Nazis deliberately engineered through the dehumanization of the arrival process. The student connects setting (the orchestrated visual horror of the flames) to characterization (Eliezer's response) to theme (the dehumanization process begins before the physical violence). The analysis shows how these elements interact to mean something the sum of their individual descriptions doesn't capture.

The Difference: A 2.0 student identifies that the setting at Birkenau is horrifying and that Eliezer is scared. A 3.0 student analyzes how the specific way Wiesel describes the setting interacts with his characterization of his own emotional state to create meaning about how dehumanization works.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate the author's choices — not just analyzing how elements create meaning, but assessing whether the choices are effective, and engaging seriously with alternative interpretations.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Author's POV and its limits: Wiesel's memoir is narrated from the perspective of the surviving Eliezer. What does his survivor's POV allow him to know and tell? What can he not know — what perspectives are absent from his narrative? How does this shape the reader's understanding of the Holocaust? 2. Multiple interpretations of one scene: The final image of Night — Eliezer looking at himself in a mirror and seeing "a corpse gazing back at me" — can be interpreted multiple ways: literal emaciation, psychological death of the pre-war self, or warning to the reader. Analyze all three interpretations using textual evidence. Which is best supported? Can they all be simultaneously true? 3. Orwell's background and 1984: Orwell wrote 1984 in 1948, drawing on his experience in the Spanish Civil War (where he was betrayed by Stalinist communists) and his observations of Soviet propaganda. Analyze how his biographical background shaped specific choices in the novel — what he included, what he emphasized, what anxieties the novel expresses.

4.0 Example: A student analyzes how Orwell constructs Winston Smith as a complex character in 1984 by making him simultaneously the reader's identification figure and a deeply unreliable narrator. Winston believes he is a rational resister who sees through the Party's lies — but his initial attraction to Julia is possessive rather than loving, his "rebellion" is passive (writing in a diary, having an affair) rather than active, and he submits to O'Brien's authority almost immediately once the torture begins. The student argues that Orwell made Winston complex in this specific way — heroic aspiration but limited courage — because he wanted to write about how ordinary people are complicit in their own oppression, not just passive victims.

docs.title: Reading Library Texts for Practice
docs.text:
• Night — Elie Wiesel — Complex characterization of Eliezer; fire symbolism; setting-character interaction; survivor's POV; multiple interpretations of the memoir's ending
  • 1984 — George Orwell — Complex characters (Winston, O'Brien, Julia); setting as political allegory; symbolism (the glass paperweight, the prole woman, Room 101); Orwell's historical POV shaping the text
  • Additional literary texts — reading library being expanded; see Phase 4 ingest queue

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose one literary text. Write an analysis showing how TWO literary elements interact to create meaning. (Don't analyze each element separately — show the interaction.) Use at least three specific passages as evidence. 2 Extended Writing Write a 300–400 word literary analysis focused on one complex character. Analyze: (1) what makes them complex (contradictions, changes), (2) how the author uses characterization techniques to reveal the complexity, (3) what the character's complexity means thematically. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 2-minute explanation: (1) Identify one scene from a literary text that supports at least two different interpretations. (2) Explain each interpretation with evidence. (3) State which you find more convincing and why — or argue that both are simultaneously valid. 4 Visual/Creative Choose a symbol from Night or 1984. Create a visual analysis: (1) draw or describe the symbol's literal object, (2) map the moments in the text where it appears with brief descriptions, (3) analyze how its meaning develops or deepens across the text, (4) connect it to the theme it supports. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2-minute "Literary Commentary" video on one scene from Night or 1984. Read a passage aloud, then analyze: what literary elements are at work, how do they interact, what meaning does this create, and why did the author make these choices? 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project where you wrote about a literary text. Write a 150-word reflection: Did you identify literary elements in isolation or analyze their interaction? Did you consider the author's POV and background? What would your analysis look like if you addressed the interaction of elements?

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Identify one example each of: a complex character, a symbol, a mood-creating passage, and a moment where the author's POV is visible. For each, provide a specific textual reference.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph analyzing how two literary elements interact in one scene — showing how they work together to create a meaning neither would create alone.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write 150 words engaging with a competing interpretation of one scene. Present both interpretations with evidence, then evaluate which is better supported — or argue they are equally valid and explain why.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• R.8 Scale — Full NDSBL scale for this standard
  • R.7 Style and Word Choice — Closely related: style is how literary elements are expressed
  • R.2 Comprehension and Inference — Foundation for literary analysis
  • R.4 Theme — Theme development is closely related to how literary elements develop
  • Night — Elie Wiesel — Primary literary text for R.8 practice
  • 1984 — George Orwell — Primary literary text for R.8 practice

How to use this skinny: 1. Start with the 2.0 vocabulary and processes. 2. Read the 3.0 example carefully. That's the target. 3. Choose your demonstration mode and begin gathering evidence. 4. Pull additional skinnies if you hit a gap. 5. Check your scale tasks as you complete them.
