docs.title: Informational and Argumentative Text Analysis — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: 9-10.R.9 — Analyze the development and interaction of informational and argumentative elements over the course of a nonfiction text and how they impact purpose, using textual evidence (includes: author's POV and bias; multiple accounts in different media; argumentative reasoning and rhetorical techniques) Applies across: All studios — most heavily used; nearly every primary source involves R.9 Integrated with: W.4 (argument writing), W.3 (explanatory), R.2 (inference), WH/USH standards (all primary source work) NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 45–60 minutes Subject: ELA — Informational/Argumentative Text

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
When you read a nonfiction text — a political speech, a historical document, a news article, an essay — it is either providing information, making an argument, or both. To read these texts at the 9-10 level, you need to identify how the argument is built, what techniques the author uses, and what the author's perspective and biases might be.

  • Informational Text: A nonfiction text whose primary purpose is to convey facts, explain concepts, or describe events without arguing for a particular position.
  • Argumentative Text: A nonfiction text whose primary purpose is to persuade the reader to accept a claim or take an action. Argumentative texts have: a claim (position), evidence (support for the claim), and reasoning (the logic connecting evidence to claim).
  • Rhetorical Techniques: Strategies an author uses to persuade an audience.
  • Logos: Appeal to logic and reason (statistics, facts, logical arguments)
  • Ethos: Appeal to credibility and authority (establishing why the speaker should be trusted)
  • Pathos: Appeal to emotion (stories, vivid language, appeals to shared values)
  • Kairos: Appeal to timeliness — "this is the right moment to act"
  • Author's Point of View (POV): The perspective from which the author writes — shaped by their background, experiences, social position, and historical moment.
  • Bias: A systematic tendency to favor certain perspectives or conclusions over others, often unconsciously. Bias does not automatically make a source invalid — it means you must read it knowing where the author is coming from.
  • Counterclaim: An argument that opposes the author's claim. Strong argumentative texts address counterclaims and explain why they are wrong or insufficient.
  • Fallacy: A flawed argument — reasoning that seems logical but has a flaw. Common fallacies: ad hominem (attacking the person instead of the argument), straw man (misrepresenting the opponent's position), false dilemma (presenting only two options when more exist), slippery slope (claiming one thing will automatically lead to an extreme outcome).

Basic Processes: - [ ] Identify whether a text is primarily informational or argumentative — or both. - [ ] State the central claim of an argumentative text in one precise sentence. - [ ] Identify the three rhetorical appeals (logos, ethos, pathos) used in a text, with specific examples. - [ ] Explain the author's point of view and identify at least one way it shapes the text. - [ ] Identify one potential bias in a text and explain how it might affect the text's reliability.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can identify that MLK uses emotional language (pathos) and logical arguments (logos) in "Letter from Birmingham Jail" — without yet analyzing how his specific rhetorical choices work together to address the specific audience (white clergy) and counter the specific objection (timing), or evaluating the strength of his reasoning.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
9-10.R.9 — Analyze the development and interaction of informational and argumentative elements and how they impact purpose

To meet this standard, you must analyze how argumentative elements (claim, evidence, reasoning, rhetorical techniques, POV) work together in a text — and connect that analysis to the author's purpose. You're not just identifying the parts; you're showing how they build toward the author's goal.

3.0 Example: Reading MLK's "Letter from Birmingham Jail," a student achieves a 3.0 by analyzing how the letter's argumentative structure directly serves its purpose. The letter is addressed to white moderate clergy who argued that the civil rights movement should "wait" and use the courts rather than direct action. MLK's argumentative strategy is calibrated precisely for this audience: (1) He establishes ethos through extensive biblical and historical allusion — showing he is not a radical outsider but part of a revered tradition the clergy respect. (2) He dismantles the "wait" argument not with anger but with logos — defining "just" vs. "unjust" laws and showing that waiting has never produced justice. (3) He uses pathos strategically — the example of explaining to a young daughter why she cannot go to the public amusement park — to make the injustice humanly concrete without alienating his audience. The student shows how these elements work together to serve the specific purpose: not to inspire civil rights supporters, but to persuade skeptical moderates.

The Difference: A 2.0 student identifies the three rhetorical appeals in the letter. A 3.0 student shows how the specific deployment of those appeals serves the specific purpose with the specific audience — how the argument is designed for these readers.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate the quality of the argument — not just analyze how it works, but assess whether it works, whether the reasoning is sound, and whether the rhetorical techniques are honest or manipulative.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Cross-text comparison: Both Thomas Paine (Common Sense) and MLK ("Letter from Birmingham Jail") use ethos to establish their authority. Analyze: What is each author's ethos claim? What is its basis? Is it honest? Is it effective with the intended audience? How do their approaches to ethos differ? 2. Fallacy evaluation: Find one potentially flawed argument in any reading library text — not a factual error, but a reasoning error (slippery slope, false dilemma, ad hominem, straw man). Analyze: Is it actually a fallacy, or a legitimate simplification for a broad audience? Does the presence of this weakness undermine the overall argument? 3. Multiple accounts: The Magna Carta and MLK's Letter were both produced by people challenging authority, using similar arguments (unjust power must be limited, rights cannot be arbitrarily violated). Compare how they develop the same type of argument differently for their different audiences and historical moments. What does comparing them reveal about how the same underlying argument works in different contexts?

4.0 Example: A student evaluates Wollstonecraft's argument in Vindication for its logical strength and its strategic limits. Logically, the argument is strong: if Enlightenment principles (reason is the basis of rights, rational beings deserve freedom) are true, then women — who are rational — deserve freedom and education. The reasoning follows from premises that her opponents already accept. BUT the student identifies a strategic limit: Wollstonecraft's argument is premised on reason being the basis of all rights — which implicitly excludes people deemed "irrational" (enslaved people, colonized peoples, people with cognitive disabilities). Wollstonecraft never addresses this, which reveals how her own class position (educated, white, European) shaped a blind spot in her "universal" argument. The student evaluates: This doesn't disprove her argument about women, but it reveals the argument's dependence on premises she hasn't examined.

docs.title: Reading Library Texts for Practice
docs.text: All reading library texts with argumentative elements are R.9 practice texts: - MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — Best single text for R.9 practice; rich rhetorical analysis; specific audience and purpose - Common Sense — Thomas Paine — Ethos, logos, pathos all present; fallacy analysis possible; specific historical audience - Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789) — Argumentative structure of a founding document; universalist claims vs. specific exclusions; compare to U.S. Declaration - Wollstonecraft — Vindication (1792) — Logical argument built on Enlightenment premises; implicit limitations; strong for 4.0 evaluation - ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk — W.4 crosswalk for Birmingham Letter and Common Sense

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose one argumentative text from the reading library. Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) the central claim and audience, (2) how two specific rhetorical techniques serve the author's purpose with that specific audience, (3) the author's POV and how it shapes the argument. 2 Extended Writing Write a 300–400 word rhetorical analysis of one reading library text. Show how the text's argumentative elements (claim, evidence, reasoning, appeals, POV) work together to serve the author's purpose. Do not summarize — analyze how the argument is constructed. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 2-minute analysis: (1) State the text's central claim precisely, (2) explain who the audience is and how you know, (3) analyze one specific rhetorical technique and explain why it was chosen for this specific audience and purpose. 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Rhetorical Map" of one argumentative text. Show: the claim at the center; branches for logos, ethos, and pathos with specific textual examples; the author's POV and how it shapes the argument; any counterclaims addressed; and your evaluation of which appeal is strongest. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2-minute "Argument Breakdown" video. Take one argument from a reading library text and break it down on camera: (1) State the claim, (2) identify the evidence and reasoning, (3) explain one rhetorical technique and why it was used, (4) evaluate one strength and one weakness of the argument. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project where you analyzed a primary source. Write a 150-word reflection: Did you analyze the argumentative structure, or just use the source as evidence? Did you consider the author's POV and bias? What would a genuine R.9 analysis have added?

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Choose an argumentative text. Identify: (1) the central claim in one sentence, (2) one example each of logos, ethos, and pathos with a specific quote, (3) one aspect of the author's POV.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph analyzing how two argumentative elements work together to serve the author's purpose with the specific audience. Show the interaction, not just the identification.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write 150 words evaluating the quality of one argument: Does the reasoning hold up? Is there a logical fallacy or unstated assumption? Does the author's POV create a blind spot? Use specific evidence.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• R.9 Scale — Full NDSBL scale for this standard
  • W.4 Argument Writing — Building the argument you can now analyze in others
  • R.4 Main Idea / Claim — Claim identification is the foundation of R.9
  • IR.1–5 Research Bundle — Evaluating sources (IR.4) closely relates to R.9 bias analysis
  • ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk — R.9 crosswalk examples with historical texts
  • Magna Carta

How to use this skinny: 1. Start with the 2.0 vocabulary and processes. 2. Read the 3.0 example carefully. That's the target. 3. Choose your demonstration mode and begin gathering evidence. 4. Pull additional skinnies if you hit a gap. 5. Check your scale tasks as you complete them.

docs.title: Explanatory Writing — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: 9-10.W.3 — Write to inform an audience and to explain complex information by creating a clear thesis and providing supporting claims, details, and evidence from a variety of relevant and reliable sources Applies across: All studios — writing to explain a historical event, a literary analysis, or a research finding Integrated with: W.2 (organization), W.6 (process), IR.1–5 (research), R.9 (informational text analysis) NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes (includes drafting) Subject: ELA — Writing

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Explanatory writing (also called "informational" or "expository" writing) explains something to an audience that doesn't already know it. The goal is not to persuade — it's to make something complex clear. At the 2.0 level, you can write an explanation that covers the basic facts. At the 3.0 level, you can write one that genuinely teaches the reader something complex.

  • Thesis: The controlling idea of your explanatory piece — what you're explaining and your specific angle on it. Not just a topic ("The Industrial Revolution") but a specific, informative claim ("The Industrial Revolution started in England because of a specific combination of coal resources, labor supply, patent law, and colonial markets — not simply because England was more innovative").
  • Supporting Claim: A specific sub-point that supports and develops your thesis. An explanatory essay typically has 2–3 supporting claims, each with its own evidence.
  • Evidence: Facts, examples, statistics, quotations, or expert analysis that support your claims. Evidence should be from relevant and reliable sources.
  • Relevant Source: A source that actually addresses your topic — not just vaguely related.
  • Reliable Source: A source that is accurate, credible, and trustworthy — expert, peer-reviewed, or with verifiable facts.
  • Explanation vs. Argument: Explanatory writing explains how or why something is the case. Argument writing tries to persuade someone to accept a contested position. In explanatory writing, you're not defending a contested claim — you're making complex information accessible.
  • Integration: Weaving evidence smoothly into your writing, not just dumping quotes. Quote integration requires: introducing the quote (who said it?), presenting the quote (exact words), and explaining it (what does it mean and why does it matter for your claim?).
  • Clarity: The quality of being easy to understand despite the complexity of the subject. Clarity is the primary goal of explanatory writing.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Write a thesis that announces both the topic AND your specific explanatory angle — not just the topic alone. - [ ] Identify evidence from at least two different sources. - [ ] Introduce a quote with a signal phrase (who is speaking and why their voice matters). - [ ] Follow a quote with an explanation of what it means and how it supports your claim. - [ ] Use transitional phrases to connect claims and show how your explanation develops.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can write a paragraph explaining the causes of the French Revolution with some factual evidence — but whose thesis just states the topic ("I will explain the causes of the French Revolution") rather than offering a specific explanatory claim, and who drops quotes without explaining their connection to the argument.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
9-10.W.3 — Write to inform and explain complex information with a clear thesis, supporting claims, evidence from a variety of relevant and reliable sources

To meet this standard, your explanatory writing must teach the reader something they didn't know before, using a clear structure, a specific thesis, and integrated evidence from multiple sources.

3.0 Example: A student writing about the Black Death explains not just what it was and when it happened, but why it had such long-lasting social consequences. The thesis: "The Black Death's most significant consequence was not the death toll itself, but the labor shortage it created — which shifted bargaining power to surviving peasants and accelerated the end of feudal serfdom." Supporting claims: (1) pre-plague social conditions — serfs had no bargaining power; (2) the plague's demographic impact — 30–50% mortality rates; (3) the specific mechanism — lords needed labor, surviving serfs could demand better terms; (4) the long-term result — wages rose, serfdom declined across Western Europe. Evidence is integrated from at least two sources, each introduced and explained. The thesis is specific enough to teach the reader something, not just cover the topic.

The Difference: A 2.0 student writes an explanation of the Black Death covering what it was, where it spread, and how many people died. A 3.0 student writes an explanation with a specific explanatory thesis — explaining a mechanism or consequence — supported by evidence from multiple sources, each properly integrated and explained.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, your explanatory writing must address complexity, nuance, and competing explanations — acknowledging what is contested or uncertain while still producing a clear, organized explanation.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Explaining complexity: Choose a historical topic where the "obvious" explanation is incomplete or oversimplified (Rome's fall, WWI's causes, the Depression's origins). Write an explanation that presents the fuller, more complex picture without losing clarity. 2. Explaining an opposing view: Some explanatory writing needs to explain why reasonable people disagree — not just what the facts are. Write a 2–3 paragraph explanation of why historians disagree about one historical question. 3. Source integration challenge: Find three sources on the same topic that have different emphases or angles. Write a paragraph that integrates information from all three — weaving them together rather than presenting them sequentially.

4.0 Example: A student writing about why the Haitian Revolution succeeded when other slave revolts failed writes a thesis that acknowledges uncertainty: "While historians debate the relative importance of ideology, military strategy, and disease in the Haitian Revolution's success, the decisive factor appears to have been the combination of yellow fever killing French troops and the revolutionary leadership's ability to unify former enemies under a clear political goal — not any single cause alone." The essay then explains each factor, integrates evidence from multiple sources, and acknowledges where the historical evidence is contested.

docs.title: Reading Library Texts as Models
docs.text: These texts are exemplary W.3 models — they explain complex ideas clearly: - MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — Sections explaining the distinction between just and unjust laws are W.3 models: thesis → specific criteria → examples → explanation - Common Sense — Thomas Paine — Paine's explanation of why hereditary monarchy is irrational is a W.3 model: thesis → logical steps → examples → conclusion - Wollstonecraft — Vindication — Her explanation of how education creates the inferiority she observes is W.3: thesis → mechanism → evidence → implication

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Write a 3–4 paragraph explanatory piece on a topic connected to your current studio. Include: a thesis that makes a specific explanatory claim, 2 supporting claims with evidence, at least one properly integrated quote, and a brief conclusion. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–500 word explanatory essay explaining a complex historical event, mechanism, or concept from your studio. Your thesis should explain a mechanism or consequence, not just introduce a topic. Use at least two different sources. 3 Verbal Conversation Record yourself explaining something complex clearly (2–3 minutes). Show: a clear opening statement of what you're explaining (like a thesis), organized supporting explanations, and integrated evidence at least twice. 4 Visual/Creative Create an explanatory visual (infographic, diagram, or annotated map) that explains a complex idea from your studio. Include a clear title statement (like a thesis), labeled supporting information with sources, and an explanatory note for each major claim. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute explanatory video on a complex topic from your studio. Imagine your audience is smart but doesn't know anything about this topic. Show: a clear opening claim, organized explanation, at least two pieces of evidence. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous explanatory piece. Write a 150-word reflection: Does your thesis make a specific explanatory claim or just introduce a topic? Is your evidence introduced, quoted, and explained — or just dropped? What would you revise?

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Write a thesis for an explanatory piece that states both a topic AND a specific explanatory angle (not just "I will explain X" but "X happened because Y and Z").
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a body paragraph with: a supporting claim, at least two pieces of evidence from different sources, and an explanation of how the evidence supports the claim. Check: Is each quote introduced? Is each quote explained after?
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a paragraph explaining a topic where historians or experts disagree. Explain what each side argues and what evidence supports each view — without taking a side yourself. Focus on making the disagreement clear to a reader unfamiliar with it.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• W.3 Scale — Full NDSBL scale for this standard
  • W.4 Argument Writing — Closely related: explanatory writing explains, argument writing persuades
  • W.6 Writing Process — Using feedback to improve explanatory writing
  • IR.1–5 Research Bundle — Finding and evaluating the sources your W.3 needs

How to use this skinny: 1. Start with the 2.0 vocabulary and processes. 2. Read the 3.0 example carefully. That's the target. 3. Choose your demonstration mode and begin gathering evidence. 4. Pull additional skinnies if you hit a gap. 5. Check your scale tasks as you complete them.

docs.title: Argument Writing — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: 9-10.W.4 — Write to persuade an audience by establishing relevant context, stating a clear position/thesis, incorporating valid and reliable evidence from a variety of sources to support specific claims and to refute counterclaims, and using logical reasoning to avoid fallacies Applies across: All studios — the most frequently used writing standard Integrated with: W.2 (organization), W.6 (process), R.9 (argument analysis), IR.1–5 (research) NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes (includes drafting) Subject: ELA — Writing

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Argument writing is not just "writing about something you believe." It is a structured attempt to change what a specific reader thinks or does, using evidence and logic — not emotion, assertion, or insult. At the 2.0 level, you can write a basic argument with a position and some evidence. At the 3.0 level, you build a full argument that can hold up against opposing views.

  • Argument (Academic): A claim supported by evidence and reasoning, designed to persuade a specific audience. Academic argument is NOT a fight — it is a structured case.
  • Claim / Thesis / Position: The central statement you are arguing — what you want the reader to believe or accept. A good thesis is: (1) specific (not vague), (2) arguable (reasonable people could disagree), (3) significant (worth arguing about), and (4) stated in a way that implies or previews your reasoning.
  • Evidence: Facts, data, expert opinion, examples, or textual quotations that support your claim. Evidence must be valid (from reliable sources) and relevant (actually connects to your claim).
  • Reasoning: The explanation of HOW the evidence supports the claim — the logical link. Evidence without reasoning is just a list of facts; reasoning without evidence is just assertion. The combination is argument.
  • Counterclaim: The strongest opposing position — what someone who disagrees with you would say. Addressing the counterclaim shows you've considered the other side and strengthens your own argument.
  • Refutation: Your response to the counterclaim — explaining why the opposing position is wrong, incomplete, or less well-supported than your own.
  • Logical Fallacy: A flaw in reasoning that makes an argument invalid, even if the conclusion might be correct.
  • Ad hominem: Attacking the person instead of the argument
  • Straw man: Misrepresenting the opposing argument to make it easier to attack
  • False dilemma: Presenting only two options when more exist
  • Slippery slope: Claiming X will inevitably lead to extreme Y without showing why
  • Appeal to authority (inappropriate): Citing an expert outside their area of expertise
  • Relevant Context: Background information that helps the reader understand why the question matters and what's at stake.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Write a thesis that is arguable (someone could reasonably disagree), specific (not vague), and significant. - [ ] Provide at least two pieces of evidence from different sources, each properly introduced and explained. - [ ] Write at least one complete counterclaim (what the other side would say) and refute it. - [ ] Explain the reasoning connecting each piece of evidence to your claim. - [ ] Identify and avoid at least one logical fallacy.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who writes: "I think the atomic bomb was justified because it ended the war faster and saved lives" — without yet providing evidence beyond assertion, acknowledging the strongest counterargument (the bomb was not the only way to end the war and targeted civilians), or addressing whether "saving lives" is an adequate justification for deliberately killing 200,000 civilians.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
9-10.W.4 — Write to persuade with a clear position, valid evidence, counterclaim refutation, and logical reasoning

To meet this standard, your argument must include all the components and use them correctly: a specific arguable thesis, evidence from multiple reliable sources, explicit reasoning connecting evidence to claim, a genuine counterclaim (not a straw man), and a refutation that actually addresses the opposing argument.

3.0 Example: A student arguing that the Versailles Treaty's punitive terms were more responsible for WWII than Hitler's personal ideology writes: Thesis: "While Hitler's ideology was necessary for WWII, it was not sufficient — without the specific economic and political destabilization created by the Versailles Treaty, Hitler would have remained a fringe figure rather than a national leader." Supporting claim 1: German hyperinflation and unemployment created by war reparations gave extremist movements an audience (evidence: German unemployment statistics 1930–1933). Supporting claim 2: The "war guilt clause" provided a nationalist grievance that Hitler exploited (evidence: Hitler's Mein Kampf citations of Versailles). Counterclaim: Some historians argue that Versailles was actually lenient by historical standards, and that the real cause was Hitler's specific ideology and organizational genius. Refutation: Even if Versailles was lenient in absolute terms, it was perceived as unjust by a majority of Germans — and political grievance is about perception, not objective comparison. The student's reasoning is explicit throughout: connecting each piece of evidence to the thesis and explaining why the counterclaim doesn't disprove the argument.

The Difference: A 2.0 student writes: "Versailles caused WWII because it made Germany angry." (Assertion, no evidence, no counterclaim.) A 3.0 student constructs the full argument with thesis, evidence + reasoning, genuine counterclaim, and refutation.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, your argument must address the strongest version of the opposing position — not a straw man — and acknowledge the genuine complexity of the question while still defending a clear position.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Steel-manning the opposition: Before writing your argument, write the strongest possible case for the opposing position — the best version, not the weakest. Then write your argument so it genuinely addresses this strong opposing case. 2. Qualified claim: Some questions don't have a clean yes/no answer — the honest argument requires a qualified claim. ("Was the Haitian Revolution successful? Yes — in achieving independence. No — in achieving the economic freedom that true liberation would require.") Write an argument with a qualified, nuanced thesis that does justice to the complexity. 3. Argument from analogy: One type of reasoning uses historical parallels: "Appeasement failed in 1938; therefore, appeasement will fail today." Analyze the limits of this reasoning. When are historical analogies valid evidence? When are they misleading?

4.0 Example: A student argues that MLK was wrong to be optimistic about white moderate support in the Letter from Birmingham Jail. The student steel-mans King's position: King was writing to persuade, and optimism about his audience's potential is a valid rhetorical choice. But the student then argues: King's critique of the white moderate as "more devoted to order than justice" is ultimately more accurate than his hope for their transformation — and the subsequent history of civil rights backlash (white flight, anti-busing movements, Southern political realignment) supports this darker reading. The student acknowledges this is a complex historical judgment, provides specific evidence, and defends the claim while acknowledging what MLK got right.

docs.title: Reading Library Texts as W.4 Models
docs.text: These texts are exemplary argument models — read them as how to argue, not just what they argue: - MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — Counterclaim and refutation throughout; ethos, logos, pathos working together; qualified claims ("I did not engage in direct action without first seeking to negotiate") - Common Sense — Thomas Paine — Progressive escalation of claim; demolishing the "stability" argument for monarchy; appeals to colonial self-interest - Wollstonecraft — Vindication — Logic-first argument; acknowledging what opponents have gotten right before dismantling their conclusions - ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk — W.4 crosswalk examples using Birmingham Letter and Common Sense

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Write a 4–5 paragraph argument on a question connected to your current studio. Must include: (1) thesis with context, (2) at least two body paragraphs with evidence + reasoning, (3) a genuine counterclaim paragraph with refutation, (4) a conclusion. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word argument essay on a historically or currently contested question. Steel-man the opposition, then construct your argument so it genuinely addresses the strongest version of the opposing case. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute spoken argument: (1) state your thesis, (2) give your strongest piece of evidence with reasoning, (3) state the best opposing argument, (4) refute it, (5) conclude. No reading from a script — arguing, not reciting. 4 Visual/Creative Create an "Argument Blueprint": a visual outline of your argument showing: thesis box, evidence + reasoning boxes (color-coded by source), counterclaim box, refutation box, and conclusion. Annotate each box with your actual content. Use the blueprint to self-assess: Is each connection between boxes explained? 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute "Debate Position" video. Present your argument on one side of a contested historical question. Show: your thesis, your evidence, your response to the strongest counterargument. Assume your audience disagrees with you and that you need to change their mind. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous argument piece. Write a 200-word reflection using this checklist: Does the thesis make an arguable claim? Is there evidence from at least two sources? Is the reasoning explicit? Is there a genuine counterclaim (not a straw man)? Is there a refutation? What would you revise?

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Write a thesis for an argument. Then test it: Is it arguable? (Can someone reasonably disagree?) Is it specific? (Could you tell me which evidence you'd need?) Is it significant? (Does it matter?) Revise if it fails any test.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a counterclaim + refutation paragraph. First, write the strongest version of the opposing argument (not a straw man). Then write your refutation — explaining specifically why this argument is wrong, incomplete, or less well-supported than your own.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write 200 words acknowledging what is genuinely uncertain or complicated about your argument. What evidence would change your mind? What is the strongest piece of evidence your opponent has? How do you deal with it honestly?

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• W.4 Scale — Full NDSBL scale for this standard
  • W.3 Explanatory Writing — Closely related: argument persuades, explanation informs
  • R.9 Informational/Argumentative — Reading argument well makes you write it better
  • IR.1–5 Research Bundle — Finding the evidence your argument needs
  • ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk — W.4 crosswalk using Birmingham Letter

How to use this skinny: 1. Start with the 2.0 vocabulary and processes. 2. Read the 3.0 example carefully. That's the target. 3. Choose your demonstration mode and begin gathering evidence. 4. Pull additional skinnies if you hit a gap. 5. Check your scale tasks as you complete them.

docs.title: The Writing Process — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: 9-10.W.6 — Develop and strengthen writing through the writing process to produce a quality product for a specific purpose and audience Applies across: All studios — scaffolds every writing standard; active during any multi-draft writing task Integrated with: W.3 (explanatory), W.4 (argument), W.1 (clarity), W.2 (organization) NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: Ongoing — applies throughout a writing project Subject: ELA — Writing

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Good writing is not written in one sitting. The writing process is a set of iterative stages that, used deliberately, produce stronger writing than any first draft. At the 2.0 level, you can move through the stages. At the 3.0 level, you can use feedback from each stage to make targeted, meaningful revisions.

  • Prewriting: The planning stage — before you write a draft. Includes brainstorming, outlining, gathering sources, clarifying your thesis, and deciding on your audience and purpose. Time spent prewriting reduces revision time.
  • Drafting: Writing your first full version. A draft is not supposed to be perfect — it's supposed to exist. The goal of drafting is to get ideas on paper so you can see what you have and what you need.
  • Feedback: Input from another reader (teacher, peer, yourself after stepping away) about what is and isn't working. Useful feedback is specific: not "this is confusing" but "this transition between paragraph 2 and paragraph 3 is confusing because you don't explain how the two ideas connect."
  • Revision: Changing the content of your draft — adding, deleting, moving, clarifying ideas. Revision is not the same as editing. Revision might mean completely rewriting a paragraph, restructuring your argument, or cutting half your evidence because it isn't actually relevant.
  • Editing: Fixing technical errors — grammar, spelling, punctuation, sentence structure. Editing happens after revision. You don't edit a draft you're about to throw out.
  • Purpose: Why you're writing this piece — to inform, to persuade, to analyze, to entertain. Purpose should shape every decision: word choice, structure, evidence selection, tone.
  • Audience: Who will read this. The same information explained to a younger student vs. a professor vs. a peer requires different vocabulary, different assumed knowledge, and different levels of explanation.
  • Quality Product: A finished piece that accomplishes its purpose for its intended audience — not necessarily perfect, but deliberate and complete.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Before drafting: write a 2–3 sentence statement of purpose and audience. - [ ] Before drafting: outline your main claims (even roughly). - [ ] After receiving feedback: identify the 2–3 most important changes to make. - [ ] Distinguish between revision (content changes) and editing (technical changes) — do them separately. - [ ] Read your draft aloud — you'll catch problems you miss reading silently.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who drafts, receives feedback ("your argument is unclear in paragraph 3"), and then only edits for grammar — not revising the unclear argument because revising is harder than fixing commas.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
9-10.W.6 — Develop and strengthen writing through the writing process to produce a quality product for a specific purpose and audience

To meet this standard, your revisions must be targeted, substantive, and responsive — showing that you understood the feedback, diagnosed the problem, and made a deliberate change that actually solves it.

3.0 Example: A student drafts an argument essay about the Versailles Treaty. Feedback: "Your counterclaim paragraph doesn't address the strongest opposing argument — it addresses a weak version." The student's revision process: (1) Rereads the counterclaim and identifies what argument they actually made vs. what the strongest opposing argument is; (2) Researches the strongest version of the opposing position; (3) Rewrites the counterclaim paragraph to address the stronger version; (4) Revises the refutation to respond to this stronger claim. The revision is targeted (focused on the specific problem identified), substantive (the paragraph is substantially rewritten, not just tweaked), and responsive (it actually addresses the feedback, not a different problem).

The Difference: A 2.0 student receives feedback and fixes surface-level issues (word choice, comma errors) while leaving the structural problems untouched. A 3.0 student diagnoses the actual problem in the feedback, makes substantive revisions that address the root issue, and produces a stronger draft.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must use the writing process to identify and address problems you find yourself — not just respond to feedback from others. Self-revision requires being a ruthless reader of your own work.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Self-assessment before feedback: Before submitting a draft for feedback, read it as if you are the intended audience — someone who disagrees with your argument and will look for weaknesses. Write a 100-word critique of your own draft. Then revise based on your self-critique before getting outside feedback. 2. Purpose revision: Take a piece of writing you completed for one purpose (e.g., an informational explanation) and revise it for a completely different purpose (e.g., an argument) with the same content. What changes? This exercise reveals how purpose shapes every writing decision. 3. Feedback quality: Not all feedback is helpful. Analyze the feedback you've received on past writing: What was specific and actionable? What was vague? What was wrong — feedback that would have made your writing worse if you'd followed it? Develop your own standard for what makes feedback useful.

4.0 Example: A student revising their essay on the causes of WWI realizes, while rereading, that their thesis and their body paragraphs are arguing slightly different things — the thesis says nationalism was the primary cause, but the body paragraphs build a stronger case for the alliance system. Without any external feedback, the student recognizes this as a coherence problem (thesis and evidence don't align), diagnoses the cause (the thesis was written before the research was complete), and makes a substantive revision: either revising the thesis to match the body paragraphs' argument, or restructuring the body paragraphs to make the thesis's claim more convincingly.

docs.title: Applying the Process to Your Studio Writing
docs.text:
Before you draft (15 minutes): - What is my argument or explanation in one sentence? - Who is my audience? What do they already know? What do I need to explain? - What are my 2–3 main points? Do I have evidence for each?

After drafting (self-read): - Read aloud. Where do you stumble or lose the thread? That's where revision is needed. - Does every paragraph have: a clear point, evidence, and explanation? - Is your counterclaim the strongest version of the opposing argument, or a straw man?

When revising from feedback: - What is the specific problem the feedback identifies? - What is causing that problem in the draft? - What change would fix the root cause — not just the symptom?

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Submit two drafts of one piece: original and revised. With a 100-word reflection explaining: What specific feedback or self-assessment problem did you identify? What specific revision did you make? How does the revision address the problem? 2 Extended Writing Write a "Process Document" for one studio writing project. Include: your prewriting notes, your first draft, the feedback you received (or self-critique), a list of the 3 most important revisions you made, and the revised draft. Show your work. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 2-minute explanation of one revision you made to a piece of writing: (1) What did the original draft do? (2) What was the problem? (3) How did you diagnose the problem — what was causing it? (4) What specific change did you make? (5) Why does the revision solve the problem? 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Before/After" visual for one paragraph: show the original version, annotate the problems, and show the revised version with annotations explaining what changed and why. 5 Multimedia/Performance Record a 2-minute "Writer's Commentary" video showing one revision: open to the original paragraph on screen, explain what the problem is, then show the revised version and explain why the revision solves it. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select any two drafts from your portfolio. Write a 200-word reflection analyzing: What changed between drafts? Were the changes responses to specific feedback or your own self-revision? Which changes made the writing stronger, and how do you know?

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Before drafting a piece, write 2–3 sentences stating your purpose and your intended audience. After drafting, list the 3 things you are least satisfied with in the draft.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: After receiving feedback on a draft, write a 100-word "revision plan": (1) What is the specific problem identified? (2) What is causing it? (3) What specific change will you make? Then make the revision.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Read your draft as if you are the intended reader who disagrees with your argument. Write a 100-word self-critique identifying the 2–3 weakest points in your own writing. Then revise based on your own critique — before getting outside feedback.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• W.6 Scale — Full NDSBL scale for this standard
  • W.4 Argument Writing — The process applies to all writing types; W.4 is most commonly where process matters
  • W.3 Explanatory Writing — Process applies equally to explanatory writing
  • C.6 Discussion and Debate — Peer feedback in discussion is a form of the writing process

How to use this skinny: 1. Start with the 2.0 vocabulary and processes. 2. Read the 3.0 example carefully. That's the target. 3. Choose your demonstration mode and begin gathering evidence. 4. Pull additional skinnies if you hit a gap. 5. Check your scale tasks as you complete them.

"A student who analyzes a primary source speech, identifies the author's purpose and specific rhetorical choices (parallel structure, emotional appeals), and explains how those choices serve the purpose — with textual evidence — is at 3.0."]

docs.text:

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas — the thing you make and exhibit. Your deliverable should show you took what you demonstrated you know and built something real with it.

  1. [Deliverable Idea 1 Title] — [Description: what format, who the audience is, what the deliverable does in the world. E.g., "A podcast episode interviewing two 'historians' (role-played by students or community members) who argue different interpretations of the same event."]

  2. [Deliverable Idea 2 Title] — [Description: another format with different audience/purpose. E.g., "A visual essay comparing political propaganda posters from two different era/units, with analytical captions."]

  3. [Deliverable Idea 3 Title] — [Description: a third option. E.g., "A community education piece — a one-page annotated timeline — designed for a local library display connecting this historical topic to present-day Fargo."]

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
Work through these in order. The 2.0 task is the foundation. The 3.0 task is the standard. The 4.0 task is the extension.

  • [ ] 2.0 task: [Concrete, completable foundation task — a student can independently check this off. E.g., "Identify three specific examples of cause-and-effect relationships in [primary source]. Write one sentence explaining each."]
  • [ ] 3.0 task: [Standard-demonstrating task — matches exact standard language. E.g., "Analyze [specific primary source] for the author's point of view, specific biases, and how rhetorical choices (style, word choice, structure) support the author's purpose. Provide textual evidence for each claim."]
  • [ ] 4.0 task: [Extension, transfer, or synthesis task. E.g., "Compare two primary sources from different time periods or media that address the same topic. Evaluate which account is more effective for a specific audience and purpose — and defend that evaluation."]

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.1 Science & Technology · Unit 1 Industrial Revolution
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.1 — Science, Technology, and Society Unit: 1 — Industrial Revolution & Progressive Era (1870s–1920) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did new technologies reshape American society during industrialization? What were the consequences — intended and unintended — of those changes?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The Industrial Revolution was driven by technology: steam power, railroads, the telegraph, the assembly line, electricity. Each new technology changed not just what people could produce but how they lived, worked, and organized society. Technology created wealth — and created new forms of inequality.

docs.title: Key Content — Unit 1
docs.text: Technology What It Did Consequence Railroads Connected national market; enabled mass production Created monopolies; displaced Native communities Telegraph / telephone Instant long-distance communication Accelerated business; shrank perceived geography Assembly line / interchangeable parts Faster, cheaper manufacturing Deskilled labor; created repetitive factory work Electricity (Edison) Powered factories; lit cities Extended workday; accelerated urbanization Steel (Bessemer process) Cheaper, stronger building material Enabled skyscrapers, bridges, railroads

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
When you write about US.6_12.1, you're not just describing technology — you're analyzing cause and effect and tradeoffs. The question is always: who benefited from this technology, who paid the cost, and did anyone choose that distribution?

Example argument move:

"The railroad connected the continent, but it also enabled the displacement of Plains Indians and the consolidation of corporate power. Technology rarely has neutral effects — it tends to amplify the power of whoever controls it."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection W.3 — Explanatory Explain how a specific technology changed American life W.4 — Argument Argue whether industrialization's benefits outweighed its costs R.9 — Argument Analysis Analyze Sinclair's argument about what technology did to workers

docs.title: Connect to Reading Library
docs.text: • The Jungle — Sinclair — technology and labor conditions; factory system as argument

docs.title: Connect to Studio Packets
docs.text:
• USH-01 — When the System Fails

US.6_12.1 Unit 1 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.2 Ideas, Beliefs & Ideology · Unit 1 Industrial Revolution
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.2 — Ideas, Beliefs, and Institutions Unit: 1 — Industrial Revolution & Progressive Era (1870s–1920) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What ideas and beliefs shaped how Americans understood industrialization? What ideologies justified the new economic order — and what ideologies challenged it?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: Two big ideas clashed during industrialization. Social Darwinism (Herbert Spencer, Andrew Carnegie's "Gospel of Wealth") argued that the wealthy deserved their wealth — competition was natural, inequality was the result of merit. Progressive reform argued the opposite: inequality was the result of an unfair system, and democracy required correcting it. The fight between these ideas shaped every political debate of the era.

docs.title: Key Content — Unit 1
docs.text: Idea / Belief Core Claim Who Held It Effect Social Darwinism Wealth = merit; poverty = weakness; don't interfere Carnegie, Rockefeller, most politicians Blocked labor reform for decades Gospel of Wealth Rich have a duty to use wealth for social good (charity, not redistribution) Carnegie Funded libraries, universities — but didn't change wages Muckraker journalism Truth-telling creates public pressure for reform Tarbell, Sinclair, Riis Led to antitrust laws, food safety regulations Labor movement ideology Workers have collective power; capitalism needs democratic limits Knights of Labor, AFL Slow gains; major backlash; red-baiting Progressivism Democracy requires active government intervention Roosevelt, La Follette Sherman Antitrust enforcement, FDA, income tax

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.2 asks you to analyze ideas as arguments. Social Darwinism wasn't just a belief — it was an argument for a policy position (don't regulate business). Progressivism was a counter-argument. When you write about this era, you're analyzing a debate.

Example argument move:

"Carnegie's 'Gospel of Wealth' argued that concentrated wealth was natural and beneficial — but it conveniently justified the very conditions that made Carnegie wealthy. The Progressives argued this was ideology, not science."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.9 — Argument Analysis Analyze Carnegie's Gospel of Wealth as an argument W.4 — Argument Argue whether Social Darwinism or Progressivism better describes industrial America

docs.title: Connect to Reading Library
docs.text:
• The Jungle — Sinclair — muckraker journalism as counter-ideology to Social Darwinism

US.6_12.2 Unit 1 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.3 Political Systems · Unit 1 Industrial Revolution
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.3 — Political Systems and Democratic Institutions Unit: 1 — Industrial Revolution & Progressive Era (1870s–1920) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did political institutions respond to industrialization? What reforms changed American democracy during the Progressive Era — and what did they fail to change?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The Progressive Era produced the most significant expansion of American democracy since Reconstruction — and most of it happened through constitutional amendments and new federal agencies. The era shows both the capacity of democratic systems to reform themselves and the limits of those reforms (especially for Black Americans and agricultural workers).

docs.title: Key Content — Unit 1
docs.text:
Reform What It Did When Sherman Antitrust Act (1890) Gave federal government power to break up monopolies 1890 (weakly enforced until TR) 16th Amendment Authorized federal income tax 1913 17th Amendment Direct election of US Senators (was state legislature appointment) 1913 Federal Reserve Act Created central banking system 1913 Clayton Antitrust Act Strengthened antitrust; exempted unions 1914 Pure Food and Drug Act Required labeling; banned adulterated food 1906 18th Amendment (Prohibition) Banned alcohol 1919 19th Amendment Women's suffrage 1920

What did NOT change: Black voting rights (suppressed by Jim Crow); labor rights for agricultural/domestic workers; immigration restriction (Chinese Exclusion Act already in place)

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.3 is about evaluating democratic institutions — not just listing reforms. The key question: did the political system actually change the distribution of power, or did it manage the symptoms?

Example argument move:

"The Progressive Era produced real democratic reforms — direct election of senators, women's suffrage, antitrust enforcement. But it systematically excluded Black Americans from its benefits, which reveals whose voices were considered part of 'the people' the reforms were for."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection W.3 — Explanatory Explain how Progressive Era reforms changed American democracy W.4 — Argument Argue whether the Progressive Era's reforms addressed the right problems

docs.title: Connect to Studio Packets
docs.text:
• USH-01 — When the System Fails

US.6_12.3 Unit 1 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.4 Social & Economic Change · Unit 1 Industrial Revolution
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.4 — Social and Economic Change Unit: 1 — Industrial Revolution & Progressive Era (1870s–1920) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did industrialization change American society? Who benefited, who was harmed, and how did different groups respond to the new economic order?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: Industrialization didn't just change how goods were made — it changed how people lived. The shift from farm to factory created a new working class, drew millions of immigrants to American cities, produced new forms of poverty alongside new wealth, and created the conditions for labor organizing, muckraker journalism, and the Progressive movement. This is the standard where social history lives.

docs.title: Key Content — Unit 1
docs.text:
Group Experience of Industrialization Industrial workers 12-hour days, dangerous conditions, no safety net, low wages, child labor Immigrants Often took hardest, lowest-paid jobs; faced discrimination; formed ethnic communities Women Limited to domestic work or low-wage factory jobs; suffrage movement builds Farmers Squeezed by railroad monopolies and falling crop prices; Populist movement Black Americans Jim Crow in South; limited factory access in North; Great Migration begins later Wealthy industrialists Enormous wealth accumulation; social status; political influence

Key social patterns: - Urbanization — cities grow from immigration + rural migration - Rise of the middle class — new professional and managerial jobs - Child labor — 2 million children working in mines, factories, fields in 1900 - Settlement houses — Jane Addams' Hull House; social reform through community organizing

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.4 is about who bears the costs of economic change. The richest argument move is comparing groups: who benefited from the same system that harmed someone else, and what does that reveal about how power works?

Example argument move:

"Carnegie made $480 million in his lifetime. The steelworkers who produced his wealth averaged $10 a week for 12-hour days. This wasn't a coincidence — it was a business model. Understanding industrialization means understanding whose sacrifice produced whose wealth."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.9 — Argument Analysis Sinclair's The Jungle as argument about who bears the cost of industrialization W.4 — Argument Argue whether the benefits of industrialization justified its human costs W.3 — Explanatory Explain how industrialization changed the lives of a specific group

docs.text: • The Jungle — Sinclair — social conditions in factory system; muckraker argument

docs.text:

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.5 Causes & Effects · Unit 1 Industrial Revolution
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.5 — Causes and Effects of Historical Events Unit: 1 — Industrial Revolution & Progressive Era (1870s–1920) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What caused the Industrial Revolution in America? What did it produce — intended and unintended consequences?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: Causal chains in history are rarely simple. The Industrial Revolution had multiple causes (technology, capital, natural resources, immigration, policy) and produced effects that rippled across economics, politics, society, and culture. US.6_12.5 asks you to trace those chains — and to think about which causes were most important and which effects were most consequential.

docs.title: Causes of American Industrialization
docs.text: Cause Explanation Natural resources Coal, iron ore, timber — abundant and accessible Capital accumulation Post-Civil War wealth; investment in production Immigration Millions of workers willing to take low-wage industrial jobs Railroads Created national market; enabled mass distribution Patent system Protected invention; incentivized innovation Laissez-faire policy Government largely stayed out; no regulations to slow growth Post-Civil War contracts Federal demand for goods during war jump-started production

docs.title: Effects of Industrialization
docs.text: Effect Type Time Frame Massive wealth creation Economic Immediate Labor exploitation / unsafe conditions Social Immediate Urban growth Social/Demographic 1870–1920 Immigration surge Demographic 1880–1920 Monopolies and trusts Economic/Political 1880–1910 Progressive movement / labor reform Political 1890–1920 Environmental degradation Environmental Ongoing American economic dominance by 1900 Economic/Global By 1900

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.5 is where you show historical thinking, not just knowledge. The most powerful move: argue which cause was most important or which effect was most consequential — and defend that with evidence.

Example argument move:

"Immigration was the most indispensable cause of industrialization — not because workers were needed (any population would have provided workers), but because the specific conditions of immigration created a labor force with few alternatives and limited political power, which allowed the exploitation that made industrial profits possible."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection W.3 — Explanatory Explain the causes of industrialization or trace a specific effect W.4 — Argument Argue which cause or effect was most significant R.9 — Argument Analysis Analyze how Green (CrashCourse) constructs his causal argument

docs.title: Connect to CrashCourse
docs.text:
• Ep USH-06 — Industrial Revolution

US.6_12.5 Unit 1 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.6 Past to Present · Unit 1 Industrial Revolution
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.6 — Connecting History to the Present Unit: 1 — Industrial Revolution & Progressive Era (1870s–1920) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What from the Industrial Revolution is still present today? How does understanding this period help explain the world students live in?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The Industrial Revolution didn't end — it continued and accelerated. The companies, regulations, economic patterns, and political debates it created are still active. The debates of the Progressive Era (corporate power vs. democracy; worker protections vs. economic freedom; immigration and national identity) are live debates right now.

docs.title: Then → Now Connections
docs.text: Then (1870–1920) Now Standard Oil monopoly → Sherman Antitrust Act FTC antitrust cases against Google, Amazon, Meta Meatpacking conditions → Pure Food and Drug Act FDA regulates food and drugs today Child labor in factories Child labor still exists globally; US debates minimum wage ages 12-hour workdays, no weekends 40-hour work week, OSHA, minimum wage — all result of labor movement Immigration surge from Southern/Eastern Europe Immigration debates about Southern/Western hemisphere "Robber barons" vs. "Captains of Industry" debate Billionaire class today — are Elon Musk/Jeff Bezos Carnegies? Muckraker journalism Investigative journalism — ProPublica, NYT, WaPo Social Darwinism ("the rich deserve it") Meritocracy discourse today

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.6 is your chance to make history feel relevant. The strongest move: find a specific present-day situation that directly connects to a Unit 1 dynamic, and argue that understanding the history illuminates the present.

Example argument move:

"Amazon's warehouse workers today face conditions that muckrakers like Upton Sinclair would recognize — long hours, surveillance, injury rates that exceed industry averages, and aggressive resistance to unionization. Understanding the Industrial Revolution means understanding that this pattern isn't new, and that the Progressive Era shows both how workers can change it and why change is so slow."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection W.4 — Argument Argue that a current situation is best understood through an Industrial Era lens R.9 — Argument Analysis Find and analyze a contemporary article that uses Industrial Era history

US.6_12.6 Unit 1 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.1 Science & Technology · Unit 2 Imperialism & WWI
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.1 — Science, Technology, and Society Unit: 2 — Imperialism & World War I (1890s–1919) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did new technologies shape American imperialism and the conduct of WWI? What did these technologies make possible — and what did they make terrible?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: Technology enabled American imperial expansion (steam-powered navies, telegraph cables, weapons) and made WWI uniquely destructive (machine guns, poison gas, artillery barrages, trench warfare). The same industrial technology that built American prosperity was applied to warfare — with results that shocked everyone who experienced it.

docs.title: Key Content — Unit 2
docs.text: Technology Role in Imperialism Role in WWI Steam-powered warships Projected US military power into Pacific and Caribbean Naval warfare; submarine warfare Telegraph / undersea cables Enabled coordination of empire; US controlled key cables Military command and communication Machine gun — Made frontal assault suicidal; created stalemate Poison gas (chlorine, mustard) — New horror; violated previous rules of war Artillery — 60% of WWI casualties; destroyed landscape Airplanes — Reconnaissance; early bombing; air combat Submarines — German U-boats sink merchant ships; key US entry trigger

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
Technology in Unit 2 is most powerful as a cause-and-effect argument: the same industrial capacity that made America a great power also made the war it entered into the most destructive in human history.

Example argument move:

"The industrialization of warfare produced a paradox: the countries with the most advanced technology fought to a bloody stalemate for four years. Machine guns made attack suicidal; artillery reduced villages to rubble; poison gas violated every previous understanding of acceptable combat. Technology didn't produce quick victory — it produced the Western Front."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.9 — Argument Analysis Dulce et Decorum Est as argument against the technology of industrialized war W.4 — Argument Argue whether technological advantage justified American entry into WWI

docs.title: Connect to Reading Library
docs.text: • Dulce et Decorum Est — Owen — technology of war; R.9

docs.text:

US.6_12.1 Unit 2 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.2 Ideas, Beliefs & Ideology · Unit 2 Imperialism & WWI
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.2 — Ideas, Beliefs, and Institutions Unit: 2 — Imperialism & World War I (1890s–1919) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What ideas justified American imperialism and entry into WWI? How did war propaganda work — and what beliefs did it create or reinforce?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: American imperialism was justified by a cluster of ideas: racial hierarchy ("the White Man's Burden"), Manifest Destiny extended beyond US borders, Social Darwinism applied internationally (strong nations should dominate weak ones), and religious mission ("civilizing" non-Christian peoples). WWI was sold to the American public through propaganda — posters, speeches, films — that created a simple moral story out of a complicated geopolitical conflict.

docs.title: Key Ideas — Unit 2
docs.text: Idea Core Claim Used To Justify "White Man's Burden" Racial hierarchy; "civilized" nations have duty to govern "lesser" peoples Philippine annexation, Caribbean interventions Monroe Doctrine / Roosevelt Corollary US has right to intervene in Latin America Cuba, Panama, Haiti, Dominican Republic Manifest Destiny (extended) America's expansion is divinely ordained Pacific territories; Philippines Wilsonian idealism WWI is a war to "make the world safe for democracy" US entry into WWI; Fourteen Points War propaganda Simple moral story: we are good, they are evil Draft, war bonds, public support for war Anti-war / Sedition Some Americans believed war was corporate-driven Espionage Act prosecutions

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.2 in Unit 2 is about interrogating official justifications. The most powerful argument: the ideas used to justify imperialism and war were not neutral beliefs — they were arguments that served specific interests.

Example argument move:

"Kipling's 'White Man's Burden' wasn't just a poem — it was an argument for a policy position. It claimed that racial hierarchy was natural and imperial domination was a moral obligation. H.T. Johnson's rebuttal showed that the same facts could produce the opposite conclusion. Ideology is always an argument."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.9 — Argument Analysis Kipling + Johnson as competing arguments about the same facts W.4 — Argument Argue whether American imperialism's ideological justifications were honest

docs.title: Connect to Reading Library
docs.text: • Kipling + H.T. Johnson — competing arguments about imperial ideology; R.9 + W.4

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.3 Political Systems · Unit 2 Imperialism & WWI
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.3 — Political Systems and Democratic Institutions Unit: 2 — Imperialism & World War I (1890s–1919) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did imperialism and WWI change American political institutions? What happened to civil liberties during wartime — and what does that reveal about how democracy works under pressure?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: Wartime tests democratic institutions. During WWI, the US government suppressed dissent through the Espionage and Sedition Acts, used propaganda to manufacture consent, and prosecuted anti-war activists. The tension between national security and civil liberties — which recurs in American history (WWII internment, McCarthy era, post-9/11 Patriot Act) — is first clearly visible here.

docs.title: Key Content — Unit 2
docs.text: Event / Law What It Did Democratic Implications Platt Amendment (1901) Made Cuba a US protectorate after Spanish-American War Sovereignty of "liberated" nations limited by US Roosevelt Corollary (1904) US claims right to intervene in Latin America Monroe Doctrine as imperial claim, not just defensive Espionage Act (1917) Made it illegal to interfere with military recruitment Anti-war speech criminalized Sedition Act (1918) Made it illegal to criticize the government, war, or flag 1,500+ prosecuted; free speech curtailed Committee on Public Information Government propaganda bureau State-managed public opinion Schenck v. US (1919) Supreme Court upholds Espionage Act; "clear and present danger" First First Amendment ruling; sets 20th century precedent

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.3 in Unit 2 asks: what do democratic governments do when they believe the stakes are high enough — and is that justified? The argument is about the limits of democracy, not just its institutions.

Example argument move:

"The Espionage and Sedition Acts reveal something important about democracy under pressure: when governments believe national survival is at stake, they often treat dissent as the enemy. The question isn't whether this happened — it clearly did — but whether it was justified. Justice Holmes said 'clear and present danger.' Eugene Debs, who was sentenced to 10 years for an anti-war speech, thought otherwise."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection W.4 — Argument Argue whether the Espionage/Sedition Acts were justified wartime measures or civil liberties violations R.9 — Argument Analysis Analyze a WWI propaganda poster or speech as an argument

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.4 Social & Economic Change · Unit 2 Imperialism & WWI
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.4 — Social and Economic Change Unit: 2 — Imperialism & World War I (1890s–1919) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did imperialism and WWI reshape American society? Who moved, who worked, who fought — and what happened to them?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: WWI transformed American society in ways that outlasted the war. Women moved into the industrial workforce in large numbers. Black Americans began the Great Migration northward, escaping Jim Crow and finding war-industry jobs. Immigrants from Southern and Eastern Europe faced new nativist pressure. The war demanded sacrifice from everyone — but not equally.

docs.title: Key Social Changes — Unit 2
docs.text:
Group Experience Women War industries employ women in factories; suffrage movement gains momentum; 19th Amendment (1920) follows Black Americans Great Migration begins (500,000 north 1916–1920); factory jobs; still face discrimination in North Immigrants War-era nativism; German Americans face hostility; immigration restriction builds momentum Working class War production raises wages temporarily; labor rights expand slightly Soldiers 117,000 Americans die; many return with physical/psychological wounds; "shell shock" Colonized peoples Philippines, Puerto Rico, Guam — absorbed into US empire; limited self-determination

Economic effects of WWI: - US shifts from debtor to creditor nation - American businesses profit enormously from Allied war contracts (pre-1917) - War production ends the labor surplus; wages rise temporarily - War industries draw workers from South and rural areas → urbanization accelerates

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.4 in Unit 2 asks whose social position changed and why. The richest argument: the war created opportunities for some groups (women, Black workers) while simultaneously exposing the limits of those opportunities (they weren't structural changes — they were temporary).

Example argument move:

"The Great Migration was one of the most significant demographic shifts in American history — and it happened because of WWI. Black Americans didn't move north because conditions improved; they moved because war industries needed workers and the South's Jim Crow system had become intolerable. The North was better, but not equal. Understanding the Great Migration means understanding both the pull and the limits of that pull."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection W.3 — Explanatory Explain how WWI changed the social position of a specific group R.9 — Argument Analysis Dulce et Decorum Est — argument about what war costs soldiers

docs.title: Connect to Reading Library
docs.text:
• Dulce et Decorum Est — Owen — the human cost of WWI; R.9

US.6_12.4 Unit 2 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.5 Causes & Effects · Unit 2 Imperialism & WWI
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.5 — Causes and Effects of Historical Events Unit: 2 — Imperialism & World War I (1890s–1919) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What caused American imperialism? What caused US entry into WWI? What did each produce?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: Imperialism and WWI both followed from the same underlying cause: American industrial power needed markets and resources beyond its borders. The effects cascaded across politics, society, and international relations — and set up every conflict of the 20th century.

docs.title: Causes of American Imperialism
docs.text: Cause Explanation Economic expansion Industrial overproduction needed overseas markets Alfred Thayer Mahan's sea power theory Naval power = global power; need coaling stations; need colonies Social Darwinism / racial ideology "Civilized" nations have right/duty to govern "lesser" ones Strategic competition Britain, France, Germany building empires; US falls behind or joins Yellow journalism Hearst and Pulitzer newspapers inflame public opinion about Cuba

docs.title: Causes of US Entry into WWI
docs.text: Cause Weight Economic ties to Britain/France Heavy; US banks had loaned Allied powers $2.3 billion Submarine warfare / Lusitania Gradual; Germany sinks US ships Zimmermann Telegram Immediate trigger; Germany proposes Mexico attack US Wilson's idealism "Make the world safe for democracy"

docs.title: Effects — Imperialism
docs.text: Effect Significance Spanish-American War (1898) US gains Cuba, Philippines, Puerto Rico, Guam Anti-imperialist movement Mark Twain, Carnegie argue against empire Philippine-American War (1899–1902) 200,000+ Filipino deaths; brutal suppression of independence movement Panama Canal (1914) Strategic and economic asset; acquired through quasi-imperial means

docs.title: Effects — WWI
docs.text: Effect Significance Treaty of Versailles Punishes Germany; plants seeds of WWII League of Nations Wilson's project; Senate rejects US membership Great Migration Black workers move north Women's suffrage (1920) War contribution argument clinches it Red Scare (1919–20) Anti-communist hysteria; Palmer Raids Immigration restriction (1921, 1924) Nativist backlash; quotas severely limit Eastern European immigration

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.5 in Unit 2 is richest when you trace unintended consequences. The war to end all wars planted the seeds of the next one.

Example argument move:

"The Treaty of Versailles is the clearest example in American history of a military victory producing the conditions for a worse conflict. The US helped win WWI, then refused to join the League of Nations that might have managed the peace. Twenty years later, the consequences arrived."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection W.3 — Explanatory Trace the causes of US entry into WWI or the effects of the Treaty of Versailles W.4 — Argument Argue whether US entry into WWI was the right decision

docs.text:

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.6 Past to Present · Unit 2 Imperialism & WWI
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.6 — Connecting History to the Present Unit: 2 — Imperialism & World War I (1890s–1919) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What from the era of American imperialism and WWI is still present today? How does understanding this period explain current events?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The debates of Unit 2 — when should America use military force? what are the limits of free speech in wartime? what does the US owe the countries it has dominated? — are live debates. American foreign policy, civil liberties law, and immigration policy all trace directly back to this era.

docs.title: Then → Now Connections
docs.text: Then (1890s–1919) Now Monroe Doctrine / Roosevelt Corollary US policy in Latin America still shaped by these frameworks Philippine-American War; annexation debate Puerto Rico's status as unincorporated territory — still unresolved Espionage Act (1917) Still on the books; used to prosecute Edward Snowden "Clear and present danger" (Schenck v. US) First Amendment law today descends from this case War propaganda / Committee on Public Information Government media strategy; embedded journalism in Iraq/Afghanistan Zimmermann Telegram (intelligence intelligence shaping policy) Intelligence community role in foreign policy decisions Immigration restriction (1921, 1924 quotas) Immigration debates; quota system echoes today "Make the world safe for democracy" American foreign policy rhetoric from Wilson to Bush to Obama

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.6 in Unit 2 is about foreign policy continuity. The argument: American imperialism wasn't a phase that ended — it transformed.

Example argument move:

"The Espionage Act of 1917 was passed to stop anti-war speech during WWI. Over 100 years later, it was used to prosecute Edward Snowden for leaking classified information about NSA surveillance. The law's authors couldn't have imagined the internet — but the legal tool they created to suppress dissent is still being used for the same basic purpose."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection W.4 — Argument Argue that a current event is best understood through the lens of American imperialism R.9 — Argument Analysis Find and analyze a contemporary source arguing about US foreign policy

docs.title: Connect to Reading Library
docs.text:
• Kipling + H.T. Johnson — imperial ideology still relevant today
