docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.1 Science & Technology · Unit 3 Roaring 20s & Depression
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.1 — Science, Technology, and Society Unit: 3 — Roaring 20s & Great Depression (1919–1939) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did new technologies define the 1920s boom — and what role did technology play in the crash and the Depression that followed?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The 1920s were the first modern consumer technology decade: radio, automobiles, film, refrigerators, washing machines. Mass production made these goods affordable; consumer credit made them purchasable. The same economic logic that created the boom — buy now, pay later — applied to the stock market and contributed to the crash. The Depression showed that technology creates wealth but not stability.

docs.title: Key Content — Unit 3
docs.text: Technology 1920s Role Darker Side Automobile (Ford Model T) Mass consumer product; suburbs; road culture Displaced horses; changed city design; oil dependency Radio Mass media; news, entertainment, advertising Propaganda tool; homogenizes culture Film (Hollywood) Mass entertainment; celebrity culture Racial segregation in film; propaganda potential Consumer credit Made expensive goods affordable; drove demand When credit collapsed, demand collapsed with it Stock market speculation Technology of leveraged investment (buying on margin) Margin calls amplified the crash Assembly line (Fordism) Made mass production possible Workers become interchangeable; deskilling

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.1 in Unit 3 connects technology to economic cycles. The core argument: the same technology that created the boom contained the seeds of the crash.

Example argument move:

"Consumer credit was the technology that made the 1920s boom possible — and it was the same technology that made the crash catastrophic. When the economy turned, millions of Americans owed money on cars, radios, and appliances they suddenly couldn't afford to keep. The Depression wasn't just a stock market crash; it was a consumer credit crisis."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text:
ELA Standard Connection W.3 — Explanatory Explain how consumer technology drove the 1920s economy W.4 — Argument Argue whether technology caused or merely accelerated the Depression

US.6_12.1 Unit 3 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.2 Ideas, Beliefs & Ideology · Unit 3 Roaring 20s & Depression
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.2 — Ideas, Beliefs, and Institutions Unit: 3 — Roaring 20s & Great Depression (1919–1939) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What competing ideas defined the 1920s — modernism vs. traditionalism, prosperity vs. reform? How did the Depression change what Americans believed about the economy and government?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The 1920s were a decade of cultural war: modernism (evolution, jazz, women's liberation, immigration) vs. traditionalism (Prohibition, the KKK's revival, the Scopes Trial, immigration restriction). The Depression killed the idea that the economy was self-correcting and individual virtue was enough. Keynesian economics — government as economic manager — replaced laissez-faire as the dominant framework.

docs.title: Key Ideas — Unit 3
docs.text: Idea / Movement Core Claim Significance Modernism / Jazz Age Tradition should be challenged; new is better; freedom from Victorian norms Harlem Renaissance; flapper culture; secularism Nativism / KKK revival America is for "real Americans" (white, Protestant, native-born) Immigration acts of 1921/1924; racial terror Fundamentalism (Scopes Trial) Bible is literal truth; evolution threatens society Scopes convicted; modernism wins culturally Prohibition Alcohol is moral evil; government can legislate morality Organized crime grows; eventually repealed (1933) Harlem Renaissance Black art, literature, music as affirmation of dignity and identity Hughes, Hurston, Louis Armstrong, Duke Ellington Keynesianism Government spending can stabilize the economy FDR's New Deal; permanent shift in economic thinking Laissez-faire (Hoover) Economy self-corrects; government should not interfere Discredited by Depression; Hoover loses 1932

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.2 in Unit 3 is about a battle of ideas — and one side losing dramatically.

Example argument move:

"The Depression didn't just destroy wealth — it destroyed a belief system. Hoover's faith in laissez-faire economics — that the market would self-correct and charity would handle the rest — was tested by reality and failed. FDR won in 1932 not just because of the Depression, but because Hoover had no ideological response to it. The Depression made Keynesianism possible."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text:
ELA Standard Connection R.9 — Argument Analysis Analyze FDR's Fireside Chats as ideology-building argument W.4 — Argument Argue whether modernism or traditionalism better represented America in the 1920s

US.6_12.2 Unit 3 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.3 Political Systems · Unit 3 Roaring 20s & Depression
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.3 — Political Systems and Democratic Institutions Unit: 3 — Roaring 20s & Great Depression (1919–1939) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did political institutions respond to the prosperity of the 1920s and the crisis of the Depression? What did the New Deal do to the relationship between citizens and government?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The New Deal permanently expanded the role of the federal government in American life. Before 1933, the federal government did not provide unemployment insurance, old-age pensions, or direct economic regulation at scale. After the New Deal, it did. This was a structural change to American democracy — and it was contested then and is contested now.

docs.title: Key Content — Unit 3
docs.text:
Program / Reform What It Did Significance Emergency Banking Act (1933) Closed failed banks; FDR's first act Restored confidence in banking system FDIC (Federal Deposit Insurance) Insured bank deposits up to $5,000 Bank runs become rare; still exists today Social Security Act (1935) Old-age pensions, unemployment insurance First US social safety net; still active Wagner Act (1935) Protected workers' right to organize unions Enabled labor movement growth Securities and Exchange Commission Regulated stock market Prevented some speculation abuses Supreme Court battle (1937) FDR tries to "pack" Court with pro-New Deal justices Court backs down; FDR wins; separation of powers tested 20th Amendment (1933) Shortened lame-duck period Response to Hoover's paralysis

What the New Deal did NOT do: End the Depression (WWII did); include Black workers in most programs (agricultural/domestic workers excluded); challenge racial segregation

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.3 in Unit 3 is about a permanent shift in what government does.

Example argument move:

"Social Security was the most consequential political reform of the 20th century — not because it solved the Depression, but because it permanently changed what Americans expected from government. Before 1935, old age meant depending on family or charity. After 1935, it meant a government check. That expectation has never been reversed."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection W.3 — Explanatory Explain how the New Deal changed the role of federal government W.4 — Argument Argue whether the New Deal's expansion of government was justified

docs.title: Connect to CrashCourse
docs.text:

US.6_12.3 Unit 3 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.4 Social & Economic Change · Unit 3 Roaring 20s & Depression
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.4 — Social and Economic Change Unit: 3 — Roaring 20s & Great Depression (1919–1939) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did the prosperity of the 1920s and the collapse of the Depression change American society? Who rose, who fell, and who was left behind?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The 1920s produced enormous wealth and a new consumer culture — but not for everyone equally. Then the Depression erased much of that wealth for most Americans while the wealthy lost proportionally less. The Dust Bowl added an environmental disaster on top of an economic one. Social safety nets didn't exist, which meant the poor had nowhere to fall to.

docs.title: Key Social Changes — Unit 3
docs.text:
Group 1920s Depression Middle class New consumer goods; rising incomes; suburb growth Lost savings in bank failures; unemployment Working class Wages rose slightly; limited union power 25% unemployment; breadlines; Hoovervilles Black Americans Harlem Renaissance; Great Migration continues; still face intense racism Last hired, first fired; excluded from New Deal programs Women New freedoms (flapper culture, more workforce participation) Told to give jobs to men; domestic roles reasserted Farmers Struggled even in 1920s (crop prices fall post-WWI) Dust Bowl adds catastrophe; 500,000 displaced Immigrants Restricted by 1921/1924 acts; ethnic communities under pressure Further marginalized; some deported

The Harlem Renaissance (1920s): - Langston Hughes, Zora Neale Hurston, Countee Cullen — literary movement - Louis Armstrong, Duke Ellington — jazz as Black American art form - Political dimension: Harlem Renaissance as assertion of dignity and cultural power

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.4 in Unit 3 is about how crises reveal existing inequality.

Example argument move:

"The Depression didn't create inequality — it exposed it. Black Americans were the first fired because they were already in the most precarious positions. Women were pushed back to domestic roles because their workforce participation had always been conditional on male approval. Crises don't produce inequality from nowhere; they accelerate patterns that were already there."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection W.3 — Explanatory Explain how the Depression affected a specific group differently W.4 — Argument Argue whether the 1920s prosperity was shared broadly enough to be called prosperity

docs.title: Connect to CrashCourse
docs.text:
• Ep USH-09 — Great Depression

US.6_12.4 Unit 3 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.5 Causes & Effects · Unit 3 Roaring 20s & Depression
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.5 — Causes and Effects of Historical Events Unit: 3 — Roaring 20s & Great Depression (1919–1939) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What caused the Great Depression? What did it produce — and what ended it?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The Great Depression had multiple, interconnected causes — no single factor explains it. Its effects reshaped American government, society, and culture in ways that lasted for decades. Understanding the Depression requires holding multiple causes simultaneously and tracing effects across different domains.

docs.title: Causes of the Great Depression
docs.text: Cause Explanation Stock market speculation / Black Tuesday Buying on margin; overleveraged investors; crash triggers panic Bank failures 9,000 banks fail 1930–33; deposits uninsured; savings wiped out Overproduction / underconsumption Factories producing more than Americans could afford to buy Agricultural crisis Farmers had been struggling since 1920; crop prices fell post-WWI Smoot-Hawley Tariff (1930) Raised import tariffs; trading partners retaliate; international trade collapses Federal Reserve contraction Fed raised interest rates during the crisis; made it worse Consumer credit collapse Buying on credit stopped when confidence fell; demand collapsed

docs.title: Effects of the Great Depression
docs.text: Effect Significance 25% unemployment (peak 1933) 1 in 4 workers without jobs Bank failures / savings lost Middle-class savings wiped out Dust Bowl / migration 500,000 displaced; Okies to California Hoovervilles Shantytowns of unemployed; visible public poverty New Deal Permanent expansion of federal government WWII ends it War production employs everyone; not New Deal programs Social Security, FDIC Permanent safety net built from Depression experience Political shift FDR coalition (unions, Black voters, immigrants, South) shapes Democratic Party for 40 years

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.5 in Unit 3 invites a causal argument about complexity.

Example argument move:

"The single most important cause of the Depression was the Federal Reserve's decision to raise interest rates after the crash. By contracting the money supply when the economy needed liquidity, the Fed turned a serious recession into a decade-long catastrophe. Economist Milton Friedman argued this 40 years later. The Fed has never made the same mistake again — which is why recent recessions haven't become depressions."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection W.3 — Explanatory Explain the multiple causes of the Depression and how they interacted W.4 — Argument Argue which cause was most responsible for the Depression's severity

docs.title: Connect to CrashCourse
docs.text:
• Ep USH-09 — Great Depression

US.6_12.5 Unit 3 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.6 Past to Present · Unit 3 Roaring 20s & Depression
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.6 — Connecting History to the Present Unit: 3 — Roaring 20s & Great Depression (1919–1939) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What from the 1920s and Depression is still present today? How does understanding this period explain current economic and political debates?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The New Deal's legacy is everywhere: Social Security, FDIC, SEC, labor law, unemployment insurance. Every political debate about the size of government, the role of regulation, and the social safety net is in some ways a debate about the New Deal. The 1929 crash's lessons shaped the 2008 response.

docs.title: Then → Now Connections
docs.text: Then (1919–1939) Now Social Security (1935) 70 million Americans receive Social Security today; central political issue FDIC bank insurance Every US bank account insured up to $250,000 SEC stock market regulation Stock market regulation; insider trading law Wagner Act / labor unions Union rights still governed by NLRA; union membership debates Dust Bowl / environmental disaster Climate-driven agricultural disruption; drought cycles 1929 crash → 1930s Depression 2008 financial crisis; Fed learning from 1930s mistakes Hoovervilles / visible poverty Homelessness crisis in US cities Immigration restriction (1924 quotas) Immigration policy debates; quota system influence Keynesian economics (deficit spending) COVID relief spending, infrastructure bills — all Keynesian Harlem Renaissance Ongoing centrality of Black American art and culture

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.6 in Unit 3 is about New Deal permanence and economic cycle repetition.

Example argument move:

"The 2008 financial crisis was the first major test of whether the US had learned from 1929. The Fed did the opposite of what it did in the 1930s — it flooded the economy with money instead of contracting the supply. The bank bailouts were politically unpopular but economically sound for the same reason the New Deal was: the alternative was worse. Understanding 1929 is how policymakers avoided repeating it in 2008."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text:

US.6_12.6 Unit 3 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.1 Science & Technology · Unit 4 WWII
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.1 — Science, Technology, and Society Unit: 4 — World War II (1939–1945) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did technology shape WWII — and what moral questions did new weapons create?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: WWII was the most technologically intensive war in history to that point. Industrial capacity determined the outcome: the US could outproduce Germany and Japan combined. But technology also produced the atomic bomb — a weapon so destructive it raised questions about whether there were limits on what humanity should build.

docs.title: Key Technologies — Unit 4
docs.text:
Technology Role Moral Dimension Mass production / war mobilization US factories outproduce Axis; GDP doubles Civilian economy converted to war machine Radar Early warning; air defense; naval advantage — Codebreaking (Enigma / Magic) Allies read Axis communications Intelligence as decisive weapon Aircraft (bombers, fighters) Strategic bombing; air superiority Firebombing of Dresden, Tokyo; civilian casualties Atomic bomb (Manhattan Project) Destroys Hiroshima and Nagasaki; Japan surrenders 200,000+ dead; raises question of limits V-2 rockets (German) First ballistic missiles Precursor to nuclear delivery; Cold War arms race

Manhattan Project: US spent $2 billion (1940s dollars); 130,000 people worked on it; only a handful knew what it was building. First test July 16, 1945. Hiroshima August 6; Nagasaki August 9; Japan surrenders August 15.

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.1 in Unit 4 is about the moral responsibility that comes with technological capability.

Example argument move:

"The atomic bomb worked exactly as designed — which is precisely the problem. A weapon that kills 200,000 people in two days raises a question technology cannot answer: just because we can build something, does that mean we should use it? Truman said yes. Many of the scientists who built it said no. The debate has never ended."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection W.4 — Argument Argue whether the use of atomic bombs on Japan was justified R.9 — Argument Analysis Analyze FDR's Four Freedoms speech — what technology is being mobilized for

US.6_12.1 Unit 4 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.2 Ideas, Beliefs & Ideology · Unit 4 WWII
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.2 — Ideas, Beliefs, and Institutions Unit: 4 — World War II (1939–1945) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What ideologies drove WWII — fascism, democracy, imperial Japan's state ideology? How did the US use ideas to mobilize its population, and what contradictions did the war expose?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: WWII was framed as an ideological war: democracy vs. fascism. This framing was largely accurate — but it created a contradiction. The US was fighting against racial ideology in Europe while practicing racial segregation at home. The gap between the war's stated ideals and American reality was a central tension that the Civil Rights Movement would later exploit.

docs.title: Key Ideologies — Unit 4
docs.text: Ideology Core Claims Who Held It Fascism (Nazism) Racial hierarchy; national superiority; authoritarian state; antisemitism Nazi Germany Imperial Japan's ideology Japanese racial/cultural superiority; Asian co-prosperity sphere; emperor as divine Imperial Japan American war ideology Democracy vs. tyranny; freedom vs. slavery; "Four Freedoms" US government; public American contradiction Fighting fascism while practicing racism; Japanese American internment — Anti-fascist universalism All humans have equal dignity; Holocaust reveals cost of racial ideology Postwar foundation for UN UDHR

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.2 in Unit 4 is most powerful when you hold the contradiction.

Example argument move:

"FDR said America was fighting for 'freedom from fear' and 'freedom from want' for all people. Simultaneously, his administration imprisoned 120,000 Japanese Americans without charges, maintained a segregated military, and turned away Jewish refugees fleeing the Holocaust. Understanding WWII as an ideological war means grappling with the gap between what America claimed to stand for and what it actually did."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.9 — Argument Analysis FDR's Four Freedoms — analyze the argument; what does he claim? who is the audience? R.8 — Literary Elements Night — ideology made flesh; what fascism looks like from inside its logic W.4 — Argument Argue whether the US was truly fighting for freedom given its domestic practices

docs.text:

US.6_12.2 Unit 4 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.3 Political Systems · Unit 4 WWII
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.3 — Political Systems and Democratic Institutions Unit: 4 — World War II (1939–1945) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did WWII test American democratic institutions? What decisions were made that expanded or contracted civil liberties — and what were the consequences?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: Wartime always tests democracy. WWII produced the most dramatic civil liberties violation of the 20th century (Japanese American internment) while simultaneously mobilizing democratic institutions for the most successful military effort in American history. The war also set up the postwar international order — the UN, the Nuremberg trials, the UDHR — which reflected democratic values at a global scale.

docs.title: Key Political Events — Unit 4
docs.text: Event Democratic Implications Japanese American Internment (EO 9066, 1942) 120,000 citizens imprisoned without charges; Korematsu v. US (1944) upholds it Office of War Information / propaganda Government manages public information; limits on press freedom War Powers Act Congress delegates enormous authority to executive during wartime Lend-Lease Act FDR aids allies before US entry; executive initiative Nuremberg Trials (1945–46) International law applied to war crimes; "following orders" rejected as defense United Nations (1945) International democratic/security framework; US as founding member Bretton Woods / IMF / World Bank US designs postwar international economic order

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.3 in Unit 4 is about the paradox of protecting democracy by limiting it.

Example argument move:

"Japanese American internment is the clearest example of democratic institutions failing under pressure. The Supreme Court — in Korematsu — upheld imprisonment of citizens based solely on their ancestry. The government's argument was military necessity. But 40 years later, the government admitted there was no military justification. The lesson: democratic institutions can be used to violate democratic principles, and the Supreme Court is not immune."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection W.4 — Argument Argue whether Japanese American internment was justified R.9 — Argument Analysis Analyze FDR's Four Freedoms against the reality of internment

docs.title: Connect to Reading Library
docs.text:

US.6_12.3 Unit 4 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.4 Social & Economic Change · Unit 4 WWII
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.4 — Social and Economic Change Unit: 4 — World War II (1939–1945) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did WWII transform American society? Who moved, who worked, who fought — and what changed permanently vs. what snapped back after the war?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: WWII ended the Depression and created the modern American middle class — but not equally. Women entered the workforce in millions and were sent home when the men returned. Black Americans fought for a country that didn't treat them as equal — and returned to the same inequality. The GI Bill built white middle-class prosperity while largely excluding Black veterans. The war changed everything and left the fundamental inequality untouched.

docs.title: Key Social Changes — Unit 4
docs.text:
Group During the War After the War Women 6 million enter industrial workforce; "Rosie the Riveter" Largely returned to domestic roles; Feminine Mystique builds Black Americans 1.2 million serve in segregated military; Double V campaign Return to Jim Crow; GI Bill largely excludes them; Civil Rights builds Japanese Americans Imprisoned in internment camps; some serve in military from camps Released 1944–45; property lost; eventual reparations (1988) Working class Full employment; wages rise; union membership peaks GI Bill creates college-educated middle class White veterans GI Bill → college → suburbs → middle class Postwar prosperity largely for white men Black veterans Served; returned; denied GI Bill benefits in South Radicalized; Civil Rights Movement builds from veteran base

GI Bill (1944): Provided college tuition, low-interest home loans, and job training to veterans. Transformed American society — but banks and universities discriminated, so Black veterans received far less benefit than white veterans.

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.4 in Unit 4 is about the gap between promise and reality.

Example argument move:

"The GI Bill is often called the greatest social legislation in American history. For white veterans, it was — it created the suburban middle class. For Black veterans, it was largely inaccessible: banks refused mortgages in Black neighborhoods, universities refused admission, and local VA offices in the South simply didn't serve Black veterans. The same law produced completely different outcomes depending on race."

US.6_12.4 Unit 4 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.5 Causes & Effects · Unit 4 WWII
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.5 — Causes and Effects of Historical Events Unit: 4 — World War II (1939–1945) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What caused WWII and US entry into it? What did the war produce — intended and unintended?

docs.title: Causes of WWII / US Entry
docs.text: Cause Explanation Treaty of Versailles (1919) Punitive terms humiliated Germany; economic devastation; Hitler exploits resentment Great Depression (global) Destroyed faith in liberal democracy; made authoritarian "solutions" attractive Rise of fascism (Italy 1922, Germany 1933) Exploited economic despair and nationalist grievance Appeasement policy Britain/France allow Hitler's territorial expansion hoping to avoid war; Munich 1938 Invasion of Poland (Sept 1, 1939) Britain and France declare war on Germany Pearl Harbor (Dec 7, 1941) Japan attacks US Pacific fleet; US declares war on Japan; Germany declares war on US Atlantic Charter (1941) US and Britain align before formal US entry

docs.title: Effects of WWII
docs.text: Effect Domain Significance 60+ million dead (global) Human Largest death toll in human history Holocaust — 6 million Jews murdered Human Genocide as deliberate state policy Atomic bombs; Japan surrenders Military/Political New era of nuclear weapons US becomes global superpower Geopolitical Britain, France, USSR weakened; US economy intact Cold War begins Geopolitical WWII alliance splits into two-superpower world United Nations founded (1945) International New framework for international law and cooperation Nuremberg Principles Legal International law of war crimes; "following orders" rejected Decolonization wave Global WWII weakens European empires; independence movements accelerate American economic dominance Economic US produces 50% of world GDP in 1945 GI Bill / middle class Domestic Postwar prosperity; suburbs; college enrollment explodes

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.5 in Unit 4 invites a multi-level causal argument.

Example argument move:

"The most consequential unintended effect of WWII was the Cold War. The US and USSR had been suspicious allies. The war gave them both reason to maintain large military establishments and created a power vacuum in Europe that both rushed to fill. Nobody planned the Cold War — it was the war's most important unintended consequence."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection W.3 — Explanatory Trace the causal chain from WWI → Depression → WWII W.4 — Argument Argue which effect of WWII was most consequential

  • Ep WH-41 — WWII Part 2

US.6_12.5 Unit 4 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.6 Past to Present · Unit 4 WWII
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.6 — Connecting History to the Present Unit: 4 — World War II (1939–1945) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What from WWII is still present today? How does understanding this period explain the postwar world students live in?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The world order students live in was designed in 1945 by the United States. The UN, NATO, IMF, World Bank, Bretton Woods currency system, nuclear deterrence, the Nuremberg Principles — all are WWII products. Every international institution that shapes current events was built in response to the catastrophe of the war.

docs.title: Then → Now Connections
docs.text: Then (1939–1945) Now United Nations (1945) Still the primary international forum; 193 member states Nuremberg Trials / international law International Criminal Court; war crimes prosecutions (Putin, etc.) NATO (1949) Still active; Russia-Ukraine war tests it Nuclear weapons (1945) 9 nations have nuclear weapons; nonproliferation treaties Korematsu v. US (1944) Cited in Muslim travel ban cases (2018) GI Bill model Student loan debate; college affordability Holocaust / genocide "Never again" — used to justify interventions (Bosnia, Rwanda) Holocaust denial Remains live issue; antisemitism monitoring Japanese American reparations (1988) Model for reparations debates today "The Greatest Generation" mythology How wars are remembered; veteran treatment debates

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.6 in Unit 4 is about institutional inheritance.

Example argument move:

"When Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022, the international response ran entirely through institutions built in 1945: UN Security Council debate, NATO Article 5 discussions, ICC war crimes investigations, economic sanctions coordinated through Bretton Woods institutions. The world was responding to a 21st century war using tools designed after a 20th century one. Understanding WWII is understanding the architecture of the current world."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.9 — Argument Analysis UN UDHR as argument — what rights does it claim, and for whom? W.4 — Argument Argue whether a current international institution is serving its original purpose

docs.title: Connect to Reading Library
docs.text:
• UN Declaration of Human Rights — WWII's primary ideological legacy; R.9

US.6_12.6 Unit 4 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.1 Science & Technology · Unit 5 Cold War
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.1 — Science, Technology, and Society Unit: 5 — Cold War (1945–1991) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did technology shape the Cold War? What did the nuclear arms race, the space race, and new surveillance technologies mean for American society?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The Cold War was fundamentally a technological competition. Both superpowers raced to build bigger bombs, launch satellites, develop missiles, and intercept each other's communications. This competition produced enormous technological innovation — and an existential threat. The Cold War shows technology as both achievement and danger simultaneously.

docs.title: Key Technologies — Unit 5
docs.text: Technology Cold War Role Social Effect Nuclear weapons / H-bomb Mutual Assured Destruction (MAD); existential deterrence Permanent anxiety; civil defense culture; "duck and cover" ICBMs (Intercontinental Ballistic Missiles) Delivery system for nuclear weapons; makes oceans irrelevant Arms race; arms control treaties Sputnik (1957) USSR launches first satellite; US shocked Space race; NASA founded; science education funded U-2 spy planes High-altitude reconnaissance; surveillance technology Powers incident (1960); escalates Cold War Television Mass communication; Cold War propaganda; Vietnam War footage Living-room war; shapes public opinion Computer / ARPANET Military communication network; foundation of internet Cold War produces the internet Nuclear submarines Second-strike capability; undetectable; stabilizes MAD Permanent military presence underwater

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.1 in Unit 5 is about the relationship between technology and fear.

Example argument move:

"The internet was built by ARPA (Advanced Research Projects Agency) to maintain military communication after a nuclear attack. The fear of nuclear war produced one of the most transformative technologies in human history. The Cold War didn't just threaten civilization — it also accidentally created the infrastructure civilization now runs on."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.8 — Literary Elements Fahrenheit 451 and 1984 as responses to Cold War technology and surveillance W.4 — Argument Argue whether the nuclear arms race made America safer or more dangerous

docs.title: Connect to Reading Library
docs.text:
• Fahrenheit 451 — Bradbury — R.8; technology and conformism
  • 1984 — Orwell — R.8; surveillance state

docs.title: Connect to CrashCourse
docs.text:
• Ep WH-42 — The Cold War

US.6_12.1 Unit 5 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.2 Ideas, Beliefs & Ideology · Unit 5 Cold War
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.2 — Ideas, Beliefs, and Institutions Unit: 5 — Cold War (1945–1991) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What ideologies drove the Cold War? How did anti-communism shape American culture, politics, and civil liberties — and what did American literature say about it?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The Cold War was an ideological war: capitalism + democracy vs. communism + authoritarianism. But the American side's commitment to democracy was tested by McCarthyism — a period of political persecution that used fear of communism to destroy careers and silence dissent. The literature of the Cold War (1984, Fahrenheit 451) responded to exactly this — the danger of conformism and the suppression of thought.

docs.title: Key Ideologies — Unit 5
docs.text:
Ideology Core Claims Effect on America Anti-communism / containment Communism must be stopped everywhere it tries to spread Korea, Vietnam, proxy wars worldwide McCarthyism Communist sympathizers are everywhere; loyalty must be proven 1950–54; Hollywood blacklist; careers destroyed; civil liberties curtailed Domino theory If one country goes communist, neighbors follow Justification for Vietnam Massive retaliation Threaten nuclear response to Soviet aggression Nuclear arms race escalation Mutually Assured Destruction Both sides destroyed in nuclear war → nobody launches Deterrence strategy; stable but terrifying Liberalism vs. conservatism Cold War era reshapes both parties GOP becomes anti-communist party; Democrats split

The literary response: - 1984 (1949) — Orwell writes about Soviet-style totalitarianism but the critique applies to any conformist state - Fahrenheit 451 (1953) — Bradbury responds directly to McCarthyism and book-burning - Both books became central Cold War texts — and are still assigned today

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.2 in Unit 5 is about ideology creating its own dangers.

Example argument move:

"McCarthyism shows what happens when the ideology of fighting tyranny becomes tyrannical itself. McCarthy destroyed careers based on accusation alone, in the name of protecting freedom. Fahrenheit 451 and 1984 were written in response to exactly this pattern — the book-burners always believe they're burning books to protect something. That's the ideology of McCarthyism."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.8 — Literary Elements 1984 and Fahrenheit 451 — analyze how each author uses literary elements to embody Cold War fear W.4 — Argument Argue whether McCarthyism was a greater threat to American democracy than communism

docs.title: Connect to Reading Library
docs.text:
• 1984 — Orwell — R.8 + US.6_12.2
  • Fahrenheit 451 — Bradbury — R.8 + US.6_12.2

docs.title: Connect to Studio Packets
docs.text:
• USH-02 — Literature of Fear

US.6_12.2 Unit 5 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.3 Political Systems · Unit 5 Cold War
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.3 — Political Systems and Democratic Institutions Unit: 5 — Cold War (1945–1991) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did the Cold War reshape American political institutions? What new powers did the executive branch claim — and what happened to checks and balances?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The Cold War produced a permanent national security state. The CIA, NSA, Pentagon, and the military-industrial complex all grew from Cold War imperatives. The executive branch accumulated enormous power — to wage secret wars, conduct surveillance, and override Congress — justified by the permanent emergency of superpower competition. This concentration of power is still with us.

docs.title: Key Institutional Changes — Unit 5
docs.text: Institution / Law What It Created Significance National Security Act (1947) Created CIA, NSC, Joint Chiefs; unified military Permanent intelligence/security apparatus Truman Doctrine (1947) US will support any government threatened by communism Open-ended commitment; proxy wars worldwide Marshall Plan (1948) US rebuilds Western Europe economically Soft power; economic containment of communism McCarthyism (1950–54) Senate hearings; accusations of communist sympathy Civil liberties crisis; "Are you now or have you ever been..." HUAC / Hollywood blacklist House investigation of communist influence in culture Artists, writers, actors blacklisted; free speech curtailed Military-industrial complex Eisenhower's warning (1961) — defense contractors + military + Congress Permanent lobbying for military spending War Powers Resolution (1973) Attempted to limit presidential war-making Executive largely ignored it

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.3 in Unit 5 is about institutional drift — powers created for emergencies that never go away.

Example argument move:

"Eisenhower, who built the national security state, warned in his farewell address about the 'military-industrial complex' — the alliance between defense contractors, the military, and Congress that would perpetually justify military spending. He was right. The US now spends more on defense than the next 10 countries combined. Understanding the Cold War is understanding how an emergency security apparatus becomes permanent."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.8 — Literary Elements 1984 — the political system Orwell describes; how does he make it believable? W.4 — Argument Argue whether McCarthyism or Soviet communism posed a greater threat to American democracy

docs.title: Connect to Reading Library
docs.text:
• 1984 — Orwell

US.6_12.3 Unit 5 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.4 Social & Economic Change · Unit 5 Cold War
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.4 — Social and Economic Change Unit: 5 — Cold War (1945–1991) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did the Cold War reshape American society and economy? What did the postwar boom look like — and who was included?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The postwar era (1945–1973) was the longest sustained period of economic growth in American history — but it was built on specific foundations: the GI Bill, cheap suburban housing, a booming manufacturing economy, and the exclusion of Black Americans from most of these benefits. The consumer culture of the 1950s was real, but it was not universal.

docs.title: Key Social Changes — Unit 5
docs.text:
Change What Happened Who Benefited Suburbanization Levittown and suburbs built for returning veterans White families; FHA mortgages explicitly excluded Black homebuyers Baby boom (1946–64) 76 million births; youngest generation ever All groups; but unequal economic foundations Consumer culture TV, cars, appliances; credit cards (1950s) Primarily white middle class Cold War conformism Social pressure to be "normal"; fear of being labeled subversive LGBTQ+ Americans; political dissidents; artists Women's roles "Feminine mystique" — pressure to be housewives; Betty Friedan responds Women pushed back to domestic sphere Black Americans Excluded from suburbs, GI Bill; civil rights movement builds in response — Korean War / Veterans 36,000 Americans die; "forgotten war" Veterans underserved compared to WWII vets

Economic context: - US produces 50% of world GDP in 1945; 25% by 1970 (as Europe/Japan recover) - Union membership peaks at 35% in 1954; provides middle-class wages - Military spending (4–10% of GDP) drives economic growth

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.4 in Unit 5 is about the gap between the 1950s myth and the 1950s reality.

Example argument move:

"The 1950s are remembered as an era of prosperity and normalcy. But that prosperity was built on deliberate exclusion: FHA mortgage policies explicitly denied loans in 'redlined' neighborhoods (majority Black), meaning the suburb boom was for white families only. The prosperity was real; it just wasn't shared."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.8 — Literary Elements Fahrenheit 451 — conformism as social control; what is being suppressed? W.3 — Explanatory Explain why postwar prosperity was experienced differently by different groups

docs.title: Connect to Reading Library
docs.text:
• Fahrenheit 451 — Bradbury — conformism in postwar America; R.8

US.6_12.4 Unit 5 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.5 Causes & Effects · Unit 5 Cold War
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.5 — Causes and Effects of Historical Events Unit: 5 — Cold War (1945–1991) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What caused the Cold War? What were its major effects — on American society, foreign policy, and the international system?

docs.title: Causes of the Cold War
docs.text: Cause Explanation WWII alliance fracture US and USSR were allies of necessity; fundamentally different systems Soviet sphere in Eastern Europe USSR establishes communist governments in occupied nations Nuclear weapons / US monopoly US had bomb (1945); USSR gets it (1949); arms race begins Ideological incompatibility Democracy + capitalism vs. communism + authoritarianism Truman Doctrine (1947) US commits to opposing communist expansion globally Berlin Blockade (1948–49) USSR blockades West Berlin; US airlifts supplies; showdown

docs.title: Key Cold War Events and Effects
docs.text: Event Effect Korean War (1950–53) 36,000 Americans die; 38th parallel restored; "limited war" concept McCarthy era (1950–54) Civil liberties crisis; careers destroyed; conformism enforced Sputnik (1957) Science education reform; NASA; space race Bay of Pigs (1961) Failed CIA invasion of Cuba; US credibility damaged Cuban Missile Crisis (1962) Closest to nuclear war; back-channel deal; "eyeball to eyeball" Vietnam War (1965–73) 58,000 Americans die; massive social upheaval; military credibility damaged Nixon-China (1972) US recognizes China; triangular diplomacy; détente Soviet collapse (1991) Cold War ends; US becomes sole superpower; "end of history" claimed

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.5 in Unit 5 invites argument about who "won" — and what that means.

Example argument move:

"The US 'won' the Cold War, but the winning was complicated. Vietnam cost 58,000 American lives and deeply damaged public trust in government. McCarthyism damaged civil liberties for a decade. The military-industrial complex Eisenhower warned about became permanent. And the Soviet collapse didn't produce the stable democratic world promised — it produced a power vacuum that enabled authoritarian resurgence. Winning had costs."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection W.3 — Explanatory Explain the causes of the Cold War and how it began W.4 — Argument Argue who was more responsible for the Cold War — the US or USSR

docs.title: Connect to CrashCourse
docs.text:
• Ep WH-42 — The Cold War

US.6_12.5 Unit 5 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.6 Past to Present · Unit 5 Cold War
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.6 — Connecting History to the Present Unit: 5 — Cold War (1945–1991) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What from the Cold War is still present today? How does understanding this period explain current geopolitics and domestic politics?

docs.title: Then → Now Connections
docs.text: Then (1945–1991) Now Nuclear weapons / MAD 9 nations have nuclear weapons; nonproliferation treaties under pressure NATO (1949) Still active; Russia-Ukraine war triggers Article 5 debate CIA covert operations Intelligence community role in elections, foreign policy McCarthyism / loyalty oaths Parallels in contemporary political accusations 1984 and Fahrenheit 451 as Cold War texts Still assigned; still relevant to surveillance debates Military-industrial complex US defense spending $800B+/year; defense contractor lobbying Cold War proxy wars Russia-Ukraine, Israel-Palestine — major power competition by proxy US-China competition New Cold War framing; technology competition; Taiwan Soviet collapse → Russian nationalism Putin's revisionism; annexation of Crimea → Ukraine Détente / arms control treaties INF Treaty collapsed 2019; START treaty under pressure

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.6 in Unit 5 is about Cold War frameworks returning.

Example argument move:

"Analysts increasingly describe US-China competition as a 'new Cold War.' The comparison is partly accurate and partly misleading. Accurate: two superpowers with incompatible systems competing for global influence. Misleading: the US and China are economically interdependent in ways the US and USSR never were — which makes the conflict more complicated and the stakes different. Understanding the original Cold War helps you see both why the comparison is tempting and where it breaks down."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.9 — Argument Analysis Find a contemporary article arguing the US-China competition is a "new Cold War" — evaluate the argument W.4 — Argument Argue whether the "new Cold War" framing is accurate

docs.title: Connect to Reading Library
docs.text:
• 1984 — Orwell — surveillance state; still relevant
  • Fahrenheit 451 — Bradbury — censorship; conformism

US.6_12.6 Unit 5 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.1 Science & Technology · Unit 6 Civil Rights
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.1 — Science, Technology, and Society Unit: 6 — Civil Rights Movement (1954–1968) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did technology shape the Civil Rights Movement? What role did media, communication, and documentation play in the movement's strategy?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: Television was the Civil Rights Movement's most powerful ally — not because activists planned it that way, but because Bull Connor's fire hoses and police dogs in Birmingham, broadcast into living rooms across America, turned public opinion. The movement understood this and used it strategically. Technology didn't create the movement, but it amplified its message to an audience that would otherwise never have seen what segregation looked like.

docs.title: Key Technologies — Unit 6
docs.text:
Technology Role in Civil Rights Television Broadcast Birmingham, Selma violence into white homes; turned public opinion Radio Movement communication; spread speeches; freedom songs Phone / telegraph networks NAACP legal coordination; SNCC organizing Print journalism / photography Emmett Till's open casket photograph — Jet magazine; galvanized movement Recording technology MLK speeches preserved and distributed; oral history Long-distance bus system Freedom Rides used existing transportation infrastructure as protest target

Emmett Till photograph (1955): Mamie Till insisted on an open casket for her son, who was murdered in Mississippi. Jet magazine published the photographs. The visual evidence of what racism did galvanized support for the movement in ways words alone hadn't.

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.1 in Unit 6 is about technology as a tool of social change.

Example argument move:

"MLK said he was grateful to Bull Connor — because Connor gave the movement television. The strategy of nonviolent direct action only works if the violence of the opposition becomes visible. Without television, the Birmingham campaign is a local news story. With television, it becomes the moment public opinion shifts. The Civil Rights Movement didn't just use technology — it understood how to make technology work for justice."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.9 — Argument Analysis MLK "Letter from Birmingham Jail" — how does he use media awareness in his argument? W.4 — Argument Argue whether social media today plays the same role television played for the Civil Rights Movement

US.6_12.1 Unit 6 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.2 Ideas, Beliefs & Ideology · Unit 6 Civil Rights
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.2 — Ideas, Beliefs, and Institutions Unit: 6 — Civil Rights Movement (1954–1968) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What ideologies shaped the Civil Rights Movement — and what ideologies opposed it? What was the movement arguing for, and how did it argue?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The Civil Rights Movement was a battle of ideas as much as a political campaign. MLK's philosophy of nonviolent direct action was a deliberate ideological choice — not just a moral position but a strategic one. The white moderate's objection (you're moving too fast, use legal channels) was itself an ideological position. Malcolm X's challenge to integration and nonviolence was a competing ideology. Understanding the movement means understanding the debate within it.

docs.title: Key Ideologies — Unit 6
docs.text: Ideology Core Claim Who Held It Nonviolent direct action (MLK / Gandhi) Strategic exposure of opposition's violence; moral high ground; builds coalition SCLC; MLK; many SNCC members early Legal incrementalism (NAACP) Use courts; change law one case at a time; Brown v. Board strategy Thurgood Marshall; NAACP Black nationalism / self-determination (Malcolm X) Integration is not the goal; Black dignity requires Black self-determination Nation of Islam; Malcolm X Black Power (Stokely Carmichael, 1966) Pride, self-reliance, political power — not just legal equality SNCC later; Black Panther Party White moderate objection Agree with goals; oppose timing/methods; "law and order" White clergy (MLK's "Letter" audience); most white Americans Massive resistance (white South) Segregation is right; federal government has no authority here Southern states; Citizens Councils; KKK

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.2 in Unit 6 is about a debate within a movement — and an argument about strategy.

Example argument move:

"MLK's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' is addressed to white clergymen who said they supported civil rights but opposed the demonstrations. MLK's argument is that the 'white moderate who prefers a negative peace which is the absence of tension to a positive peace which is the presence of justice' is a greater obstacle than the outright racist. He's identifying ideology as the problem — the belief that injustice can be corrected slowly, on the oppressor's timeline."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.9 — Argument Analysis MLK Letter as argument — identify his claim, audience, evidence, and rhetorical strategy W.4 — Argument Argue whether nonviolent protest or legal challenge was the more effective Civil Rights strategy

docs.title: Connect to Reading Library
docs.text:
• MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — R.9 + W.4; crosswalk guide

US.6_12.2 Unit 6 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.3 Political Systems · Unit 6 Civil Rights
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.3 — Political Systems and Democratic Institutions Unit: 6 — Civil Rights Movement (1954–1968) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did the Civil Rights Movement change American political institutions — and what resistance did those changes face?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The Civil Rights Movement produced the most significant legislative expansion of civil rights since Reconstruction — but only after years of activism forced the political system to act. The movement shows how democratic institutions respond to pressure from below — and also how slowly and incompletely they respond.

docs.title: Key Political Moments — Unit 6
docs.text: Event What Happened Significance Brown v. Board of Education (1954) Supreme Court unanimously overturns Plessy v. Ferguson "Separate but equal" unconstitutional Montgomery Bus Boycott (1955–56) 381-day boycott; Supreme Court rules bus segregation unconstitutional Proves economic pressure works; MLK emerges Little Rock Crisis (1957) Eisenhower sends 101st Airborne to enforce school integration Federal vs. state authority Civil Rights Act (1964) Prohibits discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, national origin Most comprehensive civil rights law since Reconstruction 24th Amendment (1964) Abolished poll tax in federal elections One barrier to Black voting removed Voting Rights Act (1965) Eliminated literacy tests; federal oversight of voting Most powerful voting rights law ever; gutted by Shelby County v. Holder (2013) Fair Housing Act (1968) Prohibited housing discrimination Least enforced; housing segregation persists

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.3 in Unit 6 is about the relationship between protest and policy.

Example argument move:

"The Civil Rights Act passed in 1964 after a decade of organized protest — sit-ins, Freedom Rides, the Birmingham campaign, the March on Washington. Before those events, Congress had blocked civil rights legislation for decades. The movement didn't work through the political system; it created the conditions that forced the political system to act. That's the lesson: legal change follows political pressure, not the other way around."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.9 — Argument Analysis MLK Letter — he addresses the political system directly; what does he demand of it? W.4 — Argument Argue whether legal change or cultural change is more fundamental to civil rights progress

docs.text:

  • Warriors Don't Cry — Beals — individual experience of legal change

US.6_12.3 Unit 6 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.4 Social & Economic Change · Unit 6 Civil Rights
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.4 — Social and Economic Change Unit: 6 — Civil Rights Movement (1954–1968) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did the Civil Rights Movement change American society? What did integration cost — and who bore that cost?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The Civil Rights Movement achieved legal equality but not economic equality. School integration was real but produced white flight and resegregation. Voting rights were secured but economic gaps persisted. The movement revealed that formal equality and actual equality are different things — and that the burden of producing integration often fell on Black Americans, not on those who had built segregation.

docs.title: Key Social Changes — Unit 6
docs.text: Change What Happened What Remained School integration Brown (1954); Little Rock (1957); gradual, contested desegregation White flight; private schools; resegregation by 1980s Voting rights Voting Rights Act (1965) dramatically increases Black voter registration Voter suppression tactics evolve; ID laws, polling place closures Employment Civil Rights Act (1964) prohibits workplace discrimination Wage gap persists; employment discrimination difficult to prove Housing Fair Housing Act (1968) Redlining effects persist; wealth gap in homeownership Cultural recognition Harlem Renaissance → broader American culture Ongoing debates about representation Personal cost of integration Melba Beals at Little Rock; daily harassment; physical danger Warriors Don't Cry documents this cost

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.4 in Unit 6 is about the gap between legal change and lived experience.

Example argument move:

"Melba Pattillo Beals integrated Central High School in 1957 and was spat on, threatened, and assaulted every day for a year. The law said she had the right to be there. The law did not make anyone want her there. Warriors Don't Cry documents what it cost one person to exercise a right that the white students around her took for granted. Understanding the Civil Rights Movement means understanding that legal change and social change are not the same thing."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.8 — Literary Elements Warriors Don't Cry — memoir as social history; perspective, voice, detail W.3 — Explanatory Explain what integration demanded of the people who integrated W.4 — Argument Argue whether the burden of desegregation was fairly distributed

docs.title: Connect to Reading Library
docs.text:

  • MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail

US.6_12.4 Unit 6 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.5 Causes & Effects · Unit 6 Civil Rights
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.5 — Causes and Effects of Historical Events Unit: 6 — Civil Rights Movement (1954–1968) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What caused the Civil Rights Movement? What did it produce — immediate and long-term?

docs.title: Causes of the Civil Rights Movement
docs.text: Cause Explanation Accumulated injustice Jim Crow laws; lynching; disenfranchisement; economic exclusion — decades of organized oppression WWII veterans radicalized Black men fought for freedom abroad, returned to Jim Crow; demanded consistency Great Migration (North) Black Americans in Northern cities had more political power; Northern white liberals as potential allies Brown v. Board (1954) Legal victory showed courts could be used; built momentum Emmett Till murder (1955) Publicized horror of racial violence; galvanized movement Montgomery Bus Boycott (1955) Showed economic pressure works; MLK emerges Cold War context US fighting for "freedom" internationally while practicing apartheid domestically; international embarrassment

docs.title: Effects of the Civil Rights Movement
docs.text: Effect Significance Civil Rights Act (1964) Most comprehensive anti-discrimination law ever Voting Rights Act (1965) Black voter registration dramatically increases Fair Housing Act (1968) Housing discrimination prohibited Cultural shift Black culture enters mainstream American culture White backlash / Southern Strategy Nixon's 1968 campaign exploits white resentment of Civil Rights Black Power movement Radicalization of younger activists; Malcolm X; Black Panthers Women's movement Civil Rights Movement provides model and language

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.5 in Unit 6 is about strategic causation — the movement understood what would produce change.

Example argument move:

"The Civil Rights Movement's most important strategic insight was this: moral suasion alone doesn't produce change, but moral suasion combined with economic pressure and publicized violence does. The Montgomery Bus Boycott cost the city money. The Birmingham campaign made segregationists look barbaric on television. The March on Washington put 250,000 people on the National Mall. Each was designed to produce a specific political effect. The movement understood causation."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection W.3 — Explanatory Explain what combination of factors made the Civil Rights Movement successful W.4 — Argument Argue which cause of the movement was most important

docs.title: Connect to CrashCourse
docs.text:

US.6_12.5 Unit 6 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.6 Past to Present · Unit 6 Civil Rights
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.6 — Connecting History to the Present Unit: 6 — Civil Rights Movement (1954–1968) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What from the Civil Rights Movement is still present today? How does understanding this period explain current debates about race, equality, and justice?

docs.title: Then → Now Connections
docs.text: Then (1954–1968) Now Voting Rights Act (1965) Shelby County v. Holder (2013) guts key provisions; voter ID laws Fair Housing Act (1968) Racial wealth gap in homeownership persists Redlining Federal Reserve studies show redlined neighborhoods still lag today Black Power / self-determination Black Lives Matter movement; community organizing MLK's "Letter from Birmingham Jail" Still cited in debates about protest tactics Brown v. Board (1954) School resegregation debate; charter schools; school choice Civil disobedience as tactic Protests worldwide; Standing Rock; climate activism White backlash → Southern Strategy Political realignment of South; culture war politics Police violence against Black Americans Same pattern as Civil Rights era; now on video "I Have a Dream" (selective memory) MLK quoted to oppose things he supported

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.6 in Unit 6 is about what "incomplete" means.

Example argument move:

"The Voting Rights Act of 1965 was called the movement's crowning achievement. In 2013, the Supreme Court ruled in Shelby County v. Holder that the formula used to determine which states needed federal oversight was outdated — effectively gutting the law. Within hours of the ruling, several states passed new voting restrictions. Understanding the Civil Rights Movement means understanding that its victories were not permanent — they require ongoing defense."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.9 — Argument Analysis Find and analyze a contemporary source arguing about voting rights W.4 — Argument Argue whether the Civil Rights Movement's goals have been achieved

docs.text:

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.1 Science & Technology · Unit 7 Vietnam Era
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.1 — Science, Technology, and Society Unit: 7 — Vietnam Era & Watergate (1964–1974) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did technology shape the Vietnam War — and how did media technology change how Americans experienced it?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: Vietnam was the first televised war. For the first time in American history, combat footage appeared on nightly news broadcasts. The gap between the government's claims ("we are winning") and what Americans saw on their televisions ("we are not winning") created the "credibility gap" that destroyed public support for the war. Technology didn't cause the war — but television ended it politically.

docs.title: Key Technologies — Unit 7
docs.text:
Technology Role Television (evening news) Brought war footage into living rooms; Tet Offensive coverage pivotal Napalm Incendiary weapon; burns on contact with water; civilians affected; iconic photographs Agent Orange Herbicide defoliant; destroyed jungle cover; long-term health effects on veterans Helicopters Changed battlefield mobility; air cavalry; medevac Body count metrics Misused technology of measurement; created perverse incentives Photocopier (Pentagon Papers) Ellsberg photocopied classified documents; enabled leak Recording equipment (Nixon tapes) Nixon's own taping system provided the evidence that ended his presidency

The Pentagon Papers (1971): Daniel Ellsberg photocopied 7,000 pages of classified Defense Department history of Vietnam, showing systematic government deception. He gave them to the New York Times. Nixon tried to suppress publication; Supreme Court ruled 6-3 for publication. First major First Amendment / classified information case.

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.1 in Unit 7 is about technology as truth-teller — or truth-revealer.

Example argument move:

"Nixon's downfall came from his own technology. He installed a taping system to create a historical record — and the tapes proved he had ordered the Watergate cover-up. The Pentagon Papers were leaked because a photocopier existed. The Vietnam War's support collapsed because of television. In the 1960s and 70s, technology kept creating evidence that governments would rather not have existed."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.9 — Argument Analysis Pentagon Papers excerpts — what were the government's actual arguments vs. its public statements? W.4 — Argument Argue whether media coverage of Vietnam turned public opinion or reflected it

docs.title: Connect to CrashCourse
docs.text:
• Ep USH-12 — Vietnam and Watergate

US.6_12.1 Unit 7 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.2 Ideas, Beliefs & Ideology · Unit 7 Vietnam Era
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.2 — Ideas, Beliefs, and Institutions Unit: 7 — Vietnam Era & Watergate (1964–1974) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What ideologies drove US involvement in Vietnam — and what ideologies drove the opposition? What did Watergate reveal about the beliefs underlying executive power?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: Vietnam was fought in the name of containment ideology — stopping communism from spreading. The anti-war movement challenged that ideology, arguing the war was unwinnable, unjust, and based on deception. The clash produced the most significant domestic political fracture since the Civil War. Watergate then revealed that the administration believed it could operate above the law — and was wrong.

docs.title: Key Ideologies — Unit 7
docs.text: Ideology Core Claim Who Held It Containment / domino theory Communism must be stopped everywhere; Vietnam falling means Southeast Asia falls LBJ, Nixon, military establishment Anti-war ideology War is unwinnable; based on deception; immoral; waste of lives Students, veterans (VVAW), civil rights leaders MLK's anti-war stance (1967) War is connected to racism and poverty; can't fight for freedom abroad while denying it at home MLK; moral/religious anti-war argument Nixon's "silent majority" Most Americans support the war; protesters are fringe Nixon strategy; backlash politics Imperial presidency Executive has vast secret powers in national security; Congress and courts shouldn't interfere Nixon White House Constitutional constraint No one is above the law; executive power has limits Congress, courts during Watergate

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.2 in Unit 7 is about ideology colliding with reality.

Example argument move:

"The domino theory required believing that if Vietnam fell to communism, all of Southeast Asia would follow. After Vietnam fell in 1975, Cambodia went communist. Laos went communist. Thailand, Malaysia, and Indonesia did not. The domino theory was partly wrong — which means the argument used to justify 58,000 American deaths was built on a prediction that didn't fully come true."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.9 — Argument Analysis Anti-war speeches as arguments — analyze MLK's "Beyond Vietnam" (1967) W.4 — Argument Argue whether the US should have entered/escalated Vietnam given the ideological justification

docs.title: Connect to CrashCourse
docs.text:
• Ep USH-12 — Vietnam and Watergate

US.6_12.2 Unit 7 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.3 Political Systems · Unit 7 Vietnam Era
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.3 — Political Systems and Democratic Institutions Unit: 7 — Vietnam Era & Watergate (1964–1974) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did the Vietnam War and Watergate test American democratic institutions? What constitutional questions did they raise — and how were they resolved?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: Watergate is the clearest test of American democratic institutions in the 20th century — and the institutions held. Nixon believed the President could authorize illegal acts in the name of national security. The Congress, courts, and press proved him wrong. But the era also showed how close the system came to failing.

docs.title: Key Constitutional Moments — Unit 7
docs.text: Event Constitutional Issue Resolution Gulf of Tonkin Resolution (1964) Congress authorized war based on exaggerated/false report War Powers Resolution (1973) attempted to prevent recurrence Pentagon Papers (1971) Government tried to suppress publication; press freedom vs. national security Supreme Court rules 6-3 for press; NY Times v. US Watergate break-in (1972) Campaign operatives break into opposition HQ; White House covers up Nixon resigns; aides convicted Saturday Night Massacre (Oct 1973) Nixon fires special prosecutor; two AGs resign rather than obey House begins impeachment inquiry Nixon tapes subpoena Nixon claims executive privilege; refuses to release tapes Supreme Court rules 8-0 tapes must be released; US v. Nixon Nixon resignation (Aug 9, 1974) Faced certain impeachment and conviction; resigns First presidential resignation in US history Ford pardons Nixon (Sept 1974) Ford prevents Nixon prosecution Controversial; Ford loses 1976 election partly because of it

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.3 in Unit 7 is about institutional resilience — the system works, barely.

Example argument move:

"Watergate is often taught as a civics lesson about how American democracy works. But a more accurate lesson is how close it came to not working. Nixon had 18 months of cover-up during which he could have destroyed the tapes. The House moved slowly on impeachment. The Saturday Night Massacre showed a president willing to fire anyone who stood in his way. The institutions held — but they held because individuals within them made courageous choices, not because the rules automatically prevailed."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection W.4 — Argument Argue whether Watergate proved American democracy works or revealed how fragile it is R.9 — Argument Analysis Nixon's resignation speech as argument — what does he claim? what does he avoid saying?

US.6_12.3 Unit 7 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.4 Social & Economic Change · Unit 7 Vietnam Era
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.4 — Social and Economic Change Unit: 7 — Vietnam Era & Watergate (1964–1974) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did Vietnam and the social upheavals of the 1960s change American society? What movements emerged — and what did they achieve?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The 1960s and early 70s produced simultaneous social movements: Civil Rights, anti-war, women's liberation, American Indian Movement, Chicano movement, LGBTQ+ rights (Stonewall, 1969). These movements were interconnected — they shared language, tactics, and sometimes leadership. The era shows what happens when multiple marginalized groups demand change at the same time.

docs.title: Key Social Changes — Unit 7
docs.text: Movement / Event What Happened Legacy Anti-war protest Teach-ins, marches, draft card burning, Kent State Changed public opinion; built coalition politics Women's liberation NOW (1966); Title IX (1972); Roe v. Wade (1973) Expanded women's rights in education, workplace, reproduction American Indian Movement (AIM) Wounded Knee standoff (1973); Indian education rights Limited legislative gains; heightened awareness United Farm Workers (Chavez, Huerta) Grape boycott; labor rights for agricultural workers California labor gains; model for boycott tactics Stonewall (1969) LGBTQ+ resistance to police raid; beginning of gay rights movement Annual Pride commemorations; path to legal equality Great Society / War on Poverty LBJ's domestic programs; Medicare, Medicaid, Head Start Permanent social programs; debated effectiveness Vietnam veteran experience 58,000 dead; 304,000 wounded; PTSD not recognized Agent Orange lawsuits; VA inadequacy; belated recognition

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.4 in Unit 7 is about movement interconnection.

Example argument move:

"The 1960s social movements were interconnected in ways that went beyond their specific demands. The Civil Rights Movement taught the anti-war movement how to organize. The anti-war movement's tactics were borrowed by the women's liberation movement. The United Farm Workers borrowed from the Montgomery Bus Boycott. Each movement learned from the others — and the era's social transformation was larger than any single movement could have produced alone."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text:
ELA Standard Connection W.3 — Explanatory Explain how one social movement of the 1960s changed American society W.4 — Argument Argue whether the 1960s social movements succeeded or failed

US.6_12.4 Unit 7 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.5 Causes & Effects · Unit 7 Vietnam Era
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.5 — Causes and Effects of Historical Events Unit: 7 — Vietnam Era & Watergate (1964–1974) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What caused US involvement in Vietnam? What caused Watergate? What did each produce?

docs.title: Causes of US Vietnam Involvement
docs.text: Cause Explanation Containment doctrine US committed to stopping communism globally since Truman Domino theory Fear Southeast Asia falls if Vietnam goes communist French colonial war ends (1954) US steps in after France leaves; supports South Vietnam's Diem government Gulf of Tonkin (1964) Reported attack on US ships (later found exaggerated); Congress authorizes war Political cost of "losing" No president wanted to be the one who "lost" Vietnam Military credibility US had to show it could win to deter Soviet aggression elsewhere

docs.title: Effects of Vietnam
docs.text: Effect Significance 58,000 Americans dead; 2+ million Vietnamese Human cost Public trust in government collapses "Credibility gap" → permanent skepticism War Powers Resolution (1973) Congress tries to limit presidential war-making Military volunteers only (draft ends 1973) Draft causes too much domestic unrest; all-volunteer military Vietnam syndrome US reluctance to commit ground troops for decades Pentagon Papers reveal systematic deception Media-government relationship permanently changed

docs.title: Causes and Effects of Watergate
docs.text: Stage Event Cause Nixon's paranoia; "dirty tricks" campaign; plumbers unit Event June 1972 break-in at DNC headquarters Escalation Cover-up: hush money, FBI interference, obstruction of justice Unraveling Senate hearings; tapes; Saturday Night Massacre; US v. Nixon Effect Nixon resigns; 40 aides convicted; campaign finance reform; inspector general offices

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.5 in Unit 7 is about compounding effects — one event making another possible.

Example argument move:

"Watergate was caused by Vietnam. Nixon's 'plumbers unit' was created to stop leaks about the war — specifically the Pentagon Papers. The same people who broke into the DNC had been doing illegal intelligence work related to Vietnam. If there had been no Pentagon Papers, there might have been no plumbers. If there had been no plumbers, there might have been no Watergate. The two events aren't separate stories."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection W.3 — Explanatory Explain the causal chain from Gulf of Tonkin to US withdrawal W.4 — Argument Argue whether Watergate was primarily about Nixon's character or systemic issues
