US.6_12.5 Unit 7 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.6 Past to Present · Unit 7 Vietnam Era
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.6 — Connecting History to the Present Unit: 7 — Vietnam Era & Watergate (1964–1974) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What from the Vietnam era and Watergate is still present today? How does this period explain current debates about presidential power, the press, and public trust?

docs.title: Then → Now Connections
docs.text: Then (1964–1974) Now Pentagon Papers → press freedom WikiLeaks; Snowden; classified information debates Watergate tapes → executive privilege Every presidency since has claimed executive privilege War Powers Resolution (1973) Presidents have largely ignored it; still debated Vietnam syndrome Iraq and Afghanistan brought it back; "endless wars" debate Draft resistance All-volunteer military; debates about who serves Credibility gap "Fake news"; public distrust of institutions at record high Watergate reform (campaign finance) Citizens United (2010) reversed much of it PTSD recognition (delayed, Vietnam era) Still inadequate VA care; veteran suicide rates Stonewall / LGBTQ+ movement Marriage equality (2015); ongoing rights debates Nixon's "silent majority" framing Still used to claim mainstream support against protesters

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.6 in Unit 7 is about institutional memory — we keep facing the same questions.

Example argument move:

"The Espionage Act (1917) was used to prosecute Edward Snowden (2013) for leaking NSA surveillance documents. The Pentagon Papers precedent (NY Times v. US, 1971) was cited in Snowden's defense. The War Powers Resolution (1973) was invoked when Obama conducted operations in Libya (2011). The legal and political frameworks of the Vietnam era are still the tools we use to argue about government power and accountability — because we haven't replaced them."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text:
ELA Standard Connection R.9 — Argument Analysis Find a contemporary source arguing about presidential war powers; trace it to War Powers Resolution W.4 — Argument Argue whether the War Powers Resolution has been effective

US.6_12.6 Unit 7 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.1 Science & Technology · Unit 8 1970s–80s
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.1 — Science, Technology, and Society Unit: 8 — 1970s–1980s (Reagan Era, Cold War End) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did technology change American life in the 1970s and 1980s? What new technologies emerged — and what did they displace?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The 1970s and 80s were the transition decade: from industrial to post-industrial economy, from analog to digital technology. Personal computers, VCRs, cable television, the Walkman, video games — the consumer technology of the 80s set the stage for the internet age. Meanwhile, deindustrialization removed the manufacturing jobs that had sustained the working class since WWII.

docs.title: Key Technologies — Unit 8
docs.text: Technology 1970s–80s Role Social Effect Personal computer (Apple II, 1977; IBM PC, 1981) Computing moves from institutions to individuals New economy; knowledge workers; deskilling others Cable television (CNN, 1980; MTV, 1981) 24-hour news; music videos; niche channels Media fragmentation; 24-hour news cycle begins VCR / home video Movies at home; entertainment industry transformed Blockbuster; time-shifting; piracy debates Reagan's SDI ("Star Wars") Space-based missile defense; ultimately doesn't work Arms race escalation; $100B+ spent Nuclear power / Three Mile Island (1979) Near-meltdown; fear of nuclear power Nuclear energy expansion halted in US Deindustrialization Manufacturing jobs move overseas as technology changes production Rust Belt; working-class devastation

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.1 in Unit 8 is about technology and economic inequality.

Example argument move:

"The personal computer revolution created enormous wealth in Silicon Valley and destroyed manufacturing jobs in Detroit. Technology didn't just change what people did — it changed who had economic power. The same decade that produced the Apple II produced the Rust Belt. Understanding the 1980s means understanding that technological progress and economic equality can move in opposite directions."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text:
ELA Standard Connection W.3 — Explanatory Explain how deindustrialization changed American working-class communities W.4 — Argument Argue whether technological change justifies the economic disruption it causes

US.6_12.1 Unit 8 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.2 Ideas, Beliefs & Ideology · Unit 8 1970s–80s
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.2 — Ideas, Beliefs, and Institutions Unit: 8 — 1970s–1980s (Reagan Era, Cold War End) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What ideologies defined the 1970s and 80s? How did Reagan conservatism change American politics — and what did it argue against?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: Reagan's 1980 election marked the most significant ideological shift in American politics since the New Deal. Reagan argued that the government was the problem — that New Deal liberalism had created a dependency culture, overtaxed the productive, and weakened American strength internationally. This was a direct challenge to the Keynesian consensus of the previous 40 years. Some of Reagan's ideas worked; some didn't; all of them reshaped the debate.

docs.title: Key Ideologies — Unit 8
docs.text: Ideology Core Claim Significance Reaganism / supply-side economics Cut taxes on wealthy; reduce regulation; wealth will "trickle down" Taxes cut dramatically; deficits explode; debate continues Anti-government conservatism "Government is not the solution to our problem; government is the problem" Permanent shift in how Americans talk about government Religious right / Moral Majority Christianity should shape public policy; social conservatism New political constituency; abortion, school prayer, family values Neoliberalism Free markets, deregulation, privatization as global model Spreads through IMF, World Bank; affects global South Anti-communism (renewed) Détente failed; Soviet Union is "evil empire"; must be opposed everywhere Arms buildup; proxy wars (Nicaragua, Afghanistan) Feminist counterculture ERA; reproductive rights; women in workforce ERA fails; Roe under attack; women enter workforce anyway

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.2 in Unit 8 is about the ideological reversal — an argument that wins.

Example argument move:

"Reagan's most lasting achievement wasn't his tax cuts or his arms buildup — it was redefining the ideological terms of debate. After Reagan, even Democrats argued about how to cut government, not whether to. The Overton window shifted: positions that were mainstream Democratic positions in 1970 (strong union support, robust social programs, high taxes on wealthy) became fringe positions by 1990. Ideology shapes politics long after the politicians who created it are gone."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text:
ELA Standard Connection R.9 — Argument Analysis Reagan's first inaugural — analyze the argument against government W.4 — Argument Argue whether Reagan's economic ideology produced the effects he claimed it would

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.3 Political Systems · Unit 8 1970s–80s
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.3 — Political Systems and Democratic Institutions Unit: 8 — 1970s–1980s (Reagan Era, Cold War End) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did the political system change in the 1970s and 80s? What were the consequences of Watergate reform, Reagan's political realignment, and the Cold War's end?

docs.title: Key Political Changes — Unit 8
docs.text: Change What Happened Significance Post-Watergate reforms Campaign finance law; ethics rules; independent counsel Attempt to prevent future Watergate; mixed success Carter presidency (1977–81) Stagflation; Iran hostage crisis; Camp David Accords Liberalism discredited; Reagan possible Reagan revolution (1980–88) Tax cuts; deregulation; defense buildup; "Morning in America" Conservative coalition; South shifts to Republican Iran-Contra (1986–87) Reagan administration secretly sold arms to Iran; funded Nicaraguan rebels illegally Potential impeachment avoided; executive power again tested Deregulation Airlines, banks, telecommunications deregulated Savings & Loan crisis (1980s); airline competition Soviet collapse (1989–91) Berlin Wall falls Nov 9, 1989; USSR dissolves Dec 25, 1991 Cold War ends; US sole superpower; "end of history" Southern Strategy completion South becomes solidly Republican by 1984 Parties realign; Civil Rights backlash locked in

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.3 in Unit 8 is about realignment — the political system reorganizes.

Example argument move:

"The 1980 election wasn't just a Republican victory — it was a realignment. The South, which had been solidly Democratic since the Civil War (voting against the party of Lincoln), became solidly Republican in response to Civil Rights legislation. This is the Southern Strategy completing its work. Understanding American politics today requires understanding that the parties switched positions on race — and why."

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.4 Social & Economic Change · Unit 8 1970s–80s
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.4 — Social and Economic Change Unit: 8 — 1970s–1980s (Reagan Era, Cold War End) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did the economy and society change in the 1970s and 80s? Who gained and who lost?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The 1970s ended the postwar prosperity consensus. Stagflation (high inflation + high unemployment — previously thought impossible simultaneously), the oil crisis, and deindustrialization devastated the working and middle class. Reagan's response — cut taxes on wealthy, deregulate, break unions — restored growth but also dramatically increased inequality. The 1980s began the income inequality trend that has continued to the present.

docs.title: Key Social and Economic Changes — Unit 8
docs.text: Change What Happened Who Was Affected Stagflation (1970s) Oil crisis + Nixon price controls → 12% inflation + 10% unemployment Everyone; especially working/middle class Deindustrialization Manufacturing moves overseas; Rust Belt collapses Working class; Midwest/Northeast cities Union decline Reagan fires PATCO strikers (1981); union membership falls Working class loses wage bargaining power Tax cuts (ERTA, 1981) Top marginal rate cut from 70% to 28% Wealthy benefit most; deficit explodes Savings & Loan crisis (1980s) Deregulated S&Ls make risky bets; 1,000+ fail; $130B bailout Taxpayers; housing market AIDS crisis (1981–) Disproportionately affects gay men and Black/Latino communities; Reagan silent LGBTQ+ community; delayed federal response costs lives Crack cocaine epidemic Crack hits urban communities; harsh sentencing laws Black communities; mass incarceration begins "War on Drugs" / mass incarceration Nixon origin; Reagan escalation; mandatory minimums Disproportionately Black and Latino; prison population explodes

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.4 in Unit 8 is about who paid for "morning in America."

Example argument move:

"The Reagan economic boom of the 1980s was real — GDP grew, inflation fell, unemployment dropped. But income inequality grew simultaneously. The richest 1%'s share of income rose dramatically; working-class wages stagnated. At the same time, the crack epidemic devastated urban Black communities, the AIDS crisis went unaddressed, and mass incarceration began. 'Morning in America' depended on what time zone you were in."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text:

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.5 Causes & Effects · Unit 8 1970s–80s
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.5 — Causes and Effects of Historical Events Unit: 8 — 1970s–1980s (Reagan Era, Cold War End) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What caused the political and economic shifts of the 1970s and 80s? What did they produce?

docs.title: Causes of the 1970s Crisis
docs.text: Cause Explanation Oil shock (OPEC embargo, 1973) Arab states embargo US for supporting Israel in Yom Kippur War; oil prices quadruple Vietnam economic cost War spending + Great Society = inflation Nixon ends gold standard (1971) Dollar no longer convertible to gold; floating exchange rates; instability Deindustrialization Global competition; US manufacturing less competitive; jobs move Watergate / post-Vietnam cynicism Public trust in government collapses; political paralysis

docs.title: Causes of Reagan's Rise
docs.text: Cause Explanation Carter's failures Stagflation; Iran hostage crisis; "malaise" speech Southern realignment Civil Rights backlash; white Southerners leave Democratic Party Religious right mobilized Roe v. Wade (1973) galvanizes evangelical political organizing Tax revolt (Prop 13, 1978) California property tax revolt; anti-government mood

docs.title: Effects of the Reagan Era
docs.text: Effect Significance Tax cuts / deficit National debt triples; supply-side economics becomes GOP orthodoxy Union decline Union membership falls from 35% to 17%; wage stagnation follows Cold War end Reagan's arms buildup + Gorbachev's reforms → Soviet collapse 1991 Mass incarceration "War on Drugs" → prison population from 500K to 2.3M by 2008 Deregulation Financial deregulation → S&L crisis and eventually 2008 crisis Income inequality rise Begins in 1980s; accelerates through present

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.5 in Unit 8 is about long causal chains.

Example argument move:

"The 2008 financial crisis was caused by financial deregulation that began in the 1980s. The deregulation was caused by Reagan-era ideology that markets self-regulate. That ideology was shaped by the stagflation of the 1970s, which discredited Keynesian economics. The 2008 crisis traces back to decisions made in 1973 and 1981. Understanding long causal chains is how you understand why the present looks the way it does."

docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.6 — Connecting History to the Present Unit: 8 — 1970s–1980s (Reagan Era, Cold War End) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What from the 1970s and 80s is still present today? How does this period explain current political and economic debates?

docs.title: Then → Now Connections
docs.text: Then (1970s–80s) Now Supply-side / trickle-down economics Still GOP orthodoxy; 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act Anti-government conservatism Tea Party → MAGA; government shutdown politics Religious right / Moral Majority Evangelical political influence; Dobbs v. Jackson (2022) Mass incarceration ("War on Drugs") 2.3 million incarcerated; bipartisan criminal justice reform debate Deindustrialization / Rust Belt Persistent; 2016 election driven partly by deindustrialized communities Union decline Ongoing; UAW strikes 2023; Amazon/Starbucks organizing AIDS crisis (Reagan's silence) Model for pandemic response debates; LGBTQ+ health equity Income inequality acceleration Highest since 1920s; central political issue Cold War end / unipolar moment US military interventions of 1990s–2000s follow from "sole superpower" Financial deregulation 2008 crisis; Dodd-Frank reform; debates about re-regulation

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.6 in Unit 8 is about ideological persistence.

Example argument move:

"Reagan's argument — 'government is not the solution to our problem; government is the problem' — is still the operating premise of one major American political party 45 years later. It has shaped every tax debate, every healthcare debate, every infrastructure debate since 1981. Understanding why that argument was persuasive in 1980 (stagflation, Vietnam, Watergate) helps you understand why it persists — and why its critics argue it's become a reflex rather than a response to current conditions."

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.1 Science & Technology · Unit 9 1990s–2000s
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.1 — Science, Technology, and Society Unit: 9 — 1990s–2000s (Globalization, 9/11, Contemporary) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did the internet, globalization, and post-9/11 surveillance technology reshape American life? What does the most recent technology revolution mean for democracy, privacy, and society?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The internet changed everything — and students live on the other side of that change. Understanding the 1990s means understanding how the technology students use daily was built, how it changed the economy and culture, and what tradeoffs it created. Post-9/11, surveillance technology raised questions about privacy and security that remain unresolved.

docs.title: Key Technologies — Unit 9
docs.text: Technology 1990s–2000s Role Social Effect World Wide Web (1991) Public internet; changes commerce, communication, information Everything — eventually Cell phones → smartphones Communication anywhere; iPhone (2007) changes everything Constant connectivity; attention economy Social media (2004–) Facebook, YouTube, Twitter; user-generated content Information fragmentation; polarization; new organizing tools GPS Navigation; location tracking Convenience; surveillance potential Post-9/11 surveillance (NSA, PATRIOT Act) Mass data collection; phone metadata; PRISM Privacy vs. security; Snowden reveals scope (2013) Amazon / e-commerce Retail transformation; warehouse jobs replace retail Consumer convenience; labor conditions; local retail destroyed Google Information access democratized Also: algorithmic control of what information reaches you

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.1 in Unit 9 is the most personally relevant — students are living inside this technology shift.

Example argument move:

"The PATRIOT Act (2001) was passed six weeks after 9/11 with almost no debate. It authorized surveillance the NSA used to collect phone data on virtually every American. When Edward Snowden revealed the scope of this in 2013, the debate that should have happened in 2001 finally happened. The technology existed before the legal framework to govern it did — and still does. Understanding this pattern (technology outpacing governance) is one of the most important things you can take from this unit."

docs.text: ELA Standard Connection IR.4 — Source Credibility Contemporary internet sources; how do you know what's true? R.9 — Argument Analysis PATRIOT Act debates; tech company testimony to Congress W.4 — Argument Argue whether the internet has strengthened or weakened democracy

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.2 Ideas, Beliefs & Ideology · Unit 9 1990s–2000s
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.2 — Ideas, Beliefs, and Institutions Unit: 9 — 1990s–2000s (Globalization, 9/11, Contemporary) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What ideologies shaped the post-Cold War era, 9/11 response, and contemporary America? What arguments justified the War on Terror — and what arguments challenged it?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: After the Cold War, American foreign policy needed a new ideological framework. Two emerged: liberal internationalism (spread democracy through international institutions) and neoconservatism (spread democracy through force). 9/11 gave neoconservatism its moment. The War on Terror was justified through a specific ideological argument — you are either with us or against us; preemptive war is legitimate; democracy can be imposed. The failures of Iraq and Afghanistan tested those arguments.

docs.title: Key Ideologies — Unit 9
docs.text: Ideology Core Claim Significance "End of history" (Fukuyama, 1992) Liberal democracy has won; it will spread everywhere Hubris before 9/11; challenged by events Neoconservatism US should use military force to spread democracy; no need for international consensus Bush Doctrine; Iraq War Bush Doctrine Preemptive war is legitimate; US will act unilaterally if necessary Break from post-WWII international law framework Islamist terrorism (Al-Qaeda's ideology) Western influence corrupts Muslim societies; violence is legitimate response 9/11; ongoing "War on Terror" framework Terrorism is an existential threat requiring wartime response PATRIOT Act; surveillance state; Abu Ghraib Polarization / culture war American political identity increasingly tribal; facts contested Accelerates from 1990s to present

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.2 in Unit 9 is about how crises produce ideological commitments that outlast their context.

Example argument move:

"The Bush Doctrine — preemptive war — was a major break from the post-WWII international legal framework the US had helped build. Nuremburg established that aggressive war is a crime. The UN Charter prohibits it. The Bush administration argued that 9/11 created an exception. The Iraq War was the result. Understanding the ideology is understanding how governments justify breaking the rules they themselves created."

docs.title: ELA Crosswalk
docs.text: ELA Standard Connection R.9 — Argument Analysis Bush's 9/20/01 address to Congress — analyze the argument for the War on Terror W.4 — Argument Argue whether the War on Terror's ideological framework was sound

docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.3 — Political Systems and Democratic Institutions Unit: 9 — 1990s–2000s (Globalization, 9/11, Contemporary) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did American political institutions respond to the post-Cold War era and 9/11? What constitutional questions did the War on Terror raise?

docs.title: Key Political Moments — Unit 9
docs.text: Event Constitutional Issue Outcome Clinton impeachment (1998) Lying under oath about affair; partisan impeachment Acquitted by Senate; precedent: partisan impeachment possible Bush v. Gore (2000) Supreme Court stops Florida recount; Bush wins 5-4 partisan ruling; Court's legitimacy questioned PATRIOT Act (2001) Broad surveillance authority; limited judicial oversight Passed 98-1 in Senate six weeks after 9/11 Department of Homeland Security New cabinet department; largest reorganization since 1947 New bureaucracy; intelligence coordination Authorization for Use of Military Force (AUMF, 2001) Gave president authority to use force against those responsible for 9/11 Used to justify everything from Afghanistan to drone strikes Abu Ghraib (2004) US military tortures Iraqi prisoners; photographs surface International credibility; Geneva Conventions debate Hamdi v. Rumsfeld (2004) Can US citizen be held as enemy combatant? Supreme Court says no; due process required Citizens United (2010) Corporations can spend unlimited money in elections Campaign finance transformed; dark money

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.3 in Unit 9 is about democratic backsliding — or the fear of it.

Example argument move:

"Bush v. Gore (2000) is the most controversial Supreme Court decision since Dred Scott. Five justices stopped a recount that might have changed the presidential election, then explicitly said the ruling applied only to this case and couldn't be used as precedent. A court that decides a presidential election on partisan lines and refuses to set precedent raises serious questions about institutional legitimacy — questions that have only intensified since."

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.4 Social & Economic Change · Unit 9 1990s–2000s
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.4 — Social and Economic Change Unit: 9 — 1990s–2000s (Globalization, 9/11, Contemporary) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: How did globalization, the tech boom, 9/11, and the 2008 financial crisis change American society and economy? Who benefited, who was harmed?

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: The 1990s–2000s produced the most rapid economic and cultural change since industrialization. Globalization redistributed manufacturing jobs globally. The dot-com boom created enormous wealth in tech — then crashed. 9/11 changed how Americans thought about security. The 2008 financial crisis wiped out middle-class wealth accumulated over decades. These changes happened fast and were experienced very differently depending on class, race, and location.

docs.title: Key Social and Economic Changes — Unit 9
docs.text: Change What Happened Who Was Affected Tech boom / dot-com (1990s) Internet economy; tech stock explosion; NASDAQ crashes 2000 Tech workers boom; traditional retail decline NAFTA (1994) Free trade agreement US-Canada-Mexico Manufacturing jobs move to Mexico; consumer prices lower Globalization acceleration WTO; China enters global trade; US manufacturing hollows out Working class devastated; consumer prices lower 9/11 and aftermath 3,000 killed; security state expands; two wars Muslim Americans face discrimination; veterans' lives changed 2008 financial crisis Housing bubble collapses; banks fail; recession Worst since Depression; 8 million jobs lost; Black/Latino wealth wiped out disproportionately Opioid epidemic (begins 1990s) Pharmaceutical companies oversell opioids; addiction crisis Rural white communities particularly affected Income inequality Top 1% share grows dramatically; middle class stagnates Everyone below top; especially working class

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.4 in Unit 9 is about proximity — these events are recent or ongoing.

Example argument move:

"The 2008 financial crisis is often described as affecting 'everyone.' It didn't affect everyone equally. Black and Latino families had a higher percentage of their wealth in home equity (because they were excluded from stock market wealth-building). When housing prices collapsed, they lost a disproportionate share of their net worth. The racial wealth gap — already large from decades of exclusion — widened dramatically. Understanding the 2008 crisis means understanding who bore the cost of decisions they didn't make."

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.5 Causes & Effects · Unit 9 1990s–2000s
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.5 — Causes and Effects of Historical Events Unit: 9 — 1990s–2000s (Globalization, 9/11, Contemporary) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What caused 9/11, the Iraq War, and the 2008 financial crisis? What did they produce?

docs.title: Causes of 9/11
docs.text: Cause Explanation US foreign policy in Middle East Support for Israel; troops in Saudi Arabia after Gulf War; support for authoritarian governments Cold War blowback US armed mujahideen in Afghanistan (including Bin Laden's networks) to fight Soviets Al-Qaeda's ideology Islamist extremism; opposition to US presence in Muslim lands Intelligence failures CIA and FBI had warnings they didn't connect; "failure of imagination"

docs.title: Effects of 9/11
docs.text: Effect Significance Afghanistan War (2001–2021) Longest US war in history; Taliban returns to power after US withdrawal Iraq War (2003–2011) Based on false WMD intelligence; 4,500 Americans dead; ISIS emerges from power vacuum PATRIOT Act / surveillance state Mass surveillance; Snowden revelations (2013) Department of Homeland Security New bureaucracy; TSA; security theater Muslim American discrimination Hate crimes; surveillance; civil liberties violations $6+ trillion spent Wars and security costs; national debt

docs.title: Causes of 2008 Financial Crisis
docs.text: Cause Explanation Financial deregulation (1990s–2000s) Glass-Steagall repealed (1999); banks take more risk Subprime mortgage lending Banks lend to people who can't afford it; package and sell risk Mortgage-backed securities Complex financial instruments obscure the risk Rating agency failure Moody's, S&P rate toxic assets AAA Housing bubble Prices rise on speculation; inevitable collapse

docs.title: Effects of 2008 Crisis
docs.text: Effect Significance 8 million jobs lost Worst recession since Depression Bank bailouts ($700B TARP) Unpopular; debate about who got saved Dodd-Frank (2010) Financial regulation; partially repealed 2018 Tea Party / Occupy Wall Street Populist responses on right and left Income inequality accelerates Wealth recovers faster for top than for bottom

docs.title: The Argument Move
docs.text:
US.6_12.5 in Unit 9 is about blowback — actions creating their opposite.

Example argument move:

"The Iraq War was intended to stabilize the Middle East and spread democracy. Its effects included: the creation of ISIS (which emerged from the power vacuum after Saddam's removal), the destabilization of the entire region, the strengthening of Iran (Saddam had been Iran's counterweight), and the erosion of American credibility globally. This is blowback — an intervention producing outcomes opposite to its stated goals. It's one of the most important causal patterns in modern history."

docs.text:

docs.title: Skinny — US.6_12.6 Past to Present · Unit 9 1990s–2000s
docs.text: Standard: US.6_12.6 — Connecting History to the Present Unit: 9 — 1990s–2000s (Globalization, 9/11, Contemporary) Grade: 10 · US History

docs.title: What This Standard Asks
docs.text: What from the 1990s–2000s is still present today? This is the closest unit to students' lived experience — the connections are most direct here.

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text: Unit 9 isn't quite history yet — it's recent enough that many of its events are still unfolding. The Afghanistan War ended in 2021. The 2008 financial crisis shaped the economy students' families live in. The surveillance infrastructure built after 9/11 is still active. The political polarization of the 1990s accelerated through the 2010s and 2020s. Understanding Unit 9 means understanding the world students actually live in.

docs.title: Then → Now Connections
docs.text: Then (1990s–2000s) Now PATRIOT Act (2001) / NSA surveillance Still active; Section 215 expired but other authorities remain Iraq War / WMD false intelligence Trust in intelligence community still damaged Afghanistan War (2001) Ended 2021 with Taliban return; 20 years, same result 2008 financial crisis / bank bailouts Dodd-Frank partially repealed; similar risks accumulating Income inequality acceleration Now at highest since 1920s Social media emergence Now central to democracy, misinformation, mental health Globalization / manufacturing loss Reshoring debate; US-China trade war Opioid epidemic Still killing 80,000+/year; fentanyl crisis Political polarization Intensified; January 6, 2021; election denial Climate change (awareness growing) Now visible in weather; policy paralysis continues

docs.title: The Argument Move — Capstone Standard
docs.text:
US.6_12.6 in Unit 9 is the capstone argument of the entire two-year course. The question isn't just what connects to the present — it's what the whole arc of American history tells us about where we are now.

Example argument move:

"Students who have studied US History from industrialization to the present have seen the same patterns repeat: technological change produces economic dislocation that falls hardest on those with least power; crises produce rights violations justified as temporary that become permanent; the gap between what America claims to stand for and what it actually does is the central tension of the whole story. Understanding that pattern doesn't tell you what to do — but it tells you what questions to ask."

docs.title: This is also the right place to ask: What does the past owe the present?
docs.text: The full arc: Hammurabi to Malala; Hammurabi's Code to the UN UDHR; Pericles' Funeral Oration to MLK's "Letter from Birmingham Jail." What have we learned? What's been gained? What's still unfinished?

US.6_12.6 Unit 9 Skinny — SDA Commons · South High School This is the last skinny in the USH series. Well done getting here.

docs.title: Ancient Science and Technology — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.1 — Analyze historical achievements related to science and technology Era/Unit (if SS): Era 1 — Emergence of Civilizations and Religions Around the Globe ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.W.4 — Produce clear, coherent argument writing; 9-10.R.9 — Analyze informational texts NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Early civilizations didn't just survive — they invented. The technologies they created solved specific problems (feeding more people, tracking time, moving water) and ended up reshaping human society entirely.

  • Technology: A tool, method, or system that humans develop to solve a problem or improve their ability to do work.
  • Agriculture: The deliberate cultivation of crops and domestication of animals. The shift from foraging to farming (~10,000 BCE) is called the Neolithic Revolution — it made permanent settlements possible.
  • Irrigation: A system of channels, canals, or dikes that moves water from a source to crops. Mesopotamian farmers used irrigation to farm land far from the Tigris and Euphrates rivers.
  • Cuneiform: The world's earliest writing system, developed in Mesopotamia around 3200 BCE. Created by pressing a wedge-shaped reed into clay tablets; used for record-keeping.
  • Hieroglyphics: Egyptian writing system using pictorial symbols; used on monuments, papyrus, and tomb walls.
  • Metallurgy: The science of working with metals — smelting, alloying, shaping. The Bronze Age (~3300–1200 BCE) and Iron Age (~1200–550 BCE) are named for the dominant metals of each era.
  • Astronomy: The study of stars, planets, and celestial cycles. Early astronomers in Mesopotamia and Egypt used the sky to create calendars and predict seasonal changes for planting.
  • Pythagorean Theorem: The mathematical relationship a² + b² = c² in right triangles, formalized by Greek mathematician Pythagoras (~570–495 BCE); used in construction and navigation.
  • Aqueduct: A Roman engineering structure that transported water from distant sources into cities. Roman aqueducts moved millions of gallons of water daily without pumps — purely by gravity.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Identify whether a technology is a tool (physical object) or a system (organized method or process). - [ ] Explain what problem a specific ancient technology solved. - [ ] Distinguish between technologies that spread within one civilization vs. those that spread across civilizations through trade or conquest. - [ ] Identify the era in which a technology emerged (Bronze Age vs. Iron Age, etc.). - [ ] Connect a technology to a primary source that demonstrates its use.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can explain that the wheel was invented in Mesopotamia around 3500 BCE and that it made transporting heavy goods faster — without yet explaining how that changed trade networks or social stratification.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.1 — Analyze historical achievements related to science and technology

To meet this standard, you must move beyond naming inventions — you must analyze what they achieved. That means explaining the problem the technology solved, the social or political changes it caused, and what that tells us about the civilization that created it.

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by analyzing how writing (cuneiform) transformed Mesopotamian society beyond communication. Because scribes could now record laws, debts, and trade agreements, political authority could extend beyond what a ruler could personally oversee — written records made large-scale empire possible. The student explains both what writing did and what it enabled: bureaucracy, law codes (Hammurabi's Code), and long-distance trade with standardized contracts.

The Difference: A 2.0 student identifies that Mesopotamians invented cuneiform and used it to keep records. A 3.0 student explains why that invention made political and economic systems fundamentally different — that once you can write down laws and debts, you no longer need the king in the room.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must transfer your analysis across eras, civilizations, or time periods — connecting ancient technological achievement to consequences that echo today.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Cross-civilization comparison: Compare how Egyptian and Roman engineering (pyramids vs. aqueducts) reflect each civilization's different priorities and political systems. What does each choice of technology reveal about what each society valued? 2. Technology and inequality: Analyze how the development of metallurgy (bronze, then iron weapons) changed military power — and how military advantage translated into political domination and social hierarchy. 3. Long-term consequence: The Neolithic Revolution (the shift to agriculture) produced surpluses, which enabled specialization of labor, which led to social stratification. Trace this chain from technology to social structure. Does the same pattern apply to a modern technology (e.g., the printing press or the internet)?

4.0 Example: A student produces an argument that the development of the iron plow was a more significant achievement than the wheel because it enabled agriculture to expand into previously unworkable land — which directly caused population growth, territorial expansion, and the intensification of slavery as a labor system to work new farmland.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: The Agricultural Revolution — CrashCourse World History #1 · Episode in reading library Also: The Silk Road and Ancient Trade — CrashCourse World History #9 · Episode in reading library Additional: Mesopotamia: Crash Course World History #3 — coming soon in reading library

Key Timestamps (Agricultural Revolution ep): - [01:45]: The shift from foraging to farming — watch for WHY farming required more work per calorie but produced surpluses. Why it matters: Surplus = specialization = non-farming roles (scribes, soldiers, priests) possible. - [06:00]: Domestication of animals. Why it matters: Animal labor scales agricultural output; also produces persistent diseases that Europeans later exported to the Americas. - [10:30]: The consequences of settling — written records, taxes, armies. Why it matters: This is the direct link between agricultural technology and government.

Watch Prompt: As you watch, track this chain: What problem did the technology solve? → What did it make possible that wasn't possible before? → What were the unintended consequences?

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Hammurabi's Code — Written law as a technology of governance: analyze how the existence of a written code changed the administration of justice. What could NOT have been done without writing?
  • Silk Road and Trade Connections — Technology moved along trade routes: the silk road spread not just goods but techniques (paper-making, navigation tools, astronomical knowledge).

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose ONE ancient technology (writing, irrigation, metallurgy, or the wheel). Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) what problem it solved, (2) what social/political changes it caused, (3) why this qualifies as a significant historical achievement. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay arguing which single ancient technological achievement had the greatest impact on human civilization. Use evidence from at least two civilizations (e.g., Mesopotamia and Rome, or Egypt and Greece). 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute explanation: (1) What is the Neolithic Revolution and what technologies did it produce? (2) Pick one technology and explain what it made possible that wasn't possible before. (3) Why does this matter for understanding ancient political power? 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Technology Impact Map." Select three ancient technologies and for each: draw or find an image of the technology, label what problem it solved, and draw arrows showing at least two downstream social/political effects. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a "Technology Hall of Fame" video (2–3 minutes). Induct two ancient technologies into your hall of fame, explaining your selection criteria and why each technology transformed human civilization — not just improved it. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project that involved an ancient civilization. Write a 200-word reflection connecting your work to WH.6_12.1 by identifying how a specific technology shaped the events or people you studied.

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. The "Ancient Patent Office" — Design patent applications for three ancient inventions (in the style of a real patent: problem statement, solution description, diagram, novelty claim). Include a 1-paragraph analysis of what each patent reveals about the civilization's priorities.
  2. Technology Timeline with Consequences — Build an annotated timeline of 8–10 ancient technologies from 10,000 BCE to 500 CE. For each entry: name the technology, the civilization, the date, and one sentence about its most significant historical consequence.
  3. Comparative Engineering Report — Write a formal 2-page "engineering analysis" comparing Egyptian pyramid construction to Roman aqueduct construction. Focus on: the problem each solved, the scale of organization required, what it reveals about each civilization's values and political structure.

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Identify and describe three ancient technologies (one from each of: agriculture, writing/record-keeping, and construction/engineering). For each, name the civilization and approximate date.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Choose one technology and write a paragraph explaining both what it achieved AND one major social or political change it caused — beyond just "it made things easier."
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word argument connecting an ancient technology's development to a consequence still visible in today's world. Use at least one specific piece of evidence.

docs.title: How Early Belief Systems Shaped Civilization — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.2 — Explain historical changes related to religions and ideologies Era/Unit (if SS): Era 1 — Emergence of Civilizations and Religions Around the Globe ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.R.9 — Analyze informational/argumentative elements, author POV and bias NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Before we look at how civilizations grew, we have to understand the "why" behind their actions. These terms help us describe what people believed and how those beliefs turned into rules for living.

  • Belief System: A set of shared ideas, values, and practices that a group of people holds to be true. It provides a roadmap for how to behave and what to expect from the world.
  • Monotheism: The belief in only one god (e.g., early Judaism).
  • Polytheism: The belief in multiple gods (e.g., ancient Greek or Egyptian religions).
  • Ideology: A system of ideas and ideals, especially one which forms the basis for economic or political theory. While a belief system is often spiritual, an ideology is often about how to organize a government or society.
  • Primary Source: An immediate, first-hand account of an event or time period (e.g., a diary, a physical artifact like a crown, or a law code carved in stone).
  • Code of Law: A systematic collection of laws and rules. Example: Hammurabi’s Code was one of the earliest sets of laws, establishing that "an eye for an eye" was the standard for justice to maintain social order.
  • Dharma (Hindu concept): The religious and moral law governing personal conduct; it is the "right way" of living according to one's role in the universe.
  • The Four Noble Truths (Buddhism): A set of teachings by the Buddha explaining that suffering exists, has a cause (attachment), can be ended, and has a specific path to follow.
  • Afterlife / Ka (Egyptian concept): The belief in life after death. The Ka was the spiritual "double" or life force of a person; Egyptians believed they had to preserve the body so the Ka could survive.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Identify the difference between a religious belief and a political ideology. - [ ] Distinguish between primary sources (like the Book of the Dead) and secondary sources (like a modern history textbook). - [ ] Locate specific laws within Hammurabi’s Code. - [ ] Define how "fear" or "hope" in a religion can influence how a government makes rules. - [ ] Identify common themes across different cultures (e.g., the desire for order).

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can identify that Hammurabi’s Code was a way to organize society and define what "polytheism" means in the context of ancient Mesopotamia.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.2 — Explain historical changes related to religions and ideologies

To meet this standard, you must move beyond just naming a religion; you must explain how that belief system actually shaped the way people lived, worked, and were governed. You are looking for the "cause and effect" between what people believed about the gods/universe and the laws they wrote down to manage their cities.

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by analyzing how the Egyptian belief in the Afterlife (Ka) directly shaped their economy and labor; because they believed the soul needed a preserved body and grave goods, they built massive pyramids and developed complex systems of state-sponsored construction. Similarly, a student might explain how Hammurabi’s Code wasn't just a list of punishments, but a way to create "social order" by reflecting the belief that the King ruled with divine authority to keep the universe in balance.

The Difference: A 2.0 student can define what "Dharma" is; a 3.0 student can explain how the concept of Dharma influenced social roles and daily behaviors in ancient Indian society.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must synthesize information by comparing different civilizations or connecting ancient ideologies to modern-day structures.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Comparative Analysis: Compare how the Egyptian "Afterlife" and the Buddhist "Four Noble Truths" offer different ways of dealing with the concept of human mortality. 2. Cross-Era Connection: Analyze how an ancient "Code of Law" (like Hammurabi's) shares similarities or differences with a modern legal system in terms of its underlying ideology (e.g., justice vs. retribution). 3. Synthesis of Power: Evaluate how the transition from polytheism to monotheism in various regions changed the way kings and leaders justified their right to rule.

4.0 Example: A student produces a comparative essay arguing that while both Egyptian and Mesopotamian civilizations were polytheistic, the Egyptian focus on the afterlife led to a more centralized, state-run economy for monument building, whereas the Mesopotamian focus on city-state protection led to a legal system focused on civil commerce and property rights.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: Mesopotamia: CrashCourse World History #3 — https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sohXPx_XZ6Y Also: The Indus Valley Civilization — https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=n7ndRwqJYDM

Key Timestamps: - [04:12] (Mesopotamia): Watch the section on the "Ziggurat." Why it matters: It shows how religious architecture was the literal and figurative center of city life, blending religion with urban planning. - [08:45] (Mesopotamia): Note the discussion on Hammurabi’s Code. Why it matters: This is a primary example of an ideology (justice/order) being codified into law to stabilize a growing population. - [12:30] (Indus Valley): Observe the mention of urban planning and standardized weights. Why it matters: It shows how shared social values (even if we don't know their specific gods) led to organized, massive city structures.

Watch Prompt: As you watch, keep a T-chart in your notes. On the left side, list "Religious Beliefs" mentioned; on the right side, write down the "Social Result" (e.g., if they believed in many gods $\rightarrow$ they built huge temples).

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Hammurabi's Code — This text shows how Mesopotamian law used "retributive justice" to maintain social order under a divine mandate.
  • Book of the Dead — This primary source reveals the Egyptian obsession with the afterlife and the role of priests in navigating the journey of the soul.
  • Dhammapada — These verses provide insight into how early Buddhist followers sought to escape suffering through internal discipline rather than external ritual.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Select ONE primary source (Hammurabi's Code, Book of the Dead, or Dhammapada). Write a 3-paragraph analysis explaining how that specific text reveals the core beliefs of that culture and how those beliefs dictated their laws or social hierarchy. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400-600 word essay comparing two civilizations (e.g., Egypt and Mesopotamia). Explain how their different religious views on the "afterlife" or "divine order" resulted in different ways of organizing their governments and labor forces. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute "podcast segment" where you explain: 1) What a belief system is, 2) How Hammurabi's Code functioned as an ideology for justice, and 3) One way the Egyptian view of the Ka influenced their construction projects. 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Comparison Infographic." You must include two side-by-side panels (Egypt vs. Mesopotamia). Each panel must include a visual representation of a religious site, a quote from a primary source, and a bulleted list showing how that belief shaped daily life for the average person. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a "Time Traveler's Guide." Produce a 2-minute video where you play a guide explaining to modern tourists why the pyramids are so big (Egyptian beliefs) or why Hammurabi’s laws were so strict (Mesopotamian beliefs). 6 Portfolio Annotation Select your previous work on "Early Civilizations." Write a 200-word reflection connecting your past work to WH.6_12.2 by identifying one specific way that religion acted as the "glue" for those societies' social structures.

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. The "Law & Lore" Museum Exhibit — Create a physical or digital museum display where you curate three artifacts (one from each culture). Each must include a "curator's card" explaining how that item represents a specific belief system and how it influenced the civilization’s power structure.
  2. Comparative Policy Brief — Write a formal policy brief for a fictional "Global Heritage Council." You must argue which ancient civilization had the most effective way of using religion to maintain social order, using evidence from Hammurabi's Code and the Book of the Dead.
  3. The Sacred City Map — Create an interactive digital map (using tools like Genially or Google My Maps). Users can click on different "zones" in a city (The Temple, The Marketplace, The Palace) to see how religious beliefs dictated the rules for each area.

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Identify and define three key terms: Monotheism, Polytheism, and Primary Source within your notes.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph explaining how the Egyptian belief in the Ka led to specific social behaviors (like mummification or pyramid building), citing the Book of the Dead.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Create a comparative chart showing how two different civilizations used their unique beliefs to solve the same problem (e.g., "How do we keep people following the rules?").

docs.title: How Greek and Roman Politics Shape Our World — Skinny
What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Before we can analyze how these systems changed history, you must understand the "building blocks" of political science. These are the terms used to describe how people organize power and make decisions.

Key Terms: * Democracy: A system of government where power is held by the people. * Direct Democracy (Athens): Citizens vote directly on laws and policies (e.g., "Everyone in the room votes today"). * Representative Democracy (Modern): Citizens elect officials to make decisions on their behalf. * Republic: A form of government where citizens elect representatives to rule, but it is not a "pure" democracy because not every citizen has an equal say in every decision. * Senate: A deliberative body or council of people who represent a region or group and make laws. (The Roman Senate was the primary governing body). * Assembly (Ekklesia): In ancient Athens, this was the gathering of all citizens to make decisions about the city-state. * Citizenship: The status of being a legal member of a community with specific rights (like voting) and responsibilities (like paying taxes or serving in the military). * Note: In both Athens and Rome, "citizenship" was exclusive—it did not include women, enslaved people, or most non-citizens. * Oligarchy: A government ruled by a small group of people (usually based on wealth or family status). * Tyranny: Rule by one person who takes power by force and uses it unfairly. * Veto: From the Latin veto, meaning "I forbid." It is the power to stop a law or action from happening. * Checks and Balances: A system that ensures no single branch of government becomes too powerful by giving other branches the ability to "check" (limit) its power. * Primary Source: An immediate, first-hand account of an event or time period (e.g., a speech given in 400 BCE, not a textbook written in 2024).

Core Skills Checklist: - [ ] Define the difference between a Direct Democracy and a Republic. - [ ] Identify who was included—and excluded—from political life in Athens. - [ ] Explain how the Roman Senate functioned as a governing body. - [ ] Identify the "Veto" as a tool used to balance power. - [ ] Distinguish between an Oligarchy and a Democracy.

2.0 Proficiency Example: A student at this level can identify that while both Athens and Rome influenced modern government, they were different: Athens practiced a direct democracy for its citizens, while Rome established a republic with a senate.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
Standard WH.6_12.3: Analyze the effects of different political systems on people.

To meet this standard, you must move beyond just defining these terms; you must explain how they affected real people. You need to look at how a system like the Roman Republic or Athenian Democracy created winners and losers—specifically looking at how power was distributed among men, women, enslaved people, and non-citizens.

3.0 Proficiency Example: A student at this level can analyze how the Roman Republic’s structure affected different groups differently. For example: "While the Roman Republic gave male citizens a voice through the Senate and Assemblies, it systematically excluded women and enslaved people from any political power, creating a hierarchy where only wealthy, land-owning men held the most influence."

The Difference: A 2.0 student can tell you what a "Senate" is; a 3.0 student can explain how the existence of a Senate limited or expanded the power of certain groups of people in ancient times.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach the 4.0 level, you must perform synthesis. This means taking two different ideas and combining them to create a new insight about our world today.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Comparative Analysis: Compare the Athenian "Assembly" with modern-day "Town Hall" meetings or local government sessions. How do both systems attempt to give people a voice? 2. Evolutionary Tracking: Trace the concept of the "Veto" from its origins in the Roman Republic to its current use in the United States government (e.g., the Presidential Veto). 3. Systemic Critique: Argue which system—the Athenian Direct Democracy or the Roman Republic—provided a more stable foundation for a large, diverse population, and explain why.

4.0 Proficiency Example: A student at this level can synthesize these ideas: "While Athens' direct democracy allowed for immediate citizen participation, it was only sustainable for a small city-state. In contrast, the Roman Republic’s use of representative bodies like the Senate created a scalable system that influenced modern republics by balancing the need for representation with the practicalities of governing a large territory."

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video 1: Ancient Greece (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OP4SL0hkRBE) * [03:45] – Discussion on the Ekklesia. Note how this allowed for direct participation but was limited by strict citizenship laws. * [07:12] – Explanation of Athenian Democracy's unique "lottery" system for government roles. * [10:30] – Analysis of who was excluded (women, slaves, metics), which is vital for the 3.0 standard of analyzing effects on different groups.

Video 2: Roman Republic (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ePSME3kJBGQ) * [04:20] – The role of the Senate and how it provided stability for the Republic. * [08:15] – The "Conflict of the Orders" (Patricians vs. Plebeians) and how this shaped the rights of different classes. * [12:40] – The introduction of the Veto as a mechanism to check the power of magistrates.

Watch Prompt: As you watch, keep a T-chart in your notes. On the left side, list "Features of Athenian Democracy." On the right side, list "Features of the Roman Republic." Under each feature, write one sentence explaining who that specific feature helped or harmed (e.g., "The Senate gave power to wealthy landowners but limited the influence of the poor").

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Pericles Funeral Oration: Use this to see how a leader in a direct democracy spoke directly to the citizens about their rights and duties.
  • Twelve Tables Rome: Use this to understand the first attempt at written laws for all Roman citizens, showing the move toward a structured Republic.
  • Roman Republic Polybius: Use this to see how early historians analyzed the "mixed" nature of the Roman government (Consuls, Senate, and Assemblies).

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text: # Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Write a 300-word analysis comparing the "inclusion" levels of Athens and Rome. Use at least one primary source (Pericles or the Twelve Tables) to support your claim about who was allowed to participate in government. 2 Extended Writing Write a 500-word argumentative essay: "Which system—the Athenian Direct Democracy or the Roman Republic—had a more lasting impact on modern Western governance?" You must include a counter-argument regarding the limitations of both systems (e.g., the exclusion of women). 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute "podcast segment" where you explain to a peer how the Roman "Veto" and the Athenian "Assembly" serve different purposes in their respective governments. 4 Visual/Creative Create an annotated Venn Diagram. One circle is Athens, one is Rome. In the overlapping center, place features they shared (like citizenship requirements). In the outer sections, highlight how each system uniquely impacted its citizens. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a "News Report" video. You are a reporter in 44 BCE reporting on the Roman Republic and a reporter in 431 BCE reporting on Athens. Compare how your "citizens" feel about their rights in each city-state. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous essay on Greek/Roman history. Annotate it using three colors: Green for evidence of 2.0 (definitions), Yellow for 3.0 (impact on groups), and Pink for 4.0 (connections to modern systems).

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
1. The "Legacy" Infographic: Create a digital infographic showing the "Evolution of Power." Trace three concepts (Democracy, Senate, Veto) from their ancient roots to their current use in the U.S. Capitol.
  2. Comparative Case Study: A 4-page research paper analyzing how the exclusion of women and enslaved people in both Athens and Rome shaped the "definition" of a citizen for centuries.
  3. The "Trial of Democracy" Debate: A recorded debate where you argue whether modern representative democracy is a "diluted" version of Athenian direct democracy or a "refined" evolution of the Roman Republic.

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Identify and define 5 key terms (Democracy, Republic, Senate, Veto, Citizenship) and explain the difference between Athens and Rome's versions of these systems.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph explaining how the Roman Republic specifically affected the power balance between Patricians and Plebeians using evidence from the Twelve Tables.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Create a "Synthesis Statement" that explains how modern American government uses a combination of both Greek and Roman ideas to solve the problem of governing a large population.

docs.title: Social, Cultural, and Economic Development in Ancient Civilizations — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.4 — Analyze the influence of social, cultural, and economic development on individuals Era/Unit (if SS): Era 1 — Emergence of Civilizations and Religions Around the Globe ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.R.9 — Analyze informational texts, author POV; 9-10.W.3 — Explanatory/narrative writing NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Civilizations didn't just build walls and write laws — they created systems that shaped what individual people could do, own, believe, and become. Understanding how social, cultural, and economic systems worked in the ancient world means understanding why individuals in those societies lived the way they did.

  • Social Stratification: The hierarchical organization of people in a society into layers based on wealth, birth, occupation, or legal status. Most ancient civilizations had rigid hierarchies where a person's position at birth largely determined their life.
  • Social Class: A group of people who share similar economic and social status. Common ancient classes: rulers, priests, soldiers, artisans/craftspeople, farmers, slaves.
  • Slavery: A labor system in which people are legally owned by others and forced to work without compensation. Slavery existed in virtually every ancient civilization and was often connected to conquest (prisoners of war became enslaved people).
  • Patriarchy: A social system in which men hold primary power over political, cultural, and economic institutions. Most ancient civilizations were patriarchal; women's legal rights and social roles were severely limited.
  • Trade Network: A system of routes and relationships through which goods, ideas, and technologies move between regions. Ancient networks: the Silk Road (connecting China to the Mediterranean), Saharan caravan routes, Mediterranean sea trade.
  • Surplus: More food or goods than a society needs to immediately consume. Agricultural surplus enabled specialization of labor — when not everyone had to farm, some could become potters, scribes, soldiers, or priests.
  • Specialization of Labor: The division of work so that different people focus on different tasks. Specialization made individual workers more skilled but also made them dependent on the broader economy.
  • Cultural Diffusion: The spread of cultural elements (ideas, styles, technologies, religions) from one society to another through trade, migration, conquest, or intermarriage.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Identify where an individual would fall in a specific ancient society's class structure. - [ ] Distinguish between how social class was determined (birth, occupation, wealth, conquest) in at least two ancient civilizations. - [ ] Explain what "surplus" has to do with social stratification. - [ ] Identify at least one specific example of cultural diffusion along an ancient trade route. - [ ] Describe how economic systems (barter, tribute, early coinage) functioned in ancient societies.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can identify that ancient Egyptian society had pharaohs at the top, priests below them, and farmers and enslaved people at the bottom — without yet explaining how the belief system (divine kingship) was used to justify and maintain that hierarchy.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.4 — Analyze the influence of social, cultural, and economic development on individuals

To meet this standard, you must connect the large-scale systems (social hierarchies, trade networks, economic structures) to what they meant for individual people's lived experience. You are analyzing how a system shaped what was possible for a specific person — what they could own, who they could marry, what work they could do, and whether they had legal rights.

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by analyzing how the Roman economic system of latifundia (large slave-worked agricultural estates) shaped the lives of free small farmers. As wealthy Romans bought up land and worked it with enslaved people captured in wars, small Roman farmers couldn't compete economically — they were pushed off the land, drifted into Rome as unemployed poor citizens, and became dependent on government grain distributions ("bread and circuses"). The student explains how an economic development (large-scale slave agriculture) directly reshaped the lives of individual Roman farmers who were legally free but economically destroyed.

The Difference: A 2.0 student knows that Rome had slavery and that slaves worked farms. A 3.0 student explains what happened to the free Roman farmers because of slavery — how the economic system affected people who weren't enslaved.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must synthesize across civilizations or time periods, evaluating how similar social/economic structures produced different outcomes for individuals in different contexts — or similar outcomes across very different civilizations.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Cross-civilization class comparison: Compare how social stratification worked in ancient Egypt vs. ancient Athens. In Athens, male citizens had political rights — in Egypt, position was more rigid. What accounts for the difference? What does it reveal about each civilization's underlying values? 2. Trade and cultural identity: Analyze how the Silk Road changed what it meant to be a citizen in a Roman or Han Chinese city. What goods, ideas, and beliefs traveled along with silk and spices? How did those imports change daily life and cultural identity? 3. Who benefits, who pays: Choose one ancient economic system (tribute, slavery, or early coinage) and analyze who benefited from it and who bore the costs. Is there a modern economic parallel you can identify?

4.0 Example: A student argues that while Athenian democracy is celebrated as a political achievement, it was economically dependent on slavery — approximately 1/3 of Athens' population was enslaved — meaning the political freedom of citizens was structurally funded by the unfreedom of enslaved workers. The student connects this to a contemporary debate about the relationship between political freedom and economic exploitation.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: The Silk Road and Ancient Trade — CrashCourse World History #9 · Episode in reading library Also: The Agricultural Revolution — CrashCourse World History #1 · Episode in reading library Also: Alexander the Great — CrashCourse WH #8 · Episode in reading library

Key Timestamps (Silk Road ep): - [02:00]: What actually traveled on the Silk Road — not just silk. Why it matters: Cultural diffusion is the mechanism by which social and economic development spreads across civilizations. - [07:30]: Disease and the Silk Road. Why it matters: Trade networks move more than goods — the Antonine Plague (~165 CE) and later the Black Death traveled along trade routes, with massive social consequences. - [11:00]: The role of merchants and their social status. Why it matters: In Rome and China, merchants were often lower-status despite being economically powerful — the gap between wealth and social prestige is a recurring theme.

Watch Prompt: Track how trade changed two things: what goods were available to ordinary people, and what ideas moved between civilizations. For each example in the video, ask: Who benefited from this exchange? Who was harmed or left out?

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Hammurabi's Code — Hammurabi's Code is as much a document about social and economic relationships as it is about law. Read the sections on debt, labor, and women's status. What do these laws reveal about how ancient Babylonian society was organized?
  • Silk Road Episode — Use the CrashCourse episode as a secondary source analysis: what does it claim about how trade affected social development, and what evidence does it use?

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose ONE ancient civilization. Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) how its social hierarchy was organized, (2) how the economic system shaped individual lives — specifically the lives of a farmer, a craftsperson, or an enslaved person, and (3) what belief system was used to justify the hierarchy. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay: "Who paid the price for ancient civilization?" Choose one ancient economic achievement (Silk Road trade, Roman agriculture, Egyptian monument building) and argue who bore the costs that made it possible. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute explanation: (1) What is social stratification and how did it work in one specific ancient civilization? (2) How did the economic system of that civilization affect someone at the bottom of the hierarchy? (3) What role did cultural or religious beliefs play in maintaining the social order? 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Day in the Life" visual for three different people in one ancient civilization: one elite, one middle-status worker, and one enslaved or lowest-status person. For each, show their housing, food access, work, legal status, and religious role. Include a written explanation of what economic forces created those differences. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute video documentary: "The Hidden Costs of Ancient Civilization." Profile two groups who paid the costs that made a famous ancient achievement possible (e.g., the enslaved people who built the pyramids, or the farmers who were displaced by Roman slave agriculture). 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project on ancient civilizations. Write a 200-word reflection identifying how social or economic development shaped the specific people or events you studied — and whether your original work overlooked anyone's perspective.

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. The "Social Ladder" Illustrated Book — Create a 6-page illustrated guide to social stratification in one ancient civilization. Each page covers one social class: what they did, what they owned, what legal rights they had, and what belief justified their position. Final page: your argument about whether the system was "just" by any standard you choose to define.
  2. Trade Route Consequence Map — Create an annotated map tracing one ancient trade route (Silk Road, Mediterranean, Saharan). For each civilization the route connected, annotate: what they exported, what they imported, and one specific social or cultural change that resulted from the exchange.
  3. The "Who Benefits?" Economic Analysis — Choose three ancient economic systems (tribute, slavery, early coinage). For each, create a T-chart showing who benefited and who paid the costs. Write a 1-paragraph conclusion arguing which system was most exploitative and why.

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Draw and label a social hierarchy pyramid for one ancient civilization. Include at least four distinct levels and identify what determined each person's place (birth, occupation, conquest, gender).
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph explaining how one specific economic development (agricultural surplus, the slave economy, or the Silk Road) changed what was possible — or impossible — for individuals at the bottom of the social hierarchy.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word argument connecting an ancient social or economic structure to a modern parallel. What is similar, what is different, and what does the comparison reveal?
