docs.title: Causes and Effects of Global Events in the Ancient World — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.5 — Analyze causes and effects of global events using primary and/or secondary sources Era/Unit (if SS): Era 1 — Emergence of Civilizations and Religions Around the Globe ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.W.4 — Argument writing; 9-10.IR.1–5 — Research and source evaluation NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
History doesn't just "happen" — events have causes that explain why they occurred, and effects that show what changed as a result. In the ancient world, events that seemed local (a drought, a military defeat, a king's death) often had consequences that reached across entire regions.

  • Cause: The reason(s) an event happened. Causes can be immediate (the direct trigger) or underlying (the deeper conditions that made the event possible).
  • Effect: The result or consequence of an event. Effects can be short-term (immediate, visible changes) or long-term (changes that unfold over decades or centuries).
  • Primary Source: A document, artifact, or record created by someone who directly witnessed or participated in an event. Examples: Hammurabi's Code, Egyptian papyrus records, Greek military accounts.
  • Secondary Source: An interpretation or analysis of events created after the fact by someone who wasn't there. Examples: a textbook chapter on the fall of Rome, a documentary about ancient Greece.
  • Source Reliability: How trustworthy a source is, based on factors like author's purpose, proximity to events, potential bias, and corroboration by other sources.
  • Empire: A large political unit that controls many peoples, lands, and cultures under a single authority, usually through military conquest and political domination.
  • Plague/Epidemic: A widespread disease outbreak. The Antonine Plague (~165–180 CE) killed 5–10 million people across the Roman Empire, destabilizing agriculture, the military, and trade.
  • Migration: Large-scale movement of people from one region to another — often caused by drought, military defeat, famine, or political instability; often causes cascading effects in receiving regions.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Distinguish between an immediate cause and an underlying cause for a specific ancient event. - [ ] Identify whether a source about an ancient event is primary or secondary, and explain what that distinction means for reliability. - [ ] List at least two effects of a major ancient event (short-term and long-term). - [ ] Explain what "multiple causation" means — that most historical events have more than one cause. - [ ] Identify who wrote a primary source, who they wrote it for, and what that tells you about potential bias.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can state that Rome fell because of military invasions from Germanic tribes — without analyzing the underlying causes (economic instability, overextension, internal political conflict, plague) that made Rome vulnerable to those invasions.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.5 — Analyze causes and effects of global events using primary and/or secondary sources

To meet this standard, you must use primary and/or secondary sources to support a cause-and-effect analysis. You're not just listing causes and effects — you're arguing for the most significant causes and using sources as evidence to support your analysis.

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by analyzing the fall of the Western Roman Empire with multiple causes: not just the Germanic invasions (immediate cause) but the Antonine Plague (which killed an estimated 10% of the population and depleted the military), the economic strain of maintaining an overstretched empire (taxation rose as the empire expanded, which drove small farmers off the land), and internal political instability (the "Crisis of the Third Century" saw 26 different emperors in 50 years). The student uses at least one primary or secondary source as evidence — for example, citing Tacitus's accounts of Germanic military pressure or citing a secondary historian's analysis of plague mortality data.

The Difference: A 2.0 student lists Germanic invasions as the cause of Rome's fall. A 3.0 student identifies multiple causes, ranks them by importance, and uses at least one source to support the analysis.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate the quality of your sources (not just cite them), synthesize multiple sources with conflicting interpretations, or draw connections between ancient cause-and-effect chains and contemporary events.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Source evaluation: Find two different secondary sources with different interpretations of why Rome fell (e.g., economic decline vs. military weakness vs. cultural change). Evaluate which interpretation is better supported by primary source evidence. Which do you find more convincing, and why? 2. Cross-civilization causation: The Persian Wars (~490–479 BCE) had far-reaching effects on Greek culture — the victory produced the cultural confidence that fueled the Classical Age of Athens. Trace the chain from military event to cultural consequence, using primary sources (Herodotus, Thucydides) to support your analysis. 3. Ancient to modern: Identify an ancient cause-and-effect chain that is still visible in today's world. Example: Alexander the Great's conquests spread Greek language and culture across three continents — which is why the New Testament was written in Greek. What does this tell us about how long historical effects can last?

4.0 Example: A student argues that the Antonine Plague was the most under-recognized cause of Rome's decline by analyzing its multiple effects: military manpower shortage that required the empire to hire Germanic mercenaries (who later became the invaders), economic contraction from loss of agricultural labor, and destabilization of the succession system (Marcus Aurelius broke the "five good emperors" tradition by appointing his own son Commodus, partly because he feared the plague had shortened his own life). The student uses Galen's medical writings (primary source) and modern demographic estimates (secondary) to build the argument.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: The Agricultural Revolution — CrashCourse World History #1 · Episode in reading library Also: The Silk Road and Ancient Trade — CrashCourse World History #9 · Episode in reading library Also: Fall of Rome — CrashCourse WH #12 · Episode in reading library

Key Timestamps (Silk Road ep): - [07:30]: Disease spreading along trade routes. Why it matters: The Antonine Plague and later the Black Death both spread along the Silk Road — trade that created prosperity also enabled catastrophic disease transmission. - [11:00]: What happened when the Silk Road contracted (due to political instability). Why it matters: Shows how political causes (fall of empires) have economic effects that cascade across multiple civilizations simultaneously.

Watch Prompt: For any event mentioned in the video, ask yourself three questions: (1) What was the underlying condition that made this possible? (2) What was the immediate trigger? (3) Who felt the effects first, and who felt them last?

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Hammurabi's Code — Use Hammurabi's Code as a primary source for cause-and-effect analysis. The laws themselves are evidence of problems the society faced: laws against debt slavery tell you debt slavery was common; laws about irrigation disputes tell you water rights were a source of conflict. What causes can you infer from the effects Hammurabi was trying to prevent?
  • Secondary source practice: When you read any textbook or summary about an ancient event, ask: Is the author claiming one primary cause, or multiple? Is that claim supported by specific evidence, or just asserted?

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose ONE major ancient event (fall of Rome, Alexander's conquests, the Persian Wars, or the Antonine Plague). Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) immediate cause, (2) two underlying causes, (3) one short-term and one long-term effect. Cite at least one specific primary or secondary source. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay arguing what the most significant cause of one ancient civilization's decline was. Challenge the most common explanation (e.g., "It wasn't just Germanic invasions that destroyed Rome — the empire had been dying from the inside for a century"). Use at least two sources. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute analysis: (1) Name an ancient event and explain its immediate cause. (2) Identify two underlying causes most people overlook. (3) Explain why the distinction between immediate and underlying causes matters for understanding history — and use one source to support a claim. 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Cause-and-Effect Web" for one major ancient event. The event goes in the center. At least 4 causes branch out (labeled immediate or underlying). At least 4 effects branch out (labeled short-term or long-term). Include one primary source quote as an annotation somewhere on the web. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute "Breaking News" video from ancient times. You're the reporter covering an event as it happens (e.g., the fall of Rome, the death of Alexander, the Antonine Plague). Include the immediate cause, interview a "witness" (you play the role), and close with a prediction of long-term effects. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project about an ancient event. Write a 200-word reflection: What caused the event you studied? Did your original work identify underlying causes, or only immediate ones? What would you add now?

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. The "Hidden Causes" Research Brief — Choose one ancient event whose commonly-taught cause is an oversimplification (e.g., "Rome fell because of Germanic invasions"). Research three additional causes and write a 1-page brief arguing that the conventional explanation is incomplete. Cite at least three sources (at least one primary).
  2. Cause-and-Effect Timeline with Sources — Build an annotated timeline of one ancient empire's rise and fall. For each turning point, note the causes, the effects, and cite the source where you found the evidence. Distinguish primary from secondary sources throughout.
  3. The "Ripple Effects" Documentary Script — Write a 3-minute documentary script tracing one ancient event's effects across at least two civilizations and two centuries. Show how the ripples kept moving long after the original event.

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Choose one ancient event. List: one immediate cause, one underlying cause, one short-term effect, one long-term effect. Identify whether each source you used was primary or secondary.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph analyzing multiple causes for one ancient event. Rank the causes by importance and defend your ranking with at least one specific piece of evidence from a primary or secondary source.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word evaluation of two sources on the same ancient event. Which is more reliable and why? What does each source reveal that the other doesn't?

  • Hammurabi's Code — Primary source for Era 1 cause-and-effect analysis

  • IR.1–5 Research Skills — Research and source evaluation bundle

How to use this skinny: 1. Start with the 2.0 vocabulary and processes. 2. Read the 3.0 example carefully. That's the target. 3. Choose your demonstration mode and begin gathering evidence. 4. Pull additional skinnies if you hit a gap. 5. Check your scale tasks as you complete them.

docs.title: Ancient Foundations to the Present — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.6 — Explain how past events connect to the present Era/Unit (if SS): Era 1 — Emergence of Civilizations and Religions Around the Globe ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.W.4 — Argument writing; 9-10.IR.1–5 — Research and source evaluation NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
The ancient world did not disappear when ancient civilizations fell. The decisions, systems, and ideas created thousands of years ago still shape the laws, governments, religions, and languages of today. To score at the 2.0 level, you need to identify specific ancient foundations that are still present in the modern world.

  • Legacy: What a civilization left behind — the lasting contributions to law, culture, language, religion, or political systems that continue to influence later societies.
  • Democracy: A political system in which citizens participate in governance. The Athenian version (5th century BCE) was limited to adult male citizens — but its principles of popular assembly, debate, and accountability influenced every democratic system that followed.
  • Republic: A government in which elected representatives make decisions on behalf of citizens. The Roman Republic (509–27 BCE) developed the Senate, separation of powers, and the concept of civic virtue — all directly borrowed by the framers of the U.S. Constitution.
  • Common Law / Legal Tradition: The principle that laws should be written down, publicly known, and consistently applied. Hammurabi's Code (~1754 BCE) and the Twelve Tables of Rome (~450 BCE) are ancestors of this tradition.
  • Monotheistic Religions: Judaism, Christianity, and Islam — all rooted in the ancient Middle East, specifically in the Abrahamic tradition that emerged in the ancient Near East. ~4 billion people today practice a religion with ancient origins in this region.
  • Alphabet: Most Western alphabets (Latin, Greek, Cyrillic) trace back to the Phoenician alphabet (~1050 BCE), which in turn influenced Greek, which influenced Roman/Latin, which became the basis for modern Western script.
  • Olympic Games: Originally a Greek religious festival at Olympia (~776 BCE). The modern Olympic movement (1896–present) consciously borrowed the name, the multi-sport format, and the ideal of international peaceful competition.
  • Philosophical Tradition: Greek philosophy — Socrates, Plato, Aristotle — created the frameworks for logic, ethics, political theory, and scientific method that still underlie Western intellectual traditions.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Identify at least three modern institutions, practices, or ideas with direct ancient roots. - [ ] Explain what "legacy" means and why identifying it matters for historical understanding. - [ ] Distinguish between a direct inheritance (e.g., the U.S. Senate was consciously modeled on Rome's) and an indirect influence (e.g., ancient Greek philosophy generally shaped Enlightenment thinking). - [ ] Identify a modern religion that has ancient roots and name the specific ancient source. - [ ] Read a modern law or government document and identify language or concepts with ancient precedents.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can state that the United States has a Senate because of ancient Rome — without explaining what specific features of the Roman Senate the founders consciously replicated, or why they chose Rome as a model rather than ancient Athens.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.6 — Explain how past events connect to the present

To meet this standard, you must explain the mechanism of the connection — not just assert that the ancient world influenced the modern one. You need to trace the specific path by which an ancient idea, institution, or practice survived, was transmitted, was transformed, and arrived in today's world.

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by explaining how the Roman concept of a republic influenced the U.S. Constitution — tracing the specific path: Roman Republic (509–27 BCE) → preserved in Latin texts through the medieval period → rediscovered during the Renaissance → incorporated into Enlightenment political theory (Montesquieu, Locke) → James Madison and the Constitutional Convention explicitly cited Roman precedents in The Federalist Papers → the U.S. Senate, the concept of a republic vs. a direct democracy, and the separation of legislative/executive/judicial power all echo Roman design choices. The student doesn't just say "Rome influenced America" — they trace the transmission chain.

The Difference: A 2.0 student knows the U.S. has a Senate like Rome. A 3.0 student explains the specific path by which the Roman model survived, was transmitted, and was deliberately borrowed — and what was changed vs. kept.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must critically evaluate the connection: not just trace it, but assess whether the modern version is a genuine continuation, a selective borrowing, or a distortion of the ancient original. You might also identify ancient ideas that should inform modern debates but are being ignored.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Critical evaluation: The founders cited ancient Rome as a model for the republic. But Rome was also a slave society whose republic lasted less than 500 years before becoming an empire. What did the founders borrow, what did they ignore, and what does that selective borrowing reveal about their own political goals? 2. Religious legacy and conflict: Three of the world's major religions (Judaism, Christianity, Islam) all have ancient roots in the same geographic region (the Levant). Trace how the shared origins of these faiths contributed to both cooperation and conflict across history — and explain how understanding those origins illuminates modern religious conflicts. 3. What we forgot: Identify one ancient idea or practice that has been lost or forgotten but could be valuable today. Argue why it matters. Examples: the Greek concept of isonomia (equality under the law for all citizens), the Iroquois Confederacy's democratic model, or ancient water management systems.

4.0 Example: A student argues that the Western legal tradition's concept of "innocent until proven guilty" is usually attributed to English common law but actually has roots in Roman legal procedure — specifically in the Digest of Justinian (534 CE), which codified the principle that "proof lies upon the one who affirms, not the one who denies." The student then evaluates what happened to that principle in the U.S. criminal justice system today (mass incarceration, plea bargain pressure), arguing that the nominal inheritance of the principle has been undermined in practice.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: The Agricultural Revolution — CrashCourse World History #1 · Episode in reading library Also: The Silk Road — CrashCourse World History #9 · Episode in reading library Additional: Ancient Greece — CrashCourse WH #5 — coming soon in reading library Additional: The Roman Empire, or Republic, or... — CrashCourse WH #10 — coming soon in reading library

Key Timestamps (Agricultural Revolution ep): - [09:00]: The spread of agriculture as a world-changing event still shaping today. Why it matters: The shift to farming created the population densities and food surpluses that made cities, governments, and civilizations possible — the entire modern world is downstream of this. - [12:00]: John Green's point about "the worst mistake in human history" debate. Why it matters: Forces you to evaluate whether ancient "progress" actually made everyone better off — a 4.0-level question about legacies.

Watch Prompt: For each ancient development mentioned, ask: Is this still with us today? In what form? Has it changed for the better, worse, or beyond recognition?

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Hammurabi's Code — This text is a direct ancestor of written legal tradition. Read several laws and ask: What modern legal principle does this resemble? What principle does it violate? Where did the "innocent until proven guilty" standard come from, and how does Hammurabi's approach compare?
  • Magna Carta — The Magna Carta (1215 CE) is an important bridge document: it connects ancient Roman concepts of law and rights to the modern constitutional tradition. Reading it alongside Hammurabi's Code shows the long continuity of the "rule of law" concept.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose ONE modern institution, practice, or concept with ancient roots (democracy, legal tradition, monotheistic religion, Olympic Games, or philosophical tradition). Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) the ancient origin, (2) the transmission path from ancient to modern, (3) what was preserved and what was changed. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay: "The Ancient World Is Still With Us." Identify three specific modern realities (institutions, laws, practices, beliefs) that have direct ancient roots and argue which ancient legacy has had the greatest ongoing impact on modern life. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute explanation: (1) Name two modern things with ancient roots. (2) Trace the specific path from ancient origin to modern form. (3) Evaluate: is today's version a genuine continuation of the original, or has it been so transformed that calling it an "inheritance" is misleading? 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Then and Now" split-page visual for three ancient concepts and their modern equivalents. For each: show the ancient original (with a brief description and date), show the modern version, and write 2 sentences explaining what changed and what stayed the same. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute "Time Traveler's Report" video. You've traveled from ancient Athens (or Rome, or Babylon) to 2026. What do you recognize? What surprises you? What did you expect to still exist that's gone? 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project on the ancient world. Write a 200-word reflection: What legacies of the civilization you studied are still present today? Did your original work acknowledge these connections? What would you add now?

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. "Ancient DNA" of Modern Democracy — Create an annotated political family tree tracing the concept of democratic governance from Athens → Rome → Magna Carta → Enlightenment → U.S. Constitution. For each node, include: what was inherited, what was modified, and what was lost.
  2. Living Religions Research Brief — Research one of the three Abrahamic religions (Judaism, Christianity, or Islam) and trace its ancient origins. Create a 1-page brief: ancient roots, key texts that survived, major moments of transformation, and current global reach. Evaluate: what from the ancient version is still central today?
  3. The "What We Owe Ancient Rome" Argument — Identify five specific features of modern American civic life that are directly inherited from ancient Rome (legal terminology, architectural style, Senate structure, concept of "republic," civic virtue). Write a 1-page argument about which inheritance has been most valuable and which has been most problematic.

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: List three modern institutions, practices, or concepts with direct ancient roots. For each, name the ancient civilization, the specific idea or institution, and its modern form.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Choose one of the above and write a paragraph tracing the transmission path — how did it get from ancient to modern? Who preserved it, who borrowed it, and what changed along the way?
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word critical evaluation: Is the modern version of one ancient idea a genuine continuation or a selective distortion? What was preserved, what was dropped, and what does that tell you about the people who did the borrowing?

docs.title: Medieval and Renaissance Science and Technology — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.1 — Analyze historical achievements related to science and technology Era/Unit (if SS): Era 2 — Middle Ages and the Renaissance (~500–1600 CE) ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.W.4 — Argument writing; 9-10.R.9 — Analyzing informational texts NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
The "Dark Ages" label is a myth invented by Renaissance scholars who wanted to make their own era look better. The medieval period produced remarkable technologies — some entirely original, some preserved and transmitted from the ancient world through Islamic scholars, Byzantine copyists, and monastic libraries.

  • Feudalism: The medieval political and economic system in which lords granted land (fiefs) to vassals in exchange for military service. Not a technology itself, but shaped which technologies got funded and which didn't.
  • Water Mill / Windmill: Technologies for converting natural energy (water flow, wind) into mechanical work. Water mills ground grain, pumped water, and powered early industrial processes. Windmills spread from Persia to Europe in the medieval period.
  • Heavy Plow (Moldboard Plow): An iron-bladed plow that could cut through the heavy clay soils of Northern Europe, enabling more land to be farmed. The heavy plow transformed Northern European agriculture and contributed to population growth in the 10th–12th centuries.
  • Three-Field System: A crop rotation system dividing farmland into three sections: one growing a spring crop, one a fall crop, one left fallow. Increased agricultural output by ~50% compared to two-field systems — a medieval "Green Revolution."
  • Islamic Golden Age (~750–1258 CE): The period when the Abbasid Caliphate centered in Baghdad was the world's leading center of science, mathematics, and philosophy. Scholars like Al-Khwarizmi (algebra), Ibn Sina/Avicenna (medicine), and Al-Haytham (optics) made foundational advances.
  • Algebra: The branch of mathematics dealing with equations and unknown quantities. The word comes from the Arabic al-jabr, from Al-Khwarizmi's The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing (~820 CE).
  • Printing Press: Johannes Gutenberg's movable type printing press (~1440 CE) dramatically reduced the cost of producing books, enabling mass literacy and making the Protestant Reformation possible. (Movable type was invented in China centuries earlier.)
  • Gothic Architecture: A medieval European architectural style using the pointed arch, flying buttress, and ribbed vault — allowing cathedrals to be built taller and with larger windows than Romanesque predecessors. A synthesis of engineering and religious purpose.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Identify at least three specific medieval technologies and explain what problem each solved. - [ ] Explain the role of Islamic scholars in preserving and advancing ancient Greek scientific knowledge. - [ ] Distinguish between original medieval inventions and technologies that medieval Europeans borrowed or received from other civilizations. - [ ] Explain what the printing press made possible that wasn't possible before. - [ ] Identify the "Islamic Golden Age" and name at least two scholars and their contributions.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can state that the printing press was invented around 1440 by Gutenberg and that it made books cheaper to produce — without yet analyzing what specific social and political changes that caused (mass literacy, Reformation, spread of scientific ideas).

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.1 — Analyze historical achievements related to science and technology (Era 2)

To meet this standard, you must analyze the significance of a medieval technology: what it achieved, what social or political changes it caused, and why it matters for understanding the era. In Era 2, a key theme is the preservation and transmission of knowledge — the Islamic world and Byzantine Empire kept Greek science alive during a period when Western Europe largely lost it.

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by analyzing how Al-Haytham's Book of Optics (~1011 CE) achieved something the ancient Greeks had not: a correct theory of how vision works (light enters the eye rather than emanating from it), accompanied by experimental evidence produced through controlled experiments. The student explains both the scientific achievement (empirical optics) and its transmission: the book was translated into Latin in the 12th century and influenced Roger Bacon and later Renaissance scientists like Kepler. Without Al-Haytham, modern optics, the scientific method, and eventually the telescope might not have developed when they did.

The Difference: A 2.0 student knows the Islamic Golden Age produced important scientists. A 3.0 student analyzes one specific achievement, explains what was novel about it, and traces how it was transmitted to and transformed later science.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate the conditions that enabled or suppressed technological achievement — why did the Islamic world lead global science from 750–1258, and what happened when those conditions changed?

Transfer Tasks: 1. Conditions for scientific flourishing: The Abbasid Caliphate actively funded the House of Wisdom in Baghdad, hiring translators and scholars from across the known world. What specific conditions (political, economic, ideological) enabled this? What can modern institutions learn from it? 2. Technology and social change: The printing press spread ideas that destabilized the Catholic Church's information monopoly. Analyze the printing press as a political technology — not just a communication tool. How does controlling information relate to controlling power? What's the modern parallel? 3. Technology and empire: The Mongol destruction of Baghdad (1258) and its library at the House of Wisdom ended the Islamic Golden Age. Analyze how political events (conquest) can destroy technological and intellectual infrastructure — and what that tells us about the fragility of civilizational achievement.

4.0 Example: A student argues that the Islamic Golden Age was made possible by three specific conditions — a unified empire with a common language (Arabic), a religious tradition that encouraged the seeking of knowledge, and a political will to fund translation projects — and that the loss of those conditions (Mongol invasion, later political fragmentation) explains why the Islamic world lost its scientific leadership. The student then argues that the same conditions-based analysis applies to contemporary science funding debates.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: The Silk Road — CrashCourse World History #9 · Episode in reading library Also: Islam, the Quran & the Five Pillars — CrashCourse WH #13 · Episode in reading library Also: The Dark Ages — CrashCourse WH #14 · Episode in reading library

Key Timestamps (Silk Road ep): - [03:30]: The role of Islamic merchants and scholars in transmitting goods AND ideas. Why it matters: The Silk Road wasn't just about trade — it was an information highway. Understanding this changes how you see the "origin" of any medieval technology. - [09:00]: What happened to the Silk Road when the Mongols disrupted it. Why it matters: Technology transfer depends on stable trade networks — disruption of networks means disruption of knowledge transmission.

Watch Prompt: Track every time a technology or scientific idea is described as traveling from one civilization to another. Who was the transmitter of knowledge in each case? Who usually gets credit for it in the history books?

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text: • Magna Carta (1215) — Not a scientific text, but use it as evidence for medieval institutional development. What "technology of governance" does the Magna Carta represent? How does constraining royal power relate to creating conditions for other kinds of innovation?

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose ONE medieval technology or scientific achievement (heavy plow, printing press, Islamic optics/algebra, Gothic architecture, water mill). Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) what it achieved, (2) what conditions or civilizations made it possible, (3) what it changed — socially, politically, or intellectually. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay arguing: "The Islamic Golden Age was the most important intellectual development of the medieval period." Agree or disagree, using evidence from at least two specific scholars or works. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute analysis: (1) Name a medieval technological achievement most people don't know about. (2) Explain where it came from and how it traveled to other civilizations. (3) Argue why it matters for understanding the modern world. 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Knowledge Transmission Map" for one medieval technology or scientific idea. Show: where it originated, which civilizations transmitted it, when it arrived in Western Europe, and what it made possible when it got there. Include a written analysis of 100 words. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute "Museum Audio Tour" for a medieval technology exhibit. Pick three technologies, describe each for a general audience, and explain (in terms accessible to a museum visitor) why each was significant — not just what it was. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project related to medieval history. Write a 200-word reflection: What technology or scientific achievement shaped the events you studied? Did your original work analyze the significance of the technology, or just describe it?

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. "Forgotten Scientists" Profile Collection — Research three medieval scientists from the Islamic Golden Age who are rarely taught in Western textbooks. Create a one-page profile for each: biography, key contributions, where their work was transmitted, and what modern science owes them.
  2. The Printing Press's Political Impact — Build a visual argument showing how the printing press changed the political landscape of Europe between 1440 and 1560. Include: what it made possible (mass Bibles, pamphlets, Protestant tracts), who it threatened (the Church), and one specific historical event that might not have happened without it.
  3. Medieval Agricultural Revolution Brief — Write a 1-page research brief arguing that the heavy plow and three-field system together constituted a "medieval agricultural revolution" that enabled the population growth that made the Renaissance possible.

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Identify three medieval technologies — one from Western Europe, one from the Islamic world, and one transmitted between civilizations. For each, state what it was, where it came from, and what it did.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph analyzing ONE medieval technology: what it achieved, what conditions made it possible, and what it changed socially or politically.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word argument evaluating: what conditions are necessary for technological/scientific flourishing? Use the Islamic Golden Age as your primary evidence.

docs.title: Medieval Religion, Church Power, and Ideology — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.2 — Explain historical changes related to religions and ideologies Era/Unit (if SS): Era 2 — Middle Ages and the Renaissance (~500–1600 CE) ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.R.9 — Analyze informational/argumentative texts, author POV; 9-10.W.4 — Argument writing NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
In the medieval period, religion wasn't just a personal belief — it was the operating system of political authority, social order, scientific knowledge, and daily life. Understanding the medieval Church means understanding the era's entire power structure.

  • The Catholic Church (Western Christendom): The dominant institution in Western Europe from roughly 500–1500 CE. The Pope was simultaneously the religious head of Christianity AND a major political player — commanding armies, excommunicating kings, and controlling vast landholdings.
  • Excommunication: The Pope's power to formally expel someone from the Church, cutting them off from sacraments, salvation, and Christian community. For a medieval ruler, excommunication could mean their subjects were released from loyalty oaths — a devastating political weapon.
  • The Crusades (1095–1291 CE): A series of religiously motivated military campaigns launched by the Catholic Church to recover Jerusalem and the Holy Land from Muslim control. The Crusades were driven by a mix of genuine religious belief, political ambition, and economic incentive.
  • Islamic Belief System: Islam, founded by Muhammad (~610 CE), is a monotheistic religion based on the Quran. The five pillars (declaration of faith, prayer, alms, fasting, pilgrimage) structure daily life. The Islamic world was both a religious community and, during much of this era, a political empire.
  • Crusader Ideology: The belief that Christians had a sacred duty to liberate Jerusalem; that dying in a crusade guaranteed entry to heaven; that Muslims ("infidels") were legitimate targets of holy war. This ideology was constructed and promoted by the Church — it did not emerge spontaneously.
  • The Great Schism (1054 CE): The permanent split between the Western (Roman Catholic) and Eastern (Eastern Orthodox) Christian churches. Theological and political disputes over the nature of the Pope's authority drove the split — and it still exists today.
  • Renaissance Humanism: The philosophical movement centered on human potential, classical learning, and the value of earthly life — a significant shift away from the medieval emphasis on sin, suffering, and preparation for death. Humanists challenged Church authority over education and knowledge.
  • Indulgences: Payments made to the Church in exchange for reduction of punishment for sins in purgatory. The sale of indulgences was a major source of Church revenue — and the specific practice that triggered Martin Luther's 95 Theses (1517).

Basic Processes: - [ ] Explain why the Catholic Church held political as well as religious power in medieval Europe. - [ ] Distinguish between the religious justification for the Crusades and the political/economic motivations. - [ ] Identify the Great Schism and explain what caused it. - [ ] Explain what "Renaissance Humanism" challenged about the medieval worldview. - [ ] Define "indulgences" and explain why their sale was considered corrupt.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can state that the Crusades were religious wars to recover Jerusalem and that Pope Urban II called the First Crusade in 1095 — without yet analyzing how the Church constructed crusader ideology or what the Crusades revealed about the intersection of religion and political power.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.2 — Explain historical changes related to religions and ideologies (Era 2)

To meet this standard, you must explain how religious and ideological systems changed during this era, and how those changes affected people's lives and political structures. In Era 2, the key changes are: the consolidation of Church power → the Crusades as a product of that power → the seeds of its erosion through the Renaissance → the Protestant Reformation on the horizon.

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by explaining how the Crusades changed the relationship between Church and political power in Europe. Before the Crusades, kings and lords pursued primarily local and regional wars; the Church was an important authority but not a military mobilizer. After Pope Urban II's call in 1095, the Church demonstrated it could mobilize armies across national lines in the name of a shared religious identity — a massive expansion of papal power. But the Crusades' repeated failures and the exposure of European knights to Muslim culture (which was, in many ways, more sophisticated than European culture at the time) began to undermine the Church's moral authority. The student traces how the Crusades both demonstrated and eroded Church power — the same movement that was the Church's greatest display of authority was also a cause of its eventual decline.

The Difference: A 2.0 student knows the Crusades were religious wars. A 3.0 student explains how the Crusades both demonstrated and undermined Church authority — and what that tells us about how ideology can produce unintended consequences.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must synthesize across religious/ideological traditions — comparing the Church's role in Western Europe to Islamic political-religious structures, or evaluating the Crusades from multiple religious perspectives simultaneously.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Multiple perspectives on the Crusades: Analyze the Crusades from three perspectives: a European crusader knight, a Muslim defender of Jerusalem, and a Jewish resident of a European city targeted in pogroms during the "People's Crusade." What were they each fighting for or against? How does the same set of events look different from each vantage point? 2. Church authority and its limits: The Pope excommunicated Holy Roman Emperor Henry IV in 1076 (the "Investiture Controversy") — but Henry waited outside the Pope's castle in the snow for three days until the Pope lifted the excommunication. What does this episode reveal about the limits of religious authority over political power? Does religion or political power ultimately win? 3. Religious ideology and social control: Analyze how both the Catholic Church and the Islamic caliphate used religion to justify their political authority. What is similar about these two systems? What is different? Is it possible to separate "genuine religious belief" from "using religion for political purposes"?

4.0 Example: A student argues that the Crusades were not primarily religious in motivation — they were a product of population pressure (too many young knights with no land inheritance), political instability in Western Europe, and papal ambition — and that the "religious ideology" was a tool used to recruit and motivate crusaders rather than the actual driving force. The student supports this with evidence: Pope Urban II's speech (promising heaven and land), the behavior of crusaders who attacked Jewish communities in Europe before they even reached the Holy Land (revealing anti-Semitic opportunism alongside religious fervor), and the Fourth Crusade (which never reached Jerusalem — it sacked Constantinople instead, a Christian city, for economic gain).

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: The Silk Road — CrashCourse World History #9 · Episode in reading library Also: The Crusades — CrashCourse WH #15 · Episode in reading library Also: Islam, the Quran & the Five Pillars — CrashCourse WH #13 · Episode in reading library

Key Timestamps (Silk Road ep): - [04:00]: The role of religious networks in trade and cultural diffusion. Why it matters: Religion wasn't just a belief system — it created networks of trust (Muslim traders could do business across vast distances because of shared religious practices), which shaped how trade and cultural exchange worked.

Watch Prompt: Track how religion functioned as a political and economic tool in addition to a spiritual one. Every time a religious institution, leader, or belief is mentioned, ask: Who holds power here, and how does religion help them maintain it?

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Magna Carta (1215) — The Magna Carta is partially about limiting the king's power in favor of the barons — but it was signed in the context of Church authority over all of them. Read the document and identify where Church power is assumed, protected, or negotiated.
  • Medieval Political Skinny — This companion skinny covers WH.6_12.3 (political systems) in Era 2. Church power and political power are inseparable in this era — read both together.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) How did the Catholic Church exercise power in medieval Europe beyond purely religious authority? (2) What was one way that power was challenged or limited? (3) How did that challenge connect to later changes (Renaissance, Reformation)? 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay arguing whether the Crusades were primarily driven by religious belief or political/economic calculation. Use evidence from at least two specific historical events or sources. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute explanation: (1) What made the medieval Catholic Church so politically powerful? (2) How did the Crusades demonstrate that power AND begin to erode it? (3) How did Renaissance Humanism change the ideological landscape? 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Power Map" of medieval Europe showing the relationship between the Pope, kings/emperors, nobles, clergy, and peasants. For each relationship, show: who had power over whom, and what religious ideology justified that power arrangement. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute "Competing Perspectives" video on the Crusades. Present the crusader perspective AND the Muslim perspective on the same events, using primary source language where possible. Close with your own analysis of whose framing is more historically accurate. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project about the medieval period. Write a 200-word reflection: How did religious ideology shape the events or people you studied? Did your original work treat religion as background detail or as a central force?

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. Pope vs. King: The Investiture Controversy Debate Brief — Research the Investiture Controversy (1076–1122). Write a 1-page argument from the Pope's perspective AND a 1-page argument from the Emperor's perspective. Close with your own assessment: who had the stronger argument for why religion and political power should or should not be separated?
  2. Crusades from Three Perspectives: Documentary Script — Write a 3-page documentary script covering the First Crusade from three perspectives: a French knight, a Muslim soldier, and a Jewish resident of a Rhineland city. Cite primary sources for each perspective.
  3. "How the Church Lost Europe" Timeline — Build an annotated timeline from 1095 (First Crusade) to 1517 (Luther's 95 Theses). For each event, annotate: did this increase or decrease Church authority, and why? Culminating entry: your argument about which single event most damaged the Church's long-term authority.

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Identify and explain three ways the medieval Catholic Church exercised power beyond purely religious functions. For each, give a specific historical example.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph explaining how the Crusades both demonstrated and undermined Church authority. Use at least one specific historical event or detail as evidence.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word argument evaluating the Crusades from two different religious perspectives. What does comparing these perspectives reveal about how ideology functions in history?

docs.title: How Medieval Political Systems Controlled People — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.3 — Analyze the effects of different political systems on people Era/Unit (if SS): Era 2 — Middle Ages and the Renaissance (~500–1600 CE) ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.W.4 — Write to persuade; argument writing with evidence and counterclaims NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Before we can analyze how these systems controlled people, you must understand the "mechanics" of the Middle Ages. Use these definitions as your foundation:

  • Feudalism: A social and political system where land was exchanged for service or loyalty. It created a strict hierarchy: King $\rightarrow$ Lord $\rightarrow$ Knight $\rightarrow$ Serf.
  • Vassal: A person (usually a noble or knight) who received land from a higher lord in exchange for military service and political loyalty.
  • Fief: The actual piece of land granted to a vassal.
  • Serf vs. Peasant: While both were farmers, a peasant was a tenant farmer who could technically move or own small plots; a serf was "bound" to the land—they were not allowed to leave the manor without permission and were essentially tied to the lord's estate.
  • Manorial System: The economic side of feudalism. It was the system of running a large estate (a manor) where peasants lived and worked in exchange for protection.
  • Pope / Papacy: The Pope is the head of the Catholic Church. In the Middle Ages, the "Papacy" held massive political power, often rivaling the power of kings.
  • Excommunication: A "social death sentence." If a person was excommunicated by the Church, they were kicked out of the church and banned from all religious services—a devastating tool for controlling kings.
  • Investiture Controversy: A major conflict between the Pope and the Holy Roman Emperor over who had the right to appoint church officials (basically: Who is the boss of the Church?).
  • Magna Carta (1215): The "Great Charter." It was a document that forced King John of England to admit that even the King was not above the law.
  • Common Law: A system of law based on established customs and previous court rulings rather than just the whim of a ruler.
  • Divine Right of Kings: The belief that a king’s authority comes directly from God, meaning they only had to answer to God, not to their people.

Checklist for 2.0 Mastery: - [ ] I can identify who was at the top and bottom of the feudal pyramid. - [ ] I can explain the difference between a peasant and a serf. - [ ] I can define "Excommunication" as a political weapon. - [ ] I can state what the Magna Carta did for English law. - [ ] I can identify who held power in the Manorial system.

2.0 Example: A student looks at an illustration of a medieval manor and correctly identifies that the serfs are "bound" to the land while the knights provide protection, explaining how this trade-off kept both groups in their specific roles.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.3: Analyze the effects of different political systems on people.

To hit a 3.0, you must move beyond just defining these terms. You must explain how these systems actually changed lives. In the Middle Ages, power was split between two main "bosses": the Feudal Lord (who controlled your land and safety) and the Church (which controlled your soul and social standing).

3.0 Analysis Example: Instead of just saying "The Church was powerful," a 3.0 student would say: "Because of the threat of Excommunication, even powerful kings were forced to compromise with the Pope during the Investiture Controversy. This meant that for common people like peasants and merchants, their daily lives were governed by both local lords who controlled their physical safety and the Church, which dictated their social behavior."

Distinction: - 2.0: "The Magna Carta limited the King's power." - 3.0: "The Magna Carta was a turning point because it established that even the King had to follow Common Law, beginning the shift from 'Divine Right' toward a system where laws protected certain rights of the people."

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must perform a "transfer" task. This means taking these medieval concepts and applying them to a different context or making a complex comparison.

4.0 Example: Compare the "Divine Right of Kings" with modern-day "Constitutional Republics." A student might write an analysis arguing that while the Magna Carta was originally only meant to protect the rights of wealthy nobles, it created the legal precedent for modern democracy by establishing that no leader is above the law.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: The Dark Ages...How Dark Were They, Really? — https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NX7monqP_-A Also: Islam, the Quran, and the Five Pillars — https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PnQLpGqQDTU (Use this to compare how religious leaders held political power in different cultures).

Watch Prompt: As you watch the "Dark Ages" video, keep a T-chart. On the left side, list Sources of Power for Nobles (land, military, titles). On the right side, list Sources of Power for the Church (faith, excommunication, land ownership).

Timestamp Notes: - 2:45: Note how the lack of a central government led to the rise of local lords. - 5:10: Observe the role of the Church as the primary provider of education and social services. - 8:30: Watch for the transition toward more centralized power (the beginning of the end for pure feudalism).

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Magna Carta 1215 — A primary source showing how the Magna Carta limited royal power.
  • Feudal Obligations Primary Source — A breakdown of what a knight owed his lord in exchange for a fief.
  • Pope Gregory Emperor Henry Correspondence — Primary letters showing the tension between Church and State during the Investiture Controversy.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text: # Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Write a paragraph analyzing how the Manorial System specifically limited the freedom of serfs compared to modern-day workers. Use at least two vocabulary terms from the "What You Need to Know" section. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400-600 word persuasive essay: Was the Magna Carta a victory for the common people, or just a way for rich nobles to get more power? Use evidence from the text to support your claim and address one counter-argument. 3 Verbal Conversation In a 3-minute "speed dating" style talk, explain to a peer how Excommunication served as a political tool rather than just a religious punishment. 4 Visual/Creative Create an annotated "Power Map." Draw the feudal pyramid and the Church's influence circle overlapping it. Label where these two powers collided (e.g., Investiture Controversy). 5 Multimedia/Performance Record a 2-minute "Podcast Segment" where you play two characters: a King who believes in Divine Right and a Noble who wants to sign the Magna Carta. Debate the terms of the charter. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a primary source from the Reading Library. Write a 3-sentence annotation: 1) Identify the main power structure shown; 2) Explain how it controlled people; 3) Label your analysis as a 2.0, 3.0, or 4.0 based on the criteria above.

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
1. The "Power & Policy" Brief: A one-page briefing for a modern government official explaining how the Magna Carta's limitation of royal power influenced our current system of checks and balances.
  2. The "Life in the Manor" Narrative: A creative non-fiction piece written from the perspective of a serf, describing their daily life under the Manorial System and the restrictions placed upon them by their lord.
  3. The Great Debate Script: A script for a mock debate between a 13th-century Bishop and a 21st-century politician regarding the role of religion in government policy.

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Identify the roles of the King, Lord, Knight, and Serf in a diagram and explain what each person "gave" to get their status.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Analyze how the combination of Feudalism (land control) and Church Authority (spiritual control) created a system where common people had very little personal freedom. Use the Magna Carta as evidence of the beginning of change.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Evaluate whether the Magna Carta was a "genuine" step toward democracy or simply a pragmatic deal between elites, and connect this to how modern constitutions still attempt to limit executive power today.

docs.title: Medieval Social, Cultural, and Economic Life — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.4 — Analyze the influence of social, cultural, and economic development on individuals Era/Unit (if SS): Era 2 — Middle Ages and the Renaissance (~500–1600 CE) ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.R.9 — Analyze informational texts; 9-10.W.3 — Explanatory/narrative writing NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Medieval society was organized around obligations — lords owed protection, serfs owed labor, the Church owed salvation. Understanding these systems means understanding why individuals in medieval Europe had very different lives depending on where they were born in the hierarchy — and how the Black Death changed everything.

  • Feudalism: The hierarchical system of land ownership and political allegiance: king → lords → knights → serfs. Land was the primary form of wealth; control over land determined almost everything else about a person's life.
  • Serf: A peasant bound to the land in a feudal system. Unlike enslaved people, serfs were legally tied to a specific lord's land, not to the lord personally — but their freedom of movement, marriage, and occupation was severely restricted. Serfs made up the majority of the medieval European population.
  • Guild: An organization of craftspeople or merchants who set standards, trained apprentices, regulated prices, and protected members' interests. Guilds were the medieval version of both a union and a professional licensing board.
  • The Black Death (~1347–1353 CE): A bubonic plague pandemic that killed an estimated 30–50% of Europe's population in six years. The Black Death traveled along the Silk Road from Central Asia to Europe and devastated every social class — but its effects were socially transformative in ways that undermined feudalism.
  • Social Mobility: Movement up or down the social hierarchy. Medieval society had very limited social mobility — a serf was expected to remain a serf. But the Black Death, paradoxically, created upward mobility by creating a severe labor shortage: surviving serfs could demand better wages and terms.
  • Renaissance: The "rebirth" of classical learning and art, centered in Italy from approximately 1300–1600 CE. The Renaissance represented a cultural shift toward humanism, individual achievement, and secular values — a reaction against medieval religious hierarchy.
  • Patron: A wealthy individual or institution that funded artists, writers, and scholars. The Medici family of Florence were the most famous Renaissance patrons; patronage systems shaped which art got made and whose ideas got spread.
  • Merchant Class: The wealthy traders and bankers who accumulated wealth through commerce rather than land ownership. In the medieval and Renaissance periods, the merchant class gradually accumulated political power that rivaled the nobility — changing the social structure from below.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Describe the daily life of a serf vs. a lord vs. a guild member in medieval Europe. - [ ] Explain how the feudal hierarchy determined what was possible for an individual's life. - [ ] Identify the Black Death's most significant social effect (labor shortage → bargaining power for survivors). - [ ] Explain what "patronage" meant for Renaissance art and how it shaped which ideas were expressed. - [ ] Distinguish between the medieval concept of individual identity (defined by role/duty) and the Renaissance humanist concept (individual potential and achievement).

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can describe the feudal hierarchy and state that the Black Death killed a large percentage of Europe's population — without yet analyzing how the labor shortage created by the Black Death altered the bargaining power of serfs and contributed to the end of feudalism.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.4 — Analyze the influence of social, cultural, and economic development on individuals (Era 2)

To meet this standard, you must connect large-scale social and economic systems to how they shaped specific individuals' lived experience. In Era 2, the central tension is between the rigidity of the feudal hierarchy and the forces that disrupted it: the Black Death, the growth of trade and the merchant class, and the Renaissance's new ideas about individual worth.

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by analyzing how the Black Death affected a specific category of individual: the surviving serf. Before 1347, serfs were legally bound to the land with little bargaining power — lords could set terms, and serfs had to accept them. After 1347–1353, with 30–50% of the population dead, lords faced an acute labor shortage: crops needed to be harvested, farms needed to be worked, and there were fewer people to do it. Surviving serfs discovered they could leave unfavorable lords and find better terms elsewhere — or simply refuse to work under the old conditions. The student explains how an economic development (catastrophic population loss creating labor scarcity) directly reshaped the social and legal condition of individuals who had previously been at the bottom of the hierarchy.

The Difference: A 2.0 student knows the Black Death killed millions. A 3.0 student analyzes how it changed the bargaining position of those who survived — and why that led to the eventual decline of serfdom.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate how multiple forces (plague, trade, religious change, Renaissance ideas) interacted to transform medieval society — and connect this transformation to the modern world.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Economic disruption and social change: The Black Death is one of history's clearest cases of an economic shock (labor shortage) producing rapid social change (end of serfdom, rise of wages). Analyze how it parallels or differs from a modern economic disruption that changed social hierarchies. 2. The Renaissance and individual identity: Medieval people were primarily defined by their social role and religious duty; Renaissance humanism introduced the idea that individuals could be judged by their achievements. Analyze how this ideological shift changed what was possible for different people — especially women and the merchant class. 3. Who the Renaissance excluded: The Renaissance celebrated individual achievement, classical learning, and humanist values — but primarily for wealthy men in Italian city-states. Who was excluded from the Renaissance? What was happening to serfs, women, Jews, and enslaved Africans (the Atlantic Slave Trade began during this era) at the same time?

4.0 Example: A student argues that the Black Death was more socially transformative than the Renaissance because it materially changed the conditions of ordinary people's lives (labor rights, wages, mobility) — whereas the Renaissance primarily changed the cultural production of elites. The student then argues that this hierarchy of "material conditions vs. cultural ideas" is a framework that can be applied to other historical transformations.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: The Silk Road — CrashCourse World History #9 · Episode in reading library Also: The Black Death & Printing Press — CrashCourse WH #28 · Episode in reading library Also: The Renaissance — CrashCourse WH #22 · Episode in reading library

Key Timestamps (Silk Road ep): - [07:30]: Disease spreading along trade routes — the mechanism by which plague traveled from Central Asia to Europe. Why it matters: The Black Death came WITH the Silk Road's economic benefits — you can't have global trade without global disease transmission. This is a recurring theme in history.

Watch Prompt: For each social group mentioned in the video (merchants, artisans, peasants, rulers, clergy), track: What was their role in the economic system? What happened to their position when that system was disrupted?

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text: • Magna Carta (1215) — Read this as a document about social and economic relationships, not just political ones. What does the Magna Carta reveal about how different classes (barons, merchants, freemen, serfs) were positioned relative to each other and relative to the king?

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Write a 3-paragraph analysis of how ONE social or economic development affected the life of an individual in a specific position in medieval society. Choose: a serf during the Black Death, a guild craftsperson, or a woman during the Renaissance. Be specific about what changed and what caused the change. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay: "Before and After the Black Death." Describe what daily life was like for a peasant in 1340 CE vs. a peasant in 1380 CE. Argue: was the Black Death ultimately a positive or negative force for the social position of survivors? 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute analysis: (1) How did the feudal system determine an individual's options in medieval Europe? (2) What was the specific mechanism by which the Black Death changed those options for survivors? (3) What does this tell us about how economic forces can change social hierarchies? 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Before and After" visual showing the social hierarchy in 1300 CE vs. 1450 CE. Show what changed (merchant class rising, serfdom declining, Church authority eroding) and what caused those changes. Include a written paragraph explaining your visual. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute "First-Person Account" video. Play a character who lived through the Black Death — describe your life before (your role, your options, your obligations), what you experienced during the plague, and what changed for you and people like you afterward. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project about the medieval period. Write a 200-word reflection: whose perspective was represented in your work? Whose was missing? How did social position shape what different people in this era experienced?

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. "The World Turned Upside Down" — Black Death Social History — Research and present a case study of how the Black Death changed ONE specific social relationship in medieval Europe (e.g., serf-lord labor relations in England, the decline of a specific guild, the Church's loss of credibility). Use at least two sources. Present as a 2-page research brief with an argument about the most significant change.
  2. Renaissance Exclusions — Who Was Left Out? — Research the experiences of one group excluded from Renaissance opportunity: women, Jews, enslaved Africans, or rural peasants. Create a 6-panel visual story documenting their experience during this "rebirth" era.
  3. Medieval to Modern Social Mobility Study — Compare social mobility in medieval Europe (how difficult was it to change your social position?) to social mobility in the United States today (using current research data). Write a 1-page argument: which society has/had greater barriers to social mobility, and why?

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Describe the daily life of three people at different levels of the medieval hierarchy (a serf, a guild craftsperson, and a noble). For each, identify: their obligations, their freedoms, and what determined their position.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph analyzing how one social or economic development (Black Death labor shortage, rise of the merchant class, or the Renaissance's new ideas about individual worth) changed what was possible for a specific category of person.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word argument evaluating: did the Renaissance make life better for ordinary people, or was its celebration of individual potential primarily for the wealthy elite? Use specific evidence.
