docs.title: Causes and Effects of Medieval Global Events — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.5 — Analyze causes and effects of global events using primary and/or secondary sources Era/Unit (if SS): Era 2 — Middle Ages and the Renaissance (~500–1600 CE) ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.W.4 — Argument writing; 9-10.IR.1–5 — Research and source evaluation NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
The medieval period is full of events that look like they happened locally — a plague in Central Asia, a Mongol conquest in China — but whose effects rippled across civilizations. Understanding Era 2 means understanding how connected the medieval world already was, centuries before European colonialism made that connection explicit.

  • Immediate Cause: The direct trigger for a historical event — what happened right before that set the event in motion.
  • Underlying Cause: The deeper conditions that made an event possible — what had been building for years or decades before the immediate trigger.
  • Mongol Conquests (1206–1368 CE): Genghis Khan and his successors built the largest land empire in history, from China to Eastern Europe. Effects: destruction of the Abbasid Caliphate and the House of Wisdom (1258), facilitation of the Silk Road's expansion under the Pax Mongolica, and the creation of conditions for the Black Death's westward spread.
  • Pax Mongolica ("Mongol Peace"): The period of relative stability across the Mongol Empire (~1270–1350 CE) that allowed unprecedented trade and travel across Eurasia. Marco Polo's journey was made possible by the Pax Mongolica.
  • The Black Death (~1347–1353 CE): Caused by the bacterium Yersinia pestis, spread by fleas on rats. Traveled from Central Asia along trade routes to the Middle East and then to Europe. Killed 30–50% of Europe's population; also devastated the Middle East and Central Asia.
  • The Fall of Constantinople (1453 CE): The Ottoman Turks conquered Constantinople, ending the Byzantine Empire and blocking the overland Silk Road to Europe. This event accelerated European interest in finding sea routes to Asia — directly motivating the Age of Exploration.
  • Primary Source Evaluation: In Era 2, primary sources include chronicles written by medieval monks, court records, personal letters (like those of medieval merchants), and diplomatic correspondence. Key evaluation questions: Who wrote it? Who was the intended audience? What did the writer have to gain?
  • Corroboration: Checking one source's claims against other sources. A medieval chronicle written by a monk may exaggerate Church heroism; comparing it to a secular court record or a Muslim account of the same events helps identify bias.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Identify immediate vs. underlying causes for at least two major medieval events. - [ ] Explain how the Black Death's spread was a consequence of trade routes (the Silk Road) — connecting economic development to disease transmission. - [ ] Evaluate a medieval primary source by identifying its author, purpose, and potential bias. - [ ] Explain how the Fall of Constantinople caused effects far beyond the Mediterranean (motivating European sea exploration). - [ ] Distinguish between short-term and long-term effects of a medieval event.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can state that the Fall of Constantinople in 1453 ended the Byzantine Empire — without yet analyzing how this single event set off a chain of consequences that led to Columbus's voyage and the European colonization of the Americas.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.5 — Analyze causes and effects of global events using primary and/or secondary sources (Era 2)

To meet this standard, you must trace cause-and-effect chains that cross civilizations and use primary or secondary sources as evidence. In Era 2, the key is that medieval events were more globally connected than most people assume — and understanding those connections requires source analysis.

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by tracing the causal chain from the Mongol Conquests → Pax Mongolica → Silk Road expansion → Black Death spread → European population collapse → labor shortage → end of serfdom → economic transformation → Renaissance conditions. Each step in the chain is a connection that needs to be explained, not just listed. The student uses at least one primary source as evidence — for instance, a medieval chronicle describing the plague's arrival in a specific city, and analyzes what that source reveals about how contemporaries understood the event vs. what historians now know about its actual causes.

The Difference: A 2.0 student can list causes and effects for the Black Death. A 3.0 student traces a multi-step causal chain that crosses civilizations and uses a primary source to support the analysis.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate competing interpretations of causation — historians disagree about what caused major medieval events, and you need to assess the evidence for different interpretations.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Competing interpretations: Some historians argue the Crusades were primarily caused by religious fervor; others argue they were caused by political and economic pressure. Research both arguments and evaluate which is better supported by primary source evidence. 2. Hidden connections: The Fall of Constantinople (1453) → Age of Exploration → Columbus (1492) → Columbian Exchange → European wealth → Reformation (1517) is a causal chain that spans 64 years and three continents. Analyze this chain and identify the link you think was most contingent — the one that could most plausibly have NOT happened if circumstances were slightly different. 3. Whose sources? The Black Death's primary sources are mostly written by European monks and merchants. We have very few first-person accounts from the Middle East or Central Asia, where the plague also devastated populations. How does the skewed survival of sources affect what historians can claim about the Black Death's causes and effects? What are we missing?

4.0 Example: A student argues that the Fall of Constantinople was the most consequential event of the 15th century — not because it ended the Byzantine Empire (which had been weakening for a century), but because it catalyzed European sea exploration by blocking the overland Silk Road. The student then evaluates this claim: Was the Silk Road really blocked? (Partly yes, partly no — trade continued via Ottoman middlemen, but at higher cost and risk.) The student concludes that the "blocked Silk Road" explanation is somewhat overstated by European historians, who had an interest in justifying exploration as a necessity rather than as expansionist ambition.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: The Silk Road — CrashCourse World History #9 · Episode in reading library Also: The Mongols — CrashCourse WH #17 · Episode in reading library Also: The Black Death & Printing Press — CrashCourse WH #28 · Episode in reading library

Key Timestamps (Silk Road ep): - [07:30]: Disease traveling along trade routes. Why it matters: This is the direct cause-and-effect chain for the Black Death — you cannot understand the pandemic without understanding the trade network that transmitted it. - [10:00]: The Mongols and the Silk Road. Why it matters: The Mongols simultaneously destroyed the Abbasid Caliphate AND facilitated trade through the Pax Mongolica — the same force that caused destruction also enabled connection. This is a recurring pattern in history.

Watch Prompt: Track every cause-and-effect chain in the video. For each event, ask: (1) What was the immediate cause? (2) What made it possible (underlying cause)? (3) What were the effects, and how far did they reach geographically?

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text: • Magna Carta (1215) — Use the Magna Carta as a primary source for cause-and-effect analysis. What caused King John to sign it (1215)? What were its intended effects vs. its actual long-term effects? Read it as a historian: What can this document alone tell you, and what can't it tell you?

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose ONE major medieval event (Mongol Conquests, Black Death, Fall of Constantinople, or the Crusades). Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) immediate and underlying causes, (2) short-term effects, (3) long-term effects that crossed civilizations. Cite at least one source. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay tracing a causal chain across at least two civilizations and two centuries. Example: Mongol Conquests → Black Death → end of serfdom → conditions for the Renaissance. Argue which link in the chain was most significant. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute analysis: (1) Name a medieval event whose causes are more complex than usually taught. (2) Identify immediate and underlying causes. (3) Evaluate a primary source related to the event: What does it reveal, and what does it conceal? 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Global Ripple" map for one medieval event. Draw the event at the center, then draw arrows showing effects spreading outward — to different civilizations, over different time periods. Annotate each arrow with the specific effect and how it reached that far. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute "Unintended Consequences" video. Pick one medieval event and focus on consequences that no one at the time could have predicted. Explain why those consequences were unforeseeable and what they tell us about the complexity of historical causation. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project about the medieval period. Write a 200-word reflection: What caused the events you studied? Did your original work trace chains across civilizations, or only within one? What connections did you miss?

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. "The Most Consequential Medieval Event" Argument — Research three candidate events (Mongol Conquests, Black Death, Fall of Constantinople). Write a 1-page argument defending your choice for which had the most far-reaching and long-lasting effects. Support with at least three sources.
  2. Source Reliability Audit — Select three primary sources from the medieval period (a Church chronicle, a merchant's letter, and a Muslim account of the Crusades). For each, analyze: author's purpose, potential bias, what it corroborates, and what it omits. Write a 1-page conclusion about which is most reliable for understanding the event.
  3. The Silk Road Cause-and-Effect Map — Build a comprehensive annotated map of the Silk Road. For each major event connected to the Silk Road (spread of Buddhism, Mongol rise, Black Death spread, knowledge transmission, Fall of Constantinople and its effects), annotate the specific causal connection.

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Choose one medieval event. List: one immediate cause, one underlying cause, one short-term effect, one long-term effect. Identify whether each source you used was primary or secondary.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph tracing a causal chain that crosses at least two civilizations. Identify each link in the chain and explain the connection.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word evaluation of two historians' competing interpretations of the cause of one medieval event. Which is better supported by primary source evidence? Why?

docs.title: Medieval Legacies to the Present — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.6 — Explain how past events connect to the present Era/Unit (if SS): Era 2 — Middle Ages and the Renaissance (~500–1600 CE) ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.W.4 — Argument writing; 9-10.IR.1–5 — Research and source evaluation NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
The medieval world built much of the institutional and cultural infrastructure that modern Western societies still use. From constitutional law to university systems to the architecture of European cities, the Middle Ages left a deeper mark on today than most people realize.

  • Constitutional Tradition: The principle that political authority is limited by law. The Magna Carta (1215) is the first major document in the Western constitutional tradition establishing that the king was not above the law — a principle that runs through the English Bill of Rights, the U.S. Constitution, and every modern democratic constitution.
  • University System: The medieval Catholic Church created the first European universities (Bologna, Oxford, Cambridge, Paris — all founded before 1215). The university model — faculty, degrees, disciplines, academic freedom — still shapes higher education today.
  • Common Law: The system of law based on precedent (past court decisions), developed in medieval England. The U.S. legal system is based on English common law — which is based on medieval legal development.
  • Nation-State: The concept that a specific people with shared language, culture, and history should have their own sovereign government. Nation-states didn't fully exist in the medieval period, but the seeds were planted as feudal kingdoms consolidated into proto-national identities (France, England, Spain).
  • Cathedral / Gothic Architecture: Medieval Gothic cathedrals (Notre Dame de Paris, Cologne Cathedral, Westminster Abbey) are still standing, still functioning, and still visited by millions annually. The architectural techniques developed for cathedrals (pointed arches, ribbed vaults, flying buttresses) influenced later European architecture.
  • The Protestant Reformation (1517): Began by Martin Luther at the very end of Era 2, the Reformation split Western Christianity into Catholic and Protestant branches. The religious conflicts it spawned (Thirty Years' War, English Civil War) shaped the political map of Europe that still exists. Most Protestant denominations in existence today trace back to the Reformation.
  • Printing Press and Mass Literacy: Gutenberg's printing press (~1440) made mass literacy possible; the Bible was the first major book printed; the Reformation was driven by the ability to spread ideas cheaply. Mass literacy and mass media are still with us in transformed form.
  • The Black Death's Demographic Legacy: The Black Death killed 30–50% of Europe's population — and shaped everything that came after it: labor markets, wages, social mobility, the conditions for the Renaissance. Modern understanding of how pandemics spread draws directly on Black Death research.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Identify at least three modern institutions with direct medieval roots. - [ ] Explain what the Magna Carta established and why it matters for modern constitutional law. - [ ] Connect a specific medieval event (Black Death, Fall of Constantinople, printing press) to a specific modern reality. - [ ] Identify a modern religion that traces its specific form to the Protestant Reformation. - [ ] Explain why the university system in its current form is a medieval invention.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can state that the Magna Carta was an important document about limiting royal power — without yet explaining the specific path from Magna Carta (1215) → English common law → English Bill of Rights (1689) → U.S. Constitution (1787) → modern American civil liberties.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.6 — Explain how past events connect to the present (Era 2)

To meet this standard, you must trace the specific transmission path from a medieval event or institution to its modern form. "Things changed" is not enough — you need to explain how they changed, who transmitted them, and what was modified along the way.

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by tracing the constitutional tradition from the Magna Carta (1215) to the present. The student explains: the Magna Carta established that the king could not imprison free men without legal judgment and that the king could not impose taxes without baronial consent. These principles were largely ignored for centuries but were revived by English Parliamentarians in the 1600s as justification for limiting Charles I's power. The English Civil War and Bill of Rights (1689) codified these limits. The American colonists, educated in English common law and Parliament's arguments, cited Magna Carta in their arguments against British taxation. The U.S. Constitution's Fifth Amendment ("No person shall be...deprived of life, liberty, or property, without due process of law") is direct descendant language. The student traces the specific path — 1215 → 1689 → 1787 — and identifies what was preserved, modified, and why.

The Difference: A 2.0 student knows the Magna Carta was important. A 3.0 student traces the specific transmission path and identifies what survived and why.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate the medieval legacies critically — not just identify that they exist, but assess whether they are being honored, distorted, or selectively invoked in modern contexts.

Transfer Tasks: 1. The Magna Carta today: The Magna Carta is cited as a foundation of civil liberties — but it was originally a document protecting the rights of wealthy barons, not ordinary people. Evaluate: Is citing Magna Carta as a source of democratic rights historically accurate? Who has benefited from this legacy, and who has been excluded? 2. Religious legacy and conflict: The Protestant Reformation split Western Christianity into factions that have been fighting — sometimes literally — ever since. The modern conflict between Catholics and Protestants in Northern Ireland (the "Troubles," 1968–1998) is a direct descendant of Reformation-era divisions. Trace this connection. 3. Pandemic response: Modern epidemiology (the science of how diseases spread) drew heavily on analysis of the Black Death. Evaluate: What lessons from the Black Death were applied to COVID-19 pandemic response? What lessons weren't applied, and with what consequences?

4.0 Example: A student argues that the medieval concept of academic freedom — the idea that universities are protected spaces for intellectual inquiry separate from direct Church or state control — is under pressure in modern contexts (government attempts to defund or politically influence universities, cancel culture debates, academic freedom cases). The student traces this principle from its medieval origins (the University of Bologna's student-controlled model, Oxford's faculty autonomy fights with the Church) to its current contested status, and argues that understanding the medieval origins clarifies what is and isn't being protected in contemporary debates.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: The Silk Road — CrashCourse World History #9 · Episode in reading library Also: The Renaissance — CrashCourse WH #22 · Episode in reading library Also: The Black Death & Printing Press — CrashCourse WH #28 · Episode in reading library

Key Timestamps (Silk Road ep): - [11:30]: The long-term consequences of trade network disruption. Why it matters: The fall of the Silk Road's eastern terminus (Constantinople, 1453) is still with us — it motivated European exploration, which motivated colonialism, which shaped the modern world.

Watch Prompt: For each medieval development, ask: What is its modern form? Is the modern version a genuine continuation, or has it changed so much that calling it an "inheritance" is misleading?

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text: • Magna Carta (1215) — Read the document and identify three specific clauses that have modern parallels. For each, find the modern legal parallel (a specific U.S. law or constitutional provision) and explain the connection. Where did the language change, and what does that tell you about what each era valued?

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose ONE medieval legacy still present today (constitutional tradition, university system, Protestant religious denominations, common law, or pandemic response lessons). Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) the medieval origin, (2) the transmission path to today, (3) what has been preserved and what has changed. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay: "The Middle Ages Are Not Over." Identify three specific realities of modern life that are direct medieval inheritances. Argue which has had the greatest positive or negative impact on today's society. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute explanation: (1) Name two things in modern life with direct medieval roots. (2) Trace the specific path from medieval origin to modern form. (3) Critically evaluate: is the modern version a faithful continuation, or has it been distorted from its original purpose? 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Medieval DNA" visual showing 4 modern institutions and tracing their roots to medieval predecessors. For each: show the medieval version, show the modern version, and draw a transmission line with key moments of change annotated. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute video: "Things You Use That Were Invented in the Middle Ages." Identify and explain 4–5 medieval innovations that you encounter in daily life — be specific and accurate, not vague. For each, explain why it was invented and how it reached its modern form. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project on medieval history. Write a 200-word reflection: What legacy of the events you studied is still present today? Did your original work make these connections explicit? What would you add?

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. Constitutional Lineage Chart — Create an annotated lineage chart tracing constitutional rights from Magna Carta (1215) → English Petition of Right (1628) → English Bill of Rights (1689) → U.S. Constitution (1787) → Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948). For each document, identify: what right was established, who it protected, who it excluded, and what was the political context.
  2. "We Are Still Reforming" — Reformation Legacy Brief — Research how the Protestant Reformation's divisions still shape contemporary religious, political, and cultural conflicts. Pick one specific modern conflict (Northern Ireland, culture war in the U.S., European secular vs. religious political parties) and trace its Reformation-era roots.
  3. Black Death and COVID-19: A Comparative Response Analysis — Research pandemic response to the Black Death (quarantine, flagellant movements, scapegoating of Jews, medical understanding) and compare to COVID-19 response. Write a 1-page argument: What lessons from the Black Death were applied in 2020? What lessons were not applied, and what were the consequences?

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: List three modern institutions or practices with direct medieval roots. For each, name the medieval origin, the modern form, and one way they are similar.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Choose one of the above and trace the specific transmission path — how did it survive from medieval times to today? Identify at least two key moments where it was transmitted, transformed, or nearly lost.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word critical evaluation of one medieval legacy: Is the modern version a genuine continuation of the original intent, or has it been selectively borrowed to serve different purposes? Use specific evidence.

docs.title: Age of Revolutions: Science, Technology, and the Scientific Revolution — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.1 — Analyze historical achievements related to science and technology Era/Unit (if SS): Era 3 — Age of Revolutions (~1400–1900 CE) ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.W.4 — Argument writing; 9-10.R.9 — Analyzing informational texts NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Era 3 is the era when science stopped being a tool of religion and started becoming a tool of human mastery over the natural world. The Scientific Revolution, Industrial Revolution, and Age of Exploration were all driven by new technologies — and those technologies were driven by new ways of thinking about how the world worked.

  • Scientific Revolution (~1543–1687 CE): The period when European thinkers fundamentally challenged ancient and religious authority on questions of nature, replacing observation-and-authority with observation-and-experiment. Key figures: Copernicus, Galileo, Kepler, Newton.
  • Heliocentric Model: Copernicus's 1543 theory that the Earth revolves around the Sun — challenging the geocentric (Earth-centered) model that the Catholic Church had endorsed. Galileo's telescopic observations confirmed it; he was placed under house arrest.
  • Scientific Method: The systematic process of forming a hypothesis, designing an experiment, collecting data, and drawing evidence-based conclusions. Francis Bacon and René Descartes formalized the method; it is still the foundation of modern science.
  • Printing Press (legacy): By Era 3, Gutenberg's 1440 invention had transformed scientific communication — scientific findings could be published and replicated across Europe. Newton's Principia Mathematica (1687) was only possible because printing made international scientific exchange possible.
  • Industrial Revolution (~1760–1840 CE): The transformation from hand production to machine production, centered in England. Key technologies: the steam engine (James Watt, 1769), spinning jenny, power loom, steam-powered railroads.
  • Steam Engine: The mechanical engine that converted heat (from burning coal) into motion. The steam engine powered factories, railroads, and ships — transforming manufacturing, transportation, and global trade.
  • Navigation Technology: The Age of Exploration required new navigational tools: the caravel (a Portuguese sailing ship that could sail against the wind), the astrolabe (for measuring latitude), improved magnetic compass use, and better cartography (mapmaking). These technologies made long-distance ocean voyages possible.
  • Germ Theory: Louis Pasteur's 1859–1861 experiments proved that microorganisms caused disease — replacing the miasma theory (bad air causes disease). Germ theory enabled modern medicine: vaccination, antiseptic surgery, pasteurization of milk.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Explain what the Scientific Revolution challenged and what it replaced. - [ ] Identify the key technologies that made the Age of Exploration possible. - [ ] Distinguish between the Scientific Revolution (new ways of thinking) and the Industrial Revolution (new ways of producing). - [ ] Explain why the steam engine was transformative beyond just being a more powerful machine. - [ ] Identify at least two scientific achievements of Era 3 that are still directly relevant to daily life today.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can state that Newton discovered gravity and that James Watt improved the steam engine — without yet analyzing what these achievements changed about how humans related to the natural world, or why they happened in this particular era and not earlier.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.1 — Analyze historical achievements related to science and technology (Era 3)

To meet this standard, you must analyze what a technological or scientific achievement made possible — and why it happened when and where it did. In Era 3, a key theme is the relationship between scientific thinking and political/economic power: the Industrial Revolution made Britain the world's dominant power; the Scientific Revolution challenged religious authority.

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by analyzing why the Industrial Revolution started in England and not France, China, or India. The student identifies the specific conditions in England that made it possible: (1) coal deposits close to the surface in Wales and the Midlands, providing cheap fuel; (2) enclosure movement that drove peasants off the land into cities, creating a labor force; (3) strong patent law that incentivized invention; (4) a merchant class with capital to invest in machinery; (5) an empire that provided raw materials (cotton from India and the American South) and markets for finished goods. The student argues that the Industrial Revolution was not an inevitable result of human ingenuity — it happened in England because England had the specific economic and political conditions that made it profitable.

The Difference: A 2.0 student knows the steam engine was important and that the Industrial Revolution started in England. A 3.0 student analyzes WHY it started in England — what conditions made it possible, and why those conditions didn't exist elsewhere at the same time.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate the costs of technological progress — who paid for the Industrial Revolution, what was destroyed to make it possible, and what lessons this holds for technological change today.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Technology and empire: The Industrial Revolution gave Britain a massive military and economic advantage that it used to build a global empire. Analyze the relationship between industrial technology and imperial power — was empire a side effect of industrialization, or was industrialization only possible because of empire (raw materials, markets)? 2. Science vs. religion: Galileo's house arrest and the Scopes Trial (1925) are both cases of science conflicting with religious authority. Analyze what is similar and what is different about these two cases. Is science-religion conflict inevitable, or is it a product of specific historical conditions? 3. Who paid? The Industrial Revolution produced enormous wealth — and required child labor, 16-hour workdays, deadly factory conditions, and environmental destruction. Analyze: was the Industrial Revolution a positive development for humanity? Who benefited, who paid the costs, and does your answer change depending on whose perspective you take?

4.0 Example: A student argues that the Scientific Revolution and the Industrial Revolution together created the conditions for both the greatest material improvements in human life (medicine, food security, transportation) AND the greatest threats to human survival (climate change, nuclear weapons, industrial pollution). The student evaluates whether this trade-off was inevitable — could the benefits have been achieved without the harms? — and argues that the historical answer to this question shapes how we think about technological development today.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: The French Revolution — CrashCourse World History #33 · Episode in reading library Also: The Industrial Revolution — CrashCourse WH #35 · Episode in reading library Also: The Renaissance (incl. Scientific Revolution context) — CrashCourse WH #22 · Episode in reading library

Key Timestamps (French Revolution ep): - [02:00]: The role of Enlightenment ideas in the Revolution. Why it matters: The Scientific Revolution produced the Enlightenment — the belief that reason, not tradition, should guide human affairs. The French Revolution was in part an attempt to apply scientific/rational thinking to political organization.

Watch Prompt: Track the relationship between new ideas (scientific, philosophical) and political upheaval. Do new ways of thinking about nature lead to new ways of thinking about politics? Find at least two examples in the video.

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen (1789) — The Declaration draws on Enlightenment ideas that were themselves products of the Scientific Revolution's rational approach to human affairs. Identify language in the Declaration that reflects Enlightenment thinking about "natural rights" and "reason."
  • Common Sense — Thomas Paine (1776) — Paine explicitly invokes reason and natural law (products of Enlightenment thinking shaped by the Scientific Revolution). Identify where Paine uses scientific/rational arguments rather than religious ones.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose ONE technological or scientific achievement of Era 3 (steam engine, Scientific Revolution, navigation technology, germ theory). Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) what it achieved, (2) what conditions made it possible in this specific era and place, (3) what it changed — economically, politically, or socially. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay arguing whether the Industrial Revolution was a net positive or net negative for humanity. Consider: who benefited, who paid the costs, what were the short-term vs. long-term effects. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute analysis: (1) What was the Scientific Revolution and what authority did it challenge? (2) Why did the Industrial Revolution start in England? (3) What is the relationship between scientific thinking and political revolution in this era? 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Technology and Power" diagram showing how three Era 3 technologies (navigation, steam engine, or scientific method) created or shifted political power. For each technology, show: who invented/controlled it, who benefited, and how it changed the global balance of power. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute "Industrial Revolution Documentary Clip." Focus on one technological achievement and explain: what it was, what problem it solved, what conditions made it possible, and what it cost — humanly, socially, environmentally. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project about Era 3. Write a 200-word reflection: What technology shaped the events you studied? Did your original work analyze WHY that technology emerged when and where it did?

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. "Why England?" Industrial Revolution Conditions Analysis — Research the specific conditions (geographic, economic, political, labor) that made England the birthplace of the Industrial Revolution. Build a 1-page visual argument showing the causal factors and arguing which was most important.
  2. Galileo vs. the Church: A Primary Source Analysis — Research Galileo's trial (1633). Find primary sources from both the Church's perspective and Galileo's writings. Analyze: what was really at stake — was this about astronomy, or about who had authority to define truth?
  3. The Costs of Industrialization: Data Brief — Research the working conditions, wages, child labor, and life expectancy of English factory workers during the Industrial Revolution. Build a data-driven brief arguing whether workers' lives were better or worse during industrialization than before.

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Identify three technological achievements of Era 3. For each, state: what it was, when and where it was developed, and what problem it solved.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph explaining why one Era 3 technology happened in a specific place at a specific time. What conditions made it possible there and then but not elsewhere?
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word argument evaluating the costs and benefits of one Era 3 technology. Who benefited? Who paid the costs? Does your assessment change depending on the time frame (short-term vs. long-term)?

docs.title: Age of Revolutions: Religion, Ideology, and the Reformation — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.2 — Explain historical changes related to religions and ideologies Era/Unit (if SS): Era 3 — Age of Revolutions (~1400–1900 CE) ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.R.9 — Analyze informational/argumentative texts; 9-10.W.4 — Argument writing NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Era 3 is when religious and ideological monopolies broke apart. The Protestant Reformation shattered Western Christian unity; the Enlightenment challenged the idea that religion should govern political life; the Age of Revolution produced new ideologies (liberalism, nationalism, later socialism) that competed directly with religious authority for people's ultimate loyalty.

  • Protestant Reformation (1517 CE): Martin Luther's publication of his 95 Theses challenged the Catholic Church's authority — specifically the sale of indulgences. The Reformation split Western Christianity permanently into Catholic and Protestant branches.
  • Martin Luther: German monk who argued (1) that salvation comes through faith alone, not through Church rituals or payments; (2) that the Bible, not the Pope, was the ultimate religious authority; (3) that all believers were equal before God — the "priesthood of all believers." The printing press made his ideas impossible to suppress.
  • Calvinism / Predestination: John Calvin's doctrine that God had predestined (pre-selected) who would be saved and who would be damned — removing the Church's power to grant or withhold salvation. Calvinism spread to France (Huguenots), Scotland (Presbyterians), and England (Puritans, later Pilgrims).
  • Enlightenment (~1685–1815 CE): A philosophical movement arguing that reason, not religious tradition, should guide human affairs. Key Enlightenment thinkers: Locke (natural rights), Rousseau (social contract), Voltaire (religious tolerance), Montesquieu (separation of powers). The Enlightenment provided the ideological foundation for the American and French Revolutions.
  • Natural Rights: The Enlightenment concept (associated with John Locke) that all humans are born with inherent rights — life, liberty, property — that no government can legitimately take away. This idea directly inspired the American Declaration of Independence and French Declaration of the Rights of Man.
  • Secularism: The principle that government and civic institutions should be separate from religion. The French Revolution's radical secularism (closing churches, executing priests, renaming months) was a product of Enlightenment ideology applied violently to political life.
  • Nationalism: The ideology that a people sharing language, culture, and history should form their own sovereign nation-state. Nationalism emerged in the late 18th and 19th centuries as a rival to both religious identity and dynastic loyalty. It drove the unification of Italy and Germany and the breakup of the Ottoman and Austro-Hungarian empires.
  • Abolition: The ideology and movement to end slavery. Abolitionists used both religious arguments (slavery violates the God-given dignity of humans) and Enlightenment arguments (slavery violates natural rights). The tension between these frameworks was central to abolitionist debates.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Explain what Luther's "95 Theses" specifically challenged about the Catholic Church. - [ ] Distinguish between the religious arguments and the Enlightenment arguments for natural rights. - [ ] Explain what "secularism" means and how it differs from atheism. - [ ] Identify at least two modern political ideas that have roots in Enlightenment ideology. - [ ] Explain what "nationalism" is and why it was a new ideology in the 18th–19th centuries.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can state that Martin Luther challenged the Catholic Church in 1517 and that the Enlightenment produced ideas about natural rights — without yet explaining how the Reformation's "priesthood of all believers" logic connects to democratic ideas, or how Enlightenment rationalism led directly to the French Revolution's violence.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.2 — Explain historical changes related to religions and ideologies (Era 3)

To meet this standard, you must explain the causal connections between Era 3's ideological changes and their political consequences. How did a religious challenge (the Reformation) lead to political changes? How did a philosophical movement (Enlightenment) become a revolutionary force? How did new secular ideologies (nationalism, liberalism) compete with and transform religious ones?

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by explaining how the Protestant Reformation's core logic — that individuals could read and interpret the Bible themselves, without Church mediation — had political consequences that Luther himself did not intend. If ordinary people could interpret Scripture for themselves, why couldn't they also interpret law, or evaluate their rulers' legitimacy? The student traces the path from Luther's "priesthood of all believers" → Calvinist resistance theory (Protestants resisting Catholic monarchs) → English Civil War Puritans arguing for Parliament's authority over the king → Locke's natural rights theory (influenced by Puritan political thought) → American and French Revolutions. The student shows how a religious idea about Scripture interpretation became a political idea about popular sovereignty.

The Difference: A 2.0 student knows the Reformation challenged the Church. A 3.0 student traces how the Reformation's logic led to democratic political ideas — a consequence Luther feared and opposed.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate competing ideologies of Era 3 and assess what they got right and wrong — and how their competition shaped the modern world.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Ideology and violence: Both the Protestant Reformation and the French Revolution were ideological movements that produced enormous violence (Thirty Years' War, Terror). Is violence an inherent product of ideological revolution, or is it a product of specific circumstances? Use both cases as evidence. 2. Religion vs. Enlightenment: The Enlightenment was critical of religion, and many Enlightenment thinkers were deists or atheists. But many abolitionists used religious arguments while Enlightenment ideas were used to justify slavery (e.g., arguments about "natural" racial hierarchy). Analyze: was the Enlightenment a progressive force? Were religious arguments more effective against slavery than secular ones? 3. Nationalism's double edge: Nationalism in Era 3 was often a force for liberation (independence movements, unification of fragmented peoples). By Era 4, it had become the force that produced World War I and the Holocaust. Explain how the same ideology could produce liberation in one century and catastrophe in the next.

4.0 Example: A student argues that the Enlightenment's "reason" was not the neutral, universal force its proponents claimed — it was a tool deployed by specific groups (white, male, European property owners) to argue for their freedom while simultaneously rationalizing the enslavement of Africans, colonization of indigenous peoples, and exclusion of women. The student cites Locke's investments in slave-trading companies and Jefferson's slave-owning as evidence that Enlightenment "natural rights" were systematically not applied universally, and argues that this selective application was not a contradiction of Enlightenment ideology but a feature of how it was practiced.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: The French Revolution — CrashCourse World History #33 · Episode in reading library Also: Protestant Reformation — CrashCourse WH #25 · Episode in reading library Also: The Enlightenment — CrashCourse WH #30 · Episode in reading library

Key Timestamps (French Revolution ep): - [04:30]: The role of Enlightenment ideology in the Revolution. Why it matters: Rousseau, Voltaire, and Montesquieu are cited as direct influences on the revolutionaries. This is your evidence for how ideology drove political action. - [08:00]: The Declaration of the Rights of Man — note how it uses both natural rights language (Enlightenment) and some implied religious language. Why it matters: Even in a secular revolution, religious framing didn't fully disappear. - [11:00]: The Terror. Why it matters: The same Enlightenment ideology that produced the Declaration of the Rights of Man also produced the Committee of Public Safety and mass executions. Ideology is not a guarantee of humane outcomes.

Watch Prompt: Track how ideas (not just events) drive the French Revolution. For each major development, identify which ideology — religious, Enlightenment rationalist, nationalist, or proto-socialist — is driving it.

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen (1789) — Read this as an ideological document. Identify: Which ideas are Enlightenment? Which are nationalist? Is there any religious language? What does this combination tell you about the competing ideologies in revolutionary France?
  • Mary Wollstonecraft — A Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792) — Wollstonecraft turned Enlightenment logic against itself: if natural rights belong to all rational beings, why are women excluded? Analyze her argument as an example of using the dominant ideology to challenge the dominant ideology's own exclusions.
  • Common Sense — Thomas Paine (1776) — Paine's argument draws on Enlightenment natural rights ideology but is aimed at a mass audience, not philosophers. Analyze how he translates elite ideology into popular argument.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) How did the Protestant Reformation challenge Catholic ideological authority? (2) How did Enlightenment ideology challenge religious authority over political life? (3) What were the political consequences of these ideological challenges — using one specific revolution as evidence? 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay arguing whether the Enlightenment was a genuinely universal movement or whether its claims of universal rights were selective and hypocritical. Use specific evidence from at least two sources. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute analysis: (1) What did Luther's Reformation challenge, and what political logic did it inadvertently unleash? (2) What is Enlightenment ideology and how did it drive political revolutions? (3) What does Wollstonecraft's argument tell us about the limits of Enlightenment universalism? 4 Visual/Creative Create an "Ideology Family Tree" for Era 3. Start with two roots: Protestant Reformation and Enlightenment. Show how these produced branches: nationalism, liberalism, abolitionism, feminism. For each branch, include a key thinker and a key text. Annotate where the branches conflict with each other. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute "Competing Ideologies" video. Present the argument for one Era 3 ideology (Reformation, Enlightenment rationalism, nationalism, or abolition) as its strongest proponents would have made it — then present the strongest counterargument. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project on Era 3. Write a 200-word reflection: What ideology drove the events you studied? Did your original work identify the ideology explicitly? Where did the ideology's claims not match its practice?

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. "The Logic of Rebellion" — Reformation to Revolution — Trace the ideological pathway from Luther's "priesthood of all believers" (1517) to the American Declaration of Independence (1776) to the Haitian Revolution (1791). Show how the same core idea (individuals can interpret truth/authority for themselves) produced very different political revolutions depending on who was invoking it.
  2. Wollstonecraft's Challenge: Then and Now — Analyze Wollstonecraft's argument in Vindication (1792). Write a 1-page analysis: what was her argument, why was it radical for its time, and where do the same arguments appear in contemporary feminist discourse?
  3. The Terror: When Ideology Goes Wrong — Research the Reign of Terror (1793–1794). Write a 1-page argument: Was the Terror a betrayal of Enlightenment ideals, or was it a logical consequence of applying Enlightenment rationalism to political life without limits?

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Identify and explain three ideological changes of Era 3 (Protestant Reformation, Enlightenment, nationalism, or abolitionism). For each, state what it challenged and what it proposed in its place.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph tracing how one Era 3 ideology led to a specific political consequence — explaining the causal connection, not just listing both things.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word argument evaluating whether the Enlightenment's claims of universal rights were genuinely universal or selectively applied. Use specific evidence.

  • Wollstonecraft — Vindication — Primary source: challenging Enlightenment exclusions
  • Common Sense — Paine — Primary source: Enlightenment ideology for mass audiences

  • W.4 Argument Writing — ELA crosswalk

How to use this skinny: 1. Start with the 2.0 vocabulary and processes. 2. Read the 3.0 example carefully. That's the target. 3. Choose your demonstration mode and begin gathering evidence. 4. Pull additional skinnies if you hit a gap. 5. Check your scale tasks as you complete them.

docs.title: Age of Revolutions: Political Systems, Revolution, and the Nation-State — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.3 — Analyze the effects of different political systems on people Era/Unit (if SS): Era 3 — Age of Revolutions (~1400–1900 CE) ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.W.4 — Argument writing; 9-10.R.9 — Analyzing informational/argumentative texts NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Era 3 is the era of political revolution. Absolute monarchies were challenged and overthrown; republics were established; constitutions were written; empires were built and challenged. The central question of Era 3 is: what gives a government its legitimacy — divine right, military power, popular consent, or something else?

  • Absolute Monarchy: A political system in which the monarch holds supreme, unchecked authority. The theoretical justification: kings rule by divine right — God ordained their authority, and challenging the king was challenging God. Louis XIV of France ("L'état, c'est moi" — "I am the state") was the model absolute monarch.
  • Divine Right of Kings: The doctrine that monarchs derive their authority from God and are accountable to God alone — not to parliaments, courts, or subjects. Challenged by the Reformation (why should the king interpret God's will?) and the Enlightenment (why should God, not reason, determine political authority?).
  • Constitutional Monarchy: A political system in which the monarch's power is limited by a constitution or parliament. England's Glorious Revolution (1688) produced a constitutional monarchy with the English Bill of Rights.
  • Republic: A political system in which sovereignty rests with the people (or their elected representatives), not with a hereditary monarch. The United States (1776) and France (1792) both became republics after revolution — with very different results.
  • Social Contract: The Enlightenment idea (Locke, Rousseau) that government derives its legitimacy from the consent of the governed. Locke argued that if a government violates natural rights, the people have the right to revolt. This idea directly justified the American and French Revolutions.
  • Colonialism: The political and economic system by which European powers controlled territories and peoples in Africa, Asia, and the Americas. Colonialism was a political system from the colonized people's perspective — one imposed by conquest, maintained by force, and justified by racial ideology.
  • Revolution: A rapid, fundamental change in political power and organization. Era 3 revolutions: the Glorious Revolution (England, 1688), the American Revolution (1776), the French Revolution (1789), the Haitian Revolution (1791–1804), and multiple Latin American independence movements.
  • Haitian Revolution (1791–1804): The only successful slave revolution in history. Enslaved Africans in Saint-Domingue (Haiti) used French Revolutionary ideology (liberté, égalité, fraternité) against France itself to win independence. Haiti became the world's first Black republic — and was immediately punished economically by France and the United States.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Explain the difference between absolute monarchy and constitutional monarchy. - [ ] Define the "social contract" and identify which thinker developed it. - [ ] Explain why the Haitian Revolution was more radical than either the American or French Revolutions. - [ ] Identify at least two effects of colonialism on colonized peoples. - [ ] Distinguish between a republic and a democracy (all democracies are republics, not all republics are democracies).

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can describe the structure of absolute monarchy and state that the French Revolution overthrew the king — without yet analyzing how different political systems affected ordinary people's lives, or why the Haitian Revolution was a more radical challenge to the political order than the American or French Revolutions.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.3 — Analyze the effects of different political systems on people (Era 3)

To meet this standard, you must analyze how specific political systems shaped individuals' lives — not just describe the systems. In Era 3, the key analytical question is: what did political revolution actually change for ordinary people, and for whom did it change nothing (or make things worse)?

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by analyzing how the American Revolution's political outcomes differed based on who you were. For wealthy white male property owners: the revolution produced a republic with legal rights, voting, and representation — a genuine political transformation. For enslaved Black Americans: the revolution changed nothing — the Constitution explicitly protected slavery, and many founders increased their enslaved workforces after independence. For Native Americans: the revolution was a catastrophe — the new republic was more aggressive in territorial expansion than the British crown had been. For women: they gained no political rights. The student analyzes the same political event — the creation of a republic — and shows its radically different effects based on social position.

The Difference: A 2.0 student knows the American Revolution created a republic with democratic principles. A 3.0 student analyzes who actually benefited from those principles — and who didn't.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate competing political systems and their outcomes across multiple cases — comparing revolutions, analyzing what determines whether political change is genuine or cosmetic, and connecting Era 3 political transformations to today.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Comparing revolutions: The American and Haitian Revolutions both used "liberty" and "natural rights" as justifications. Analyze how the same ideology produced different outcomes — and why the Haitian Revolution was more threatening to the existing political order. 2. Colonialism as a political system: Analyze colonialism as a political system from the colonized people's perspective. What were its effects on individuals? How did colonized people resist, and what political ideologies did they use? (Hint: many anti-colonial movements used Enlightenment ideology against colonial powers — the same move Wollstonecraft made with gender.) 3. Revolution and stability: The French Revolution went from a constitutional monarchy to a republic to the Terror to Napoleon's empire in 10 years. The American Revolution produced a relatively stable republic. Analyze: what made the difference? Was it ideology, social conditions, external threats, or something else?

4.0 Example: A student argues that the Haitian Revolution (1791–1804) was the true test of Enlightenment political ideology — because it demanded that "all men are created equal" actually mean all men, including enslaved Africans. The student analyzes how the international response (France, Britain, and the United States all worked to isolate and economically punish Haiti after independence) reveals what the American and French Revolutions' political principles actually meant in practice: liberty for white men, hostility to liberty for everyone else. The student then connects Haiti's economic isolation to the country's ongoing poverty and argues that the political choices made in 1804–1825 have never been fully reversed.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: The French Revolution — CrashCourse World History #33 · Episode in reading library Also: Haitian Revolution — CrashCourse WH #31 · Episode in reading library Also: Imperialism — CrashCourse WH #37 · Episode in reading library

Key Timestamps (French Revolution ep): - [05:00]: The estates system — who had political power before the Revolution. Why it matters: The Revolution's starting point was a profoundly unequal political system; understanding what it overthrew helps you analyze what it actually changed. - [10:00]: The Declaration of Rights of Man and Citizen — who was included. Why it matters: The Declaration's language was universal, but its application was not. This gap is the central political problem of Era 3. - [13:00]: The Terror. Why it matters: Revolutionary political systems can become more oppressive than what they replaced. This is a pattern to analyze across revolutions.

Watch Prompt: For each political system change described, ask: (1) Who gained power? (2) Who lost power? (3) Who was not included in the new system at all?

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Common Sense — Thomas Paine (1776) — Read as an argument against monarchy and for republican government. Identify Paine's specific arguments: What is wrong with monarchy? What makes a republic better? How does he respond to the "we've always had a king" argument?
  • Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen (1789) — Read as a founding document of a new political system. What rights does it establish? What obligations does it impose on the government? Who does it exclude? Compare to the American Declaration of Independence.
  • Mary Wollstonecraft — Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792) — Wollstonecraft wrote this in direct response to the Declaration of Rights of Man. She asked: if these rights are natural and universal, why are women excluded? This is the most direct contemporary challenge to the new political systems' exclusions.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose ONE Era 3 revolution. Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) what political system it overthrew and how that system affected ordinary people, (2) what new political system it created, (3) who benefited from the new system and who was excluded or harmed. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay comparing two Era 3 revolutions (American, French, or Haitian). Argue: which revolution was more radical in its effects on ordinary people? Support with specific evidence. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute analysis: (1) What was wrong with absolute monarchy as a political system for ordinary people? (2) What did the new republican political systems actually deliver — and for whom? (3) Why was the Haitian Revolution more threatening to the international order than the American or French Revolutions? 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Political System Effects" chart comparing three Era 3 political systems (absolute monarchy, constitutional monarchy, republic). For each system, show: who held power, what rights ordinary people had, and what the experience was for: a wealthy man, a poor man, a woman, and an enslaved person. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute "Who Won the Revolution?" video. Choose one Era 3 revolution and analyze its outcomes from three different perspectives: a wealthy property owner, a poor urban worker, and an enslaved person. For each, did the revolution improve their political situation? 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project about Era 3 politics. Write a 200-word reflection: Did your original work analyze who was excluded from the new political system, or did it focus only on what the revolution achieved for those who benefited?

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. "Revolution's Broken Promises" Research Brief — Choose one Era 3 revolution (American, French, or Haitian). Research what it promised vs. what it delivered for three specific groups: women, enslaved people, and poor workers. Write a 1-page brief arguing whether the revolution fulfilled its ideological promises.
  2. The Haitian Revolution: Why It Mattered — Research the Haitian Revolution in depth. Create a visual presentation arguing why the Haitian Revolution was the most radical political challenge to the existing world order in this era — and why it was punished so severely by France and the United States.
  3. Colonialism as Political System: A Colonized Person's Analysis — Research one example of colonial political governance (British India, French Algeria, Belgian Congo). Analyze what the colonial system meant for ordinary people living under it. Write a 1-page analysis from the colonized person's perspective.

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Describe three different political systems from Era 3 (absolute monarchy, constitutional monarchy, republic). For each, state: who holds power, how they got it, and what limits (if any) exist on that power.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph analyzing how one Era 3 political revolution affected ordinary people differently depending on their social position — at minimum, compare outcomes for wealthy elites and at least one marginalized group.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word argument evaluating whether one Era 3 revolution fulfilled its stated ideological principles. Use specific evidence about who was included and who was excluded from the new political system.

docs.title: The Age of Revolutions: Social & Economic Shifts
What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Before we dive into the "why" of these revolutions, we need to define the "what." Use these terms as your foundation:

  • Revolution: A fundamental and sudden change in political power or social structure.
    • Political: Changing who is in charge (e.g., overthrowing a King).
    • Social: Changing how people live and their status in society (e.g., ending slavery).
    • Economic: Changing how wealth is distributed or how goods are traded.
  • The Estates System: The social hierarchy in pre-revolutionary France.
    • First Estate: Clergy (church officials).
    • Second Estate: Nobility (landowners/aristocrats).
    • Third Estate: Everyone else (peasants, city workers, and the middle class).
  • Bourgeoisie: The "middle class" of the 18th century—merchants, lawyers, and manufacturers who had money but no political power.
  • Sans-culottes: Literally "those without breeches." These were the urban laborers and shopkeepers of the French Revolution who pushed for more radical changes than the wealthy bourgeoisie.
  • Haitian Revolution: The only successful slave revolt in history that resulted in the creation of an independent nation (Haiti).
  • Toussaint Louverture: A former slave and military leader who became the primary leader of the Haitian Revolution.
  • Mercantilism: An economic system where a mother country (like France) controlled trade and resources from colonies to build wealth. This created tension between the "mother" country and the "colonies."
  • Social Mobility: The ability for an individual to move up in social status or wealth. Revolutions often happen when social mobility is blocked by the ruling class.
  • Primary Source Bias: The idea that every historical document is filtered through the author's perspective. A wealthy merchant will describe a revolution differently than a starving peasant.
  • Propaganda: Information, especially of a biased or misleading nature, used to promote a particular political cause or point of view.

2.0 Example: A student can identify that the Third Estate in France was unhappy because they paid all the taxes while the First and Second Estates held all the power.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 3.0, you must move beyond just naming facts to analyzing impact. You need to explain how economic struggles (like high bread prices or unfair taxes) and social inequalities (like the lack of rights for commoners or enslaved people) actually drove people to take action.

The Standard (WH.6_12.4): Analyze how the economic causes of the French Revolution and the social causes of the Haitian Revolution differently affected individuals based on their status in society.

3.0 Example: A student analyzes a primary source from 1791, such as a merchant's diary during the French Revolution, and explains how rising bread prices led to urban unrest among the sans-culottes, while noting that the author’s wealthy background makes them less likely to mention the specific hardships of the peasantry.

The Difference: * 2.0: "The French people were hungry because of bad harvests." (Fact) * 3.0: "Because the Third Estate bore the entire tax burden while bread prices skyrocketed, members of the sans-culottes organized protests to demand economic relief from the monarchy." (Analysis of cause and specific group impact).

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must synthesize and evaluate. This means comparing two different revolutions and making a judgment about which one achieved more lasting change for the "common person."

4.0 Example: A student compares the French Revolution's goal of "Liberty, Equality, Fraternity" with the Haitian Revolution’s goal of ending chattel slavery. They argue that while the French Revolution provided legal rights to the bourgeoisie, the Haitian Revolution achieved a more radical and permanent social shift by dismantling the entire economic system of slavery.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: The French Revolution * 02:15 – Explains the "Three Estates" and why the Third Estate was frustrated by the lack of social mobility. * 04:30 – Discusses how debt and bread prices acted as economic triggers for the revolution. * 07:10 – Shows how the radicalization of the movement changed the goals from "reform" to "revolution."

Video: The Haitian Revolution * 03:20 – Explains the brutal reality of the plantation economy and mercantilism. * 05:45 – Highlights Toussaint Louverture’s role in organizing a massive, multi-layered resistance. * 08:10 – Discusses the unique social impact of ending slavery as a form of "social revolution."

Watch Prompt: As you watch, keep a T-chart. On one side, list Economic Causes (money, food, trade). On the other, list Social Causes (rights, status, freedom). Note which cause seems more "urgent" for each group.

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Declaration Rights Man 1789 — This document shows how the bourgeoisie wanted to move away from monarchy toward a system where wealth and talent (not just birth) determined status.
  • Haitian Declaration Independence 1804 — This highlights the unique social urgency of the Haitian Revolution: the immediate need for freedom from slavery.
  • Declaration Woman — Use this to identify bias; it shows that even "revolutionary" documents often excluded women, revealing the limits of the revolution's social reach.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text: # Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Write a 3-paragraph analysis: Identify one economic cause and one social cause for the French Revolution. Use two specific quotes from the Declaration of Rights of Man to show how those causes were addressed. 2 Extended Writing Write a 500-word comparative essay. Compare the "Social Mobility" of a merchant in France vs. an enslaved person in Haiti. How did their respective revolutions change their daily lives? 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute "podcast segment" where you explain to a peer why the Haitian Revolution is considered a "social revolution" while the French Revolution had significant "economic" components. 4 Visual/Creative Create an annotated infographic. One side shows the "Old Regime" (pre-revolution) and the other shows the "New Order." Use icons to represent different social classes and labels to show what changed for them. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a "Point/Counterpoint" video. Act out a debate between a French merchant and a Haitian plantation owner, each arguing why their revolution was more necessary for the survival of their people. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select two primary sources (one from France, one from Haiti). Annotate them using three colors: Green for economic factors, Blue for social status, and Red for the author's potential bias/perspective.

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
1. The Revolution Report: A digital magazine layout featuring "interviews" with a member of the sans-culottes and a Haitian revolutionary, detailing their specific grievances.
  2. The Comparison Map: An interactive map where clicking on Paris or Port-au-Prince reveals a pop-up summary of the social/economic shifts that occurred in those cities during the 1790s.
  3. "Voices of Change" Podcast: A 5-minute audio piece exploring how "freedom" meant different things to different people depending on their wealth and social standing.

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Define all 10 key terms in the "What You Need to Know" section and identify one primary economic cause for both the French and Haitian Revolutions.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a response explaining how the specific social status of an individual (e.g., a peasant vs. a merchant) changed their experience of the revolution, using at least one piece of evidence from the reading library.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Construct a nuanced argument comparing the long-term success of the French and Haitian revolutions in creating social mobility for the "lowest" members of society.
