docs.title: Age of Revolutions: Causes and Effects of Global Events — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.5 — Analyze causes and effects of global events using primary and/or secondary sources Era/Unit (if SS): Era 3 — Age of Revolutions (~1400–1900 CE) ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.W.4 — Argument writing; 9-10.IR.1–5 — Research and source evaluation NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Era 3's revolutions and global transformations were deeply interconnected — the American Revolution inspired the French Revolution; the French Revolution's ideology fueled the Haitian Revolution; the Industrial Revolution caused European imperialism. Understanding Era 3 means tracing the chains of cause and effect that crossed oceans and continents.

  • Immediate Cause vs. Underlying Cause: An immediate cause is the direct trigger; an underlying cause is the deeper condition that made the event possible. Example: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand was the immediate cause of WWI — but the underlying causes (imperial competition, alliance system, nationalism) made the assassination the trigger for a war that would have started eventually anyway.
  • Columbian Exchange: The transfer of plants, animals, diseases, and ideas between the Americas and the Old World after 1492. Effects: European diseases killed ~90% of the indigenous American population; New World crops (potatoes, corn, tomatoes) fed European population growth that fueled industrialization and empire.
  • Atlantic Slave Trade (~1500–1800): The forced transportation of ~12 million enslaved Africans to the Americas. Causes: European demand for labor on sugar, tobacco, and cotton plantations; European military advantage; profit motive. Effects: decimation of West African societies, foundation of New World economies, emergence of racial ideology to justify slavery.
  • Industrial Revolution Causes: Multiple converging causes: coal and iron resources in England; labor supply from enclosure movement; capital from colonial trade; patent law incentivizing invention; demand from colonial markets; political stability.
  • Primary Source Reliability in Era 3: Abundant sources survive from Era 3: pamphlets (like Common Sense), government documents, personal letters, newspapers, political cartoons. Evaluating these requires identifying: Who wrote it? What was their purpose? What audience were they writing for? What did they have to gain?
  • Corroboration: Comparing multiple sources to verify claims. A revolutionary pamphlet will describe the government differently than a loyalist pamphlet describing the same government. Both are primary sources; corroborating them helps you understand what actually happened vs. what each side wanted people to believe.
  • Imperialism: The policy of extending a nation's power through colonization, military force, or economic dominance. European industrialization produced the military and economic advantage that drove 19th-century imperialism: by 1914, European powers controlled ~84% of the Earth's land surface.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Distinguish between immediate and underlying causes for at least one Era 3 revolution. - [ ] Explain how the Columbian Exchange connected European population growth to American indigenous population collapse. - [ ] Identify at least two causes of the Industrial Revolution and explain why they converged in England specifically. - [ ] Evaluate a primary source from Era 3 for reliability — identify author, purpose, audience, and potential bias. - [ ] Explain the causal chain connecting the Atlantic Slave Trade to the racial ideology used to justify it.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can state that the French Revolution was caused by inequality and Enlightenment ideas — without yet tracing the multiple underlying causes (fiscal crisis, food shortage, political exclusion of the bourgeoisie, failure of reform) or analyzing how sources from the period reveal different perspectives on those causes.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.5 — Analyze causes and effects of global events using primary and/or secondary sources (Era 3)

To meet this standard, you must trace multi-step causal chains that cross civilizations, and use primary or secondary sources as evidence. In Era 3, the central causal chains cross the Atlantic Ocean: the Columbian Exchange, the slave trade, the revolutions, and the Industrial Revolution are all connected.

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by tracing the causal chain from the Columbian Exchange → New World crops in Europe → European population growth → labor supply for Industrial Revolution → Industrial Revolution → European military advantage → 19th-century imperialism. The student explains each link: New World crops (especially the potato) allowed larger populations to survive on less land, which drove people off farms into cities, which provided the factory labor supply the Industrial Revolution needed; the Industrial Revolution produced cheap manufactured goods and military technology (steamships, rifles) that enabled European powers to conquer large parts of Africa and Asia. The student uses a primary source at some point — perhaps Thomas Paine's Common Sense to show how revolutionary rhetoric about rights coexisted with the slave trade, or Adam Smith's Wealth of Nations to show the economic ideology that justified both industrialization and empire.

The Difference: A 2.0 student can list causes and effects of the French Revolution. A 3.0 student traces a causal chain across multiple events and civilizations — and uses a source as evidence for at least one link in the chain.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate competing interpretations of causation and assess the quality of the sources used to support them.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Who caused the Atlantic Slave Trade? Historians debate the primary causes: European demand, African participation in slave trading, or capitalism's logic of maximizing profit. Research this debate and evaluate which explanation is best supported by primary source evidence. What does each explanation conceal? 2. The domino effect of revolutions: Trace how the American Revolution affected the French Revolution, which affected the Haitian Revolution. At each step: What was borrowed? What was transformed? What was rejected? Use primary sources from each revolution to show the connections. 3. Cause or coincidence? The Industrial Revolution and European imperialism happened at roughly the same time. Some historians argue industrialization caused imperialism (it created demand for raw materials and markets). Others argue imperialism caused industrialization (the profits and materials from colonial trade funded industrial investment). Evaluate both arguments using specific evidence.

4.0 Example: A student argues that the standard causal account of the Industrial Revolution (England's coal + geography + institutions) is incomplete without including colonialism: the capital that funded early industrial investment came substantially from the Atlantic Slave Trade and colonial plantation agriculture. The student cites specific historical data (estimates of slave trade profits as a percentage of British investment capital) and evaluates the quality of that data — acknowledging that the precise numbers are contested. The student then argues that this complicates the standard "England was uniquely innovative" narrative and raises questions about who should be credited for industrialization's benefits.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: The French Revolution — CrashCourse World History #33 · Episode in reading library Also: Atlantic Slave Trade — CrashCourse WH #23 · Episode in reading library Also: Columbian Exchange — CrashCourse WH #24 · Episode in reading library

Key Timestamps (French Revolution ep): - [03:00]: The multiple causes of the French Revolution. Why it matters: The episode models good causal analysis — not just "Enlightenment ideas" but also fiscal crisis, food shortage, and political exclusion. Follow this model. - [12:00]: How the French Revolution's effects reached beyond France. Why it matters: The Revolution's effects weren't contained to France — Napoleonic wars, Latin American independence movements, Haitian Revolution were all effects.

Watch Prompt: For every cause mentioned in the video, classify it: immediate or underlying? For every effect, classify it: short-term or long-term, local or global? Build a cause-and-effect map as you watch.

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Common Sense — Thomas Paine (1776) — Use as a primary source to analyze the causes of the American Revolution from a revolutionary's perspective. What does Paine identify as the causes? What does he want his readers to conclude? How does his framing of causes shape his call to action?
  • Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789) — Use as a source for analyzing effects: What did the Revolution's leaders claim they were creating? How do these claims compare to what actually resulted? The gap between stated intentions and actual outcomes is itself a historical cause-and-effect problem.
  • Wollstonecraft — Vindication (1792) — Use as a source that reveals an effect the French Revolution's leaders didn't intend: a feminist movement challenging the new order's exclusions.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose ONE major Era 3 event (the French Revolution, the Industrial Revolution, the Atlantic Slave Trade, or the Columbian Exchange). Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) immediate and underlying causes, (2) effects across at least two civilizations, (3) use of at least one primary or secondary source as evidence. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay tracing a causal chain that crosses at least two major Era 3 events (e.g., Columbian Exchange → Industrial Revolution → imperialism). Argue which link in the chain was most significant. Use at least two sources. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute analysis: (1) Identify a major Era 3 causal chain that crosses civilizations. (2) Explain each link in the chain. (3) Evaluate a primary source: What does it reveal about the causes or effects you traced? What might it conceal? 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Global Cause-and-Effect Web" for one Era 3 event. Show causes (immediate and underlying) and effects (short- and long-term, global reach). Annotate at least two connections with a specific piece of source evidence. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute "History Detectives" video. Present a major Era 3 event and investigate its hidden or overlooked causes. Challenge the most common explanation and offer your own better-supported analysis. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project about Era 3. Write a 200-word reflection: Did your original work trace causes that crossed civilizations and time periods? What connections did you miss? What sources would you now use to support a more complete analysis?

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. "The Revolution That Caused Other Revolutions" — Trace how the American Revolution (1776) caused or influenced the French Revolution (1789), which caused or influenced the Haitian Revolution (1791). Create an annotated causal map showing the connections. For each revolution, identify one primary source that shows it was influenced by the preceding one.
  2. Atlantic Slave Trade: A Cause-and-Effect Analysis — Research the Atlantic Slave Trade's causes and effects. Create a 2-page analysis: (1) multiple causes (European demand, African participation, economic incentives, racial ideology), (2) effects on West African societies, Caribbean plantation economies, North American culture, and Atlantic capitalism, (3) a source evaluation — whose primary sources survive, and whose don't?
  3. Columbian Exchange Long-Term Effects Brief — Research the Columbian Exchange's most significant long-term effects. Build a 1-page argument: what was the single most important consequence of the Columbian Exchange — the population collapse of indigenous Americans, the New World crops that fed European growth, or the slave trade that built New World agriculture?

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Choose one Era 3 event. List: one immediate cause, two underlying causes, one short-term effect, one long-term effect. Identify whether each source you used was primary or secondary.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph tracing a causal chain that connects at least two Era 3 events across different civilizations. Identify each link and explain the connection.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word evaluation of two sources on the same Era 3 event or causal chain. Which interpretation is better supported? What does each source reveal and conceal?

docs.title: Age of Revolutions: Legacies to the Present — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.6 — Explain how past events connect to the present Era/Unit (if SS): Era 3 — Age of Revolutions (~1400–1900 CE) ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.W.4 — Argument writing; 9-10.IR.1–5 — Research and source evaluation NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Era 3 produced the ideological, political, and economic frameworks that still govern the modern world. Democratic constitutions, capitalism, industrialism, modern racism, global inequality, and anti-colonial movements — all have direct roots in this era. Understanding these legacies is essential for understanding why the world looks the way it does today.

  • Democratic Tradition: The American and French Revolutions produced the template for modern democratic constitutions. The U.S. Constitution (1787) and French Declaration of Rights of Man (1789) are ancestors of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) and virtually every modern democratic constitution.
  • Capitalism: The economic system in which private individuals own the means of production and compete in markets. The Industrial Revolution produced modern capitalism; the ideological justification came from Adam Smith's Wealth of Nations (1776). Modern globalization, multinational corporations, and wealth inequality are all descendants of Industrial Revolution capitalism.
  • Modern Racism: The ideology that divides humans into ranked "races" based on physical characteristics and assigns different rights and worth based on race. Modern racial ideology was systematically developed in the 17th–19th centuries primarily to justify the Atlantic Slave Trade and later colonialism.
  • Anti-Colonial Movements: Resistance to European imperialism, rooted in Era 3. The Haitian Revolution (1804) was the first successful anti-colonial independence movement; 20th-century decolonization (India, Algeria, Congo) used arguments and strategies with roots in Era 3 revolutionary ideology.
  • Women's Rights Movement: Mary Wollstonecraft's Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792) is the founding document of modern feminism. The 19th–20th century suffrage movement, 1960s feminist movement, and contemporary gender equality debates all draw on arguments Wollstonecraft articulated in response to the French Revolution.
  • Global Inequality: The economic gap between the industrialized Global North and the Global South (former colonial territories) was created during Era 3. The wealth extracted through colonial trade, the Atlantic Slave Trade, and industrial production for colonial markets built the economic advantage that still separates wealthy from poor nations.
  • Environmental Impact: The Industrial Revolution introduced fossil fuel burning at scale. The CO₂ currently trapping heat in the atmosphere includes molecules emitted by 19th-century British coal burning. Climate change is an Era 3 legacy.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Identify at least three modern realities with direct roots in Era 3. - [ ] Explain the specific path from the Atlantic Slave Trade to modern racial ideology. - [ ] Connect the Industrial Revolution to climate change as a causal chain. - [ ] Identify the founding document of modern feminism and explain its argument. - [ ] Explain how the Haitian Revolution's legacy connects to contemporary debates about reparations.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can state that the American Revolution produced democracy and the Industrial Revolution produced capitalism — without yet tracing the specific paths by which these systems created modern global inequality, or analyzing how Era 3 legacies shape specific contemporary debates.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.6 — Explain how past events connect to the present (Era 3)

To meet this standard, you must trace the specific transmission path from an Era 3 event to a modern reality — not just assert that they are connected. You need to explain the mechanism: how did this thing survive, transform, and arrive in its current form?

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by tracing the legacy of the Atlantic Slave Trade to the modern racial wealth gap in the United States. The path: enslaved Africans produced the agricultural wealth that capitalized early American industrialization → Emancipation (1865) freed enslaved people without land, capital, or legal protection → Reconstruction promises were broken → Jim Crow system kept Black Americans economically subordinate for a century → discriminatory policies (redlining, exclusion from GI Bill benefits) prevented wealth accumulation in the 20th century → the current racial wealth gap in America (the median white family has ~8× the wealth of the median Black family) is the direct result of this centuries-long system. The student traces the specific mechanism from Era 3's slave trade to the 2026 racial wealth gap.

The Difference: A 2.0 student knows slavery was wrong and that racism exists today. A 3.0 student traces the specific economic mechanism linking the Atlantic Slave Trade to contemporary wealth inequality.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must critically evaluate how Era 3 legacies are interpreted and contested today — who invokes them, how they are used, and whether the modern invocation accurately represents the historical reality.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Founding documents today: The U.S. Constitution and Declaration of Independence are regularly cited in contemporary political debates. Evaluate: Do the modern interpretations of these documents accurately reflect their historical context? Who was included vs. excluded from their original scope, and how has that scope changed over time? 2. Climate legacy: The atmospheric CO₂ causing climate change was primarily produced by the wealthy industrialized nations during Era 3 and Era 4. The countries most affected by climate change are primarily the former colonial territories of the Global South. Analyze: Is this a coincidence, or is it a direct consequence of the same colonial system that produced Era 3 industrialization? 3. Reparations debate: France required Haiti to pay 150 million gold francs in reparations for the property (including enslaved people) that Haitian revolutionaries "destroyed" when they freed themselves. Haiti finished paying this debt in 1947. Some argue France (and other nations) should pay reparations to Haiti and to descendants of enslaved Africans in the Americas. Analyze this debate using Era 3 history: What was Haiti's debt? Who benefited from slavery? Who bears continuing consequences?

4.0 Example: A student argues that the most important Era 3 legacy is one rarely named as such: the fossil fuel economy. The Industrial Revolution's adoption of coal burning as the primary energy source created an economic infrastructure — coal companies, railroad companies, industrial cities, global trade in manufactured goods — that became so embedded in the global economy that transitioning away from it has proven politically impossible for 150 years. The student then evaluates whether the current climate crisis is primarily a technological problem (we need better energy technology) or a political problem (we have the technology but lack the political will to disrupt Era 3's fossil fuel interests).

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: The French Revolution — CrashCourse World History #33 · Episode in reading library Also: Imperialism — CrashCourse WH #37 · Episode in reading library Also: The Industrial Revolution — CrashCourse WH #35 · Episode in reading library

Key Timestamps (French Revolution ep): - [14:00]: The Revolution's long legacy — what it left behind ideologically. Why it matters: The French Revolution is the source of the "left vs. right" political spectrum (from the seating positions in the revolutionary assembly) still used today. - [11:00]: The gap between the Revolution's ideals and its reality. Why it matters: Understanding this gap is essential for evaluating Era 3 legacies — did the revolution deliver what it promised, for whom, and when?

Watch Prompt: For each event in the video, ask: Is this still with us? In what form? Who carries the consequences today, and are they the same people who carried them in 1789?

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Common Sense — Thomas Paine (1776) — Paine's arguments about democracy and natural rights are still invoked in contemporary political debates. Read the text and identify: How has Paine been used since 1776? Has his argument been applied consistently to all people, or selectively?
  • Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789) — Read alongside the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948). What did the UDHR add that the 1789 Declaration lacked? What does that evolution tell you about what "rights" have meant in different eras?
  • Wollstonecraft — Vindication (1792) — Read as the founding document of a movement still ongoing. What arguments does Wollstonecraft make in 1792 that are still being made today? What has been won? What hasn't?

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose ONE Era 3 legacy still present today (democratic tradition, modern racism, capitalism/global inequality, feminism, or climate change). Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) the Era 3 origin, (2) the transmission path to today, (3) a critical evaluation — is the modern form a genuine continuation, a selective borrowing, or a distortion of the original? 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay: "We Are Still Living the Age of Revolutions." Identify three specific contemporary debates or realities that are direct consequences of Era 3 events. Argue which Era 3 legacy has the greatest ongoing impact on your daily life. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute explanation: (1) Name two Era 3 events and trace their specific paths to contemporary realities. (2) Critically evaluate one legacy: has it been honestly acknowledged and addressed, or has it been selectively remembered? (3) What would need to change for this legacy to be fully reckoned with? 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Legacy Timeline" connecting Era 3 events to contemporary realities. For each connection, show: the original event, key transmission moments, and the modern form. Annotate at least two connections with a specific piece of evidence. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute "Historical Reckoning" video. Pick one Era 3 legacy that is contested or under-acknowledged today. Present the historical case for its ongoing significance and argue why it matters for how we understand and address contemporary problems. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project about Era 3. Write a 200-word reflection: What legacy of the events you studied is still present today? Did your original work make these connections? What contemporary debate would be better understood with more knowledge of this history?

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. Haiti's Debt: A Historical Justice Analysis — Research France's demand that Haiti pay reparations for lost "property" (including enslaved people), Haiti's 150-year debt, and the contemporary reparations debate. Write a 1-page argument: What does Haiti's history tell us about the relationship between Era 3 colonialism and contemporary global inequality?
  2. "The Rights of Woman" Then and Now — Create a comparative analysis of Wollstonecraft's 1792 arguments and a contemporary feminist text or movement. Identify what has been achieved, what remains contested, and what new arguments have been added. Include a 1-page written analysis.
  3. Industrial Revolution → Climate Crisis: The Causal Chain — Build a documented causal chain from the first English coal-burning factories (~1760) to the current atmospheric CO₂ level (~420 ppm). For each major step, identify: who made the decision, what economic incentives were involved, and whether the decision-makers knew the risks. Conclude with a 1-paragraph argument about moral responsibility.

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: List three modern realities with direct roots in Era 3. For each, name the Era 3 origin and the modern form.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Choose one of the above and trace the specific transmission path — how did it survive from Era 3 to today? Identify at least two key moments of transmission or transformation.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word critical evaluation of one Era 3 legacy: Is it being honestly acknowledged and addressed, or selectively remembered? Use specific evidence from both the historical record and the contemporary context.

docs.title: Age of Global War and Globalization: Science, Technology, and Weapons of Mass Destruction — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.1 — Analyze historical achievements related to science and technology Era/Unit (if SS): Era 4 — Age of Global War and Globalization (~1900–present) ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.W.4 — Argument writing; 9-10.R.9 — Analyzing informational texts NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Era 4's scientific and technological achievements are inseparable from their political and military contexts. The 20th century produced the most transformative technologies in human history — and the most destructive. Radar, nuclear weapons, the internet, satellites, and vaccines were all products of wartime or Cold War competition. Understanding these technologies means understanding the political systems that funded and shaped them.

  • Nuclear Weapons: The atomic bomb, first tested in July 1945 and dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki in August 1945, used nuclear fission to release enormous energy. The bombs killed ~200,000 people immediately. Nuclear weapons fundamentally changed warfare and international relations: the logic of "mutually assured destruction" (MAD) shaped the Cold War.
  • Manhattan Project: The U.S. government's secret project to develop atomic weapons during WWII (~1942–1945). Employed ~130,000 people, including many of the world's leading physicists. The project produced the atomic bomb at a cost of ~$2 billion (about $28 billion today).
  • The Internet (ARPANET): Originally a U.S. Defense Department project (ARPANET, 1969) to create a communication network that could survive nuclear attack. Became the public internet in the 1990s. Now the infrastructure for global communication, commerce, and social interaction.
  • Satellite Technology: Military satellites for reconnaissance and communication developed during the Cold War now provide GPS navigation, weather forecasting, and telecommunications that are invisible but essential infrastructure for daily life.
  • Nuclear Energy: The same fission reaction used in nuclear weapons can be used to generate electricity in nuclear power plants. After WWII, nuclear energy was promoted as "atoms for peace" — an alternative to fossil fuels. Today, nuclear power generates ~10% of the world's electricity.
  • Green Revolution: The 1940s–1970s development of high-yield crop varieties, synthetic fertilizers, and pesticides that dramatically increased agricultural output globally. Credited with preventing mass famine; also criticized for environmental damage, dependence on fossil-fuel-derived fertilizers, and concentration of agricultural power.
  • Medical Technology: The 20th century produced antibiotics (penicillin, 1928), vaccines (polio, 1955; smallpox eradicated 1980), and imaging technology (X-ray, MRI). These achievements extended average human lifespan from ~50 years at 1900 to ~70+ years globally.
  • Climate Science: The scientific consensus that human CO₂ emissions are causing global warming was established by the 1970s and overwhelmingly confirmed by subsequent research. Climate change is itself a technological and scientific problem produced by Era 3–4 industrialization.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Explain why nuclear weapons changed international relations — not just warfare. - [ ] Trace the path from Cold War military research to the public internet. - [ ] Distinguish between nuclear weapons and nuclear energy — same underlying physics, radically different applications. - [ ] Explain what the Green Revolution achieved and what it cost. - [ ] Identify at least two medical technological achievements of the 20th century and their global impact.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can state that the atomic bomb was dropped on Japan in 1945 and that it was very powerful — without yet analyzing what the decision to use it revealed about the relationship between technological achievement and political decision-making, or how nuclear weapons changed the logic of international relations.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.1 — Analyze historical achievements related to science and technology (Era 4)

To meet this standard, you must analyze the political and social context that produced a technology, not just describe what it does. In Era 4, almost every major technology was shaped by war, Cold War competition, or the profit motive — and understanding a technology means understanding those shaping forces.

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by analyzing the decision to drop the atomic bomb as both a technological and political question. The Manhattan Project produced the technology; the political decision to use it was shaped by multiple factors: (1) the stated military rationale — avoiding a costly land invasion of Japan; (2) unstated factors — demonstrating U.S. power to the Soviet Union at the dawn of the Cold War; (3) the racial dimension — the bomb was used against a non-white enemy in the Pacific theater but was not used in Europe, where it was also available by war's end. The student analyzes not just what the technology was, but what it meant politically — as an act of war, as a geopolitical message, and as a racial choice — and evaluates these claims using primary sources (Truman's diary, Stimson's memos, Japanese survivor accounts).

The Difference: A 2.0 student knows the atomic bomb was dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. A 3.0 student analyzes why it was dropped — the full political and military context — and uses primary sources to evaluate competing explanations.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate the long-term consequences of Era 4 technological achievements and assess the ethical frameworks used to justify them.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Technology and ethics: The scientists who built the first nuclear weapons included many who immediately regretted doing so (J. Robert Oppenheimer: "Now I am become Death, the destroyer of worlds"). Analyze: what ethical frameworks did the scientists and decision-makers use to justify developing and using the bomb? Were those frameworks adequate? What ethical framework should govern the development of dangerous technologies? 2. Technology and inequality: The Green Revolution increased food production globally — but its benefits were distributed unequally (wealthy farmers with access to fertilizers and irrigation benefited; small farmers in the developing world were often displaced). Analyze: Is this pattern of unequal distribution of technological benefits and costs a recurring pattern in Era 4? 3. Technology and surveillance: The internet was designed for military communication resilience; it became a commercial platform for global connection; it has now also become a surveillance infrastructure. Analyze: When a technology is developed for one purpose and repurposed for another, who is responsible for the consequences of the repurposing?

4.0 Example: A student argues that climate science is the most important technological/scientific achievement of Era 4 — not because it produced a technology, but because it produced knowledge of a civilizational threat that human civilization has proven unable to act on despite 50+ years of warning. The student evaluates: Why have societies been unable to respond adequately to climate science's findings? Is this a failure of the science (unclear), the political systems (compromised by fossil fuel interests), the economic systems (externalized costs), or human psychology (inability to act on long-term threats)? The student uses primary sources — IPCC reports, fossil fuel industry internal documents showing they knew about climate risks — to support the analysis.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: World War I — CrashCourse WH #38 · Episode in reading library Also: WWII Part 1 — CrashCourse WH #40 · Episode in reading library Also: WWII Part 2 — CrashCourse WH #41 · Episode in reading library Also: China's Revolutions — CrashCourse WH #39 · Episode in reading library Also: The Cold War — CrashCourse WH #42 · Episode in reading library

Watch Prompt (general): For any Era 4 technology mentioned, ask: (1) What political or military context funded its development? (2) What was the intended use vs. the actual use? (3) Who benefited from it, and who bore its costs? (4) Is it still with us today?

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Night — Elie Wiesel — Not a text about technology directly, but Wiesel's memoir reveals how technology was deployed in the Holocaust: industrial-scale killing required industrial-scale logistics (railroads, crematoriums, bureaucratic record-keeping). The Holocaust was, in part, a technological achievement — which raises the question of what "achievement" means.
  • 1984 — George Orwell — Orwell's novel imagined a future of surveillance technology used for political control. Written in 1948, it anticipated systems now largely in place. Read the "telescreens" and "thought police" sections: how close is Orwell's fiction to contemporary surveillance technology?

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose ONE Era 4 technology (nuclear weapons, the internet, the Green Revolution, satellites, medical technology, or climate science). Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) the political/military/economic context that produced it, (2) what it achieved and what it cost, (3) what ethical questions it raises. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay arguing whether a specific Era 4 technology has been a net benefit or net harm for humanity. Use evidence from multiple perspectives (not just the perspective of those who developed it). 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute analysis: (1) Name an Era 4 technology and explain the political context that produced it. (2) Analyze who benefited and who paid the costs. (3) Evaluate: was this technology's development ethical? What ethical framework should govern the development of dangerous technologies? 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Technology Context Map" for one Era 4 technology. Show: the political/military/economic forces that funded it, the intended purpose, the actual uses (including unintended ones), and the beneficiaries and those who paid the costs. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute "Ethics Review Board" video. You are a committee reviewing the decision to develop (choose one): the atomic bomb, or a contemporary technology (facial recognition, lethal autonomous weapons). Present: the benefits claimed, the costs, and your committee's verdict. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project about Era 4. Write a 200-word reflection: What technology shaped the events you studied? Did your original work analyze the political context that produced it, and the ethics of its development and use?

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. "Should We Have Built It?" — Atomic Bomb Ethics Analysis — Research the decision to build and use the atomic bomb. Analyze from three perspectives: the scientists who built it, the decision-makers who used it, and the survivors of Hiroshima and Nagasaki. Write a 1-page argument: was building the bomb an ethical technological achievement or an ethical failure?
  2. Internet: Military Tool to Global Infrastructure — Trace the path from ARPANET (1969) to the 2026 internet. For each major development, identify: What was the intended use? What was the actual use? Who controlled it? Who benefited? Build a visual timeline with a 1-paragraph argument about whether the internet has fulfilled its democratic potential.
  3. Green Revolution Winners and Losers — Research the Green Revolution's effects in one specific country (India, Mexico, or the Philippines). Analyze: who benefited, who was harmed, and what the long-term environmental consequences were. Write a 1-page brief arguing whether the Green Revolution should be considered a success or a failure.

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Identify three Era 4 technologies. For each, state: what it was, when it was developed, what political/military context produced it, and what it achieved.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph analyzing one Era 4 technology in full context — not just what it is, but why it was developed, who funded it, what it was intended for, what it was actually used for, and who bore the costs.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word argument evaluating the ethics of one Era 4 technology's development or use. What ethical framework applies? What does that framework conclude, and do you agree?

docs.title: Age of Global War: Ideology, Totalitarianism, and Resistance — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.2 — Explain historical changes related to religions and ideologies Era/Unit (if SS): Era 4 — Age of Global War and Globalization (~1900–present) ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.R.9 — Analyze informational/argumentative texts; 9-10.R.8 — Literary elements and author's craft NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Era 4 is the era of ideological warfare. Fascism, communism, liberal democracy, and religious nationalism competed violently for control of nations and peoples. Understanding this era means understanding these ideologies as systems — what they promised, who they appealed to, how they justified violence, and what happened when they gained state power.

  • Fascism: A far-right political ideology characterized by dictatorial power, forcible suppression of opposition, ultranationalism, and often racial hierarchy. Mussolini's Italy (1922) and Hitler's Germany (1933) were fascist states. Fascism rejected both liberal democracy and communism.
  • Nazism (National Socialism): Hitler's specific form of fascism, characterized by extreme antisemitism, racial hierarchy (Aryan supremacy), territorial expansionism (Lebensraum), and the belief that Germany's defeat in WWI was caused by a Jewish "stab in the back." Nazism produced the Holocaust.
  • Communism: The ideology that private property and class distinctions should be abolished, with collective ownership of the means of production. Soviet communism under Stalin became a totalitarian system — the ideology of collective liberation was used to justify mass murder, forced labor camps (Gulag), and political terror.
  • Totalitarianism: A political system in which the state attempts to control all aspects of public and private life — not just government, but culture, religion, family, and thought. Both Nazi Germany and Soviet Russia were totalitarian states.
  • Propaganda: Information, especially biased or misleading, used to promote a political ideology or cause. In Era 4, propaganda became a technological product — film, radio, and mass-produced print enabled governments to reach every citizen simultaneously.
  • Antisemitism: Hostility, prejudice, and discrimination against Jewish people. Long-standing in European history, antisemitism was systematized and weaponized by the Nazi regime into a program of genocide — the Holocaust.
  • Holocaust: The Nazi genocide of approximately 6 million Jewish people (and additional millions of Roma, disabled people, LGBTQ+ people, political prisoners, and others) between 1933 and 1945. The Holocaust was industrialized murder — organized, documented, and bureaucratically administered by the German state.
  • Liberation Theology / Religious Resistance: Movements that used religious belief as a basis for resisting oppression — e.g., Martin Luther King Jr.'s Christianity-grounded civil rights movement, and liberation theology in Latin America, which argued that faith required active opposition to poverty and injustice.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Define fascism, communism, and liberal democracy as ideological systems and identify what each rejects. - [ ] Explain what "totalitarianism" means and how it differs from ordinary authoritarian government. - [ ] Explain how propaganda functions — how governments use it to shape belief and suppress opposition. - [ ] Define the Holocaust and identify the specific ideology that produced it. - [ ] Distinguish between ideology as a belief system and ideology as a tool for state power.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can state that Hitler was a fascist who caused the Holocaust and that Stalin was a communist who killed millions — without yet analyzing how specific ideological beliefs enabled ordinary people to participate in mass murder, or why fascism appealed to large populations in the 1930s.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.2 — Explain historical changes related to religions and ideologies (Era 4)

To meet this standard, you must explain how specific ideologies shaped people's choices and actions — not just what the ideologies were. In Era 4, the central analytical question is: how do ideologies justify violence, and why do ordinary people accept or participate in that justification?

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by analyzing how Nazi ideology made the Holocaust possible — not just that Hitler gave orders, but that hundreds of thousands of ordinary Germans participated in killing, transportation, administration, and cover-up. The student uses historian Christopher Browning's work on "ordinary men" who became killers not out of ideological fanaticism but out of conformity, peer pressure, and career incentives. The student connects this to the ideology: Nazi propaganda had systematically dehumanized Jewish people for years before the killing began — and the ideology's specific claim that Germany was under existential threat from Jewish people gave participants a defensive justification. The student analyzes how ideology + propaganda + peer pressure + bureaucratic structure together explain mass participation in genocide — not individual evil alone.

The Difference: A 2.0 student knows Hitler and Nazi ideology caused the Holocaust. A 3.0 student analyzes the specific mechanisms by which ideology enabled ordinary people's participation in mass murder.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate competing ideologies of Era 4 — assessing not just what they claimed but what they produced, and connecting them to contemporary ideological conflicts.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Ideology and genocide: The Holocaust, the Rwandan genocide (1994), and the Cambodian genocide under the Khmer Rouge (1975–79) all have ideological roots. Compare the ideological foundations of two of these genocides. What do they have in common? What was specific to each context? Is there a pattern that could serve as an early warning system? 2. Propaganda then and now: Nazi propaganda techniques (emotional manipulation, scapegoating, repetition, enemy creation) have been analyzed extensively. Evaluate: are these techniques present in contemporary political discourse? Use specific examples. Does identifying these techniques as "propaganda" require us to label all political persuasion as manipulation? 3. Religious resistance: MLK's civil rights movement and liberation theology both used religious ideology to resist oppression. How do these uses of religion compare to the way fascism or nationalism used religion as an ideological tool? Is religion inherently a force for justice, inherently a tool for power, or neither?

4.0 Example: A student argues that totalitarianism's defining feature — the attempt to control not just behavior but thought — explains why it always fails eventually, but not before causing enormous suffering. The student analyzes both Nazi Germany and Stalinist Russia: both created elaborate systems (secret police, informants, propaganda, fear) to achieve ideological conformity; both produced massive resistance and eventual collapse; both left societies traumatized for generations. The student then evaluates: what does this tell us about human nature and the limits of ideological control? And what does it tell us about the societies that produced these regimes — what conditions made totalitarianism possible?

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text:
Video: World War I — CrashCourse WH #38 · Episode in reading library Also: WWII Part 1 — CrashCourse WH #40 · Episode in reading library Also: The Cold War — CrashCourse WH #42 · Episode in reading library

Watch Prompt (general): Track how different ideologies (fascism, communism, liberal democracy, religious nationalism) explain the same events differently. Who are the heroes and villains from each ideological perspective? What does each ideology blame for the world's problems, and what solution does each propose?

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Night — Elie Wiesel — Night is the primary source text for understanding what Nazi ideology produced at the human level. Read it not just as a survivor's account but as a document of how ideology dehumanizes people: what specific beliefs and systems had to be in place for Auschwitz to exist?
  • 1984 — George Orwell — Orwell wrote 1984 in 1948 as a warning about totalitarianism based on what he observed in Nazi Germany and Stalinist Russia. Read the "doublethink," "thoughtcrime," and "Two Minutes Hate" sections as analyses of how totalitarian ideology works — not just science fiction.
  • MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — MLK's letter demonstrates religious ideology as a tool of resistance and justice — compare to the use of ideology for oppression in this era.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Write a 3-paragraph analysis of how ONE Era 4 ideology (fascism, communism, or liberation theology) changed what people believed about themselves, their enemies, and their government. Focus on the specific mechanisms — propaganda, dehumanization, martyrdom, or moral obligation — by which the ideology shaped behavior. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay arguing: "Ideology made the Holocaust possible — not just Hitler." Use evidence from Night, historical research on ordinary perpetrators, and analysis of Nazi propaganda to support your argument. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute analysis: (1) What is totalitarianism and how does it differ from ordinary dictatorship? (2) How did Nazi ideology enable ordinary people's participation in the Holocaust? (3) What does Orwell's 1984 tell us about how totalitarian ideology works? 4 Visual/Creative Create an "Ideology Comparison Chart" for three Era 4 ideologies (fascism, communism, and religious resistance). For each: what problem does it claim to solve? Who is the enemy? What sacrifice does it demand? What does it promise? What did it actually produce? 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute "Propaganda Analysis" video. Find two pieces of propaganda from different Era 4 ideologies (Nazi, Soviet, or Allied). Analyze each using the same framework: What claim is it making? What emotion is it targeting? What evidence does it omit? Whose perspective is it centering? 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project about Era 4. Write a 200-word reflection: How did ideology shape the events you studied? Did your original work analyze how specific beliefs enabled specific actions — or did it treat ideology as background?

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. "Ordinary Men": Why People Participate in Genocide — Research the historiography of perpetrator studies (Browning, Goldhagen, Arendt's "banality of evil"). Write a 1-page analysis: What psychological, social, and ideological mechanisms enabled ordinary people to participate in the Holocaust? What does this tell us about human nature and the importance of resisting ideological dehumanization?
  2. Propaganda: Then and Now — Select three Nazi propaganda techniques (scapegoating, false urgency, enemy creation, repetition, etc.). For each, find a contemporary example from any political context (anywhere in the world, any side of any debate). Write a 1-page analysis: Are these the same techniques? What does their persistence tell us about propaganda's effectiveness?
  3. Liberation Theology: Faith as Resistance — Research liberation theology in Latin America (1960s–1990s). Analyze how religious ideology was used to resist authoritarian governments backed by the United States. Write a 1-page argument: Is using religion to justify resistance fundamentally different from using religion to justify oppression?

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Define three Era 4 ideologies (fascism, communism, liberal democracy). For each: what does it value, what does it reject, and what political system does it prefer?
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph explaining how one Era 4 ideology enabled specific historical actions — not just what people believed, but how those beliefs justified specific behaviors or policies.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word argument evaluating whether contemporary political discourse contains elements of totalitarian or propaganda techniques from Era 4. Use specific evidence. Be precise about what qualifies and what doesn't.

docs.title: Age of Global War: Political Systems, War, and Decolonization — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.3 — Analyze the effects of different political systems on people Era/Unit (if SS): Era 4 — Age of Global War and Globalization (~1900–present) ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.W.4 — Argument writing; 9-10.R.9 — Analyzing informational/argumentative texts NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Era 4 saw more political systems compete for dominance than any previous era — and more people die as a result of those competitions. Fascism, communism, liberal democracy, colonial empires, and newly independent nations all shaped the lives of people caught inside them. Understanding this era means analyzing how different political systems affected the people who lived under them.

  • Weimar Republic: Germany's democratic government (1919–1933), created after WWI. The Weimar Republic was destroyed by the Nazi Party through legal means: Hitler was elected, then used emergency powers to dismantle democracy. Lesson: democracies can legally vote themselves out of existence.
  • Totalitarian State: A government that exercises total control over all aspects of life — politics, culture, religion, family, and thought. Nazi Germany, Soviet Russia, and Maoist China were totalitarian states. Effects on individuals: loss of privacy, constant surveillance, forced conformity, and violent punishment for dissent.
  • Colonial Rule: The political system imposed by European powers on colonized peoples. Colonial governments were explicitly designed to extract resources and maintain control — not to serve the colonized population. Effects: economic exploitation, suppression of local governance, cultural destruction, and racial hierarchy enforced by law.
  • Decolonization (~1940s–1970s): The process by which European colonies gained independence. Most of Africa and Asia achieved independence in this period, often through a combination of armed struggle, civil disobedience, and international pressure. Key examples: India (1947), Ghana (1957), Algeria (1962), Zimbabwe (1980).
  • Civil Rights and Apartheid: Racial political systems — Jim Crow in the U.S. and apartheid in South Africa — used law to enforce racial hierarchy after the colonial era. These systems affected every aspect of individuals' lives: where they lived, worked, could vote, and whom they could marry.
  • The United Nations (1945): The international political body created after WWII to prevent future wars through collective security, diplomacy, and human rights standards. The UN's Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) represented a new attempt to define the political rights owed to all individuals regardless of their nation's political system.
  • Cold War Political Competition: The U.S. and Soviet Union backed opposing political systems in proxy conflicts around the world. Often, U.S. "support for democracy" meant supporting authoritarian governments that opposed communism; Soviet "support for liberation" meant supporting authoritarian governments that opposed capitalism. Both superpowers' actual commitments were to strategic interest, not political principle.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Explain how the Nazi Party legally dismantled the Weimar Republic's democratic system. - [ ] Distinguish between colonial rule and decolonization — what changed politically, what remained economically? - [ ] Explain what "decolonization" achieved and what its limits were. - [ ] Identify at least two specific effects of totalitarian political systems on ordinary people's daily lives. - [ ] Explain what the UN and Universal Declaration of Human Rights were created in response to.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can describe totalitarian political systems as controlling and identify WWI and WWII as major wars — without yet analyzing how specific political systems affected different categories of people differently, or why decolonization produced formal independence without economic independence.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.3 — Analyze the effects of different political systems on people (Era 4)

To meet this standard, you must analyze how a specific political system affected specific individuals or groups — showing that the same political system had very different effects depending on who you were within it.

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by analyzing how apartheid South Africa (1948–1994) functioned as a political system and what it meant for people at different positions within it. For white South Africans: legally protected economic and political privilege — first-class schools, housing, employment, and political rights. For Black South Africans: the Pass Laws (required to carry identification at all times), the Bantustans (forced relocation to "homelands"), prohibition from most skilled employment, separate and inferior schools, and no political representation. The student analyzes what the system was designed to achieve (maintaining white economic and political control), how it was maintained (law enforcement, imprisonment of ANC leaders, international silence), and what its effects were on individual daily life — not just its historical summary.

The Difference: A 2.0 student knows apartheid was a racist political system in South Africa. A 3.0 student analyzes how apartheid specifically shaped the daily experience of people at different positions within it.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate competing political systems and their promises vs. their realities — and connect Era 4 political developments to contemporary debates about political systems.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Decolonization's unfulfilled promises: Decolonization gave African and Asian nations formal political independence, but economic structures (debt, commodity dependence, neocolonialism) kept many nations economically subordinate to former colonial powers. Evaluate: was decolonization complete? What would genuine decolonization have required? 2. Democracy under pressure: The Weimar Republic fell legally — not through a coup but through elections and emergency powers. Analyze how democracies can be dismantled through legal means. What safeguards are necessary? Where do you see contemporary parallels? 3. Cold War "democracy": The United States consistently supported anti-communist dictatorships during the Cold War (e.g., Pinochet in Chile, the Shah in Iran, Marcos in the Philippines). Evaluate: what does this tell us about the actual meaning of the U.S. commitment to "democracy" in this era? How does this affect how we should evaluate Cold War history?

4.0 Example: A student argues that the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) was a genuine ideological achievement — but its enforcement mechanisms were too weak to prevent its signatories from violating it. The student analyzes the gap between the UDHR's stated principles and the actual behavior of its signatories (the U.S. enforcing Jim Crow while signing; the Soviet Union's Gulag; France fighting a colonial war in Algeria the same year it signed). The student concludes that the UDHR represents the articulation of a standard, not its achievement — and that the gap between the articulated standard and actual practice is itself a historical and political problem that continues today.

docs.text: Video: China's Revolutions — CrashCourse WH #39 · Episode in reading library Also: World War I — CrashCourse WH #38 · Episode in reading library Also: The Cold War — CrashCourse WH #42 · Episode in reading library Watch Prompt (general): For any political system described, ask: (1) Who held power? (2) How did they maintain it? (3) What were the effects on ordinary people at different positions within the system? (4) How did people resist? (5) What replaced it, and was the replacement better for most people?

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Night — Elie Wiesel — Read as a document of what a totalitarian political system (Nazi Germany) did to individual human beings. What specific aspects of the Nazi state's power made Auschwitz possible?
  • 1984 — George Orwell — Orwell's analysis of how a totalitarian political system maintains control. Identify the specific political mechanisms: surveillance, doublethink, perpetual war, rewriting history. Are any of these present in contemporary political systems?
  • MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — Read as a political document about operating within an unjust political system. What does MLK say about the responsibility of individuals living under unjust political systems? What obligations does he argue citizens have?

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose ONE Era 4 political system (totalitarian state, colonial rule, apartheid, or Cold War proxy state). Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) how the system was organized and maintained, (2) its effects on different groups within it (at minimum, analyze one privileged and one marginalized group), (3) how people resisted or challenged the system. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay arguing whether decolonization was a genuine political revolution or an incomplete one. Use specific evidence about what changed and what remained the same for people living in post-colonial nations. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute analysis: (1) How did a specific Era 4 political system affect daily life for people at different positions within it? (2) What were the mechanisms of control? (3) How does Elie Wiesel's Night or Orwell's 1984 illuminate how political systems shape individual experience? 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Political System Effects Grid" comparing two Era 4 political systems (your choice). For each system, analyze its effects on: a wealthy elite, an ordinary worker, a minority group, and a political dissenter. Annotate with specific historical examples. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute "Political Survivor" video. Play a character living under one Era 4 political system (a Jewish German under Nazism, a Black South African under apartheid, or a Soviet citizen under Stalin). Describe your daily experience, the system's mechanisms of control, and what resistance is possible. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project about Era 4. Write a 200-word reflection: Whose political experience did your work center? Whose was missing? What would a fuller analysis of how the political system affected different groups have added?

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. Decolonization: Independence Without Economic Freedom? — Research one post-colonial nation (Kenya, Algeria, or Democratic Republic of Congo). Analyze: what did independence in the 1950s–1960s actually change for ordinary people? What economic structures remained from the colonial era? Write a 1-page brief arguing whether political independence was sufficient.
  2. The UDHR: Ideal vs. Reality — Research the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948). Identify three specific articles and evaluate: how well were these rights protected by UN member nations in the decade after the UDHR was signed? Use specific examples. Write a 1-page argument: was the UDHR a genuine achievement or a hollow promise?
  3. How Democracies Die: Weimar Then and Now — Research the fall of the Weimar Republic (1930–1933). Identify the specific legal and political mechanisms Hitler used to dismantle democracy. Evaluate: Are any of these mechanisms present in contemporary democratic systems? What safeguards exist or don't exist?

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Describe three Era 4 political systems (totalitarian, colonial, or post-colonial democracy). For each, state: who holds power, how they maintain it, and what the effects are on ordinary people.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph analyzing how one Era 4 political system affected people differently based on their social position — at minimum, compare one privileged and one marginalized group.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word argument evaluating whether a specific Era 4 political change (decolonization, UDHR, end of apartheid) fulfilled its stated goals. Use evidence about what changed and what remained the same.

docs.title: Age of Global War: Social, Cultural, and Economic Life — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.4 — Analyze the influence of social, cultural, and economic development on individuals Era/Unit (if SS): Era 4 — Age of Global War and Globalization (~1900–present) ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.R.9 — Analyze informational texts; 9-10.W.3 — Explanatory/narrative writing NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Era 4 produced the modern world's social and economic structures — global capitalism, the welfare state, mass media, and globalization. Understanding how these systems shaped individual lives means understanding the specific mechanisms: how did the Great Depression change daily life? How did WWII change women's roles? How has globalization changed what ordinary workers can earn?

  • Great Depression (1929–1939): A global economic collapse triggered by the 1929 U.S. stock market crash. At its peak, ~25% of Americans were unemployed; similar rates in Germany, France, and the UK. The Depression transformed the social contract: it demonstrated that unregulated capitalism could destroy ordinary people's lives, leading to government intervention (New Deal, welfare states).
  • Welfare State: A political system in which the government provides basic economic protections for citizens: unemployment insurance, social security, public healthcare, public education. Developed in Western Europe and the U.S. in response to the Depression and WWII's social disruptions.
  • Women and WWII: WWII dramatically changed women's economic role. With men at war, women in Britain, the U.S., and the Soviet Union entered the industrial workforce in unprecedented numbers — "Rosie the Riveter" symbolized this shift. After the war, pressure to return to domestic roles created the post-war "feminine mystique" tension.
  • Decolonization and Economic Development: When colonies gained independence, they inherited economic structures designed to extract resources for colonial powers, not develop local economies. This "colonial legacy" — debt, single-commodity dependence, lack of industrial infrastructure — shaped the economic options available to individuals in post-colonial nations.
  • Globalization: The increasing integration of the world's economies, cultures, and populations through trade, communication, and migration. Globalization has created wealth globally but distributed it unequally: manufacturing jobs moved from wealthy to poor countries; workers in both benefited from cheap goods but many in wealthy countries lost manufacturing employment.
  • Mass Media and Consumer Culture: Radio, film, television, and the internet transformed how culture spread. Consumer culture — the social norm of defining identity and status through purchased goods — became the dominant cultural framework of the late 20th century in wealthy nations.
  • Migration and Diaspora: Wars, decolonization, economic inequality, and climate disruption have driven massive migration in Era 4. Today there are ~100 million displaced people globally (refugees + internally displaced). Migration creates diasporic communities that transform the cultures of both sending and receiving nations.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Explain how the Great Depression changed the relationship between individuals and the government. - [ ] Identify how WWII changed women's economic roles — and what happened after the war. - [ ] Explain what "globalization" means and how it affects wages in different countries. - [ ] Distinguish between the economic legacy of colonialism and formal political independence. - [ ] Identify at least one way mass media shaped social or cultural development in Era 4.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can state that the Great Depression caused high unemployment and that WWII changed women's roles — without yet analyzing the specific social mechanisms (how unemployment relief programs changed the social contract, or how the post-war pressure for women to return home created the conditions for the 1960s feminist movement).

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.4 — Analyze the influence of social, cultural, and economic development on individuals (Era 4)

To meet this standard, you must connect large-scale economic and social developments to how they shaped individual lives. In Era 4, the key theme is the gap between the wealth produced by industrial capitalism and globalization and how that wealth was distributed — and who was left out.

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by analyzing how globalization in the 1990s–2000s affected manufacturing workers in both the United States and China. In the U.S.: manufacturing jobs moved to countries with cheaper labor; American factory workers in the Midwest saw wages decline, job security disappear, and communities devastated as factories closed. In China: manufacturing employment grew rapidly; hundreds of millions of rural Chinese workers moved to cities and entered the cash economy; wages were low but higher than rural poverty. The student analyzes how the same economic development (globalization) had different effects on individuals depending on where they lived in the global economy — devastation for some, economic emergence for others — and argues that understanding this asymmetry is essential for understanding contemporary political movements in both countries.

The Difference: A 2.0 student knows globalization involved moving jobs to cheaper labor markets. A 3.0 student analyzes what this meant for specific individuals in different positions in the global economy.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate the systems that produced these social and economic outcomes — and assess competing explanations for why they happened and what should be done.

Transfer Tasks: 1. The social contract's limits: The welfare state was designed to protect individuals from economic catastrophe. Evaluate: has it succeeded? Use the 2008 financial crisis as a case study — what did the welfare state protect, what did it fail to protect, and who bore the costs of the bailouts? 2. Globalization and inequality: Globalization has reduced poverty globally (measured by absolute poverty rates) while increasing inequality within nations (the wealthy have gotten much richer; the middle class has stagnated). Evaluate: is globalization a net positive or net negative for human well-being? Does your answer change depending on whether you measure globally or within specific nations? 3. Migration and identity: Mass migration has transformed the cultural identities of receiving nations. Analyze: how does migration change culture — both the culture of migrants and the culture of receiving societies? Use at least one specific contemporary example.

4.0 Example: A student argues that the 2008 financial crisis revealed the limits of the 20th-century social contract: the welfare state protected individuals from unemployment and poverty but could not protect them from the systematic wealth extraction built into the financial system. The student analyzes how the bailouts (protecting banks while ordinary homeowners lost their homes) created the political conditions for both the Tea Party and Occupy Wall Street — populist movements of the right and left that both expressed the same underlying perception that the economic system was rigged. The student connects this to contemporary political polarization and argues that the 2008 crisis set the conditions for much of the political instability of the 2010s–2020s.

docs.text: Video: World War I — CrashCourse WH #38 · Episode in reading library Also: WWII Part 1 — CrashCourse WH #40 · Episode in reading library Also: WWII Part 2 — CrashCourse WH #41 · Episode in reading library Also: China's Revolutions — CrashCourse WH #39 · Episode in reading library Also: The Cold War — CrashCourse WH #42 · Episode in reading library Watch Prompt (general): For each social or economic development mentioned, track: (1) What problem was this development responding to? (2) Who benefited from it? (3) Who was left out or harmed? (4) What individual lives does this development explain or transform?

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Night — Elie Wiesel — Read Night as a document of how social and economic conditions shaped Wiesel's family's choices before the Holocaust. Why did his family stay in Sighet when they might have fled? What social networks, economic ties, and cultural identity held them in place? How did the economic conditions of Jews in Hungary shape their vulnerability?
  • MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — Read as a document about how economic and social systems shaped Black Americans' lives. MLK explicitly connects racial injustice to economic exploitation. Identify the specific economic and social mechanisms he describes.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose ONE Era 4 social or economic development (Great Depression, WWII women workers, globalization, or decolonization's economic legacy). Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) what the development was, (2) how it affected individuals differently based on their position, (3) what long-term social changes it produced. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay: "Globalization: Who Won, Who Lost?" Analyze how economic globalization in the 1990s–2010s affected workers in at least two different countries. Argue: was globalization a net positive for the people most affected by it — or only for those who owned the capital? 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute analysis: (1) How did the Great Depression change the relationship between individuals and the government? (2) How did WWII change women's social and economic roles — and what happened after the war? (3) How does globalization create both winners and losers, depending on where you sit in the global economy? 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Social Change Timeline" for one Era 4 social group (women, manufacturing workers, colonized peoples, or refugees). Show how their social and economic position changed across Era 4 — and what caused each change. Annotate with specific evidence. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute "Two Worlds" video. Profile two individuals whose lives were shaped by the same Era 4 economic development (one who benefited, one who was harmed). Show how the same system produced radically different outcomes. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project about Era 4. Write a 200-word reflection: Whose social or economic experience was centered in your work? Whose was missing? How would including a different perspective change your analysis?

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. "Rosie After the War" — Research what happened to women who worked in U.S. factories during WWII after the war ended. Analyze: what were they told to do, what did many of them want, and how did this tension produce the conditions for the 1960s feminist movement? Write a 1-page brief with a specific argument.
  2. Globalization: A Worker's Story from Two Continents — Research the specific wages, working conditions, and life changes for workers in one industry (e.g., textiles or electronics) in both a wealthy sending country (U.S. or UK) and a receiving country (Bangladesh or China) between 1990 and 2020. Write a 1-page comparative analysis.
  3. The Colonial Legacy: Why Some Nations Stay Poor — Research the economic situation of one post-colonial nation (your choice). Analyze: what economic structures did colonialism leave behind? What has the country been able to change, and what has it not been able to change? Write a 1-page brief arguing whether contemporary poverty in former colonial nations is primarily a colonial legacy or primarily a result of post-independence choices.

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Describe three Era 4 social or economic developments. For each, identify: what changed, who was primarily affected, and whether the change was positive, negative, or mixed for most people.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph analyzing how one Era 4 economic development affected people differently based on their position — at minimum, compare one group that benefited and one that bore the costs.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word argument evaluating whether one Era 4 economic system (globalization, welfare state, or post-colonial economy) fulfilled its stated purpose for most people. Use specific evidence.
