docs.title: Age of Global War: Causes and Effects of WWI, WWII, and Beyond — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.5 — Analyze causes and effects of global events using primary and/or secondary sources Era/Unit (if SS): Era 4 — Age of Global War and Globalization (~1900–present) ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.W.4 — Argument writing; 9-10.IR.1–5 — Research and source evaluation NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Era 4's wars and crises had causes that built up over decades — and effects that shaped the entire second half of the 20th century and continue today. Understanding Era 4 means tracing these chains, using primary sources to understand how events were perceived at the time vs. how historians now understand them.

  • Immediate vs. Underlying Causes (WWI): The immediate cause of WWI was the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand (June 28, 1914). The underlying causes: the alliance system (if one nation fought, all its allies had to), imperial competition, nationalism, military buildup ("the war everyone expected"). The assassination was the trigger; the underlying causes were the powder keg.
  • Versailles Treaty (1919): The peace settlement after WWI, which imposed massive reparations, territorial losses, and a "war guilt clause" on Germany. Historians debate whether Versailles caused WWII by creating the resentment that Hitler exploited — this is a primary cause-and-effect question.
  • The Holocaust as a Bureaucratic Process: The Holocaust was not just Hitler's decision — it was implemented through a bureaucratic chain of decisions, orders, and compliance. The Wannsee Conference (1942) documented senior Nazi officials' systematic planning of genocide. Understanding the Holocaust requires understanding how organizations implement decisions — not just who gave orders.
  • Nuclear Deterrence and the Cold War: After 1945, the logic of the Cold War was shaped by nuclear weapons: neither the U.S. nor USSR could directly attack each other without risking nuclear annihilation ("Mutually Assured Destruction"). This produced proxy wars in Korea, Vietnam, Angola, and elsewhere — the superpowers fought each other through other nations' soldiers.
  • Primary Source Evaluation in Era 4: Abundant primary sources exist for Era 4: speeches, government documents, newspapers, photographs, film footage, personal letters, survivor testimony. Evaluating them requires: Who produced this? What was their purpose? Who was the intended audience? What do they have to gain from their framing? What do they omit?
  • Propaganda as Primary Source: Era 4 governments produced propaganda extensively — wartime posters, radio broadcasts, film. Propaganda is a primary source for what governments wanted people to believe, not for what was actually true. It must be evaluated accordingly.
  • Postwar Decolonization Causes: WWII undermined European powers' claim to "civilizational superiority" (they had just committed the Holocaust and total war), weakened their economies and military reach, and empowered anti-colonial movements with new ideology (Enlightenment natural rights, UN human rights framework).

Basic Processes: - [ ] Distinguish immediate from underlying causes for WWI or WWII. - [ ] Explain what the Versailles Treaty did and how historians debate its role in causing WWII. - [ ] Explain "Mutually Assured Destruction" and how it shaped Cold War military strategy. - [ ] Evaluate a WWII-era propaganda poster as a primary source — what does it reveal and conceal? - [ ] Explain why WWII accelerated decolonization.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can state that WWI was caused by the assassination of Franz Ferdinand and that WWII was caused by Hitler — without yet analyzing the underlying causes that made a world war possible in both cases, or using primary sources to understand how people at the time understood these events.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.5 — Analyze causes and effects of global events using primary and/or secondary sources (Era 4)

To meet this standard, you must trace multi-step causal chains using primary and secondary sources as evidence. In Era 4, the central analytical challenge is that the same causes (nationalism, imperial competition, economic crisis) recur — and historians debate whether these patterns were inevitable or contingent.

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by analyzing the causes of WWII using primary source evidence. The student identifies: (1) Versailles Treaty resentment (evidence: Hitler's speeches citing the "stab in the back" myth and Versailles injustices — primary sources that show how the treaty was used politically, not just what it said); (2) global economic crisis of the 1930s Depression, which created unemployment and desperation that made extremist promises appealing (evidence: German unemployment statistics 1929–1933); (3) the failure of appeasement — British and French leaders' willingness to allow Hitler to annex Austria and the Sudetenland to avoid war (evidence: Chamberlain's "peace for our time" speech after Munich, 1938 — a primary source that shows appeasement's logic). The student uses sources to support each step in the causal chain, not just to narrate events.

The Difference: A 2.0 student knows Hitler and the Great Depression caused WWII. A 3.0 student analyzes multiple causes using primary sources as evidence and explains why each cause contributed.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must evaluate competing interpretations of causation and assess what the historical evidence actually supports.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Did Versailles cause WWII? This is one of history's most debated counterfactuals. Research the arguments on both sides (Keynes's critique of Versailles vs. revisionist arguments that Versailles was actually lenient by historical standards). Evaluate: does the evidence support the claim that Versailles caused WWII? 2. Bystander nations and the Holocaust: The Holocaust required bystanders — nations and individuals who knew it was happening and did not act. Research what information was available to Allied governments about the Holocaust by 1942 and what actions were taken or not taken. Evaluate: what does bystander behavior reveal about the causes of the Holocaust's scale? 3. Postwar world order: The UN, World Bank, IMF, and NATO were all created in the period 1944–1949 as responses to WWII and the Depression. Evaluate: Did they solve the problems they were created to solve? Use specific evidence about their effects on global stability, poverty, and war prevention.

4.0 Example: A student argues that the conventional causal narrative of WWI (alliance system + assassination) is incomplete without including the racial ideology of imperialism. European powers had spent decades using racial hierarchy to justify mass violence against colonized peoples — the same governments that were "shocked" by WWI's brutality had just conducted genocides in Namibia (Germany, 1904–1908) and Congo (Belgium, 1885–1908). The student argues that Europe's tolerance for industrial-scale violence against non-white peoples normalized the methods that were then turned inward during WWI. This argument uses primary sources from both colonial violence and WWI to support the causal connection.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text: Video: World War I — CrashCourse WH #38 · Episode in reading library Also: WWII Part 1 — CrashCourse WH #40 · Episode in reading library Also: WWII Part 2 — CrashCourse WH #41 · Episode in reading library Also: China's Revolutions — CrashCourse WH #39 · Episode in reading library Also: The Cold War — CrashCourse WH #42 · Episode in reading library Watch Prompt (general): For any Era 4 event described, classify its causes: immediate or underlying? Local or global? Economic, political, or ideological? Classify its effects: short-term or long-term? Expected or unintended? Track which primary sources the presenter uses — and which they don't.

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Night — Elie Wiesel — Use Night as a primary source for understanding the Holocaust's effects on individuals. What does Wiesel's memoir reveal about the causal chain — the sequence of decisions and dehumanizations that led from 1933 Nazi rise to 1944 Auschwitz? What does it leave unexplained that requires secondary sources to fill in?
  • MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — Use as a source for understanding the causes and effects of the civil rights movement in the U.S. context. What were the immediate vs. underlying causes of the movement MLK describes? What effects did he predict from nonviolent direct action?

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose ONE Era 4 major event (WWI, WWII, the Holocaust, the Cold War, or decolonization). Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) immediate and underlying causes, (2) effects across multiple nations, (3) use of at least one primary source as evidence. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay arguing which underlying cause of WWI or WWII was most significant. Support your argument with at least two primary or secondary sources. Address the strongest counterargument to your claim. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute analysis: (1) Name an Era 4 event and explain its immediate and underlying causes. (2) Evaluate a primary source related to the event: what does it reveal, and what might it conceal? (3) Trace one long-term effect that is still visible today. 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Causal Chain Web" for one Era 4 event. Show immediate and underlying causes, short- and long-term effects across multiple nations. Annotate at least two connections with specific evidence from a primary or secondary source. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute "History Detectives" video. Pick an Era 4 event and investigate the hidden or under-analyzed causes. Challenge the most common explanation and offer your evidence-based alternative. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project about Era 4. Write a 200-word reflection: What primary sources did you use? What were their limitations? What would a fuller causal analysis have required?

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. "Did Versailles Cause WWII?" — Research this question using at least two secondary sources with different interpretations. Evaluate the evidence and write a 1-page argument defending your position. What does each interpretation's evidence actually support?
  2. Holocaust: A Bureaucratic Analysis — Research the Wannsee Conference (1942) and the bureaucratic chain of decision-making that produced the "Final Solution." Write a 1-page analysis: what does the bureaucratic nature of the Holocaust reveal about how organizations implement decisions, and what does this tell us about the conditions necessary for genocide?
  3. Cold War Proxy Wars: A Cause-and-Effect Map — Research one Cold War proxy conflict (Korea, Vietnam, Angola, or Nicaragua). Create an annotated causal map showing: U.S. and Soviet interests, local political conditions, immediate triggers, and long-term effects on the nation where the war was fought. Include one primary source analysis.

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: Choose one Era 4 event. List: one immediate cause, two underlying causes, one short-term effect, one long-term effect. Identify whether each source you used was primary or secondary.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Write a paragraph tracing a multi-step causal chain for one Era 4 event. Use at least one primary or secondary source as evidence for at least one link in the chain.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word evaluation of a competing historical interpretation. What evidence supports it? What evidence challenges it? Which interpretation do you find more convincing and why?

docs.title: Age of Global War: Legacies to the Present — Skinny
docs.text: Standard: WH.6_12.6 — Explain how past events connect to the present Era/Unit (if SS): Era 4 — Age of Global War and Globalization (~1900–present) ELA Crosswalk: 9-10.W.4 — Argument writing; 9-10.IR.1–5 — Research and source evaluation NDSBL Target: 3.0 score Estimated Time: 60–90 minutes Subject: World History + ELA crosswalk

docs.title: What You Need to Know (Score 2.0)
docs.text:
Era 4 is not past — it is the immediate origin of the world we live in. The political borders, international institutions, economic systems, social movements, and unresolved conflicts of today were almost all shaped by events of the 20th century. Understanding this era means understanding the present.

  • UN and International Law Legacy: The United Nations (1945), Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948), and international humanitarian law (Geneva Conventions) were all created in response to WWII. They define the international legal framework within which nations operate today — imperfectly, but persistently.
  • Nuclear Legacy: The atomic bombs dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki created the world of nuclear deterrence that still exists. Today, nine nations possess nuclear weapons. The Non-Proliferation Treaty (1968) and ongoing arms control debates are direct descendants of the decisions made in 1945.
  • Cold War Borders and Conflicts: Many current conflicts trace directly to Cold War interventions: U.S. support for the mujahideen in Afghanistan (1980s) → Taliban → 9/11 → 20-year U.S. war in Afghanistan. The Cold War's proxy wars left behind armed factions, damaged institutions, and unresolved grievances that still produce violence.
  • Decolonization's Unfinished Business: The borders drawn by colonial powers — often arbitrary, cutting across ethnic and cultural groups — became the borders of independent nations. These borders are still a primary cause of conflict in Africa and the Middle East.
  • The Holocaust and Genocide Prevention: The Holocaust produced the 1948 Genocide Convention (the first international law making genocide a crime) and the concept of "never again." But genocide has occurred since 1948: Cambodia (1975–79), Bosnia (1995), Rwanda (1994), Darfur (2003–). The gap between "never again" and what actually happens is a live political and moral problem.
  • Civil Rights Movement Legacy: The U.S. civil rights movement (1954–1968) produced the Civil Rights Act (1964) and Voting Rights Act (1965). These legal changes were significant — but racial wealth gaps, mass incarceration, and ongoing debates about voting rights show that the movement's legal achievements have not produced full social and economic equality.
  • Climate Change Legacy: Greenhouse gas emissions began their rapid rise during Era 3's Industrial Revolution and accelerated throughout Era 4. The atmospheric CO₂ causing contemporary climate change was accumulated over 200 years of fossil fuel burning. Contemporary climate negotiations are, in part, an argument about who is responsible for this inherited problem.

Basic Processes: - [ ] Identify at least three contemporary realities with direct roots in Era 4 events. - [ ] Explain how Cold War proxy war decisions still shape contemporary conflicts. - [ ] Connect the Genocide Convention to specific post-Holocaust genocides — and evaluate why "never again" has not been enforced. - [ ] Identify the specific civil rights legislation of the 1960s and explain what it achieved and what it left undone. - [ ] Explain the climate change legacy of Era 4 industrialization.

A student who can score a 2.0: A student who can state that WWII created the United Nations and that the Holocaust produced "never again" — without yet analyzing why genocides have continued after 1948, or tracing the specific path from Cold War decisions to contemporary conflicts.

docs.title: The Standard (Score 3.0)
docs.text:
WH.6_12.6 — Explain how past events connect to the present (Era 4)

To meet this standard, you must trace the specific mechanism by which an Era 4 event shaped contemporary reality — not just assert that they are connected. You need to explain how the connection works: who made what decision, how it was transmitted, and what it means for the present.

3.0 Example: A student achieves a 3.0 by tracing the path from U.S. Cold War policy in the Middle East to 9/11 and the subsequent War on Terror. The specific chain: the U.S. backed the Shah of Iran (an authoritarian ruler who suppressed opposition) → the Iranian Revolution (1979) installed an anti-American Islamic government → the U.S. backed Iraq's Saddam Hussein against Iran in the 1980s → the U.S. deployed troops in Saudi Arabia during the Gulf War (1990–91) → Osama bin Laden cited U.S. troops on Muslim holy land as a primary grievance → 9/11 → the U.S. invaded Afghanistan (2001) and Iraq (2003) → 20 years of war → the Taliban returned to power in Afghanistan in 2021. The student traces each step, explains the causal mechanism, and argues that understanding 9/11 requires understanding the Cold War decisions made decades earlier.

The Difference: A 2.0 student knows 9/11 happened because of terrorist attacks. A 3.0 student traces the specific Cold War decisions that created the conditions for 9/11 — and what this tells us about how past decisions constrain present options.

docs.title: Going Further (Score 4.0)
docs.text:
To reach a 4.0, you must critically evaluate how Era 4 legacies are being addressed — or ignored — in contemporary policy and public discourse.

Transfer Tasks: 1. Genocide convention's limits: The Genocide Convention (1948) requires signatories to "prevent and punish" genocide. Evaluate: has it worked? Research the international response to Rwanda (1994) and Bosnia (1995). Why did nations that had pledged "never again" fail to intervene, or intervene too late? What does this reveal about the gap between international law and political will? 2. The nuclear question: Nine nations currently possess nuclear weapons; the Non-Proliferation Treaty is widely agreed to be failing. Evaluate: Is a world with nuclear weapons stable (MAD logic) or inherently unstable (accidents, terrorists, regional conflicts)? Use both historical precedents and current analysis. 3. Reparations and historical responsibility: Several nations have acknowledged historical wrongs from Era 4: Germany pays reparations to Holocaust survivors; Japan has (inadequately, many argue) addressed comfort women. The U.S. has not formally addressed slavery or Jim Crow. Evaluate: What principles should govern historical responsibility for past wrongs? Does time limit responsibility?

4.0 Example: A student argues that the current global refugee crisis (100+ million displaced people in 2026) is the most direct and least acknowledged legacy of Era 4 policy decisions. The student traces the specific origins of major contemporary refugee populations: Rohingya (Burma/Myanmar military violence rooted in colonial-era ethnic classification); Syrian refugees (Syrian civil war rooted in Cold War-era Ba'athist dictatorship backed by the USSR, and U.S. intervention in Iraq that destabilized the region); Afghan refugees (20 years of U.S. war). The student argues that wealthier nations' resistance to accepting refugees is a refusal to acknowledge their own causal responsibility for producing the conditions that created displacement.

docs.title: CrashCourse Connection
docs.text: Video: World War I — CrashCourse WH #38 · Episode in reading library Also: WWII Part 1 — CrashCourse WH #40 · Episode in reading library Also: WWII Part 2 — CrashCourse WH #41 · Episode in reading library Also: China's Revolutions — CrashCourse WH #39 · Episode in reading library Also: The Cold War — CrashCourse WH #42 · Episode in reading library Watch Prompt (general): For each Era 4 event, ask: Is this still happening? In what form? Who in 2026 is still living the consequences of this decision? Who in 2026 benefits from ignoring the connection?

docs.title: Reading Library Connection
docs.text:
• Night — Elie Wiesel — The Holocaust's legacy: Wiesel spent much of his life arguing that "never again" required active witness and intervention, not just rhetoric. Read his 1986 Nobel Prize speech (not in the library yet — coming soon) alongside Night to see how he connected personal experience to contemporary obligation.
  • 1984 — George Orwell — Orwell's warning about surveillance, doublethink, and historical revisionism. How close is 2026 to Orwell's 1984? Read the "memory hole" section: how does erasure of the past shape the present? Is this a metaphor or a description?
  • MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — MLK's letter connects the civil rights movement to a larger arc of history. Read his references to history: what legacies does he invoke? What does he argue is still unfinished? Is his argument still unfinished today?

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning — Choose One
docs.text:
How will you show you know this standard? Pick one mode.

# Mode What You Do for This Specific Standard 1 Written Assessment Choose ONE Era 4 legacy still present today (Cold War foreign policy consequences, nuclear weapons, Holocaust/genocide prevention, civil rights movement's unfinished work, or climate change). Write a 3-paragraph analysis: (1) the Era 4 origin, (2) the specific transmission path to today, (3) a critical evaluation of how this legacy is being addressed or ignored. 2 Extended Writing Write a 400–600 word essay: "The 20th Century Is Not Over." Identify three specific contemporary political realities that are direct consequences of Era 4 decisions. Argue which legacy has the most urgent unaddressed implications for today's world. 3 Verbal Conversation Record a 3-minute explanation: (1) Name an Era 4 event and trace its specific path to a contemporary reality. (2) Evaluate: is this legacy being honestly acknowledged and addressed? (3) What would it take to genuinely reckon with this inheritance? 4 Visual/Creative Create a "Living Legacy" visual connecting 4 Era 4 events to 4 contemporary realities. For each connection, annotate: the transmission mechanism, who is still affected, and whether the connection is widely acknowledged or under-recognized. 5 Multimedia/Performance Create a 2–3 minute "Historical Accountability" video. Identify an Era 4 decision whose consequences are still being lived by people today — and argue what accountability for that decision would require. Be specific about who made the decision, who bears the consequences, and what accountability would look like. 6 Portfolio Annotation Select a previous project about Era 4. Write a 200-word reflection: What legacy of the events you studied is still present today? Is it widely acknowledged? What would it mean for contemporary policy to fully reckon with this history?

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas (Your Studio Product)
docs.text:
These are NOT the demonstration of learning. These are PBL artifact ideas.

  1. "Never Again" Audit — Research the history of genocide since 1948. Create an annotated timeline showing each major genocide, the international response, and what "never again" actually meant in practice for each case. Write a 1-page argument: What would genuine enforcement of the Genocide Convention require, and why hasn't it happened?
  2. Cold War → Present Day: A Conflict Genealogy — Choose one current conflict (Yemen, Syria, or Democratic Republic of Congo). Trace its origins back to Cold War decisions. Create a documented causal map from Cold War to present. Write a 1-page conclusion: What would peace require, given this history?
  3. MLK's Unfinished Agenda: A 2026 Assessment — Research the specific goals MLK articulated in the Letter from Birmingham Jail and in his later writings (especially on economic justice). Create a report card: which goals have been achieved, which partially achieved, which have not been addressed? Write a 1-page argument about the most important unfinished item on MLK's agenda.

docs.title: Scale Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] 2.0 task: List three contemporary realities with direct Era 4 roots. For each, name the origin event, the modern form, and how they are connected.
  • [ ] 3.0 task: Choose one of the above and trace the specific transmission path — what decisions were made, who made them, and how they produced the contemporary reality.
  • [ ] 4.0 task: Write a 200-word argument evaluating whether one Era 4 legacy is being honestly acknowledged and addressed in contemporary public discourse. Use specific evidence from both the historical record and the current context.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Era 4 — Global War — Era 4 overview with all 6 standards
  • WH.6_12.6 Overview — Full standard with all era applications
  • Night — Elie Wiesel — Holocaust legacy
  • 1984 — George Orwell — Surveillance and historical memory
  • MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail — Civil rights movement legacy
  • W.4 Argument Writing — ELA crosswalk
  • IR.1–5 Research Skills — Research and source evaluation
  • Universal Declaration of Human Rights

How to use this skinny: 1. Start with the 2.0 vocabulary and processes. 2. Read the 3.0 example carefully. That's the target. 3. Choose your demonstration mode and begin gathering evidence. 4. Pull additional skinnies if you hit a gap. 5. Check your scale tasks as you complete them.

Skinny v1.0 — SDA Commons Wiki · June 2026 For the full anatomy of a skinny, see Skinny Anatomy

docs.title: ELA 9–10 Priority Standards for SDA
docs.text:
What this page is: Which ELA standards are active in each studio, how they rotate, and what "bundled" means for IR.1–IR.5.

For the complete scale breakdowns of each standard, see ELA 9-10 Scales.

docs.title: The Active Standard Slots
docs.text:
Each six-week studio has four to five standard slots. Here's how they work:

Slot Standard(s) How It Rotates Notes Social Studies 1 WH or USH standard + era/unit Set per studio — tied to the content era Always specified as code + era. Never just the code alone. Reading One of: R.2, R.4, R.7, R.8, or R.9 Rotates each studio Chosen based on the type of text the studio uses Writing W.3 OR W.4 Alternates every studio Even-numbered studios = W.3; Odd-numbered = W.4 (or advisor-specified) Research IR.1 + IR.2 + IR.3 + IR.4 + IR.5 Active every studio — always Bundled and assessed together Communication C.1 and/or C.6 Assessed at exhibition every cycle C.2 and C.7 are ongoing habits — not tied to one studio

Total standards per studio: 4–5 scored standards (history + reading + writing + IR bundle + communication).

docs.title: The Writing Standard Rotation
docs.text:
W.3 and W.4 alternate every studio. Here's why this matters:

  • W.3 (Explanatory) — You're explaining something complex. You have a thesis and you support it with evidence from multiple sources. The goal is that the reader learns and understands.
  • W.4 (Argumentative) — You're taking a position and defending it. You have a thesis AND you address the counterargument. The goal is to persuade a reader your position is correct.

These are different skills. Students who only ever write arguments don't develop the ability to explain complex ideas without taking sides. Students who only explain never develop the ability to argue a position under pressure. SDA alternates them so you build both.

See the writing standards deep dive: Writing Standards

docs.title: Studio Writing Standard Rotation (Pre-Packaged Catalog)
docs.text:
Studio Writing Standard Studio 1 — Power & Belief W.3 (Explanatory) Studio 2 — Builders & Breakers W.4 (Argument) Studio 3 — The Medieval Crisis W.4 (Argument) Studio 4 — Renaissance & Rebirth W.3 (Explanatory) Studio 5 — The Age of Revolution W.4 (Argument) Studio 6 — Propaganda & Truth W.4 (Argument) Studio 7 — War & Memory W.3 (Explanatory) Studio 8 — Globalization W.4 (Argument)

Note: For Phoenix-track students proposing their own studio, the writing standard is specified in the proposal and must match the type of writing the deliverable requires.

docs.title: The Reading Standard Rotation
docs.text:
The reading standard is selected based on what type of reading the studio involves. Here's the logic:

Reading Standard Best Matched To R.2 — Comprehension + inference Studios with complex primary sources requiring close reading (ancient texts, era documents) R.4 — Main idea / theme development Studios where a long text or sustained argument is the anchor (letters, essays, speeches) R.7 — Style, syntax, word choice Studios where how something was written matters as much as what (propaganda, literature, rhetoric) R.8 — Literary elements (fiction/memoir) Studios that include a literary text (novels, memoirs, narrative nonfiction) R.9 — Informational and argumentative analysis Studios built around nonfiction sources with bias, perspective, or rhetorical purpose

Studio reading standard pairing (example for WH Grade 9 — pre-packaged catalog):

Studio Reading Standard Why 1 — Power & Belief R.9 Analyzing religious and political texts for author purpose and bias 2 — Builders & Breakers R.2 Reading complex informational texts about early science and civilization 3 — The Medieval Crisis R.4 Tracking how arguments about order/obedience develop across a text 4 — Renaissance & Rebirth R.7 Renaissance texts — how stylistic choices changed with the period 5 — The Age of Revolution R.9 Revolutionary-era pamphlets and speeches — argument, rhetoric, bias 6 — Propaganda & Truth R.7 Propaganda is all about style and word choice 7 — War & Memory R.8 Literary texts (memoir, narrative nonfiction) alongside historical accounts 8 — Globalization R.9 Contemporary nonfiction, multiple accounts, differing perspectives

docs.title: The IR Bundle Explained
docs.text:
IR.1, IR.2, IR.3, IR.4, and IR.5 are always active, every studio. They are assessed as a bundle.

This means you don't get one studio to "do IR.4" and then move on. Research skills are cumulative — you use them every single cycle, and your evidence of growth in the bundle is tracked across the whole year.

docs.title: What Each IR Standard Is Doing in the Studio
docs.text: Standard When in the Studio It Shows Up IR.1 — Develop research questions Week 1 — your guiding question in your proposal IR.2 — Gather + interpret sources Weeks 2–3 — your source log and annotations IR.3 — Organize information Weeks 2–4 — how your notes and sources are structured IR.4 — Evaluate source credibility Weeks 2–3 — your source evaluations in your log IR.5 — Integrate sources (citations) Weeks 3–5 — in-text citations and bibliography in your final product

docs.title: How the Bundle Is Scored
docs.text:
The IR bundle is scored as one holistic proficiency score per studio. To earn a 3.0 on the bundle, you need to show evidence of all five standards working together: - A well-crafted research question that evolved from your Dig - Sources from multiple types (at least one primary, at least one secondary) - Organized notes/source log accessible for use - Written source evaluations noting bias, perspective, and purpose - Correct in-text citations and a complete bibliography

If you're missing one component significantly, you're likely at 2.0 or 2.5 on the bundle even if you've done the rest well.

Skinnies for the IR bundle: Research Questions Ir1 · Primary Vs Secondary Sources · Source Log Template · Source Credibility Ir4 · Mla Citation Ir5

docs.title: Communication Standards: Assessed at Exhibition
docs.text:
C.1 and C.6 are assessed at the Week 6 exhibition every cycle.

  • C.1 — Your presentation: formal or informal, multimedia choices, delivery
  • C.6 — Your discussion/debate engagement: listening, responding, asking questions, sharing research

C.2 (verbal/nonverbal) and C.7 (collaboration) are ongoing habits assessed through advisory and seminar participation — not tied to a single exhibition. These appear in your overall communication score across the semester.

docs.title: Standards Not in the SDA Active Rotation
docs.text:
These are valid ND standards but are not the primary focus of SDA 9–10 studios. They appear in the NDSBL framework but are either assessed through other means or come in at 11–12:

Standard Status in SDA 9-10.R.3a/b Summarizing — embedded in IR work, not separately assessed 9-10.R.6 Text structure — embedded in R.9 work 9-10.W.5 Creative/narrative writing — available as deliverable form but not primary standard 9-10.W.7 Vocabulary and figurative language — embedded in L.1 and writing work 9-10.C.5 Preparing for discussions — embedded in C.6 preparation 9-10.C.7 Collaboration — assessed as ongoing habit through advisory, primarily 11–12

docs.title: Reading Library — ELA Mentor Texts
docs.text: • ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk — Master table matching reading library texts to ELA standards by studio

docs.title: CrashCourse Episodes — ELA Focus
docs.text: • ELA-focused CrashCourse entries — coming soon

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Reading and writing are not school skills — they're the skills that determine whether a person can participate meaningfully in a democracy. The ability to read a contract, evaluate a news source, construct an argument with evidence, and communicate clearly in a formal register are not optional life skills. The ELA standards in this wiki are framed around what students actually need to do with texts — not to summarize them, but to analyze them, argue about them, and use them to build knowledge. In a world where information moves fast and the gap between people who can evaluate sources and people who can't is enormous, these standards are civic infrastructure. For the texts where these standards come alive most directly, see the Reading Library and the Studio Archive.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The ELA standards in SDA are not arbitrary checkboxes — they are a map of specific intellectual moves that matter in the world outside school. Reading a difficult text closely and identifying its argument (R.9) is the skill you need when you're trying to figure out whether a news source is reliable, whether a legal document means what someone claims it means, or whether a political speech is making an honest case. Writing a structured argument with evidence (W.4) is what every professional, every citizen, and every person who wants to change anything needs to be able to do. The research process (IR.1–5) is how you find out what's actually true in a world full of confident misinformation. These aren't English class skills; they're power skills. The studio model at SDA puts them in the context of content you're actually investigating — which is the point. See the ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk for how ELA and SS standards work together.

ELA Priority Standards page — SDA Commons Wiki See also: ELA 9-10 Scales · Writing Standards · Crosswalk Ela Ss

docs.title: ELA Grades 9–10 Proficiency Scales
docs.text:
Source: North Dakota Standards-Based Learning (NDSBL) Educator Scales · Updated 10.2023 Applies to: All ELA standards active in SDA Studios

This page contains the complete proficiency scales for every ELA 9–10 standard used in SDA. Each standard includes: - The exact standard language - What each score point (1.0–4.0) looks like in plain language - Specific "What 2.0 looks like" and "What 3.0 looks like" examples - Links to relevant skinnies

For the priority rotation (which standards are active in each studio), see Priority by Studio. For writing standards in depth, see Writing Standards.

docs.title: How to Read a Scale
docs.text:
Every standard uses the same structure:

  • 4.0 — Beyond the standard. You make connections and inferences that weren't directly taught.
  • 3.5 — You're going beyond 3.0 but didn't fully get there. Partial success at the 4.0 level.
  • 3.0 — Meeting the standard. No major errors or omissions. This is the target.
  • 2.5 — Solid on all the 2.0 stuff and partially showing 3.0.
  • 2.0 — You know the vocabulary and can do basic processes, but you're missing the complex ideas.
  • 1.5 — Partial 2.0 knowledge. Still working on the fundamentals.
  • 1.0 — With help, partial understanding of 2.0 content.

Your goal every studio: 3.0. That means no major errors or omissions on the standard as written. 4.0 is the extension — it's available to you if you go further.

docs.title: 9-10.C.1 — Presentational Communication
docs.text:
Standard: Construct and deliver formal and informal presentations, incorporating multimedia components when appropriate for the audience and purpose.

Plain-language version: You can build and deliver presentations — knowing the difference between formal and informal — and you make smart choices about when to use video, images, slides, or other media.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
4.0 — Beyond the Standard You construct and deliver presentations for maximum impact. You use multimedia strategically — not just to add visuals, but to make the whole message more unified and effective. A student at 4.0 would say: "I chose to use a map animation in my presentation because it showed the spread of Islam more clearly than any words could."

3.5 — Demonstrates 4.0 ideas with partial success.

3.0 — Meeting the Standard You construct and deliver formal and informal presentations and make appropriate choices about including multimedia.

What this looks like: A student presents their studio research at the Week 6 exhibition. They've prepared slides, know when to look at the audience vs. the screen, and their multimedia components (maps, photographs, clips) are genuinely connected to the content — not decorative.

2.5 — Strong on 2.0 content, starting to show 3.0.

2.0 — Foundation Vocabulary you know: formal, informal, audience, purpose. Processes you can do: clarify the difference between formal and informal presentations; identify the audience; identify the purpose; use multimedia components; construct a presentation with supports like an outline or graphic organizer.

What 2.0 looks like: A student can tell you what "formal presentation" means and can put together a slide deck. But when they present, they read off the screen without engaging the audience, or they add a video clip that doesn't connect to the content.

1.5 — Partial 2.0 knowledge. 1.0 — With help, partial 2.0 understanding.

Skinnies: Presentation Basics · Multimedia Choices

docs.title: 9-10.C.2 — Verbal and Nonverbal Communication
docs.text:
Standard: Implement proper verbal and nonverbal communication for tasks and situations.

Plain-language version: Your voice, body language, eye contact, and speed all communicate. You use these strategically, not accidentally.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
4.0 — Beyond the Standard You use verbal techniques — pauses, conversational tone, vocal inflection — deliberately and for effect. A 4.0 student doesn't just speak clearly; they make choices about how they speak to land a point.

3.0 — Meeting the Standard You implement proper verbal and nonverbal communication for the specific task and situation.

What this looks like: During a Socratic seminar, you make consistent eye contact with the person you're responding to (not just the teacher), your volume is appropriate to the room, you speak at a pace people can follow, and your gestures support rather than distract from what you're saying.

2.0 — Foundation Vocabulary: verbal communication, nonverbal communication. Processes: make eye contact with the audience; use body and gestures to communicate; speak at an appropriate volume; speak with intonation; enunciate words; speak at an understandable speed.

What 2.0 looks like: A student knows they should make eye contact and speaks loudly enough to be heard, but their presentation body language is stiff, or they unconsciously turn away from the audience when nervous.

Skinnies: Verbal Nonverbal Communication

docs.title: 9-10.C.6 — Collaboration: Discussions and Debates
docs.text:
Standard: Engage in respectful discussions or debates: listen to acknowledge varying perspectives and evaluate speaker's logic or argument; present or share synthesized research and information; ask and respond to questions to propel discussion.

Plain-language version: In a class discussion or debate, you're not just waiting to talk. You're actively listening, evaluating other people's reasoning, adding your research, and asking questions that push the conversation forward.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
4.0 — Beyond the Standard You synthesize the points of the whole discussion, make connections across what others have said, and defend your position in light of those connections. You also actively create space for others — you notice when someone hasn't spoken and prompt them in.

3.0 — Meeting the Standard You engage respectfully: (a) listen and acknowledge varying perspectives and evaluate the speaker's logic; (b) share synthesized research and information; (c) ask and respond to questions that propel discussion.

What this looks like: In a debate about whether revolutionary governments actually improved people's lives, you listen to the other side, identify one place their logic holds and one place it doesn't, share a primary source you found that adds evidence, and ask a follow-up question that the class hasn't addressed yet.

2.0 — Foundation Vocabulary: debate, perspective, logic, argument, synthesize. Processes: use nonverbal communication to show you're listening; respond to others' ideas respectfully; acknowledge validity in others' arguments; notice limitations in others' arguments; provide research and textual evidence to support ideas; ask questions about others' perspectives.

What 2.0 looks like: A student nods when others speak and can respond to a direct question, but rarely adds new evidence or asks questions that move the discussion in a new direction.

Skinnies: Socratic Seminar Prep · Debate Structure

docs.title: 9-10.C.7 — Collaboration: Task Climate
docs.text:
Standard: Collaborate on a specific task or purpose in a productive climate by following norms, processes, and roles.

Plain-language version: When you're working with others, you pull your weight. You know the norms, follow the process, and take on your role — and you help the group function even when it's bumpy.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
4.0 — Beyond the Standard You respectfully hold peers accountable. If someone's not following norms, you redirect them — not as a cop, but as a collaborator.

3.0 — Meeting the Standard You collaborate on a specific task in a productive climate by following norms, processes, and roles.

What this looks like: In a group project, you know your role (researcher, scribe, presenter), you stick to it, you meet your part of the agreed-upon deadline, and when someone goes off-task, you redirect the group without it becoming a conflict.

2.0 — Foundation Vocabulary: climate, norms, process, role. Processes: determine necessary norms and processes for the task; cooperate with peers; recognize when a norm, process, or role is not being followed.

Skinnies: Group Norms · Collaboration Roles

docs.title: 9-10.R.2 — Reading: Comprehension
docs.text:
Standard: Comprehend a variety of texts with multiple levels of complexity while developing inferences and providing relevant textual evidence and reasoning.

Plain-language version: You can read hard texts — not just decode the words, but make inferences (smart educated guesses backed by the text) and support those inferences with actual evidence and reasoning.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
4.0 — Beyond the Standard You describe connections across genres, content areas, and/or time periods. A 4.0 reader pulls a thread from an ancient Roman text and traces it forward into a contemporary political speech — and explains why that connection matters.

3.0 — Meeting the Standard You comprehend a variety of complex texts, develop inferences, and provide relevant textual evidence and reasoning to support those inferences.

What this looks like: You're reading a primary source — say, an excerpt from Hammurabi's Code. A 3.0 response doesn't just say "this is about laws." It says: "The repetition of 'if a man...' throughout the code suggests this was designed as a public-facing document — one where the logic of punishment had to be visible to the population." Then you point to specific lines that support that inference.

2.0 — Foundation Vocabulary: complexity, inference, relevant textual evidence, reasoning. Processes: determine what the text says explicitly; make inferences from the text; support inferences with specific examples from the text; present reasoning behind inferences.

What 2.0 looks like: A student can identify what happened in a text ("Hammurabi listed punishments for different crimes") and can make a simple inference ("This shows he wanted order"), but can't yet support that inference with specific textual evidence and a reasoned explanation.

Skinnies: Making Inferences · Textual Evidence

docs.title: 9-10.R.4 — Reading: Main Idea and Theme
docs.text:
Standard: Determine main idea(s), claim(s), or theme(s) as they develop over the course of the text and support with textual evidence.

Plain-language version: You can track the big idea of a text not just as a single statement, but as something that builds and evolves from the beginning to the end. You can show where in the text that development happens.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
4.0 — Beyond the Standard You explain how multiple main ideas interact with one another throughout a text, OR you trace how a theme connects and evolves across genres, content areas, or time periods. Example: tracking how the theme of "order through fear" appears in both Hammurabi's Code (Era 1) and the Nuremberg Laws (Era 4).

3.0 — Meeting the Standard You determine the main idea(s), claim(s), or theme(s) and track how they develop over the course of the text, supported with textual evidence.

What this looks like: Reading MLK's "Letter from Birmingham Jail." A 3.0 response identifies that the central claim is not just "unjust laws should be broken" but that this claim evolves over the letter — starting with legal/philosophical grounding, then moving to emotional appeal, then closing with a prophetic vision. Evidence: specific paragraphs where each shift happens.

2.0 — Foundation Vocabulary: main idea, claim, theme, textual evidence. Processes: identify the main idea, claim, or theme of a text; support the main idea, claim, or theme using specific examples throughout the text.

What 2.0 looks like: A student can state the main idea ("MLK argues that peaceful protest is necessary") and find one example from the text. But they don't yet show how that idea builds and changes across the full text.

Skinnies: Main Idea Vs Theme · Tracking Argument Development

docs.title: 9-10.R.7 — Text Analysis: Style and Language
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze the impact of specific style, syntax, and word choices on meaning, mood, and tone, including figurative and ambiguous language.

Plain-language version: You can look at how a writer says something — not just what they say — and explain how those choices create a specific feeling or meaning. You notice when language is deliberately slippery or multi-layered.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
4.0 — Beyond the Standard You determine the cumulative impact of an author's choices — not just analyzing individual moments, but explaining how the full pattern of choices creates meaning. You can also evaluate whether those choices are effective for the purpose.

3.0 — Meeting the Standard You analyze the impact of specific style, syntax, and word choices on meaning, mood, and tone — including figurative and ambiguous language.

What this looks like: Reading propaganda from the Age of Global War. A 3.0 analysis says: "The passive voice in 'enemies of the state were dealt with' removes the perpetrators from the sentence entirely, which serves the government's goal of making violence seem procedural rather than personal. The ambiguous word 'dealt with' functions as a euphemism — its vagueness invites compliance because readers don't have to confront what it means."

2.0 — Foundation Vocabulary: style, syntax, mood, tone, figurative language, ambiguous language. Processes: identify style, syntax, and word choice in a text; identify figurative language in context; identify ambiguous language in context; determine the implied meaning of figurative language; determine the implied meaning of ambiguous language; determine mood and tone in a work.

What 2.0 looks like: A student can label a metaphor ("'iron curtain' is a metaphor") and can identify the general tone of a passage ("angry" or "persuasive"). But they haven't yet connected specific word choices to the reason those choices create that tone.

Skinnies: Style Syntax Wordchoice · Figurative Ambiguous Language

docs.title: 9-10.R.8 — Text Analysis: Literary Elements
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze the development and interaction of literary elements and determine how they impact meaning, using strong textual evidence to support the analysis.

Sub-standards: - (a) How complex characters, themes, and literary elements (symbolism, mood, setting, etc.) develop over the course of a text, interact with each other, and advance the plot or develop the theme(s). - (b) How the character's or author's point of view is influenced by background, environment, or culture — and how that affects the telling of the story. - (c) Multiple interpretations of the same scene or work across time periods — evaluating the choices of each.

Plain-language version: You look at how the pieces of a literary text (character, setting, symbolism, mood) work together to create meaning. Not just "what happens" but "how does the way this is constructed shape what it means."

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
4.0 — Beyond the Standard You synthesize multiple literary elements to explain how they work together as a system affecting the whole work. OR you create an original work that incorporates the literary elements you've been studying, with analysis of your own choices.

3.0 — Meeting the Standard You analyze how literary elements develop and interact, and determine how they impact meaning — supported with strong textual evidence. You can handle all three sub-standards: element development, author POV, and multiple interpretations.

What this looks like: Analyzing Night by Elie Wiesel: "The setting shifts from the warmth of Sighet to the ash-filled air of Auschwitz — and Wiesel uses this shift to mirror Eliezer's loss of faith. The recurring symbolism of fire, which begins as Shabbat candles, ends as the crematoria. That reversal — fire as life becoming fire as death — is the emotional architecture of the whole memoir. Wiesel's Transylvanian Jewish background shapes what he chooses to say and what he cannot say: the silences in the text are as important as the words."

2.0 — Foundation Vocabulary: literary elements, textual evidence, theme, point of view, complex characters, interpretations. Processes: identify complex characters (static vs. dynamic); identify themes; identify literary elements (symbolism, mood, setting); identify character's or author's background, environment, and culture; compare and contrast multiple interpretations of the same scene or work.

What 2.0 looks like: A student can identify that Eliezer is a complex character (he changes) and can point to symbolism (fire). But they don't yet explain how those elements interact with each other to build meaning across the full text.

Skinnies: Literary Elements Interaction · Author Pov Culture · Multiple Interpretations

docs.title: 9-10.R.9 — Text Analysis: Informational and Argumentative Texts
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze the development and interaction of informational and argumentative elements over the course of a nonfiction text and how they impact purpose using textual evidence to support the analysis.

Sub-standards: - (a) Analyze the author's point of view or purpose and possible biases; compare texts from differing perspectives; analyze the author's style, content, characterization, and presentation choices. - (b) Analyze various accounts of a subject told in different media — determining which details are emphasized or omitted in each account. - (c) Delineate and evaluate how a text's argumentative reasoning, rhetorical techniques, and/or logical fallacies support or undermine the author's purpose.

Plain-language version: With nonfiction texts (speeches, articles, propaganda, historical documents), you analyze not just what the author argues but how and why — including what they choose not to say.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
4.0 — Beyond the Standard You justify how the author's purpose, context, and audience shapes their choices of rhetorical techniques, reasoning, and appeals. You evaluate multiple accounts of the same subject and determine which is most effective for a specific purpose or audience — and explain why.

3.0 — Meeting the Standard You analyze informational and argumentative elements across a nonfiction text. You can handle all three sub-standards: author POV and bias; multiple media accounts; argumentative reasoning and rhetorical techniques.

What this looks like (reading a WWI propaganda poster + a soldier's diary entry): "The propaganda poster omits any mention of death or injury — the visual only shows soldiers marching confidently toward a bright horizon. The diary entry emphasizes mud, cold, and waiting. The government poster was designed to recruit; the diary was private, with no audience to perform for. The omission in the poster is the argument — it makes joining seem simple by making dying invisible. This is a rhetorical choice, not an oversight."

2.0 — Foundation Vocabulary: point of view, bias, perspective, rhetorical techniques, logical fallacy. Processes: identify the author's point of view or purpose; identify how an author shows bias; determine the author's style and characterization of a topic; identify the author's presentation choices; compare and contrast different accounts of a subject; identify argumentative reasoning and possible logical fallacies; identify rhetorical techniques.

What 2.0 looks like: A student can identify that a speech has bias ("the speaker is pro-war, so they leave out the costs") but can't yet explain how specific rhetorical choices (word choice, structure, what's omitted) create that bias.

Skinnies: Reading For Bias · Rhetorical Techniques · Multiple Media Accounts

docs.title: WRITING STANDARDS
docs.text: For a full deep dive on the writing standards and how they work together, see Writing Standards.

docs.title: 9-10.W.1 — Writing Clarity, Format, and Style
docs.text:
Standard: Write clearly and coherently with appropriate content, format, and style to accomplish a specific purpose for a target audience.

Plain-language version: Your writing is clear. You chose the right format and style for your purpose and audience — and it actually works for those specific readers.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
4.0 — Beyond the Standard You adapt your writing for multiple target audiences, recognizing the most effective style and format for each. You write with sophistication — not just correctness, but voice that lands.

3.0 — Meeting the Standard Your writing is clear and coherent. Content, format, and style are appropriate for your specific purpose and target audience.

What this looks like: A student writing an informational essay for a community audience about the Industrial Revolution writes differently than they would for a history professor. At 3.0, they've actually made that audience-based adjustment — the vocabulary, the sentence length, the examples chosen all reflect knowledge of who's reading.

2.0 — Foundation Vocabulary: format, style, purpose. Processes: identify purpose and audience; describe the appropriate style and format for a given task; use language appropriate to audience and purpose; notice and correct when language is redundant or vague.

Skinnies: Audience Purpose Writing · Style Register

docs.title: 9-10.W.2 — Organizational Structure
docs.text:
Standard: Create a logical organizational structure with a relevant introduction, transitional words or phrases to connect major sections, paragraphs, and sentences, and an appropriate conclusion.

Plain-language version: Your writing has a clear shape. The intro sets up what's coming. Transitions connect your ideas so a reader can follow the logic. The conclusion wraps it cleanly.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
4.0 — Beyond the Standard Your transitions create a unified piece — not just connecting paragraphs but weaving the whole argument into a cohesive whole. The organization itself advances your meaning.

3.0 — Meeting the Standard Your writing has: (a) a relevant introduction, (b) transitional words/phrases connecting major sections, paragraphs, and sentences, and (c) an appropriate conclusion.

What this looks like: The intro doesn't just say "I'm going to talk about the Industrial Revolution." It establishes a question or problem, gives the reader context, and ends with a clear thesis that the body will prove. Transitions between paragraphs carry the logic forward — not just "Another point is..." but something that shows how each point follows from the last.

2.0 — Foundation Vocabulary: organizational structure, introduction, transitional words or phrases, conclusion. Processes: determine an appropriate introduction for the prompt or purpose; provide examples of effective transitional words and phrases; determine when and where transitions are necessary; organize information into separate paragraphs relevant to the overall topic; determine an appropriate conclusion.

Skinnies: Introductions · Transitions · Conclusions

docs.title: 9-10.W.3 — Explanatory/Informational Writing
docs.text:
Standard: Write to inform an audience and to explain complex information by creating a clear thesis and providing supporting claims, details, and evidence from a variety of relevant and reliable sources.

Plain-language version: Explanatory writing teaches. You take something complex, make a clear claim about it, and support that claim with evidence from multiple credible sources. The reader learns something real.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
4.0 — Beyond the Standard You synthesize multiple sources with analysis — not just stacking evidence, but weaving sources together so they speak to each other and build a more complex understanding of the thesis. You explain where sources agree, where they diverge, and what that means.

3.0 — Meeting the Standard You write to inform. You have a clear thesis. You provide supporting claims, details, and evidence from a variety of relevant and reliable sources.

What this looks like: A student writes about how belief systems shaped political power in early civilizations (Studio 1). A 3.0 response has a specific thesis ("Early Islamic governance used the concept of caliphate to fuse religious and political authority in ways that had no precedent in pre-Islamic Arabia"), then presents three paragraphs each with a claim and evidence from at least two different sources. The sources are not just Wikipedia — they're primary or credible secondary sources.

2.0 — Foundation Vocabulary: thesis, supporting claim, supporting detail, evidence, relevant source, reliable source. Processes: identify audience and purpose of informative writing; identify relevant and reliable sources; identify qualities of an effective thesis; write clear claims/topic sentences that support the thesis; support claims with relevant and reliable evidence.

What 2.0 looks like: A student writes a thesis ("Islam shaped politics") and finds some evidence. But the thesis is too broad, the claims don't have supporting evidence from multiple sources, and the connection between evidence and claims isn't explained.

Skinnies: Thesis Writing W3 · Explanatory Essay Structure · Sourcing For Explanatory

docs.title: 9-10.W.4 — Argumentative Writing
docs.text:
Standard: Write to persuade an audience by establishing relevant context, stating a clear position/thesis, incorporating valid and reliable evidence from a variety of sources to support specific claims and to refute counterclaims, and using logical reasoning to avoid fallacies.

Plain-language version: Argument writing takes a position and defends it. Unlike explanatory writing, you're not just teaching — you're trying to convince. That means you acknowledge the other side and argue against it, not just pile up evidence for your side.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
4.0 — Beyond the Standard You validate the opposing side with evidence and reasoning before refuting it — not dismissing counterclaims but engaging them seriously. You use rhetorical techniques and appeals to make your argument more persuasive. Includes logos (logic), ethos (credibility), pathos (emotion).

3.0 — Meeting the Standard You: (a) establish relevant context; (b) state a clear position/thesis; (c) incorporate valid and reliable evidence from multiple sources to support claims AND to refute counterclaims; (d) use logical reasoning to avoid fallacies.

What this looks like: A student argues that the Industrial Revolution ultimately harmed more workers than it helped. A 3.0 argument establishes the historical context (rise of factories, labor conditions, 1880s–1900s), states a clear thesis, uses specific evidence from primary sources (testimony of factory workers, labor statistics), addresses the counterclaim ("but industrialization raised living standards") with specific evidence and then refutes it, and avoids logical fallacies like "post hoc" or "false dilemma."

2.0 — Foundation Vocabulary: persuade, context, thesis, refute, claim, counterclaim, fallacy. Processes: identify relevant context surrounding an argument (time, place, audience); identify qualities of an effective thesis; provide rationale for the thesis; establish topic sentences for support; recognize valid and invalid evidence; acknowledge counterclaims in an argument.

What 2.0 looks like: A student takes a position and finds evidence for it. But they ignore the other side entirely, or acknowledge it only to dismiss it ("Some people think X, but they're wrong") without evidence.

Skinnies: Argument Thesis W4 · Counterclaims · Logical Fallacies

docs.title: 9-10.W.6 — Writing Process
docs.text:
Standard: Develop and strengthen writing through the writing process to produce a quality product for a specific purpose and audience.

Plain-language version: Good writing doesn't come out perfect the first time. You use the writing process (drafting, getting feedback, revising, editing) to make your writing better — and you can show what changed and why.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
4.0 — Beyond the Standard Your revision process shows intentional, purposeful decision-making. You can explain every significant change you made and the effect that change creates.

3.0 — Meeting the Standard You develop and strengthen writing through the full writing process, producing a quality product for your specific purpose and audience.

What this looks like: You have a draft with advisor and peer feedback. Your revision is not just fixing grammar — it's restructuring a paragraph because the evidence wasn't yet supporting the claim, or changing your opening because it didn't establish context clearly. You submit a revision note with at least three substantive changes explained.

2.0 — Foundation Processes: identify the stages of the writing process; revise for clarity and coherence; edit for grammar and usage; seek and incorporate feedback from others.

Skinnies: Writing Process · Revision Strategies

docs.title: 9-10.L.1 — Language Knowledge
docs.text:
Standard: Apply language knowledge for a specific task, purpose, intention, and audience, resolving issues of usage as needed.

Sub-standards include: syntax, phrases, colon and semicolon usage, parentheses, misplaced modifiers, pronoun reference, parallel structure.

Plain-language version: You apply grammar and usage conventions purposefully — not as arbitrary rules, but because specific choices (punctuation, syntax, word placement) affect meaning and how a reader receives your writing.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
4.0 — Beyond the Standard You make strategic language choices — using semicolons, parallel structure, or syntactic variation not just correctly, but for deliberate stylistic effect.

3.0 — Meeting the Standard You apply language knowledge for your specific task, purpose, intention, and audience — resolving issues of usage as they arise.

What this looks like: Your writing is grammatically clear, and when you're unsure about a construction (comma splice? pronoun reference?), you recognize the issue and resolve it. Parallel structure in your thesis makes the writing feel controlled.

2.0 — Foundation Vocabulary and processes: syntax, phrases, colon, semicolon, parentheses, misplaced modifier, pronoun, parallel structure. Can identify and correct these issues when pointed out.

Skinnies: Parallel Structure · Punctuation Usage · Pronoun Reference

docs.title: INQUIRY AND RESEARCH STANDARDS (IR Bundle)
docs.text: These five standards are bundled and active every single studio. They are always assessed together. See Priority by Studio for how the bundle is evaluated.

docs.title: 9-10.IR.1 — Developing Research Questions
docs.text:
Standard: Develop pertinent research questions and narrow or broaden the inquiry.

Plain-language version: You write a research question that is actually researchable — specific enough to investigate, broad enough to have real evidence. And you can adjust it when you discover the question needs to change.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
3.0 — Meeting the Standard You develop pertinent research questions and adjust the scope (narrowing or broadening) based on what the inquiry reveals.

What this looks like: You start Week 1 with a question: "How did the Industrial Revolution affect workers?" By Week 2, after initial research, you narrow it: "How did child labor practices in textile mills between 1870–1910 shape the progressive reform movement?" The narrowing reflects what you actually found in your sources.

2.0 — Foundation You can write a basic research question on a topic. You know the difference between a question that's too broad ("What is history?") and one that's specific ("What caused the crash of 1929?").

Skinnies: Research Questions Ir1 · Narrowing Broadening Inquiry

docs.title: 9-10.IR.2 — Gathering and Interpreting Sources
docs.text:
Standard: Gather and interpret relevant information from primary and secondary sources for a variety of purposes.

Plain-language version: You can find primary and secondary sources, understand the difference, and pull relevant information from them for a specific purpose — not just copy-paste, but interpret.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
3.0 — Meeting the Standard You gather and interpret relevant information from primary and secondary sources for a variety of purposes.

What this looks like: You're researching the causes of WWI. You find both a primary source (an excerpt from the July Crisis correspondence) and a secondary source (a historian's analysis of the alliance system). You interpret what each reveals: the primary source shows the emotional tone of decision-making; the secondary source provides structural analysis. You use both deliberately in your writing for different purposes.

2.0 — Foundation You know the difference between primary and secondary sources. You can find sources on a topic. You can pull information from a source and note where it came from.

Skinnies: Primary Vs Secondary Sources · Annotating Sources Ir2

docs.title: 9-10.IR.3 — Organizing Information
docs.text:
Standard: Organize relevant information from a variety of sources.

Plain-language version: You don't just have a pile of notes. Your research is organized so you can find and use it — grouped by idea, claim, or argument.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
3.0 — Meeting the Standard You organize relevant information from a variety of sources into a structure that serves your inquiry or writing purpose.

What this looks like: Your source log has columns: source, type, key information, where it connects to your argument. When you sit down to write, you can find your evidence for each claim quickly because it's organized, not buried in a notebook.

2.0 — Foundation You record information from sources. You have a system, even if basic — some students use sticky notes, some use a notebook, some use a digital doc. The key at 2.0 is that information isn't lost.

Skinnies: Source Organization Ir3 · Source Log Template

docs.title: 9-10.IR.4 — Evaluating Source Credibility
docs.text:
Standard: Evaluate the credibility of a source based on bias, perspective, and purpose.

Plain-language version: Not all sources are equal. You can look at a source and figure out who wrote it, why, when, and from what perspective — and you can explain how that affects how much to trust it.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
3.0 — Meeting the Standard You evaluate the credibility of sources based on bias, perspective, and purpose.

What this looks like: You find a 1942 U.S. government pamphlet justifying Japanese American internment. A 3.0 evaluation says: "This is a government document produced during wartime. Its purpose is to justify a policy that had already been decided — not to evaluate whether the policy was right. The bias is institutional and wartime-propagandistic. It is valuable as a primary source showing what the government wanted the public to believe, but it cannot be trusted as an objective account of whether internment was necessary."

2.0 — Foundation Vocabulary: bias, perspective, purpose, credibility. Processes: identify who created a source; identify when it was created; identify the purpose; note whether the source has an apparent perspective or bias.

Skinnies: Source Credibility Ir4 · Lateral Reading

docs.title: 9-10.IR.5 — Integrating Sources
docs.text:
Standard: Integrate information from sources using a standardized format.

Sub-standards: - (a) Reference sources for quotations, paraphrases, and/or summaries to avoid plagiarism and maintain academic integrity. - (b) Generate a citation/bibliography page using a standardized format.

Plain-language version: When you use someone else's words or ideas, you give them credit — in-text and in a works cited page. You know the difference between quoting, paraphrasing, and summarizing, and you use all three.

docs.title: The Scale
docs.text:
3.0 — Meeting the Standard You integrate information from sources using a standardized format (MLA or whatever Reese specifies for the studio). In-text citations are accurate. Your bibliography is complete.

What this looks like: Every piece of evidence in your essay has a citation. Your works cited page uses consistent MLA format. When you paraphrase (put someone's idea in your own words), you still cite the source. You don't commit plagiarism — not because you're afraid of getting caught, but because attribution is how intellectual honesty works.

2.0 — Foundation Vocabulary: quotation, paraphrase, summary, plagiarism, academic integrity, citation, bibliography. Processes: identify the difference between a quote, paraphrase, and summary; know what plagiarism is; attempt in-text citation.

Skinnies: Mla Citation Ir5 · Quoting Paraphrasing Summarizing

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Hammurabi
  • Letter from Birmingham Jail
  • Night

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: The scales in this document describe what real growth in reading and writing actually looks like — from recognizing what a text says (2.0) to analyzing how it works (3.0) to using it as a tool for original thinking (4.0). That progression mirrors how expertise develops in every field: you start by understanding what's in front of you, then you learn to interrogate it, then you learn to build on it. Students who reach 4.0 on R.9 — evaluating whether an author's reasoning actually supports their argument — have a skill that applies directly to political speeches, scientific papers, news articles, and legal documents. That's not an academic skill. It's the skill that makes the difference between a citizen and a passive audience member. For the texts where these standards are practiced at the deepest level, see the Studio Archive and the Reading Library.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The proficiency scales on this page describe a progression from "I can identify it" through "I can use it independently" to "I can transfer it to new situations I haven't seen before." That progression is not an academic abstraction — it's a description of the difference between having a skill and owning it. The ability to read an argument carefully enough to know whether it's making a valid claim (R.9 at 3.0–4.0) is not a school skill; it's the skill that determines whether you get manipulated by misinformation or whether you catch it. The ability to write a clear argument with evidence (W.4 at 3.0+) is the skill that gets you taken seriously in a job, in a legal dispute, in a civic argument. The scales exist so you can see exactly where you are in that progression — which means you can see what to do next. See Writing Standards for the deep dive on W standards, and Priority by Studio for which standards are active in each studio.

ELA 9–10 Scales page — SDA Commons Wiki Source: NDSBL Educator Scales Updated 10.2023 · Maintained by Hermes See also: Priority by Studio · Writing Standards · Crosswalk Ela Ss

docs.title: Writing Standards Deep Dive
docs.text:
Standards covered: W.1 · W.2 · W.3 · W.4 · W.6 · L.1

Writing is the spine of SDA. Every studio requires a writing standard. This page explains how the six writing-related standards work together, why W.3 and W.4 are different, and what the "writing chain" means.

For scale breakdowns (1.0–4.0 scores) of each standard, see ELA 9-10 Scales. For the per-studio rotation, see Priority by Studio.

docs.title: The Writing Chain
docs.text:
Writing Standards → Written Demonstration → Showcase

Every studio produces a written product of some kind. That product serves two purposes at once: 1. It is your demonstration of learning — evidence that you can do the writing standard 2. It is the substance of your deliverable — the thing that gets exhibited

The chain means these aren't separate. You don't "do the writing standard" in one document and then "do the deliverable" in another. They are the same act. Writing is how you demonstrate learning, and it is what gets put into the world.

docs.title: The Six Writing Standards and How They Fit Together
docs.text:
Think of these six standards as a system, not a checklist:

Standard What It Controls Analogy W.1 Clarity, format, and style for purpose + audience The what of your writing: is it the right kind of document for this situation? W.2 Organizational structure The shape of your writing: does it have a beginning, middle, and end that make sense? W.3 Explanatory/informational writing The informational mode: you teach the reader something with a clear thesis + evidence W.4 Argumentative/persuasive writing The argumentative mode: you convince the reader of a position + refute counterarguments W.6 The writing process The how you got there: drafting, feedback, revision, refinement L.1 Language and usage The mechanical layer: syntax, punctuation, parallel structure, pronoun reference

W.1 and W.2 are always operating beneath the surface. Even in a studio where W.4 is the active standard, your writing still needs to be clear (W.1) and organized (W.2). You're just not being scored on W.1 and W.2 separately — they're expected as baseline competencies.

W.6 and L.1 are also always present. The writing process happens every cycle. Language correctness is always a factor. But the primary scored writing standard per studio is W.3 or W.4.

docs.title: W.3 vs W.4: The Critical Distinction
docs.text: This is the most important distinction in SDA writing. Students who mix these up will struggle.

docs.title: W.3 — Explanatory Writing
docs.text:
What it is: Informational writing. You take something complex and explain it clearly. Your job is to help the reader understand — not to persuade them of anything.

Key moves in W.3: - A clear thesis that makes a specific claim about your topic (not just a topic announcement) - Supporting claims that each address one aspect of the thesis - Evidence from multiple relevant and reliable sources (not just one) - The evidence is explained — not just dropped into the paragraph - Sources are cited

What W.3 is NOT: W.3 does not have a counterclaim section. You're not arguing against anything. You're explaining.

A W.3 thesis sounds like: "The development of Islam in the 7th century CE fundamentally altered the political structure of the Arabian Peninsula by creating a legal and spiritual authority that overrode existing tribal systems."

That's a claim that can be explained and supported with evidence. It doesn't argue "and therefore Islam was good/bad." It explains how and what.

Example studio context: Studio 1 — Power & Belief uses W.3. Students explain how early religions justified political authority. The thesis makes a specific explanatory claim. The essay teaches the reader how that justification worked.

docs.title: W.4 — Argumentative Writing
docs.text:
What it is: Persuasive writing. You take a position and defend it. Your job is to convince the reader your argument is correct — which means you have to acknowledge the opposing argument and beat it.

Key moves in W.4 that W.3 does NOT have: - Establishing context — the situation, stakes, and background the reader needs before you argue - A clear position/thesis — not just a topic, but a defensible stance - Evidence to support your claims (same as W.3 — this part is similar) - Counterclaim — acknowledge what the other side would say, then refute it with evidence and logic - Logical reasoning — your argument must hold together without fallacies

What W.4 is NOT: W.4 is not "writing that sounds aggressive." Many students write emotional arguments with no evidence, or they state their position repeatedly without engaging the counterargument. That is not W.4 at 3.0.

A W.4 thesis sounds like: "The French Revolution, while ideologically rooted in Enlightenment principles of liberty, ultimately produced a political structure more repressive than the monarchy it replaced — a consequence of its internal contradictions, not external threats."

That's a position. Someone could disagree with it. You have to defend it against people who disagree. That defense, done with evidence and logic, is what W.4 is.

Example studio context: Studio 3 — The Medieval Crisis asks "Is order worth obedience?" That's a W.4 studio. Students take a position on whether feudal order justified the constraints it placed on people. They argue a position, acknowledge the counterargument (yes, order provided stability and protection), and refute it or qualify it with specific historical evidence.

docs.title: Why They Alternate
docs.text:
The alternation is intentional pedagogy. Over a full year in SDA, you'll write both types in equal measure. That matters because:

  1. Explanatory writing builds different muscles. You have to understand something deeply enough to explain it clearly. You can't hide behind rhetorical moves — the explanation either makes sense or it doesn't.
