  2. Argumentative writing builds different muscles. You have to be willing to take a position, know what the opposition says, and defeat it with evidence. Students who only write explanatory pieces tend to write arguments that never take a real stance.

  3. Real writing in the world is both. A historian writes explanatory prose. An op-ed writer argues. A policy brief does both. You need both modes.

docs.title: W.6 — The Writing Process
docs.text:
W.6 is the metacognitive standard. It's not about what you write — it's about how you got there.

The writing process in SDA:

Stage When What Happens Planning Week 1–2 Outline, thesis development, source identification Drafting Week 3 First draft — get it down, don't over-edit Feedback Week 4 Peer review + advisor conference. Document specific feedback received. Revision Week 4–5 Substantive changes based on feedback — not just grammar fixes Editing Week 5 Grammar, citation, formatting pass Final Week 5–6 Exhibition-ready product

The difference between revision and editing: Revision is big-picture — restructuring a paragraph, replacing a piece of evidence, rewriting a thesis. Editing is small-picture — fixing commas, correcting citations, cleaning prose. Most students skip revision and only edit. That's why their writing doesn't improve.

W.6 at 3.0 means you can show your process — a draft with changes, feedback documented, a revision note explaining what you changed and why. It's not just "I made it better." It's "I changed the second paragraph because the evidence I had (X) didn't actually support my claim (Y). I replaced it with (Z) because..."

docs.title: L.1 — Language Knowledge in Context
docs.text:
L.1 is the standard that handles grammar and usage — but not as a list of rules to memorize. L.1 is about applying language knowledge for your specific task, purpose, and audience.

The L.1 sub-skills that show up most in SDA writing:

Sub-skill What It Means in Practice Syntax and phrases Sentence variety — not all simple, not all compound. Using subordinate clauses to show relationships between ideas. Colon and semicolon Colons introduce what follows; semicolons connect two complete related thoughts. Both show sentence-level control. Misplaced modifiers "Walking down the hall, the fire alarm surprised me." (The alarm wasn't walking.) These matter — they create unintended meanings. Pronoun reference Every pronoun needs a clear antecedent. "They did it because of this" — who is "they"? What is "this"? Parallel structure "She studied, worked, and slept" — not "She studied, went to work, and sleeping." Parallel structure in thesis statements and topic sentences signals control.

L.1 at 3.0 means you apply these skills for your specific task. A formal argument essay should read differently than a podcast script. The vocabulary, sentence structure, and punctuation choices should reflect knowledge of audience and purpose.

docs.title: The Writing Standards in the Skinny Library
docs.text:
Each writing standard has dedicated skinnies you can pull when you hit a gap:

W.3 Skinnies: - Thesis Writing W3 — How to write a real thesis for explanatory writing - Explanatory Essay Structure — Body paragraph structure for W.3 - Sourcing For Explanatory — How to integrate multiple sources in an explanatory essay

W.4 Skinnies: - Argument Thesis W4 — How to write a position thesis (not just a topic statement) - Counterclaims — How to write a real counterclaim paragraph (not "some people think X, but they're wrong") - Logical Fallacies — Common logical fallacies and how to avoid them in your argument

W.6 Skinnies: - Writing Process — The full process, week by week - Revision Strategies — How to revise substantively (not just proofread)

W.1, W.2 Skinnies: - Audience Purpose Writing — How to adapt style and format to audience and purpose - Introductions — What a relevant introduction actually does - Transitions — Transitions that carry logical weight (not just "First, Second, Third") - Conclusions — How to close without just summarizing

L.1 Skinnies: - Parallel Structure — What parallel structure is and why it matters - Punctuation Usage — Colon, semicolon, parentheses in practice - Pronoun Reference — Fixing unclear pronoun reference

docs.title: Reading Library — Writing Mentor Texts
docs.text:
• Common Sense — Thomas Paine — Model for W.4 argument writing · Claim-evidence-reasoning structure · Primary Source
  • MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail (1963) — Model for W.4 argument + R.7 rhetorical choices · Primary Source
  • ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk — Full standards ↔ texts alignment table

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Writing is how people make their thinking visible and persuasive to others — it is not primarily a school task. W.4 (argument), W.3 (narrative), and W.6 (research) cover the three reasons anyone actually writes: to persuade, to make meaning of experience, and to synthesize information from multiple sources. The most powerful writers in history — Paine, MLK, Orwell, Douglass — were effective precisely because they mastered these modes. Every studio in the SDA model requires writing in one of these modes because demonstration of learning means being able to say what you understand and why it matters, to an audience that hasn't been inside your head all week. These standards describe not grammar rules but what effective writing does at different levels of sophistication — and that difference is real, measurable, and learnable. For examples of mentor texts that show these standards in action, see Common Sense as a W.4 Mentor Text and the Letter from Birmingham Jail W.4 Crosswalk.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Writing is how you make your thinking visible and how you make your thinking count. The writing standards in SDA — especially W.4 (argument with evidence) and W.3 (narrative) — are not separate from the historical and literary content; they're the mechanism for engaging with it. An argument essay about whether Hammurabi's legal code is just is simultaneously practicing W.4 AND building evidence for WH.6_12.2. A personal essay connecting your own experience to Beloved is simultaneously practicing W.3 AND engaging with the novel's central argument about memory and survival. The reason writing standards matter in the world outside school: every public argument you will encounter — in news, in politics, in professional life — is making a claim and using evidence to support it. The writing skills here are the skills for reading those arguments critically and for making your own. See ELA 9-10 Scales for proficiency levels and Priority by Studio for which standards are active when.

Writing Standards Deep Dive — SDA Commons Wiki See also: ELA 9-10 Scales · Priority by Studio · Crosswalk Ela Ss

docs.title: US History Standards Overview
docs.text: Course: English II + US History (Grade 10) Standards: US.6_12.1 through US.6_12.6

docs.title: The Key Fact: Six Standards, Nine Units
docs.text:
US History in SDA uses the same logic as World History: six standards that repeat across all nine units. The standard code stays the same — what changes is the historical content you're applying it to.

When a studio uses a US History standard, it must always specify both the code AND the unit:

US.6_12.3 + Unit 1 = Explain the cause-and-effect relationship of events during the Industrial Revolution and Progressive Era. US.6_12.3 + Unit 6 = Explain the cause-and-effect relationship of events during the Civil Rights Movement.

Same skill. Different content. Always specify both.

docs.title: The Six Standards
docs.text: Standard What It Measures US.6_12.1 Analyze primary and secondary sources with attention to reliability, impact, and purpose US.6_12.2 Examine the impact of multiple perspectives on social, political, and cultural development US.6_12.3 Explain the relationship of events focusing on the link(s) between cause and effect US.6_12.4 Compare how historical elements change over time US.6_12.5 Analyze the significant contributions of people, policy, and the influence on an era US.6_12.6 Connect the past to the present using current events

docs.title: FPS Social Studies Skill Strands (Cross-Cutting)
docs.text:
Same three skill strands as World History — they cut across all units and standards:

Skill Strand What It Means Argumentation Produce well-reasoned arguments with valid, relevant, and sufficient evidence Interpretation & Analysis Draw accurate, relevant, and sufficient conclusions Obtain, Evaluate & Communicate (OEC) Research, evaluate source credibility, and communicate valid/reliable findings

docs.title: Full Standards × Units Table
docs.text: Unit 1 Industrial Unit 2 Imperialism/WWI Unit 3 Roaring 20s Unit 4 WWII Unit 5 Cold War US.6_12.1 Analyze primary sources about Industrial Revolution causes and consequences Analyze sources on U.S. transformation into imperialist power Analyze sources on 1920s and 1930s political/social/economic changes Analyze sources on causes and consequences of U.S. involvement in WWII Analyze sources on U.S. reactions to communism and rapid technological change US.6_12.2 Examine multiple perspectives on Industrial Revolution's impact Examine multiple perspectives on imperialism and WWI Examine perspectives on 1920s cultural changes Examine multiple perspectives on U.S. WWII involvement Examine multiple perspectives on communism, Cold War, and Post-WWII era US.6_12.3 Cause and effect: Industrial Revolution on American society Cause and effect: U.S. entry into WWI and imperialist expansion Cause and effect: 1920s and 1930s economic events Cause and effect: U.S. involvement in WWII Cause and effect: U.S. reactions to communism US.6_12.4 Compare how progressive reforms changed over time Compare elements of change from 1890s–1930s Apply historical knowledge to current events Compare WWII-era changes Compare how Cold War policies changed over time US.6_12.5 Analyze contributions of people and policy in the Industrial/Progressive era Analyze key figures and policies (TR, Imperialism, Isolationism) Evaluate people and policy: Hoover, FDR, New Deal Analyze contributions of people and policy in WWII Analyze significant Cold War contributions; presidents' domestic/foreign policy US.6_12.6 Connect Industrial Revolution to present-day economic patterns Connect early 20th-century isolationism/imperialism to present Connect 1920s/Depression to present economic policy Connect WWII to present international order Connect Cold War to present U.S.-Russia and global politics Unit 6 Civil Rights Unit 7 Vietnam Unit 8 1970s–1980s Unit 9 1990s–2000s US.6_12.1 Analyze sources on civil rights post-WWII; compare movement effectiveness Analyze sources on domestic issues changing perceptions of government Analyze sources on post-Cold War economic/social issues Analyze sources on U.S. as global superpower; changing political/social climate US.6_12.2 Examine multiple perspectives on civil rights and equality movements Examine multiple perspectives on Vietnam, anti-war movement, and counterculture Examine perspectives on post-Cold War era Examine multiple perspectives on U.S. as global superpower US.6_12.3 Cause and effect: different groups' competition for equality Cause and effect: domestic issues changing perceptions of government Cause and effect: post-Cold War era economic/social issues Cause and effect: changing political/social climate and U.S. global role US.6_12.4 Compare how different equality movements changed over time Judge multiple perspectives on social/political/cultural developments 1941–2001 Compare: 1970s–1980s as period of change Compare historical changes over time in modern era US.6_12.5 Analyze key people and policy in civil rights era Analyze contributions of people, policy, and influence on Vietnam era Analyze key people and policy of 1970s–1980s Analyze key figures and turning points; people who changed history US.6_12.6 Connect civil rights to present movements Connect 1960s–Vietnam to present government distrust Connect 1970s–1980s to current political/social debates Connect 1990s–2000s to present

docs.title: The Nine Units at a Glance
docs.text: Unit Content Focus Approximate Dates Unit 1 Industrial Revolution and the Progressive Era 1870s–1920 Unit 2 Imperialism and World War I 1890s–1920 Unit 3 Roaring 20s and the Great Depression 1920–1941 Unit 4 World War II 1941–1945 Unit 5 Cold War 1945–1991 Unit 6 Civil Rights and Social Movements 1945–1970s Unit 7 1960s, Vietnam War, and Counterculture 1960–1975 Unit 8 1970s and 1980s 1970–1991 Unit 9 1990s and Early 2000s 1991–2010

docs.title: Unit Pages
docs.text:
Each unit has its own page with all six US standards applied to that unit's content, learning targets, FPS skill strand connections, and inquiry angles:

  • Unit 1 — Industrial Revolution — Unit 1: Industrial Revolution and Progressive Era
  • Unit 2 — Imperialism & WWI — Unit 2: Imperialism and WWI
  • Unit 3 — Roaring 20s — Unit 3: Roaring 20s and Great Depression
  • Unit 4 — WWII — Unit 4: WWII
  • Unit 5 — Cold War — Unit 5: Cold War
  • Unit 6 — Civil Rights — Unit 6: Civil Rights and Social Movements
  • Unit 7 — Vietnam — Unit 7: 1960s, Vietnam, Counterculture
  • Unit 8 — 1970s & 1980s — Unit 8: 1970s and 1980s
  • Unit 9 — 1990s–2000s — Unit 9: 1990s and Early 2000s

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: US History at SDA is built around one argument: the story of America is not a story of continuous progress toward ideals, but a story of real people making difficult decisions in difficult circumstances — some of which made things better, many of which made things worse, and all of which have consequences you can still see. The US standards (US.6_12.1 through US.6_12.5) ask you to understand those decisions in their context AND to evaluate them against stated American values. That combination — historical empathy plus critical evaluation — is exactly the kind of thinking democratic citizenship requires. The debates in Unit 1 about who benefits from economic growth, the debates in Unit 6 about what "freedom" actually means for people who were enslaved, the debates in Unit 9 about what responsibility comes with being the world's most powerful nation: these are not settled. They're the debates happening in this country right now. See the Grade 10 Crosswalk to see how all the units connect.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
US History is not background to the present — it is the present. The laws, institutions, arguments, and wounds that define American life right now were all created, contested, or reshaped in the periods Unit 1 through Unit 9 cover. Students who understand the Progressive Era can read current debates about monopoly, labor rights, and corporate regulation differently. Students who understand the Civil Rights Movement have the analytical vocabulary for current police reform debates, voting rights cases, and affirmative action arguments. Students who understand Watergate can evaluate current debates about executive power and congressional oversight with a reference point. The USH standards are a toolkit for civic literacy — not because you need to know the past to pass a test, but because you cannot understand what is actually happening around you without it. For the texts that make these connections vivid, see the Reading Library and the Studio Archive.

US History Standards Overview — SDA Commons Wiki See also: WH Standards Overview · ELA 9-10 Scales

docs.title: Unit 1: Industrial Revolution and the Progressive Era
docs.text: Course: English II + US History (Grade 10) Approximate period: 1870s–1920 FPS Learning Goal: Explain the causes and consequences of the Industrial Revolution on American society.

docs.title: What This Unit Is About
docs.text:
In the decades after the Civil War, the United States transformed from a mostly agricultural nation into the world's leading industrial economy. That transformation created enormous wealth — and enormous inequality. The progressive movement was the political response to that inequality: the first major organized effort in American history to use government power to regulate capitalism.

This unit covers: - The rise of industrial capitalism: railroads, steel, oil, and the men who controlled them - Immigration and the ethnic transformation of American cities (Ellis Island, Angel Island, nativism) - The Homestead Act and its effects on Native American displacement - Labor conditions: the Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire, child labor, the 14-hour workday - The Progressive movement: muckrakers, settlement houses, political reform - Key figures: Carnegie, Rockefeller, J.P. Morgan, Teddy Roosevelt, Jane Addams, Upton Sinclair - Gilded Age political cartoons as a primary source form

docs.title: All Six Standards Applied to Unit 1
US.6_12.1 + Unit 1 — Primary and Secondary Sources
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze primary and secondary sources with attention to reliability, impact, and purpose.

In Unit 1: Analyze primary sources about the Industrial Revolution — factory testimony, political cartoons, newspaper reporting, government documents — with attention to who created them and why.

Key sources: - Gilded Age political cartoons (Thomas Nast on robber barons, Tammany Hall corruption) - Congressional testimony from factory workers and child laborers - Upton Sinclair's The Jungle (1906) — muckraker journalism as a primary source type - Teddy Roosevelt's speeches on trust-busting - Company reports vs. worker testimony on the same factory

FPS Skill Strand: OEC — Explain the causes and consequences of the Industrial Revolution on American society.

Learning targets: - I can analyze a Gilded Age political cartoon for its purpose, bias, and argument. - I can compare a factory owner's account of working conditions to a worker's account and explain what each source emphasizes or omits. - I can evaluate the reliability of a muckraker article as a primary source.

Inquiry angles: - "How do political cartoons make an argument — and are they reliable historical sources?" - "How did Upton Sinclair's The Jungle change U.S. food safety law? What does that tell us about the relationship between journalism and policy?"

docs.title: US.6_12.2 + Unit 1 — Multiple Perspectives
docs.text:
Standard: Examine the impact of multiple perspectives on social, political, and cultural development.

In Unit 1: Examine how different groups experienced the Industrial Revolution — factory owners vs. workers, immigrants vs. native-born Americans, progressives vs. laissez-faire conservatives.

Key perspectives: - Industrialists' view: free enterprise, innovation, job creation; Carnegie's "Gospel of Wealth" — the wealthy have a duty to give back - Workers' view: exploitation, dangerous conditions, no safety net; labor organizing as self-defense - Immigrant experience: the promise of America vs. the reality of tenement housing and industrial labor; nativism and anti-immigrant violence - Native American perspective: the Homestead Act opened land taken through displacement; the Dawes Act (1887) attacking tribal identity and land ownership - Progressive reformers: government has a responsibility to regulate capitalism for public good; Jane Addams, Ida Tarbell, TR

Learning targets: - I can explain how the Industrial Revolution looked different depending on a person's class, ethnicity, and gender. - I can analyze how nativism shaped immigration policy and immigrant experience.

docs.title: US.6_12.3 + Unit 1 — Cause and Effect
docs.text:
Standard: Explain the relationship of events focusing on the link(s) between cause and effect.

In Unit 1 — the core FPS learning target: Explain the causes and consequences of the Industrial Revolution on American society.

Key cause-and-effect chains: - Transcontinental Railroad (1869) → national market economy: the railroad connected previously isolated regional economies; created demand for steel, coal, and lumber; enabled mass distribution of manufactured goods - Industrialization → immigration: labor demand attracted 20+ million immigrants between 1880–1920; most settled in industrial cities - Industrial labor conditions → Progressive movement: the Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire (1911, 146 dead, factory doors locked) → labor safety laws; Sinclair's The Jungle → FDA (1906); child labor photos → child labor laws - Monopolies and trusts → antitrust law: Standard Oil, Carnegie Steel, railroad trusts → Sherman Antitrust Act (1890); TR's trust-busting - Homestead Act (1862) → Native displacement: 160-acre land grants encouraged westward settlement on land taken from Native nations; Wounded Knee (1890) as the end of armed Native resistance

Learning targets: - I can trace a cause-and-effect chain from an Industrial Revolution development to a political or social consequence. - I can explain how the Triangle Factory Fire changed labor law. - I can explain how the Homestead Act's "opportunity" was built on Native American dispossession.

docs.title: US.6_12.4 + Unit 1 — Change Over Time
docs.text:
Standard: Compare how historical elements change over time.

In Unit 1: Apply historical knowledge as a tool for examining current events — how do patterns from the Industrial Era (economic inequality, immigration debates, labor conditions) compare to the present?

Key comparisons: - Gilded Age wealth concentration → 21st-century wealth inequality (top 1% in the 1880s vs. today) - Anti-immigrant nativism of the 1890s → current immigration debates - Child labor in 1900 American factories → child labor in supply chains of companies selling to American consumers today - Early labor organizing (AFL, Knights of Labor) → current labor movements (Amazon unionization) - 1890s Populist political movement → contemporary populism (left and right)

Learning targets: - I can identify a specific pattern from Unit 1 and explain whether and how it is visible in the present. - I can compare the Progressive Era's response to inequality to contemporary responses.

docs.title: US.6_12.5 + Unit 1 — Significant Contributions of People and Policy
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze the significant contributions of people, policy, and the influence on an era.

Key people and policies: | Person/Policy | Contribution | |--------------|-------------| | Andrew Carnegie | Dominated steel production; "Gospel of Wealth" — philanthropy as justification for monopoly | | John D. Rockefeller | Standard Oil trust — controlled 90% of U.S. oil refining; model for monopoly capitalism | | Teddy Roosevelt | Trust-busting; Pure Food and Drug Act; conservation; presidency as bully pulpit for reform | | Jane Addams | Hull House settlement; social work profession; women's suffrage; Nobel Peace Prize | | Ida Tarbell | Investigative journalism exposing Standard Oil; model for muckraker journalism | | Upton Sinclair | The Jungle — exposing meatpacking industry; directly caused the FDA | | Sherman Antitrust Act (1890) | First federal attempt to break up monopolies | | James J. Hill | Great Northern Railroad — railroad baron; complicated figure in Upper Midwest history (Fargo-connected) |

FPS Skill Strand: Argumentation — Formulate an argument identifying a key figure or policy that had the most significant impact.

Learning target: I can argue which person or policy in the Industrial/Progressive era had the greatest impact — positive or negative — using specific evidence.

docs.title: US.6_12.6 + Unit 1 — Past to Present
docs.text:
Standard: Connect the past to the present using current events.

In Unit 1: Design, develop, and create a primary or secondary source rooted in events from Unit 1 — OR connect Industrial Revolution patterns to present-day America.

Key connections: - Standard Oil → contemporary antitrust debates (Facebook, Google, Amazon) - Child labor laws → supply chain labor standards debates - Gilded Age immigration → DACA, immigration reform - Progressive Era government regulation → ongoing debates about the role of government in regulating business - Teddy Roosevelt's conservation legacy → contemporary environmental policy

Learning targets: - I can connect a specific Unit 1 event or policy to a present-day equivalent or debate. - I can design a primary source document (political cartoon, letter, editorial) rooted in Industrial Era events that connects to a current issue.

docs.title: Key Vocabulary for Unit 1
docs.text: Term Definition Industrial Revolution The shift from agricultural/artisan production to factory manufacturing, beginning in Britain and accelerating in the U.S. after the Civil War monopoly Control of a market or industry by a single company or group trust A combination of businesses that reduces competition (Standard Oil Trust) robber baron A pejorative term for 19th-century industrial tycoons who used aggressive and sometimes illegal tactics to build their fortunes muckraker An investigative journalist who exposed corruption, dangerous conditions, or corporate abuses during the Progressive Era Progressive Era The period of American reform (~1890–1920) characterized by efforts to regulate corporate power, improve labor conditions, and expand democracy nativism A preference for native-born citizens over immigrants, often expressed as political opposition to immigration Homestead Act The 1862 law that gave 160-acre land grants to settlers — distributed land taken from Native nations settlement house A community center in poor urban neighborhoods (Jane Addams's Hull House) offering services to immigrants and workers antitrust Laws or policies intended to prevent monopolies and promote market competition

docs.title: Skinnies for Unit 1
docs.text: Standard Skinny US.1 Science & Tech Unit 1 Skinny US.2 Ideas & Beliefs Unit 1 Skinny US.3 Political Systems Unit 1 Skinny US.4 Social & Economic Unit 1 Skinny US.5 Causes & Effects Unit 1 Skinny US.6 Past to Present Unit 1 Skinny

docs.title: Reading Library — Unit 1 Texts
docs.text:
• The Jungle — Upton Sinclair — Muckraking fiction exposing meatpacking industry conditions · US.6_12.4 + W.3 · Literary Text
  • ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk — ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk master table

docs.text:

  • CrashCourse US History Index — available USH episodes

docs.title: Related Standards
docs.text: ELA crosswalk standards most used in Unit 1: - W.3 — Explanatory writing (explain causes/effects of industrialization; explain how Progressive Era reforms changed working conditions) - R.9 — Informational/argumentative analysis (muckraker journalism, labor union documents, Progressive reform pamphlets) - IR.1–5 — Research bundle (active every studio)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Gilded Age described in Unit 1 — massive wealth concentration, industrial monopolies, dangerous working conditions, child labor — is not ancient history. The debates about antitrust law, labor organizing rights, and the regulatory state that emerged from the Progressive Era are being argued again today: tech monopolies, gig economy workers without benefits, minimum wage fights, and union organizing at Amazon and Starbucks. The Jungle's exposé of meatpacking conditions directly produced the FDA — and debates about food safety regulation, OSHA enforcement, and corporate liability continue. The income inequality ratio between the wealthiest and everyone else in the 2020s is comparable to Gilded Age levels, which is why the history of how the Progressive Era responded — and what it didn't fix — matters right now. See USH-01: When the System Fails for the studio that works most directly with this unit.

Unit 1: Industrial Revolution — SDA Commons Wiki See also: USH Standards Overview · Unit 2 — Imperialism & WWI · Primary Sources

docs.title: Unit 2: Imperialism and World War I
docs.text: Course: English II + US History (Grade 10) Approximate period: 1890s–1920 FPS Learning Goals: Explain the impact of the United States' transformation into an imperialist power. Explain the causes and effects of the United States' entrance into World War I.

docs.title: What This Unit Is About
docs.text:
At the turn of the 20th century, the United States made a deliberate decision to join the ranks of global imperial powers. The Spanish-American War (1898), the annexation of Hawaii, and the acquisition of the Philippines and Puerto Rico marked America's transformation from a continental to a global power.

Then, in 1917, America entered the deadliest war in history to that point — a European conflict it had spent three years trying to avoid. The decision to enter WWI, the way the war was sold to the American public through propaganda, and the failure of the Versailles peace treaty to produce a lasting peace all shaped the next two decades of world history.

This unit covers: - The logic and ideology of American imperialism - Spanish-American War (1898): Cuba, Philippines, Puerto Rico - Hawaii annexation and the overthrow of Queen Liliuokalani - WWI causes: alliance systems, nationalism, militarism, imperialism (MAIN) - U.S. entry into WWI: Zimmermann Telegram, unrestricted submarine warfare, Lusitania - WWI homefront: propaganda, espionage and sedition acts, war bonds - Woodrow Wilson's 14 Points and the League of Nations - The Treaty of Versailles and why it failed to prevent WWII

docs.title: All Six Standards Applied to Unit 2
US.6_12.1 + Unit 2 — Primary and Secondary Sources
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze primary and secondary sources with attention to reliability, impact, and purpose.

In Unit 2: Analyze sources on U.S. imperialism and WWI with attention to who created them, what they were trying to achieve, and what they left out.

Key sources: - Henry Cabot Lodge's 1915 speech on U.S. imperialism (pro-expansion) - William Jennings Bryan's anti-imperialist arguments - WWI propaganda posters (Uncle Sam "I Want You"; anti-German imagery) - The Zimmermann Telegram text - Wilson's 14 Points speech - The Treaty of Versailles (relevant sections) - Accounts from soldiers in the trenches vs. political leaders' justifications

FPS Skill Strand: OEC — Explain the impact of the United States' transformation into an imperialist power.

Learning targets: - I can analyze a WWI propaganda poster for its purpose, argument, and the emotions it is designed to produce. - I can compare Lodge's pro-imperialist argument to Bryan's anti-imperialist argument and identify the evidence and reasoning each uses. - I can evaluate the Treaty of Versailles as a historical document — what it actually says, who it blamed, and why it sowed the seeds of WWII.

docs.title: US.6_12.2 + Unit 2 — Multiple Perspectives
docs.text:
Standard: Examine the impact of multiple perspectives on social, political, and cultural development.

In Unit 2: Examine how different groups viewed American imperialism and WWI entry — supporters vs. opponents, colonized peoples vs. American policymakers, soldiers vs. politicians.

Key perspectives: - American expansionists (Lodge, TR, Alfred Thayer Mahan): the U.S. must build a global navy, acquire overseas bases, and compete with European powers - Anti-imperialists (Mark Twain, Andrew Carnegie, Bryan): imperialism contradicts American democratic values; you can't govern people without their consent - Filipino perspective: the Philippine-American War (1899–1902) was a brutal counter-insurgency; 200,000–600,000 Filipino civilians died; this history is largely absent from American textbooks - Puerto Rican and Hawaiian perspectives: annexation without a referendum; still U.S. territories without full statehood in 2026 - WWI soldiers: the gap between the rhetoric of the war ("the war to end all wars," "making the world safe for democracy") and the reality of trench warfare - African American soldiers: fought for a country practicing Jim Crow; the "Double V" idea (victory abroad and at home) beginning in WWI

Learning targets: - I can explain the impact of multiple perspectives on U.S. entry into WWI and on American imperialism. - I can compare an American government perspective on colonialism with the perspective of the colonized.

docs.title: US.6_12.3 + Unit 2 — Cause and Effect
docs.text:
Standard: Explain the relationship of events focusing on the link(s) between cause and effect.

In Unit 2 — the core FPS learning target: Explain the impact of U.S. entry into WWI and its imperialist transformation. Produce a WWI argumentation piece (2 documents, 2 pieces of evidence).

Key cause-and-effect chains: - Spanish-American War → American empire: the "splendid little war" (Secretary of State John Hay) produces the Philippines, Guam, Puerto Rico, and a protectorate over Cuba — the U.S. becomes an imperial power overnight - European alliance system + assassination of Franz Ferdinand → WWI: the interconnected treaty system means a regional conflict in the Balkans triggers a continental war - Unrestricted submarine warfare + Zimmermann Telegram → U.S. entry: Germany's decision to sink neutral ships (including American) combined with the diplomatic insult of the Zimmermann Telegram breaks Wilson's neutrality - WWI propaganda + espionage acts → civil liberties crisis: the Committee on Public Information produces pro-war propaganda; the Espionage Act (1917) and Sedition Act (1918) criminalize dissent - Treaty of Versailles war guilt clause → German resentment → WWII: the punishing terms imposed on Germany create the economic and psychological conditions for the rise of fascism

Learning targets: - I can trace the causes of U.S. entry into WWI from European alliance systems to specific incidents. - I can explain how the Treaty of Versailles caused the conditions that led to WWII. - I can produce a written argument about U.S. imperialism using two documents and two pieces of evidence.

docs.title: US.6_12.4 + Unit 2 — Change Over Time
docs.text:
Standard: Compare how historical elements change over time.

In Unit 2: Design, develop, and create a primary or secondary source rooted in events from 1890–1930s. Evaluate the product's reliability, impact, and purpose.

Key comparisons: - U.S. imperialism of the 1890s → U.S. "global leadership" and military bases in 2026 - WWI propaganda → contemporary media and political messaging - Wilson's League of Nations failure → the United Nations (post-WWII) — what changed? - WWI anti-dissent laws (Espionage Act) → post-9/11 Patriot Act; ongoing tensions between national security and civil liberties

Learning targets: - I can identify a pattern from Unit 2 that is still visible in U.S. foreign policy or domestic politics today. - I can design a primary source document (propaganda poster, speech, editorial) rooted in Unit 2 content.

docs.title: US.6_12.5 + Unit 2 — Significant Contributions of People and Policy
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze the significant contributions of people, policy, and the influence on an era.

In Unit 2 — FPS target: Formulate an argument identifying a key figure or policy that had the most significant impact (positive or negative): Herbert Hoover, Theodore Roosevelt, Isolationism, Imperialism, FDR, New Deal, Prohibition, Political Machines, etc.

Key people and policies: | Person/Policy | Contribution | |--------------|-------------| | Woodrow Wilson | 14 Points for a just peace; League of Nations (rejected by the U.S. Senate); Mexican and Caribbean interventions | | Theodore Roosevelt | Roosevelt Corollary to Monroe Doctrine (U.S. as Western Hemisphere police); Panama Canal; "speak softly and carry a big stick" | | Alfred Thayer Mahan | The Influence of Sea Power upon History — the intellectual framework for American naval imperialism | | Queen Liliuokalani | Last Hawaiian monarch; overthrown in a U.S.-backed coup; her story is a primary source on the costs of imperialism | | Zimmermann Telegram | German communication to Mexico proposing an alliance against the U.S.; intercepted by Britain and published; turned American public opinion toward war | | Espionage and Sedition Acts | Criminalized anti-war speech and action during WWI; Eugene Debs imprisoned for a speech |

docs.title: US.6_12.6 + Unit 2 — Past to Present
docs.text:
Standard: Connect the past to the present using current events.

Key connections: - U.S. military bases worldwide → inherited from imperialist expansion (Philippines, Guam, Puerto Rico still U.S. territories) - WWI propaganda techniques → contemporary media influence campaigns - League of Nations failure → U.S. debates about multilateralism vs. unilateralism today - Anti-immigrant sentiment in WWI (against Germans, then all Europeans) → current immigration debates - Puerto Rico: acquired in 1898, still not a state; U.S. citizens without full voting rights

docs.title: Key Vocabulary for Unit 2
docs.text: Term Definition imperialism A policy of extending a nation's power through colonization, military force, or economic domination isolationism The belief that the U.S. should avoid involvement in foreign conflicts and entanglements Zimmermann Telegram A 1917 German diplomatic communication to Mexico proposing a military alliance against the U.S.; intercepted by Britain and published; accelerated U.S. entry into WWI alliance system The network of mutual defense treaties in pre-WWI Europe that turned a regional conflict into a continental war MAIN The four causes of WWI: Militarism, Alliances, Imperialism, Nationalism trench warfare The defensive static warfare of WWI; opposing armies dug into parallel trenches facing each other propaganda Information designed to promote a particular political cause; WWI produced the first systematic government propaganda campaigns 14 Points Woodrow Wilson's 1918 proposals for a just peace; included the League of Nations League of Nations Wilson's proposed international organization to maintain peace; rejected by the U.S. Senate; the precursor to the United Nations Treaty of Versailles The 1919 peace agreement ending WWI; blamed Germany and imposed punishing reparations; widely credited with creating conditions for WWII

docs.title: Skinnies for Unit 2
docs.text: Standard Skinny US.1 Science & Tech Unit 2 Skinny US.2 Ideas & Beliefs Unit 2 Skinny US.3 Political Systems Unit 2 Skinny US.4 Social & Economic Unit 2 Skinny US.5 Causes & Effects Unit 2 Skinny US.6 Past to Present Unit 2 Skinny

• Kipling "The White Man's Burden" + H.T. Johnson response — Competing perspectives on American imperialism · US.6_12.2 + US.6_12.4 + W.4 · Primary Sources
  • Dulce et Decorum Est — Wilfred Owen — Anti-war WWI poetry · US.6_12.2 + US.6_12.5 + R.8 · Literary Text

docs.title: Related Standards
docs.text: ELA crosswalk standards most used in Unit 2: - W.4 — Argument writing (was American imperialism justified? should the US have entered WWI?) - R.9 — Informational/argumentative analysis (war propaganda posters, Wilson's speeches, anti-war arguments) - IR.1–5 — Research bundle (active every studio)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
American imperialism in Unit 2 raises a question that is still unresolved: what does the United States owe the people in countries where it has exercised military, economic, or political power? Puerto Rico, the Philippines, Hawaii, Cuba, Panama — the people of these places have been governed by or deeply shaped by American decisions they had no vote in. The overseas territories the U.S. acquired in the 1890s are still U.S. territories, and the people living there still have a different legal relationship to American democracy than people in the 50 states. WWI adds the parallel question: what are the unintended consequences of alliances, nationalism, and arms races? The 1914 war happened partly because interconnected agreements meant a local conflict could become a global one — a dynamic that has not disappeared. See WH-05: The Cost of War and Dulce et Decorum Est.

Unit 2: Imperialism and WWI — SDA Commons Wiki See also: USH Standards Overview · Unit 1 — Industrial Revolution · Unit 3 — Roaring 20s · Primary Sources

docs.title: Unit 3: Roaring 20s and the Great Depression
docs.text: Course: English II + US History (Grade 10) Approximate period: 1920–1941 FPS Learning Goals: Explain the political, social, and economic changes of the 1920s and 1930s.

docs.title: What This Unit Is About
docs.text:
The 1920s felt, to many Americans, like the country had finally arrived. The war was over, the economy was booming, consumer culture exploded, and new forms of music, fashion, and social freedom challenged Victorian norms. It was also a decade of profound reaction: the Red Scare, the revival of the KKK, Prohibition, anti-immigrant legislation, and racial violence made the decade far more complicated than "roaring."

Then the crash came. The Great Depression (1929–1941) was the worst economic catastrophe in American history. One in four workers was unemployed at its peak. Farmers watched their land blow away in the Dust Bowl. President Hoover believed government should not intervene in the economy — so FDR was elected on a platform promising that it would.

This unit covers: - The Red Scare (1919–1920): fear of communism and anarchism - Consumer culture of the 1920s: cars, radios, advertising, credit - Cultural shifts: Flappers, Jazz Age, Harlem Renaissance, Prohibition - The KKK revival and racist violence - Stock market crash (1929) and its causes - The Great Depression: unemployment, Dust Bowl, Hoovervilles - FDR's New Deal: relief, recovery, reform - Political cartoons as a primary source type in this era

docs.title: All Six Standards Applied to Unit 3
US.6_12.1 + Unit 3 — Primary and Secondary Sources
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze primary and secondary sources with attention to reliability, impact, and purpose.

In Unit 3: Analyze sources on 1920s and 1930s political, social, and economic changes.

Key sources: - 1920s political cartoons (New Deal era) - Dust Bowl photographs (Dorothea Lange — "Migrant Mother" as primary source) - FDR's inaugural address ("We have nothing to fear but fear itself") - Federal Emergency Relief Administration documents - Harlem Renaissance literary and artistic works as primary sources - Hoover vs. FDR on the role of government in economic crisis

FPS Skill Strand: OEC — Explain the political, social, and economic changes of the 1920s and 1930s.

Learning targets: - I can analyze Dorothea Lange's Depression photographs for their purpose, choices, and argument. - I can compare Hoover's and FDR's statements on government's role in economic crisis. - I can evaluate a New Deal policy document for its goals and the assumptions behind them.

docs.title: US.6_12.2 + Unit 3 — Multiple Perspectives
docs.text:
Standard: Examine the impact of multiple perspectives on social, political, and cultural development.

In Unit 3: Examine how 1920s cultural changes (jazz, flappers, consumer culture) and the Depression looked from different perspectives.

Key perspectives: - The Roaring 20s' winners: middle-class consumers with access to credit, cars, radios; the cultural elite of the Harlem Renaissance - The 1920s' losers: Black Americans facing continued racial violence; farmers already in depression before 1929; immigrants facing restriction (Immigration Act of 1924 dramatically reduced immigration) - Harlem Renaissance perspective: Black intellectual and artistic flourishing in the 1920s — Langston Hughes, Zora Neale Hurston, Duke Ellington; a counter-narrative to the dominant white cultural story - Prohibition's contested reception: celebrated by temperance advocates and religious conservatives; resisted by immigrant communities, workers, and anyone who liked alcohol; created organized crime - Depression-era perspectives: unemployed workers vs. Hoover's "pull yourself up by your bootstraps"; FDR's coalition including Southern Democrats (who supported the New Deal but maintained Jim Crow)

Learning targets: - I can explain how the 1920s looked different for white middle-class Americans vs. Black Americans vs. recent immigrants. - I can analyze the Harlem Renaissance as a specific cultural and intellectual response to racial oppression.

docs.title: US.6_12.3 + Unit 3 — Cause and Effect
docs.text:
Standard: Explain the relationship of events focusing on the link(s) between cause and effect.

In Unit 3 — FPS target: Explain the political, social, and economic events of the 1930s. Produce a 1930s argumentation summative.

Key cause-and-effect chains: - Overproduction + speculation + easy credit → stock market crash (1929): the economy of the 1920s was built on credit and speculation; the crash revealed how fragile the foundation was - Stock market crash → bank failures → unemployment → Great Depression: the financial panic triggered a cascade of bank runs, business closures, and mass unemployment - Drought + agricultural overproduction + Great Plains soil depletion → Dust Bowl: the "3 B's" (Black Sunday, Black Blizzard, Black Roller) driving 3.5 million people off the land - Great Depression + Hoover's inaction → FDR's New Deal election: the political consequence of economic disaster - New Deal → modern American welfare state: Social Security (1935), FDIC, SEC, minimum wage — institutions still governing American economic life

Learning targets: - I can explain the causes of the Great Depression using multiple connected cause-and-effect chains. - I can analyze how the New Deal changed the relationship between the federal government and the economy. - I can produce a written argument about a specific 1930s event or policy.

docs.title: US.6_12.4 + Unit 3 — Change Over Time
docs.text:
Standard: Compare how historical elements change over time.

In Unit 3 — FPS target: Apply historical knowledge as a tool for examining current events: Great Depression, current economic policy, voting rights, government aid programs (stimulus), unionism, imperialism, U.S. involvement in Americas.

Key comparisons: - 1929 crash → 2008 financial crisis: the same patterns (speculation, deregulation, cascading financial failure); same debates (should government bail out banks and businesses?) - Dust Bowl → climate change migration: environmental crisis driving population movement - New Deal government programs → current debates about the size of government (stimulus checks, unemployment benefits) - 1930s labor movement → current labor organizing (Amazon, Starbucks) - Prohibition → current drug policy debates

docs.title: US.6_12.5 + Unit 3 — Significant Contributions of People and Policy
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze the significant contributions of people, policy, and the influence on an era.

In Unit 3 — FPS target: Design, develop, and create a primary or secondary source rooted in Unit 3; evaluate its reliability, impact, and purpose.

Key people and policies: | Person/Policy | Contribution | |--------------|-------------| | FDR (Franklin Delano Roosevelt) | New Deal architect; 3 R's: Relief, Recovery, Reform; transformed the presidency and federal government's role | | Herbert Hoover | Believed in "rugged individualism"; opposed direct federal relief; blamed for the Depression deepening; Hoovervilles named after him | | Eleanor Roosevelt | Expanded the role of First Lady; advocate for New Deal; civil rights ally; her own column and political network | | Dorothea Lange | FSA photographer; "Migrant Mother" became the iconic image of the Depression | | Social Security Act (1935) | Retirement insurance for workers; still the foundation of American retirement security | | New Deal (1933–1939) | Complex of relief, job creation, financial regulation, and agricultural programs; restructured the relationship between government and economy |

docs.title: US.6_12.6 + Unit 3 — Past to Present
docs.text:
Standard: Connect the past to the present using current events.

Key connections: - Social Security and FDIC → still functioning institutions - New Deal coalition → Democratic Party politics for 40 years - Labor movement gains → minimum wage, 40-hour week, workplace safety - Dust Bowl environmental lessons → still largely ignored in Great Plains farming practices - Government stimulus debates (COVID relief, infrastructure) trace to New Deal arguments

docs.title: Key Vocabulary for Unit 3
docs.text: Term Definition Roaring 20s The 1920s decade of economic prosperity, consumer culture, and cultural change Red Scare The 1919–1920 fear of communist revolution in the U.S., following the Russian Revolution Prohibition The constitutional ban on alcohol production and sale (18th Amendment, 1920–1933) Harlem Renaissance The African American cultural, artistic, and intellectual movement centered in Harlem, NYC, in the 1920s stock market crash The collapse of stock prices in October 1929, triggering the Great Depression Great Depression The severe economic crisis of the 1930s; 25% unemployment at its peak Dust Bowl The ecological disaster in the Great Plains (~1930–1936) caused by drought and unsustainable farming New Deal FDR's program of relief, recovery, and reform programs designed to respond to the Great Depression rugged individualism Hoover's belief that self-reliance and private charity, not government, should address economic hardship Social Security The 1935 federal program providing retirement income for American workers

docs.title: Skinnies for Unit 3
docs.text: Standard Skinny US.1 Science & Tech Unit 3 Skinny US.2 Ideas & Beliefs Unit 3 Skinny US.3 Political Systems Unit 3 Skinny US.4 Social & Economic Unit 3 Skinny US.5 Causes & Effects Unit 3 Skinny US.6 Past to Present Unit 3 Skinny

docs.title: Related Standards
docs.text: ELA crosswalk standards most used in Unit 3: - W.3 — Explanatory writing (explain causes of the Great Depression; explain how New Deal programs worked) - R.9 — Informational/argumentative analysis (FDR speeches, Depression-era journalism, New Deal debates) - IR.1–5 — Research bundle (active every studio)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The 1920s are remembered for prosperity and cultural explosion — jazz, the Harlem Renaissance, mass consumer culture, the automobile, the radio. They're less remembered for what caused the 1929 crash: financial speculation, income inequality, agricultural collapse, and an international system that had no mechanism for managing simultaneous economic failure in multiple countries at once. The Great Depression that followed put 25% of Americans out of work and forced the country to rethink the relationship between government and the economy in ways that are still contested. Every debate about government spending in a recession, about the "automatic stabilizers" built into the welfare state, about whether inequality is self-correcting or requires intervention — those arguments were first made and tested in the 1930s. See USH-03: When the Floor Falls Out and The Great Gatsby.

Unit 3: Roaring 20s and Great Depression — SDA Commons Wiki See also: USH Standards Overview · Unit 2 — Imperialism & WWI · Unit 4 — WWII · Primary Sources

docs.title: Unit 4: World War II
docs.text: Course: English II + US History (Grade 10) Approximate period: 1939–1945 (U.S. involvement: 1941–1945) FPS Learning Goal: Explain the causes and consequences of U.S. involvement in World War II.

docs.title: What This Unit Is About
docs.text:
WWII is the defining event of the 20th century. It produced the deadliest conflict in human history (70–85 million deaths), the Holocaust, two atomic bombings, and the international order that still governs the world today.

The United States spent the first two years of the war officially neutral while supplying Britain through Lend-Lease. Pearl Harbor (December 7, 1941) ended neutrality. The U.S. fought simultaneously in the Pacific (against Japan) and in Europe and North Africa (against Nazi Germany and Italy). The war reshaped American society: it ended the Great Depression, brought women into the industrial workforce in unprecedented numbers, exposed the contradiction between fighting fascism abroad and practicing racism at home, and ended with the U.S. as the world's dominant power.

This unit covers: - Causes of WWII: Treaty of Versailles, rise of fascism, Great Depression, appeasement - The Holocaust: causes, implementation, Allied knowledge, Nuremberg Trials - Pacific theater: Pearl Harbor, island-hopping, Midway, the decision to use atomic bombs - Atlantic/European theater: D-Day (June 6, 1944), liberation of France, fall of Berlin - Homefront: Rosie the Riveter, war bonds, rationing, Japanese American internment - FDR's Four Freedoms speech as a primary source - The decision to drop the atomic bomb — the most contested moral question of Unit 4

docs.title: All Six Standards Applied to Unit 4
US.6_12.1 + Unit 4 — Primary and Secondary Sources
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze primary and secondary sources with attention to reliability, impact, and purpose.

In Unit 4: Analyze sources on the causes and consequences of U.S. WWII involvement.

Key sources: - War bonds propaganda and Hollywood war films - FDR's "Day of Infamy" speech after Pearl Harbor - FDR's Four Freedoms speech - Executive Order 9066 (Japanese American internment) - Wannsee Conference minutes (planning of the Holocaust) - Hiroshima survivor testimony (Hibakusha accounts) - Truman's justification for using atomic bombs - Newspaper coverage of D-Day

FPS Skill Strand: OEC — Explain the causes and consequences of U.S. involvement in WWII.

Learning targets: - I can analyze FDR's "Day of Infamy" speech for its rhetorical purpose and choices. - I can compare the Wannsee Conference minutes (a government document planning genocide) to survivor testimony about the same events. - I can evaluate Executive Order 9066 for what it reveals about how democratic governments rationalize civil liberties violations during wartime.

docs.title: US.6_12.2 + Unit 4 — Multiple Perspectives
docs.text:
Standard: Examine the impact of multiple perspectives on social, political, and cultural development.

In Unit 4 — primary focus: the Atomic Bomb debate.

Key perspectives: - Pro-bomb (U.S. government): the bomb ended the war faster, saving the lives of hundreds of thousands of American soldiers who would have died in a land invasion of Japan - Anti-bomb (critics then and now): Japan was already defeated; the bomb was primarily a message to the Soviet Union; dropping on civilians was a war crime - Japanese perspective: Hiroshima (August 6) and Nagasaki (August 9) killed 130,000–226,000 people, most civilians; survivors (hibakusha) faced radiation sickness and social stigma - Japanese American internment: 120,000 Japanese Americans, two-thirds of them U.S. citizens, incarcerated without charge or trial; Supreme Court upheld it (Korematsu v. United States, 1944); formally apologized in 1988 - African American soldiers: WWII accelerated the civil rights movement — the Double V campaign; Tuskegee Airmen; the irony of fighting Nazi racial ideology for a segregated country

Learning targets: - I can examine multiple perspectives on the atomic bomb decision, using primary sources from multiple sides. - I can analyze Japanese American internment from the perspective of both the government and the incarcerated families.

docs.title: US.6_12.3 + Unit 4 — Cause and Effect
docs.text:
Standard: Explain the relationship of events focusing on the link(s) between cause and effect.

In Unit 4: Explain the causes and consequences of U.S. involvement in WWII.

Key cause-and-effect chains: - Tripartite Pact (1940) → Axis alliance: Germany, Italy, Japan commit to mutual defense; the alliance that draws the U.S. into a two-front war - Pearl Harbor attack → U.S. declaration of war: December 7, 1941; Germany and Italy declare war on the U.S. four days later (per Tripartite Pact) - Allied Forces coordination → D-Day success: the logistics of coordinating 150,000 troops from multiple nations in the largest seaborne invasion in history - Holocaust implementation → Nuremberg Trials → international law: the systematic murder of Jews, Roma, disabled people, and others → war crimes tribunals → Geneva Conventions, Genocide Convention, concept of crimes against humanity - Atomic bomb → Japanese surrender → Cold War nuclear arms race: the bomb ends the Pacific war but begins a new kind of global threat

Learning targets: - I can trace the cause-and-effect chain from Pearl Harbor to U.S. entry into the Pacific and European theaters. - I can explain how the Holocaust produced changes in international law. - I can explain the cause-and-effect relationship between the atomic bomb's use and the Cold War arms race.

docs.title: US.6_12.4 + Unit 4 — Change Over Time
docs.text:
Standard: Compare how historical elements change over time.

In Unit 4: Compare WWII-era changes over time; analyze the significant changes produced by the war.

Key comparisons: - Women in the workforce during WWII → "pushed back" after the war → feminist movement of the 1960s–70s responding to that reversal - The Holocaust → "Never Again" promise → genocides in Rwanda, Bosnia, Darfur; how well the world kept that promise - Atomic bomb → nuclear proliferation; the number of nuclear-armed states has grown since 1945 - WWII military-industrial complex → still the dominant structure of U.S. defense spending

docs.title: US.6_12.5 + Unit 4 — Significant Contributions of People and Policy
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze the significant contributions of people, policy, and the influence on an era.

Key people and policies: | Person/Policy | Contribution | |--------------|-------------| | FDR | Led the U.S. through WWII; Four Freedoms speech; death in April 1945, just before Allied victory | | Dwight D. Eisenhower | Supreme Allied Commander in Europe; D-Day planning; later president who warned of military-industrial complex | | Harry Truman | Authorized atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki; Marshall Plan; Truman Doctrine | | Manhattan Project | The U.S. scientific program that developed nuclear weapons; included over 100,000 people | | Tuskegee Airmen | African American military aviators who flew over 1,500 missions; a direct challenge to segregation's logic | | Executive Order 9066 | Authorized Japanese American incarceration; one of the most significant civil liberties violations in U.S. history | | Lend-Lease Act (1941) | Allowed the U.S. to supply Britain and later the Soviet Union before entering the war |

docs.title: US.6_12.6 + Unit 4 — Past to Present
docs.text:
Standard: Connect the past to the present using current events.

Key connections: - United Nations (1945) → still the primary international organization; WWII-era architecture still in place - Nuclear weapons → 13,000 still exist globally; non-proliferation challenges; Iran, North Korea - NATO → created 1949 post-WWII; still central to European security (Russia-Ukraine war) - German reparations to Holocaust survivors → still paid; contrast to U.S. never paying reparations for slavery - Japanese American internment → formal apology and reparations (1988); lessons for post-9/11 civil liberties debates

docs.title: Key Vocabulary for Unit 4
docs.text: Term Definition Holocaust The systematic, state-sponsored murder of six million Jews and millions of others by the Nazi regime appeasement The policy of making concessions to aggressive powers to avoid war; associated with Chamberlain's agreement at Munich (1938) D-Day June 6, 1944: the Allied amphibious invasion of Normandy, France; the turning point of the European theater Tripartite Pact The 1940 mutual defense agreement between Germany, Italy, and Japan Manhattan Project The secret U.S. program that developed the first nuclear weapons internment Confinement of a group of people, especially in wartime; Japanese American internment was the incarceration of 120,000 Japanese Americans under Executive Order 9066 hibakusha Japanese term for atomic bomb survivors; people who experienced Hiroshima or Nagasaki Nuremberg Trials Post-WWII trials of Nazi war criminals; established the precedent for international criminal accountability Double V The African American WWII-era slogan calling for victory against fascism abroad and racism at home Lend-Lease The U.S. policy of supplying Allied nations with war materials before entering WWII

docs.title: Skinnies for Unit 4
docs.text: Standard Skinny US.1 Science & Tech Unit 4 Skinny US.2 Ideas & Beliefs Unit 4 Skinny US.3 Political Systems Unit 4 Skinny US.4 Social & Economic Unit 4 Skinny US.5 Causes & Effects Unit 4 Skinny US.6 Past to Present Unit 4 Skinny

docs.title: Related Standards
docs.text: ELA crosswalk standards most used in Unit 4: - R.8 — Literary elements (Night — character under impossible circumstances, Wiesel's narrative choices, symbolism) - W.4 — Argument writing (was dropping the atomic bomb justified? was Japanese internment constitutional?) - R.9 — Informational/argumentative analysis (FDR speeches, wartime propaganda, Nuremberg trial testimony) - IR.1–5 — Research bundle (active every studio)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Unit 4 forces a question that does not have a clean answer: what values can a nation claim to hold while fighting for a "good cause" and still do deeply wrong things in pursuit of victory? The United States interned 120,000 Japanese Americans, firebombed civilian populations, and used atomic weapons against two cities — while simultaneously fighting a war against fascism and genocide. The moral complexity is not a reason to avoid the war or conclude it wasn't worth fighting; it's a reason to think carefully about what "fighting for the right side" actually means and what it authorizes. Every debate about drone strikes, civilian casualties, torture, and the standards of conduct for military action traces back to the questions Unit 4 raises. See USH-04: The Cost of Victory and Night.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Every contemporary debate about "just war," civilian casualties, drone strikes, and military necessity takes place in the framework Unit 4 built. The internment of Japanese Americans — upheld by the Supreme Court in Korematsu (1944) and finally repudiated in 2018 — is the clearest test case for what civil liberties protections are actually worth under national security pressure, and that repudiation came 74 years late. The Holocaust's "never again" pledge has been tested repeatedly since 1945 in Rwanda, Bosnia, Darfur, and the Uyghur detention system in Xinjiang, and the international community's responses reveal the gap between the pledge and the mechanisms for keeping it. The post-WWII international order — the UN, NATO, the Geneva Conventions — was built in direct response to WWII's catastrophes, and understanding why those institutions were built explains what is at stake when they are undermined. See USH-04: The Cost of Victory for the studio that asks what the "good war" actually cost.

Unit 4: WWII — SDA Commons Wiki See also: USH Standards Overview · Unit 3 — Roaring 20s · Unit 5 — Cold War · Primary Sources

docs.title: Unit 5: Cold War
docs.text: Course: English II + US History (Grade 10) Approximate period: 1945–1991 FPS Learning Goals: Explain the United States' reactions toward the growth of communism. Explain the impact of rapid technological change on society. Explain the economic and/or social issues of the Post-WWII era (1945–1962) and their long-lasting effects.

docs.title: What This Unit Is About
docs.text:
The Cold War was a 45-year confrontation between the United States and the Soviet Union — the world's two nuclear-armed superpowers. It was "cold" because they never directly fought each other, but it shaped every hot war, every political crisis, and most domestic policy in both countries for half a century.

The Cold War was simultaneously: - An ideological conflict (capitalism vs. communism; liberal democracy vs. one-party state) - A military competition (nuclear arms race, NATO vs. Warsaw Pact, conventional forces in Europe) - A technological race (space race, nuclear weapons delivery systems, spy satellites) - A series of proxy conflicts (Korea, Vietnam, Angola, Nicaragua — all fought with U.S. or Soviet support) - A domestic political phenomenon (McCarthyism, the Red Scare, civil liberties crisis)

This unit covers: - Origins: the division of Germany, Iron Curtain, containment policy, Truman Doctrine, Marshall Plan - NATO, Warsaw Pact, Berlin Airlift - Korean War (1950–1953): the first Cold War "hot" proxy conflict - McCarthyism and the domestic Red Scare (1950–1954) - Sputnik (1957) and the space race - Eisenhower era: massive retaliation, brinkmanship, U-2 incident - Cuban Missile Crisis (1962) — thirteen days that almost ended the world - Cold War culture: fallout shelters, CONELRAD, duck-and-cover drills

docs.title: All Six Standards Applied to Unit 5
US.6_12.1 + Unit 5 — Primary and Secondary Sources
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze primary and secondary sources with attention to reliability, impact, and purpose.

In Unit 5: Analyze sources on U.S. reactions to communism and on the post-WWII era.

Key sources: - McCarthy's 1950 speech claiming 205 Communists in the State Department - NSC-68 (the 1950 policy document defining U.S. Cold War strategy) - Kennan's "Long Telegram" and "X Article" on containment - Eisenhower's Farewell Address ("beware the military-industrial complex") - Khrushchev's "Secret Speech" (1956) denouncing Stalin - Documents from the Cuban Missile Crisis (JFK's ExComm meetings)

FPS Skill Strand: OEC — Explain the United States' reactions toward the growth of communism and the impact of rapid technological change.

Learning targets: - I can analyze McCarthy's speech for its rhetorical techniques and evaluate it as a historical source. - I can explain why Eisenhower's Farewell Address warning about the military-industrial complex is still cited today. - I can analyze the Cuban Missile Crisis through primary source documents from both the American and Soviet sides.

docs.title: US.6_12.2 + Unit 5 — Multiple Perspectives
docs.text:
Standard: Examine the impact of multiple perspectives on social, political, and cultural development.

In Unit 5 — key document: McCarthy's speech.

Key perspectives: - American Cold Warriors: containment is necessary; communism must be stopped everywhere or it will spread (domino theory); nuclear deterrence is preferable to disarmament - Soviet perspective: the U.S. is encircling the Soviet Union with military alliances; the Marshall Plan is economic imperialism; the West refused to open a second front in WWII, costing millions of Soviet lives - Post-WWII Americans: GI Bill prosperity vs. the fear of nuclear annihilation; the contradiction of building suburbs while building bomb shelters - McCarthy's targets: scientists, artists, union organizers, civil rights activists — anyone deemed "un-American"; 10,000+ careers destroyed - Developing world perspective: Cold War proxy conflicts (Korea, Vietnam, Angola) fought on their soil, with their populations; superpower competition as a form of ongoing imperialism

Learning targets: - I can examine multiple perspectives on U.S. Cold War policy, including from the Soviet side. - I can analyze McCarthyism from the perspective of both its supporters and its targets.

docs.title: US.6_12.3 + Unit 5 — Cause and Effect
docs.text:
Standard: Explain the relationship of events focusing on the link(s) between cause and effect.

In Unit 5 — FPS target: Post-WWII U.S. foreign policy: argue about a specific Cold War decision.

Key cause-and-effect chains: - Soviet atomic bomb (1949) → NSC-68 → arms race: the U.S. loses its nuclear monopoly; NSC-68 recommends massively increasing military spending; the arms race escalates - Communist victory in China (1949) + Korean War (1950) → McCarthyism intensifies: "Who lost China?" becomes a political attack; fear of communist infiltration at home grows - Sputnik (1957) → NASA, NDEA → space race and science education reform: Soviet satellite shocks American confidence; Congress creates NASA and funds science education - Bay of Pigs failure (1961) → Cuban Missile Crisis (1962): U.S.-backed invasion of Cuba fails; Khrushchev sees weakness; installs missiles in Cuba; thirteen days of nuclear standoff - Containment doctrine → Vietnam commitment: the same logic that defended Korea commits the U.S. to defending South Vietnam

Learning targets: - I can trace the cause-and-effect chain from Soviet atomic bomb to the arms race. - I can explain how Sputnik changed American education and science policy. - I can explain how the Cuban Missile Crisis was resolved and what it revealed about Cold War decision-making.

docs.title: US.6_12.4 + Unit 5 — Change Over Time
docs.text:
Standard: Compare how historical elements change over time.

In Unit 5 — FPS targets: Analyze significant contributions of presidents on domestic/foreign policy. Create a logical prediction on the absence of an existing historical event (counterfactual).

Key comparisons: - Truman Doctrine (1947) → Reagan Doctrine (1980s) → Bush Doctrine (2002): how U.S. interventionism has changed across Cold War and post-Cold War eras - Cold War civil defense (fallout shelters, duck-and-cover) → contemporary emergency preparedness (how fear shapes public behavior) - McCarthy's communist witch hunt → post-9/11 surveillance and civil liberties debates

Counterfactual question: "If JFK had not been assassinated, would the U.S. have escalated in Vietnam? Create a logical prediction based on existing historical evidence."

docs.title: US.6_12.5 + Unit 5 — Significant Contributions of People and Policy
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze the significant contributions of people, policy, and the influence on an era.

Key people and policies: | Person/Policy | Contribution | |--------------|-------------| | Harry Truman | Truman Doctrine ("support free peoples who are resisting attempted subjugation"); Marshall Plan (economic rebuilding of Western Europe) | | George Kennan | Author of containment doctrine; argued the Soviet Union would eventually collapse if Western democracies held firm | | Dwight Eisenhower | Massive retaliation and brinkmanship; ended Korean War; warned of military-industrial complex | | Joseph McCarthy | Gave his name to McCarthyism; destroyed careers through accusation without evidence; "Have you no decency?" (Welch to McCarthy) | | John F. Kennedy | Bay of Pigs; Cuban Missile Crisis management; New Frontier; assassination (1963) | | Containment doctrine | The foundational U.S. strategy for the Cold War: prevent Soviet expansion without direct military conflict | | Marshall Plan (1948) | $13 billion in economic aid to rebuild Western Europe; simultaneously humanitarian and strategic (rebuild allies, prevent communist political gains) | | Warren Court decisions | Additional: Brown v. Board (1954); Gideon v. Wainwright; Miranda v. Arizona — Cold War era domestic constitutional developments |

docs.title: US.6_12.6 + Unit 5 — Past to Present
docs.text:
Standard: Connect the past to the present using current events.

Key connections: - NATO → still the central Western military alliance; Russia-Ukraine war reactivated NATO expansion debates - U.S.-Russia relationship → Cold War patterns still visible; nuclear arsenals still deployed - Space race legacy → GPS, weather satellites, telecommunications satellites all trace to Cold War space investment - Marshall Plan model → still cited in debates about foreign aid as a tool of stability - McCarthyism → civil liberties debates about surveillance, loyalty oaths, political persecution

docs.title: Key Vocabulary for Unit 5
docs.text: Term Definition containment The U.S. Cold War strategy of preventing Soviet expansion without seeking to destroy the Soviet Union Iron Curtain Churchill's term for the political and ideological divide between Soviet-controlled Eastern Europe and the West NATO The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (1949); collective defense agreement among Western democracies Warsaw Pact The Soviet-led military alliance of Eastern European communist states domino theory The belief that if one country fell to communism, neighboring countries would fall too McCarthyism The practice of making unfounded accusations of communist sympathy or subversion; associated with Senator Joseph McCarthy Truman Doctrine The 1947 U.S. policy committing to support free peoples resisting communism, used to justify aid to Greece and Turkey Marshall Plan The 1948 U.S. program providing $13 billion to rebuild Western European economies after WWII brinkmanship The Cold War strategy of pushing dangerous situations to the brink of war to force the opponent to back down Cuban Missile Crisis The 13-day confrontation (October 1962) between the U.S. and Soviet Union over Soviet missiles in Cuba; the closest the world came to nuclear war proxy war A conflict in which two powers support opposing sides without fighting each other directly military-industrial complex Eisenhower's term for the relationship between the military and the defense industry; his warning that it could distort government priorities

docs.text: Standard Skinny US.1 Science & Tech Unit 5 Skinny US.2 Ideas & Beliefs Unit 5 Skinny US.3 Political Systems Unit 5 Skinny US.4 Social & Economic Unit 5 Skinny US.5 Causes & Effects Unit 5 Skinny US.6 Past to Present Unit 5 Skinny

  • Fahrenheit 451 — Ray Bradbury — Censorship and conformity in Cold War America · US.6_12.2 + US.6_12.3 + R.8 · Literary Text
