docs.title: Related Standards
docs.text: ELA crosswalk standards most used in Unit 5: - R.8 — Literary elements (1984 as Cold War allegory — surveillance, doublethink, propaganda as tools of political control) - W.4 — Argument writing (was containment the right Cold War strategy? was McCarthyism justified?) - R.9 — Informational/argumentative analysis (policy documents, competing US/Soviet accounts, propaganda analysis) - IR.1–5 — Research bundle (active every studio)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: The Cold War created the world most people alive today were born into: nuclear deterrence, the military-industrial complex, proxy wars fought in other people's countries, ideological competition framed as democracy vs. communism, mass surveillance of citizens justified by national security. Many of those structures didn't end when the Soviet Union did. Nuclear arsenals still exist. The logic of deterrence still governs international relations. The national security state built during the Cold War — intelligence agencies, surveillance capabilities, emergency powers — is still operating. And the foreign policy debates of the Cold War (when does the U.S. intervene? who counts as an ally? what human rights violations are acceptable in a partner?) are the same debates happening now with different countries involved. See USH-02: The Literature of Fear and 1984.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Cold War infrastructure is still running: nuclear weapons in the quantities built for mutual deterrence, NATO alliances, intelligence agencies built for superpower competition, and proxy war patterns that shaped today's Middle East, Southeast Asia, and Africa. McCarthyism's lesson — that fear of an external enemy can justify suspending civil liberties and destroying lives — is directly relevant to post-9/11 surveillance debates, to the use of "communist" and "socialist" as political attacks, and to how "loyalty" gets defined in political crises. The nuclear deterrence logic — Mutually Assured Destruction as stability — requires both sides to believe the other will actually use the weapons, which means the stability depends on maintaining credible threats of civilizational destruction. And the Cold War produced the civil rights movement's most consequential decade: the U.S. needed to demonstrate democracy worked on the world stage, which gave activists leverage they used to force legal change. See USH-02: The Literature of Fear for the studio that works with the Cold War's cultural anxieties through dystopian literature.

Unit 5: Cold War — SDA Commons Wiki See also: USH Standards Overview · Unit 4 — WWII · Unit 6 — Civil Rights · Primary Sources

docs.title: Unit 6: Civil Rights and Social Movements
docs.text: Course: English II + US History (Grade 10) Approximate period: 1945–1970s FPS Learning Goals: Explain the economic and/or social issues of the Post-WWII era and their long-lasting effects. Compare and contrast how different groups competed for equality and the effectiveness of various movements.

docs.title: What This Unit Is About
docs.text:
The civil rights movement is one of the most significant chapters in American history — and one of the most contested in how it is taught. In SDA, we treat it fully: the victories, the violence, the strategy debates, and the movement's connection to other equality struggles happening simultaneously around the world and in the U.S.

This unit covers: - The legal and social architecture of Jim Crow - The Montgomery Bus Boycott (1955–1956) - Little Rock Nine and "The Lost Year" (1957–1958) - Greensboro sit-ins (1960) and the student movement - Freedom Riders (1961) - Birmingham Children's March (1963) - MLK's "Letter from Birmingham Jail" as a primary source - March on Washington and "I Have a Dream" speech - Civil Rights Act (1964), Voting Rights Act (1965) - Malcolm X and Black Power - Women's Rights Movement - Native American Rights Movement - Latino Rights Movement - LGBTQ Rights Movement - Asian American Rights Movement

The core argument of this unit: the civil rights movement was not a single, unified campaign that achieved everything it set out to do in 1965 and stopped. It was a complex of overlapping movements, strategic debates, and ongoing struggles — some still unresolved.

docs.title: All Six Standards Applied to Unit 6
US.6_12.1 + Unit 6 — Primary and Secondary Sources
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze primary and secondary sources with attention to reliability, impact, and purpose.

In Unit 6: Analyze sources on the Post-WWII era and civil rights with attention to perspective, bias, and purpose.

Key sources: - MLK's "Letter from Birmingham Jail" (1963) — one of the most important primary sources in American history; written in response to white moderate clergy who asked him to slow down - MLK's "I Have a Dream" speech (August 28, 1963) - FBI surveillance files on MLK — a primary source that reveals government hostility to the civil rights movement - Malcolm X speeches and interviews - Newspaper coverage of Birmingham vs. civil rights organizations' documentation of the same events - Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC) pamphlets and position statements

FPS Skill Strand: OEC — Explain the economic and/or social issues of the Post-WWII era and their long-lasting effects.

Learning targets: - I can analyze "Letter from Birmingham Jail" for its argument, its audience (white moderate clergy), and its rhetorical strategy. - I can compare FBI surveillance documents on MLK to civil rights organizations' own materials and explain what each source reveals. - I can evaluate different sources on the same civil rights event for what they emphasize and omit.

docs.title: US.6_12.2 + Unit 6 — Multiple Perspectives
docs.text:
Standard: Examine the impact of multiple perspectives on social, political, and cultural development.

In Unit 6 — key document: "Letter from Birmingham Jail."

Key perspectives: - MLK and SCLC (Southern Christian Leadership Conference): nonviolent direct action; moral appeal; pressure on the conscience of white America; integration as the goal - SNCC (Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee): more radical; grassroots organizing; questioning integration as sufficient; later led by Stokely Carmichael (Black Power) - Malcolm X and the Nation of Islam: Black nationalism; self-defense; integration as accommodation to a white supremacist system; "by any means necessary" - White moderate response (the audience of "Letter from Birmingham Jail"): support the goal but not the timing or the tactics; MLK's critique of the white moderate is one of the letter's most powerful sections - Southern white resistance: Citizens' Councils, the Ku Klux Klan, George Wallace ("segregation now, segregation tomorrow, segregation forever"), FBI under Hoover actively working against the movement - Women's perspective within the civil rights movement: women did the organizing work but were often excluded from leadership; Fannie Lou Hamer, Diane Nash, Ella Baker — leaders often written out of standard accounts

Learning targets: - I can compare the strategic approaches of MLK, Malcolm X, and SNCC and analyze what each approach was responding to. - I can analyze how white moderate opposition (as described in "Letter from Birmingham Jail") differed from outright white supremacist opposition.

docs.title: US.6_12.3 + Unit 6 — Cause and Effect
docs.text:
Standard: Explain the relationship of events focusing on the link(s) between cause and effect.

In Unit 6 — FPS target: Argumentation: compare and contrast how different groups competed for equality and the effectiveness of various movements.

Key cause-and-effect chains: - WWII (Double V campaign) + Truman desegregating the military (1948) → civil rights momentum: returning Black veterans unwilling to accept Jim Crow after fighting fascism abroad - Brown v. Board of Education (1954) → "massive resistance" in the South → Little Rock: school desegregation ruling triggers a wave of Southern resistance, including Governor Faubus's use of the National Guard to block Black students - Montgomery Bus Boycott (1955–56) → rise of MLK as a national figure: a 381-day boycott organized largely by women (including Rosa Parks, Jo Ann Robinson); successful; launches MLK's national profile - Birmingham Children's March (1963) + Bull Connor's fire hoses and police dogs → national outrage → Civil Rights Act (1964): the televised violence against children changed white Northern public opinion and provided political cover for LBJ to pass the Civil Rights Act - Voting Rights Act (1965) → Selma → Bloody Sunday → passage: the brutal attack on marchers on the Edmund Pettus Bridge, televised nationally, produced sufficient political pressure to pass the Voting Rights Act

Learning targets: - I can trace the cause-and-effect chain from a specific civil rights event to a legal or political consequence. - I can compare the strategies of different equality movements and analyze which were more effective under what conditions. - I can explain why nonviolent direct action was strategic, not just moral.

docs.title: US.6_12.4 + Unit 6 — Change Over Time
docs.text:
Standard: Compare how historical elements change over time.

In Unit 6: Compare how different equality movements changed over time; evaluate the long-lasting effects of the civil rights era.

Key comparisons: - Civil Rights Act (1964) → Voting Rights Act (1965) → Shelby County v. Holder (2013, gutted key VRA provisions) — rights can be won and then partially reversed - 1960s nonviolent direct action → 1992 LA Riots → 2020 George Floyd protests: different moments in the ongoing struggle; what changes and what stays the same? - Women's suffrage (1920) → women excluded from early civil rights leadership → feminist movement of 1960s–70s → #MeToo (2017) - American Indian Movement (AIM) and its connections to civil rights and the current indigenous rights movement

docs.title: US.6_12.5 + Unit 6 — Significant Contributions of People and Policy
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze the significant contributions of people, policy, and the influence on an era.

Key people and policies: | Person/Policy | Contribution | |--------------|-------------| | Martin Luther King Jr. | SCLC; "Letter from Birmingham Jail"; "I Have a Dream"; assassinated April 4, 1968 | | Rosa Parks | Montgomery Bus Boycott; her arrest was strategic, not accidental — she was a trained NAACP activist | | Fannie Lou Hamer | Mississippi voting rights organizer; beaten for trying to register to vote; powerful testimony before the 1964 Democratic Convention | | Malcolm X | Nation of Islam; Black nationalism; "by any means necessary"; assassinated February 21, 1965 | | Thurgood Marshall | NAACP legal director; argued Brown v. Board; first Black Supreme Court Justice | | Civil Rights Act (1964) | Prohibited discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin in employment and public accommodations | | Voting Rights Act (1965) | Prohibited discriminatory voting practices; authorized federal oversight of elections in states with history of discrimination |

docs.title: US.6_12.6 + Unit 6 — Past to Present
docs.text:
Standard: Connect the past to the present using current events.

Key connections: - Civil Rights Act and Voting Rights Act → ongoing litigation about voting rights, gerrymandering, voter ID laws - MLK's "Letter from Birmingham Jail" → still directly quoted in contemporary justice movements - Black Lives Matter (2013–present) → direct descendant of civil rights movement; same debates about tactics, goals, white moderate response - Women's Rights Movement → Title IX, Roe v. Wade (1973) and its 2022 overturn, pay equity debates - Native American rights → Standing Rock (2016), land and water rights, tribal sovereignty debates

docs.title: Key Vocabulary for Unit 6
docs.text: Term Definition Jim Crow The system of state and local laws enforcing racial segregation in the South from the 1870s through the 1960s nonviolent direct action The strategy of using peaceful protest, civil disobedience, and economic pressure to challenge unjust systems civil disobedience The deliberate, nonviolent refusal to obey unjust laws as a form of political protest desegregation The process of ending racial segregation in public institutions NAACP The National Association for the Advancement of Colored People; founded 1909; pursued legal challenges to segregation SNCC The Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee; student-led civil rights organization; Freedom Riders, sit-ins Black Power A political and cultural movement emphasizing Black self-determination, pride, and community organization Civil Rights Act (1964) Federal law prohibiting discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin Voting Rights Act (1965) Federal law prohibiting discriminatory voting practices; one of the most important pieces of civil rights legislation massive resistance The Southern strategy of organized opposition to racial desegregation after Brown v. Board

docs.text: Standard Skinny US.1 Science & Tech Unit 6 Skinny US.2 Ideas & Beliefs Unit 6 Skinny US.3 Political Systems Unit 6 Skinny US.4 Social & Economic Unit 6 Skinny US.5 Causes & Effects Unit 6 Skinny US.6 Past to Present Unit 6 Skinny

docs.title: Related Standards
docs.text: ELA crosswalk standards most used in Unit 6: - R.9 — Informational/argumentative analysis (MLK Letter, Birmingham manifesto, movement speeches — all argumentative primary sources) - W.4 — Argument writing (was civil disobedience the right strategy? should the Voting Rights Act have come sooner?) - R.7 — Style and word choice (MLK's rhetorical choices — allusion, metaphor, parallel structure) - IR.1–5 — Research bundle (active every studio)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • Luther

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: The Civil Rights Movement was not a completed project — it was an ongoing negotiation between the promise of American democracy and the reality of American exclusion, and that negotiation is still happening. The gains of the 1950s and 60s — the Civil Rights Act, the Voting Rights Act, desegregation — were real, and they are still being contested. Voting rights protections are being rolled back in states. Fair housing enforcement gaps persist. The criminal justice system continues to show racial disparities in every stage from arrest through sentencing. The arguments activists made then — that formal legal equality is not enough if structural inequalities remain, that moral urgency requires direct action, that bystander silence is a form of complicity — are the arguments of current movements as well. This unit is not history; it's the foundation of current debates. See MLK Letter from Birmingham Jail and USH-01: When the System Fails.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Every contemporary argument about voting rights, police accountability, affirmative action, and systemic racism is a direct continuation of debates that Unit 6 covers — many involving the same legal frameworks, the same tactics, and often the same geographic locations. The Civil Rights Movement's toolkit — nonviolent direct action, coalition building, litigation strategy, economic pressure — is still the most studied model for social movements globally; climate activists, disability rights organizers, and pro-democracy movements worldwide draw on it. MLK's Letter from Birmingham Jail argued that unjust laws have no moral authority — an argument that appears in every civil disobedience debate worldwide. The Voting Rights Act of 1965 has been systematically weakened by Supreme Court decisions in 2013 and 2021, and the debate about what "equal protection" actually requires is ongoing. See USH-01: When the System Fails and the Letter from Birmingham Jail in the Reading Library.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Civil Rights movement is living history — the people who marched are still alive, the laws they won are still being contested, and the conditions they fought are not fully resolved. The Voting Rights Act of 1965 was gutted by the Supreme Court in 2013 (Shelby County v. Holder); the subsequent proliferation of voter restriction laws is a direct consequence. The fair housing gains of the 1960s have partially reversed due to discriminatory lending, zoning, and disinvestment that created the residential segregation that still structures American cities and school districts. The standards that apply here (social movements, cause and consequence, civil disobedience as a political strategy) are the standards for evaluating every subsequent social movement — for understanding what strategies work, what conditions are necessary, and what opposition looks like when it uses legal mechanisms rather than fire hoses. See USH-01: When the System Fails for the studio that traces the reform thread from the Progressive Era through this movement.

Unit 6: Civil Rights and Social Movements — SDA Commons Wiki See also: USH Standards Overview · Unit 5 — Cold War · Unit 7 — Vietnam · Primary Sources

docs.title: Unit 7: 1960s, Vietnam War, and Counterculture
docs.text: Course: English II + US History (Grade 10) Approximate period: 1960–1975 FPS Learning Goal: Identify and explain how domestic issues changed the United States' perceptions of government.

docs.title: What This Unit Is About
docs.text:
The 1960s began with idealism — the New Frontier, the space race, the civil rights movement gaining momentum — and ended with assassinations, riots, and a profound crisis of confidence in American institutions.

Vietnam was the event that crystallized that crisis. The United States spent 10 years fighting a war in Southeast Asia that it could not win, that it misled the American public about, and that killed 58,000 Americans and an estimated 3.5 million Vietnamese. The anti-war movement, the counterculture, and the revelations of the Pentagon Papers shattered the assumption that the government told the truth.

This unit covers: - JFK's presidency: New Frontier, Bay of Pigs, Cuban Missile Crisis, civil rights, assassination - The Great Society: LBJ's domestic programs (Medicare, Medicaid, Voting Rights Act) - Vietnam: origins, escalation, Gulf of Tonkin Resolution (1964), anti-war movement - The military draft and its inequities - Kent State massacre (1970): National Guard kills four student protesters - Pentagon Papers (1971): Daniel Ellsberg leaks evidence that the government systematically lied about Vietnam - The assassinations: JFK (1963), MLK (April 1968), RFK (June 1968) - Counterculture: Woodstock, hippies, protest music, second-wave feminism - Anti-war songs as primary sources: "Eve of Destruction" (Barry McGuire), "Okie from Muskogee" (Merle Haggard) — the debate AS the cultural artifact

docs.title: All Six Standards Applied to Unit 7
US.6_12.1 + Unit 7 — Primary and Secondary Sources
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze primary and secondary sources with attention to reliability, impact, and purpose.

In Unit 7: Analyze sources on how domestic issues changed perceptions of government.

Key sources: - Pentagon Papers (declassified 1971) — government documents proving systematic deception about Vietnam - 1960s political assassinations: Oswald-Ruby-Conspiracy debates as a source analysis problem - Anti-war music: "Eve of Destruction" (1965) vs. "Okie from Muskogee" (1969) — competing visions of America in song - Kent State photographs and news coverage - Walter Cronkite's 1968 Vietnam editorial ("We are mired in stalemate") — when a trusted TV anchor said this, LBJ reportedly said "If I've lost Cronkite, I've lost Middle America" - Gulf of Tonkin Resolution documents vs. later revelations about what actually happened

FPS Skill Strand: OEC — Identify and explain how domestic issues changed the United States' perceptions of government.

Learning targets: - I can analyze an anti-war song as a primary source for its argument, audience, and historical context. - I can explain what the Pentagon Papers revealed and why their publication was significant. - I can compare Gulf of Tonkin resolution documents to later declassified materials and explain what was concealed.

docs.title: US.6_12.2 + Unit 7 — Multiple Perspectives
docs.text:
Standard: Examine the impact of multiple perspectives on social, political, and cultural development.

In Unit 7: Examine multiple perspectives on the Vietnam War, the anti-war movement, and the counterculture.

Key perspectives: - Pro-war perspective: domino theory; containment; Cold War commitment; the military's belief that more resources and less political interference would have produced victory; veterans who felt betrayed by anti-war protesters - Anti-war perspective: the war was unjust, unwinnable, and based on lies; the draft was disproportionately falling on poor and Black men; the Vietnamese had a right to self-determination - Vietnamese perspective: for the North Vietnamese and Viet Cong, this was a war of independence against foreign intervention (first French, then American); the "communist invasion" narrative doesn't account for how many South Vietnamese supported reunification - Military families: people with loved ones deployed in Vietnam; "support the troops" as distinct from support for the war - Merle Haggard's "Okie from Muskogee": written from the perspective of rural, conservative America that viewed the counterculture with contempt; a genuine counter-perspective, not a parody

Learning targets: - I can analyze anti-war and pro-war perspectives on Vietnam using primary sources from both sides. - I can explain the counterculture's values and the backlash against them. - I can analyze "Eve of Destruction" and "Okie from Muskogee" as competing primary sources representing different American perspectives in the late 1960s.

docs.title: US.6_12.3 + Unit 7 — Cause and Effect
docs.text:
Standard: Explain the relationship of events focusing on the link(s) between cause and effect.

In Unit 7 — FPS target: Domestic response to Vietnam argumentation.

Key cause-and-effect chains: - Gulf of Tonkin Resolution (1964) → massive escalation: Congress authorizes the president to use force without a formal declaration of war; U.S. troop levels go from ~16,000 advisors (1963) to 543,000 combat troops (1969) - Tet Offensive (January 1968) → shift in American public opinion: massive coordinated Viet Cong attack on 100+ South Vietnamese cities; a military failure for North Vietnam but a psychological victory; showed the American public that the government's optimistic briefings were lies - MLK assassination (April 4, 1968) → riots in 100+ American cities: the death of the advocate for nonviolent change triggered the violence he had argued against - Kent State (May 4, 1970) → massive student strikes: National Guard kills four students at Kent State University, Ohio; 4 million students go on strike; 900 colleges close - Pentagon Papers (1971) → Nixon's "Plumbers" → Watergate: Ellsberg's leak leads Nixon to create an illegal covert operations unit that eventually breaks into the Democratic National Committee

Learning targets: - I can trace the cause-and-effect chain from the Gulf of Tonkin Resolution to the domestic anti-war movement. - I can explain how specific events (Tet Offensive, Kent State) changed American public opinion about Vietnam. - I can produce a written argument about how domestic issues changed Americans' perceptions of government.

docs.title: US.6_12.4 + Unit 7 — Change Over Time
docs.text:
Standard: Compare how historical elements change over time.

In Unit 7 — FPS target: Judge the multiple perspectives that emerged regarding social, political, and cultural developments in the era 1941–2001.

Key comparisons: - Vietnam War support and opposition → Iraq War support and opposition (2003); "weapons of mass destruction" as the 21st-century Gulf of Tonkin - Draft protest → All-volunteer military (post-Vietnam); who serves and who doesn't - Pentagon Papers → subsequent government deception exposed (Iran-Contra, WMD intelligence) - Counterculture's influence: civil rights, feminism, environmentalism, LGBTQ rights — all connected to 1960s social movements - Public trust in government: pre-Vietnam high trust → post-Vietnam/Watergate collapse → still not recovered

docs.title: US.6_12.5 + Unit 7 — Significant Contributions of People and Policy
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze the significant contributions of people, policy, and the influence on an era.

Key people and policies: | Person/Policy | Contribution | |--------------|-------------| | LBJ (Lyndon B. Johnson) | Great Society (Medicare, Medicaid, Voting Rights Act); escalated Vietnam; decided not to run in 1968 because of Vietnam | | JFK (John F. Kennedy) | New Frontier; Cuban Missile Crisis; civil rights; assassination created the myth of "Camelot" that shaped his legacy | | Daniel Ellsberg | Pentagon Papers leak; proved the government had systematically deceived the public about Vietnam | | Ho Chi Minh | North Vietnamese leader; framed the war as anti-colonial independence struggle; fought the French before the Americans | | Robert McNamara | Secretary of Defense who prosecuted the Vietnam War and later admitted it was a mistake | | Gulf of Tonkin Resolution (1964) | The congressional authorization that enabled massive Vietnam escalation; based on an exaggerated or fabricated incident |

docs.title: US.6_12.6 + Unit 7 — Past to Present
docs.text:
Standard: Connect the past to the present using current events.

Key connections: - Government credibility crisis from Vietnam/Watergate → still not recovered; baseline public distrust of institutions - Anti-war music as protest art → hip-hop, punk, contemporary political music - Vietnam veterans' treatment → PTSD recognition, veterans' services, the politics of "supporting the troops" - All-volunteer military → who serves, economic draft, military-civilian gap - "Never another Vietnam": the lesson politicians claimed to draw → used to justify both military restraint AND military action in subsequent conflicts

docs.title: Key Vocabulary for Unit 7
docs.text: Term Definition domino theory The Cold War belief that if one country fell to communism, neighboring countries would follow Gulf of Tonkin Resolution The 1964 congressional authorization allowing the president to use military force in Southeast Asia; the legal basis for massive Vietnam escalation draft Compulsory military service; the Vietnam-era draft fell disproportionately on working-class and Black men Viet Cong The South Vietnamese communist guerrilla force allied with North Vietnam Tet Offensive The January 1968 coordinated North Vietnamese/Viet Cong attack on 100+ South Vietnamese targets; psychologically decisive in turning American public opinion counterculture The 1960s youth culture that rejected mainstream American values; associated with anti-war activism, folk and rock music, civil rights, and social experimentation Pentagon Papers The classified Defense Department study of U.S. involvement in Vietnam, leaked by Daniel Ellsberg in 1971; revealed systematic government deception Kent State May 4, 1970: Ohio National Guard killed four student anti-war protesters at Kent State University; turned millions more Americans against the war New Frontier JFK's domestic program; framed as a challenge to Americans to achieve greatness through public service and innovation Great Society LBJ's ambitious domestic reform program: Medicare, Medicaid, Voting Rights Act, Elementary and Secondary Education Act, immigration reform

docs.title: Skinnies for Unit 7
docs.text: Standard Skinny US.1 Science & Tech Unit 7 Skinny US.2 Ideas & Beliefs Unit 7 Skinny US.3 Political Systems Unit 7 Skinny US.4 Social & Economic Unit 7 Skinny US.5 Causes & Effects Unit 7 Skinny US.6 Past to Present Unit 7 Skinny

docs.text:

docs.text:

  • CrashCourse US History Index — available USH episodes

docs.title: Related Standards
docs.text: ELA crosswalk standards most used in Unit 7: - W.4 — Argument writing (was US involvement in Vietnam justified? was the counterculture a positive social force?) - R.9 — Informational/argumentative analysis (anti-war speeches, Pentagon Papers excerpts, LBJ speeches — competing political arguments) - IR.1–5 — Research bundle (active every studio)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The standards in Unit 7 cover Vietnam and the Pentagon Papers — which means they're the standards for understanding how democratic governments manage information, control narrative, and respond when the official story is exposed as false. The Espionage Act used to prosecute Daniel Ellsberg for leaking the Pentagon Papers in 1971 was the same Act used to prosecute Chelsea Manning in 2010 and Edward Snowden in 2013. The press freedom questions raised by the Pentagon Papers publication — whether the government can suppress the publication of classified documents about an ongoing war — were directly re-litigated when Wikileaks published classified material in 2010–16. The pattern of government deception, whistleblower prosecution, and press freedom litigation that Unit 7 establishes is a recurring structure in American political history that has not stopped recurring. See USH-05: What the Government Didn't Say for the studio built around this unit.

Unit 7: Vietnam War and Counterculture — SDA Commons Wiki See also: USH Standards Overview · Unit 6 — Civil Rights · Unit 8 — 1970s & 1980s · Primary Sources

docs.title: Unit 8: 1970s and 1980s
docs.text: Course: English II + US History (Grade 10) Approximate period: 1970–1991 FPS Learning Goal: Explain the economic and/or social issues of the post-Cold War era and their long-lasting effects.

docs.title: What This Unit Is About
docs.text:
The 1970s began with Watergate and ended with an energy crisis, stagflation, and a hostage crisis in Iran. It was a decade of national humiliation and declining confidence. The 1980s were, for many Americans, a decade of recovery and renewal — but the Reagan Revolution also produced growing inequality, the AIDS crisis, the Iran-Contra scandal, and the beginning of the end of the Cold War.

This unit covers: - Watergate scandal (1972–1974): the break-in, the cover-up, Nixon's resignation - Nixon's other legacy: EPA, détente, opening China - Environmental movement: first Earth Day (1970), Clean Air Act, MADD (Mothers Against Drunk Driving) - Gerald Ford and Jimmy Carter: economic malaise, energy crisis, Iran hostage crisis - Reagan and Reaganomics: supply-side economics, deregulation, union-busting (PATCO) - Nicaragua and the Iran-Contra affair: another government lie, another scandal - AIDS crisis and the government's slow response - Challenger disaster (1986) - Cold War endgame: Reagan's military buildup, Gorbachev, fall of the Berlin Wall (1989) - Pop culture: music, video games, MTV — culture as a primary source

docs.title: All Six Standards Applied to Unit 8
US.6_12.1 + Unit 8 — Primary and Secondary Sources
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze primary and secondary sources with attention to reliability, impact, and purpose.

In Unit 8: Analyze sources on post-Cold War era social and economic issues.

Key sources: - Nixon's resignation speech (August 8, 1974) — a primary source for rhetorical analysis; does he accept responsibility? - Republican Party National Platform Plank on the Equal Rights Amendment — the party's shift on women's rights as a primary source - Oliver North Email to National Security Advisor John Poindexter (August 23, 1986) — Iran-Contra documents revealing covert operations - Nixon's 1972 Environmental Program message to Congress — Nixon as environmental president - Jimmy Carter's "Crisis of Confidence" speech (July 15, 1979) — Carter tells America it has a malaise; the political consequences - Reagan's First Inaugural Address

FPS Skill Strand: OEC + Argumentation — Explain the economic and/or social issues of the post-Cold War era. Develop an argument about a 1941–2001 topic and how historical interpretation has changed over time.

Learning targets: - I can analyze Nixon's resignation speech for what he admits and what he denies — and evaluate it as a primary source. - I can analyze Oliver North's emails for what they reveal about how covert government operations work around democratic oversight. - I can compare Carter's "Crisis of Confidence" to Reagan's rhetoric about American renewal and analyze the different political approaches.

docs.title: US.6_12.2 + Unit 8 — Multiple Perspectives
docs.text:
Standard: Examine the impact of multiple perspectives on social, political, and cultural development.

In Unit 8: Examine multiple perspectives on Watergate, Reagan, and 1970s–1980s social and political changes.

Key perspectives: - Nixon's defenders: Watergate was no worse than what other presidents did; Nixon accomplished real things (EPA, China, détente); the press destroyed him - Nixon's critics: the rule of law required accountability; a president who believed he could authorize crimes destroyed the foundations of constitutional government - Reagan supporters: morning in America; economic growth; military strength ending the Cold War; optimism - Reagan critics: growing inequality; union destruction; AIDS response failure (Reagan didn't say the word "AIDS" publicly until 1987, when 20,000 Americans had already died); Iran-Contra criminality; homelessness increasing during economic boom - AIDS activism perspective: Act Up, Larry Kramer, the quilt project; the government's failure to respond to a public health crisis because it primarily affected gay men - Working-class perspective on Reaganomics: tax cuts for the wealthy; trickle-down economics that didn't trickle; PATCO strike-breaking as a message to all unions

Learning targets: - I can examine multiple perspectives on Watergate's significance and on Reagan's legacy. - I can analyze how different political perspectives led to different interpretations of the same historical events.

docs.title: US.6_12.3 + Unit 8 — Cause and Effect
docs.text:
Standard: Explain the relationship of events focusing on the link(s) between cause and effect.

In Unit 8 — FPS target: To what degree were the 1970s and 1980s a period of change in United States history?

Key cause-and-effect chains: - Watergate → Nixon resignation → erosion of trust in government: the cover-up worse than the crime; Congress passes War Powers Act (1973) and campaign finance reform; public cynicism about government deepens - OPEC oil embargo (1973) → energy crisis → Carter's solar panels on the White House → Reagan's removal of solar panels: a story about U.S. energy policy and political signaling - AIDS crisis + government inaction → LGBTQ political mobilization: the decimation of the gay community by AIDS, combined with Reagan's silence, accelerated the formation of a politically organized LGBTQ rights movement - Reagan's PATCO strike-breaking (1981) → weakening of American labor movement: when Reagan fired 11,000 striking air traffic controllers, it signaled that union-busting was acceptable; private sector unions decline sharply through the 1980s - Reagan military buildup + Gorbachev's reforms → Cold War end (1989): the mechanisms of the Soviet collapse — Reagan's challenge + internal Soviet dysfunction + Gorbachev's glasnost and perestroika

Learning targets: - I can trace the cause-and-effect chain from Watergate to the post-Watergate reforms and changes in government trust. - I can explain how Reagan's labor policies changed the balance of power between workers and employers. - I can analyze the factors that led to the Cold War's end.

docs.title: US.6_12.4 + Unit 8 — Change Over Time
docs.text:
Standard: Compare how historical elements change over time.

In Unit 8 — FPS target: Develop an argument about a 1941–2001 topic (civil rights, nuclear power, technology/privacy, perceptions of political leaders, U.S. foreign policy) and how historical interpretation has changed over time.

Key comparisons: - Nixon's resignation → Bill Clinton's impeachment → Trump's two impeachments: how presidential accountability has evolved - 1973 oil crisis → 2020s energy debates: the politics of fossil fuel dependence - Reagan's trickle-down economics → current debates about taxation and wealth inequality - AIDS crisis government response → COVID-19 pandemic response: how does government respond when a disease initially affects a stigmatized group? - Environmental regulation (1970s EPA) → contemporary climate debates: what changed?

docs.title: US.6_12.5 + Unit 8 — Significant Contributions of People and Policy
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze the significant contributions of people, policy, and the influence on an era.

Key people and policies: | Person/Policy | Contribution | |--------------|-------------| | Richard Nixon | Watergate and resignation; also EPA, OSHA, Title IX, opening China, détente with Soviet Union — one of the most complicated presidential legacies | | Gerald Ford | Pardoned Nixon ("Our long national nightmare is over"); most unelected president in history; the pardon may have cost him the 1976 election | | Jimmy Carter | Energy policy, Camp David Accords, Iran hostage crisis; after presidency, Habitat for Humanity, election monitoring — arguably a better ex-president than president | | Ronald Reagan | Supply-side economics, PATCO, AIDS response failure, Iran-Contra, Cold War buildup, Berlin Wall speech | | Reaganomics | Supply-side/trickle-down economics: cut top marginal tax rates, deregulate industries, reduce government spending (except military) | | Iran-Contra | Illegal sale of weapons to Iran; profits funneled to Nicaraguan rebels (Contras) in violation of congressional ban; Oliver North took the fall |

docs.title: US.6_12.6 + Unit 8 — Past to Present
docs.text:
Standard: Connect the past to the present using current events.

Key connections: - Watergate → Nixon pardoned → debate about presidential accountability still unresolved in 2026 - Reaganomics → wealth inequality trends from 1980s to present; tax debates - AIDS crisis → contemporary healthcare disparities; LGBTQ rights movement - Environmental policy battles of 1970s–1980s → current climate debates - Cold War end (1989) → the 30-year post-Cold War era; Russia's re-emergence as adversary

docs.title: Key Vocabulary for Unit 8
docs.text: Term Definition Watergate The 1972 break-in at the Democratic National Committee headquarters, the subsequent cover-up, and Nixon's resignation when facing impeachment stagflation The combination of high inflation and high unemployment; seemed impossible under standard economic theory; characterized the 1970s détente The relaxation of Cold War tensions between the U.S. and Soviet Union under Nixon; included arms control agreements and opening of China relations Reaganomics Reagan's economic policy: cut top tax rates, deregulate industries, reduce social spending, build up military; also called "supply-side economics" or "trickle-down economics" Iran-Contra The illegal scheme in which Reagan administration officials sold weapons to Iran and used the profits to fund Nicaraguan Contras in violation of Congressional law PATCO Professional Air Traffic Controllers Organization; Reagan fired striking PATCO workers in 1981, signaling that union-busting was permissible glasnost Gorbachev's policy of political openness in the Soviet Union perestroika Gorbachev's policy of economic restructuring in the Soviet Union AIDS crisis The epidemic of acquired immunodeficiency syndrome; first identified in 1981 primarily in gay men; Reagan's government was slow to respond malaise Carter's term for America's "crisis of confidence" in 1979; his speech about it contributed to his electoral defeat

docs.title: Skinnies for Unit 8
docs.text: Standard Skinny US.1 Science & Tech Unit 8 Skinny US.2 Ideas & Beliefs Unit 8 Skinny US.3 Political Systems Unit 8 Skinny US.4 Social & Economic Unit 8 Skinny US.5 Causes & Effects Unit 8 Skinny US.6 Past to Present Unit 8 Skinny

docs.title: Related Standards
docs.text: ELA crosswalk standards most used in Unit 8: - W.4 — Argument writing (did Reaganomics help or hurt working Americans? was Nixon's Watergate resignation the right outcome?) - R.9 — Informational/argumentative analysis (Nixon/Reagan speeches, Watergate transcripts, competing economic policy arguments) - US.6_12.4 — Change over time (comparing 1970s stagflation/malaise to 1980s economic optimism) - IR.1–5 — Research bundle (active every studio)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Watergate established a principle that is still being tested: that no person in the U.S. government is above the law, and that the institutions designed to enforce that principle — the courts, Congress, the press — are only as strong as the people willing to use them. Nixon resigned because the system worked — but the system worked because specific people (the special prosecutor, the Senate committee, the Washington Post) chose to do their jobs at personal risk. The Reagan Revolution that followed reframed the core political question of American governance: is government the problem, or the solution? That debate — about regulation, taxes, the welfare state, and whether government can be trusted to solve social problems — is the central argument of U.S. domestic politics in 2026. See USH-06: When the Rules Don't Apply and The Handmaid's Tale.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Watergate established that presidents are not above the law and that the system of checks and balances will hold — but only if enough people in enough institutions refuse to go along with lawbreaking. Every subsequent presidential accountability debate asks whether Watergate's lesson was "the system works" or "the system barely worked and only because of specific accidents of personality and courage that may not repeat." Reagan's domestic legacy — deregulation, tax cuts for the wealthy, anti-union policy, defunding social services — produced structural changes that are still in effect and still debated: the decline of union membership correlates directly with rising inequality, and the income tax rates of the 1980s are the baseline every subsequent tax debate references. The Iran-Contra affair's lesson — that the executive branch will sometimes fund illegal operations while lying to Congress — is directly relevant to contemporary debates about executive power and congressional oversight. See USH-06: When the Rules Don't Apply for the studio built around accountability and institutional limits.

Unit 8: 1970s and 1980s — SDA Commons Wiki See also: USH Standards Overview · Unit 7 — Vietnam · Unit 9 — 1990s–2000s · Primary Sources

docs.title: Unit 9: 1990s and Early 2000s
docs.text: Course: English II + US History (Grade 10) Approximate period: 1991–2010 FPS Learning Goal: Connect the changing political and social climate to the United States' involvement as a global superpower.

docs.title: What This Unit Is About
docs.text:
The Cold War ended in 1991. For a moment, it seemed like the world would become simpler. It didn't.

The 1990s brought the internet, the longest economic expansion in U.S. history, and the sense that America had "won" the 20th century. But it also brought: the OJ Simpson trial as a national racial Rorschach test, the LA Riots, the Oklahoma City bombing, ethnic cleansing in Bosnia and Rwanda that the U.S. watched without intervening, and Bill Clinton's impeachment over a personal scandal.

Then September 11, 2001, changed everything. Two wars (Afghanistan and Iraq) that the U.S. is still processing. The PATRIOT Act and civil liberties debates that echo McCarthy-era fears. And the 2008 financial crisis, which revealed how fragile the global economic system built on deregulation actually was.

This unit covers: - Cold War end and its aftermath: Desert Storm (1990–91), the "peace dividend" - 1990s economy: technology boom, NAFTA, rising inequality despite prosperity - Social and political events: OJ Simpson trial, LA Riots (1992), Clinton impeachment - Rise of the internet and social media - 2000 election and its contested resolution (Bush v. Gore) - September 11, 2001: the attacks, immediate response, context - War on Terror: Afghanistan and Iraq invasions, WMDs that weren't there - PATRIOT Act and civil liberties - Great Recession (2008): causes, consequences, bailouts

docs.title: All Six Standards Applied to Unit 9
US.6_12.1 + Unit 9 — Primary and Secondary Sources
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze primary and secondary sources with attention to reliability, impact, and purpose.

In Unit 9: Analyze sources on the U.S. as global superpower and changing political/social climate.

Key sources: - NAFTA speeches: Clinton (pro) vs. Pat Buchanan (anti) — two different visions of globalization - George W. Bush's "Mission Accomplished" speech (May 2003) — a primary source for analyzing political messaging and accountability - Osama bin Laden's post-9/11 communications — understanding the other side's perspective - Congressional Authorization for Use of Military Force (AUMF, 2001) — the legal basis for two decades of war - Intelligence community assessment of WMDs in Iraq (2002) — what did the government know, what did it claim to know? - 2008 financial crisis: congressional testimony from bank executives; TARP documents

FPS Skill Strand: OEC — Connect the changing political and social climate to the United States' involvement as a global superpower.

Learning targets: - I can compare Clinton's and Buchanan's arguments about NAFTA and identify the evidence and reasoning each uses. - I can analyze the Bush administration's WMD claims using the intelligence documents that have since been released. - I can evaluate Bush's "Mission Accomplished" speech for what it claimed and what actually happened.

docs.title: US.6_12.2 + Unit 9 — Multiple Perspectives
docs.text:
Standard: Examine the impact of multiple perspectives on social, political, and cultural development.

In Unit 9: Examine multiple perspectives on U.S. global superpower status and the events of the 1990s–2000s.

Key perspectives: - The "unipolar moment" (American triumphalism): the U.S. won the Cold War; democracy and capitalism should spread globally; American military power should enforce this - International perspectives on U.S. power: many nations saw U.S. power as threatening rather than liberating; the Iraq invasion without UN Security Council approval alienated allies - Muslim world perspectives on 9/11 context: U.S. foreign policy (support for authoritarian governments, troops in Saudi Arabia, Israeli-Palestinian conflict) as the context for anti-American radicalization — distinct from endorsing terrorism - OJ Simpson trial and race: Black Americans disproportionately believed Simpson was innocent (given history of police evidence planting); white Americans disproportionately believed he was guilty; the same evidence, two different readings based on lived experience - The 2000 election: 537 votes in Florida; Supreme Court decision (Bush v. Gore); questions about democratic legitimacy that predate contemporary debates

Learning targets: - I can examine the 9/11 attacks from multiple perspectives, including context that explains (not justifies) the attacks. - I can analyze the OJ Simpson trial verdict through the lens of different racial communities' relationships with the justice system.

docs.title: US.6_12.3 + Unit 9 — Cause and Effect
docs.text:
Standard: Explain the relationship of events focusing on the link(s) between cause and effect.

In Unit 9 — FPS target: Connect the changing political and social climate to the United States' involvement as a global superpower.

Key cause-and-effect chains: - Cold War end → "peace dividend" debate → continuation of military spending: after the Soviet Union collapsed, the question was whether the U.S. would reduce military spending; instead, new threats were found - Desert Storm (1990–91) → U.S. troops in Saudi Arabia → al-Qaeda's stated grievances → 9/11: this is not a justification of the attacks; it is the causal chain that explains how they occurred - 9/11 attacks → PATRIOT Act → NSA surveillance → Snowden revelations (2013): the civil liberties trade-offs made in the name of security - 2001 AUMF → Afghanistan and Iraq wars → ongoing U.S. military involvement: the authorization that has never been repealed still legally justifies military action in 2026 - Deregulation of financial industry (1990s–2000s) → housing bubble → 2008 crash → Great Recession: the same logic that produced the 1929 crash produced the 2008 crash; what's different and what's the same?

Learning targets: - I can trace the causal chain from the Cold War's end to September 11. - I can explain how the decision to invade Iraq without WMDs being found changed the credibility of U.S. intelligence claims. - I can trace the cause-and-effect chain from financial deregulation to the 2008 crash.

docs.title: US.6_12.4 + Unit 9 — Change Over Time
docs.text:
Standard: Compare how historical elements change over time.

In Unit 9: Compare historical changes over time in the modern era.

Key comparisons: - The internet in 1995 → the internet in 2026: the same technology with completely different political and social implications - 1990s globalization enthusiasm (NAFTA) → 2016 anti-globalization politics (Trump, Brexit): what changed? - 9/11 civil liberties erosion → ongoing debates about surveillance, due process, habeas corpus - 2008 financial crisis → COVID-19 economic crisis → response comparison - Clinton impeachment (1998, perjury about an affair) → Trump impeachments (2019, 2021, different charges): how does the threshold for impeachment function?

docs.title: US.6_12.5 + Unit 9 — Significant Contributions of People and Policy
docs.text:
Standard: Analyze the significant contributions of people, policy, and the influence on an era.

In Unit 9 — FPS target: Project identifying a person who changed history during this era and a turning point in U.S. history during this era.

Key people and policies: | Person/Policy | Contribution | |--------------|-------------| | George H.W. Bush | Desert Storm coalition; NAFTA negotiation; handling of Cold War end; lost 1992 election to Clinton partly due to recession | | Bill Clinton | Longest peacetime economic expansion; NAFTA; welfare reform; "Don't Ask Don't Tell"; impeachment over Lewinsky affair | | George W. Bush | 9/11 response; Afghanistan invasion; Iraq invasion based on false WMD intelligence; PATRIOT Act; tax cuts; 2008 financial crisis under his watch | | NAFTA (1994) | North American Free Trade Agreement; eliminated tariffs between U.S., Canada, Mexico; boosted trade, eliminated manufacturing jobs in the U.S. | | PATRIOT Act (2001) | Expanded government surveillance powers; weakened Fourth Amendment protections; still largely in force | | 2008 TARP bailout | Troubled Asset Relief Program: $700 billion to rescue financial institutions; prevented collapse but created political backlash about "too big to fail" |

docs.title: US.6_12.6 + Unit 9 — Past to Present
docs.text:
Standard: Connect the past to the present using current events.

Key connections: - 9/11 → still shaping U.S. foreign policy, civil liberties debates, and Middle East policy in 2026 - 2008 financial crisis → rising populism (Occupy Wall Street, Tea Party), anti-establishment politics, Bernie Sanders, Trump - NAFTA → USMCA (2020 revision); ongoing globalization and manufacturing debates - Rise of social media → misinformation, election interference, polarization in 2024 and beyond - War on Terror → end of Afghanistan war (2021); ongoing questions about lessons learned - Obama presidency (2009–) as the beginning of this era's continuation → racial politics, healthcare (ACA)

docs.title: Key Vocabulary for Unit 9
docs.text: Term Definition Desert Storm The 1990–91 U.S.-led coalition war to expel Iraq from Kuwait; the first major post-Cold War military operation NAFTA North American Free Trade Agreement (1994); eliminated tariffs among the U.S., Canada, and Mexico; controversial for its effects on American manufacturing workers unipolar moment The brief post-Cold War period when the United States was the world's sole superpower with no rival September 11 The September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks by al-Qaeda on the World Trade Center and Pentagon; 2,977 killed; transformed U.S. foreign and domestic policy War on Terror The U.S.-led military and political campaign in response to 9/11; included invasions of Afghanistan (2001) and Iraq (2003) PATRIOT Act The 2001 law expanding government surveillance powers; weakened Fourth Amendment protections in the name of national security WMD Weapons of Mass Destruction; the stated primary justification for the 2003 Iraq invasion; no such weapons were found Great Recession The severe economic downturn (2007–2009) triggered by the housing market collapse and financial system crisis TARP Troubled Asset Relief Program; $700 billion government program that purchased toxic assets from banks to prevent financial system collapse subprime mortgage A mortgage loan issued to borrowers with poor credit; the proliferation of subprime mortgages and their bundling into financial products was a primary cause of the 2008 crash

docs.text: Standard Skinny US.1 Science & Tech Unit 9 Skinny US.2 Ideas & Beliefs Unit 9 Skinny US.3 Political Systems Unit 9 Skinny US.4 Social & Economic Unit 9 Skinny US.5 Causes & Effects Unit 9 Skinny US.6 Past to Present Unit 9 Skinny

  • CrashCourse US History Index — available USH episodes

docs.title: Related Standards
docs.text: ELA crosswalk standards most used in Unit 9: - W.4 — Argument writing (was the Iraq War justified? has globalization been a net positive for Americans?) - R.9 — Informational/argumentative analysis (Clinton/Bush speeches, 9/11 Commission excerpts, competing globalization arguments) - US.6_12.6 — Past to present (most of Unit 9 events still shape today's foreign policy, economy, and politics) - IR.1–5 — Research bundle (active every studio)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Unit 9 ends in the early 2000s, but the decisions made in that era are still producing consequences: the wars in Afghanistan and Iraq, the surveillance infrastructure built after 9/11, the financial deregulation that produced the 2008 crash, and the globalization that restructured American manufacturing are all present-tense facts. The "unipolar moment" — when the U.S. had more military and economic power than any nation in history and had to decide what to do with it — produced choices whose consequences were not clear at the time and are still being evaluated. Whether American global power has been, on balance, a stabilizing or destabilizing force; whether the post-Cold War optimism about democracy and free markets was justified; and what the U.S. owes to people in countries affected by its decisions — these are the core questions of contemporary foreign policy. See USH-07: The Unipolar Moment and The Kite Runner.

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
9/11 and its aftermath are not historical in the sense of being resolved. The PATRIOT Act's surveillance authorities, the Authorization for Use of Military Force (AUMF) that legally underpins the "War on Terror," the Guantanamo detention facility, and the drone strike program are all still active. The legal frameworks created in 2001–2003 have never been fully unwound — and many students in high school today were born after 9/11, making this the background noise of their entire lives rather than a chapter in a textbook. The internet's mass commercialization, the globalization of supply chains, and the financial deregulation that led to 2008 are all Unit 9 stories that continue to shape the economy students are entering. The question of what the U.S. should do with its superpower status — posed most sharply after the Cold War ended — is the question the entire world has been trying to answer since 1991. See USH-07: The Unipolar Moment for the studio built around this era.

Unit 9: 1990s and Early 2000s — SDA Commons Wiki See also: USH Standards Overview · Unit 8 — 1970s & 1980s · Primary Sources

docs.title: Era 1: Emergence of Civilizations and Religions Around the Globe
docs.text: Course: English I + World History (Grade 9) Era span: Prehistory through approximately 600 CE Studios that use this era: Studio 1 — Power & Belief · Studio 2 — Builders & Breakers

docs.title: What This Era Is About
docs.text:
Before nations, before borders, before the internet, before books — human beings figured out how to organize large societies. That took about ten thousand years and happened simultaneously in multiple places across the globe.

Era 1 covers the emergence of: - Agriculture — when humans stopped following food and started growing it (Fertile Crescent, Yellow River Valley, Indus Valley, Mesoamerica) - Cities and civilizations — Mesopotamia (Sumer, Babylon, Assyria), Egypt, the Indus Valley, China's first dynasties, Greece, Rome - Writing systems — cuneiform, hieroglyphics, early Chinese characters — because complex societies need records - Political systems — from tribal leadership to city-states to empires; from Hammurabi's Code to Roman law to Greek democracy - World religions — the foundational belief systems that still shape billions of lives: Hinduism (earliest organized), Judaism, Buddhism, Christianity, Islam (emerging at the end of this era) - Science and technology — irrigation systems, astronomical observation, mathematics, medical practices, engineering (the Colosseum, the Pyramids, the Great Wall's early forms) - Trade networks — the beginning of global economic connection: early Silk Road routes, Mediterranean sea trade, trans-Saharan connections

The essential question running through this era: What does it take to build a society — and what does it cost?

docs.title: All Six Standards Applied to Era 1
WH.6_12.1 + Era 1 — Science and Technology of Early Civilizations
docs.text:
Full standard: Analyze historical achievements related to science and technology.

In Era 1, this means: Analyze the scientific and technological achievements of early civilizations and explain how they shaped society and human development.

Key topics: - Mesopotamian contributions: writing (cuneiform), the wheel, the plow, irrigation systems, the 60-minute hour - Egyptian contributions: pyramids (engineering + mathematics), papyrus, embalming/medicine, astronomy for calendar - Greek contributions: geometry (Euclid), logic (Aristotle), the foundations of Western medicine (Hippocrates), the atomic theory (Democritus) - Roman contributions: concrete, aqueducts, roads, the Julian calendar - Chinese contributions: papermaking, bronze casting, silk production, early gunpowder precursors - Indus Valley: advanced urban planning, sewage systems, standardized weights and measures

FPS Skill Strand: OEC (Obtain, Evaluate, Communicate) Students read complex informational texts about early scientific and technological achievements and support inferences with textual evidence.

ELA connection: 9-10.R.2 — Reading complex informational texts, developing inferences with textual evidence.

Learning targets: - I can identify a specific technological achievement from an early civilization and explain how it worked. - I can analyze how that achievement changed how people lived (not just what it was, but what it did to society). - I can connect a specific early technology to a present-day equivalent or descendant.

Example inquiry angles: - "How did Roman engineering (aqueducts, concrete, roads) make empire expansion possible — and what happened to infrastructure when the empire collapsed?" - "How did the development of writing systems change the nature of political power? (You can't enforce a law you can't write down.)" - "How did Islamic Golden Age mathematical and astronomical discoveries preserve and advance Greek knowledge during Europe's early medieval period?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.2 + Era 1 — Religions and Ideologies of Early Civilizations
docs.text:
Full standard: Explain historical changes related to religions and ideologies.

In Era 1, this means: Explain how early belief systems developed and shaped civilizations — how religion and politics, religion and law, religion and daily life were often inseparable.

Key topics: - Hinduism — oldest continuous religious tradition; caste system, dharma, karma, multiple deities as aspects of one divine reality; emerged in Indus Valley, developed in Vedic period - Judaism — monotheism, covenant, law (Torah), the concept of a chosen people; Babylonian exile; diaspora; foundational to Christianity and Islam - Buddhism — founded by Siddhartha Gautama (~500 BCE); Four Noble Truths, Noble Eightfold Path, concept of impermanence; spread along Silk Road trade routes into China, Southeast Asia - Christianity — rooted in Judaism; Jesus of Nazareth; Pauline missions spreading throughout Roman Empire; Constantine's Edict of Milan (313 CE) and Christianity as Roman state religion - Early Islam — Muhammad's revelations beginning 610 CE; the Quran; five pillars; immediate political unification of Arabian Peninsula; rapid spread after Muhammad's death (632 CE) - Greek and Roman polytheism — gods as personifications of natural forces and human qualities; religious festivals as political events; the relationship between civic religion and political authority (the emperor as divine or semi-divine) - Confucianism — not a religion in the traditional sense, but a framework for social and political order (filial piety, hierarchy, virtue in governance); shaped Chinese bureaucracy for millennia

FPS Skill Strand: Argumentation Students write explanatory pieces describing how early belief systems developed and shaped civilizations.

ELA connection: 9-10.W.3 — Explanatory writing with a clear thesis and evidence from multiple reliable sources.

Learning targets: - I can explain the origins and core beliefs of at least two early world religions. - I can explain how a specific religion shaped the political structure or social organization of a civilization. - I can identify a place where religious authority and political authority were fused — and analyze what that fusion meant for ordinary people.

Example inquiry angles: - "How did the concept of divine kingship (pharaoh in Egypt, emperor in China, Roman emperor-gods) function as a political tool?" - "How did early Islam create a political system at the same time it created a religious one — and why did that dual nature make it spread so fast?" - "How did Confucian values shape the Chinese civil service examination system, and how did that change who could hold power?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.3 + Era 1 — Political Systems and Their Effects on People
docs.text:
Full standard: Analyze the effects of different political systems on people.

In Era 1, this means: Analyze how early political systems — from city-states to empires, from law codes to democratic assemblies — affected the daily lives, rights, and freedoms of the people living under them.

Key topics: - Hammurabi's Code (~1750 BCE) — one of the earliest written legal codes; establishes the principle that law should be public and knowable; also reveals stark class and gender inequalities ("an eye for an eye" meant different things for slaves vs. free citizens) - Athenian democracy — limited to adult male citizens (excludes women, slaves, foreigners); but establishes the radical idea that ordinary citizens should have a voice in government; the Assembly, the Agora, ostracism - The Roman Republic and Empire — Senate and popular assemblies in the Republic; gradual slide to one-man rule; how Roman citizenship was used as a tool of empire (extend citizenship to control conquered peoples) - Chinese imperial system — Mandate of Heaven as justification for rule; Han Dynasty centralization; the bureaucracy; peasant obligations and rebellions - Mesopotamian city-states — early experiments in governance; different systems in different cities; the ziggurat as political and religious center - Caste system in early India — a political-religious system that determined occupation, marriage, and social status by birth; Brahmin (priests), Kshatriya (warriors), Vaishya (merchants), Shudra (laborers), Dalits (untouchables)

FPS Skill Strand: Interpretation & Analysis Students analyze political texts describing early governments and evaluate perspective and bias.

ELA connection: 9-10.R.9a — Analyze the author's point of view or purpose and possible biases; compare texts from differing perspectives.

Learning targets: - I can explain how a specific early political system determined what rights people had. - I can analyze Hammurabi's Code (or another primary source) for what it reveals about who had power and who didn't. - I can explain how political systems in Greece and Rome connect to present-day democratic systems.

Example inquiry angles: - "Is Hammurabi's Code evidence of justice or evidence of oppression? (Argue using the text itself.)" - "How did Roman citizenship work as a political tool — and what does that tell us about empire-building?" - "Greek democracy excluded women and enslaved people. Does that make it not actually democratic, or is it still meaningful as a political innovation?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.4 + Era 1 — Social, Cultural, and Economic Development
docs.text:
Full standard: Analyze the influence of social, cultural, and economic development on individuals.

In Era 1, this means: Analyze how the shift from hunter-gatherer to agricultural to urban society changed what individual human lives looked like — and how social stratification, trade, and cultural exchange affected ordinary people.

Key topics: - The agricultural revolution — settled farming changes everything: food surplus → population growth → specialization → social hierarchy → writing (to track the surplus) → civilization - Social stratification — every early civilization develops class systems; priests, warriors, merchants, farmers, artisans, slaves — how did your birth position determine your life? - Trade networks — Silk Road (China to Rome), trans-Saharan routes, Mediterranean sea trade; what moved along with goods: ideas, diseases, religions, technologies - Cultural diffusion — how civilizations influenced each other through trade, conquest, and migration; the spread of alphabets, crops, religious ideas, technologies - Gender and social roles — women in early civilizations had varying levels of rights depending on the society; Egyptian women had more legal rights than Greek women; Mesopotamian women could own property; Roman women's status changed across centuries - Slavery — present in virtually every early civilization; different forms (chattel, debt, war captive); how slavery shaped the economy and social structure

FPS Skill Strand: Interpretation & Analysis Students identify and support central ideas about how social and economic structures shaped daily life.

ELA connection: 9-10.R.4 — Determine main ideas, claims, or themes as they develop over the course of a text and support with textual evidence.

Learning targets: - I can explain how the development of agriculture changed social structures in early civilizations. - I can analyze how one social, cultural, or economic influence from an early civilization affected individual lives. - I can connect a social structure from Era 1 (trade network, caste system, gender roles) to its influence on the present.

Example inquiry angles: - "How did the development of agricultural surplus create social inequality? (Use at least two civilizations.)" - "How did the Silk Road change what people believed — not just what they owned?" - "What did it mean to be a woman in Athens vs. Egypt vs. Rome? (What does the variation tell us about the relationship between economic systems and gender rights?)"

docs.title: WH.6_12.5 + Era 1 — Causes and Effects Using Primary and Secondary Sources
docs.text:
Full standard: Analyze causes and effects of global events using primary and/or secondary sources.

In Era 1, this means: Use primary and secondary sources to investigate major global events of the ancient world — the rise and fall of empires, the spread of religions, the development of trade — and analyze what caused them and what they caused.

Key topics + events to investigate: - The fall of the Western Roman Empire (~476 CE) — multiple causes: military overextension, economic decline, internal political instability, external pressure from Germanic tribes; the effects: feudalism in Europe, preservation of Roman legacy in Eastern Empire (Byzantine) - The rise of Islamic civilization — causes: Muhammad's life and teachings, tribal unity, Byzantine-Persian exhaustion after prolonged war; effects: rapid spread of a new religion and political system across North Africa, Middle East, Spain within 100 years of Muhammad's death - The Mongol conquests — in Era 1/Era 2 overlap: causes in Mongolian steppe conditions and Genghis Khan's unification; effects on trade routes, plague spread, cultural destruction and preservation - The Punic Wars — Rome vs. Carthage; causes in Mediterranean trade competition; effects in Roman domination of the Mediterranean and the destruction of Carthaginian civilization - The Peloponnesian War — Athens vs. Sparta; causes in Athenian empire-building and Spartan fear; effects in Greek political fragmentation and the eventual vulnerability to Macedonian conquest

FPS Skill Strand: OEC Students compare multiple historical accounts of early global developments to analyze cause-and-effect relationships.

ELA connection: 9-10.R.9b — Analyze various accounts of a subject told in different media, including determining which details are emphasized or omitted in each account.

Learning targets: - I can identify the causes and effects of a significant global event in Era 1 using both a primary and secondary source. - I can compare two accounts of the same event and explain what each one emphasizes or omits. - I can explain why different sources might present the same event differently.

Example inquiry angles: - "The fall of Rome: was it caused by internal rot or external invasion? (Compare Roman sources vs. modern historians.)" - "How do Islamic accounts of Muhammad's early community differ from Western European accounts of early Islamic expansion?" - "What do the sources that survive from ancient civilizations tell us about who had the power to create records?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.6 + Era 1 — Past to Present
docs.text:
Full standard: Explain how past events connect to the present.

In Era 1, this means: Identify the greatest social, cultural, or economic events of early civilizations and analyze how those events still influence or inform the present day.

Key connections: - Roman law and governance → U.S. Constitutional structure (Senate, checks and balances, rule of law) - Greek democracy → modern democratic theory and practice - World religions originating in Era 1 (Christianity, Islam, Judaism, Hinduism, Buddhism) → still the dominant belief systems of the 21st century - The agricultural revolution → the food system, land ownership, and rural-urban divides that still shape economics - Early trade networks → the logic of globalization; Silk Road trade anticipates container shipping - Hammurabi's Code → the principle that law should be written, public, and knowable — foundational to modern legal systems

FPS Skill Strand: Argumentation Students present connections between early civilizations and modern systems.

ELA connection: 9-10.C.1 — Construct and deliver formal and informal presentations incorporating multimedia when appropriate.

Learning targets: - I can identify a specific development from Era 1 and trace a direct connection to a present-day institution, belief, or practice. - I can present that connection to an audience using a format appropriate to the claim. - I can answer questions about the connection, including challenges to it.

Example inquiry angles: - "How does the Roman concept of citizenship still shape who gets rights in the United States?" - "How do the foundational texts of Judaism, Christianity, and Islam still influence political decisions in 2026?" - "The Silk Road is over — or is it? How does China's Belt and Road Initiative (BRI) reproduce the logic of ancient trade networks?"

docs.title: Key Vocabulary for Era 1
docs.text: Term Definition civilization A complex society with cities, a government, social stratification, writing, and specialized labor city-state An independent city that governs itself and surrounding territory (Athens, Sparta, Uruk) polytheism Belief in multiple gods monotheism Belief in one god theocracy A government ruled by religious authority or in the name of a deity democracy Government by the people (in Athens: by some adult male citizens) republic Government where power is held by elected representatives, not a monarch empire A large territory controlled by one central power, often through conquest code of law A written, publicly available legal system (Hammurabi's Code is the earliest surviving example) caste system A hereditary social hierarchy where birth determines your occupation and social status trade network Routes along which goods, people, and ideas move between civilizations cultural diffusion The spread of ideas, practices, and technologies from one culture to another social stratification The division of society into layers (classes) with different levels of power and access agricultural revolution The transition from nomadic hunter-gatherer life to settled farming (began ~10,000 BCE)

docs.title: Skinnies for Era 1
docs.text:
• WH.6_12.2 Belief Systems Skinny — How Early Belief Systems Shaped Civilization (WH.6_12.2 + R.9)
  • WH.6_12.3 Political Systems Skinny — Political Systems in Ancient Greece and Rome (WH.6_12.3 + W.4)

docs.title: Reading Library — Era 1 Texts
docs.text:
• Hammurabi's Code — Hammurabi's Code (c. 1754 BCE) · WH.6_12.2 + WH.6_12.3 · Primary Source
  • ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk — ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk master table
