  • CC — Silk Road — The Silk Road (WH.6_12.4 + Era 1)

docs.title: Related Standards
docs.text: ELA crosswalk standards most used in Era 1: - R.9 — Informational/argumentative analysis (primary source work) - W.4 — Argument writing (is Hammurabi's justice system just?) - R.7 — Style and word choice (analyzing Hammurabi's legal language)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The questions Era 1 raises — who should govern, what authority is legitimate, what a civilization owes its people — are being argued in every democracy today. Hammurabi's Code asked who the law was actually for; that question is at the center of criminal justice reform debates right now. Confucian meritocracy is the basis of civil service systems across East Asia and has influenced debates about college admissions and credentialism in the U.S. Greek democracy and the Athenian empire exist side by side in the same texts — the same tension between self-governance at home and coercion abroad that characterizes every modern great power. The world religions that emerged in Era 1 — Hinduism, Buddhism, Judaism, Christianity, Islam — are not historical artifacts; they shape the political worldviews of billions of people and drive conflicts from the Middle East to South Asia to American school board meetings. Nothing in Era 1 is settled. See WH-01: Who Should Govern? for the studio that works most directly with these questions.

Era 1: Emergence of Civilizations — SDA Commons Wiki See also: WH Standards Overview · Era 2 — Middle Ages · Primary Sources

docs.title: Era 2: Middle Ages and the Renaissance
docs.text: Course: English I + World History (Grade 9) Era span: Approximately 500–1600 CE Studios that use this era: Studio 3 — The Medieval Crisis · Studio 4 — Renaissance & Rebirth

docs.title: What This Era Is About
docs.text:
When the Western Roman Empire collapsed in 476 CE, it left a power vacuum in Europe that would take centuries to fill. The Middle Ages (also called the Medieval period) is what happened during that filling-in: feudal hierarchies replaced Roman administration, the Catholic Church became the dominant organizing institution of Western Europe, and the remnants of Roman culture were preserved in monasteries and — crucially — in the Islamic world, which was in the middle of its own Golden Age.

Era 2 covers: - Feudalism — the political-economic system of medieval Europe: kings, lords, knights, serfs, and the obligations that bound them together - The Catholic Church's political dominance — popes who could excommunicate kings, the Inquisition, the Crusades as religiously justified warfare - The Islamic Golden Age — while Europe was in its early medieval period, Islamic civilization preserved Greek knowledge and added to it: algebra, astronomy, medicine, philosophy - The Crusades — religiously motivated wars for control of Jerusalem; also a catalyst for European-Islamic cultural exchange - The Black Death — the bubonic plague that killed one-third of Europe's population in the 14th century and destabilized the entire social order - Medieval trade networks — Silk Road at its height, Saharan trade routes, Viking exploration and trade, Hanseatic League - The Renaissance — beginning in Italian city-states (~1300), spreading north: humanism, perspective in art, the printing press, a revival of classical learning, and the seeds of the Reformation

The essential question running through this era: When does order protect people — and when does it control them?

docs.title: All Six Standards Applied to Era 2
WH.6_12.1 + Era 2 — Science and Technology of the Middle Ages and Renaissance
docs.text:
Full standard: Analyze historical achievements related to science and technology.

In Era 2, this means: Analyze the scientific and technological achievements of both the Islamic Golden Age and the Renaissance, and explain how they preserved, advanced, and eventually transformed human knowledge.

Key topics: - Islamic Golden Age contributions (~800–1200 CE): algebra (al-Khwarizmi), optics (Ibn al-Haytham), encyclopedic medicine (Ibn Sina/Avicenna), astronomical observation, preservation and translation of Greek texts, paper mills - Medieval European technology: heavy plow enabling northern European agriculture, water mills, windmills, Gothic cathedrals (an engineering achievement), advances in metallurgy and glassmaking - The printing press (Gutenberg, ~1440) — possibly the most significant technology of Era 2; mass production of text destroys the Church's information monopoly; Bibles, pamphlets, scientific texts all become accessible; the Protestant Reformation is unimaginable without it - Navigation technology: astrolabe, magnetic compass, improved ship design (lateen sail enabling sailing against the wind) — all enabling the Age of Exploration at the era's end - Renaissance scientific thought: Leonardo da Vinci's anatomical drawings and engineering sketches; Copernicus's heliocentric model (1543) challenging Church cosmology

FPS Skill Strand: OEC Students read complex informational texts from Middle Ages and Renaissance and support inferences with textual evidence.

ELA connection: 9-10.R.2 — Comprehend complex informational texts, develop inferences, and provide relevant textual evidence.

Learning targets: - I can explain a specific scientific or technological achievement from the Islamic Golden Age and analyze its impact on later European thought. - I can explain how the printing press changed the flow of information and why that mattered. - I can analyze how Renaissance thinkers challenged received knowledge and what the consequences were.

Example inquiry angles: - "How did Islamic scholars preserve and expand on Greek mathematics and science — and what happened to that knowledge when it reached Europe?" - "The printing press is often called the first 'disruptive technology.' What did it disrupt, who benefited, and who was threatened?" - "How did Copernicus's heliocentric model challenge not just astronomy but the entire worldview of the Catholic Church?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.2 + Era 2 — Religions and Ideologies of the Middle Ages and Renaissance
docs.text:
Full standard: Explain historical changes related to religions and ideologies.

In Era 2, this means: Explain how religious institutions changed — particularly how the Catholic Church rose to unprecedented power, then how that power began to fracture — and how new ideologies (humanism, early nationalism) began to challenge religious authority.

Key topics: - The Catholic Church's political power: Pope Gregory VII and the Investiture Controversy (who controls who); Pope Innocent III at the height of papal authority; excommunication as a political weapon - The Crusades (~1095–1291): Pope Urban II's call; the People's Crusade; the capture and loss of Jerusalem; the Fourth Crusade's sack of Constantinople (1204); the long-term destruction of Christian-Muslim relations - The Great Schism (1054): formal split between Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox churches; a division that still shapes global Christianity - The Avignon Papacy and Western Schism — popes moving to France; multiple simultaneous popes; the Church's credibility in crisis before the Reformation - Humanism — the Renaissance philosophical movement placing human beings and their potential at the center (not just God and sin); Petrarch, Erasmus, Pico della Mirandola - Early challenges to Church authority: John Wycliffe (England), Jan Hus (Bohemia) — both burned or condemned for criticizing Church corruption - The end of this era's religious stability: Luther's 95 Theses (1517) is technically Era 3, but the ground for it is entirely prepared in Era 2

FPS Skill Strand: Argumentation Students write explanatory texts describing shifts in religious power and ideology.

ELA connection: 9-10.W.1 and W.2 — Write clearly and coherently with a logical organizational structure.

Learning targets: - I can explain how the Catholic Church accumulated political power during the Middle Ages and analyze the methods it used. - I can identify at least two historical changes in religious thought or ideology during Era 2. - I can explain the emergence of cultural interaction between Europe and other civilizations through this era.

Example inquiry angles: - "How did the Crusades change both European and Islamic society — and not in the ways either side intended?" - "How did Renaissance humanism represent a challenge to medieval Christianity without initially intending to be one?" - "The Church's political power was at its height in 1200 and in crisis by 1500. What changed?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.3 + Era 2 — Political Systems and Their Effects on People
docs.text:
Full standard: Analyze the effects of different political systems on people.

In Era 2, this means: Analyze how feudalism, the Church's political authority, and emerging nation-states affected the lives of ordinary people — what they owed, what they could do, and what they had no choice about.

Key topics: - Feudalism's structure: king → lords → knights → serfs; the manorial system; what each level owed to those above and below; how feudal contracts worked - What feudalism meant for serfs: tied to the land; unable to leave without permission; owed labor and crops to the lord; few legal rights; but also: protection, land use, community structure - The Magna Carta (1215) — English barons forced King John to sign a document limiting royal power; for the first time in English history, the principle that even the king is subject to law; seeds of modern constitutionalism - Church courts and Church law — the Church had its own legal system (canon law); clergy could only be tried in Church courts (benefice of clergy); powerful people could appeal to Church courts instead of royal courts - The Holy Roman Empire — the complex, decentralized political structure of Central Europe; the conflict between popes and emperors over who had ultimate authority - Italian city-states — by 1300, northern Italian cities (Florence, Venice, Genoa, Milan) are economically powerful enough to operate as independent republics; merchant class rises; early capitalism; patronage of art

FPS Skill Strand: Argumentation Students argue which political systems or institutions most affected individuals' lives.

ELA connection: 9-10.W.4 — Argumentative writing; students argue whether feudal political systems improved or harmed ordinary people's lives.

Learning targets: - I can explain what feudalism required of people at different levels of the hierarchy. - I can analyze how the Magna Carta represented a shift in the principle of political authority. - I can evaluate the growth of political institutions from the Middle Ages to the Renaissance.

Example inquiry angles: - "Was feudalism a protection system or an exploitation system? (Argue using specific evidence about serfs' obligations AND protections.)" - "How did the Magna Carta matter for ordinary English people — or did it? (Who benefited from the 1215 document, and who didn't?)" - "How did Italian city-state republicanism preview later European political development?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.4 + Era 2 — Social, Cultural, and Economic Development
docs.text:
Full standard: Analyze the influence of social, cultural, and economic development on individuals.

In Era 2, this means: Analyze how feudalism, the medieval guild system, trade networks, the Black Death, and the cultural explosion of the Renaissance affected individual lives — what people could aspire to, what they produced, who they interacted with.

Key topics: - The guild system — medieval trade associations controlling the production and sale of goods; apprentice → journeyman → master; economic protection AND economic control; guilds determined who could work in a trade - Silk Road at its height — 8th–13th centuries; not just silk and spices but glass, paper, mathematics, disease, and religion flowing in both directions; the Mongol Empire (early Era 2/Era 3 overlap) briefly made the entire route safe under "Pax Mongolica" - The Black Death (~1347–1351) — bubonic plague from Central Asia, carried by fleas on rats; killed 30–50% of Europe's population; shattered the feudal order (fewer peasants = peasants with more bargaining power); accelerated the end of serfdom in Western Europe; psychological devastation; anti-Jewish pogroms (Jews blamed for plague) - Renaissance art and culture — Florentine banking families (Medici) as patrons; perspective in painting (Brunelleschi, Masaccio); humanist literature (Petrarch, Dante, Boccaccio); the Decameron as a window into Black Death society; Michelangelo, Leonardo, Raphael - Women in medieval and Renaissance society — heavily circumscribed by Church doctrine; but noblewomen had some agency through arranged marriages; convents as the one pathway to literacy and administrative roles; Christine de Pizan as a rare female writer and intellectual - Trans-Saharan trade — gold and salt trade; rise of Mali Empire (Mansa Musa); Islamic scholarship in Timbuktu; how West African societies connected to the broader Islamic world

FPS Skill Strand: Interpretation & Analysis Students explain how feudalism, trade, or cultural movements influenced people's lives.

ELA connection: 9-10.W.3 — Explanatory writing about how feudalism, trade, or cultural movements influenced people's lives.

Learning targets: - I can explain how the Black Death disrupted feudal social structures. - I can analyze how the guild system controlled economic life for medieval workers. - I can evaluate contributions of the Middle Ages and Renaissance to social, cultural, and economic development.

Example inquiry angles: - "How did the Black Death change what peasants and workers could demand — and what does that tell us about how social power works?" - "How did the Medici banking family's wealth reshape what art looked like and what ideas were considered acceptable to express?" - "Why did the Renaissance start in Italian city-states specifically — and what economic conditions made that possible?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.5 + Era 2 — Causes and Effects Using Primary and Secondary Sources
docs.text:
Full standard: Analyze causes and effects of global events using primary and/or secondary sources.

In Era 2, this means: Use primary and secondary sources to analyze the causes and effects of major medieval and Renaissance events — especially the Crusades, the Black Death, and the fall of Constantinople.

Key events to investigate: - The Crusades — causes: Pope Urban II's 1095 call, Muslim control of Jerusalem, European feudal surplus of fighting-age men; effects: cultural exchange, intensified anti-Semitism in Europe, long-term Muslim-Christian hostility, trade routes opened, military orders (Knights Templar) - The Black Death — causes: Central Asian origins, trade routes as vectors, poor sanitation in medieval cities, flea-rat-human transmission; effects: demographic collapse, labor market shift, psychological transformation, accelerated end of serfdom - Fall of Constantinople (1453) — causes: Ottoman military expansion, Eastern Roman Empire's long decline, failure of Western Crusader support; effects: end of Byzantine Empire, Ottoman control of eastern trade routes (motivating European interest in sea routes to Asia), Greek scholars flee to Italy carrying classical texts (fueling Renaissance) - The Hundred Years War (1337–1453) — English-French conflict over territory and succession; effects include rise of French national identity, Joan of Arc, decline of feudal cavalry, rise of professional armies

FPS Skill Strand: Argumentation + OEC Students analyze differing accounts of events like the Crusades or Black Death to evaluate causes and consequences.

ELA connection: 9-10.R.9a and R.9b — Analyze author's POV and bias; analyze various accounts of a subject told in different media.

Learning targets: - I can use both a primary and secondary source to analyze the causes and effects of one major Era 2 event. - I can compare two accounts of the same event (e.g., a Crusader chronicle vs. an Islamic historian's account) and explain what each emphasizes or omits. - I can identify recurring historical themes across Era 1 and Era 2 (e.g., religion justifying violence; plague reshaping social structures).

Example inquiry angles: - "The Crusaders and Islamic historians tell the story of the Crusades very differently. What does each version emphasize — and why?" - "Was the Black Death primarily a medical event or a social and political one? (Use evidence from multiple types of sources.)" - "How did the fall of Constantinople in 1453 accidentally accelerate the Renaissance and eventually the Age of Exploration?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.6 + Era 2 — Past to Present
docs.text:
Full standard: Explain how past events connect to the present.

In Era 2, this means: Identify the greatest social, cultural, or economic events of the Medieval and Renaissance era and analyze how those events influenced or informed the present day.

Key connections: - Magna Carta → modern constitutionalism: the principle that authority is subject to law; foundational to English common law, U.S. Constitution, Universal Declaration of Human Rights - Feudal social structure → class systems in modern economies: the ways wealth is inherited and concentrated; land ownership patterns in Europe still partially trace to feudal grants - Printing press → information technology and democratic culture: the logic of the printing press anticipates the internet — both technologies democratize access to information and destabilize existing power structures - The Crusades → Muslim-Christian relations today: ongoing patterns of mistrust and conflict with roots in medieval history; the language of "crusade" still politically charged - Renaissance humanism → Enlightenment → modern liberal values: individual rights, secular governance, scientific rationalism all trace through Renaissance humanist thought - Islamic Golden Age → European Renaissance: the transmission of mathematics, science, and philosophy from the Islamic world to Europe is a direct ancestor of modern STEM fields

FPS Skill Strand: Argumentation Students participate in discussions connecting medieval and Renaissance developments to modern institutions.

ELA connection: 9-10.C.6 — Engage in respectful discussions or debates.

Learning targets: - I can identify a specific medieval or Renaissance development and trace its direct influence on present-day institutions, ideas, or conflicts. - I can contribute to a discussion about past-to-present connections using evidence from my research. - I can respond to a peer's argument with a question or counterpoint that advances the discussion.

Example inquiry angles: - "The Magna Carta is 800 years old. Which of its principles still function as law in the United States today?" - "How do the Crusades still shape the politics and culture of the Middle East and Europe?" - "Is the printing press or the internet the more significant information revolution? (What does the comparison reveal about how information technology changes power?)"

docs.title: Key Vocabulary for Era 2
docs.text: Term Definition feudalism The political-economic system of medieval Europe based on the exchange of land for military service and labor serf A feudal peasant bound to the land; not legally enslaved, but unable to leave without the lord's permission lord A feudal landowner who held land from a king and owed military service in return knight A mounted warrior who owed military service to a lord in exchange for land (a fief) fief Land granted by a lord to a vassal in exchange for military service and loyalty monastery A community of monks living under religious rules; preserved classical knowledge through the early medieval period Crusades A series of religiously sanctioned military campaigns (~1095–1291) by European Christians to recapture the Holy Land from Muslim control Magna Carta A 1215 charter signed by King John of England, limiting royal power and establishing the principle that the king is subject to the law guild A medieval association of craftspeople or merchants controlling production and trade in a specific craft or industry Black Death The bubonic plague that killed approximately 30–50% of Europe's population between 1347 and 1351 Renaissance The "rebirth" of classical learning and arts in Europe, beginning in Italian city-states (~1300) and spreading north through 1600 humanism The Renaissance intellectual movement emphasizing human potential, classical learning, and reason rather than religious dogma patron A wealthy person who financially supported artists, architects, and scholars (e.g., the Medici family in Florence) Ottoman Empire The Turkish empire that conquered Constantinople in 1453 and controlled much of the Middle East, North Africa, and southeastern Europe Silk Road The network of trade routes connecting China to the Mediterranean world; at its height during Era 2

docs.title: Related Standards
docs.text: ELA crosswalk standards most used in Era 2: - W.4 — Argument writing (is feudal order just? was the Church's power legitimate?) - R.9 — Informational/argumentative analysis (Magna Carta, papal decrees, chronicler accounts) - R.2 — Comprehension and inference (reading medieval primary sources) - IR.1–5 — Research bundle (active every studio; primary source annotation)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • Universal Declaration of Human Rights

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The Middle Ages invented institutions that still structure modern life: universities, hospitals, international banking, the concept of legal due process, the early arguments for limits on state power. The Magna Carta's argument that even kings must operate within law is the ancestor of every written constitution. The Reformation's argument that individuals can interpret sacred texts directly — without institutional intermediaries — is the ancestor of every argument about personal authority over institutional expertise, including contemporary debates about science, medicine, and media. The conflict between religious authority and political authority that dominated medieval Europe is still being fought in every debate about the role of religious institutions in public life. Ibn Battuta's travels remind us that the medieval world was globally connected long before European exploration — and that "globalization" is not a modern invention. See WH-03: Writing Against Empire and the Magna Carta in the Reading Library.

Era 2: Middle Ages and Renaissance — SDA Commons Wiki See also: WH Standards Overview · Era 1 — Civilizations · Era 3 — Revolutions · Primary Sources

docs.title: Era 3: Age of Revolutions
docs.text: Course: English I + World History (Grade 9) Era span: Approximately 1400–1900 CE Studios that use this era: Studio 5 — The Age of Revolution

docs.title: What This Era Is About
docs.text:
If Era 2 is about the old order of church, king, and feudal hierarchy — Era 3 is about all of that breaking apart.

The Age of Revolutions is one of the most compressed periods of change in human history. In roughly five centuries, the world was turned upside down:

  • European explorers "discovered" the Americas (from their perspective) and set off centuries of colonization, genocide, and the transatlantic slave trade
  • The Protestant Reformation shattered the Catholic Church's monopoly on religious authority in Europe
  • The Scientific Revolution replaced theology with observation as the basis for understanding the natural world
  • The Enlightenment generated the idea that people have natural rights — and that governments derive legitimacy from the consent of the governed
  • Those ideas produced actual revolutions: American (1776), French (1789), Haitian (1791), Latin American (1810s–1820s)
  • The Industrial Revolution transformed economies from agricultural to manufacturing and created the modern working class
  • Imperialism spread European power across Africa, Asia, and the Americas — while simultaneously generating resistance to it

The essential question running through this era: When is revolution justified — and who decides?

docs.title: All Six Standards Applied to Era 3
WH.6_12.1 + Era 3 — Science and Technology of the Age of Revolutions
docs.text:
Full standard: Analyze historical achievements related to science and technology.

In Era 3, this means: Explain advancements related to science and technology during this era — the Scientific Revolution, the Industrial Revolution's machinery, and the navigational technology that enabled global exploration — and analyze the global impact of these advancements.

Key topics: - The Scientific Revolution (~1543–1687): Copernicus (heliocentric model), Galileo (telescope, experimental method), Kepler (planetary motion), Newton (gravity, laws of motion), Harvey (circulation of blood); the shift from authority (Church) to observation (evidence) as the basis for knowledge - Age of Exploration technology (~1400–1600): the caravel (ship design), the astrolabe (navigation by stars), the magnetic compass, improved maps; without these technologies, the transatlantic crossing is impossible - Industrial Revolution technology (~1760–1900): steam engine (Watt), spinning jenny (Hargreaves), power loom (Cartwright), locomotive (Stephenson), telegraph (Morse); the factory system replacing cottage industry - Military technology: gunpowder weapons spreading from China to Europe; muskets replacing feudal knights; artillery changing siege warfare; how military technology accelerated the spread of European colonialism - Print and communication: expansion of printing press technology; rise of newspapers and pamphlets as political tools; the role of the Common Sense pamphlet (Paine) and revolutionary newspapers in spreading Enlightenment ideas

FPS Skill Strand: OEC Students analyze complex texts describing scientific and industrial change.

ELA connection: 9-10.R.2 — Comprehend a variety of complex texts, developing inferences and providing relevant textual evidence.

Learning targets: - I can explain the causes and global effects of the Scientific Revolution. - I can analyze how Industrial Revolution technology changed how people worked and lived. - I can explain how technological advancements during this era enabled exploration, colonization, and imperialism.

Example inquiry angles: - "How did the Scientific Revolution change the relationship between religion and knowledge in European society?" - "Did the Industrial Revolution raise the standard of living or lower it — for whom, and how do you measure?" - "How did European military and navigational technology make colonialism possible — and is there a direct line from the caravel to the colony?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.2 + Era 3 — Religions and Ideologies of the Age of Revolutions
docs.text:
Full standard: Explain historical changes related to religions and ideologies.

In Era 3, this means: Determine and explain how religious division (the Protestant Reformation) and ideological change (Enlightenment, nationalism, revolutionary liberalism) drove or were driven by the major events of this era.

Key topics: - The Protestant Reformation (1517 onward): Luther's 95 Theses; the specific grievances (indulgences, Church corruption, lack of access to the Bible); Calvin's predestination and theocratic Geneva; Henry VIII and the Church of England (different reasons, same break); the Wars of Religion in Europe; Thirty Years War - How religious division led to exploration: Spanish and Portuguese colonization justified partly through religious conversion; competing Catholic powers seeking alternate routes to Asia after Ottoman control of eastern routes - The Enlightenment's challenge to religion: Voltaire, Locke, Rousseau, Montesquieu; deism (God as clockmaker, not interventionist); the idea that human reason, not divine revelation, should govern society; Jefferson's "separation of church and state" - Revolutionary ideology as quasi-religion: the French Revolution's "Cult of Reason" literally replacing Catholicism with state-sponsored secular worship; the Declaration of the Rights of Man drawing on Enlightenment natural law theory; how revolutionary ideals (liberty, equality, fraternity) functioned as a moral framework - Indigenous religions and colonialism: missionary activity as a tool of conquest; forced conversion; the destruction of Aztec, Inca, and West African religious practices and their partial survival underground

FPS Skill Strand: Argumentation Students explain how religious and ideological change influenced revolutions.

ELA connection: 9-10.W.1 and W.2 — Write clearly and coherently with a logical organizational structure.

Learning targets: - I can explain how the Protestant Reformation changed the religious and political landscape of Europe. - I can analyze how Enlightenment ideology provided the intellectual framework for democratic revolutions. - I can trace how religious division contributed to the Age of Exploration.

Example inquiry angles: - "Luther didn't intend to split Christianity. How did a theological debate become a political revolution?" - "How did Enlightenment thinkers turn religion from an explanation for authority into a challenge to it?" - "How did the Haitian Revolution take French Revolutionary ideals more seriously than France did?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.3 + Era 3 — Political Systems and Their Effects on People
docs.text:
Full standard: Analyze the effects of different political systems on people.

In Era 3, this means: Analyze how revolutionary political systems — absolute monarchy, constitutional government, republic, and empire — affected people's lives. Argue whether revolutionary political changes actually improved conditions for ordinary people.

Key topics: - Absolute monarchy: Louis XIV ("L'état, c'est moi" — I am the state); the Versailles system as political control (nobles at court can't revolt); how absolutism worked and why it produced revolutions - Constitutional monarchy: the Glorious Revolution (1688) in England; the English Bill of Rights (1689); the model of parliament limiting royal power - The American Revolution (1776): Declaration of Independence drawing on Locke; the new republic's Constitution and its contradictions (slavery, Native displacement); who was actually included in "all men are created equal" - The French Revolution (1789): the three estates; the Declaration of the Rights of Man; the Reign of Terror (3000 executions by guillotine); Napoleon's rise as "revolution that produced an emperor" - The Haitian Revolution (1791–1804): enslaved Africans using revolutionary ideology against the slaveholding revolutionaries; the only successful slave revolution in history; France's brutal response; the $150 million "debt" Haiti paid France for its own freedom until 1947 - Latin American independence (1810s–1820s): Simón Bolívar, José de San Martín; independence from Spain and Portugal; but who inherited power after colonizers left?

FPS Skill Strand: Argumentation Students argue whether revolutionary political systems improved life for individuals.

ELA connection: 9-10.W.4 — Argumentative writing: argue whether revolutionary political change improved or harmed individual lives.

Learning targets: - I can explain how a specific revolutionary event changed the political system of a society. - I can analyze who benefited and who was excluded from the promises of a given revolution. - I can argue a position on whether a revolutionary government improved or worsened conditions for ordinary people.

Example inquiry angles: - "The French Revolution promised liberty, equality, and fraternity — but delivered the Reign of Terror. Was the Terror a betrayal of the Revolution's ideals or a logical consequence of them?" - "Haiti's revolutionaries achieved what America's didn't. Why has Haiti's history since 1804 been so different from America's?" - "Who was left out of every major revolution of Era 3 — and what does that pattern tell us?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.4 + Era 3 — Social, Cultural, and Economic Development
docs.text:
Full standard: Analyze the influence of social, cultural, and economic development on individuals.

In Era 3, this means: Explain how economic and social forces — mercantilism, the Atlantic slave trade, Industrial Revolution labor conditions, colonialism — drove or resulted from the revolutionary changes of this era, and how they affected individual lives.

Key topics: - Mercantilism — the economic theory driving European colonialism: accumulate gold and silver by exporting more than you import; colonies exist to supply raw materials and buy finished goods from the mother country - The transatlantic slave trade (~1500–1800s): 12.5 million enslaved Africans transported; Middle Passage conditions; plantation economies in the Caribbean and Americas; how European wealth was built on enslaved labor; the long-term economic and social consequences - Colonial social hierarchies: the racial classification systems of colonial societies (peninsulares, criollos, mestizos, indigenous, enslaved); how social status was determined by birth and race in colonial systems - Industrial Revolution labor conditions: child labor in textile mills and mines; 14-hour workdays; overcrowded urban tenements; the rise of the working class (proletariat) as a new social category; early labor unions; the conditions that produced socialist and communist thought (Marx and Engels) - The enclosure movement: common lands privatized in England; peasants driven off land they'd farmed for generations; creation of a landless laboring class who had no choice but to work in factories — the precondition for industrialization - Women and the Age of Revolutions: Mary Wollstonecraft's A Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792); women excluded from French Revolutionary rights; the irony of revolutionary ideals that stopped at gender

FPS Skill Strand: Interpretation & Analysis Students explain how economic and social forces fueled revolutionary change.

ELA connection: 9-10.W.3 — Explanatory writing: explain how social, cultural, and economic developments contributed to the Age of Revolutions.

Learning targets: - I can explain how mercantilism and the transatlantic slave trade functioned as an economic system. - I can analyze how Industrial Revolution conditions created the social conditions for political movements. - I can evaluate how social and economic changes in this era affected people differently depending on their position in society.

Example inquiry angles: - "The Industrial Revolution created modern wealth. It also created modern poverty. How do you evaluate a historical development that does both?" - "How did the transatlantic slave trade shape the economies of Europe, America, and Africa in ways that are still visible today?" - "Why did the Age of Revolutions produce ideas about freedom and equality while simultaneously intensifying slavery and colonialism?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.5 + Era 3 — Causes and Effects Using Primary and Secondary Sources
docs.text:
Full standard: Analyze causes and effects of global events using primary and/or secondary sources.

In Era 3, this means: Use primary and secondary sources to investigate the causes and effects of major Era 3 events — the Age of Exploration, the French Revolution, the Haitian Revolution, the Industrial Revolution.

Key events + source analysis: - Age of Exploration: causes include Ottoman control of eastern trade routes, printing press spreading geographic knowledge, navigational technology, Spanish and Portuguese competition; effects: destruction of indigenous civilizations, the Columbian Exchange, the transatlantic slave trade, European global dominance - The French Revolution: primary sources include the Declaration of the Rights of Man, speeches from the National Assembly, Robespierre's justifications for the Terror; compare to secondary source historians on whether the Revolution was primarily an economic or ideological event - The Haitian Revolution: Toussaint L'Ouverture's correspondence; the Haitian Declaration of Independence (1804); compare to French accounts of the same events - The Industrial Revolution: factory workers' testimony before Parliament (primary); compare to economists' analyses of living standards improvement (secondary) — who's right, and why do their sources lead to different conclusions?

FPS Skill Strand: OEC + Interpretation & Analysis Students analyze multiple accounts of revolutionary-era events to determine cause and effect.

ELA connection: 9-10.R.9b — Analyze various accounts of a subject told in different media, including determining which details are emphasized or omitted.

Learning targets: - I can identify the causes and effects of a major global event in Era 3 using primary and secondary sources. - I can compare the perspective of someone living through a revolution with a historian analyzing it afterward. - I can use primary sources to examine, explain, and defend arguments about how global events had cause-and-effect relationships on individuals.

Example inquiry angles: - "How do factory workers' testimonies and economic historians' accounts of the Industrial Revolution contradict each other — and why?" - "The French and Haitian Revolutions both began with the same ideological foundation. Why did they end so differently?" - "How do the sources that survived the Age of Exploration reflect who had the power to tell the story?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.6 + Era 3 — Past to Present
docs.text:
Full standard: Explain how past events connect to the present.

In Era 3, this means: Evaluate how the religious division, global connections, and political/social revolutions of this era connect to present-day political systems, economic inequalities, and international relationships.

Key connections: - The Protestant Reformation → religious pluralism today: the fragmentation of Christianity into hundreds of denominations; the principle of religious liberty; the separation of church and state as a political value - Enlightenment ideals → modern democratic governments: the U.S. Constitution, the UN Declaration of Human Rights, the French Fifth Republic — all traceable to Enlightenment thought - The transatlantic slave trade → contemporary racial inequality: accumulated wealth and poverty; generational effects of slavery on American society; reparations debates - Industrial Revolution → modern capitalism and labor law: child labor laws, the 8-hour workday, unions — all direct responses to Industrial Revolution conditions - Imperialism and colonialism → contemporary global inequality: the wealth gap between former colonizers and former colonies; political instability in post-colonial states; ongoing land and resource extraction patterns - The Haitian Revolution → international relations: France recognized Haitian independence only after Haiti agreed to pay reparations; the debt was paid until 1947; this history shapes Haiti's current poverty

FPS Skill Strand: Argumentation Students debate how revolutionary ideas shape modern political systems.

ELA connection: 9-10.C.6 — Engage in respectful discussions or debates.

Learning targets: - I can trace a direct line from a specific Era 3 event to a present-day institution, law, or social pattern. - I can participate in a structured debate about whether a historical event's effects are still active today. - I can support my position with specific historical evidence.

Example inquiry angles: - "How do the economic decisions of the colonial era still determine the wealth gap between nations today?" - "Is the United States government a product of the Enlightenment? What does it still have in common with the ideals of 1776 — and what has it abandoned?" - "The Haitian Revolution is the only successful slave revolution in history. Why is it taught so rarely in American high schools?"

docs.title: Key Vocabulary for Era 3
docs.text: Term Definition Protestant Reformation The 16th-century religious movement that challenged Catholic Church authority, leading to the establishment of Protestant denominations Enlightenment The 18th-century intellectual movement emphasizing reason, individual rights, and the social contract as the basis of government natural rights Rights that all people have by virtue of being human, not granted by government (life, liberty, property — Locke; liberty, equality, fraternity — French Revolution) social contract The idea that governments derive their legitimacy from the consent of the governed (Locke, Rousseau) absolute monarchy A system where the monarch has unlimited power, unchecked by constitution or parliament constitutional monarchy A system where the monarch's power is limited by a constitution or parliament mercantilism The economic theory that national wealth is built by exporting more than you import; colonies exist to supply raw materials and buy finished goods Columbian Exchange The transfer of plants, animals, diseases, and people between the Americas and the Old World following Columbus's voyages (1492 onward) Industrial Revolution The shift from agricultural to manufacturing economies, beginning in Britain (~1760) and spreading globally proletariat The working class — people who own no land or capital and must sell their labor to survive imperialism A policy of extending a nation's power through colonization, military force, or economic domination colonialism The practice of one nation establishing control over another territory, exploiting its resources and people Haitian Revolution The 1791–1804 revolution in which enslaved Africans in Saint-Domingue defeated French colonial forces and established Haiti as the first Black republic and the only successful slave revolution in history

docs.title: Related Standards
docs.text: ELA crosswalk standards most used in Era 3: - W.4 — Argument writing (when is revolution justified? was imperialism progress or exploitation?) - W.3 — Explanatory writing (explain causes of the French Revolution; explain how Enlightenment ideas spread) - R.9 — Informational/argumentative analysis (Paine, Wollstonecraft, Declaration of Rights of Man — all argumentative primary sources) - IR.1–5 — Research bundle (active every studio; primary source evaluation)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• 95 Theses
  • Luther

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Era 3's essential question — who are "the people" that revolutions claim to speak for? — is the question being asked in every populist movement today. The French Revolution gave the world "left" and "right" as political categories. The Haitian Revolution tested whether Enlightenment ideals were truly universal — and the world's response proved they weren't automatically. The Communist Manifesto's critique of industrial capitalism describes conditions (wealth inequality, worker alienation, capital concentration) that are measurable and debated right now. The Industrial Revolution's transformation of labor, cities, and economic relationships is the origin point of every contemporary debate about automation, gig work, and the future of employment. Every protest movement, independence movement, and rights movement operating today uses the vocabulary — and argues within the framework — that Era 3 created. See WH-04: One Idea, 130 Years of War and WH-02: The Rights We Wrote Down for the studios that dig deepest into this era.

Era 3: Age of Revolutions — SDA Commons Wiki See also: WH Standards Overview · Era 2 — Middle Ages · Era 4 — Global War · Primary Sources

docs.title: Era 4: Age of Global War and Globalization
docs.text: Course: English I + World History (Grade 9) Era span: Approximately 1900–present Studios that use this era: Studio 6 — Propaganda & Truth · Studio 7 — War & Memory · Studio 8 — Globalization

docs.title: What This Era Is About
docs.text:
Era 4 is the most destructive and transformative century in human history. From 1900 to the present, the world experienced:

  • Two world wars that killed over 100 million people combined
  • The rise and fall of fascist empires (Nazi Germany, Imperial Japan, Mussolini's Italy)
  • A Cold War between nuclear-armed superpowers that never became directly a "hot" war but shaped every other conflict on earth
  • The Holocaust — the systematic murder of six million Jews and millions of others by the Nazi regime
  • Decolonization — the disintegration of European empires across Africa and Asia
  • Civil rights movements on every continent fighting for the rights of people marginalized by race, gender, and class
  • The rise of television, nuclear energy, space travel, computers, and the internet
  • Globalization — the integration of world economies to the point where a supply chain crisis in one country affects prices on the other side of the world

This era is also the one students are still living inside. The international order, the technology, the political fault lines, and many of the ongoing conflicts of the 21st century have direct roots in the 20th century.

The essential questions running through this era: How do governments control information? Whose stories get told after a war? Does connection create inequality?

docs.title: All Six Standards Applied to Era 4
WH.6_12.1 + Era 4 — Science and Technology of the 20th Century and Beyond
docs.text:
Full standard: Analyze historical achievements related to science and technology.

In Era 4, this means: Analyze how scientific and technological advancements caused and/or resulted from the world wars, and how technology shaped the 20th century and globalization.

Key topics: - WWI technology: the machine gun making trench warfare defensive; poison gas (chlorine, mustard gas) as a war crime; tanks; submarine warfare; the airplane as a weapons platform; barbed wire - WWII technology: nuclear weapons (Manhattan Project, Hiroshima, Nagasaki); radar; jet aircraft; V-2 rockets (precursors to space launch vehicles); penicillin and other medical advances; the entire logistics system of industrialized warfare - Cold War technology competition: Sputnik (1957) launching the space race; ARPANET (1969) as the origin of the internet; nuclear deterrence theory; ICBMs; spy satellites; the "military-industrial complex" (Eisenhower's warning) - Medical and biological advances: vaccines (polio), antibiotics, DNA discovery (1953), cancer research, genetic engineering - Globalization's technological infrastructure: container shipping (1956), standardized international trade systems, fiber optic cables under oceans, satellite communication, the internet, social media - Nuclear technology: civilian nuclear power; Chernobyl (1986); nuclear non-proliferation challenges; the ongoing existence of ~13,000 nuclear warheads globally

FPS Skill Strand: OEC Students analyze how texts structure explanations of wartime technology and innovation.

ELA connection: 9-10.R.6 — Analyze how the author's choice in structure, form, and format supports purpose. (Note: R.6 is an adjacent standard; primary active ELA standard for this studio would be specified in the studio contract.)

Learning targets: - I can analyze the causes and global effects of the world wars, including specific technological developments. - I can explain how nuclear technology changed global politics and still shapes international relations. - I can analyze how globalization's technological infrastructure has created new forms of social, cultural, and economic change.

Example inquiry angles: - "The atomic bomb ended WWII. Did it also begin a new kind of war that has never ended?" - "How did the space race produce technologies (satellites, GPS, weather forecasting) that now govern civilian life?" - "How did containerized shipping change the global economy — and which workers lost their jobs when it did?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.2 + Era 4 — Religions and Ideologies of the Age of Global War
docs.text:
Full standard: Explain historical changes related to religions and ideologies.

In Era 4, this means: Analyze how political and social ideologies — fascism, communism, liberal democracy, nationalism, decolonization movements — shaped the 20th century and its conflicts.

Key topics: - Fascism: Italy (Mussolini), Germany (Hitler/Nazism), Spain (Franco); key features: ultranationalism, one-party dictatorship, racial hierarchy, violent suppression of opposition; fascism as a response to both communism and liberal democracy's perceived failures - Communism: Marx's theory as ideology; Soviet Union (Lenin, Stalin) as implementation; Mao's China; Cuban Revolution; how communist theory and Soviet practice diverged; the appeal of communism to colonized peoples as anti-imperialist ideology - Liberal democracy: the ideology of the Allied powers in WWII; human rights, elections, rule of law, free markets; the Allies' own contradictions (Jim Crow in America, colonialism in Britain and France) - Nationalism and decolonization: the idea that each ethnic group should have its own state; how nationalism simultaneously drove WWI (European ethnic rivalries) and decolonization (African and Asian independence movements); Gandhi's nonviolent resistance; Patrice Lumumba, Ho Chi Minh, Kwame Nkrumah - The Cold War as ideological conflict: capitalism vs. communism as global organizing frameworks; proxy wars (Korea, Vietnam, Angola, Nicaragua) as contests between the ideologies; McCarthyism in America as domestic ideological panic - Religious movements in the 20th century: the Iranian Revolution (1979) as Islamic governance replacing secular monarchy; religious nationalism in Israel/Palestine; evangelical Christianity's political rise in America

FPS Skill Strand: Argumentation Students write arguments evaluating the role of ideology in 20th-century conflicts.

ELA connection: 9-10.W.4 — Argumentative writing: evaluate whether a specific ideology improved or harmed people's lives.

Learning targets: - I can explain the core features of fascism and communism and analyze how each political system affected people's lives. - I can analyze how political and social ideologies shaped the major conflicts of the 20th century. - I can explain how cultural, social, and economic changes were influenced by globalization.

Example inquiry angles: - "How did fascism emerge from democratic societies — and what does that tell us about democracy's vulnerabilities?" - "Was communism's appeal in the 20th century about economic justice or about anti-colonialism? (Does the distinction matter?)" - "How did the Cold War shape countries that were neither the U.S. nor the Soviet Union?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.3 + Era 4 — Political Systems and Their Effects on People
docs.text:
Full standard: Analyze the effects of different political systems on people.

In Era 4, this means: Analyze how political systems of the 20th century — totalitarianism, liberal democracy, Cold War client states, post-colonial governments — affected ordinary people's lives, rights, and freedoms.

Key topics: - Totalitarianism: total control of all aspects of society by the state; Nazi Germany and the Holocaust; Stalin's Soviet Union (gulags, forced collectivization, purges); Mao's China (Cultural Revolution); what makes totalitarianism different from mere authoritarianism - The Holocaust: the systematic, state-sponsored murder of six million Jews; also Roma, disabled people, gay people, political opponents; how it was organized bureaucratically; why ordinary people participated; the Nuremberg Trials and accountability - The atomic bomb: the decision to bomb Hiroshima and Nagasaki; the debate over whether it was justified; the post-war nuclear arms race; the concept of "mutually assured destruction" (MAD) as deterrence - Post-WWII political order: the United Nations (1945); the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948); the Nuremberg Principles establishing individual criminal accountability for war crimes; NATO and the Warsaw Pact - Cold War proxy states: South Korea/North Korea; South Vietnam/North Vietnam; Cuba; Angola; Nicaragua — ordinary people in these countries lived (and died) in conflicts shaped by superpower competition - Post-colonial political instability: many post-colonial states had borders drawn by European colonizers cutting across ethnic and cultural groups; civil wars, coups, and political instability in Africa and Asia in part traceable to colonial border decisions

FPS Skill Strand: Argumentation Students debate the Atomic Bomb and post-WWII political ideologies.

ELA connection: 9-10.C.6 — Engage in respectful discussions or debates.

Learning targets: - I can explain how totalitarian systems controlled people's lives in the 20th century. - I can analyze how political decisions (the atomic bomb, Cold War proxy wars) affected ordinary people. - I can participate in a structured debate about a contested political decision, using evidence from primary and secondary sources.

Example inquiry angles: - "Was dropping the atomic bomb on Hiroshima and Nagasaki a justified military decision or a war crime? (Argue with evidence — primary sources exist from both the American government and Japanese survivors.)" - "How did the Cold War create political conditions in countries across the world that still shape their politics today?" - "The Holocaust was carried out by ordinary people following orders. What does that fact tell us about how political systems can normalize violence?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.4 + Era 4 — Social, Cultural, and Economic Development
docs.text:
Full standard: Analyze the influence of social, cultural, and economic development on individuals.

In Era 4, this means: Explain the social, cultural, and economic effects of the world wars and globalization on individuals — what changed in people's daily lives as a result of 20th-century upheaval.

Key topics: - WWI's homefront: women entering the workforce in large numbers for the first time in industrial economies; war bonds and propaganda; the Spanish flu (1918) killing more people than the war - WWII homefront: rationing, scrap drives, women in factories (Rosie the Riveter); Japanese American internment (Executive Order 9066); the Double V campaign (African Americans fighting fascism abroad and racism at home) - Post-WWII economic boom: the GI Bill and its unequal application (Black veterans largely excluded); suburbanization; the Baby Boom; television reshaping culture; consumer society - Decolonization's social effects: the partition of India (1947) and mass violence between Hindus and Muslims; the Algerian War; apartheid in South Africa; how national independence didn't always mean social liberation - Civil rights movements: the global wave — American civil rights (1950s–1970s), anti-apartheid South Africa, feminism, LGBTQ rights, indigenous rights movements; what connects them and what's specific to each - Globalization and inequality: free trade agreements (NAFTA, WTO); manufacturing leaving wealthy countries for lower-wage countries; call centers in India; cheap goods in Walmart made in Chinese factories; who benefits from globalization and who is displaced by it - Media and culture: Hollywood as global culture; the Beatles and rock music crossing national borders; hip-hop as a global form; the internet collapsing geographic distance in culture

FPS Skill Strand: Interpretation & Analysis Students explain the effects of globalization on societies and individuals.

ELA connection: 9-10.W.3 — Explanatory writing: explain how social, cultural, and economic changes were influenced by globalization.

Learning targets: - I can explain how the world wars changed the social and economic structures of societies. - I can analyze how globalization has created both opportunity and inequality for individuals. - I can explain how political and social ideologies shaped the 20th century in terms of how they affected ordinary people's lives.

Example inquiry angles: - "How did WWII change the role of women in the workforce — and what happened when the war ended?" - "Globalization has lifted hundreds of millions of people out of poverty in developing countries while eliminating manufacturing jobs in wealthy countries. Is that a good trade-off — and who gets to decide?" - "How does the American civil rights movement connect to decolonization movements in Africa and Asia? What did these movements learn from each other?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.5 + Era 4 — Causes and Effects Using Primary and Secondary Sources
docs.text:
Full standard: Analyze causes and effects of global events using primary and/or secondary sources.

In Era 4, this means: Use primary and secondary sources to investigate the causes and consequences of major 20th-century global events — WWI, WWII, the Cold War, decolonization.

Key events + source analysis: - WWI causes: primary sources — the July Crisis letters (Kaiser Wilhelm, Tsar Nicholas II); the Austro-Hungarian ultimatum to Serbia; contrast with secondary source historians on whether WWI was accidental, inevitable, or chosen - WWII and the Holocaust: primary sources — the Wannsee Conference minutes (the bureaucratic planning of genocide); survivor testimony (Elie Wiesel, Primo Levi); compare to U.S. government documents on knowledge of the Holocaust before D-Day - Dropping the bomb: Truman's decision documents vs. Stimson's justification memo vs. Japanese government communications vs. Hiroshima survivor testimony; each source tells a different part of the story - Cold War: NSC-68 (the 1950 policy paper defining U.S. Cold War strategy) vs. Soviet documents declassified after 1991; how the Cold War looked from the Soviet side - Decolonization: Gandhi's writings and speeches vs. British colonial government documents; compare the same events as seen from the colonizers and the colonized

FPS Skill Strand: OEC + Interpretation & Analysis Students analyze multiple accounts of global conflicts to evaluate causes and consequences.

ELA connection: 9-10.R.9b — Analyze various accounts of a subject told in different media, including determining which details are emphasized or omitted.

Learning targets: - I can use primary and secondary sources to interpret and apply important information about a major global event. - I can compare accounts of the same event from different perspectives and explain what each one reveals. - I can analyze cause-and-effect relationships using evidence from multiple source types.

Example inquiry angles: - "The Wannsee Conference minutes are a bureaucratic document planning genocide. What does reading it as a primary source tell us that a textbook description cannot?" - "How do WWI look different through German sources than through British and French sources — and whose version is in your history textbook?" - "Hiroshima survivors describe the bomb differently from U.S. government documents. What does each source need to omit to make its narrative work?"

docs.title: WH.6_12.6 + Era 4 — Past to Present
docs.text:
Full standard: Explain how past events connect to the present.

In Era 4, this means: Explain how historical changes from the world wars, the Cold War, and globalization affect the present day — politically, economically, culturally, and geopolitically.

Key connections: - WWII → international order today: the United Nations, NATO, the World Bank, the IMF — all created in the post-WWII period and still governing international relations - The Holocaust → "never again": the Genocide Convention (1948); the International Criminal Court; the concept of humanitarian intervention; ongoing debates about whether the world has kept its promise - Cold War → current geopolitics: the U.S.-Russia relationship; NATO expansion and its role in the Russia-Ukraine war; China's rise as a competing superpower; proxy conflicts in the Middle East with roots in Cold War alignments - Decolonization → contemporary inequality: the wealth gap between formerly colonized and colonizing nations; political instability in sub-Saharan Africa; the global South's relationship to the global North - Globalization → supply chains and COVID-19: the pandemic revealed how fragile integrated global supply chains are; masks manufactured in China; vaccines distributed unequally across wealthy and poor nations - 20th-century civil rights → present movements: Black Lives Matter, #MeToo, indigenous land rights movements all trace philosophical and strategic lineages to 20th-century civil rights organizing

FPS Skill Strand: Argumentation Students present evidence-based explanations of how global events shape the modern world.

ELA connection: 9-10.C.1 — Construct and deliver formal and informal presentations incorporating multimedia.

Learning targets: - I can identify a specific 20th-century event and trace its direct effects on present-day politics, economics, or culture. - I can present a past-to-present connection to an audience using appropriate format and multimedia. - I can respond to questions about my analysis, including challenges to my argument.

Example inquiry angles: - "How did the post-WWII international order shape the response to the Russia-Ukraine war in 2022?" - "How does the history of U.S. foreign policy in the Middle East (Cold War alliances, oil interests, support for authoritarian governments) explain the conflicts of the 21st century?" - "How is the debt Haiti was forced to pay France for its own independence still affecting Haitian poverty today?"

docs.title: Key Vocabulary for Era 4
docs.text: Term Definition totalitarianism A political system in which the state controls all aspects of public and private life; used to describe Nazi Germany, Stalinist USSR, and Mao's China fascism An extreme right-wing nationalist ideology combining a totalitarian government, racial hierarchy, and violent suppression of opposition communism A political and economic ideology advocating for collective ownership of the means of production; in practice, led to one-party states (USSR, China, Cuba) Cold War The political, military, and ideological confrontation between the United States and the Soviet Union from ~1947–1991; "cold" because it never became direct military conflict between the two superpowers proxy war A conflict in which two powers support opposing sides without fighting each other directly (Korea, Vietnam, Angola) decolonization The process by which formerly colonized territories in Africa and Asia gained independence, primarily in the 1940s–1970s Holocaust The systematic, state-sponsored persecution and murder of six million Jews by the Nazi regime and its collaborators during WWII Manhattan Project The U.S. government research program (1942–1945) that developed the first nuclear weapons deterrence The military strategy of preventing attack by threatening devastating retaliation; "mutually assured destruction" (MAD) as the Cold War nuclear deterrence logic NATO The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (1949); a military alliance of Western democratic nations; mutual defense agreement globalization The increasing integration and interdependence of national economies, cultures, and politics through trade, communication, and technology United Nations The intergovernmental organization founded in 1945 to maintain international peace and security and promote human rights apartheid The system of institutionalized racial segregation and discrimination enforced by the South African government from 1948–1994 propaganda Information — especially of a biased or misleading nature — used to promote a political cause or point of view

docs.title: Skinnies for Era 4
docs.text:

docs.title: Reading Library — Era 4 Texts

  • 1984 — George Orwell (1949) — Totalitarianism and surveillance state · WH.6_12.3 + R.8 + W.4 · Literary Text
  • Fahrenheit 451 — Ray Bradbury (1953) — Cold War fear, censorship, and conformity · WH.6_12.2 + WH.6_12.6 + R.8 · Literary Text
  • Persepolis — Marjane Satrapi (2000) — Iranian Revolution through a child's eyes · WH.6_12.3 + WH.6_12.5 + R.8 · Literary Text

docs.title: Related Standards
docs.text: ELA crosswalk standards most used in Era 4: - R.8 — Literary elements (Night, 1984 — character, symbolism, setting under totalitarianism) - R.9 — Informational/argumentative analysis (MLK Letter, political propaganda, Cold War documents) - W.4 — Argument writing (was dropping the atomic bomb justified? is propaganda ever legitimate?) - W.3 — Explanatory writing (explain causes of WWI; explain how propaganda shaped public opinion) - IR.1–5 — Research bundle (active every studio)

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
The entire international order students live in was built in Era 4: the United Nations, the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, NATO, the Geneva Conventions, nuclear deterrence. Those institutions are now being tested in ways they haven't faced since their founding — by great-power competition, by weapons that move faster than diplomacy, by countries withdrawing from international agreements, by genocides that the UN Security Council can't respond to because permanent members have veto power. The Holocaust's "never again" pledge has been tested repeatedly since 1945 — in Rwanda, Bosnia, Darfur, and ongoing — and the international community's responses (or failures) are still being argued. The question Era 4 raises — what do we build after catastrophe to make sure it doesn't happen again? — is exactly the question students will face as the post-WWII order strains under 21st-century pressures. See WH-05: The Cost of War and the Universal Declaration of Human Rights in the Reading Library.

Era 4: Age of Global War and Globalization — SDA Commons Wiki See also: WH Standards Overview · Era 3 — Revolutions · Primary Sources

docs.title: World History Standards Overview
docs.text: Course: English I + World History (Grade 9) Standards: WH.6_12.1 through WH.6_12.6

docs.title: The Key Fact: Six Standards, Four Eras
docs.text:
World History in SDA uses six standards that repeat across all four eras. This is not a mistake — it is the design.

The same standard code (WH.6_12.1) means something different depending on which era you're studying. When you see a studio standard listed, you will always see both the code and the era:

WH.6_12.3 + Era 2 = Analyze the effects of medieval political systems (feudalism, church power) on people's lives. WH.6_12.3 + Era 4 = Analyze the effects of 20th-century political systems (fascism, communism, democracy) on people's lives.

Same skill. Different content. That's how standards work in SDA.

How to read a standard + era tag: [Standard Code] + [Era Number] Example: WH.6_12.5 + Era 3 = Analyze causes and effects of global events during the Age of Revolutions using primary and secondary sources.

docs.title: WH.6_12.1 — Science & Technology
docs.text: Analyze historical achievements related to science and technology.

docs.title: WH.6_12.2 — Religions & Ideologies
docs.text: Explain historical changes related to religions and ideologies.

docs.title: WH.6_12.3 — Political Systems
docs.text: Analyze the effects of different political systems on people.

docs.title: WH.6_12.4 — Social & Economic Development
docs.text: Analyze the influence of social, cultural, and economic development on individuals.

docs.title: WH.6_12.5 — Causes & Effects
docs.text: Analyze causes and effects of global events using primary and/or secondary sources.

docs.title: WH.6_12.6 — Past to Present
docs.text: Explain how past events connect to the present.

docs.title: FPS Social Studies Skill Strands (Cross-Cutting)
docs.text:
These three skill strands cut across all six standards in all four eras. Every assessment in Social Studies touches at least one of these:

Skill Strand What It Means Argumentation Produce well-reasoned arguments supported with valid, relevant, and sufficient evidence Interpretation & Analysis Draw accurate, relevant, and sufficient conclusions from historical sources and information Obtain, Evaluate & Communicate (OEC) Research, evaluate source credibility, and communicate valid/reliable findings

docs.title: Full Standards × Eras Table
docs.text:
This is the full map of all six standards applied to all four eras. Use this to understand what any studio standard combination means.

Era 1 — Civilizations Era 2 — Middle Ages Era 3 — Revolutions Era 4 — Global War WH.6_12.1 Science and tech of early civilizations: agriculture, architecture, early medicine, mathematical systems Science and tech of Middle Ages and Renaissance: printing press, astrolabe, medieval medicine, Islamic Golden Age discoveries Science and tech of the Age of Revolutions: Scientific Revolution, Industrial machinery, Enlightenment thought, exploration technology 20th-century science and technology: WWI/WWII weapons, nuclear technology, space race, globalization's tech infrastructure WH.6_12.2 Origins of world religions: Judaism, Christianity, Islam, Hinduism, Buddhism; how early belief systems shaped societies Religious change in medieval period: Catholic Church dominance, Crusades, Protestant Reformation, Renaissance humanism challenging church authority Religious division and ideological change: Protestant Reformation driving exploration, Enlightenment secularism, revolutionary ideologies (liberty, nationalism, democracy) Ideological conflict of 20th century: fascism, communism, liberal democracy; Cold War ideology; decolonization movements WH.6_12.3 Effects of early political systems: Greek democracy, Roman republic and empire, Mesopotamian codes of law, Chinese imperial bureaucracy Effects of medieval political systems: feudalism, Magna Carta, church-state conflict, Ottoman governance Effects of revolutionary political systems: absolute monarchies vs. constitutional government, outcomes of French/Haitian/American Revolutions Effects of 20th-century political systems: Nazi Germany, Soviet Union, Cold War proxy states, post-colonial governments WH.6_12.4 Social, cultural, and economic development: agricultural revolution, caste systems, trade networks, early urbanization, cultural diffusion Social, cultural, and economic development: feudal social hierarchy, Silk Road and Saharan trade, guild systems, Renaissance cultural explosion Social, cultural, and economic development: mercantilism, colonialism's effects on indigenous peoples, industrial labor conditions, revolutionary social upheaval Social, cultural, and economic development: globalization, Cold War consumer culture, post-colonial economic inequality, contemporary global trade WH.6_12.5 Causes and effects of global events using primary/secondary sources: fall of Rome, rise of Islamic caliphates, Mongol conquests Causes and effects using primary/secondary sources: Black Death, Crusades, fall of Constantinople, Hundred Years War Causes and effects using primary/secondary sources: French Revolution, Haitian Revolution, Napoleonic Wars, Industrial Revolution spreading Causes and effects using primary/secondary sources: WWI, WWII (Atlantic + Pacific theaters), Cold War conflicts, decolonization WH.6_12.6 Past to present: How Greek democracy connects to modern government; how Silk Road trade anticipates modern globalization; how early religious codes inform modern law Past to present: How Renaissance ideas led to Enlightenment; how Magna Carta principles live in modern constitutions; how feudal economics connect to capitalism Past to present: How revolutionary ideals of liberty shaped modern human rights discourse; how Industrial Revolution created modern economic inequality Past to present: How WWII shaped the UN and modern international order; how Cold War still shapes global geopolitics; how decolonization's legacies persist

docs.title: The Four Eras at a Glance
docs.text: Era Approximate Dates Core Focus Era 1 — Emergence of Civilizations and Religions Around the Globe Prehistory through ~600 CE Ancient empires, early religions, Greek and Roman political systems, development of agriculture, first trade networks Era 2 — Middle Ages and the Renaissance ~500–1600 CE Feudalism, Catholic Church dominance, Crusades, Black Death, Islamic Golden Age, Renaissance art and thought, early global trade Era 3 — Age of Revolutions ~1400–1900 Protestant Reformation, Age of Exploration, Scientific Revolution, Enlightenment, French/Haitian/American Revolutions, Imperialism, Industrial Revolution Era 4 — Age of Global War and Globalization ~1900–present WWI, Russian Revolution, WWII, Cold War, decolonization, civil rights movements, globalization, contemporary geopolitics

docs.title: How Studios Use These Standards
docs.text:
Each six-week studio uses exactly one WH standard combined with exactly one era. That combination is the social studies anchor for the studio.

Examples from the pre-packaged studio catalog:

Studio Standard + Era Essential Question Power & Belief WH.6_12.2 + Era 1 How did early religions justify political authority? Builders & Breakers WH.6_12.1 + Era 1 What makes a civilization rise or collapse? The Medieval Crisis WH.6_12.3 + Era 2 Is order worth obedience? Renaissance & Rebirth WH.6_12.2 + Era 2 Can art change the way people think? The Age of Revolution WH.6_12.4 + Era 3 When is rebellion justified? Propaganda & Truth WH.6_12.3 + Era 4 How do governments control information? War & Memory WH.6_12.5 + Era 4 Whose stories get told after a war? Globalization WH.6_12.6 + Era 4 Does connection create inequality?

docs.title: Full Era Pages
docs.text:
Each era has its own page with all six standards applied to that era's content, specific learning targets, and inquiry angles:

  • Era 1 — Civilizations — Era 1: Emergence of Civilizations and Religions
  • Era 2 — Middle Ages — Era 2: Middle Ages and Renaissance
  • Era 3 — Revolutions — Era 3: Age of Revolutions
  • Era 4 — Global War — Era 4: Age of Global War and Globalization

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • Magna Carta

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text:
Students sometimes ask why they study history when "it's in the past." The answer is that the past is not finished: it lives in the laws we follow, the borders we live inside, the institutions we rely on, and the arguments we repeat without knowing where they came from. The WH standards aren't a list of facts to memorize — they're a toolkit for understanding how the world got to be the way it is. Every standard in this framework was designed to help students see patterns: how power gets organized, how ideas spread, how societies respond to crisis, how the language people use to justify power changes while the underlying dynamics stay the same. That kind of pattern recognition is the most useful skill there is when the world is changing fast and the information environment is designed to confuse. For the texts that make these patterns visible, see the Reading Library and the Studio Archive.

World History Standards Overview — SDA Commons Wiki See also: USH Standards Overview · Crosswalk Ela Ss

docs.title: Studio Archive
docs.text:
SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

Pre-packaged studio packets for SDA six-week cycles. Each packet is a complete, ready-to-run studio with: essential question, active standards, reading list, inquiry angle menu, six-week arc, demonstration of learning options, deliverable ideas, scale tasks, and exhibition format.

Packets are starting points, not scripts. Students propose their own inquiry angles within the essential question; the packets give advisors and students a tested framework to build from.

docs.title: How to Use a Studio Packet
docs.text:
1. Advisor: Review the packet before Week 1. Decide which inquiry angles to feature, whether to add or swap texts from the reading library, and what the Week 6 exhibition format will be.
  2. Student (Week 1): Read the Essential Question and the Inquiry Angle Menu. Read one passage from each text in the reading list. Write a first reaction. Submit your Studio Proposal choosing your angle, demo mode, and deliverable concept.
  3. Both: The packet's six-week arc is a scaffold, not a rule. Adjust the pacing based on what the class needs.

docs.title: World History Studios (Grade 9, Year 1)
docs.text: Packet Essential Question Primary Era Key Texts WH-01: Who Should Govern? Who should govern, and what gives them the right? Era 1 Hammurabi, Confucius, Pericles, Melian Dialogue WH-04: One Idea, 130 Years of War Can one document change the world — and if so, how? Era 2–3 Luther's 95 Theses, Common Sense, Communist Manifesto WH-06: The Connected World What connects the world — and who decides who counts as part of it? Era 2 Ibn Battuta, optional: Confucius, UN UDHR WH-02: The Rights We Wrote Down When people write down their rights, what changes — and what doesn't? Era 3 (Era 1–4 chain) Rights chain: Hammurabi through Malala WH-03: Writing Against Empire When a powerful system is doing harm, what does it take to challenge it? Era 2–3 Las Casas, Kipling + H.T. Johnson, Douglass WH-05: The Cost of War Who pays the cost of war — and who gets to tell the story? Era 4 Owen, FDR, Night, Persepolis

docs.title: US History Studios (Grade 10, Year 2)
docs.text: Packet Essential Question Primary Units Key Texts USH-01: When the System Fails When an institution fails the people it's supposed to serve, what does it take to fix it? Units 1–2, 6 The Jungle, Warriors Don't Cry, MLK Letter USH-02: The Literature of Fear What does a society's fiction reveal about its fears — and whose fears count? Unit 5 + WH Era 4 Fahrenheit 451, 1984, Persepolis USH-03: When the Floor Falls Out When the economy collapses and millions lose everything, what does the government owe them? Unit 3 The Great Gatsby, FDR Four Freedoms USH-04: The Cost of Victory When a nation believes it is fighting on the right side of history, which of its own values can it sacrifice? Unit 4 Night, FDR Four Freedoms, Slaughterhouse-Five USH-05: What the Government Didn't Say When a government lies about a war and gets caught — what changes, what doesn't, who pays? Unit 7 Fahrenheit 451, 1984, Politics and the English Language USH-06: When the Rules Don't Apply When people in power break the rules they're supposed to enforce, what holds them accountable? Unit 8 The Handmaid's Tale, Politics and the English Language USH-07: The Unipolar Moment After winning the Cold War, the U.S. had more power than any nation in history. What did it do with it? Unit 9 The Kite Runner, Persepolis, Malala's UN Speech

docs.title: Adding Packets
docs.text: When new studio packets are developed, add them to this index and to the relevant standards pages. A packet is "archive-ready" when it has: - Essential question tested with at least one student cohort - All reading list texts present in the Reading Library - All referenced skinnies confirmed in the Skinny Library - Demonstration of learning options aligned to the skinnies for those standards
