docs.title: When the System Fails — Studio Packet
docs.text:
Studio Code: USH-01 Subject Area: US History — Units 1–2 (Progressive Era) + Unit 6 (Civil Rights) Suggested Cycle: Year 2, Cycle 1 Duration: 6 weeks

US History Unit Timeline — highlighted segment(s) indicate this studio's historical period.

docs.title: The Essential Question
docs.text: When an institution fails the people it's supposed to serve, what does it take to fix it — and who has to do the work?

docs.title: Why This Studio Matters
docs.text:
Upton Sinclair exposed the meatpacking industry in 1906 and got food safety regulation — not the labor reform he actually wanted. Melba Beals walked through a mob to integrate a high school in 1957 because nine students and a federal order forced what a decade of legal victories hadn't. Martin Luther King Jr. wrote a letter from a jail cell in 1963 addressed to white Christian clergy who agreed with him in principle but kept asking for patience.

This studio spans 60 years of American history — the Progressive Era to the Civil Rights Movement — through a single through-line: What does it look like when people force a broken system to change? It builds US.6_12.1 (progressive reform), US.6_12.2 (rights and citizenship), and US.6_12.4 (economics and labor), while developing the full ELA argument-writing and primary-source analysis toolkit.

docs.title: Active Standards
docs.text: Standard What You're Targeting US.6_12.1 + Units 1–2 Progressive Era reform movements and the role of exposure journalism US.6_12.2 + Unit 6 Civil rights movement: strategies, arguments, and the limits of legal progress US.6_12.4 + Unit 1 Labor conditions, economic inequality, and reform 9-10.R.9 Analyzing argument in primary sources: How does each text construct its case for change? 9-10.W.4 Argument writing: claim, evidence, counterargument, qualification 9-10.R.8 Literary elements in memoir: How does Beals' narrative choices construct historical meaning?

docs.title: ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk
docs.text:
Every text in this studio was written to change something — which means analyzing the writing (ELA) and analyzing the historical argument (SS) are the same work.

  • MLK, "Letter from Birmingham Jail" — This is the #1 highest-leverage crosswalk text in the entire library. Analyzing its argument structure for R.9 and using it as a W.4 mentor text is the same as building US.6_12.3-4 + Unit 6 evidence about Civil Rights strategy and the argument for urgent nonviolent action. There's a dedicated crosswalk resource: Letter from Birmingham + W.4 Crosswalk. One text, multiple standards, with the ELA and SS questions identical.

  • The Jungle (Sinclair) — Reading it for R.8 (how does Sinclair use literary narrative — scene, character, detail — to make his argument?) simultaneously builds US.6_12.5 + Unit 1 evidence about the Progressive Era and the role of muckraking journalism in reform. It's in the ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk.

  • Warriors Don't Cry (Beals) — Literary analysis of the memoir for R.8 (how does Beals use personal narrative, detail, and scene to function as historical testimony?) is directly building US.6_12.2 + Unit 6 evidence about the Civil Rights movement and what nine students had to do in person to accomplish what years of legal victories hadn't.

A single Demo of Learning can satisfy both an ELA and a USH standard if your Studio Contract names both. Talk to Reese in Week 1 if you want to use a crosswalk approach.

See the ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk for the full map of how texts serve both subject areas across all studios.

docs.title: Reading List
docs.text:
Text Why It's Here The Jungle — Sinclair Muckraking journalism as reform tool; Sinclair's own words: "I aimed at the heart and hit the stomach" Warriors Don't Cry — Beals Civil Rights primary source; memoir as historical testimony; nine children doing what Brown v. Board couldn't MLK — Letter from Birmingham Jail The argument for urgent action against the "white moderate"; written from a jail cell to people who agreed with him in principle Narrative of Frederick Douglass Optional extension: the 1845 precedent — reform arguments that work by forcing readers to witness Malala — UN Speech Optional contemporary connection: institutional failure + individual response

Required minimum: Two texts from the core three (Jungle, Warriors, MLK). The studio works best with all three — they're in direct conversation about who has to do the work of reform.

docs.title: Inquiry Angle Menu
docs.text:
Trailblazer angles: - Sinclair wanted labor reform. He got food safety regulation. Using the text and its historical context, explain why the public responded to the stomach and not the heart. What does this reveal about how reform actually works? - MLK's Letter is addressed to "My Dear Fellow Clergymen." Why does he choose this audience? What does he know about them? How does writing to them specifically change his argument?

Maverick angles: - Compare Sinclair's strategy (exposure journalism) and Beals' strategy (embodied protest — walking into the mob) as reform tools. What does each one do that the other can't? Which created more lasting change? - MLK spends most of the Letter responding to criticism from white moderates who agree with him. Why is that audience his problem? What does his argument reveal about the difference between agreement in principle and action in practice? - All three texts describe situations where the law was either inadequate or actively complicit. How does each author argue for change when "follow the law" isn't a solution?

Phoenix angles: - "The people who fix broken systems are almost never the people the system was designed to serve — they're always people who had to fight to be included." Evaluate this claim using evidence from all three texts and at least one additional historical example of your choice. - The Progressive Era and the Civil Rights Movement both produced major legal change. But Sinclair was disappointed by what his book accomplished, and Beals describes the gap between legal integration and actual safety. What does this pattern reveal about the relationship between legal change and social change?

docs.title: Six-Week Arc
docs.text: Week Phase What You're Doing 1 Launch Read this passage from The Jungle: "I aimed at the public's heart, and by accident I hit it in the stomach." Then read MLK's first paragraph of the Birmingham Letter. What do these two men have in common about how their audience responded to them? Submit your Studio Proposal. 2 Dig Deep read your selected texts. For R.9: What is each author's argument for why the system is failing? For whom? Who is the intended audience — and how does that shape the argument? For R.8 (Beals): What narrative choices does she make that an academic history book couldn't? 3 Build Draft your deliverable. Your central question: What does it take to force a broken system to change — and who has to do the work? Use specific evidence. 4 Shape Peer review and advisor conference. Identify the assumption in your argument that you haven't examined — something you're taking for granted. Revise to either defend it or qualify it. 5 Finish Complete demonstration of learning. Polish deliverable. Prepare for exhibition. 6 Publish Exhibition. Reflection. Archive.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning Options
docs.text: Mode Prompt for This Studio 1 — Written Assessment In 500–700 words: Analyze how one of the three core texts makes its argument for reform. What is the author's claim? Who is their audience? What rhetorical strategies do they use? What is their blind spot? 2 — Extended Writing Argument essay (700–1,000 words): Compare two of the three reform strategies (Sinclair's exposure journalism, Beals' embodied protest, MLK's letter to reluctant allies). Which created more lasting change — and by what standard are you measuring "lasting change"? 3 — Verbal Conversation Advisor conference: Walk through the essential question using all three texts. For each: What was the system failure? What was the strategy? What changed — and what didn't? 4 — Visual/Creative Timeline/comparison exhibit: Map the three reform moments on a visual that shows what changed legally, socially, and economically after each. Annotate: What did the texts themselves make happen, and what required something more? 5 — Multimedia/Performance Documentary or recorded presentation: "Who Does the Work of Reform?" — using the three texts to answer the essential question for a general audience. Must demonstrate US.6_12.1+2 and R.9 evidence. 6 — Portfolio Annotation Curate prior work and annotate for US.6_12.1+2+4 and R.9+R.8 evidence. Commentary must reference specific text passages and explain your analytical reasoning.

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Aimed at the Heart, Hit the Stomach" — A research essay on Sinclair's The Jungle: What did he intend? What happened? What does the gap between intent and effect reveal about how public response to reform arguments works?

  2. "The Letter's Audience" — A rhetorical analysis of MLK's Letter: Why does he choose white Christian moderates as his audience? Analyze the specific rhetorical strategies he uses for that specific audience. Then: What would the letter look like if it were addressed to a different audience?

  3. "Nine Students" — A research exhibit or documentary on the Little Rock Nine: Using Warriors Don't Cry and secondary sources, examine why it took nine teenagers to force integration that three years of legal victories hadn't accomplished.

  4. "Then and Now" — A comparative analysis connecting one of the three reform moments to a contemporary institutional failure and reform effort. Use the analytical framework from this studio: What is the system failure? Who is being harmed? What strategies are being used? What is working and what isn't?

  5. "The Reform Playbook" — A synthesizing argument essay or presentation: Based on the evidence from all three texts, what are the conditions that make reform succeed? What are the conditions that make it fail? Present your findings as a set of evidence-based claims.

docs.title: 2.0 — Foundation
docs.text:
• Define: muckraker, Progressive Era, civil disobedience, integration, rhetoric, primary source vs. secondary source
  • Correctly describe the historical context and argument of each text
  • Identify the audience for each text and why the author chose that audience

docs.title: 3.0 — Target
docs.text:
• Analyze the rhetorical strategy of at least two texts: claim, evidence, audience, assumptions
  • Connect each reform effort to its historical context: What made the strategy effective or ineffective in that specific moment?
  • Trace what actually changed as a result of each text or movement — and identify the gap between what the author wanted and what they got

docs.title: 4.0 — Transfer
docs.text:
• Construct an argument about what makes reform succeed or fail — using specific evidence from multiple texts and a claim that goes beyond what any single text says
  • OR: Apply the reform-strategy framework to a contemporary institutional failure you identify — using the analytical tools from this studio
  • OR: Argue whether the people who pay the highest personal cost in reform movements (Beals walking into a mob; MLK writing from jail; Sinclair losing his public platform) are the same people who benefit most from the change

docs.title: Skinny Recommendations
docs.text: If you're struggling with... Pull this skinny Reading argumentative primary sources R.9 Informational/Argumentative Analyzing literary elements in memoir R.8 Literary Elements Writing an argument with multiple sources W.4 Argument Writing Managing a multi-source research process IR.1–5 Research Bundle Leading or participating in Socratic seminar C.6 Discussion and Debate

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: The reform tradition this studio traces — from Progressive Era muckrakers to Civil Rights activists — is still the main model for how people in a democracy try to fix broken institutions. Upton Sinclair's The Jungle exposed conditions in meatpacking plants and led directly to the Pure Food and Drug Act. That model (expose the specific harm, build the public case, force legislative response) is what every investigative journalist, nonprofit advocacy organization, and social movement still uses. The Civil Rights Act of 1964 passed because people like Melba Beals put their bodies on the line at Little Rock, and because MLK made an argument so powerful that dismissing it required publicly defending the indefensible. Labor rights, environmental regulation, consumer protection, disability rights, LGBTQ rights — every expansion of rights and every institutional reform in American history used the same basic playbook this studio studies. And every one of those gains is subject to rollback, which is why understanding how they were won matters. If you want to follow the thread into more recent history, USH-06: When the Rules Don't Apply asks what happens when accountability mechanisms fail.

docs.title: Exhibition Format
docs.text:
Suggested format: Town hall or panel discussion.

Students present their reform analysis to a mixed audience (classmates, advisor, one guest). Format: 5-minute presentation, then 3 questions from the panel. Panel must include at least one question that challenges the student's claim about what "worked."

What gets scored at exhibition: - C.1 — formal presentation: organized argument, register, handling of challenge questions - C.6 — discussion: responding to pushback with evidence; listening to other students' analyses

docs.title: Sensitive Content Note
docs.text: Warriors Don't Cry contains descriptions of racial violence, harassment, and threats experienced by Melba Beals and the Little Rock Nine. This content is historically accurate and essential to the text — it is not gratuitous, but it is direct. Students who need support processing this content should speak with their advisor before Week 2.

docs.title: The Literature of Fear — Studio Packet
docs.text:
Studio Code: USH-02 Subject Area: US History — Unit 5 (Cold War) + World History Era 4 Suggested Cycle: Year 2, Cycle 2 (or Year 1, Cycle 6) Duration: 6 weeks

US History Unit Timeline — highlighted segment(s) indicate this studio's historical period.

docs.title: The Essential Question
docs.text: What does a society's fiction reveal about its fears — and whose fears count?

docs.title: Why This Studio Matters
docs.text:
In 1953, Ray Bradbury published Fahrenheit 451 — about book burning — while the US government was blacklisting artists and the House Un-American Activities Committee was interrogating citizens about their reading habits. In 1949, George Orwell published 1984 — about totalitarian surveillance — in the shadow of Stalin's USSR. In 2000, Marjane Satrapi published Persepolis — about a childhood under the Iranian Revolution — as a graphic memoir that made the abstract political concrete and personal.

These are not coincidences. Literature about fear is produced in eras of fear — and the fiction a society consumes tells you exactly what that society is afraid of, what it's willing to do about it, and who it decides the fear doesn't apply to.

This studio builds the WH.6_12.2+6 (ideology and legacy) and US.6_12.2 (rights and citizenship in the Cold War) standards while developing the full R.8 (literary analysis) and R.9 (cross-text argument) toolkit. It's the studio where students learn to read historical context in fictional form.

docs.title: Active Standards
docs.text: Standard What You're Targeting WH.6_12.2 + Era 4 Cold War ideology, propaganda, and the role of culture in political systems WH.6_12.6 + Era 4 Legacy and continuity: What does the Cold War's cultural anxiety look like today? US.6_12.2 + Unit 5 Civil liberties, McCarthyism, and the tension between security and rights 9-10.R.8 Literary elements as historical evidence: How does fiction encode its cultural moment? 9-10.R.9 Cross-text argument: What is each novel's implicit claim about its historical moment? 9-10.W.4 Argument writing: using literary evidence alongside historical evidence

docs.title: ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk
docs.text:
USH-02 sits at the hinge point between two courses: it spans World History Era 4 and US History Unit 5 at the same time. That means every major text in this studio can do triple duty — ELA literary analysis, World History, and US History — often in a single Demo of Learning. Here's how:

  • 1984 (Orwell) — R.8 literary analysis (dystopian world-building, surveillance, doublethink as literary theme) simultaneously satisfies WH.6_12.2-3 + Era 4 (ideology and political systems — specifically how totalitarianism works) AND US.6_12.2 + Unit 5 (multiple perspectives on the Cold War and communist/capitalist ideological conflict). 1984 bridges World History and US History — it's one of the few texts in the library that does triple duty. If you use it here, make that connection explicit in your Demo: name both courses and both standards in your Studio Contract.

  • Fahrenheit 451 (Bradbury) — R.8 analysis (how Bradbury constructs his dystopia, what the book burning represents as a literary symbol) connects directly to US.6_12.3 + Unit 7 (cause and effect: Cold War fear culture, McCarthyism, and government information control — Fahrenheit 451 is Bradbury's direct response to the Red Scare and book-banning). It also connects to US.6_12.2 + Unit 5 (the cultural atmosphere of the Cold War). In other words, analyzing the literary symbol is also analyzing the historical cause. It appears in the ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk table.

  • Persepolis (Satrapi) — R.8 analysis (graphic memoir format; how Satrapi uses visual language and personal narrative as political argument) simultaneously satisfies WH.6_12.2-3 + Era 4 (the Iranian Revolution as a case study in Cold War-era ideological conflict) AND US.6_12.2 + Unit 9 (multiple perspectives — the Iranian perspective on the U.S.-Iran relationship). Persepolis is one of the few texts that bridges World History, US History Unit 5, AND US History Unit 9. Students who use it here have a head start for USH-07: The Unipolar Moment.

The bridge to notice: USH-02 is the studio most explicitly positioned at the hinge point between World History and US History. If you're enrolled in both courses, this is your highest-leverage studio for earning credit in both at once.

One Demo of Learning can satisfy both an ELA and an SS standard if your Studio Contract names both — you don't have to do two separate projects. Talk to Reese in Week 1 to set this up. See the ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk for the full map of dual-use texts across all studios.

docs.title: Reading List
docs.text:
Text Why It's Here Fahrenheit 451 — Bradbury Cold War cultural anxiety made fiction; Bradbury said he was trying to prevent it 1984 — Orwell Totalitarian surveillance as nightmare; written from inside the post-WWII moment that made it possible Persepolis — Satrapi The Iranian Revolution from the inside — a child's memoir that makes ideology personal FDR — Four Freedoms Historical context: the ideals these novels are testing UN UDHR Historical context: what "universal rights" looked like while 1984 was being written

Required minimum: Two of the three literary texts (Fahrenheit 451, 1984, Persepolis). The studio works best with all three — they represent three different national contexts for the same Era 4 Cold War moment.

docs.title: Inquiry Angle Menu
docs.text:
Trailblazer angles: - Choose one scene from Fahrenheit 451 and explain: What specifically is Bradbury afraid of? What historical events or trends in 1953 America does this scene reflect? - Persepolis is set in Iran during the Islamic Revolution — not in Cold War America. But it appears in a studio about Cold War cultural anxiety. What connects these two contexts?

Maverick angles: - Compare Fahrenheit 451 and 1984 as political arguments. Both are dystopian fiction. Both argue that something is wrong with the direction their society is heading. But what exactly are they afraid of — and are they afraid of the same thing? - Bradbury said "I wasn't writing about censorship, I was writing about how TV was destroying the desire to read." Orwell said 1984 was "a warning, not a prophecy." Both authors tried to control how their books were interpreted after publication. Does an author's stated intent determine a text's meaning? - Persepolis is a graphic memoir, not a novel. How does the graphic format change what Satrapi can show about the Iranian Revolution that a written memoir couldn't?

Phoenix angles: - "The literature of fear tells you what a society believes it deserves to lose — not what it's actually losing." Using evidence from all three texts, evaluate this claim. - These three texts were all written by people who were in or adjacent to the systems they were criticizing (Bradbury in Cold War America, Orwell post-Spain and post-WWII, Satrapi in revolutionary Iran). How does being inside the culture you're critiquing change what you can say about it? - Connect the "literature of fear" framework to a contemporary text you identify yourself — a novel, film, or other cultural product that encodes contemporary cultural anxiety. What does it reveal about what your era fears?

docs.title: Six-Week Arc
docs.text: Week Phase What You're Doing 1 Launch Read Beatty's speech from Fahrenheit 451 (Part I, "The Hearth and the Salamander"). Beatty is the villain arguing for book burning — and his argument is disturbingly coherent. What is his argument? Does any part of it make sense? Submit your Studio Proposal. 2 Dig Deep read your selected texts. For R.8: Mark every image, metaphor, structural choice, or scene that encodes a historical anxiety. For context: read the "Historical Context" section of your library entry — what was happening when this was written that the author was responding to? 3 Build Draft your deliverable. Your central challenge: How do you use a fictional text as historical evidence? What specifically in the text counts as evidence for your historical claim? 4 Shape Peer review and advisor conference. Find the place where your argument relies on plot summary instead of analysis. Revise to move from "this is what happens" to "this is what it means and why the author constructed it this way." 5 Finish Complete demonstration of learning. Polish deliverable. Prepare for exhibition. 6 Publish Exhibition. Reflection. Archive.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning Options
docs.text: Mode Prompt for This Studio 1 — Written Assessment In 400–600 words: Choose one passage from any text in this studio and analyze how specific literary choices (imagery, structure, word choice, point of view) encode a historical anxiety. Do not summarize — analyze. 2 — Extended Writing Argument essay (700–1,000 words): Argue what Fahrenheit 451, 1984, or Persepolis reveals about the fears of its historical moment that a history textbook couldn't. Use literary evidence as historical evidence. 3 — Verbal Conversation Advisor conference: Walk through the historical context of your chosen text, then explain how at least three specific literary choices encode that context. Demonstrate that you're reading the text as both a work of fiction and a historical artifact. 4 — Visual/Creative Annotated close reading: Select a chapter or scene and create a visual annotation that maps how the literary choices connect to the historical moment. Each annotation must make a historical claim, not just a literary observation. 5 — Multimedia/Performance Documentary or video essay: "Reading the Cold War in Fiction" — a guide for general audiences on how to read dystopian/fear literature as historical evidence. Must demonstrate R.8+9 and WH.6_12.2 standard evidence. 6 — Portfolio Annotation Curate prior work and annotate for WH.6_12.2+6, US.6_12.2, and R.8+9. Commentary must reference specific passages and demonstrate the skill of reading literary evidence historically.

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Beatty's Argument" — A rhetorical analysis of Captain Beatty's speech in Fahrenheit 451: What is Beatty arguing? Is he wrong? Bradbury gives his villain the most coherent argument in the book — why? What does that choice reveal about what Bradbury was afraid of?

  2. "Three Fears, Three Countries" — A comparative analysis of what Fahrenheit 451 (American), 1984 (British), and Persepolis (Iranian) each fear — and what the comparison reveals about how Cold War anxiety looked different in different national contexts.

  3. "When Bradbury Was Right" — A research essay examining the historical parallels between Fahrenheit 451's fictional society and 1953 American society (HUAC, McCarthyism, TV). Was Bradbury describing the present or predicting the future? Use historical evidence alongside the text.

  4. "The Child Who Saw Everything" — A literary analysis of Persepolis as a graphic memoir: How does the child's perspective reveal things about the Iranian Revolution that an adult, political perspective couldn't? What does the graphic format add? Use specific panels as evidence.

  5. "2025 Edition" — A contemporary connection exhibit: Choose one fear from this studio's texts (censorship, surveillance, ideological conformity, political violence) and research how it manifests today. Does the "literature of fear" framework from these texts help explain contemporary cultural products that encode the same fear?

docs.title: 2.0 — Foundation
docs.text:
• Define: dystopia, McCarthyism, Cold War, totalitarianism, censorship, cultural anxiety
  • Correctly describe the historical context in which each novel was written
  • Identify at least two specific literary elements used in each text

docs.title: 3.0 — Target
docs.text:
• Analyze how at least three specific literary choices in a text encode the historical moment it was written in
  • Compare two texts' implicit arguments: What does each fear? How does each construct that fear through literary choices?
  • Demonstrate that you can use fictional evidence historically — that you understand the difference between "this is what happens in the book" and "this is what the book reveals about its moment"

docs.title: 4.0 — Transfer
docs.text:
• Construct an argument about what dystopian/fear literature reveals about historical periods that straightforward historical accounts can't — using specific evidence from multiple texts
  • OR: Apply the "literature of fear" framework to a contemporary cultural product you identify yourself
  • OR: Argue whether the fears in these texts have been realized, avoided, or transformed — with specific historical evidence

docs.title: Skinny Recommendations
docs.text: If you're struggling with... Pull this skinny Reading for literary elements R.8 Literary Elements Analyzing word choice and style R.7 Style and Word Choice Comparing arguments across texts R.9 Informational/Argumentative Writing analysis rather than summary W.3 Explanatory Writing Argument writing with literary evidence W.4 Argument Writing

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Orwell and Bradbury wrote their dystopias as warnings, not predictions — but the warning has never been more directly applicable. The surveillance infrastructure that Orwell imagined — a government that can watch everything, knows what you search, tracks where you go, reads your messages — exists and is operated by both governments and corporations. The algorithmic selection of what you see on social media is a form of what Bradbury described: not book-burning, but something more efficient — making sure you never encounter the book in the first place. And the Cold War fear this studio is about — what happens to a society that organizes itself around existential terror? — maps directly onto current debates about national security, surveillance, and the trade-off between freedom and safety. 1984 and Fahrenheit 451 are not cautionary tales about the past. They're diagnostic tools for the present. If you want to connect the Cold War context to what came after, USH-05: What the Government Didn't Say covers Vietnam and the Pentagon Papers — the moment when Cold War fear became Cold War deception.

docs.title: Exhibition Format
docs.text:
Suggested format: Symposium or documentary screening + discussion.

If students produced documentary or video deliverables, screening + Q&A. If written/analytical: symposium where each student presents their central argument (5 minutes) and takes challenge questions (3 minutes).

Final question for every presenter: "What contemporary cultural product encodes the same fear you identified in your text — and how do you know?"

What gets scored at exhibition: - C.1 — formal presentation: organized argument, handling of challenge - C.6 — discussion: engaging with classmates' analyses; adding evidence

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Era 4 Standards Overview
  • USH Unit 5 — Cold War
  • Fahrenheit 451 — Full Entry
  • 1984 — Full Entry
  • Persepolis — Full Entry
  • Studio WH-05: The Cost of War — overlapping Era 4 context
  • USH-05 — What the Government Didn't Say (Fahrenheit 451 + 1984 reappear — Vietnam angle)
  • USH-07 — The Unipolar Moment (Persepolis reappears — US foreign policy angle)
  • ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk — Full map of dual-use texts across all studios

Studio Packet · USH-02 · SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

docs.title: When the Floor Falls Out — Studio Packet
docs.text:
Studio Code: USH-03 Subject Area: US History — Unit 3 (Roaring 20s and Great Depression) Suggested Cycle: Year 2, Cycle 2 Duration: 6 weeks

US History Unit Timeline — highlighted segment(s) indicate this studio's historical period.

docs.title: The Essential Question
docs.text: When the economy collapses and millions of people lose everything, what does the government owe them — and what does it cost to find out?

docs.title: Why This Studio Matters
docs.text:
The 1920s looked, to a lot of Americans, like the country had finally arrived. The war was over, the economy was booming, consumer culture exploded, and jazz was changing music everywhere. F. Scott Fitzgerald published The Great Gatsby in 1925, a novel that captured the decade's glittering surface and the rot underneath. His characters live inside a dream — beautiful, expensive, and hollow — and they keep reaching for something that keeps moving away.

Then the crash came. In October 1929, the stock market collapsed, triggering bank failures, mass unemployment (25% at its peak), and the Dust Bowl. Twelve million families lost their savings. By 1932, Hoovervilles — shantytowns named mockingly after the president — had appeared in every major American city. Hoover believed the government should not intervene directly. FDR disagreed. He won the 1932 election on that disagreement, and the New Deal he built — Social Security, the FDIC, the SEC, federal work programs — still shapes American life today.

This studio puts two questions side by side: What was the dream the 1920s were selling, and who couldn't buy in? And: When that dream collapsed, what did the people left holding nothing deserve from their government? It builds US History standards on economic change and government's role while developing argument writing (W.4) and literary analysis of how Fitzgerald uses the novel to make an argument about American society (R.8).

docs.title: Active Standards
docs.text: Standard What You're Targeting US.6_12.4 + Unit 3 Change over time: how the government's relationship to the economy transformed between Hoover's "rugged individualism" and FDR's New Deal — and what that shift meant for ordinary Americans US.6_12.5 + Unit 3 Significant contributions: FDR, Eleanor Roosevelt, Dorothea Lange; the New Deal as a set of policies still shaping modern American life US.6_12.2 + Unit 3 Multiple perspectives: who the 1920s boom included and excluded; who the New Deal helped and who it left out (Black farmers, women workers, sharecroppers) 9-10.R.8 Literary elements in The Great Gatsby: how Fitzgerald uses symbol, character, and narrative structure to critique the 1920s dream 9-10.W.4 Argument writing: what does the government owe its citizens in economic crisis? Claim, evidence, counterargument, qualification IR.1–5 Research bundle: active every studio

docs.title: ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk
docs.text:
The Great Gatsby is a dual-use text for this studio. When you analyze it for R.8 — how Fitzgerald uses symbol, character, and setting to make an argument about the American Dream — you are also building evidence for US.6_12.4 + Unit 3, because the novel is a direct window into how 1920s Americans understood prosperity and the gap between the dream and the reality. One Demo of Learning can satisfy both standards at once if your Studio Contract lists both and your analysis addresses both dimensions: the literary technique AND its historical meaning.

The same goes for the FDR Four Freedoms speech (extension): analyzing FDR's argument for R.9 simultaneously builds US.6_12.4 + Unit 3 evidence about how the New Deal philosophy extended into WWII mobilization.

See the ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk for the full map of how texts serve both subject areas across all studios.

docs.title: Reading List
docs.text:
Text Why It's Here The Great Gatsby — Fitzgerald Fitzgerald's 1925 novel captures the 1920s boom from the inside — the parties, the wealth, the illusion — while showing what it cost and who it excluded. Nick Carraway is the reader's guide through a world that looks like success and isn't. The Jungle — Sinclair Optional connection: Sinclair's 1906 muckraking novel shows the Progressive Era reform impulse that preceded the 1920s rollback. Useful for students arguing about the history of government intervention in the economy. FDR's Four Freedoms Extension: FDR's 1941 articulation of what the government owes its citizens — connects the New Deal philosophy to WWII mobilization and provides a primary source for what FDR believed government was for.

Required minimum: The Great Gatsby is the literary core. Students working with the SS argument should also read at least one FDR primary source (the Four Freedoms speech or the First Inaugural Address — "the only thing we have to fear is fear itself").

docs.title: Inquiry Angle Menu
docs.text:
Trailblazer angles: - Gatsby throws lavish parties and invites everyone — but almost nobody actually knows him. Using the novel, explain what Fitzgerald is saying about the American Dream: what it promises, who it's actually for, and what it costs people who believe in it. - Herbert Hoover believed Americans should pull themselves up by their own bootstraps — that government aid would make people dependent and weaken the national character. FDR disagreed. Using historical evidence, explain what the Depression revealed about the limits of "rugged individualism."

Maverick angles: - Fitzgerald's Gatsby was published in 1925 — four years before the crash. But reading it now, you can see the crash coming. What does the novel reveal about the 1920s economy that people living through it weren't willing to see? What were the warning signs? - The New Deal helped millions of Americans — but it explicitly excluded Black farmers, domestic workers, and many women. Using the historical record, evaluate the New Deal: was it a genuine transformation of American society, or a rescue program for white working-class men? - Dorothea Lange's photograph "Migrant Mother" (1936) became the iconic image of the Great Depression. Analyze it as a primary source: What choices did Lange make? What does the photograph argue? What does it leave out? Why did it become the image of the Depression rather than thousands of other photographs from the same period?

Phoenix angles: - Fitzgerald wrote that "the rich are different from you and me." Hemingway responded: "Yes, they have more money." Using Gatsby and the historical record of the 1920s and Depression, argue which one was right — and what the difference between their views reveals about how Americans understand wealth and failure. - The United States has now had multiple major economic crises (1929, 2008, 2020). Each time, the government has had to decide what it owes its citizens. Using the Depression/New Deal as your primary case and at least one more modern example, argue whether the American government has learned anything about what it owes the people it serves in a crisis — and what the evidence says about whether those lessons stick.

docs.title: Six-Week Arc
docs.text: Week Phase What You're Doing 1 Launch Open with this line from Gatsby: "So we beat on, boats against the current, borne back ceaselessly into the past." Read the first chapter. Then read FDR's First Inaugural: "The only thing we have to fear is fear itself." What do these two documents — written 8 years apart — say about what Americans were reaching for? Submit your Studio Contract. 2 Dig Deep read your chosen texts. For R.8: How does Fitzgerald use Gatsby's parties, the green light, and the Valley of Ashes to make an argument about the American Dream? For the SS strand: research the specific policies Hoover chose and what FDR replaced them with. Who got New Deal help and who didn't? 3 Build Draft your deliverable. Your central question: what did the 1920s dream cost, and what did the government owe when it collapsed? Use specific evidence from both the literary text and the historical record. 4 Shape Peer review and advisor conference. Find the assumption in your argument you haven't examined. Is your claim about the government's obligation based on evidence — or on what you already believed before you started? Revise to strengthen the evidence or qualify the claim. 5 Finish Complete demonstration of learning. Polish deliverable. Prepare for exhibition. 6 Publish Exhibition. Reflection. Archive.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning Options
docs.text: Mode Prompt for This Studio 1 — Written Assessment In 500–700 words: Analyze how Fitzgerald uses one symbol from The Great Gatsby (the green light, the Valley of Ashes, Gatsby's parties) to make an argument about the American Dream. What is he saying? What evidence does he use? 2 — Extended Writing Argument essay (700–1,000 words): What does the government owe its citizens when the economy collapses? Use the Depression and New Deal as your primary evidence. What were the stakes of getting this wrong — and did FDR get it right? 3 — Verbal Conversation Advisor conference: Walk through both the novel and the historical period. What was the 1920s dream? Who was included and excluded? When it collapsed, what happened? What did the New Deal change — and what did it leave unchanged? 4 — Visual/Creative Comparison exhibit: Map the 1920s boom and the Depression crash on a visual timeline. Show who thrived, who struggled, and who was left out at each stage. Annotate: What did the New Deal change for each group? 5 — Multimedia/Performance Documentary or recorded presentation: "The Dream That Crashed" — using Gatsby, Dorothea Lange's photography, and New Deal primary sources to tell the story of 1920s America for a general audience. Must demonstrate US.6_12.4+5 and R.8 evidence. 6 — Portfolio Annotation Curate prior work and annotate for US.6_12.4+5 and R.8+W.4 evidence. Commentary must use 2.0/3.0/4.0 language from the relevant skinny and connect your prior work to this studio's essential question.

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas
docs.text:
1. "The Green Light" — A literary analysis essay on The Great Gatsby: What does the American Dream look like in this novel? What does Fitzgerald say about who it's available to and what it costs to pursue it? Connect the novel to the actual 1920s economy.

  2. "Rugged Individualism vs. The New Deal" — An argument essay: Was FDR's New Deal the right response to the Depression — or did it change the relationship between government and citizens in ways that created long-term problems? Take a clear position with historical evidence.

  3. "Who the New Deal Left Out" — A research exhibit: The New Deal helped millions of Americans but deliberately excluded Black agricultural workers and domestic laborers due to political compromises with Southern Democrats. Document who was left out, why, and what this reveals about reform in America.

  4. "Migrant Mother" — A primary source analysis and visual essay: Using Dorothea Lange's Depression photographs as your central source, analyze how photographers shaped how Americans understood the Depression. What did they choose to show — and what did they leave out?

  5. "Then and Now" — A comparative analysis: Connect the 1929 crash and New Deal to a more recent economic crisis (2008 financial crisis, COVID economic collapse). What did the government do differently? What stayed the same? What does the comparison reveal about what Americans believe the government owes them in a crisis?

docs.title: 2.0 — Foundation
docs.text:
• Define: Roaring 20s, Great Depression, New Deal, rugged individualism, Dust Bowl, Social Security, Harlem Renaissance
  • Correctly summarize the plot and main characters of The Great Gatsby
  • Identify the major New Deal programs and who they were designed to help
  • Describe the basic cause-and-effect chain from the 1929 crash to the Depression

docs.title: 3.0 — Target
docs.text:
• Analyze Fitzgerald's use of at least two literary elements (symbol, character, setting, narrative structure) to make an argument about the American Dream
  • Connect the novel's themes to the actual historical context of the 1920s: what did Fitzgerald see that his contemporaries were ignoring?
  • Evaluate the New Deal: who did it help, who did it exclude, and what does that pattern reveal about how American reform works?
  • Build an argument about what the government owes its citizens in a crisis — with a clear claim, specific historical evidence, and acknowledgment of the strongest counterargument

docs.title: 4.0 — Transfer
docs.text:
• OR: Argue that The Great Gatsby is (or isn't) still a relevant critique of American economic culture — using evidence from both the novel and contemporary America
  • OR: Evaluate whether the pattern of New Deal exclusions (helping some people but not others for political reasons) reflects something structural about how American reform works — using at least one more example beyond the New Deal
  • OR: Construct a framework for what a government owes its citizens in economic crisis — applying it to the Depression and at least one other economic crisis to test whether it holds

docs.title: Skinny Recommendations
docs.text: If you're struggling with... Pull this skinny Analyzing literary elements (symbol, character, theme) R.8 Literary Elements Writing an argument essay with historical evidence W.4 Argument Writing Managing a multi-source research process IR.1–5 Research Bundle Writing an explanatory essay about cause and effect W.3 Explanatory Writing Reading and analyzing informational primary sources R.9 Informational/Argumentative

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: The Great Depression is the last time the American economy failed so completely that it collapsed the basic assumptions most people had about how economic life worked. The debates it produced — What does the government owe people when the economy fails them? How much should the government regulate markets to prevent the next crash? What's the right balance between economic freedom and economic security? — have never been settled. They're the debates behind every argument about the minimum wage, unemployment insurance, Social Security, Medicare, and the Affordable Care Act. The 2008 financial crisis — banks failing, homeowners losing their houses, unemployment spiking — was the closest the U.S. has come to repeating 1929, and the response (bail out the banks, stimulus packages, expanded unemployment) was a direct application of the New Deal framework. The COVID-19 economic shock produced the same arguments again. If you want to understand why Americans argue so bitterly about the government's role in the economy, the Great Depression is where to look. See USH-01: When the System Fails for the earlier reform tradition this studio builds on.

docs.title: Exhibition Format
docs.text:
Suggested format: Gallery walk or formal presentation.

Students display or present their deliverable to classmates, advisor, and invited guests (parents, other teachers). Format for formal presentations: 5-minute presentation, then 2 questions. Gallery walk: students circulate, leave written feedback, then the class debrief focuses on comparing different angles on the same essential question.

What gets scored at exhibition: - C.1 — Presenting your argument or analysis clearly to an audience that hasn't read your sources; handling the format professionally - C.6 — Responding to questions about your evidence and reasoning; engaging with a challenge to your claim

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• USH Unit 3 — Roaring 20s and Great Depression
  • The Great Gatsby — Full Entry
  • FDR's Four Freedoms — Full Entry
  • The Jungle — Full Entry
  • USH-01 — When the System Fails (Progressive Era thread)
  • ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk

Studio Packet · USH-03 · SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

docs.title: The Cost of Victory — Studio Packet
docs.text:
Studio Code: USH-04 Subject Area: US History — Unit 4 (World War II) Suggested Cycle: Year 2, Cycle 3 Duration: 6 weeks

US History Unit Timeline — highlighted segment(s) indicate this studio's historical period.

docs.title: The Essential Question
docs.text: When a nation believes it is fighting on the right side of history, how does it decide which of its own values it can sacrifice — and who pays the price of that decision?

docs.title: Why This Studio Matters
docs.text:
World War II is often called the "Good War" — and for good reason. The Allies were fighting fascism, genocide, and imperial conquest. The United States entered after being attacked. The stakes were genuine, the enemy was real, and the outcome mattered enormously. It's one of the clearer cases in history of fighting for something worth fighting for.

And yet. The United States — the nation fighting for the Four Freedoms, for democracy, for human dignity — imprisoned 120,000 Japanese Americans without charge, without trial, and without evidence. The military that defeated the Nazis was segregated. The country that condemned the Holocaust turned away Jewish refugees before the war. And on August 6 and 9, 1945, the United States deliberately targeted civilian populations in Hiroshima and Nagasaki with the most destructive weapons ever built, killing an estimated 130,000 to 226,000 people, the vast majority of them civilians.

This studio takes the "Good War" seriously — and asks the hardest question you can ask about it: What did the United States justify in the name of victory? Night by Elie Wiesel puts the Holocaust at human scale — not statistics, but one person's experience of what happens when a society decides some lives don't count. FDR's Four Freedoms speech tells you what America said it was fighting for. The gap between those two documents is what this studio lives in.

docs.title: Active Standards
docs.text: Standard What You're Targeting US.6_12.1 + Unit 4 Primary and secondary sources: analyze FDR's "Four Freedoms" and "Day of Infamy" speeches for rhetorical purpose; compare Executive Order 9066 (internment) to the stated values of those speeches US.6_12.2 + Unit 4 Multiple perspectives: pro-bomb vs. anti-bomb on the atomic decision; Japanese American internees' perspective; Holocaust — what the Allies knew and when; Hiroshima survivors' testimony US.6_12.5 + Unit 4 Significant contributions: FDR, Truman, Eleanor Roosevelt; the Manhattan Project; the decision to use the bomb; the Nuremberg Trials as an attempt to define war crimes after the fact 9-10.R.8 Literary elements in Night: how Wiesel uses memoir, witness, and narrative structure to function as testimony — what literary choices he makes that a history book couldn't 9-10.W.4 Argument writing: Was the decision to drop the atomic bomb on Hiroshima and Nagasaki justified? Claim, evidence, counterargument, qualification IR.1–5 Research bundle: active every studio

docs.title: ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk
docs.text:
Several texts in this studio do double duty across ELA and Social Studies standards:

  • Night (Wiesel) — When you analyze it for R.8 (how Wiesel uses narrative structure and detail to function as testimony), you are simultaneously building evidence for US.6_12.2 + Unit 4 (multiple perspectives on the Holocaust) and WH.6_12.2-6 + Era 4 (if you're connecting to the World History thread). Night appears in the ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk as one of the highest-leverage dual-use texts in the library.

  • FDR's Four Freedoms — Analyzing it for R.9 (argument structure, rhetorical choices) also satisfies US.6_12.1 + Unit 4 (primary source analysis). One speech, two standards.

  • Slaughterhouse-Five (Vonnegut) — Literary analysis for R.8 (how Vonnegut uses satire and nonlinear narrative) connects directly to US.6_12.2 + Unit 4 (multiple perspectives on the Allied bombing campaigns and civilian cost of WWII).

A single Demo of Learning can satisfy both an ELA and an SS standard at once if your Studio Contract names both and your analysis addresses both. Talk to Reese in Week 1 if you want to use a crosswalk approach.

See the ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk for the full map.

docs.title: Reading List
docs.text:
Text Why It's Here Night — Wiesel Elie Wiesel's memoir of surviving Auschwitz and Buchenwald puts the Holocaust at human scale. It's not an argument about statistics — it's one person's experience of what a society looks like when it decides some people don't count. Required for R.8 and for grounding the studio's moral question. FDR's Four Freedoms FDR's January 1941 speech defining what America was preparing to fight for: freedom of speech, freedom of worship, freedom from want, freedom from fear. The gap between this speech and Executive Order 9066 (internment) is the studio's central tension. Slaughterhouse-Five — Vonnegut Optional extension: Vonnegut's novel about the Allied firebombing of Dresden (130,000+ civilian deaths) raises the same question about civilian cost of bombing campaigns — written from the American side. Pairs with the atomic bomb decision.

Required minimum: Night and the Four Freedoms speech are the core pairing. Slaughterhouse-Five is for students extending their inquiry into the Allied bombing campaigns and the question of civilian casualties in a "just war."

docs.title: Inquiry Angle Menu
docs.text:
Trailblazer angles: - FDR said America was fighting for "freedom from fear" for "everyone, everywhere." Then he signed Executive Order 9066, imprisoning 120,000 Japanese Americans — including American citizens — without trial. Using both documents, explain what this contradiction reveals about how democratic nations justify restricting freedom during wartime. - Elie Wiesel dedicated Night to transmitting his experience: "Never shall I forget." What does Wiesel want his readers to do with this testimony? Analyze his narrative choices — what he describes in detail, what he leaves out, how he structures time — and explain what they do to the reader.

Maverick angles: - Truman's stated reason for using the atomic bomb: it would end the war faster and save the lives of hundreds of thousands of American soldiers who would have died in a land invasion of Japan. Using evidence from both sides, evaluate this justification. What does the argument assume? What does it leave out? - The Holocaust happened in modern, educated, bureaucratic Germany — not a medieval society but one of the most technologically and culturally advanced nations in Europe. What does Night reveal about how ordinary people participate in genocide? What does it suggest about the conditions that make it possible? - Compare the treatment of Japanese Americans (Executive Order 9066) with the treatment of German Americans and Italian Americans during WWII. Why the difference? What does this reveal about the role race played in how the United States defined its enemies?

Phoenix angles: - The Nuremberg Trials (1945–46) established that "following orders" is not a sufficient defense for war crimes. But American pilots who dropped atomic bombs were following orders. American soldiers who enforced Japanese internment were following orders. Where do you draw the line — and how do you draw it without applying a double standard? Construct an argument using specific evidence from the war. - Night and Slaughterhouse-Five are both books written by survivors about mass civilian death during WWII — but they're completely different in tone, style, and purpose. Wiesel is a witness demanding moral reckoning. Vonnegut is sardonic and detached. Analyze why each writer made the choices he did — and what each approach can and can't do as a response to atrocity.

docs.title: Six-Week Arc
docs.text: Week Phase What You're Doing 1 Launch Read FDR's Four Freedoms (January 6, 1941). Then read the text of Executive Order 9066 (February 19, 1942) — 44 days after Pearl Harbor. What do these two documents, written by the same president 13 months apart, reveal about what America was actually willing to fight for? Submit your Studio Contract. 2 Dig Deep read Night for R.8: How does Wiesel structure his narrative? What does he choose to describe in detail, and what does he pass over quickly? What does this tell you about what he wants you to understand? For the SS strand: research the atomic bomb decision from multiple perspectives — what did Truman know, what did he decide, and what were the alternatives? 3 Build Draft your deliverable. Your central question: What did the United States justify in the name of winning the "Good War" — and was that justification enough? Use specific evidence. 4 Shape Peer review and advisor conference. Identify where your argument is strongest and where it's relying on assumption rather than evidence. The atomic bomb question in particular: make sure you've engaged with the strongest version of the counterargument. 5 Finish Complete demonstration of learning. Polish deliverable. Prepare for exhibition. 6 Publish Exhibition. Reflection. Archive.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning Options
docs.text: Mode Prompt for This Studio 1 — Written Assessment In 500–700 words: Analyze how Wiesel structures Night as testimony. What narrative choices does he make? What do those choices do to you as a reader that a history textbook couldn't? 2 — Extended Writing Argument essay (700–1,000 words): Was the decision to drop atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki justified? Take a clear position, present the strongest evidence for it, engage with the strongest counterargument, and qualify your claim appropriately. 3 — Verbal Conversation Advisor conference: Walk through the studio's central tension. What was the United States fighting for? What did it sacrifice to win? For each case (internment, segregated military, atomic bomb), what was the justification and how strong is it? 4 — Visual/Creative Timeline exhibit: Map the key WWII decisions and their human costs on a visual that shows both the military logic and the civilian price. Annotate each decision: What did the United States say it was doing, and who paid? 5 — Multimedia/Performance Documentary or recorded presentation: "The Good War's Hidden Cost" — using Night, the Four Freedoms, and the atomic bomb decision to examine what the United States justified in the name of defeating fascism. Must demonstrate US.6_12.1+2 and R.8 evidence. 6 — Portfolio Annotation Curate prior work and annotate for US.6_12.1+5 and R.8+W.4 evidence. Commentary must use the proficiency scale language and connect your prior work to the studio's essential question about sacrifice and justification.

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas
docs.text:
1. "What FDR Promised" — A primary source analysis essay: Compare FDR's Four Freedoms speech to Executive Order 9066. What did the president say America stood for, and what did he do 13 months later? What does the gap between these two documents reveal about how democratic governments justify restricting freedom during wartime?

  2. "The Decision" — An argument essay: Evaluate Truman's decision to use the atomic bomb. Was it justified? Use evidence from multiple perspectives: the U.S. military's reasoning, Japan's situation in the summer of 1945, survivor testimony from Hiroshima, and the precedent the decision set.

  3. "Never Shall I Forget" — A literary analysis of Night: Analyze Wiesel's memoir as a deliberate act of witness. What is he trying to make his readers understand? How do his narrative choices — his structure, his details, his tone — serve that purpose?

  4. "The Good War's Other Side" — A research exhibit or documentary: Document one case where the United States' actions during WWII contradicted the values it said it was fighting for (internment, segregated military, turning away Jewish refugees, Allied firebombing campaigns). Analyze: What was the justification? Who paid? Does the justification hold up?

  5. "Hiroshima and After" — A comparative analysis: The atomic bomb was used in 1945 and has never been used in war since. Analyze the precedent the Hiroshima decision set — using the historical record of U.S. nuclear policy, the Cold War arms race, and contemporary debates about nuclear weapons. What did that decision commit the world to?

docs.title: 2.0 — Foundation
docs.text:
• Define: Holocaust, internment, atomic bomb, D-Day, Nuremberg Trials, Hiroshima, Four Freedoms, Executive Order 9066
  • Correctly summarize the major events and timeline of WWII: causes, major turning points, homefront
  • Identify and correctly describe Wiesel's experiences in Night
  • Describe the main arguments for and against using the atomic bomb

docs.title: 3.0 — Target
docs.text:
• Analyze Night for literary elements: how does Wiesel use narrative structure, detail, and witness to function as testimony rather than historical account?
  • Build a clear argument about the atomic bomb decision: take a position, present your strongest evidence, and engage honestly with the counterargument
  • Connect the gap between FDR's stated values (Four Freedoms) and American actions during the war (internment, segregated military) to the studio's essential question about what nations justify in the name of victory
  • Evaluate a specific WWII decision from multiple perspectives — naming who benefited and who paid

docs.title: 4.0 — Transfer
docs.text:
• OR: Argue whether the Nuremberg standard — "following orders is not a defense for war crimes" — can be applied consistently to American decisions during WWII. What does consistent application reveal?
  • OR: Use the WWII pattern (good cause + morally compromised means) to analyze a more recent American military decision — arguing whether the pattern holds and what it reveals about how democracies make wartime choices
  • OR: Analyze Night and Slaughterhouse-Five as two different literary responses to mass civilian death — argue which approach (witness vs. satire) is more effective, and for what purposes

docs.title: Skinny Recommendations
docs.text: If you're struggling with... Pull this skinny Analyzing literary elements in memoir R.8 Literary Elements Writing an argument with a clear claim and counterargument W.4 Argument Writing Analyzing primary source speeches and documents R.9 Informational/Argumentative Managing research with multiple perspectives IR.1–5 Research Bundle Presenting your argument to a live audience C.1 Formal Presentation

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: The question at the center of this studio — what does a country fighting for the right cause get to do in pursuit of victory? — is asked every time a democratic nation goes to war. The atomic bombs killed somewhere between 130,000 and 226,000 people, most of them civilians, and stopped a war. The internment of Japanese Americans was a direct violation of constitutional rights, carried out by the president who gave the Four Freedoms speech. These weren't anomalies — they were the pattern. And that pattern keeps repeating: enhanced interrogation (torture), drone strikes with civilian casualties, indefinite detention without trial — each justified as necessary to win a war that is, of course, on the right side of history. Elie Wiesel's Night is the most important counter to this logic: it records what happens when a nation decides that some lives are acceptable losses. The Holocaust was not an accident or an anomaly — it was the product of exactly the kind of reasoning this studio examines. If you want to follow the thread to Vietnam and beyond, USH-05: What the Government Didn't Say asks what happened when Americans found out the government was applying the same logic to them.

docs.title: Exhibition Format
docs.text:
Suggested format: Panel discussion or Socratic seminar.

Students present their analysis of the studio's central moral question to a panel that includes advisor and at least one outside guest. The panel poses one challenge question to each student's argument. Students are expected to respond with evidence, not just restatement.

What gets scored at exhibition: - C.1 — Presenting a complex moral argument clearly; using your evidence to support your position in front of an audience - C.6 — Responding to challenge questions about your reasoning; listening to other students' arguments and engaging with differences

docs.title: Sensitive Content Note
docs.text: Night contains detailed descriptions of Holocaust atrocities, including the death of family members, starvation, violence, and the systematic murder of prisoners. This content is essential to the text and treated with complete seriousness — it is not gratuitous, but it is unflinching. Students who need support should speak with their advisor before Week 2. The atomic bomb discussion involves photographs and survivor testimony from Hiroshima and Nagasaki, including civilian casualties.

docs.text:
