Studio Packet · WH-01 · SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

docs.title: The Rights We Wrote Down — Studio Packet
docs.text:
Studio Code: WH-02 Subject Area: World History — Era 3 (Age of Revolutions), with Era 1–4 connections Suggested Cycle: Year 1, Cycle 3 Duration: 6 weeks

World History Era Timeline — highlighted segment(s) indicate this studio's historical period.

docs.title: The Essential Question
docs.text: When people write down their rights, what changes — and what doesn't?

docs.title: Why This Studio Matters
docs.text:
From Hammurabi's Code (1750 BCE) to the UN Declaration of Human Rights (1948) to Malala's UN speech (2013), humans have been writing down their rights for 3,700 years. The list grows longer with each generation. And yet every document on this list was written in the shadow of specific injustices that were still happening — or that would happen again.

This studio traces a single thread across all four WH eras: the recurring human project of codifying rights in writing. Students work with five to seven documents from the Rights Chain and investigate a deceptively simple question: Does writing rights down actually work?

docs.title: Active Standards
docs.text: Standard What You're Targeting WH.6_12.3 + Era 3 Revolutionary ideologies and the spread of rights-based political thought WH.6_12.5 + Era 3 Causes and effects of the Atlantic Revolutions WH.6_12.6 Historical continuity and change: how the rights tradition evolves 9-10.R.9 Analyzing argument — how each document constructs its claims about rights 9-10.W.4 Argument writing with multiple sources and a clearly defended claim

docs.title: ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk
docs.text:
This studio is built for crosswalk — analyzing how rights arguments are constructed is both the ELA skill and the SS content at the same time.

  • Common Sense (Paine) — It's one of the highest-leverage dual-use texts in the library. Analyzing Paine's argument structure for R.9 and W.4 (it's a masterclass in persuasive writing) simultaneously satisfies WH.6_12.4-5 + Era 3. There's a dedicated crosswalk entry for it: Common Sense + W.4 Crosswalk.

  • Wollstonecraft, A Vindication of the Rights of Woman — R.9 analysis (how does Wollstonecraft construct her argument? who is her audience? what does she assume?) is identical to WH.6_12.4 + Era 3 analysis (how did Enlightenment ideas about rights extend — and fail to extend — to women?). It's in the ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk.

  • UN Declaration of Human Rights — Analyzing it for R.9 (what claims does it make? what evidence or reasoning does it provide? what does it assume?) also satisfies WH.6_12.6 + Era 4 (historical continuity: how does the rights tradition culminate here?). In the crosswalk table.

  • The rights chain as a whole — A Demo of Learning that traces how rights language evolves from Hammurabi to the UN Declaration is both a W.4 argument (ELA) and a WH.6_12.6 analysis of historical continuity (SS). One demo, two standards.

A single Demo of Learning can satisfy both an ELA and a WH standard if your Studio Contract names both. See the ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk for the full map.

docs.title: The Rights Chain
docs.text:
These documents trace a single lineage of rights-claiming across 3,700 years. You don't need to use all of them — choose at least three that span at least two eras.

Document Date What Rights It Claims What It Leaves Out Hammurabi's Code c. 1750 BCE Protection under law; limits on arbitrary power Enslaved people; women; non-Babylonians Pericles' Funeral Oration 431 BCE Democratic participation; equal access to law Women; enslaved; metics (non-citizens) Magna Carta 1215 CE Limits on royal power; due process Commoners; anyone not a baron Common Sense — Paine 1776 Popular sovereignty; self-governance Enslaved people; women Declaration of the Rights of Man 1789 Liberty, equality, and the consent of the governed Women (see Wollstonecraft); enslaved in colonies Wollstonecraft — Vindication 1792 Women's full citizenship and education Class (she focuses on middle-class women) Haitian Declaration of Independence 1804 Racial equality; end to colonial slavery How the reparations demand complicates the legacy FDR — Four Freedoms 1941 Freedom of speech, religion, from want, from fear — globally Still "as a matter of principle" — no enforcement UN UDHR 1948 Universal rights for all humans everywhere No enforcement mechanism; 48–0 with 8 abstentions Malala — UN Speech 2013 Girls' right to education globally Still happening; UDHR Article 26 is 65 years old

docs.title: Inquiry Angle Menu
docs.text:
Trailblazer angles: - Choose three documents from the Rights Chain and trace how the definition of "rights" changes across them. Who is included in each? Who is excluded? - Compare the Declaration of Rights of Man (1789) and the Haitian Declaration of Independence (1804). Why did Haiti have to write its own declaration? What did France's version leave out?

Maverick angles: - The Rights Chain keeps getting longer — more documents, more rights, more people included. Does this mean progress is real? Or does the growth of the list reveal how incomplete each document was? - Wollstonecraft read the Declaration of Rights of Man and immediately wrote the Vindication of the Rights of Woman. What exactly did she see in the document that required her response? Analyze both. - Three documents on this list were written by people under direct threat: the Haitian Declaration (Dessalines was leading an armed revolution), Malala (nine months after being shot), Mandela (facing death at his sentencing). How does writing rights under threat change the document?

Phoenix angles: - Design the argument for a rights document that would genuinely include everyone the previous ones left out. What would it say? What enforcement mechanism would it have? Use evidence from every document that preceded it. - The UN UDHR was adopted by 48-0 with 8 abstentions. Research: who abstained, and why? What does the abstention pattern reveal about the limits of "universal" rights?

docs.title: Six-Week Arc
docs.text: Week Phase What You're Doing 1 Launch Read the Rights Chain overview table above. Choose three or more documents that interest you. Write a one-paragraph first reaction: Does this chain represent genuine progress, or the same exclusions repeated with different words? Submit your Studio Proposal. 2 Dig Deep read your selected documents. For each: What rights does it claim? For whom? Who wrote it and why? What did they leave out — and was that omission deliberate? Build your source log with at least one secondary source (scholarly context on your chosen era). 3 Build Draft your deliverable. Your draft should take a clear position on your inquiry angle — you're not just describing the documents, you're arguing something about them. 4 Shape Peer review and advisor conference. Bring one passage from your texts that you think is the strongest evidence for your argument — and one that complicates your argument. Revise with those in mind. 5 Finish Complete demonstration of learning. Polish deliverable. Prepare for exhibition. 6 Publish Exhibition. Reflection. Archive.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning Options
docs.text: Mode Prompt for This Studio 1 — Written Assessment In 500–700 words: Select two documents from the Rights Chain at least 100 years apart. Analyze how the argument for rights changes between them — what's the same, what's new, and what each still leaves unresolved. 2 — Extended Writing Argument essay (700–1,000 words): Does the Rights Chain represent genuine progress toward universal rights, or does it represent the same exclusions being redefined with each generation? Argue a clear position with evidence from at least three documents. 3 — Verbal Conversation Advisor conference: Walk through your selected documents, explaining the central rights claim of each, what rhetorical strategy it uses, and what your analysis found about the continuity and change across the chain. 4 — Visual/Creative Visual timeline or infographic: Map the Rights Chain with annotations showing what each document adds, what it leaves out, and how each responds to the documents before it. Must include analysis, not just dates and names. 5 — Multimedia/Performance Documentary-style video or recorded presentation walking the audience through the Rights Chain — what the documents say, what the gaps reveal, and what your argument about the chain is. Minimum 8 minutes. 6 — Portfolio Annotation Curate existing work that demonstrates WH.6_12.3+6 and R.9. Write metacognitive commentary connecting your work to specific passages from at least two Rights Chain documents.

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas
docs.text:
1. "The Rights Gap Report" — A designed document that maps every Rights Chain document against a specific population (women, enslaved people, colonized peoples) and analyzes what each document promises them vs. what they actually received.

  2. "The Next Document" — A draft of a new rights document, written for 2025, that addresses what the UN UDHR still hasn't delivered. Include: the rights you're claiming, who is currently denied them, and an argument for why they must be recognized now. Accompanied by a research essay explaining your choices.

  3. "The Rights Chain Timeline" — A large-format visual exhibit tracing the 10 documents with annotations, quotes, and analysis. Exhibition format: gallery walk where you explain your analysis to visitors.

  4. "Two Declarations" — A comparative analysis essay or website examining the Declaration of Rights of Man (1789) and the Haitian Declaration of Independence (1804) — what Haiti's document reveals about France's, and what both reveal about the limits of "universal" in 1804.

  5. "Rights on Paper, Rights in Practice" — A research presentation examining one right from the UDHR (e.g., Article 26: right to education) and tracing the gap between what the document promises and what actually happened. Uses Malala's speech as a case study.

docs.title: 2.0 — Foundation
docs.text:
• Correctly describe what each Rights Chain document claims and when it was written
  • Define: popular sovereignty, natural rights, declaration vs. treaty, civil rights, human rights
  • Identify at least one group excluded from each document

docs.title: 3.0 — Target
docs.text:
• Analyze the rhetorical strategy of at least two documents: How does each author argue for the rights they're claiming? What assumptions do they share? Where do they differ?
  • Trace the pattern across at least three documents: What grows in the Rights Chain? What never changes?
  • Identify the tension in the chain: every document responds to an injustice that was already happening. Does writing rights down create rights, or just name them?

docs.title: 4.0 — Transfer
docs.text:
• Argue a clear position on whether the Rights Chain represents real progress — using evidence that cuts against your position
  • OR: Identify the structural reason why every rights document leaves someone out — and argue whether this is a flaw in the specific documents or an unavoidable feature of how rights-claiming works
  • OR: Apply the Rights Chain framework to a contemporary rights claim that isn't on the list

docs.title: Skinny Recommendations
docs.text: If you're struggling with... Pull this skinny Reading argumentative texts R.9 Informational/Argumentative Writing a multi-source argument W.4 Argument Writing Tracking main ideas across multiple texts R.4 Main Idea and Theme Source credibility and evaluation IR.1–5 Research Bundle Formal presentation of findings C.1 Formal Presentation

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: The rights chain this studio traces — from Hammurabi to Pericles to Magna Carta to the Enlightenment to the UDHR to Malala — is not finished. Every link in that chain was contested at the time, and every link is still contested now. The Magna Carta's argument that rulers must follow laws is the foundation of every constitutionalism debate happening in any country today. Wollstonecraft's argument that women have the same rational capacity as men — which seemed radical in 1792 — is still being contested in countries where women cannot vote, drive, or travel without permission. The UN Declaration of Human Rights was adopted in 1948 and has no enforcement mechanism; the gap between what it says and what governments do is a live political question every day. When students look at debates about refugee rights, indigenous rights, LGBTQ rights, or disability rights, they are looking at the current edge of this chain — arguments about who the rights humans wrote down actually apply to. Malala isn't historical — she's still alive, still working, and the question she's arguing (does every child have a right to education regardless of gender?) is still unsettled in dozens of countries.

docs.title: Exhibition Format
docs.text:
Suggested format: Symposium with rotating presentations.

Each student presents their deliverable for 5–7 minutes, then takes 3 minutes of questions. Audience members (classmates, invited guests) ask at least one question each.

Alternatively: a gallery walk if deliverables are visual/designed artifacts.

What gets scored at exhibition: - C.1 — formal presentation: register, organization, eye contact, handling of questions - C.6 — discussion engagement: how you respond to questions; how you engage with classmates' work

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:

  • Era 4 Standards Overview

  • Haitian Declaration — Full Entry
  • ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk
  • Common Sense + W.4 Crosswalk

Studio Packet · WH-02 · SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

docs.title: Writing Against Empire — Studio Packet
docs.text:
Studio Code: WH-03 Subject Area: World History — Era 2–3 (Colonialism and Imperialism) Suggested Cycle: Year 1, Cycle 4 Duration: 6 weeks

World History Era Timeline — highlighted segment(s) indicate this studio's historical period.

docs.title: The Essential Question
docs.text: When a powerful system is doing harm, what does it take to challenge it — and what does it cost?

docs.title: Why This Studio Matters
docs.text:
Las Casas was a Spanish priest who condemned the conquest. Kipling was a poet who celebrated empire; H.T. Johnson was the Black minister who responded to him in the same meter. Frederick Douglass was a man who had been enslaved, who learned to read in secret, and who risked his freedom to tell the world what slavery was. Three different centuries, three different forms of empire, three different costs — and all of them writing.

This studio examines what "writing against power" looks like, sounds like, and costs. It builds the R.9 (argument analysis) and IR.4 (source evaluation) skills that every WH studio depends on, while developing the WH.6_12.4 and WH.6_12.5 foundation for understanding colonialism and imperialism — the two systems that shaped the modern world more than almost anything else.

docs.title: Active Standards
docs.text: Standard What You're Targeting WH.6_12.4 + Era 2–3 Cultural contact, exchange, and the mechanisms of colonial control WH.6_12.5 + Era 2–3 Causes and effects of colonialism and imperialism 9-10.R.9 Analyzing argument — rhetorical strategy, audience, purpose, assumptions 9-10.IR.4 Source evaluation — credibility, perspective, bias, what a source sees and misses 9-10.W.4 Argument writing: claim, evidence, counterargument, qualification

docs.title: ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk
docs.text:
Every text in this studio is a rhetorical act — someone writing to challenge a powerful system. Analyzing how they make that argument (ELA: R.9) is the same as analyzing what they were challenging and why (SS: WH.6_12.4-5).

  • Las Casas, A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies — Reading it for R.9 (how does Las Casas construct his argument for stopping the conquest? who is his audience? what does he include and leave out?) simultaneously builds WH.6_12.4-5 + Era 2–3 evidence (colonialism, the role of the Church, how indigenous peoples were treated and described). One text, two standards.

  • Kipling, "The White Man's Burden" — Analyzing Kipling's argument for R.9 (what is he actually arguing? what assumptions does he make that he doesn't state?) is directly building WH.6_12.4-5 + Era 3 evidence about the ideology of imperialism. The ELA question IS the SS question: What argument is imperialism making about itself?

  • Narrative of Frederick Douglass (if used) — R.8 literary analysis (how does Douglass use memoir, narrative, and scene to make his argument?) connects directly to WH.6_12.4-5 + Era 3 (resistance to colonial/slavery systems; the abolitionist movement as a counter-argument to imperial ideology).

A single Demo of Learning can satisfy both an ELA and a WH standard if your Studio Contract names both. See the ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk for the full map of how texts serve both subject areas across all studios.

docs.title: Reading List
docs.text:
Text Why It's Here A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies — Las Casas The insider's denunciation — writing against a system you were once part of; limits and all "The White Man's Burden" — Kipling + H.T. Johnson response Two poems, same meter, opposite arguments — the best source evaluation exercise in the library Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass Writing that puts the author at mortal risk; the relationship between literacy and freedom The Melian Dialogue Optional extension: the ancient version of "the strong do what they can"

Suggested groupings: - Las Casas + Douglass — both insiders to a system they condemn; both using the system's own moral values - Kipling + H.T. Johnson — direct confrontation across the same form; IR.4 source comparison exercise - All four — the full arc from 416 BCE to 1899: Has the argument for empire ever actually changed?

docs.title: Inquiry Angle Menu
docs.text:
Trailblazer angles: - Compare the arguments Las Casas and Kipling make about empire. How does each author justify (or condemn) colonial power? What assumptions does each depend on? - Kipling and H.T. Johnson both wrote poems in 1899. Read both. What does Johnson's response reveal about Kipling's argument that Kipling's poem doesn't acknowledge?

Maverick angles: - Douglass says: "From that moment, I understood the pathway from slavery to freedom." He's talking about learning to read. Why is literacy specifically the threat that slaveholders fear most? Use evidence from his Narrative and from at least one other text. - Las Casas initially proposed importing enslaved Africans as a substitute for indigenous labor before he later repudiated this position. What does this reveal about how even a principled critic of a system can be trapped within its logic? - All three writers (Las Casas, Kipling, Douglass) are writing to an audience that has power over the people they're writing about. How does each author use that power dynamic rhetorically?

Phoenix angles: - "Writing against empire" assumes that written argument can change political structures. Does it? Build a case using specific evidence from these texts about when written argument against power actually worked, and what conditions were necessary. - Design a comparison between the arguments for empire in the 16th century (Las Casas' Spain), the 19th century (Kipling's Britain), and a contemporary example of your choice. Has the argument for imperial or colonial power fundamentally changed, or does it just use different language?

docs.title: Six-Week Arc
docs.text: Week Phase What You're Doing 1 Launch Read the H.T. Johnson poem first — it's shorter and was written as a response. Then read the Kipling poem. What does reading them in this order feel like vs. reading Kipling first? Submit your Studio Proposal with your inquiry angle and deliverable concept. 2 Dig Deep read your primary texts. For IR.4: for each text, ask: Who wrote this? For whom? From what position of power or risk? What do they see that others in their time didn't? What do they miss? Build your source log. 3 Build Draft your deliverable. Get your argument down — what is your answer to the essential question in the context of your specific angle? 4 Shape Peer review and advisor conference. Identify the moment in your draft where your argument is weakest — where a counterargument could defeat you. Revise with that weakness addressed. 5 Finish Complete demonstration of learning. Polish deliverable. Prepare for exhibition. 6 Publish Exhibition. Reflection. Archive.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning Options
docs.text: Mode Prompt for This Studio 1 — Written Assessment In 400–600 words: Choose one text from the reading list. Analyze: What is the author's argument? What rhetorical strategy do they use? Who is their intended audience — and how does that audience shape the argument? 2 — Extended Writing Argument essay (600–900 words): Argue whether "writing against power" — as demonstrated by these texts — is an effective strategy for changing political systems. Use specific evidence from at least two texts. 3 — Verbal Conversation Advisor conference: Walk through the Kipling and H.T. Johnson poems as a source evaluation exercise. Explain what each source sees, what each misses, and what reading them together reveals. 4 — Visual/Creative Side-by-side analysis: Create a visual that puts Kipling and H.T. Johnson in direct conversation — matching stanza by stanza — with annotations explaining how Johnson's poem dismantles Kipling's argument. 5 — Multimedia/Performance Perform a close reading of the Kipling and H.T. Johnson poems — or record a documentary-style analysis of the Las Casas text. Must demonstrate R.9 and IR.4 standard evidence. 6 — Portfolio Annotation Curate prior work and annotate for WH.6_12.4+5, R.9, and IR.4. Commentary must reference specific text passages and explain what changed in your understanding.

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Two Poems, Two Arguments" — A designed, annotated side-by-side of the Kipling and H.T. Johnson poems with an analysis essay. For each stanza: what is Kipling claiming? What is Johnson's response? What does the exchange reveal about how imperial ideology works?

  2. "The Cost of Writing" — A biographical-analytical essay on one of the three writers (Las Casas, Douglass, or H.T. Johnson) focused on the personal risk they took by writing what they wrote. What did they risk? What did they gain? Did it work?

  3. "From the Inside" — A comparative essay on Las Casas and Douglass: both condemned a system they had some relationship to from the inside (Las Casas was a priest and initial colonizer; Douglass was enslaved). How does writing from inside a system you oppose differ from writing from outside it?

  4. "Empire's Arguments" — A research presentation tracing how the argument for colonial or imperial power has been made across three eras — 16th century (Spain), 19th century (Britain), and a third example of your choice. Does the argument ever fundamentally change?

  5. "What Literacy Does" — A research essay or documentary: Why do authoritarian systems attack literacy? Use Douglass as the primary source and find at least two additional historical or contemporary examples (Malala, Fahrenheit 451, another of your choice).

docs.title: 2.0 — Foundation
docs.text:
• Define: colonialism, imperialism, encomienda, chattel slavery, source bias, rhetorical strategy
  • Correctly describe what each author argues and their relationship to the system they're writing about
  • Identify the audience for each text and how that audience shapes the document

docs.title: 3.0 — Target
docs.text:
• Analyze the rhetorical strategy of at least two texts: claim, evidence, audience, what the author assumes the audience already believes
  • Evaluate each text as a source (IR.4): What can you trust? What must you evaluate? What does the author's position cause them to see and miss?
  • Connect each text to its specific historical context: What was happening that made this document necessary? What changed (or didn't) as a result?

docs.title: 4.0 — Transfer
docs.text:
• Construct an argument about "writing against power" that requires synthesis across multiple texts AND a claim that goes beyond what any single text says
  • OR: Apply the analytical framework from this studio to a contemporary example of resistance writing — a text you find that was written against a system of power in your own time
  • OR: Identify what limits all three texts — what none of them can challenge — and argue why that limit exists

docs.title: Skinny Recommendations
docs.text: If you're struggling with... Pull this skinny Analyzing argument structure R.9 Informational/Argumentative Evaluating sources for bias and perspective IR.1–5 Research Bundle Word choice and style in literary texts R.7 Style and Word Choice Writing an argument with a counterargument W.4 Argument Writing

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: Empire didn't end when colonial flags came down. Economic dominance, military presence, cultural influence, and political dependency are all forms of power that the countries that built formal empires still exercise — and the debate about whether that constitutes a continuation of imperialism is very much alive. Las Casas' question — does a powerful country have the right to impose its systems on a weaker one, even if those systems are "better"? — is asked about U.S. foreign policy, Chinese infrastructure investment in Africa, and Russian influence in former Soviet states right now. Kipling's argument (the "white man's burden" is to civilize the world) didn't disappear when formal colonialism ended; it resurfaced in the "nation-building" arguments used to justify the Iraq and Afghanistan wars. And the tradition Las Casas, Douglass, and the counter-poets of imperialism represent — writing against power from inside the system that benefits from it — is what investigative journalists, human rights advocates, and whistleblowers do today. If you're interested in where empire shows up in the present, USH-07: The Unipolar Moment picks up this thread in the American context.

docs.title: Exhibition Format
docs.text:
Suggested format: Panel discussion or structured debate.

Students present their arguments and take questions from a panel (classmates, advisor, invited guest). Alternatively: a Socratic seminar on the essential question using the texts as evidence.

What gets scored at exhibition: - C.1 — if presenting: formal register, organized delivery, handling questions - C.6 — if seminar: active listening, idea building, evidence use

docs.text:

docs.title: One Idea, 130 Years of War — Studio Packet
docs.text:
Studio Code: WH-04 Subject Area: World History — Era 2 (Reformation) and Era 3 (Atlantic Revolutions) Suggested Cycle: Year 1, Cycle 2 Duration: 6 weeks

World History Era Timeline — highlighted segment(s) indicate this studio's historical period.

docs.title: The Essential Question
docs.text: Can one document change the world — and if so, how?

docs.title: Why This Studio Matters
docs.text:
In 1517, a German friar nailed 95 arguments to a church door. He wanted a debate. He got the Thirty Years War. In 1848, two philosophers published a pamphlet. They wanted to describe the future of capitalism. They got the Cold War. In 1776, a pamphlet called Common Sense sold 500,000 copies in a country of 2.5 million people.

This studio investigates the mechanics of how written documents trigger historical change — and why the change is almost never what the author intended. It builds a powerful WH.6_12.5 (causes and effects) skill set alongside the WH.6_12.2 (ideology and belief) framework, while developing W.4 argument writing and R.9 analytical reading.

docs.title: Active Standards
docs.text: Standard What You're Targeting WH.6_12.2 + Era 2–3 How ideological and religious systems drive political change WH.6_12.5 + Era 2–3 Tracing causes and effects through historical causal chains WH.6_12.1 + Era 2 Technology and innovation: the printing press as the accelerant of the Reformation 9-10.R.9 Analyzing the argumentative structure of historical documents 9-10.W.4 Argument writing: building a causal claim with historical evidence

docs.title: ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk
docs.text:
Every primary document in this studio does double duty across ELA and Social Studies:

  • Luther's 95 Theses — When you analyze it for R.9 (argumentative structure: what claims is Luther making, and how does he build his case?), you're simultaneously building evidence for WH.6_12.2-5 + Era 2 (how ideological challenges triggered political and religious change). One document, one Demo, two standards. Luther is also a case study in how R.9 analysis reveals what a document actually says vs. what readers heard — and the gap between those two things is the entire SS argument.

  • Common Sense (Paine) — This is the #2 highest-leverage crosswalk text in the reading library. Reading it for R.9 (argument structure, rhetorical choices, how Paine constructs his case) simultaneously satisfies WH.6_12.4-5 + Era 3 (causes and effects of the Atlantic Revolutions; why Common Sense didn't just describe revolution but helped create it). It has its own dedicated crosswalk entry because it's also the best W.4 mentor text in the library — the structure Paine uses is exactly what a strong argument essay looks like. See the Common Sense + W.4 Crosswalk for the full breakdown.

  • The Communist Manifesto (selections) — Literary analysis for R.9 (how Marx and Engels build a causal argument — their evidence, their structure, what they assume vs. what they prove) connects directly to WH.6_12.5 + Era 3 (causes and effects: what did the Manifesto predict, and what actually happened — and what does the gap between those two things reveal?). The Manifesto is the clearest case in this studio where close reading (ELA) is the historical analysis (SS).

A single Demo of Learning can satisfy both an ELA and an SS standard if your Studio Contract names both and your analysis addresses both dimensions. Talk to Reese in Week 1 if you want to use a crosswalk approach.

See the ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk for the full map of how texts serve both subject areas across all studios.

docs.title: Reading List
docs.text:
Text Why It's Here The 95 Theses — Luther The document that split Western Christianity and triggered 130 years of warfare — and Luther didn't mean for any of it Common Sense — Paine The pamphlet that turned a tax dispute into a revolution — 500,000 copies in a country of 2.5 million The Communist Manifesto — Marx & Engels A pamphlet describing "inevitable" revolution — used to justify systems Marx himself would have rejected Declaration of the Rights of Man Optional: the document that inspired the French Revolution AND the Haitian Revolution AND later inspired its own critique

Minimum requirement: Two documents from the list; at least one must be the 95 Theses or the Communist Manifesto (the cases where the gap between intent and effect is largest).

docs.title: Inquiry Angle Menu
docs.text:
Trailblazer angles: - Luther's 95 Theses led to the Thirty Years War. Walk the causal chain: 95 Theses (1517) → Reformation → German princes' political use of it → Peace of Augsburg (1555) → Thirty Years War (1618–48). At what point could this chain have been broken? What would have had to change? - Paine's Common Sense argued that monarchy was an inherently bad system — not just British monarchy, but monarchy itself. Find three arguments he makes. Which is the most persuasive? Which is the weakest?

Maverick angles: - Marx and Engels predicted an inevitable communist revolution in industrialized countries. The revolution came first in Russia (not industrialized), and the result was something Marx would have called a betrayal of communist principles. What went wrong? Did the Manifesto cause it? - The printing press made the Reformation possible: Luther's theses traveled further in three months than any document had traveled in a century. Find a contemporary parallel: a document or idea that technology spread faster than its author intended — with consequences the author didn't anticipate. - All three documents (95 Theses, Common Sense, Communist Manifesto) were written by people who thought they were describing reality. They turned out to be creating it. Is there a difference?

Phoenix angles: - "Documents don't change history. The conditions that make a document explosive are already present — the document is just the match." Argue for or against this claim using evidence from at least two documents. - Compare the reception of the 95 Theses in 1517 and the Communist Manifesto in 1848. Both were initially dismissed. Both eventually triggered massive historical change. What conditions were necessary for each to become explosive — and why did those conditions take the time they did?

docs.title: Six-Week Arc
docs.text: Week Phase What You're Doing 1 Launch Read this prompt: "In 1517, Martin Luther's academic debate memo about Church corruption accidentally launched 130 years of war, permanently fragmented Western Christianity, and created the conditions for the modern state system. He died before seeing most of it." Read the introduction to the 95 Theses. Does that claim surprise you? Submit your Studio Proposal. 2 Dig Deep read your selected documents. For each: What did the author think they were doing? What actually happened? What was already happening in the world that made the document's reception explosive? Secondary source: find historical context for at least one document's reception. 3 Build Draft your deliverable. You need a clear answer to: What makes a document historically explosive? Is it the document, the context, the technology, the opposition, or something else? 4 Shape Peer review and advisor conference. Identify the weakest link in your causal chain — the place where someone could argue that the document didn't really cause what you say it caused. Revise with that addressed. 5 Finish Complete demonstration of learning. Polish deliverable. Prepare for exhibition. 6 Publish Exhibition. Reflection. Archive.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning Options
docs.text: Mode Prompt for This Studio 1 — Written Assessment In 500–700 words: Trace the causal chain from one document to one specific historical consequence at least 50 years later. At each step in the chain, identify whether the connection is direct or requires additional context. 2 — Extended Writing Argument essay (700–1,000 words): Argue whether documents drive historical change or whether historical conditions determine which documents become important. Use evidence from at least two documents in this studio. 3 — Verbal Conversation Advisor conference: Walk through one document — explain what the author thought they were doing, what actually happened, what the causal chain looked like, and at what point the author lost control of their own idea. 4 — Visual/Creative Causal chain diagram: Map the causal chain from one document to its long-term consequences, with annotations at each step explaining what connected them and what could have broken the chain. 5 — Multimedia/Performance Documentary or recorded presentation: "How a Pamphlet Started a War" — the story of one document's unexpected historical consequences. Must demonstrate WH.6_12.5 causal chain analysis. 6 — Portfolio Annotation Curate existing work and annotate for WH.6_12.2+5 and R.9 evidence. Commentary must reference specific document passages and explain your causal reasoning.

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas
docs.text:
1. "The Chain Reaction" — A visual + essay exhibit tracing the causal chain from one document to its long-term consequences. Designed for a general audience. Annotation at each link: What connected these events? What could have broken the chain?

  2. "The Map the Author Didn't Draw" — An essay analyzing the gap between what an author intended and what their document caused. Central question: Who owns an idea once it's published? Focus on one document (95 Theses, Communist Manifesto, or Common Sense).

  3. "Technology and Ideas" — A research presentation comparing the printing press's role in the Reformation to a technology that accelerated ideological spread in the 20th or 21st century. What makes a technology an "accelerant" for ideas?

  4. "The Unintended Consequences Exhibit" — A museum-style multi-panel exhibit covering three documents and their unintended consequences. Each panel: the document, what the author intended, what actually happened, and what that gap reveals.

  5. "What Marx Got Wrong" — A research essay or documentary: What did Marx predict? What actually happened? Why did communist revolutions happen in agrarian countries (Russia, China, Cuba) instead of industrial ones? What does this reveal about the relationship between ideas and material conditions?

docs.title: 2.0 — Foundation
docs.text:
• Define: Protestant Reformation, pamphlet, causal chain, ideology, unintended consequences
  • Correctly describe what each document argues and what happened as a result
  • Identify the printing press's role in the Reformation

docs.title: 3.0 — Target
docs.text:
• Trace a complete causal chain from one document to a consequence at least two steps removed from the original
  • Analyze the gap between the author's intent and the document's historical effect — what caused the gap?
  • Compare two documents: What made each one explosive in its specific historical context? What conditions were necessary?

docs.title: 4.0 — Transfer
docs.text:
• Construct an argument about what makes documents historically consequential — using evidence from multiple documents and addressing the strongest counterargument
  • OR: Apply the causal-chain framework to a contemporary document or idea you identify yourself — something that is generating consequences its author didn't intend
  • OR: Argue whether the unintended consequences of these documents were the documents' "fault" — and what that question even means

docs.title: Skinny Recommendations
docs.text: If you're struggling with... Pull this skinny Reading complex historical arguments R.9 Informational/Argumentative Building a causal argument in writing W.4 Argument Writing Organizing a research process IR.1–5 Research Bundle Explanatory writing for causal analysis W.3 Explanatory Writing

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: The question at the center of this studio — can one document or one idea actually change the world? — is the question behind every social movement, every declaration, every manifesto written since Luther nailed his theses to a church door. The answer this era gives is: yes, but not in the way the author intended, and not without violence, and not without creating new problems alongside the ones it solved. The Protestant Reformation was supposed to reform the church; it created 130 years of religious war. The American Revolution was supposed to secure liberty; it preserved slavery for 80 more years. The Communist Manifesto was supposed to liberate the working class; it became the justification for regimes that killed millions. These aren't reasons to dismiss the ideas — they're reasons to take seriously the question of how ideas actually travel from a document to a changed world. That question is live right now in every debate about whether writing a law, signing a treaty, or passing a resolution actually does anything. If you're working in WH-02 alongside this studio, the two studios are in direct conversation.

docs.title: Exhibition Format
docs.text:
Suggested format: Symposium presentation + Q&A.

Each student presents their causal chain analysis (5–7 minutes), then takes questions specifically about the links in the chain — audience members try to find places where the chain is weak.

What gets scored at exhibition: - C.1 — formal presentation: organized argument, handling challenges to your causal chain - C.6 — discussion: responding to counterarguments with evidence

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Era 2 Standards Overview
  • Era 3 Standards Overview
  • Studio WH-02: The Rights We Wrote Down — The rights documents on that list are also candidates for the causal-chain analysis here
  • W.3 Explanatory Writing Skinny
  • ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk — Full map of dual-use texts across all studios
  • Common Sense + W.4 Crosswalk — Paine as W.4 mentor text and WH.6_12.4 Revolution cause

Studio Packet · WH-04 · SDA Commons Wiki · South High School

docs.title: The Cost of War — Studio Packet
docs.text:
Studio Code: WH-05 Subject Area: World History — Era 3–4 (WWI through WWII and Cold War) Suggested Cycle: Year 1, Cycle 5 Duration: 6 weeks

World History Era Timeline — highlighted segment(s) indicate this studio's historical period.

docs.title: The Essential Question
docs.text: Who pays the cost of war — and who gets to tell the story?

docs.title: Why This Studio Matters
docs.text:
Wilfred Owen wrote "Dulce et Decorum Est" in a field hospital, dying of the gas that would kill him eight days before the Armistice. The Kipling poem that sent his generation to die called it sweet and fitting. FDR's Four Freedoms speech asked the world to believe in a war being fought for universal ideals. Night was written by a 15-year-old who survived Auschwitz. Persepolis was drawn by a woman who lived through the Iranian Revolution as a child.

This studio builds WH.6_12.2 (ideology and propaganda) and WH.6_12.5 (war and its consequences) while developing the literary analysis skills in R.7 and R.8 — style, word choice, and literary elements as evidence of historical meaning. It asks students to read multiple genres of war narrative simultaneously and compare what each reveals.

docs.title: Active Standards
docs.text: Standard What You're Targeting WH.6_12.2 + Era 3–4 Ideology, propaganda, and the narratives used to justify and mobilize for war WH.6_12.5 + Era 4 The causes and consequences of WWI and WWII 9-10.R.7 Analyzing how word choice, syntax, and style create meaning 9-10.R.8 Literary elements: imagery, tone, irony, structure as carriers of meaning 9-10.R.9 Comparing arguments across texts: what is each text's implicit argument about war?

docs.title: ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk
docs.text:
Every text in this studio does double duty across ELA and Social Studies standards:

  • "Dulce et Decorum Est" (Owen) — When you analyze it for R.7 (how word choice, syntax, and rhythm create meaning) or R.8 (imagery, irony, and tone as carriers of argument), you're simultaneously building WH.6_12.2-5 + Era 3 evidence — because Owen's poem is a direct counter-argument to the propaganda that sent his generation to die. Analyzing how the poem works as a text is analyzing how WWI ideology was challenged.

  • Night (Wiesel) — R.8 literary analysis (how Wiesel uses memoir structure, witness, and narrative detail to function as testimony) simultaneously satisfies WH.6_12.2-6 + Era 4 (ideology, genocide, and the human cost of WWII). Night is one of the highest-leverage dual-use texts in the reading library — it appears in the ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk and also in USH-04: The Cost of Victory, where it serves the US History angle. If you've analyzed Night through a USH lens, this studio gives you the WH Era 4 angle instead — and the comparison of how you read it differently each time is itself a strong 4.0 move.

  • Persepolis (Satrapi) — R.8 analysis (how Satrapi uses graphic memoir format — panel composition, visual language, personal narrative — as argument and testimony) connects to WH.6_12.2 + Era 4 (ideology and lived experience under political upheaval). Persepolis also bridges to USH: it appears in USH-07: The Unipolar Moment with a U.S.–Iran angle. Analyzing it here gives you the WH foundation that makes that later studio go deeper.

A single Demo of Learning can satisfy both an ELA and an SS standard if your Studio Contract names both and your analysis addresses both dimensions. Talk to Reese in Week 1 if you want to use a crosswalk approach.

See the ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk for the full map of how texts serve both subject areas across all studios.

docs.title: Reading List
docs.text:
Text Genre Why It's Here "Dulce et Decorum Est" — Owen Poetry The soldier's-eye anti-war poem; uses the old justification as the punchline FDR — Four Freedoms Speech Speech The idealist justification for WWII; "everywhere in the world" Night — Wiesel Memoir/Literary text What the war was doing while politicians talked about freedom Persepolis — Satrapi Graphic memoir War from a child's perspective; the Iranian Revolution as daily life "The White Man's Burden" — Kipling Poetry Optional: the imperial ideology that sent a generation to die in WWI

Required pairing: Dulce et Decorum Est must be read against at least one justification text (FDR, Kipling, or Pericles' Funeral Oration). Owen's poem is a direct response to the "sweet and fitting" tradition.

docs.title: Inquiry Angle Menu
docs.text:
Trailblazer angles: - Owen's poem ends: "The old Lie: Dulce et decorum est / Pro patria mori." Find the "old Lie" in one other text — a passage where a leader uses the language of honor, sacrifice, or duty to justify war. Compare how Owen and that author use the same tradition differently. - FDR says the Four Freedoms should apply "everywhere in the world." Using Night or Persepolis, evaluate that claim. Did it?

Maverick angles: - Compare how three texts narrate the experience of war — Owen (poem), Wiesel (memoir), Satrapi (graphic memoir). What does each genre allow the author to show that the others can't? What does each genre hide? - FDR's Four Freedoms and Owen's "Dulce et Decorum Est" are both making arguments about what war is for. But they're not in conversation — they were written 23 years apart, about different wars. Put them in conversation: What would Owen say to FDR? What would FDR say to Owen? - Night and Persepolis are both children's-eye views of war and political violence. What does a child narrator reveal about these events that an adult narrator can't? What does a child narrator miss?

Phoenix angles: - "War narratives are always propaganda — even the anti-war ones." Argue for or against this claim using specific evidence from at least three texts in this studio. - Design a comparison of how three different governments justified the same war to their own people. Use the rhetorical analysis skills from R.9 — how does each government's narrative shape what its citizens were willing to do? - Owen died one week before the Armistice; Wiesel survived Auschwitz; Satrapi survived the Iranian Revolution. How does the survivor/non-survivor distinction affect what a war narrative can say? Does it matter that Owen didn't survive?

docs.title: Six-Week Arc
docs.text: Week Phase What You're Doing 1 Launch Read "Dulce et Decorum Est" first. Then read FDR's Four Freedoms opening paragraph. Do these two texts agree about anything? Where do they conflict? Submit your Studio Proposal. 2 Dig Deep read your selected texts. For R.7 and R.8: mark every word choice, image, or structural choice that carries meaning. For each: What is the author's implicit argument about what war does to people? How do they construct it? 3 Build Draft your deliverable. Your core analytical challenge: What does each text's genre allow it to show about war — and what does it prevent from being shown? 4 Shape Peer review and advisor conference. Find the place in your analysis where you're describing what a text says instead of analyzing how it works. Revise to push past description into analysis. 5 Finish Complete demonstration of learning. Polish deliverable. Prepare for exhibition. 6 Publish Exhibition. Reflection. Archive.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning Options
docs.text: Mode Prompt for This Studio 1 — Written Assessment In 400–600 words: Choose one passage from the reading list and analyze how specific word choices and literary techniques create the author's implicit argument about war. Don't summarize — analyze. 2 — Extended Writing Argument essay (600–900 words): Compare two texts from the reading list that approach war from opposite perspectives. What does each text's implicit argument reveal that the other can't? What is each text's blind spot? 3 — Verbal Conversation Advisor conference: Walk through "Dulce et Decorum Est" line by line, explaining how Owen uses specific images, word choices, and structural elements to make his argument. Then: Put Owen in conversation with one justification text — what is he responding to? 4 — Visual/Creative Visual analysis: Create an annotated close reading of Owen's poem — or an annotated spread from Persepolis — that maps how specific artistic/literary choices create meaning. Include written analysis of your annotations. 5 — Multimedia/Performance Dramatic reading of Owen's poem + analysis: Perform the poem, then walk the audience through your analytical reading — what each line means, how it builds the argument, what "the old Lie" is and where it comes from. 6 — Portfolio Annotation Curate prior work and annotate for R.7+8 and WH.6_12.2+5. Commentary must reference specific textual evidence and show how you moved from observation to interpretation.

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas
docs.text:
1. "The Soldier's Version vs. The Leader's Version" — A parallel close reading of Owen and FDR (or Kipling): two texts, the same war-related topic, completely different framings. Who is included in each narrative? Who is invisible?

  2. "Genre and Truth" — A research essay or documentary: Can you tell the truth about war in a poem? In a memoir? In a political speech? Compare how three genres (poetry, memoir, political speech) construct the "truth" about war — and argue which is most reliable.

  3. "The Visual Language of Trauma" — Using Persepolis or Night as the primary text: How does a visual or literary narrator represent traumatic experience? What can be shown in images that can't be written? What can be written that can't be drawn?

  4. "What the Propaganda Doesn't Show" — Research a piece of WWI or WWII propaganda and put it in dialogue with a survivor account. What does the propaganda say about the war? What does the survivor account reveal that the propaganda couldn't?

  5. "Then vs. Now" — A comparative exhibit: How does society talk about war today vs. how it talked about it in 1917 or 1944? Use the Owen/FDR/Night framework and find a contemporary war narrative to compare.

docs.title: 2.0 — Foundation
docs.text:
• Define: imagery, irony, tone, genre, propaganda, primary source vs. secondary source
  • Correctly identify the historical context for each text (what war? what moment? who is the audience?)
  • Name and describe at least two literary or rhetorical techniques used in each text

docs.title: 3.0 — Target
docs.text:
• Analyze how specific word choices and literary elements construct meaning — not "Owen uses imagery" but "Owen uses the image of drowning in green gas to replace the traditional military death-in-battle with something the 'Lie' has no language for"
  • Compare at least two texts' implicit arguments about war — not just what they say, but how their genre, form, and choices construct those arguments
  • Connect each text to its historical moment: What was happening that made this text necessary? What narrative was it responding to or opposing?

docs.title: 4.0 — Transfer
docs.text:
• Construct an argument about what war narrative genre reveals or conceals — using evidence from multiple texts and a claim that generalizes beyond the specific texts
  • OR: Apply this studio's analytical framework to a war narrative you find yourself — from a different war, genre, or perspective than the texts in the library
  • OR: Argue whether the distinction between "anti-war" and "pro-war" narratives is as clear as it seems — using specific evidence that complicates the binary

docs.title: Skinny Recommendations
docs.text: If you're struggling with... Pull this skinny Reading for literary elements R.8 Literary Elements Analyzing word choice and style R.7 Style and Word Choice Comparing arguments across texts R.9 Informational/Argumentative Writing analysis (not summary) W.3 Explanatory Writing

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: The question of who pays the cost of war — and who gets to tell the story — has never been more contested. When governments talk about military action, they talk about strategic objectives, national interests, and acceptable casualties. Wilfred Owen talked about what it felt like to watch someone drown in their own lungs from a mustard gas attack. Both are true, and the gap between them is where the real argument about war lives. In every conflict right now — Ukraine, Gaza, Sudan, Myanmar — there are official accounts and there are the accounts of people who live where the bombs fall. The question of whose story counts, whose losses get named, and who gets to define what the war was for is not an abstract question — it's the question every journalist, every filmmaker, and every person sharing a video on social media is navigating in real time. Night was written so that the Holocaust would never be told only in numbers. Persepolis was drawn so that Iran would not be only a foreign policy abstraction. That impulse — to make sure power doesn't get to be the only narrator — is what this studio is about, and it's as urgent now as it was in 1914 or 1939.

docs.title: Exhibition Format
docs.text:
Suggested format: Gallery reading + discussion.

Each student reads a key passage from their primary text aloud, then presents their analysis. Audience members ask: "What does this text say about who pays the cost of war?"

Alternatively: a timed Socratic seminar using the essential question with at least three texts represented in the discussion.

What gets scored at exhibition: - C.1 — if presenting: reading aloud, explaining analysis to a non-specialist audience - C.6 — if seminar: building on classmates' textual observations, adding evidence

docs.title: The Connected World — Studio Packet
docs.text:
Studio Code: WH-06 Subject Area: World History — Era 2 (Islamic Golden Age, Silk Road, Global Trade Networks) Suggested Cycle: Year 1, Cycle 2 (can pair with WH-04) Duration: 6 weeks

World History Era Timeline — highlighted segment(s) indicate this studio's historical period.

docs.title: The Essential Question
docs.text: What connects the world — and who decides who counts as part of it?

docs.title: Why This Studio Matters
docs.text:
Most students arrive with a mental model of the medieval world as fragmented, dark, and Europe-centered. Ibn Battuta's Rihla is the antidote: in the 14th century, a Moroccan scholar traveled 75,000 miles using a connected system of Islamic law, shared language, and trade networks that made him an insider in Cairo, Delhi, Timbuktu, and Beijing. The connected world didn't start with European exploration in 1492. It started long before — and 1492 changed whose network was central, not whether a network existed.

This studio builds WH.6_12.1 (technology and trade) and WH.6_12.4 (cultural contact and exchange) while developing IR.4 (source evaluation) and IR.1–5 (research process). It's the studio that shifts the map students are working from.

docs.title: Active Standards
docs.text: Standard What You're Targeting WH.6_12.1 + Era 2 Trade networks, technology, and the material infrastructure of the connected world WH.6_12.4 + Era 2 Cultural contact and exchange: what gets adopted, what gets rejected, and why 9-10.IR.1–5 Full research process: question, sources, synthesis, source evaluation, citation 9-10.R.9 Analyzing implicit argument: What is Ibn Battuta's implicit claim about the Islamic world?

docs.title: ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk
docs.text:
The primary source in this studio is Ibn Battuta's Rihla — a travel memoir. That makes it a natural crosswalk text: analyzing it as a piece of writing (ELA) is the same as analyzing it as historical evidence (SS).

  • Ibn Battuta's Rihla — Reading it for R.9 (what is Ibn Battuta's implicit argument about the Islamic world and its connections? what does he choose to describe, what does he leave out, and what does that reveal about his perspective and purpose?) simultaneously builds WH.6_12.4 + Era 2 evidence (cultural contact, exchange, and how trade networks operated across the connected world). You can't separate the literary analysis from the historical analysis — understanding how he writes helps you understand what he reveals.

  • UN Declaration of Human Rights (if used as extension) — R.9 analysis (what claims does the UDHR make? what does it assume about universal values?) connects to WH.6_12.6 + Era 4 (how do the trade and cultural connections of Era 2 eventually contribute to a shared international framework?). Short text, strong crosswalk.

A single Demo of Learning can satisfy both an ELA and a WH standard if your Studio Contract names both and your analysis addresses both dimensions. See the ELA ↔ SS Crosswalk for the full map of how texts serve both subject areas across all studios.

docs.title: Reading List
docs.text:
Text Why It's Here Rihla (Travels) — Ibn Battuta The primary inside-out view of the 14th-century connected world — 75,000 miles, 44 countries, one traveler The Analects — Confucius Optional: the Eastern philosophical tradition that shaped a major connected civilization Ibn Battuta visited UN UDHR Optional extension: What does "universal" connection look like in 1948 vs. 1354?

Primary source pairing: Ibn Battuta + Marco Polo — compare what two travelers from different civilizations noticed about the same 14th-century world. The Rihla library entry includes the comparison table.

docs.title: Inquiry Angle Menu
docs.text:
Trailblazer angles: - Map Ibn Battuta's route. Mark every city he describes as sophisticated or prosperous. What does this map tell you about which parts of the 14th-century world were most connected? What does it leave out? - Ibn Battuta describes the Mali Empire's sultan as almost perfectly just — and then is scandalized by women's dress. What does this combination of admiration and judgment reveal about how his framework works?

Maverick angles: - Compare Ibn Battuta's and Marco Polo's accounts of any city, region, or phenomenon both describe. What does each traveler notice? What does each miss? What does the comparison reveal about how a traveler's origin shapes what they find? - The Islamic world of the 14th century was connected by shared law (Islamic jurisprudence), shared scholarly language (Arabic), and shared trade routes. What is the equivalent today — what makes the contemporary world connected? Is it the same kind of connection? - Ibn Battuta's framework (Islamic law and scholarship) is what made him an insider in so many places. But there are places where his framework makes him an outsider — where does he fail to understand what he's observing?

Phoenix angles: - "The Islamic Golden Age was the world's most sophisticated civilization in the 14th century, and the European-focused curriculum systematically ignores it." Evaluate this claim: Is it true? Is it fair? What would need to change for the claim to be false? - Design a comparison of the dar al-Islam (Islamic connected world, 14th century), the Silk Road trade network (Han dynasty–Mongol era), and the contemporary internet as three different "connection technologies." What does each system include? Who does each exclude? What do people trade through each?

docs.title: Six-Week Arc
docs.text: Week Phase What You're Doing 1 Launch Read the Ibn Battuta entry's opening description of Cairo. Then look at a modern map of the world. Where is Cairo on that map — center or edge? Why does your mental model of "center" matter for how you read history? Submit your Studio Proposal. 2 Dig Deep read Ibn Battuta (and your secondary source on medieval Islamic trade networks or the Mali Empire). IR.4 focus: What can you trust about Ibn Battuta's account? What is he seeing through his own framework? What secondary source helps you evaluate his account? 3 Build Draft your deliverable. Your central challenge: How do you show a connected world that the default mental model ignores? What evidence will shift your audience's mental map? 4 Shape Peer review and advisor conference. Find the place in your argument where you're relying on something Ibn Battuta says without evaluating whether it's reliable. Revise to show your source evaluation work. 5 Finish Complete demonstration of learning. Polish deliverable. Prepare for exhibition. 6 Publish Exhibition. Reflection. Archive.

docs.title: Demonstration of Learning Options
docs.text: Mode Prompt for This Studio 1 — Written Assessment In 400–600 words: Analyze how Ibn Battuta's Rihla constructs an implicit argument about the Islamic world's sophistication and connectivity. What does he choose to describe, and what does that choice reveal? 2 — Extended Writing Argument essay (600–900 words): Using evidence from the Rihla and at least one secondary source, argue either that the Islamic Golden Age is systematically underrepresented in Western history curricula — or that the claim is overstated. 3 — Verbal Conversation Advisor conference: Walk through three passages from the Rihla — one where you trust Ibn Battuta completely, one where his Islamic-scholar framework shapes his judgment, and one where he's wrong by today's standards. For each, explain your reasoning. 4 — Visual/Creative Annotated map: Map Ibn Battuta's route with annotations that explain what the map reveals about medieval connectivity. Annotations must connect to specific text evidence and explain what the map can and can't show. 5 — Multimedia/Performance Documentary or presentation: "The World Ibn Battuta Saw" — a guided tour of the 14th-century connected world for an audience that doesn't know it exists. Must demonstrate WH.6_12.1+4 and IR.4 evidence. 6 — Portfolio Annotation Curate prior work and annotate for WH.6_12.1+4 and IR.4 evidence. Commentary must reference specific passages from the Rihla and explain how your source evaluation shaped your analysis.

docs.title: Deliverable Ideas
docs.text:
1. "Rethinking the Map" — A redesigned world map of the 14th century that centers the Islamic trade networks instead of Europe. Annotations explain: What made each city important? What connected them? Accompanied by an analysis essay.

  2. "Two Travelers, Two Worlds" — A comparative analysis of Ibn Battuta and Marco Polo's accounts of the same era. Who saw more? Whose account is more reliable? What does the comparison reveal about how a traveler's origin shapes their perception?

  3. "The Connected World: Then and Now" — A visual essay comparing medieval connectivity (Silk Road, dar al-Islam) to contemporary global connectivity. What's the same? What's different? Who is included? Who is excluded?

  4. "Reading Ibn Battuta" — A source evaluation essay: How do you use a 700-year-old travel account as historical evidence? What can it tell you? What can it not tell you? How do you know when to trust it? Demonstrate the full IR.4 standard using the Rihla as your case study.

  5. "The Mali Empire: What Ibn Battuta Found" — A research report using Ibn Battuta's West Africa accounts and secondary sources about the Mali Empire. What was Mansa Musa's Mali at its height? How does Ibn Battuta's account compare to modern historical scholarship?

docs.title: 2.0 — Foundation
docs.text:
• Define: dar al-Islam, Silk Road, trade network, hajj, source bias, lateral reading
  • Correctly describe Ibn Battuta's route and the major civilizations he visited
  • Identify what Ibn Battuta is — his background, profession, and what drove his travel

docs.title: 3.0 — Target
docs.text:
• Analyze Ibn Battuta's Rihla as both a historical source and an implicit argument
  • Evaluate the Rihla as a source (IR.4): What can you trust? What does his Islamic-scholar framework cause him to see and miss? What needs corroboration?
  • Compare the Rihla to at least one other source (secondary or Marco Polo comparison) and explain what the comparison adds to your understanding

docs.title: 4.0 — Transfer
docs.text:
• Construct an argument about what "connectivity" means — using the medieval Islamic world as your primary historical evidence and connecting to a contemporary example
  • OR: Apply the Ibn Battuta source-evaluation framework (credible observer, shaped perspective, incomplete) to a contemporary travel account, news report, or eyewitness source
  • OR: Argue what it reveals about WH curricula that Ibn Battuta is rarely taught — is that a curriculum problem, a source problem, or something else?

docs.title: Skinny Recommendations
docs.text: If you're struggling with... Pull this skinny Evaluating sources for bias and perspective IR.1–5 Research Bundle Analyzing implicit argument R.9 Informational/Argumentative Presenting research to an audience C.1 Formal Presentation Formulating a research question IR.1–5 Research Bundle

docs.title: Why This Is Relevant Today?
docs.text: The world Ibn Battuta traveled — a network of trade routes, shared languages, common religious practices, and cosmopolitan cities where scholars from different traditions compared notes — looks more like the internet than it looks like the nation-state world we're used to. And the questions his journey raises are the questions globalization raises: Who controls the routes? Who decides who gets to cross borders? Whose culture travels freely and whose gets stopped? The Silk Road connected China, Central Asia, the Arab world, and Europe in a network that no single power controlled. Today's equivalent — global shipping lanes, undersea internet cables, trade agreements — is also a network that multiple powers are competing to control, and the competition has exactly the same logic as the competition that killed the Silk Road. The UN Declaration of Human Rights' argument that all people have equal dignity regardless of where they were born is an argument about who "counts" as part of the connected world — and it is contested as much now as it was in 1948. If you're interested in how the connected world works today, USH-07: The Unipolar Moment covers the moment when the U.S. tried to run that network alone.

docs.title: Exhibition Format
docs.text:
Suggested format: Visual gallery walk or map presentation.

Students display their maps, visual essays, or research exhibits. Audience circulates and asks questions. The exhibitor explains their analytical choices and answers IR.4 questions: "Why do you trust this source? What did you have to corroborate?"

What gets scored at exhibition: - C.1 — formal presentation: organized explanation of your analytical process, register - C.6 — discussion: responding to source-credibility questions with evidence-based reasoning
